source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: . L De mundi creatione. Deus aute creationem mundi absqae initio foit et est, et ipse lux et splendor fuit et est et vita fait. Cum ergo deus mundum facere voluit, illum de nihilo fecit, sed in yoluntate ipsius materia mundi erat. De hyle. Nam cum voluntas dei ad operandum opus se ostendit, mox de ipsa voluntate et quomodo deus voluit materia mundi ut obscurus globus et informis processit. De angelorum creatione. Et verbum patris sonuit: fiat lux, et facta est lux et angeli lucentes.
text_translation: L De mundi creatione. Deus autem creationem mundi absque initio fuit et est, et ipse lux et splendor fuit et est et vita fuit. Cum ergo Deus mundum facere voluit, illum de nihilo fecit, sed in voluntate ipsius materia mundi erat. De hyle. Nam cum voluntas Dei ad operandum opus se ostendit, mox de ipsa voluntate et quomodo Deus voluit materia mundi ut obscurus globus et informis procedit. De angelorum creatione. Et verbum Patris sonuit: fiat lux, et facta est lux et angeli lucentes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quod dixit fiat lux, absque luminaribus et formata lux, quod Angeli sunt. Sed cum dixit fiant luminaria, hoc est lumen aerium, quod videmus. De Ludferi casu. Lucifer autem ad aquilonem locum vacantem nec aliquld operantem vidit et ibi sedem suam ponere voluit, quatinus illic plura et maiora deo operaretur, voluntatem ipsius ad creationem reliquarum creaturarum ignorans. Nam faciem patris non inspexit nec fortitudinem eius scivit nec bonitatem ipsius gustavit, quod, antequam haec sensisset, deo rebellare temptavit.
text_translation: For what he said, "Let there be light," without luminaries and formed light, which are the angels. But when he said, "Let the luminaries be made," that is, the celestial light which we see. On the Fall of Lucifer. Lucifer, however, saw an empty place in the north, nor anything operating, and there he wished to place his seat, so that there he might work more and greater things for God, ignoring his will regarding the creation of the remaining creatures. For he did not look upon the face of the Father, nor did he know his strength, nor did he taste his goodness, which, before he had sensed these things, he attempted to rebel against God.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deus enim haec nondum manifestaverat sed ea occultaverat, velut aliquis potens et fortis vir facit, qui fortitudinem suam interdum aliis hominibus eam nescientibus occultat, donec videat, quid iUi de se sentiant et quid incipere et quid facere velint. £t cum Lucifer in perversa voluntate sua se ad nihilum erigeret, quoniam nihilum fuit, quod facere voluit, in illud cecidit et stare non potuit, quia sub se fundum non habnit. Nam nec snpra se summitatem nec subtus se extremitatem habuit, quae illum sustinere posset, ne caderet.
text_translation: God indeed had not yet revealed these things but had concealed them, as a powerful and strong man does, who sometimes conceals his strength from other men who do not know it, until he sees what they feel about him and what they intend to begin and do. And when Lucifer, in his perverse will, raised himself toward nothingness, because nothing was what he wished to do, he fell into that and could not stand, because he had no foundation beneath him. For he had neither above him a summit nor below him an extremity that could support him, so that he would not fall.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim ille ad nihilum se extendit, inceptio extensionis eius malum produxit, et mox malum hoc abs que claritate et absque luce in semet ipso per zelum dei exarsit, ut rota se circumferens et circumvolvens, et ignitas tenebras in se ostendit. Et sic malum a bono declinavit, nec bonum tetigit malum nec malum bonum. Deus autem integer ut rota permansit et pater in bonitate, quia patemitas ipsius bonitate ipsius plena est, et ita iustissima et benignissima et firmissima atque fortissima est patemitas, et de hac mensura sicut rota ponitur.
text_translation: When indeed he extends himself to nothingness, the beginning of his extension produced evil, and soon this evil, without clarity and without light, in itself through the zeal of God flared up, like a wheel turning and revolving, and showing in itself burning darkness. And thus evil turned away from good, neither did evil touch good nor good touch evil. But God remained whole as a wheel, and the Father in goodness, because his patience is filled with his own goodness, and thus patience is most just and most kind and most firm and most strong, and from this measure it is set as a wheel.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nunc alicubi rota est et ipsa alicuius rei plena est. Quod si rota illa nihil aliud praeter exteriorem circulum haberet, vacua foret. Et si forte alienus superveniret et ibi operari vellet, hoc esse non posset; nam in rota una duo fabri res suas constituere non possunt. homo, aspice homineml Homo enim caelum et terram atque alias facturas in se habet et forma una est, et inipso omnia latent. ao De paternitate, Sic patemitas est quomodo circulus rotae, patemitas est plenitudo rotae.
text_translation: Now, somewhere a wheel is and it itself is full of something. If that wheel had nothing else besides the outer circle, it would be empty. And if perhaps another person were to come and wish to work there, this could not be; for in one wheel two craftsmen cannot establish their own things. Man, look upon man. For man indeed has heaven and earth and other works within himself, and one form is his, and in him all things are hidden. Ad De paternitate, Thus patemitas is as the circle of the wheel, patemitas is the fullness of the wheel.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deitas est in ipsa, et ex ipsa sunt omnia, et praeter eam creator non est. Lucifer autem integer non est, sed in dispersione divisus est, cum esse voluit, quod esse non debuit. Cum enim deus mundum fecit, in antiquo consilio habuit, quod homo fieri voluit. De animae creatione, Et cum lucem fecit, quae volatilis erat et quae ubique volare potuit, in eodem consilio suo habuit, quod spiritali vitae, quae spiraculum vitae est, molem corporalem, id est elatam formam de limo terrae daret, quae nec volaret nec flaret et quae prae impossibilitate sua se levare non posset, et ut ideo sic ligata esset, quatinus ad deum acutius aspiceret.
text_translation: Divinity is in her, and from her all things are, and apart from her there is no creator. Lucifer, however, is not whole, but in dispersion he was divided, when he wished to be what he ought not to be. For when God made the world, in the ancient counsel he had that man wished to be made. Concerning the creation of the soul, And when he made light, which was flying and which could fly everywhere, in the same counsel he had that spiritual life, which is the breath of life, should give a bodily form, that is, an elevated form from the clay of the earth, which neither would fly nor blow, and which, due to its own impossibility, could not lift itself up, and so that it might be thus bound, in order to look more sharply at God.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde ligaturam istam antiquus serpens odio habuit, quia, quamvis homo corpore gravis esset, se tamen ad deum in rationalitate erigeret. De elementis et firmamento. Et elementa mundi deus fecit, et ipsa in homine stint, et homo cum illis operatur. Nam ignis, aer, aqaa, terra snnt, et haec qaataor elementa sibi ita intricata et coniancta sont, at nallum ab alio separari possit, et se itainsimiQcontinent, qaod dicantar firmamentum. De sole et steUis.
text_translation: Therefore, this ancient serpent hated this ligature, because although the human body was heavy, yet he would raise himself to God through rationality. On the elements and the firmament. And the elements of the world God created, and they reside in man, and man works with them. For fire, air, water, earth are, and these four elements are so intricately intertwined and connected that none can be separated from another, and they so similarly contain themselves, which is called the firmament. On the sun and stars.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sol aatem paene sapremus in istis per ea splendorem et ignem saam mittit. Girca qaem stellae qaaedam tantae magnitadinis et claritatis sant, qaod velut montes per firmamentam ad terram extentae sant; ande qaanto proximiores terrae, tanto lacidiores esse yidentar. Girca yero eandem solem et aliae stellae sant lo minoris magnitadinis et claritatis, ita ut velat valles sant ad magnitudinem praedietaram stellaram, ande et minas apparent. De tempesiate.
text_translation: The sun, indeed, we almost know in these things by means of its splendor and fire, which it sends forth. Around which certain stars of such magnitude and clarity are, which are extended like mountains through the firmament to the earth; and the more they are closer to the earth, the more clear they appear. Around truly the same sun and other stars are of lesser magnitude and clarity, so that they cover valleys to the magnitude of the previously mentioned stellar, and they appear smaller. De tempore.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gam maior calor et fervor ignis in aethere est, fervor ille repentinam eballitionem et peri- calosam inandationem aqaamm aliqaando edacit et emittit in terram, et inde tempestates sant et nabiam diraptio; yelat cam olla ad magnam ignem posita repente ebalHt et spamam eicit. Et hae tempestates saepissime contingant aat propter praeterita mala per iadiciam dei aat propter iam perpetrata mala opera hominam aut propter sic significanda fatara pericala vel belloram yel famis vel sabitaneae mortis; qaod qaaelibet opera nostra tangant elementa et inde concatiuntar, quod etiam cum elementis yersantur.
text_translation: Mag maior calor et fervor ignis in aethere est, fervor ille repentinam ebulitionem et periculoram inundationem aquam aliquando excitat et emittit in terram, et inde tempestates sunt et nubiam diruptio; velat enim olla ad magnam ignem posita repente ebulit et spumam eicit. Et hae tempestates saepissime contingant aut propter praeterita mala per iudicium dei aut propter iam perpetrata mala opera hominum aut propter sic significanda fatidica pericula vel bellorum vel famis vel subitaneae mortis; quod quaelibet opera nostra tangant elementa et inde concutiuntur, quod etiam cum elementis versantur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum autem minor calor et feryor ignis in aethere est, minorem ebullitionem et inundationem aquarum emittit; yelut etiam aliqua olla, cui minor ignis suppositus est, quae inde paryam ebuUitionem et spumam eicit. Gum yero aer in igne et aqua temperatus est, suayem temperiem emittit et, ut etiam olla ad temperatum ignem posita, soayiter calet. Sed cum sol sursum ascendit, ita ut ignis eius in altitadinem caeli fortiter ardeat, tunc etiam interdum aer siccus et aridus de ardore solis est, atque ignis eiusdem solis ignem tonitrui interdum tangit.
text_translation: When, however, lesser heat and the carrying of fire in the ether is, it emits lesser ebullition and inundation of waters; as also some pot, to which lesser fire is placed, which from there emits a little ebullition and foam. When, however, air tempered in fire and water is, it emits its own temperance, and, as also a pot placed at a tempered fire, gently warms. But when the sun ascends upward, so that its fire burns strongly in the height of the heavens, then also sometimes dry and arid air is from the heat of the sun, and the fire of the same sun sometimes touches the fire of thunder.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De tonitruo. Nam in tonitruo iudicialis ignis et frigus et 3^ foetor est. Sed cum aHquando ignis tonitrui ab igne solis tangitur, tunc ille commotus aliquantum fulgura modice emittit» ^ De fv^igwtt. Et modice submurmurat et sic cessat, velut homo, qui interdum in iram motus eam ad perfectum non ducit sed comprimendo compescit. Aliquando autem de nimio ardore solis ignis tonitrui commovetnr et in magnam commotionem ducitur, ita ut fortia et periculosa Mlgura emittat et vocem suam fortiter exaltet, velut homo, qui fortiter in iram motus eam periculoso opere perficit.
text_translation: On thunder. For in thunder there is judicial fire, cold, and foul odor. But when sometimes the fire of thunder is touched by the fire of the sun, then it is moved and emits somewhat lightning moderately. About lightning. And it murmurs moderately and thus ceases, like a man who, sometimes moved to anger, does not bring it to perfection but suppresses it by compressing. Sometimes, however, from the excessive heat of the sun, the fire of thunder is moved and led into great motion, so that it emits strong and dangerous figures and strongly exalts its voice, like a man who, strongly moved to anger, accomplishes it with a perilous work.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc etiam interdum superior ignis tonitrui ab igne solis tactus frigus, quod in tonitruo est, in unum locum coadunari facit, quemadmodum aqua glaciem in unum locum congregat, et &igus illud grandinem ad nubes ducit, et nubes eam suscipiunt et dispergunt et ad terram emittunt. De gramdim, Nam grando est yelut oculus tonitrui. Sed cum sol in descensu in hieme est, ignem suum ad altitudinem caeli non transmittit et magis sub terra quam supra terram ardet et tunc in altitudine caeli non fervet.
text_translation: And then also sometimes the upper fire of thunder, touched by the fire of the sun, causes the cold that is in thunder to be gathered together into one place, just as water gathers ice into one place, and likewise that same thing leads the hail to the clouds, and the clouds receive it and disperse it and send it to the earth. Of hail, for hail is like the eye of thunder. But when the sun is in descent during winter, it does not transmit its fire to the height of the sky, but rather burns beneath the earth than above the earth, and then in the height of the sky it does not heat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De nivt. IJnde aquae, quae in superioribus sunt, velut pulyere aspersae de frigore fiunt et nivem emittunt. De pluvia, Sed cum postea in calore suaves sunt, pluviam emittunt, et cum sol interdum nec nimium calorem nec nimium frigus ostendit, tunc etiam interdum suavem pluviam emittit, quemadmodum homo, cum aliquando laetus est, lacrimas multotiens prae gaudio fundit. De ventis, Quatuor etiam cardinales venti sub sole et super solem firmamento assunt et illud continent ac totum orbem, scilicet de inferiori parte usque ad superiorem partem firmamenti, sicut cum pallio circumdant.
text_translation: De nive. Unde aquae, quae in superioribus sunt, velut pulvere aspersae de frigore fiunt et nivem emittunt. De pluvia, Sed cum postea in calore suaves sunt, pluviam emittunt, et cum sol interdum nec nimium calorem nec nimium frigus ostendit, tunc etiam interdum suavem pluviam emittit, quemadmodum homo, cum aliquando laetus est, lacrimas multotiens prae gaudio fundit. De ventis, Quatuor etiam cardinales venti sub sole et super solem firmamento assunt et illud continent ac totum orbem, scilicet de inferiori parte usque ad superiorem partem firmamenti, sicut cum pallio circumdant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Orientalis enim ventus aerem complectitur et suavissimum rorem super arida mittit. Yentus enim occidentis fluentibus nubibus se admiscet, ut aquas sustineat, ne erumpant. Australis vero ventus ignem in magistratione sua retinet et eum prohibet, ne omnia comburat. Yentus quoque septentrionalis exteriores tenebras retinet, ne modum suum excedant. Isti quatuor venti alae potentiae dei sunt. Qui cum simul movebuntur, omnia elementa complicabunt et se divident ac mare concutient et omnes aquas exsiccabunt.
text_translation: The eastern wind embraces the air and casts the most pleasant dew upon arid places. The western wind indeed mingles with flowing clouds, so as to hold back waters, lest they burst forth. The southern wind indeed retains fire within its own domain and forbids it, lest it burn everything. The northern wind also retains outer darkness, lest they exceed their measure. These four winds are the wings of God's power. When they move together, they will entwine all elements and divide themselves, shaking the sea and drying up all waters.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De iiidicii die. Sed nunc clave maiestatis dei clausi sxint, qaatinns elementa in moderatione haerebant, nec super ullum hominem ullo periculo dominabuntur, nisi in fine saeculi, quia tunc omnia purgabuntur, postea ipsi cantum in symphonia ferent; et nulla creatura est, quae una sola proprietate consistat, quin plures habeat. De nihilo. Sed nihilum nullam proprietatem habet, in qua subsistat, et ideo nihil est; unde etiam aliae creaturae, quae voluntate sua se nihilo coniungunt, proprietates suas perdunt et ad nihilum fiunt.
text_translation: On the day of judgment. But now they are closed by the key of the majesty of God, to the extent that the elements remained in moderation, nor will they rule over any man by any peril, except in the end of the world, because then all things will be purified, afterwards they themselves will carry forth the song in harmony; and no creature exists which consists in a single property, but rather has many. Of nothingness. But nothing has no property in which it subsists, and therefore it is nothing; whence also other creatures, which by their own will unite themselves to nothingness, lose their properties and become nothing.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e firmcmento et veniis. Firmamentum autem ignem, solem, Innam, stellas et ventos habet, per quae omnia consistit et quorum proprietatibus firmatur, ne dissipetur. Nam ut anima totum corpus hominis tenet, ita etiam venti totum firmamentum continent, ne cormmpatur, et invisibiles sunt, velut etiam anima invisibilis est de secreto dei yeniens. Et sicut domus absque angularibus lapidibus non constat, sic nec firmamentum nec terra nec abyssus nec totus mundus cum onmibus compositionibus suis absque his ventis esset, quia haec omnia cum ipsis com- posita et retenta sunt.
text_translation: From the firmament and winds. The firmament, however, has fire, the sun, the moon, stars, and winds, by which all things subsist and by whose properties it is made firm, so that it does not disperse. For as the soul holds the entire human body, so also the winds contain the entire firmament, so that it does not corrupt, and they are invisible, just as the soul is also invisible from the secret of God of those who come. And just as a house without angular stones does not stand, so neither the firmament nor the earth nor the abyss nor the entire world with all its compositions would stand without these winds, because all these things are composed and retained with them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam terra tota scinderetur et rumperetur, si isti venti non essent, quemadmodum etiam homo totus scinderetur, si ossa non haberet. Principalis enim ventus orientalis totam orientalem plagam continet, et principalis ventus occidentalis totam plagam occiden- talem, ac principalis ventus austraHs totam plagam australem, atque principaliB yentus septentrionalis totam plagam septentrionalem. De ventis coUateralHms. Et unusquisque ventus istorum principalium duos alios debiliores sibi astantes velutdo duo brachia habet, in quos interdum quasdam vires suas ezpirat.
text_translation: For the whole earth would be split and broken, if these winds were not, just as also the whole man would be split, if he had no bones. For indeed the principal eastern wind contains the whole eastern region, and the principal western wind the whole western region, and the principal southern wind the whole southern region, and the principal northern wind the whole northern region. Concerning the collateral winds. And each of these principal winds has two others weaker, standing beside it, like two arms, into which sometimes it exhales some of its forces.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Isti enim debiUores venti eandem naturam habent, quam et principales venti eorum tenent, ita ut unusquisque inferiorum ventorum principalem yentum yelut caput suum imitetur, quanto multo minores vires habeant et quod unam viam cum principali vento suo habent, ut duae aures imam viam auditus in capite tenent. Et cum per divmam iussionem ultorem excitantur, flatum et fortitudinem a principalibus yentis suis accipiunt, et tunc in tali inquietudine sunt et tales et tantos fragores et coUisiones pericuiorum faciunt, qualiter etiam pericula in quietudinum mali bumores in bominibus faciunt, cum eos in aegritudinem deiciunt.
text_translation: These indeed weak winds have the same nature as the principal winds of them hold, so that each of the inferior winds imitates the principal wind like its own head, how much less strength they have and that they have one path with their principal wind, like two ears hold the path of hearing in the head. And when they are excited by the divine command of the ultimate, they receive the blow and strength from their principal winds, and then they are in such turmoil and cause such and so many noises and collisions of dangers, as also dangers in the quietude of bad humors in men cause, when they cast them into illness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed iUi principales venti ab initio mundi numquam in viribus suis pleniter moti sunt nec movebuntur usque in novissimum diem. Et cum tunc fortitudinem suam ostenderint et flatus suos tunc pleniter emiserint, prae fortitudine et coUisione eorum nubes scindentur et superiora firmamenti complicabuntur et rumpentur, yelut corpus bominis scinditur et omnia membra eius deiciuntur, cum anima ipsius de corpore suo exeundo solvitur. Yentus enim orientalis duas alas babet, per quas omnem orbem ad se trahit, ita ut ala una a superioribus ad inferiora solis cursum contineat et ala altera soli occurrat, ita ut ei obstaculum sit, ne ibi ulterius procedat, quo ultra non tendit.
text_translation: But those principal winds from the beginning of the world have never been fully moved in their powers nor will they be moved until the last day. And when then they will show their strength and fully emit their blasts, because of their strength and collision, the clouds will be torn apart and the upper parts of the firmament will be entangled and broken, like the body of a man is torn and all his limbs are cast out, when his soul, departing from his body, is dissolved. For the eastern wind has two wings, through which it draws the entire orb toward itself, so that one wing contains the course of the sun from the upper to the lower, and the other wing meets the sun, so that it is an obstacle to it, so that it does not proceed further there, where it does not tend beyond.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ventus iste omnem humiditatem bumectat et omnia germina germinare facit. 20Yentus autem occidentalis velut os ad omnes aquas dissipandum et dispergendum babet, ita ut omnes aquas in recta itinera dividat et dispergat, ne ulla aqua supra aliam ascendat, sed ut recte incedat, quia potestatem babet super aerem illum, qui aquas portat. Et ventus iste omnia viridia arefacit et ea, quae iuxta eum sunt. Sed ventus australis velut ferreum baculum tenet, qui superius tres ramos babet et qui inferius acutus est.
text_translation: And this wind moistens all humidity and causes all seeds to germinate. The western wind, however, has the mouth of a vessel to dissipate and scatter all waters, so that it divides and scatters all waters into straight courses, so that no water may rise above another, but that it may proceed straight, because it has power over that air which carries waters. And this wind dries up all green things and those which are near it. But the southern wind holds a iron rod, which above has three branches and which below is sharp.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quasi calibineam fortitudinem babet, quae firmamentum et abyssum continet. Sicut enim calibs omnia aeramenta superat et domat, et ut cor bominem confortat, ita et fortitudo venti buius firmamentum et abyssum plagae illius continet, ne dilabatur. Et superius tres vires quasi tres ramos habet, quarum una calorem solis in oriente temperat et una ardorem eius in meridie deprimit et una calorem illius in occidente refrigerat, ne modos suos in plagis illis excedat.
text_translation: For it has a strength as if of heat, which contains the firmament and the abyss. For just as heat surpasses and subdues all armor, and as the heart of man comforts, so also the fortitude of this wind contains the firmament and the abyss of its afflictions, so that it does not slip away. And above, it has three forces as if three branches, of which one tempers the heat of the sun in the east, and one suppresses its heat in the south, and one cools its heat in the west, so that it does not exceed its modes in those afflictions.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed inferius acutus est, quod fortitudo eiiis etiam in abyssum fixa est, ne humiditas et frigus de abysso supra modum sursum ascendant. Et idem yentos omnia matura facit, ita ut folia silvarum et ^amina, segetes, poma, yinum et omnes reliquos terrae fructus ad maturitatem producat. Yentus vero septentrionalis quatuor columnas habet, per quas totum firmamen- tum et totum abyssum continet. Sed cum eas sursum traxerit, tuno firmamentum ad abyssum complicabitur.
text_translation: But downward it is sharp, because its strength is also fixed in the abyss, so that humidity and cold from the abyss do not ascend upward beyond measure. And the same winds make all things ripe, so that the leaves of the forests and branches, crops, fruits, wine, and all other fruits of the earth produce maturity. But the northern wind has four columns, through which it contains the entire firmament and the entire abyss. But when it has drawn them upward, then the firmament will be folded together toward the abyss.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Hae autem quatuor columnae quatuor elementa tenent, quae ibi in eadem septentrionali plaga simul congluti nantur et finiuntur et etiam velut columnis innixa sunt, n cadant. Gum vero idem ventus fortitudine sua quataor oolunmas suas in novissimo die conmioyerit, tunc etiam firmamentum complicabitur, velut tabulae complicari solent. Et ventus iste frigidus est et frigus affert et omnia frigore constringit et simul tenet, ne dilabantur. De 8ole. Et sol, ut praedictum est, in apice et quasi in medio firmamenti positus est et igneus atque aerius est et igne suo omnem sustentationem et fundamentum firmamenti 'et aerem, sidera, stellas atque nubes, ne cadant et ne diffiuant, continet, velut terra omnes creaturas, quae in ea sunt, sustentat.
text_translation: These four columns hold the four elements, which there in the same northern region are simultaneously glued together and finished, and also are fixed like columns, so that they do not fall. When indeed the same wind, by its strength, in the last day will have glued together its four columns, then also the firmament will be folded together, as tables are usually folded together. And this wind is cold and brings cold, and constrains all things with cold, and holds them together, so that they do not melt away. Of the sun. And the sun, as has been said before, is placed in the apex and as it were in the middle of the firmament, and is fiery and airy, and by its fire contains all sustentation and foundation of the firmament, and the air, stars, stars, and clouds, so that they do not fall and do not scatter, as the earth supports all creatures that are in it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aether etiam per ipsum firmatur. Cum autem sol in altitudine firmamenti stat, ignis ei occurrit et ei famulatur. Et sol totum firmamentum confirmat atque splendorem suum per totam terram spargit, unde ipsa viriditatem et flores profert. Sed tunc dies longi stmt, quod sol in altitudine firmamenti currit, et aestas est. Gum yero sol ad terram declinatur, frigus terrae de aqua ei occurrit et omnia viridia arida facit. Et quod sol tunc se ad terram declinavit, dies breves sunt, et Idems est.
text_translation: Aether is also made firm by itself. When, however, the sun stands in the height of the firmament, fire meets it and serves it. And the sun strengthens the entire firmament and spreads its splendor throughout the entire earth, from which it produces greenness and flowers. But then the days are long, because the sun runs in the height of the firmament, and summer is. When indeed the sun declines toward the earth, the cold of the earth from water meets it and makes all green things dry. And because the sun then declined toward the earth, the days are short, and the same is.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Calor etiam solis in hieme maior est sub terra quam super terram, quod si tunc frigus tantum esset sub terra quantum super terram, vel si in aestate calor tantus esset sub terra quantus est super terrara, de immoderatione ista terra tota scinderetur. Sed et cum Mems futura erit, tempestas de aqua ascendit et lumen solis obtenebrat, unde et obscuri dies erunt. Et cum aestas futura erit, tempestates sub terram cadunt, unde polcbri et laeti dies saepe sant, quia aestas adest; et sol in circnlo suo infceger est et plenus et non deficit et lumen suam in lunam mittit, cum ad eum accedit, ut yir semen suum in feminam mittit.
text_translation: Heat of the sun is also greater beneath the earth in winter than above the earth, which if then the cold were as much beneath the earth as above the earth, or if in summer the heat were as great beneath the earth as above the earth, the entire earth would be torn asunder by this immoderation. But when Mems will be future, a storm rises from water and darkens the light of the sun, whence also the days will be dark. And when summer will be future, storms fall beneath the earth, whence the days are often beautiful and joyful, because summer is near; and the sun in its circle is fixed and full and does not wane and sends its light to the moon, when it approaches it, as a male sends his semen to the female.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De luna. Luna enim ex igne et ex tenui aere est atque in aere stat et babitaculum in eo babet et ipse per eam firmatur. Quae postquam evacuatur, sub solem vadity et spbaera ab eo extenditur, quae illam ad ipsom trabit, ut acbates ferrum ad se trabit. Illam accendit, sed et ceterae planetae et steUae atque aer et cetera luminaria, quae circa lunam sunt, ad eam flagrant et ad accensionem eius ipsi succurrunt. Et postquam incensa fuerit, paulatim crescit ad plenitudinem suam, sicut etiam rogos yel domus incensa paulatim incipit ardere, usque dum tota incendatur.
text_translation: On the moon. The moon, indeed, is made from fire and from thin air, and it stands in the air and has its dwelling in it, and by means of it, [the air] is made firm. Which, after being emptied, goes under the sun and the sphere is extended from it, which draws it toward itself, as the edge of iron draws to itself. It ignites it, but also the other planets and stars and air and other luminaries, which are around the moon, flame toward it and rush to its ignition. And after it has been ignited, it gradually grows toward its fullness, just as also a pyre or a house, when ignited, gradually begins to burn, until it is entirely set on fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Interim enim, dum luna crescendo repletur, sol superiora firmamenti confirmat nec usquam ab boc cessat. Et sol diem secum et apud se babet, quia firmamentum superius lucidum est; et luna noctem, quia terra tenebrosa est. Sed et postquam luna repletur, ita quod velut mulier praegnans ei&citur, lumen suum emittit et stellis tradit; et ita stellae lucidiores efficiuntur. De rare, Tunc et stellae de eodem calore aerem calefaciunt et roborant, et aer calefactus sudorem suum, scilicet rorem suum, super terram mittit et eam fecundat.
text_translation: Meanwhile, indeed, while the moon is filling by growing, the sun confirms the upper parts of the firmament and does not cease anywhere from this. And the sun has day with itself and near itself, because the upper firmament is luminous; and the moon has night, because the earth is dark. But also after the moon is filled, so that as a pregnant woman emitting, it emits its light and gives it to the stars; and thus the stars become more luminous. From rarity, then the stars from the same heat warm and strengthen the air, and the warmed air sends forth its sweat, that is, its dew, upon the earth and makes it fertile.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde et ipsa perfusa fructus gignit. Nam interim dum luna se evacuat, ita quod lumen suum stellis dat, et dum a sole accensa ad plenitudinem suam crescit, stellae lumen et calorem, quem a luna acceperunt, in aerem ad calefaciendum et confortandum eum paulatim mittunt, et aer sudorem suum ad fecundandam terram desuper fundit, ita ut, cum luna iterum plena fuerit, stellae etiam interim evacuatae sint ad excipiendum iterum denuo aliud lumen et calorem ; et cum luna defecerit, stellae plenae sint ad confortandum aerem et terram; et cum stellae defecerint, luna denuo plena sit.
text_translation: Therefore, and she herself, being saturated, produces fruit. For in the meantime, while the moon evacuates itself, so that it gives its light to the stars, and while, ignited by the sun, it grows toward its fullness, the stars send gradually into the air the light and heat which they received from the moon, to warm and strengthen it; and the air pours down its sweat from above to fertilize the earth, so that, when the moon again becomes full, the stars also have been evacuated in the meantime to receive again another light and heat; and when the moon has failed, the stars are full to strengthen the air and the earth; and when the stars have failed, the moon again becomes full.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De aeris purgatime. Sed cum stellae in noctibus apparent, quod tunc aliquando videntur ignitae spbaerae nt ignita iacula in aere yolare, hoc est, qnod stellae ignem in aerem eum ad confortandum mittnnt, ut et ipse calore sno terram fmctificet. Unde etiam saepe yidetnr et reperitur, quod aer de igne et calore stellarnm se pnrgat, ita ut ab eo sordes quaedam quasi faeces cadant. s De aeris fUis Similiter cum aestas et hiems separantur, ita ut aestas recedat et hiems veniat, vel cum hiems recedat et aestas yeniat, quaedam coagulatio ut quaedam albedo filorum in aere volat, ubi aer se purgat.
text_translation: On the purification of the air. But when stars appear in the nights, which sometimes are seen as fiery spheres and ignited darts flying in the air, that is, when stars send fire into the air to warm it, so that the earth may be fertilized by its heat. Hence also often it appears and is found that the air purges itself of fire and heat of the stars, so that certain filth, as if feces, fall down. On the exhalations of the air. Similarly when summer and winter are separated, so that summer recedes and winter comes, or when winter recedes and summer comes, a certain coagulation, as the whiteness of threads flying in the air, occurs where the air purges itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quae in terram descendit de collisione illorum duorum lo temporum hiemis et aestatis, cum se invicem coUidunt. De eclipsi, Quod autem eclipsis lunae aliquando videtur, boc est, quod elementa et tempestates aliquando se ad invicem collidunt, velut conflictum inter se babeant. Sed luna tunc nec extinguitur nec deficit, sed tempes- tates illae eam ad boram tantum obscurant. Sed vis lunae tanta est, quod tempestates illas superat et quod iterum splendorem suum emittit, quia vis lunae maior est vi tempestatum illarum.
text_translation: What descends into the earth from the collision of those two times of winter and summer, when they meet with each other. Concerning the eclipse, that is, when the eclipse of the moon sometimes appears, that is, the elements and storms sometimes collide with each other, as if they have a conflict with each other. But the moon is not extinguished nor does it fail, but those storms obscure it only to the winds. But the power of the moon is so great that it overcomes those storms and that again emits its brilliance, because the power of the moon is greater than the power of those storms.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De quinque planetis. Ceteri quoque quinque planetae sunt, et lumen de igne et de aetbere babent et sunt robur ac firmamentum firmamenti. Atque bas circuitiones tam altitudinis quam profonditatis in firmamento babent, ut ibi fulgeant, ubi sol non radiat et ubi splendorem suum vix ostendit. Ubi ipsi sunt et currunt atque circulo solis serviunt et velocitatem solis retinent atque ignem eius tabescere faciunt, ita ut prae ipsis tantum ardorem ignis non emittat, ut faceret, si eum non retinerent.
text_translation: On the five planets. The other five planets are also present, and they have light from fire and from aether, and they are strength and firmament of the firmament. And they have circuits both of altitude and of depth in the firmament, so that they shine where the sun does not radiate and where its splendor barely shows. Where they are and run and serve the circle of the sun and retain the sun's velocity and cause its fire to fade, so that compared to them, the fire's heat does not emit as much as it would if they did not retain it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sicut quinque sensus corporis bominis corpus eius continent et omamenta eius sunt, ita etiam isti quinque planetae solem so continent atque decor ipsius sunt. De firmamenti retardatione, Sed et firmamentum in velocitate circumvolvitur , et sol cum ceteris planetis contra illud et obviam illi paulatim currit atque velocitatem eius retinet; quoniam si sol illud retardando non retineret vel si ipse cum ceteris planetis tanta velocitate curreret obviam firmamento, quanta illud circumvolvitur, omnia confanderentur et firmamentum totum dirumperetor.
text_translation: And just as the five senses of the human body contain its body and are its adornments, so also these five planets contain the sun and are its adornment. Concerning the retardation of the firmament, but also the firmament revolves in velocity, and the sun together with the other planets runs against it and towards it gradually, retaining its velocity; because if the sun did not retain its velocity by retarding or if it itself with the other planets ran towards the firmament with such velocity as it revolves around, all things would be confused and the entire firmament would be destroyed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si enim firmamentum immobile esset, ita ut non circumYolveretur, tunc sol fere per totam aestatem super terram esset sine nocte et fere per totam biemem sub terra sine die. Nunc autem ideo circumvolvitur, ut, dum ipsum soli occurrit et ille isti, tanto citius a calore illius solidetur et confirmetur, scilicet dum ille ipsum percurrit et igne suo pertransit et perfundit. Ante casum Adae firmamentum immobile fuit et non circumvolvebatur, post casum autem eius coepit moveri et circumvolvi; sed post novissimum diem immobile stabit, ut in prima creatione ante casum Adae fuit.
text_translation: If indeed the firmament were immobile, so that it did not revolve, then the sun would be nearly throughout the entire summer above the earth without night and nearly throughout the entire bimester below the earth without day. Now, however, it revolves precisely so that, when it meets the sun and that very one, it solidifies and strengthens so much more quickly from its heat, namely when that very one passes through it and its fire transmits and pours forth. Before the fall of Adam, the firmament was immobile and did not revolve; but after his fall, it began to move and revolve; yet after the last day, it will again become immobile, as it was in the first creation before the fall of Adam.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nunc autem ideo circumvolvitui, ut a sole, luna et stellis roboretur et confirmetur, quia si immobile staret, cito liquefactum et emollitum deflueret. £x boc etiam, quod modo circumvolvitur, elementa purgat, et purgatio illa interdum sunt aquosae et nigrae nubes, quas videmus, velut etiam aqua, cum ad ignem in olla ponitur, et in fervore caloris spumam eicit et purgatur. 2e firmamenti consonantiis. In ipsa etiam circum volutione sui mirabiles sonos emittit, quos tamen prae nimia altitudine et latitudine eius audire non possumus, velut etiam, cum molendinum vel plaustrum circumvolvitur, sonos suos babet.
text_translation: Now, therefore, it revolves around, as from the sun, moon, and stars, it is strengthened and confirmed, because if it stood immobile, it would quickly be liquefied and softened, flowing away. From this also, because it now revolves, it purges the elements, and that purging sometimes are watery and black clouds, which we see, just as also water, when placed into fire in a pot, and in the fervor of heat, ejects foam and purges itself. By the consonances of the firmament. In it also, by its own revolving, it emits marvelous sounds, which, however, because of its excessive altitude and breadth, we cannot hear, just as also, when a mill or a cart revolves, it has its own sounds.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et idem firmamentum ideo tantae altitudinis et amplitudinis circa terram est, ne bomines et animalia in terra moriantur , cum ab eis remotum est; quia tam bomines quam animalia de igne, ventis, aqua et nubibus morerentur, si eis in vicino esset. Sicut enim corpus et anima simul sunt et ab invicem confirmantur, ita etiam firmamentum et planetae sunt et ab invicem mutuo foventur et confirmantur. Sed et sicut anima corpus vivificat et consolidat, sic etiam sol, luna et ceteri planetae firmamentum cum igne suo fovent et confirmant.
text_translation: But also the same firmament is of such height and breadth around the earth, so that humans and animals on earth do not die, being remote from them; because both humans and animals would die from fire, winds, water, and clouds, if they were nearby. For just as body and soul are together and mutually strengthen each other, so also the firmament and planets are together and mutually warm and strengthen each other. But also, just as the soul vivifies and consolidates the body, so also the sun, moon, and other planets warm and strengthen the firmament with their fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam firmamentum est velut caput bominis, sol, luna et stellae ut oculi, aer ut auditus, venti velut odoratus, ros ut gustus, latera mundi ut bracbia et ut tactus. Et aliae creaturae, quae sunt in mundo, sunt ut venter, terra autem ut cor, quod, quemadmodum cor suCAUSAE ET LIB. I. H periora et inferiora corporis ooDtinet, ita etiam et terra aquis illis, qoae super eam fluunt, est ut arida, aquis yero, quae sub ea sunt, est obstaculum, ne contrarium modum erumpant.
text_translation: For the firmament is as the head of man, the sun, moon and stars as the eyes, air as hearing, winds as smelling, dew as taste, the sides of the world as arms and as touch. And other creatures which are in the world are as the belly, the earth however as the heart, which, just as the heart contains the superior and inferior parts of the body, so also the earth, with respect to those waters which flow over it, is as dryness, with respect to those waters which are beneath it, is an obstacle, that the contrary mode may not erupt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De LuGiferi casu et firmamenH creatione. Abyssus autem est yelut pedes et ut incessus bominis. Cum igitur diabolus de caelo corruit, qui sedere et regnare voluit et qui nullam creaturam creare et facere potuit, deus firmamentum statim fecit, ut ille videret et intelligeret, quae et quanta deus facere et creare posset. Tunc etiam so- lO lem, lunam et stellas in firmamentum posuit, ut diabolus in eis yideret et cognosceret, quantum decorem et splendorem perdidisset.
text_translation: On the Fall of Lucifer and the Creation of the Firmament. The abyss is like the feet and like the steps of man. When therefore the devil fell from heaven, who wished to sit and reign, and who could not create or make any creature, God immediately made the firmament, that he might see and understand what and how much God could make and create. Then also he placed the sun, moon, and stars in the firmament, that the devil might see and know in them how much splendor and glory he had lost.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De stellis. SteUae namque non sunt aequalis magnitudinis nec aequalis splendoris, sed quaedam sunt maiores, quaedam minores, quaedam maioris splendoris, quaedam minoris. Firmamentum autem a sole superius continetur, ne supra modum sursum ascendat, et subtus ab aere terrae, qui terram et nubes continet, ne modum suum subtus excedat. Et ita supra et infra continetur, ut praeostensum est, ut rectos modos suos excedere non possit. De dtiodecim signis et pUmetis.
text_translation: On the stars. Indeed, the stars are not of equal size nor equal brightness, but some are larger, some smaller, some brighter, some dimmer. The firmament, however, is held above by the sun, so that it does not ascend too high above, and below by the air of the earth, which contains the earth and clouds, so that it does not exceed its measure below. And thus it is contained above and below, as previously stated, so that it cannot exceed its proper measures. On the twelve signs and planets.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed sicut a septem planetis in cursu suo ducitur, sic et ipsi in duodecim signis quasi famulatum ei exbibent et seryiunt. Nam cum sol ad signum capricomi peryenerit, duo planetae, qui eum ad signum sagittarii comitati erant, ipsi quasi nutum faciunt, ut sursum ad priorem cursum suum redeat, et eum ad priorem ascensum exhortantur. Et hoc signum capricomi dicitur, qui etiam sursum ascendere nititur. Haec enim duodecim signa nihil aliud in nominibus suis sunt, nisi quod reliqui quinque planetae secundum ofdcia sua solem aut in aestate sursum ascendendo impellunt aut in hieme deorsum descendendo suscipiunt et quod ei hoc modo seryiunt.
text_translation: But just as the seven planets are led in their course, so too they serve and obey in the twelve signs as if in servitude. For when the sun has arrived at the sign of Capricorn, two planets which had accompanied it to the sign of Sagittarius make a nod, so that it may return upwards to its former course, and urge it to ascend again to its former ascent. And this sign of Capricorn is called which also strives to ascend upwards. For these twelve signs are nothing else in their names, but that the other five planets according to their offices either drive the sun upwards in summer or receive it downwards in winter, and what they thus serve to it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum sol ad signum capricorni peryenerit, reliqui tres planetae sub eo currunt et eum paulatim sursum impellunt usque ad signum aquarii. Qui CTim iam ibi surgere incipit, terram circa fandum eios calefacit et aquas, quae sub terra sunt, et ideo aquae sab terra calidiores in hieme sunt quam in aestate. Tunc ibi in signo aquarii planeta ille, qui ignem suum a sole semper recipit, scilicet ille, qui in signo cancri inf erius sole stat, et alii planetae advenientes retrorsum incedunt.
text_translation: But when the sun reaches the sign of Capricorn, the remaining three planets move beneath it and gradually push it upward until the sign of Aquarius. When it has already begun to rise there, it warms the earth around its foundation and the waters that are beneath the earth, and therefore the waters under the earth are warmer in winter than in summer. Then there, in the sign of Aquarius, that planet which always receives its fire from the sun, namely, that one which is in the sign of Cancer lower than the sun, and other planets arriving move backward.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Soleno. comitantur usque ad signum piscium, et cum ad signum illud pervenerit, iam quasi in medietate aquarum est. Et pisces, qui se prius a frigore absconderant, iam calorem eius sentiunt et ad fecunditatem procedunt. Et alius planeta, qui in signo cancri ad dextram solis erat, ei bic occurrit et ipsum sursum trabit usque ad signum arietis. Et cum sol sic ad signum arietis pervenerit, et ibi duo inferiores planetae ei occurrunt et eum suscipiunt et cum eo paulatim ascendunt et in ante tendunt velut aries cum comibus.
text_translation: Sol, comitantur usque ad signum piscium, et cum ad signum illud pervenerit, iam quasi in medietate aquarum est. Et pisces, qui se prius a frigore absconderant, iam calorem eius sentiunt et ad fecunditatem procedunt. Et alius planeta, qui in signo cancri ad dextram solis erat, ei bis occurrit et ipsum sursum trabit usque ad signum arietis. Et cum sol sic ad signum arietis pervenerit, et ibi duo inferiores planetae ei occurrunt et eum suscipiunt et cum eo paulatim ascendunt et in ante tendunt velut aries cum comibus.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum altius ascendit quasi ad signum tauri, isti duo planetae ibi remanent, et alii duo ei obviam veniunt, qui rari sunt ad videndum et qui raro ostenduntur, nisi aliqua miracula portent. Et hi solem ante impellunt multa potestate, ut taurus, qui fortiter trudit comibus, et solem ad altitudinem transferunt, ita ut, cum ad signum geminorum venerit, alter eoram altera parte solis, alter in altera parte eius vadunt se ita separantes, usque dum ad summam altitudinem suam perveniunt.
text_translation: But when it ascends higher, as if toward the sign of Taurus, those two planets remain there, and others two come toward it, who are rare to be seen and who rarely appear, unless some miracles or portents. And these push the sun before with much power, so that the bull, who forcefully thrusts with his horns, and the sun are transferred to a higher altitude, so that when it comes to the sign of Gemini, one of them goes on one part of the sun, the other on the other part of it, moving themselves thus separating, until they reach their highest altitude.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc circa signum cancri, cum sol iam ad descensum reverti debet, planeta, qui ad dextram eius vadit, modicum eum praecedit et alium quendam planetam inferius solem stantem invenit, et mox planeta ille planetam istum sentiens retrorsum incedit parumper, et iste illum sequitur. Et iteram ille revertitur, et iste prior recedit, et ille sequitur; et sic per aliquod tempus praecedentes et redeuntes velut cancri incedunt, usque dum solem ad descensum convertunt.
text_translation: And then around the sign of Cancer, when the sun should already be returning to descent, the planet which goes to the right of it slightly precedes it and finds another certain planet standing below the sun, and soon that planet, sensing that planet, moves backward a little and this one follows it. And again he returns, and this one goes back, and he follows; and thus for some time the preceding and returning ones move like Cancer, until they turn the sun to descent.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t planeta ille, qui ad sinistram solis erat, ibi remanet, et isti duo comitantur eum et in descensu tenent eum, ne supra modum in descensu currat. Et ita ducunt eum usque ad signum leonis. Et tunc planetae illi, qui in signo arietis erant, ibi soli occurrunt subCAUSAE ET LIB. I. murmurantes, et sol tonc, qnasi irascatur propter difficnltatem reversionis, mnltum aestam emittit, ita at inde fulgora et tonitma sonent, quoniam ad descensum difficulter yertitar.
text_translation: And that planet, which was to the left of the sun, remains there, and these two accompany him and in the descent hold him, so that he does not run excessively in the descent. And thus they lead him until the sign of the lion. And then those planets, which were in the sign of Aries, there they meet the sun under causes and book I., murmuring, and the sun thunders, as if it is angry because of the difficulty of reversal, it emits much heat, so that from there lightning and thunder sound, because toward the descent it turns with difficulty.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum ad signum virginis pervenerit, ibi ei illi duo planetae occurrunt, qui in signo tauri ei obviam yeneront, et tunc suavius et mitius incedit, quia calor et acerbitas eius mitigatur iam terra nullum fructum proferente sed fere in delectatione maturitatis stante. Et ita cum eo incedunt usque ad signum librae, ubi viriditas et ariditas quasi in libra sunt, ita quod viriditas/abscedit lO et ariditas accedit. Et ibi alter eorum altera parte solis, alter altera parte eius vadunt se ita separantes, ut in signo geminorum fecerunt, ita eum ducentes usque ad signum scorpionis, et ibi alter eorum remanet.
text_translation: But when it arrives at the sign of the Virgin, there the two planets encounter it, which in the sign of Taurus will have opposed it, and then it proceeds more sweetly and gently, because its heat and bitterness are now mitigated, since the earth produces no fruit but nearly remains in the delight of standing maturity. And thus with it they proceed until the sign of Libra, where greenness and dryness are as if in a balance, so that greenness departs and dryness arrives. And there one of them goes on one side of the sun, the other on the other side of it, separating themselves thus as they did in the sign of Gemini, leading him thus until the sign of Scorpio, and there one of them remains.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et tunc soli alius planeta ibi occurrit, scilicet ille, qui inferius cancri veniebat. Et etiam pkmeta, qui ad eum ibi cucurrit et retro et ante incessit, adhuc cum sole manet, et sic cum eo ambo incedunt. Tunc etiam in signo scorpionis omnia reptantia cavemulas suas quaemnt, ubi per hiemem abscondantur. Sed praefati duo planetae cum sole ita incedtmt usque ad signum sagittarii, et ibi remanent. Et tunc in signo sagittarii nullus planeta cum sole amodo incedit, ut prius cum ipso incedebant, sed suaviter et leniter per se ipsum incedere permittunt, quia iam in inferiori descensu est; velut navis in descensu fluminis interdum per se leniter ire permittitur sublatis remis et eis per aliquam moram aliquando cessantibus.
text_translation: But also then another planet appears there, namely that one which comes below Cancer. And also the planet which runs to it there and moves both behind and before, still remains with the sun, and thus both with it walk together. Then also in the sign of Scorpio all creeping things dig their caves, where they hide during winter. But the aforementioned two planets with the sun thus walk until the sign of Sagittarius, and there they remain. And then in the sign of Sagittarius no planet with the sun any longer walks as before with it walked, but gently and softly they permit themselves to walk by themselves, because now it is in the lower descent; as a ship in the descent of the river sometimes is permitted to go by itself softly with the oars lifted and them for some delay sometimes ceasing.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed quoniam sol in descensu est, calor eius praecipue sub terra est et etiam in aquis a terra remotis. Hli etiam duo planetaCy qui eum usque ad signum sagittarii comitati sunt, se in nubes tunc sursum tollunt et calore suo aerem plus solito calefaciunt. Alioquin onmia, quae in terra sunt, perirent. Et hoc modo usque ad signum capricomi soli serviunt; ubi eum idem planetae ad ascensum prioris cursus sui exhortantur et adiuvant, ut praedictum est.
text_translation: But since the sun is in descent, its heat is especially beneath the earth and also in waters remote from the earth. Here also two planets which accompanied it up to the sign of Sagittarius then lift themselves up into the clouds and by their heat more than usual warm the air. Otherwise, all things which are in the earth would perish. And in this way up to the sign of Capricornus the sun serves; where the same planets urge and assist it to the ascent of its prior course, as previously stated.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sol autem est quasi pilleus firmamenti, qui omni firmamento sunt et terrae et aquis apparet et calorem suum tribuit, sed non omnibas aequali modo. Nam in medietate terrae fortissimns est, et ibi terra de sole fortissima est, et omnia tam in fructn quam in animalibus ibi fortiora sunt quam in aliis locis. Cum enim sol in diversas terras longius se spargit, ibi in descensu terrarum et terra et fructus terrae et animalia, quae ibi sunt, debiliora sunt quam in medietate terrae.
text_translation: The sun, however, is as a cap of the firmament, which appears in all the firmament, both of the earth and of the waters, and gives its heat, but not equally to all. For in the middle of the earth it is strongest, and there the earth from the sun is strongest, and all things, both in fruit and in animals, there are stronger than in other places. For when the sun spreads itself further into various lands, there, in the descent of the earths, the earth, the fruits of the earth, and the animals that are there are weaker than in the middle of the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Yinum enim magnum calorem quaerit et de calore crescit, et in terra, ubi magnus calor solis est, vinum forte est. Frumentum etiam calorem et frigus quaerit, et ubi calor solis et etiam frigus est, ibi frumentum habundat. De fruduum differentia, Et quaedam terrae sunt^ quae calidae, quaedam, quae frigidae, quaedam, quae tepidae sunt. Et secundum hoc homines, animalia, fructua terrae sunt, et tamen omnia unius generis sunt, quamvis hic maiorem vim, ibi minorem de vi solis habent.
text_translation: Wine indeed seeks great heat and grows from heat, and in the earth, where great heat of the sun is, wine is strong. Grain also seeks heat and cold, and where heat of the sun and also cold is, there grain abounds. Of the difference of fruits, and some lands are hot, some are cold, some are warm. And according to this, humans, animals, fruits of the earth are, and yet all are of one kind, although here they have greater strength, there lesser from the power of the sun.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De firmcmenti firmitate. Firmamentum quoqiie stellis continetur, ne dilabatur, velut homo a venis sustentatur,. ne diffluat et ne separetur. Et ut venae totum corpus hominis a pede usque ad caput pertranseunt, sic etiam stellae firmamentum. Et quemadmodum sanguis in venis movetur, et ut sanguis eas movet et salire et ictus dare facit, ita etiam ignis in stellis movetur et eas moveri et quasdam scintillas velut quosdam saltus et ictus emittere facit.
text_translation: On the firmness of the firmament. The firmament is also held by the stars, so that it does not slip away, just as a man is sustained by veins, so that he does not flow away and separate. And just as veins pass through the entire body of man from foot to head, so also the stars pass through the firmament. And just as blood moves in veins, and blood causes them to move and to rise and to give blows, so also fire moves in stars and causes them to move and to emit certain sparks, as if certain jumps and blows.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc communes stellae sunt, quae velut aliquas procellas in se faciunt, secundum quod opera hominum tunc sunt. Sed planetae ita diverso modo semper non moventur, nisi secundum quod a sole et a luna accipiunt et ut illa signa maiora designant. Ab eo autem loco, quo quaelibet stella posita est, ita sursum totum firmamentum transit, velut etiam vena, quae a pede usque ad caput hominis ascendit. Et omni firmamento splendorem et calorem tribuunt, quemadmodum etiam venae, quae iecur hominis transeunt, eidem iecuri sanguinem et calorem dant.
text_translation: And this is how the common stars are, which as if they create some storms within themselves, according to what human works are at that time. But the planets do not move in such a diverse manner always, unless according to what they receive from the sun and the moon, and as they signify greater signs. From that place, however, where each star is located, the entire firmament ascends upward, just as also a vein which rises from the foot to the head of a man. And to each firmament they give splendor and heat, just as also the veins which pass through the liver give blood and heat to that same liver.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et per totum firmamentum positae sunt et tam (in firmamento illo, quod in die videmus, quam in illo, quod in nocte cemimus. Sed stellae a maximo splendore solis, qui diem a£fert, obtegantur, ita ut in die videri non possunt, quod splendor eius maior est splendore eorum, qiiemadmodum, cum principes nominantur, plebeius populus contidscit, et cum principes recedunt, plebeius populus praecedit; alioquin tam in die quam in nocte cemerentur. 2e stellarum aignificationihus, Stellae enim multa signa interdum in se ostendunt secundum quod bomines in operibus suis se tunc habent.
text_translation: And they are placed throughout the firmament, both in that firmament which we see in the day, and in that which we see in the night. But the stars are obscured by the greatest splendor of the sun, which brings the day, so that they cannot be seen in the day, because his splendor is greater than theirs, just as when princes are named, the plebeian people follows, and when princes depart, the plebeian people precedes; otherwise they would be seen both in the day and in the night. De stellarum significationibus: For stars indeed sometimes show many signs in themselves according to what men have in their works at that time.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed nec futura nec cog^tationes hominum ostendunt, sed ea tantum, quae homo iam aut ostensa voluntate aut in voce aut in opere facit, qnoniam aer illa excipit. £t stellis tribuit ille, quod statim eo modo ostendunt opera hominis. Stellas autem deus ad servitutem hominis fecit, ut ei lucerent et ministrarent. Et ideo etiam opera eius ostendunt, velut servus voluntatem et opus domini sui manifestat. Nam sicut i^ anima in corpore hominis primum lucet et tunc in opus procedit, ita etiam et stellae in firmamento fulgent et opera hominis ostendunt, cum homo iam operatur.
text_translation: But neither the future nor the thoughts of men show, but only those things which man already does by will, or in voice, or in work, because the air receives them. And he assigns to the stars that which they immediately show in the manner of man's work. The stars, however, God made for the service of man, that they might shine and minister to him. And thus also the works of man show, as a servant manifests the will and work of his master. For just as the soul in the body of man first shines and then proceeds to work, so also the stars shine in the firmament and show the works of man when man already works.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De planetarum significatione, Sol vero et luna et ceteri planetae non semper opera hominis ostendunt, sed raro. Et cum ipsi aliquid ostendunt, hoc magnum est et ad publicam rem pertinet. Quoniam summus planeta, qui oculus dicitur, et ille, qui proximus supra lunam est, qui dicitur pauper, in profonditate firmamenti velut duo clavi positi sunt et hominibus non apparent, nisi quod aliquando, cum nubes obnubilatae sunt, velut quidam falgor ab eis in nubibus apparet, cum aliquid futurum esse praesignant.
text_translation: Concerning the significance of the planets, the sun, the moon, and the other planets do not always reveal the works of humans, but rarely. And when they do reveal something, this is great and pertains to public affairs. For the highest planet, which is called the eye, and that one which is next above the moon, called the poor, are placed like two nails in the depth of the firmament and are not visible to humans, except that sometimes, when the clouds are obscured, a certain flash appears from them in the clouds, when they signify something future.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed quod in sole aliqua signa interdum ostenduntur, hoc ideo evenit, quod isti duo planetae solem aggrediuntur, ita quod hoc modo signa in ipso fiunt, cum aHquid miraculi futurum erit. Ipsi vero planetae non apparebunt, ita ut perfecte et pleniter videantur, nisi ante diem iudicii. Et tunc splendorem suum de summo ad terram insolito emittunt, et per hoc sapientes homines diein iudicii appropinquare intelligent. Planeta autem, qui pupilla dicitur et qui post summum est, diluvium produxit et ostendit.
text_translation: But that certain signs sometimes appear in the sun, this happens because these two planets attack the sun, so that in this way signs are made in it when something miraculous will happen. Indeed, these planets will not appear so that they are perfectly and fully seen, unless before the day of judgment. And then they emit their brilliance from the highest to the earth in an unusual way, and through this wise men will understand that the day of judgment is approaching. The planet, which is called the pupil and which is beyond the highest, produced and showed the flood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et iste modo ut stella non apparet. & aed Tefait iacala sigittst. £t inierdiim palfidimi fdlgorem habet ne aJiqfiia demonstraiis. Sed planeta, qm. post fleeondiim est et diyes dicitiir, Ghnstom cmitn diabolimi pngnatamm nHmstraTit. Sed nnnc non nt siella apparet sed qoasi Iblgor in caelo, et tonc miraciila fdtnra demonstzat. Sed cam in sole obtnmcatio aat coloris matatio pneter solitam ostenditor, magna in mondo portentat fatara. De amrora. Qood Tero in mane sol rabet^ cam oritar, boc de firigore et bomiditate aeris est, qfnia hamiditas et frigns, qnae tanc sant, mbedinem ocalis hominam infenmt.
text_translation: And in this manner, like a star does not appear. Autem fuit iacula significat. Et interdum palidim fulgorem habet, ne aliqua demonstratis. Sed planeta, qui post secundum est et dies dicitur, Christum cum diabolimi nuptiis demonstrat. Sed nunc non est stella apparet sed quasi fulgor in caelo, et tunc miracula futura demonstrat. Sed cum in sole obtumcatio est coloris mutatio praeter solitam ostenditur, magna in mundo portentat fatara. De aurora. Quod vero in mane sol rabet, quam oritar, hoc de frigore et humiditate aeris est, quia humiditas et frigus, quae tunc sunt, membranam oculis hominum infestant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Simili modo in sero, cnm ad Tesperam rabet» hoc de firigore aeris est, qaomam tanc ad oceanam indinatar. Yesper aatem, qai et comes dicitar, Tehit intimos et secretos anncas solis est. Framentnm et Tinnm temperat, sed alio tempore plns, alio minaSL Postea planeta ille, qni didtor paaper, exstat et signa soa ostendit, at praefatam est, et etiam paopertatem in habandantia fragam terrae &citw De Utnae significatiaHe. Sed cam in lima aliqoa signa fiont, hoc etiam isti phinetae fBudont, qoi in profunditate firmameiiti Telot dao claTi positi sont et qoi solem ad signa ostendenda aggredinntor et lnnam ant incendendo ant obambrando commoTent.
text_translation: In a similar manner in the evening, when it approaches Vespera, this is due to the coldness of the air, as it turns toward the ocean. Vesper, which is also called the companion, reveals the innermost and secret annals of the sun. Fragmentum and Tinnam temper, but at one time more, at another time less. Afterwards, that planet which is called Pater appears and shows its signs, and it is also the one that, in the abundance of the fragrant earth, signifies De Utriusque. But when in some manner signs appear, these also do the same, those who are placed in the depth of the firmament Tellus and who are positioned in the dormit and who approach the sun to show the signs and move the moon either by burning or by overshadowing.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Lnna aatem in se excipit foeditatem inatilitatis aaramm et calorem pori aeris, stabilitatem atilis anrae, pericola tempestatom, fortem aerem, qoi omnem Tiriditatem profert, aerem, qni pomifera edodt, aerem, qoi exsiccat et defectam infert, qoi hiems est. Et haec omnia in se ita coUigit, ut Tir, qoi in atrem Tinom fundit et sascipit et iteram ebibit. Sic et lona omnia ista in se colligit crescendo et ebibit decrescendo, et ideo dies eios qoidam boni, qnidam mali snnt, qoidam atiles, qoidam inntiles, qoidam fortes, qnidam debiles, qnidam foedi, qoidam Tirides, qoidam sicci, qoidam in defectione fractaom manent.
text_translation: Luna autem in se excipit foeditatem inatilitatis aaramm et calorem pori aeris, stabilitatem atilis aerae, periculum tempestatis, fortem aerem, qui omnem Tiriditatem profert, aerem, qui pomifera edoct, aerem, qui exsiccat et defectam infert, qui hiems est. Et haec omnia in se ita coUigit, ut Tir, qui in atrem Tinom fundit et sascipit et iteram ebibit. Sic et luna omnia ista in se colligit crescendo et ebibit decrescendo, et ideo dies eius quidam boni, quidam mali sunt, quidam atiles, quidam inntiles, quidam fortes, quidam debiles, quidam foedi, quidam Tirides, quidam sicci, quidam in defectione fractaom manent.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam luna hano Ticissitudinem in se habet, ideo etiam humectatio hominis Ticissitudinem et mutabilitatem in dolore, in labore, in sapientia et in prosperitate habet. Et humectationes hominam secundnm solem notari non debent, ut ab eo temperentur, q^uod ipse in uno statu stat, ita quod nec crescit nec decrescit; nec etiam secundum stellas notandae sunt, quoniam stellae non secundum se ipsas sed secundum liinam faciunt; nec secundum tempora anni, quoniam illa de luna temperantur; nec secundum auram aeris, scilicet pluviae aut siccitatis hiemis aut aestatis, quia ipsa secundum lunam faciunt.
text_translation: And since the moon has in itself Ticissitudinem, therefore also the moistening of man has Ticissitudinem and changeability in pain, in labor, in wisdom and in prosperity. And moistenings should not be noted according to the sun, so that they may be tempered by it, which itself remains in one state, neither increasing nor decreasing; nor should they be noted according to the stars, because the stars do not act according to themselves but according to a line; nor according to the times of the year, because those are tempered by the moon; nor according to the breeze of the air, namely the rain or dryness of winter or summer, because they themselves act according to the moon.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam omnia secundum lunam temperantur, quoniam illa mater omnium temporum est, quod, ut filii matris de matre numerantur, ita omnia tempora numerantur de luna. Aer etiam et stellae aliquando opera hominum sixscipiunt, et secundum haec interdum divino iudicio extenduntur et se componunt et auram emittunt. Et cum ad haec eriguntur, luna exinde movetur. Et secundum Hla dies eius erunt aut in puritate et claritate aut in tempestate. Et ideo luna multis periculis et tempestatibus opprimitur, quemadmodum etiam mater multa pericula et tristitias in genitura natorum suorum sustinet.
text_translation: For all things are tempered according to the moon, because it is the mother of all times, as children count from their mother, so all times are counted from the moon. Air and stars also sometimes receive the works of humans, and according to these, they are sometimes extended and composed and emit a breeze by divine judgment. And when they are raised, the moon is moved from there. And according to Hla, its days will be either in purity and clarity or in tempest. And therefore the moon is oppressed by many dangers and tempests, just as also a mother sustains many dangers and sorrows in the birth of her children.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ideo tempora lunae sana et non sana sunt et matura et non matura. Si enim homo ita faceret, ut positus est, tunc etiam omnia tempora et aurae in temporibus suis aequaliter facerent, scilicet hoc yemo tempore ut praeterito yerno tempore, et in hac aestate ut in praeterita aestate, et sic de ceteris. Cum autem homo et timorem et amorem dei per inobedientiam transilit, tunc omnia elementa et tempora iura sua supergrediuntur; velut viscera in homine; quia, cum homo modum suum excedit, viscera eius eum sequuntur; et cum malis operibus iustitiam supergreditur, solem et lunam aggravat et obnubilat, ita ut secundum eum in tempestatibus, in pluviis et in siccitate faciunt.
text_translation: And therefore the times of the moon are healthy and not healthy, and mature and not mature. For if man were to act as he is placed, then all times and auras would act equally in their own times, namely this time as the past time, and this summer as the past summer, and thus with the rest. When, however, man, through disobedience, both fear and love of God pass over, then all elements and times transgress their own laws; just as the viscera in man; for when man exceeds his measure, his viscera follow him; and when he transgresses justice through evil works, he aggravates and obscures the sun and moon, so that according to him they make storms, rains, and droughts.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam stomachus et vesica hominis suscipiunt omnia, quibus corpus eius nutritur. Et cum haec duo cibos et potus superflue suscipiunt, tempestates malorum humorum omni corpori inferunt, sicut etiam elementa secundum hominem faciunt. Homo enim temperato tempore caloris et frigoris semen suum seminat, et illud in fructus surgit. £t quis esset Hildegardis caosae et curae ed. Eaiser. ille tam stultns, qui in nimio calore aestatis aut in nimio frigore hiemis semen suum seminaret?
text_translation: For the stomach and bladder of man receive all things by which his body is nourished. And when these two receive food and drink in excess, tempests of bad humors bring harm to the entire body, just as the elements also do according to human nature. For man indeed sows his semen at a moderate time of heat and cold, and this arises into fruit. And who would be so foolish, that he would sow his semen in excessive heat of summer or in excessive cold of winter?

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et illud periret et non surgeret. De tempore gignendi, Ita hominibus illis contingit, qui nec maturum tempus aetatis suae nec tempus lunae considerant, sed unoquoque tempore secundum voluntatem suam gignere yolunt. Et ideo nati eorum in plunbus doloribus corporaliter deficiunt. Sed tamen, quamviscunque deficientes sint in corpore, deus gemmas suas ad se colligit. Et ideo vir maturum tempus corporis sui inspicere debet et recta tempora lunae cum tali studio quaerere, ut ille, qui orationes suas puras offert; scilicei ut eo tempore prolem gignat, quod nati eius non pereant deficiendo.
text_translation: And that thing would perish and not rise again. Concerning the time of procreation, thus it happens to those men who neither consider the mature time of their age nor the time of the moon, but wish to procreate at every time according to their own will. And therefore their children suffer more bodily deficiencies. But nevertheless, although they are deficient in body, God collects his gems to himself. And therefore a man must inspect the mature time of his body and seek the correct times of the moon with such study, so that he who offers his prayers pure; namely, so that he begets offspring at that time when his children do not perish by deficiency.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nec fiat ut homo ille, qui cibum in se tor quet, ut vorax, qui rectum tempus cibi non quaerit; sed esse debet ut ille, qui rectum tempus habet, ne sit ut vorax. Sic homo esse debet et quaerere rectum tempus geniturae suae. Yir ad feminam non accedat, cum illa puella est, sed iuvencula, quod tunc matura est, nec ipse feminam tangat ante barbam, sed cum barbam habuerit, quoniam tunc maturus est ad fecunditatem prolis. Homo namque, qui in voracitate et in crapula est, in membris suis saepe leprosus et tortuosus fit; qui autem in cibo et potu continens est, bonum sanguinem et sanum corpus habebit.
text_translation: Let it not be as that man who consumes food within himself as a glutton, who does not seek the right time for food; but he should be as he who has the right time, so that he is not as a glutton. Thus a man should be and seek the right time of his own procreation. Let the man not approach the woman when she is a girl, but a heifer, for then she is not yet mature, nor should he touch the woman before she has a beard, but when she has a beard, for then she is mature for the fertility of offspring. For indeed, a man who is in gluttony and drunkenness often becomes leprous and crooked in his limbs; but he who is continent in food and drink will have good blood and a healthy body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic etiam et ille, qui semper in libidine et in superfluitate corporis sui facit desideria sua, ut, cum tempestas geniturae suae in eo surgit, semen suum proiciendo perdit, saepe in semine suo perit. Qui autem semen suum recte fundit, rectam genituram profert. 2 e elementis.
