Elementa autem unamquamque naturam hominis bibunt, cum homo elementa in se trahit, quia homo cum illis est et illa cum homine, et secundum hoc sanguis hominis inundat. Unde etiam scriptum est: caelum et terra plangunt super hominem, quoniam inquieta bella in operibus hominum elementa saepe com- 18,80 — 19,88 iternm legnntnr in fine libri, cfr.
text_translation: Thus also he who always in lust and in the superfluity of his body fulfills his desires, so that when the tempest of his generation arises in him, he casts forth his semen and thereby loses it, often perishes in his own semen. He who however rightly casts forth his semen produces a right generation. 2 ex elementis. The elements indeed drink the nature of each man, when man draws the elements into himself, because man is with them and they are with man, and according to this the blood of man floods. Hence also it is written: heaven and earth lament over man, because the elements often commune in the works of men tumultuously—18,80—19,88 iterum leguntur in the end of the book, cfr.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: adn. crit. movent, quemadmodum homo rete in manu sua teneat et illud moveat; ita et homo elementa commovet, quod secuiidum opera eius auram emittunt. De lunae effectu, Et tempus lunae naturae bominis non dominatur, quasi deus eius sit, et velut homo ali- qnam potestatem naturae de illa accipiat; aut ut luna naturae hominis aliquid conferat aut demat aut constituat; sed homini in aereo gustu cuiusque operis vitae suae luna occurrit, et ita sanguis et humores, qui in homine sunt, post tempus commotionis lunae moventur, scilicet secun- lO dum quod luna aerem in bona temperie aut tempestate moyet et secundum quod tunc sanguis et humores in homine inundant, humectatio hominis naturam sibi in moribus contrahit.
text_translation: The critical movements are as follows, just as a man holds a net in his hand and moves it; so too does man move the elements, which according to his works emit air. Concerning the moon's effect, the time of the moon does not dominate human nature, as though it were its god, nor does man receive any power of nature from it, nor does the moon contribute, remove, or establish anything in human nature; but the moon encounters man in the aerial gust of each work of his life, and thus the blood and humors within him move after the time of the moon's motion, namely according to how the moon moves the air in good temper or tempest, and according to how then the blood and humors flood within man, the moistening of human nature draws itself into the body's habits.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed etiam cum venae hominis intumescunt, scilicet cum ira et iracundia, cum petulantia, conviviis, tristitia et infirmitate corporis et cum mutabili vicissitudine turbinis morum hominis, secundum haec humectatio hominis gustum in natura sua capit, velut unusqnisque coctus cibus gustum secundum genus suum tenet. Sed tamen onmem hanc naturam hominis, ut praescriptum est, spiritus sanctus penetrat, videlicet in prophetis, in sapientibus, in bonis, in rectis; et in omni bona electione eos ad se trahens, sicut sol tempestates, pertransit et perlustrat, ita quod haec perfusio ignei spiritus sancti mutabilem naturam hominis superat, ut scriptum est: omnequod natum est ex deo, vincit mundum; et sic non peccat.
text_translation: But also when the veins of man swell, namely with anger and wrath, with impertinence, feasts, sadness, and bodily weakness, and with the mutable alternation of the spiral of human manners, according to these the moistening of man's taste in his nature receives, as each cooked food retains taste according to its kind. But nevertheless this nature of man, as prescribed, is penetrated by the holy spirit, namely in prophets, in wise men, in good, in righteous; and drawing them to itself in every good election, as the sun passes through and traverses storms, so that this infusion of fiery holy spirit overcomes the mutable nature of man, as it is written: whatever is born of God conquers the world; and thus does not sin.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ut viles cibi de gustu aromatum in alium meliorem gustum mutantur, ita ut vilem gustum suum perdant, sic de igne spiritus sancti vilis natura hominis in meliorem naturam, quam coneeptio eius ostendat, vertitur ; et ita so homo alius in natura sua fit, quia quod caeleste est devincit et exsuperat quod terrenum est, unde oztinia in deo gaudent antiquo serpente illuso. Nunc autem, ut praedictum est, minores ceterae stellae diversa et conamunia et minora opera hominum demonstrant.
text_translation: And just as vile foods, by the taste of aromatic substances, are changed into a more pleasant taste, losing their vile taste, so too the vile nature of man, by the fire of the Holy Spirit, is turned into a better nature, as his conception shows; and thus man becomes another in his nature, because what is celestial conquers and surpasses what is earthly, whence the ostinia rejoice in God, the ancient serpent being deceived. Now, however, as has been said, the lesser stars demonstrate diverse and common and lesser works of men.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim sere- nitas in nubibus est, ita ut nulla motio ventorum nec tempestatum nec imbrium in aere sit, sed quod stellae 2 in claritate sua appareant, si tonc aliqua nubes omnes stellas obtegit, ita ut yideri non possint nulla motione aeris existente, et sic totam noctem illam manet vel etiam secundam et tertiam noctem similiter: non sine significa tione alicuius portenti est. Quod si nubes illa stellas in loco uno tantum obtegit, ita quod videri non possint, et si nubes illa cito subtrahitur, nibil portentat, quamvis nulla motio aeris sit.
text_translation: When indeed serenity is in the clouds, so that there is no motion of winds nor storms nor rains in the air, but that the stars appear in their clarity, if then some cloud covers all the stars so that they cannot be seen, with no motion of air existing, and thus the entire night remains cloudy, or even the second and third night similarly: it is not without the significance of some portent. If that cloud covers the stars only in one place, so that they cannot be seen, and if that cloud quickly withdraws, nothing portends, although there is no motion of air.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod planetae ex sui natura nihil significent. Hae autem significationes non propria virtute ipsorum planetarum seu stellarum aut nubium fiunt, sed permissione et voluntate ac constitutione dei, secundum quod deus bominibus opera ipsorum demonstrare voluerit, quemadmodum nummus imaginem domini sui ostendit. De virihus ignis. Deus itaque cum mundum creavit, quatuor elementis, scilicet igne, aere, aqua et terra eum confirmavit, ut praedictum est. Ignis enim, qui summus in firmamento et in elementis est, quinque vires babet, videlicet ardorem, geliditatem, bumorem, aerem, motionem; quemadmodum bomo quinque sensibus consistit.
text_translation: That the planets signify nothing by their own nature. These significations, however, are not brought about by the proper virtue of the planets or stars or clouds themselves, but by the permission and will and constitution of God, according to how God wished to demonstrate to men the works of them, just as a coin shows the image of its master. On the powers of fire. God, therefore, when he created the world, confirmed it with four elements, namely fire, air, water, and earth, as was said before. For fire, which is highest in the firmament and in the elements, has five powers, namely heat, cold, humidity, air, motion; just as man consists of five senses.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam ignis ardet, sed geliditas ei obstat, ne ardor eius supra modum discurrat. Et aqua bumorem ei subministrat, ut suMatus ipsius ^accendat. De aere autem accenditur, et de motione impellitur, ut flamma eius luceat. De viribtcs aeris. Sed aer quatuor vires habet, videlicet rorem emittendo, omnem viriditatem excitando, flatum movendo , cum quo flores educit , calorem dilatando, cum quo omnia maturescere facit; velut et ipse per quatuor partes mundi dilatatur.
text_translation: For fire burns, but coldness opposes it, so that its heat does not spread excessively. And water supplies moisture to it, so that its saturation may ignite. From air it is ignited, and by motion it is driven, so that its flame may shine. Of the forces of air. But air has four forces, namely by emitting moisture, by exciting all greenness, by moving wind, by which it brings forth flowers, by expanding heat, by which all things mature; as he himself expands through the four parts of the world.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aer enim spiramen est, quod in rore humorem germinantibus infundit, ita ut omnia virescaut; et quod per flatum flores educit et per calorem omnia ad maturitatem conflrmat. Aer autem, qui proximus dispositioni lunae et stellarum est, sidera bumectat, velut terrenus aer terram et animalia irrationabilia et sensibilia secundum naturam ipsorum vivificat et movet 1 20,29—21,8 = Migne 1209 D — 1211 A. nec ideo in se minoratnr. Sed cnm eadem animalia moriuotur, idem aer ad pristinum statum suum revertitur nec inde in se aug^ientatur, sed manet, ut prius fuit.
text_translation: Air is indeed the breath, which pours into the moisture of germinating humors, so that all things become green; and which through the wind brings forth flowers and through heat confirms all things toward maturity. Air, however, which is near the disposition of the moon and stars, moistens the stars, just as the earthly air vivifies and moves the earth and irrational and sensible animals according to their own nature, and is not thereby diminished in itself. But when the same animals die, the same air returns to its former state and is not thereby increased, but remains as it was before.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aer autem terrenus, qui terram humectat, arbores et herbas virescere et crescere ac moveri facit. Nec, cum in istis est, in se deficit; nec ipsis abscisis aut evulsis, cum ab eis egreditur, in se proficit, sed in statu suo, ut ante erat, permanet. De animae viribus. Anima autem hominis de caelo a deo veniens in hominem et ipsum vivificans ac rationa- lo bile faciens, cum ab homine egreditur, non moritur, sed aut ad praemia vitae seu ad torraenta mortis aetemaliter victura vadit.
text_translation: The earthly air, which moistens the earth, causes trees and herbs to turn green, grow, and move. Nor, when it is in them, does it diminish itself; nor, when they are cut off or torn away, does it profit itself when it exits from them, but remains in its state, as it was before. On the powers of the soul. The soul of man, coming from heaven from God into man and giving life to it and making it rational bile, when it exits from man, does not die, but goes either to the rewards of life or to eternal victory over the torments of death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De aquae virUnis. Aqua vero vires quindecim habet, id est calorem, aerem, humorem, inundationem, velocitatem, mobilitatem; et lignis succum dat, pomiferis gustum, herbis viriditatem; et humiditate sua in omnibus madet, volucres continet, pisces pascit, bestias in calore suo vivere facit, reptilia in spuma sua retinet ac omnia sustentat: quemadmodum decem praecepta et quinque libri Moysi veteris testamenti sunt, quae cuncta deus ad spiritalem intelligentiam convertit.
text_translation: On the virtue of water. Water indeed has fifteen powers, that is, heat, air, humor, inundation, speed, mobility; and it gives sap to trees, taste to fruit-bearing plants, greenness to herbs; and by its humidity it makes all things wet, contains birds, feeds fish, makes beasts live by its heat, retains reptiles in its foam and sustains all things: just as the ten commandments and five books of Moses of the Old Testament are, which God converts all things into spiritual understanding.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vivente namque fonte salientes aquae simt, quae omnes sordes abluunt. Nam aqua in omni mobili creatura labilis est ac etiam incendium omnis viriditatis immobilium creaturarum est. De calore humidi aeris manat, et si calorem non haberet, dura propter frigus esset. De calore quippe defluit et de humiditate aeris manat. Quod si hunc aerem non haberet, fluere non posset. De his enim tribus viribus, scilicet calore, humore et aere velox est, ita ut nihil resistat, so ubi ipsa superexcellerit.
text_translation: From the living fountain, the waters that flow are, which wash away all filth. For water is flowing in every mobile creature, and also the fire of all greenness in immobile creatures is. From the heat of the humid air flows, and if it did not have heat, it would be hard because of cold. From heat indeed flows and from the humidity of the air flows. That if it did not have this air, it could not flow. From these three forces, namely heat, humor, and air, it is swift, so that nothing resists, so where it surpasses.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et lignis succum dat et aere suo eadem mobilia facit atque calido humore pomiferis, scilicet unicuique secundum genus suum, gustum tribuit. Herbae etiam de fluente humore eius viriditatem in se habent, et lapides de humore eius sudant. Sic vis 1 21,22—21,28 » Migne p. 1211 A. aquae omnia colligit, ne deficiant, quod hnmor eius in omnibus sudat. Aquosas quoque volucres cum calido calore suo continet et pisces, quia in ipsa nati sunt et quoniam de flatu eius yivunt, pascit.
text_translation: But also to woods it gives juice, and with its air it makes the same things movable, and with warm humor it makes fruit-bearing [things], namely, to each according to its kind, it gives taste. Herbs also have greenness within themselves from its flowing humor, and stones sweat from its humor. Thus power 1 21,22—21,28 » Migne p. 1211 A. collects all waters, so that they may not fail, because its humor sweats in all things. It also contains aquatic birds with its warm heat and fish, because they are born in it and because they live from its breath, it feeds them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et bestiae, quae in ipsa manere possunt, per calorem eius durant, ac reptilia per aquosum flatum eius spiramen habent, ita quod sic yivere possunt. Hoc etenim modo cum viribus suis omnia continet et sustinet. De Jiyle et creaturarum vivificatione, Nunc itaque cum verbum dei in principio sonuit, globus creaturarum sine igne et frigidus fuit. Et spiritus domini ferebatur super aquas, qui ignis et vita est. Tunc idem spiritus unicuique formatae creaturae vitam secundum genus sumn inspiravit et in eis ignem insufflando accendit, ita ut quaeque creatura ignem et vitam secundum genus suum in se habeat.
text_translation: But also the beasts which can dwell in it endure through its heat, and reptiles have a breath of spirit through its watery exhalation, so that thus they can live. Indeed, in this way with their own powers it contains and sustains all things. Of Jiyle and the vivification of creatures, Now, indeed, when the word of God sounded in the beginning, the globe of creatures was without fire and cold. And the spirit of the Lord was moving over the waters, which is fire and life. Then the same spirit, by blowing into each formed creature, inspired life according to its kind and kindled fire within them, so that each creature has fire and life according to its own kind within itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Opus autem verbi viriditas est, sed nulla viriditas esset, si igne et calore non teneretur; et quaeque creatura sine consolatione desolaretur et divideretur et caderet, si fundamento igneae vitae spiritus non flrmaretur. De aquae labilitate. Quemadmodum enim spiritus domini ignis et vita est, et ut omni creaturae esse et vitam dedit, sic aqua in ipso labilis est, quod congregat, retinet et firmat alias creaturas, et lubrica et fragilis esfc multa vivificans et mortificans.
text_translation: The work of the word is vitality, but there would be no vitality if it were not held by fire and heat; and every creature, without consolation, would be desolate and divided and fall, if the foundation of the fiery life of the spirit were not made firm. On the liquidity of water. For indeed, the spirit of the Lord is fire and life, and as it gave being and life to every creature, so water is fluid in it, which gathers, retains, and strengthens other creatures, and being slippery and fragile, it vivifies and mortifies many things.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quaedam etiam in aliena genitura in se tenet, quae super eam inniti non possent, quia caderent; et haec interdum mortificat. Sed tamen et aquoso cursu, quo currit, ventus et ignis est. De sole et aquis eius. Aquae vero, quae quasi ad medium solis sunt, cum sol in medio firmamenti velut in corde eius est, magnam potentiam et magnum circuitum habent et de calore solis spissae et de aere fortes sunt Sed de fluente impetu non attenuantur, quia non fluunt; sed tamen vento aguntur.
text_translation: Certain things also hold within a foreign generation, which cannot be built upon it, because they would fall; and this sometimes causes death. But nevertheless, through the watery course by which it flows, there is wind and fire. Of the sun and its waters. Indeed, the waters which are as if in the middle of the sun, since the sun is in the middle of the firmament as in its heart, have great power and a great circuit, and are thick from the sun's heat and strong from the air. But they are not weakened by the flowing impulse, because they do not flow; yet they are acted upon by the wind.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sol quoque interdum ignem, qui in aquis est, attrahit; et tempestas ventorum easdem aquas in magnis undis interdum congregat ut flamma, quae aliquam rem incendit. Et tunc aqua elevatur ut ignis, qui erigitur, et aqua ignem sequitur, atque hoe modo velut coUes et montes eriguntur. Sed cum deinde cessando compescontur, semen suam, quod est sal de igne et aqua factum, deiciunt, quemadmodum herbae semina sna in maturitate proiciunt. De sale, Sal autem de igne siccum est, de humiditate irero aquae gustum habet.
text_translation: The sun also sometimes draws fire, which is in waters; and the storm of winds sometimes gathers the same waters in great waves as flame, which sets something on fire. And then water is raised up as fire, which rises up, and water follows fire, and in this way, like hills and mountains, they rise up. But when they then cease and mature, they cast out their seed, which is salt made from fire and water, as herbs cast out their seeds in maturity. About salt, Salt, however, is dry from fire, and from the humidity of water has taste.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De aquarum diversitate. Et rivuli, qui de his magnis aquis, scilicet de mari, fluunt, et fontes, qui ex his saliunt, salsi sunt et maiorem ignem maioresque vires in se habent ceteris aquis; quemadmodum etiam cor maiores vires reliquo corpore habet; quia etiam haec flumina magna, lo de quibus fluunt, maiores vires ceteris fluminibus habent, quoniam supra primam et sanam arenam ab initio positam fiuunt et non super illam, quam in cursu suo fecerunt aut detexerunt.
text_translation: On the diversity of waters. And the streams which flow from these great waters, namely from the sea, and the springs which rise from them, are salty and possess greater heat and greater forces within themselves than other waters; just as also the heart possesses greater forces than the rest of the body; for these great rivers, from which they flow, possess greater forces than other rivers, because they are formed above the first and healthy sand placed at the beginning and not upon that which they have made or uncovered in their course.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aqua vero quasi aquatile corpus terrae est et terra quasi cor aquae, quia aqua terram circum- cingit et perfiindit terram, ut corpus cor comprehendat et tegit. Et terra aquam sustentat, quemadmodum etiam cor corpus tenet. Sed et aqua magni maris, quod mundum circumcingit, est quasi latus illarum aquarum, quae super firmamentum sunt, quod summitas illarum, quae sunt super firmamentum, et extremitas illarum, quae sub firmamento sunt, sibi invicem coniunguntur.
text_translation: Water indeed is as the watery body of the earth, and the earth is as the heart of water, because water surrounds the earth and pours over the earth, so that the body encompasses and covers the heart. And the earth sustains water, just as the heart holds the body. But also the water of the great sea, which encircles the world, is as the side of those waters which are above the firmament, which the summit of those which are above the firmament and the extremity of those which are below the firmament are joined to each other.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et his aquis multiplices pelles firmamenti oppositae sunt, quae sibi ita ut folia tabularum complicatae sunt, quatinus diversos fluxus et inundationes aquarum contineant. Sed quemad- modum anima rationem, intellectum, scientiam et sensibilitatem in se continet, sic etiam firmamentum quatuor elementa secundum genus suum in se continet et sustentat. Et aquae istae uno tenore stant, ut positae sunt, et scutum suum, scilicet terram, irrigant, dum aquas infundunt et e£Fandunt, ita ut illae aliquando in terra dilatentur et interdum ascendendo surgant.
text_translation: And to these waters, multiple skins of the firmament are opposed, which are folded together like the leaves of tables, so that they may contain and hold back diverse flows and inundations of waters. But just as the soul contains within itself reason, intellect, knowledge, and sensibility, so too does the firmament contain and sustain within itself the four elements according to their kind. And these waters stand in one tenor, as they were placed, and irrigate their shield, namely the earth, while they pour out and pour forth waters, so that sometimes they spread out in the earth and at times rise upward.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et iterum in pluvia efiunduntur, et hoc modo scutum suum aqua, ventis et aere firmat, ne diffiuat et ne cadat. Arena autem maris, quae ab initio ad orientem posita est, saepe aeris indeficientis terrae tangitur; et hinc aromata et aliae medicinae in eadem arena sunt. Si homo ea habere posset, nuUa in firmitate laederetur; sed et si qaidam lapides eiusdem arenae efQaere valerentf eo quod homo eos babere posset, infirmitates, pestes et putredmes ab eo fagarent.
text_translation: And again, they are poured out in the rain, and in this way their shield is made firm by water, winds, and air, so that it may not spread nor fall. But the sand of the sea, which is placed from the beginning toward the east, often touches the earth with inadequate air; and hence, aromas and other medicines are found in the same sand. If a man could possess these, no harm would befall him in firmness; but even if certain stones of the same sand could be made effective, because a man could possess them, infirmities, plagues, and putrefactions would arise from them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed aqua ibi tam magna et tam profanda est, quod baberi non possunt. De maris effltixiane. Et quoniam in oriente magna profunditas arenae et litoris est, idcirco mare superbabundando et se dilatando ibi non effluit; in occidente autem et in austro ac in aquilone tanta profunditas arenarum et litoris non est. Ideo ibi multotiens effluit magnas et latas efFusiones ibi faciens, cum ab igne procellarum in insaniam commovetur, ut praedictum est. Unde ibi multa inutilia et sordida in se colligit atque putredines bominum, pecorum, avium et vermium sibi attrahit.
text_translation: But the water there is so great and so deep that they cannot pass through. Concerning the sea's effluvium. And because in the east there is great depth of sand and shore, therefore the sea, overflowing and expanding there, does not efflux there; in the west, however, and in the south and in the north, such depth of sands and shore is not present. Therefore there, often effluxes great and wide effusions there, making, when moved to madness by the fire of storms, as previously mentioned. Hence there, it collects many useless and dirty things into itself and attracts putrefactions of humans, animals, birds, and worms.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et idcirco fontes et flumina, quae de partibus istis de mari efQuunt, tam sana et tam bona non sunt sicut illa, quae de orientali mari effluunt. De aquarum diversitate. Nam salsi fontes et salsi rivuli, qui ex aqua illa orientalis plagae in diversis terris saliendo efQuunt, puri snnt et secundum aerem de puritate illa aliquantum virides sunt. Et in arena, ubi fluunt, in guttis saliunt et sani et utiles sunt et in potu sumi possunt et etiam ad coquendum cibum boni sunt.
text_translation: Therefore, the springs and rivers, which flow out from those parts from the sea, are not so healthy and good as those which flow out from the eastern sea. On the diversity of waters. Indeed, salty springs and salty streams, which flow out from that water of the eastern shore, rising in various lands, are pure and, in accordance with the air, somewhat green from that purity. And in the sand, where they flow, they rise in drops and are healthy and useful and can be drunk and are also good for cooking food.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et bomo, qui inflrmus est, si aquas illas frequenter biberit, sanitatem recipit, quod fiimum, foetorem et putredinem malorum bumorum velut bonum unguentum ab eo aufe25runt. Si autem in corpore sanus est, eum aHquantum laedunt, si eas biberit, et illi ulcera interius inferunt, quod in eo non inveniunt, quod purgent. Aquae vero fluminum et viyentium ac salientium fontium, qui in eadem orientali plaga absque sapore salis oriuntur et ab oriente fluunt, puri sunt suavemque colorem suavi frigore adiuncto babent, ita quod temperate calidae et frigidae sunt et utiles bominibus ad cibum, ad potum, ad balnea, ad lavandum.
text_translation: And the man who is weak, if he frequently drinks those waters, receives health, which remove the fetor, stench, and putrefaction of the bad humors as a good ointment from him. If, however, in the body he is healthy, they harm him as much as he drinks them, and they cause internal ulcers, which they do not find in him to purge. The waters of rivers and living, sprouting fountains, which arise without the taste of salt and flow from the east, are pure and have a pleasant color, joined with a sweet coldness, so that they are temperate, warm and cold, and useful to men for food, for drink, for baths, for washing.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed in manibus aliquantum acres et durae sunt. Sed salsae aquae, quae ab occidente fluunt, aliquantum turbidae sunt ut turbo; et cibus quidem cum eis coqui potest, quia per ignem aliquantum in coctione purgantur; ad potum autem crudae sumptae nocivae sunt, quoniam in occidente mari quaeque sordida et putrida sibi attrahunt et illuc quaeque cadavera mortuorum fluunt. Quod si pro aliqua necessitate et penuria aliarum aquarum vitari non possunt, quin in potu sumantur, tunc primitus coquantur, et cum deinde infrigidatae sunt, bibanlur.
text_translation: But they are somewhat sharp and hard in the hands. But the salty waters, which flow from the west, are somewhat turbid like a whirlpool; and indeed food can be cooked with them, because through fire they are somewhat purified in cooking; but when drunk raw, they are harmful, because in the western sea they attract each thing dirty and rotten, and there each thing flows of dead bodies. If, for some necessity and scarcity of other waters, they cannot be avoided but must be drunk, then they should be cooked first, and when they are then cooled, they are drunk.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed non salsa flumina et viventes ac salientes non salsi fontes, qui in occidente oriuntur et ab occidente fluunt, ubi sol occidit et deficit, candidas et spissas babent aquas nec calore nec frigore ibi ad perfectum ducuntur, quoniam calor et frigus ibi in utrisque defectum habet. Unde etiam aquae illae aliquantum inutiles sunt ^ad usus hominum, scilicet ad potum, ad lavandum et ad babieum, quia a sole non coquuntur. Quod si necessitas compellit, ut quaeque necessaria cum eis parentur, tunc fortiter igne coquantur et deinde aliquantum infrigidari permittantur ad temperiem; sed tamen ad cibum aliquantum valent, quod cum cibo fortiter coquuntur.
text_translation: But not salty rivers and living and flowing non-salty springs, which arise in the west and flow from the west, where the sun sets and declines, have white and thick waters, nor are they led to perfection by heat or cold, because heat and cold there have a defect in both. Hence also those waters are somewhat useless for human use, namely for drinking, for washing, and for bathing, because they are not cooked by the sun. If necessity compels, that each necessary thing is prepared with them, then they are strongly cooked with fire and afterwards allowed to cool somewhat to temperance; but nevertheless for food they are somewhat useful, because they are strongly cooked with food.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aquae autem, quae salsae sunt et in australi plaga de mari oriuntur, sive fontes sive fiumina sint, aliquantum albae nec tamen multum purae sunt et nec ad cibum nec ad potum valent, quoniam venenosae sint. Nam parvi et venosissimi vermes atque parva animalia, quae aliquantum venenosa sunt, illuc propter solem de frigore fugiunt et propter calorem ibi libenter habitant et in aqua se lavant et in aqua illa iacent, quod naturam salis habent et calorem pati possunt et nullum frigus.
text_translation: The waters, which are salty and arise from the sea in the southern region, whether they are springs or rivers, are somewhat white but not very pure, and neither suitable for food nor for drinking, because they are venomous. For small and very venomous worms and small animals, which are somewhat venomous, flee from the cold because of the sun and willingly dwell there because of the heat, washing themselves in the water and lying in that water, because they have the nature of salt and can endure heat and no cold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed aquae fiuminum et viventium et salientium fontium, qui sapore salis carentes in austro oriuntur et ibi in fervore ardent, si separatim ab aliis aquis disiunctae fiuerent, difficile a fervore ignis sui extinguerentur, quoniam ab extinguibilibus ignibus tactae sunt et ab eis exeunt. Et ad cibum et ad alias quasdam utilitates ita ferventes valerent, quia igne exquisitae et excoctae sunt. Sed postquam ad fngida flumina pervenerint et eis coniunctae fuerint, ita quod simul fluunt, tunc facile in undis excitantur et facile spumas eiciunt et quasi argenteum calorem habent.
text_translation: But the waters of rivers and living and springing fountains, which lack the taste of salt, arise in the south and there burn in fervor, if they were separately disjoined from other waters, would scarcely be extinguished by the fervor of their own fire, because they have been touched by extinguishable fires and from them proceed. And for food and for other certain utilities, thus fervent they would be useful, because they have been extracted and boiled by fire. But after they have reached cold rivers and have been joined to them, so that they flow together, then easily they are excited in waves and easily emit foam and have almost a silver heat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Atque ad cibum et ad potum et ad bahiea ac ad lavandum utiles sunt, quod ab immunditia et ab acerbitate sua purgatae sunt ut aqua, quae in olla per fervorem ignis purificatur. Sed tamen cames homines pingues faciunt et colorem eius nigrum. Salsae autem aquae, quae a septentrionali plaga iuxta orientem oriuntur et effluunt, nocivae sunt et tam hominibus quam pecoribus pestes facile infenmt, quod virtus salis ibi non est sana, quoniam in partibus illis immutatio coloris et frigoris est; et ideo nec ad cibum nec ad potum valent, ita quod etiam vix in ceteros usus sumi possunt.
text_translation: And useful for food and drink and for bath and for washing are they, because purified from impurity and from their bitterness they are like water which is purified in a pot by the heat of fire. But nevertheless cames make men fat and their color black. However, saline waters which arise from the northern region near the east and flow out are harmful and easily infest both men and cattle with plagues, because the virtue of salt there is not healthy, since in those regions there is a change of color and coldness; and therefore neither for food nor for drink are they useful, so that even scarcely can they be used for other purposes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aliae vero aquae non salsae, quae ab eodem septentrione iuxta orientem effluunt, frigidae et utiles sunt, quod ab aere illo, qui de oriente flat, aliquantulum tanguntur; qui sanus est, quoniam temperatus est, ita quod nec multum calidus nec multum frigidus esi Et aer iste inter montes illos flat, qui illio ab initio positi sunt, et est quasi penna de terra viventium, unde etiam alio aere salubrior et sanior est. Et de aquis istis, quae hic oriuntur, quidam rivuli et fontes educuntur, qui yelut puri sunt, sed tamen aliquantum turbidi, et alienum gustum aquae tenent, ita quod aut secundum vinum aut secundum alium modum saporem habent.
text_translation: Other waters, indeed, not salty, which flow out from the same north near the east, are cold and useful, because they are touched somewhat by that air which blows from the east; which is healthy, because it is tempered, so that it is neither very hot nor very cold. And this air blows between those mountains which were placed there from the beginning, and is as it were the wing of living things from the earth, whence also it is more wholesome and healthier by another air. And from these waters, which arise here, certain streams and springs are produced, which are as if pure, but nevertheless somewhat turbid, and hold an alien taste of water, so that they have either the taste of wine or another kind of flavor.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quae aquae interdum hydropicam pestem comprimunt et paralysim compescunt, si eas biberint, qui his malis fatigantur, quoniam natura aquarum istarum resistit naturae alterius aquae et etiam naturae rectae aquae. Et alisLS aquas, iuxta quas fluunt , constringunt et superant propter acumen naturae suae; sed ad cibum et potum et ad balnea atque ad lavandum utiles non sunt, quoniam ceteras aquas deprimunt. Sed salsae aquae, quae quasi ad medium septentrionalis plagae oriuntur et effluunt, bonae et utiles sunt tam pecoribus quam hominibus, scilicet ad cibum et ad potum atque ad reliquos usus hominum, quia in temperato aere nec nimis calido nec nimis frigido sunt.
text_translation: Which waters sometimes compress the hydropic plague and check paralysis, if they are drunk by those who are fatigued by these illnesses, because the nature of these waters resists the nature of other waters and even the nature of pure waters. And other waters, which flow near them, constrict and overcome because of the sharpness of their own nature; but they are not useful for food and drink, for baths and for washing, because they depress other waters. But salt waters, which arise as it were in the middle of the northern regions and flow out, are good and useful both for cattle and for humans, namely for food and for drink and for other uses of humans, because they are in a temperate air, neither too hot nor too cold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et etiam in potu sumptae hominem interius a malis humoribus purgant. Flumina autem non salsa et viventes et salientes et non salsi fontes , qui etiam quasi ad medium eiusdem septentrionalis plagae oriuntur et effluunt, puri suDt et cristallinum colorem habent ferreo colore interCAUSAE ET LIB. I. mixto. Ac valde Mgidi sunt et yalde utiles sunt, quia aquae istae non sunt immundae nec foetidae nec venenosae, quod diversa yicissitudo solis se illis non infigit.
text_translation: And even those taken in drink purge man internally from bad humors. Rivers, however, not salty, and living and flowing, and not salty springs, which also arise almost in the middle of the same northern region and flow out, are pure and have a crystalline color with a mixed color of iron, among causes and Book I. And they are very great and very useful, because these waters are not unclean nor foul nor venomous, because the diverse vicissitude of the sun's influence does not imprint upon them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et etiam rectum saporem aquae habent et hominibus ac ceteris animalibus utiles sunt ac bonae sunt ad cibum, ad potum, ad balnea et ad layandum ac etiam utiles sunt ad quasdam medicinas. Sed aquae salsae tam fontium quam fluminum, quae ab angulo septentrionali parte occidentali yeniunt, aliquantum subnigrae, id est brunvarOj et non multum purae sunt et nec ad cibum nec ad potum utiles sunt, lO qnia mortiferae sunt. Nam in septentrionali plaga iuxta occidentem magni et acerrimi yermes iacent, qui ab homine nec yideri nec tangi possent, nisi morerentur.
text_translation: And even they have a right taste of water and are useful to humans and other animals and are good for food, for drink, for baths and for washing and also are useful for certain medicines. But salt waters, both of springs and rivers, which come from the angle of the northern part of the western side, are somewhat dark, that is, brunvaria, and are not very pure and are neither useful for food nor for drink, for they are deadly. For in the northern region near the west lie great and very sharp worms, which from a human cannot be seen nor touched, unless they died.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hi in se de aquis istis ibi colligunt et iterum de se exspuunt, quod pastus eorum est. Et ideo aquae istae peri- culosae sunt, quia ibi et calorem et frigus habent. Sed etiam idcirco yermes isti illic habitant, quia frigidi sunt et frigus pati possunt et nullum calorem. Quae yero salsae non sunt et ab eadem septentrionali parte iuxta occidentem yeniunt, purae sunt et subalbi caloris, id est grizvarOj el inutiles sunt tam pecoribus quam hominibus, scilicet ad cibum, ad potum et ad ceteros usus eorum, quia yix digeri possunt et quod homines ab eis facile intumescunt, quia yenenosae sunt et yiscera hominis ulceribus incidunt, Nam infirmi homines ex eis multum debilitantur, sani autem eas yix digerere yalent.
text_translation: And these [waters] collect in themselves from those waters there and again expel from themselves, which is their food. And therefore those waters are dangerous, because there both heat and cold are present. But also for this reason worms dwell there, because they are cold and can endure cold and no heat. Those however which are not salty and approach from the same northern part near the west are pure and subalbida of heat, that is grizvarOj and useless both for cattle and for men, namely for food, for drink and for other uses of theirs, because they cannot be digested and because men easily swell from them, because they are venomous and the viscera of men are incited to ulcers. For weak men are greatly weakened from them, but healthy men are able to digest them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In hac autem eadem septentrionali plaga natura aquarum diyersior et multiplicior est quam natura aquarum in orientali aut in australi aut in occidentali plaga, quoniam aquae in plagis istis calore solis perfnnduntur et temperantur, quod in septentrionali plaga non fit, quod sol ad partes illas non accedit. Aquae yero paludmn, in quibuscumque partibus terrarum sint, totae quasi yenenum sunt, quoniam indignas et nociyas humiditates terrae in se portant et yenenosas spumas yermium.
text_translation: In this very same northern region, the nature of waters is more diverse and manifold than the nature of waters in the eastern, southern, or western region, because waters in those regions are poured and tempered by the heat of the sun, which does not happen in the northern region, since the sun does not reach those parts. Indeed, marsh waters, wherever they may be in the lands, are entirely like poison, because they carry within them unworthy and harmful humidities of the earth and poisonous froth of herbs.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hae pessimae sunt ad potum ac male ad reliquos usus hominum praeter ad layandum, si ad opus necessariae fuerint. Qui antem eas pro necessitate penuriae aliarum aquarum bibere voluerit, ipsas primum coquat et refrigerari permittat et sic bibat; panis autem et cibus atque cerevisia, quae cum eis coquuntur, aliquantum sumi possunt, quia igne purgantur. Putei autem et salientes fontes, qui de paludibus fluunt, aliquantum meliores aliis aquis paludum sxmt, quemadmodum de quibusdam immundis rebus munda fiunt.
text_translation: And these are most evil for drinking and badly suited for other human uses except for washing, if they are necessary for use. Whoever, however, wishes to drink them out of necessity due to the scarcity of other waters, let him first boil them and allow them to cool, and then drink them thus; bread, however, and food and beer, which are cooked with them, can be consumed to some extent, because they are purified by fire. Wells, however, and springs that flow from marshes are somewhat better than other marsh waters, just as some unclean things become clean.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hi aliquantum pati possunt, sed tamen non multum boni nec multum utiles sunt. Sed omnes aquae, quae in ortu suo orientes nocivae sunt, quanto longius ab ortu suo fiuunt, tanto saniores fiunt, quia multo et longo cursa ea, quae in se nociva et venenosa sunt, perdunt et ab eis longo et diutumo meatu excreantur et purificantur. Aqua autem puteorum, qui in terram altius fossi sunt, ita quod stat et meatum effiuendi non babet, melior et suavior est ad cibum, ad potum atque ad reliquos usus hominum quam aqua salientium fontium, qui effiuunt.
text_translation: And these can suffer to some extent, but nevertheless are not very good nor very useful. But all waters, which at their source are harmful, the longer they are from their source, the healthier they become, because through long and extended flow, those things which are harmful and venomous within them are lost, and by their long and enduring passage they are excreted and purified. But the water of wells, which are dug deeper into the earth, so that they stand and have no passage for flowing out, is better and sweeter for food, for drink and for other uses of humans than the water of springing fountains, which flow out.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et est ut suave unguentum ad comparationem salientis et effiuentis fontis, quoniam, dum non efQuit, saepius suavi temperie aeris temperatur. Aqua enim salientium fontium aspera est et asperitate sua cibis resistit, ita quod per ignem vix molliri possunt nec facile coqui. Et quia purgata est et pura, paucas spumas babet, et ideo etiam tanto minus cibos purgat et tanto minus cibi per eam purgantur, sicut per alias aquas cibi solent purgari, qui etiam per coctionem earundem aquarum purgantur.
text_translation: And it is like a sweet ointment compared to the springing and flowing fountain, because, while it does not effuse, more often the sweet temper of the air tempers it. For the water of springing fountains is harsh and by its harshness resists food, so that they can scarcely be softened by fire and are not easily cooked. And because it is purified and pure, it has few froth, and therefore also much less purges foods and much less are foods purified by it, as by other waters foods are usually purified, which are also purified by the cooking of those same waters.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam aqua, quae de salientibus fontibus fiuit, gracilior et purior est aquis fluminum, quod aut terra aut arena aut lapidibus, ubi salit et effluity purgatur et pura fit. Et ad bibendum homini prodest, quia emundata est, et etiam aspera est ac ali quantum vino associata in viribus suis, sed cibo obest ad comedendum et oculis ad lavandum pro asperitate sua. Aquae autem fiuminum, quae super terram fluunt, spissae, quia sole et aere perfunduntur, et aliquantum spumosae sunt et non sanae ad bibendum, quoniam diversa qualitas aeris et elementorum eis commiscetur, et quia etiam fumo, id est daume, ac nebulis, quae de quibusdam inflrmis montibus descendunt, inficiuntur, et etiam interdum aere.
text_translation: Nam water, which from springing fountains was, more delicate and purer is than waters of rivers, because either earth or sand or stones, where it springs and effluvia purges and becomes pure. And to drinking for man is beneficial, because it is cleansed, and also harsh and as much as wine is associated with its strength, but to food is harmful for eating and to eyes for washing because of its harshness. Waters however of rivers, which over earth flow, thick, because by sun and air are poured, and somewhat foamy are and not healthy for drinking, because diverse quality of air and elements with them are mixed, and because also smoke, that is daume, and clouds, which from certain weak mountains descend, are tainted, and also sometimes by air.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: qai aliqnando qaibusdam poenis tangitur; et ideo malae si^nt ad potandum et homines laedont, nisi primum coquuntur, ut a mala spuma purgentur et infrigidari permittantur, ut ita pro necessitate bibantur. Sed etiam pro necessitate ad escas coquendas aliquantum valent, quoniam ibi per coctionem purgantur et tunc inde suayiores fiunt et melius saporant. Nam interdum evenit, quod malus fumus ac periculosa nebula de quibusdam infirmis montibus descendunt et quod etiam aer aliquando quibusdam poenis tangitur, et ita elementa inficiuntur et lo sic aquis fluviorum ac puteorum ac fontium commiscentur.
text_translation: When certain things are touched by some punishments; and therefore, bad things are harmful to drink and injure men, unless they are first cooked, so that they may be purified from bad foam and allowed to become cold, so that thus they may be drunk by necessity. But also, by necessity, they are somewhat useful for cooking food, because there they are purified by cooking and then become sweeter and taste better. For sometimes it happens that bad smoke and dangerous mist descend from certain weak mountains, and also air is sometimes touched by certain punishments, and thus the elements are corrupted, and thus they are mixed with waters of rivers, wells, and springs.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: XJnde iterum pessimum venenum quasi mortifera pestis in eis est. Et si homines aut cetera animalia aquas istas tunc bibunt, mortem eis inferunt aut exstantia membra in deformitate, aut quod debilitatem in eis faciunt. Unde necesse est, ne tunc bibantur, nisi coctae sint, quoniam per spumam coctionis venenum de eis eiciunt. Nam si necessitas aliquem hominem coegerit, ut eas bibat, quoniam alias aliquas ad bibendum non habet, eas primum coquat et refrigerari permittat et sic bibat, quia rarum est, quin in eis aliqua pericula sint aut de aere aut de volatilibus, quae in eis lavantur, aut de mortifero malo, quod in cadaveribus mortuorum est, quae in se habent.
text_translation: Unde iterum pessimum venenum quasi mortifera pestis in eis est. Et si homines aut cetera animalia aquas istas tunc bibunt, mortem eis inferunt aut exstantia membra in deformitate, aut quod debilitatem in eis faciunt. Unde necesse est, ne tunc bibantur, nisi coctae sint, quoniam per spumam coctionis venenum de eis eiciunt. Nam si necessitas aliquem hominem coegerit, ut eas bibat, quoniam alias aliquas ad bibendum non habet, eas primum coquat et refrigerari permittat et sic bibat, quia rarum est, quin in eis aliqua pericula sint aut de aere aut de volatilibus, quae in eis lavantur, aut de mortifero malo, quod in cadaveribus mortuorum est, quae in se habent.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ideo necesse est, ne in usus hominum sumantur, nisi prius coquantur. Ubi autem parvi et lucidi et puri rivuli sunt, qui de aliis aquis quasi quaedam venae procedunt, isti per emanationem suam purgantur et boni ac utiles sunt ad quoslibet usus hominum et pecorum. Aquae autem pluviarum asperae sunt et ab inflrmis hominibus foetores et malos humores et putrida auferunt, sed sanos aliquantum laedunt, quod in eis non inveniunt, quod purg^nt. Et hae, cum in cistemis steterint, leniores fiunt et sanis et infirmis hominibus bonae sunt.
text_translation: And therefore, it is necessary that they should not be used for human purposes, unless they are first cooked. Where, however, small and clear and pure streams are, which from other waters as if certain veins proceed, these are purified by their own emanation and are good and useful for any use of humans and livestock. Rain waters, however, are harsh and remove from weak humans offensive odors and bad humors and putrid matter, but they somewhat harm the healthy, because they do not find in them what purges. And these, when they have stood in cisterns, become milder and are good for both healthy and weak humans.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen aqua salientium et fiuentium fontium multo meliores sunt. Sed et cum sol calorem suum ad se trahit, aquae maximum frigus habent et spumam suam, scilicet nivem, emittunt, quae .terram operit et viriditatem terrae impinguat ac eam HILDE6ARDIS retinet et frnges terrae non laedit. Aqua vero eius inutilis ad usus hominum est et etiam levis est et sordida; et si quis eam bibit, ulcera et scabies in eo saepissime crescunt, ac yiscera eius livore implentur.
text_translation: But indeed the water of springing and flowing fountains is much better. But also when the sun draws its heat to itself, the waters have the greatest cold and emit their foam, namely snow, which covers the earth and makes the earth's greenery fertile and retains it for Hildegard and does not harm the earth's fruits. But that water is indeed useless for human use and also light and dirty; and if someone drinks it, ulcers and scabies often grow in him, and his viscera are filled with lividness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aquae enim pluyiarum potatae livorem stomacbi acumine suo auferunt, sed viscera exulcerant. Sed aquae nivium potatae sitim ad sufficientiam non extinguunt, quod leves sunt et cito digeruntur. Aquae autem, quas scissae nubes in nimia effusione repente emittunt, et grando ita periculosae sunt, quemadmodum fer ventes aquae, in quibus setae porcorum et crines aliorum: pecorum auferuntur, si quis eas in cibo aut in potu sumeret. Nam si aliquis aquas scindentium nubium et aquas grandinis biberet, diu languesceret, et caro eius per scissuras rumperetur, ita quod etiam multi ex hoc morerentur.
text_translation: For indeed, the waters of rains, when drunk, remove the darkness of the stomach by their own sharpness, but they ulcerate the viscera. However, the waters of snow, when drunk, do not quench thirst sufficiently, because they are light and quickly digested. However, the waters which riven clouds suddenly pour forth in excessive effusion, and hail, are so dangerous, as the winds carry waters in which the hairs of pigs and the hairs of other livestock are removed, if anyone were to take them in food or drink. For if anyone were to drink the waters of raining clouds and the waters of hail, he would languish for a long time, and his flesh would be broken through cracks, so that even many would die from this.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De terrarum viribus. Terra autem naturaliter est frigida et septem vires babet, ita tamen, quod particulata in aestate frigida est, in hieme calida est, et quod viriditatem et ariditatem in se habet, et quod germina producit et animalia sustinet, et quod omnia portat; sicut et deus in sex diebus operatus est et in septima requievit, cum etiam omnia, quae creaverat, utilitati hominis subiecit. Terra namque in aestate subterius frigida est, quoniam soi per vires radiorum suorum tunc gignit, in hieme autem subterius calida, alioquin de frigida ariditate scin deretur; et sic in calore viriditatem, in frigiditate ariditatem ostendit.
text_translation: On the Powers of the Earth. The earth, however, naturally is cold and has seven powers, such that in summer it is cold in its parts, in winter warm in its parts, and that it has greenness and dryness within itself, and that it produces germs and sustains animals, and that it carries all things; just as God worked in six days and rested on the seventh, having also subjected all things that he created to the utility of man. For the earth indeed in summer is cold beneath, because the sun then generates heat through its rays, in winter however is warm beneath, otherwise it would be scorched by cold dryness; and thus it shows greenness in heat, dryness in cold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in hieme sol supra terram sterilis est et sub terram calorem suum figit, quatinus terra diversa germina servare possit, et sic illa per calorem et frigiditatem cuncta germina profert; sed et euntia et currentia animalia sustinet, ne in ipsa demergantur, quia et de calore et de frigiditate indurata est et sic omnia potenter portat. Deus enim sic terram posuit, ut congruenti tempore germinaret et congruenti quoque tempore in germine deficeret, quemadmodum etiam luna crescit et decrescit.
text_translation: For in winter the sun above the earth is barren and embeds its heat beneath the earth, so that the earth may preserve diverse seeds, and thus it produces all seeds through heat and cold; but also it sustains moving and flowing animals, so that they do not sink into it, because it is hardened from both heat and cold and thus powerfully bears all things. For God has placed the earth thus, so that it may germinate at the appropriate time and also cease germinating at the appropriate time, just as the moon waxes and wanes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De arhorum germine, frumenti et vini. Arbores etiam, quae in orientali plaga sunt et de orientalibus aquis rigantur, bene crescunt et bonum fructum in diversis frnctibus pomomm profernnt, quae bonum saporem habent. Sed durare diu non possunt. Frumentum autem ibi minutum est et ad profectum non bene tendit, quod terra ibi aliqnantum humida est; quod terra, quae habundantiam frumenti gignit, aliquantum arida esse debet, quod magnum fri gus frumentum plus laedit quam calor, quoniam frumentum aliquantum siccum est.
text_translation: On the germ of trees, grain, and wine. Trees also, which are in the eastern region and are watered by eastern waters, grow well and produce good fruit in various fruits of trees, which have a good taste. But they cannot last long. Grain, however, there is small and does not tend well to prosperity, because the soil there is somewhat moist; the soil which produces an abundance of grain should be somewhat dry, because great cold damages grain more than heat, since grain is somewhat dry.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Yineae autem ibi in oriente ad multum profectum vini proficiunt atque valde bonum vinum proferunt. Herbae autem hortorum et aliae herbae, quae in eadem orientali plaga de aquis et iuzta aquas rigantur et crescunt, quae ab oriente fluunt, virtuosae sunt et bonum odorem habent et ad medicinam yalent ac bonae sunt ad cibos; et vermes raro in eis crescunt et raro eas comedunt, quod de calore et frigore bene temperatae sunt, quod vermes fugiunt, quoniam ipsi foetida humiditas sunt, ut eruca et similes, qui de spuma aeris crescunt.
text_translation: Vineae autem ibi in oriente ad multum profectum vini proficiunt atque valde bonum vinum proferunt. Herbae autem hortorum et aliae herbae, quae in eadem orientali plaga de aquis et iuxta aquas rigantur et crescunt, quae ab oriente fluunt, virtuosae sunt et bonum odorem habent et ad medicinam valent ac bonae sunt ad cibos; et vermes raro in eis crescunt et raro eas comedunt, quod de calore et frigore bene temperatae sunt, quod vermes fugiunt, quoniam ipsi foetida humiditas sunt, ut eruca et similes, qui de spuma aeris crescunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et arbores, quae in occidentali plaga sunt et de occidentalibus aquis rigantur, bene crescunt; sed fructus diversorum pomorum, qui iuxta terram crescit et ab humiditate terrae tangitur, aliquantum nocivus est, ille autem fructus, qui in alto arborum est, ita nocivus non est, quod altitudine aeris tactus est, et bonum gustum habet et saturari multum non obest atque diu durare potest. Frumentum vero in occidentali plaga forte est, sed pingue non est, et vinum forte est, sed deliciosum non est, et durare potest, quod terra ibi calorem et frigus habet.
text_translation: But also the trees which are in the western region and are watered by western waters grow well; but the fruits of various kinds of trees which grow next to the earth and are touched by the earth's humidity are somewhat harmful, whereas that fruit which is in the height of the trees is not so harmful, because it is touched by the altitude of the air, and it has a good taste and does not cause harm from over-saturation and can last a long time. Indeed, grain in the western region is strong, but not fatty, and wine is strong, but not delicious, and can last, because the earth there has heat and cold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Herbae vero tam hortorum quam camporum, quae in eadem occidentali parte de occidentalibus aquis tanguntur et irrigantur, acutae ad libidinem sunt et omnem procellam camis, scilicet voluptatis, iracundiae et instabilitatis morum et frequentae vagationis excitant, ita quod homines, qui eas gustaverint, interdum laeti, interdum tristes et interdum veloces sunt. Et succus earundum herbarum et ipsae herbae incremento procedunt et proficiunt, quod calens calor et cadens frigus ibi cito non deficit; et ideo fortes in viriditate sunt, sed nocivae ad praefatam inutilitatem.
text_translation: The herbs indeed, both of gardens and fields, which are touched and watered by the same western waters, are sharp towards lust and excite every storm of the flesh, namely of lust, wrath, instability of manners, and frequent wandering, so that men who have tasted them are sometimes joyful, sometimes sad, and sometimes swift. And the juice of these herbs and the herbs themselves increase and progress, because the hot heat and falling cold there quickly do not fail; and therefore they are strong in greenness, but harmful towards the aforementioned uselessness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et (ad magicam artem et ad cetera fantasmata valent, sed non multam sanitatem corporibus hominum conferunt, quod dies ibi inclinatur et nox surgit; quoniam cnm rex in honore suo stetit, malitia ibi vociferabatur et summmn regem tenebris obscurare voluit; sed ipsa et acies sua torpescebat. Arbores autem, qui in australi plaga de aquis illis, quae ibi efflnunt, rigantur, bene proflciunt et plurima pomaria proferunt, quae etiam durare possunt, quod calore perfusa sunt.
text_translation: But also (they are strong toward magical art and toward other phantasmata, but they do not confer much health upon the bodies of men, because there the day inclines and the night arises; since when the king stood in his honor, malice there cried out and the supreme king wished to obscure with darkness; but its own acies became torpid. Trees, however, which in the southern region are watered by those waters which flow there, flourish well and produce many orchards, which can also endure, because they are filled with heat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et frumentum ibi habundat et pingue et deliciosum est atque vinum ibi habundat et deliciosum est et multam yim habet nec facile perit, sed durare potest, quod multo calore in partibus illis temperatum est; quoniam yinum de calore plus quam de frigore crescit et frigus yinum plus laedit quam calor. Sed herbae hortorum et camporum, quae in eisdem partibus de ipsis aquis irrigantur, quae de austro fluunt, fragiles ac debiles et liyidae sunt ac facile pereunt, quoniam recta humiditas aeris ipsis abstracta est.
text_translation: And grain is abundant there and rich and delicious, and wine is abundant there and delicious, and it has much wine and does not perish easily, but can endure, because in those parts it is tempered by much heat; for wine grows more from heat than from cold, and cold harms wine more than heat does. But the herbs of gardens and fields, which are irrigated by those waters flowing from the south, are fragile and weak and liquid and perish easily, because the proper humidity of the air is abstracted from them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nec multum utiles sunt ad cibum nec ad potum; sed et ad medicinam parum yalent, quod facile arescunt; nec etiam multum pecoribus valent, quoniam tam homines quam pecora ex eis parum proflciunt. Arbores yero, quae in septentrionali plaga de aquis illis irrigantur, quae ibi oriuntur, facile pereunt, et fructus earum in pomis et in ceteris similibus non bene proflcit, quod frigus eos laedit. Sed efc frumentum ibi multo labore acquiritur , et ideo minutum ac lolio et zizania permixtum est, sed tamen aliquantum forte est.
text_translation: Nor are they very useful for food or drink; nor do they contribute much to medicine, because they easily rot; nor are they of much benefit to livestock, since both men and livestock derive little profit from them. Trees, however, which are irrigated by those waters in the northern region, which arise there, easily perish, and their fruits in apples and other similar produce do not profit well, because the cold harms them. But grain is acquired there with much labor, and thus it is ground and mixed with oil and millet, but nevertheless it is somewhat strong.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Vinum quoque modice ibi crescit, et ideo acutum et aliquantum acerbum et non multum dulce, quod sol illud ibi non temperat. Herbae quoque tam hortorum quam camporum, qui de aquis illis septentrionalibus rigantur, pingues non sunt nec multum ad medicinam yalent. Sed sanos non laedunt, inflrmis autem obsunt, quoniam nec de recto calore nec de recto calore nec de recta humiditate crescunt sed de frigore, et ideo non multum proflciunt nec multum fertiles sunt.
text_translation: Wine also grows there in moderation, and therefore is sharp and somewhat sour and not very sweet, because that sun there does not temper it. Herbs also, both of gardens and fields, which are watered by those northern waters, are not fat nor very useful for medicine. But they do not harm the healthy, but harm the weak, because they do not grow from proper heat nor from proper heat nor from proper moisture but from cold, and therefore are not very prolific nor very fertile.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pluvia, Pluyia, quae repente et magna effusione super terram cadit, noxia est et terram et fructus terrae laedit, quia quosdam livores in se habet; moderata autem utilis est et terram irrigat et eam fructus producere facit, quoniam suavis est et ad utilitatem et fertilitatem pura ac munda est. n. De Adae casu. Deus ita creavit hominem, quod omnia animalia ad servitutem eius subiecta sunt; sed cum homo praeceptum dei transgressus est, mutatus est etiam tam corpore quam mente.
text_translation: On rain, Rain, which falls suddenly and in great abundance upon the earth, is harmful and damages both the earth and the fruits of the earth, because it contains certain discolorations; but moderate rain is useful and irrigates the earth and causes it to produce fruits, because it is sweet and pure and clean for utility and fertility. On the Fall of Adam. God created man in such a way that all animals are subject to his service; but when man transgressed God's command, he was changed both in body and in mind.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam puritas scmguinis eius in alium modum yersa est, ita quod pro puritate spumam seminis eicit. Si enim homo in paradiso mansisset, in immutabili et perfecto statu perstitisset. Sed haec omnia post transgressionem in alium et amarum modum versa sunt. De spermate. Nam sanguis hominis in ardore et calore libidinis fervens spumam de se eicit, quod nos semen dicimus, velut olla aliqua ad ignem posita spumam de fervore ignis de aqua emittit. De conceptu, Et cum aUquis homo de semine infirmi hominis concipitur vel de semine illo, quod tenue et incoctum est, sed aliqua tabe et putredine permixtum: hic saepissime in yita «sua etiam infirmus erit et velut carie plenus, velut aliquod lignum, quod vermibus perforatum cariem eicit.
text_translation: Nam puritas sanguinis eius in alium modum versa est, ita quod pro puritate spumam seminis eicit. Si enim homo in paradiso mansisset, in immutabili et perfecto statu perstitisset. Sed haec omnia post transgressionem in alium et amarum modum versa sunt. De spermate. Nam sanguis hominis in ardore et calore libidinis fervens spumam de se eicit, quod nos semen dicimus, velut olla aliqua ad ignem posita spumam de fervore ignis de aqua emittit. De conceptu. Et cum aliquis homo de semine infirmi hominis concipitur vel de semine illo, quod tenue et incoctum est, sed aliqua tabe et putredine permixtum: hic saepissime in vita sua etiam infirmus erit et velut carie plenus, velut aliquod lignum, quod vermibus perforatum cariem eicit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde iste homo ulceribus et putredine saepe repletur ac facilius tabem et putredinem escarum sibi attrahit ad putredinem illam, quam habet. Sed homo, qui his caret, sanior est. Si autem superfiuitas in semine est, tunc homo ille, qui ex hoc concipitur, incontinens, immoderatus et fragilis ac superfluus erit. Quare homo hirsutm non est. Quod autem hirsutus non est, hoc de rationalitate est, quia rationalitas et pro pilis et pro pennis est, per quam se tegit et yolat, quo voluerit.
text_translation: Wherefore this man is often filled with ulcers and putrefaction, and more easily attracts consumption and the rot of scabs to that putrefaction which he has. But the man who lacks these is healthier. If, however, superfluity is in the semen, then the man who is conceived from this will be incontinent, immoderate, fragile, and excessive. Wherefore the man is not hairy. That he is not hairy, this is from rationality, for rationality is both for hair and for feathers, by which he covers himself and flies wherever he wills.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed quod masculus barbam habet et plures crines in corpore suo quam mulier, hoc est ideo, quia masculus de terra formatus est et quod maiorem fortitudinem et calorem habet et plus ubique versatur quam mulier; quemadmodum terra imbre et calore solis perfusa Hildegardis cansae et cnrae ed. Kaiser. herbas et gramina producit et super se animalia birsuta et pemiata nutrit. Mulier autem sine barba est, quia de carne viri formata est et viro subiacet et in maiori quiete est; sicut etiam reptilia illa, quae de terra nascuntur, hirsuta non sunt| sed in terra iacent et minus imbrem et solem quam cetera animalia.
text_translation: But that the male has a beard and more hair in his body than the female, this is because the male was formed from the earth and has greater strength and heat and moves more than the female; just as the earth, flooded with rain and the heat of the sun, produces Hildegardis cansae et carnuae ed. Kaiser. herbs and grasses and nourishes on itself hairy and pilose animals. The female, however, is without a beard, because she was formed from the flesh of the man and submits to the man and is in greater rest; just as also those reptiles which are born from the earth are not hairy, but lie on the earth and receive less rain and sun than other animals.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: quae super terram sunt, sentinnt. De reptUibus, Sed et sicut animalia ad servitntem hominis creata sunt et adiutorium illi subministrant et adiutorium praebent, cum eam perforant, ita quod aqna et pluvia eam irrigat. Unde et ipsi in bumectis locis in terra semper iacent et cum eam flatu suo calefaciunt et spuma ac sudore suo humectant, sic quod de huiusmodi ezicibus et flatibus aliquantulum roboratur et continetnr. Quod vero in eo vermes venenosi sunt, hoc est de foeditate et putredine terrae.
text_translation: What is above the earth is sentient. Concerning reptiles, indeed, just as animals were created for the service of man and provide assistance to him, and offer aid by perforating it, so that water and rain irrigate it. Hence, they themselves always lie in moist places in the earth, and when they warm it with their breath and moisten it with their foam and sweat, thus such that from these kinds of exhalations and breaths it is somewhat strengthened and contained. What, however, in it are venomous worms, this is from the filth and decay of the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam pluvia et ros terram in superficie sua lavant, et sol eam calefacit; unde superius munda est et mundos fructus affert. Sordes autem et putredo ad interiora eius fluunt, et inde venenosi vermes in ipsa nascuntur, velut etiam vermes a sanie hominis in ipso crescunt, qui eum laedunt. Sic etiam vermes in terra nascuntur, qui et de se ipsa nutriuntur. Sed hi vermes fere ossibus carent, sed venenum ipsis quasi pro ossibus et pro sanguine est et eos confortat.
text_translation: For rain and dew wash the earth on its surface, and the sun warms it; therefore, the upper part is clean and brings forth clean fruits. But dirt and decay flow into its interior, and from there venomous worms are born in it, just as also worms from human filth grow in it, which harm it. Thus also worms are born in the earth, which feed on themselves. But these worms lack bones, but venom is to them as bones and as blood and comforts them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quidam ex eis hirsuti non sunt, quia de humore terrae nascuntur et in terra sunt et superiora fugiunt, ita quod nec aere nec rore caeli nec calore solis perfunduntur, de quibus cetera animalia perfusa crines habent. Et quoniam homini et superioribus animalibus contrariam naturam habent, eis etiam inimicantur et veneno suo occidunt et homines ac superiora illis laedunt. Sed quamvis venenum in se habeant, aliqui tamen tam ad medicamentum hominum quam animalium valent, et si non ex toto, aliqua pars tamen corporis eorum, quod habent de bono succo terrae, quia et bonus succus terrae bonas herbas profert, ut serpens a cervo devoratus, per quem ille iuvenescit.
text_translation: And some of them are not hairy, because they arise from the humor of the earth and are in the earth and flee from superior things, so that they are not flooded by air or dew of the sky or heat of the sun, by which other animals, flooded, have hair. And because they have a nature contrary to that of man and superior animals, they are enemies to them and kill them with their venom, and men and superior things harm them. But although they have venom within them, some are nonetheless useful for the medicine of men and animals, and if not entirely, at least some part of their body, which has the good juice of the earth, because the good juice of the earth produces good herbs, as the serpent devoured by the deer, by whom that one rejuvenates.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De volatUibtis. Volucres autem et animalia et bestiae, quae in usus hominum venire possunt et sunt, vitam de aere secandum dispositionem dei sumunt, nnde snper terram irersantnr. Vermes vero et reptilia vitam sumunt de succo terrae et ideo in terra et subtus terram libenter sunt. De piscibus, Pisces autem vitam accipiunt de aquoso aere fluminum , unde etiam in aquis sunt et siccitatem pati non possunt. Et haec omnia cum moriuntur, yita eorum defluit in came ipsorum velut nix in calore, et quod residuum fuerit aut in aerem aut in succum terrae aut in aquosum aerem fluminum transit, unde venit.
text_translation: Of birds. However, birds and animals and beasts, which can come and are for the use of humans, take life from the air according to the disposition of God, from which they go above the earth. Worms indeed and reptiles take life from the juice of the earth and therefore gladly are in the earth and beneath the earth. Of fish, Fish however take life from the watery air of rivers, from which they are also in waters and cannot endure dryness. And all these when they die, their life flows into their flesh like snow in heat, and whatever residue will be either into air or into the juice of the earth or into the watery air of rivers passes, from which they come.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nec amodo illud, quod sic evanuit, aliquod animal ad vitam lO Yiyificat, quod aruit. Nam ut succus et viredo arborum atque herbarum ipsis abscisis in eis arescit et exsiccatur nec amplius alias herbas ad yiridinem exsuscitat, quoniam exaruit: sic etiam et vita bmtorDm animalium, postquam in eis amerit et exsiccata fuerit, nullum animal amplius yiyificat, quoniam iam non est sed ex toto evanuit. De conceptus diversitate. Nunc autem cum vir in efiPusione fortis seminis sui et in recto amore caritatis, quam ad mulierem habet, ad ipsam accedit muliere quoque rectum amorem ad vimm txmc in eadem hora habente, masculus concipitur, quia sic a deo ordinatum est.
text_translation: Nor does that which thus faded give life to any animal that has withered. For just as the sap and greenness of trees and herbs, when severed from them, wither and dry up in them and no longer revive other herbs to greenness, because they have dried up: so also the life of animals, after it has withered and dried up in them, no longer gives life to any animal, because it is no longer present but has entirely faded. On the diversity of conception. Now, however, when a man, in the emission of his strong semen and in the right love of charity, which he has toward the woman, approaches the woman, who also has a right love toward the man at the same hour, a male is conceived, because thus it is ordered by God.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nec aliter fieri potest, quin masculus concipiatur, quoniam et Adam formatus est de limo, qui fortior materia est quam caro. Et hic masculus prudens et virtuosus erit, quia sic in forti semine ac in recto amore caritatis utrommque, 2& quem ad invicem habent, conceptus est. Si autem hic amor deest in muliere ad vimm, ita quod tantum vir rectum amorem caritatis in ipsa hora ad mulierem habet et non mulier ad yimm, et si semen yiri forte est: masculus tamen concipitur, quod amor caritatis viri super« sa excellit.
text_translation: It cannot be otherwise than that a male is conceived, because even Adam was formed from clay, which is a stronger material than flesh. And this male will be prudent and virtuous, because thus in strong semen and in the right love of charity, which they have towards each other, he is conceived. If, however, this love is lacking in the woman towards the man, so that only the man has the right love of charity towards the woman in that hour and not the woman towards the man, and if the man's semen is strong: nevertheless, a male is conceived, because the love of charity of the man exceeds.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed idem masculus debilis erit et non virtuosus, quoniam hic amor in muliere ad yimm defuit. Quoniam si semen yiri tenue est, qui tamen amorem caritatis ad mulierem habet, et ipsa eundem amorem ad illura, ibi femina yirtuosa procreatur. Si yero amor caritatis yiri est ad mulierem et non mulieris ad virum, vel si amor caritatis in muliere est ad virum et non viri ad mulierem, 3» Se semen antem viri in ipsa hora tenue: item femina ex hoc nascitur propter debilitatem seminis.
text_translation: But the same male will be weak and not virtuous, because this love in the woman towards the humors is lacking. Because if the man's semen is thin, yet he has the love of charity towards the woman, and she also has the same love towards him, there a virtuous woman is born. If truly the love of charity of the man is towards the woman and not the woman's towards the man, or if the love of charity in the woman is towards the man and not the man's towards the woman, then if the man's semen in that hour is thin: likewise a woman is born from this because of the weakness of the semen.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si semen viri forte est, sed tamen nec vir ad mulierem nec mnlier ad vimm amorem caritatis habet, masculns inde procreatur, qnod tamen semen forte fait, sed amarus ex amaritudine parentnm; vel si semen viri tenne est et nenter ad altemm amorem caritatis in ipsa hora tenet, femina nascitur amarae complexionis. Calor antem mnliemm, qnae pingaem natnram habent, semen viri snperat, ita qnod infans se cnndum similitudinem earnm multotiens in facie formatur.
