text_translation: But if the man's semen is strong, yet neither the man toward the woman nor the woman toward the man has love of charity, a male is generated from it, which however is strong, but bitter from the bitterness of the parents; or if the man's semen is held and neither toward the other love of charity holds in that hour, a woman is born of bitter complexion. Heat however, which women, who have fatty nature, overcomes the man's semen, so that the child is formed according to the likeness of theirs often in the face.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed mnlieres, qnae macres in natura sna sunt, infantem ad similitudinem patris in facie multotiens generant. De infirmitatibus. Quod antem qnidam homines diversas infirmitates patiuntur, hoc de flegmate est, qnod in ipsis habundat. Si enim homo in paradiso permansisset, flegmata in corpore suo non haberet, nnde multa mala procedunt, sed caro eius integra esset et absque livore. Quoniam autem malo consensit et bonnm reliqnit, similis factns est terrae, quae et bonas et utiles ac malas et inntiles herbas gignit, et quae bonam et malam humiditatem et succnm in se habet.
text_translation: But women, who are naturally lean, often give birth to infants resembling the father in face. On infirmities. As for certain men who suffer various infirmities, this is due to phlegm, which abounds in them. For if man had remained in paradise, he would not have phlegm in his body, from which many evils proceed, but his flesh would be intact and without lividity. Because, however, he consented to evil and left good behind, he became like the earth, which produces both good and useful and bad and useless herbs, and which contains both good and bad humidity and sap.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam de gustu mali versus est sangnis filiomm Adae in venenum seminis, de quo filii hominnm procreantnr. Et ideo caro eomm, nlcerata et perforata est. Qnae nlcera et foramina qnandam tempestatem et hnmectatem fnmi in hominibns faciunt, de qno flegmata orinntnr et coagulantnr, quae diversas inflrmitates corporibus hominnm infemnt. Haec de primo malo illo, qnod homo primnm incepit, orta sunt, qnoniam, si Adam in paradiso permansisset, dnlcissimam sanitatem dO optimae mansionis haberet, velnt fortissimnm balsamum optimnm odorem emittit; sed e contra nnnc homo habet in se venennm et flegma ac diversas infirmitates.
text_translation: For from the taste of evil, the poison of the sons of Adam in the venom of semen, from which the sons of men are generated. And therefore the body is ulcerated and perforated. Which ulcers and openings cause some storm and moisture in men, from which phlegm rises and coagulates, which pour various infirmities into the bodies of men. These arose from that first evil, which man first began, since, if Adam had remained in paradise, he would have had the sweetest health of the best dwelling, would emit the strongest balm with the best scent; but on the contrary, now man has in himself poison and phlegm and various infirmities.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De cofUinenHa, Sed qnidam homines snnt, qni continentes snnt, si volnnt, si antem continentes esse nolont, potentes snnt in volnntate sna. Et avari snnt et inabstinentes in pingnedine cibomm. Unde coagulatnr in eis pericnlosnm et venenosnm ac spissnm et siccnm fiegma. quod non est hnmidnm sed amarnm, et quod cames grossas et nigras atqne infirmas facit in hominibns pnllnlare; et si isti a comestione pinguium ciborum se abstinere noluerint, facile sibi lepram attrahunt.
text_translation: De coitu. Sed quidam homines sunt, qui continentes sunt, si volunt, si autem continentes esse nolunt, potentes sunt in voluntate sua. Et avari sunt et inabstinentes in pinguedine ciborum. Unde coagulatur in eis periculosum et venenum ac spissum et siccum flegma, quod non est humidum sed amarum, et quod carnem grossam et nigram atque infirmam facit in hominibus pullulare; et si isti a comestione pinguium ciborum se abstinere noluerint, facile sibi lepram attrahunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et amaritudo einsdem flegmatis facit fumum quasi fiimum melancoliae circa iecur et pulmonem eomm, et inde sunt iracundi et immites, atque humiditas sudoris eorum non est sobria sed sordida. Et isti non multum debiles sunt, sed probi sunt et audaces atque tyrannidem et rapacitatem in ira sua habent ex complexione ista. Flegma autem complexionis buius quosdam eorum cito conterit et ocddit, quia vis eius fortis est; quosdam autem aliquamdiu viyere permittit.
text_translation: But also the bitterness of the same phlegm creates smoke as if it were the smoke of melancholy around the liver and lungs and, from there, are irritable and sordid, and the humidity of their sweat is not sober but sordid. And these are not very weak, but are good and bold, and in their anger they have tyranny and greed from this complexion. However, the phlegm of this complexion quickly crushes and kills some of them, because its force is strong; others it allows to live for a while.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De incontinentia. Alii autem homines sunt, qui sunt in superfluitate naturae et magis incontinentes, ita quod se yix possunt abstinere et quod etiam aliquantum infirman- tur. Et hi in humido flegmate superhabundant, quia in eis indignus humor exsurgit et quia hoc nocivum flegma in eis coagulatur, quod malum fumum in pectus et in cerebrum eorum emittit. Et humiditas huius fumigantis flegmatis in pectore facit frigidam humiditatem in stomacho, 2o et humiditas eiusdem flegmatis in cerebro attenuat auditum in auribus, ita quod in stomacho et in auribus est velut inutilis nebula, quae bonas herbas et bonos fructus laedit.
text_translation: On incontinence. Others, however, are men who are in the superfluity of nature and more incontinent, so that they cannot abstain from themselves and are also somewhat weakened. And these are superabundant in humid phlegm, because in them an unworthy humor arises and because this noxious phlegm coagulates in them, which emits a bad smoke into their chest and into their brain. And the humidity of this smoky phlegm in the chest causes cold humidity in the stomach, and the humidity of the same phlegm in the brain weakens hearing in the ears, so that in the stomach and in the ears there is as it were a useless mist, which damages good herbs and good fruits.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc idem flegma non laedit pulmonem, quia puhno etiam humidus est, sed laedit splen, quia illud pingue est et humiditatem renuit, quoniam, si humiditatem haberet, statim deflueret et liquefieret. Atque etiam cor debiUtat, quia cor integram fortitudinem semper habebit et snperhabundantem humiditatem semper recusat. Isti autem de complexione hac mites ac laeti sunt, sed tardi, et qui- dam eorum aliquantulum diu vivunt, quia hoc flegma eos non occidit, nec omnino sanitatem eis dat.
text_translation: And this same phlegm does not harm the lung, because the lung is also moist, but it harms the spleen, because that is fatty and rejects moisture, because, if it had moisture, it would immediately flow and liquefy. And also the heart is weakened, because the heart always has complete strength and always rejects excessive moisture. Those, however, of this complexion are gentle and cheerful, but slow, and some of them live a little while, because this phlegm does not kill them, nor does it entirely grant them health.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sunt et alii homines, qui iracundi sunt, sed iram cito dimittunt et boni sunt et laeti, sed frigidi, atque yarias mentes habent et paryo cibo saturantur. Et hi de tribus flegmatibus, Bcilicet de sicco et humido atque tepido flegmate yelut aqnosam spumam sibi attrahunt, quae de eisdem flegma tibus oritur et quae velat periculosas sagittas in yenas et mednllas ac in carnem eomm emittit, ut fervens aqua, qnae ferventem spumam eicit. (De flegmcMcis.
text_translation: There are also other people who are irritable, but they quickly let go of anger and are good and joyful, but cold, and have various minds and are filled with small food. And these come from the three phlegms, scilicet from the dry and moist and warm phlegm, like they attract watery foam to themselves, which arises from the same phlegms and which conceals dangerous arrows in the veins and medullas and in the body, like boiling water, which emits boiling foam. (De flegmatis.)

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum in his hominibus diversi humores flegmatum excitantur, itft quod eadem flegmata de immoderato cibo et potu ac de inepta laetitia, tristitia et ira ac de immoderata libidine in eisdem hominibus concutiuntur, tunc ebuUiunt, quasi aqua in caldario igne supposito, et emittunt yelut ignitas guttas et eas ut sa gittas in camem et in sanguinem et in venas eoram mittunt atque homines tanta asperitate inflgunt, yelat amarus fumus oculos confandit.
text_translation: For when in these men diverse humors of phlegm are excited, thus that the same phlegms from immoderate food and drink and from inappropriate joy, sorrow, and anger and from immoderate lust in the same men are shaken, then they boil up, as water in a caldron with fire placed beneath it, and emit, as if ignited drops, and send them as arrows into the brain and into the blood and into their veins, and inflame men with such harshness, or the bitter smoke blurs the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et qui istius complexio- • nis sunt, in ira saepius exardescunt, sed eam cito obliyioni tradunt bonitatem amantes; ut, cum tempestas oritur, deinde sol apparet; ita quod etiam talis est vis complexionis flegmatls eomm, quod facile ad iram, facile ad laetitiam insurgunt; sed ad plenam senectutem non perveniunt. De melancolicis, Sunt yero alii homines, qui tristes ac timidi ac yagi in mentibus suis sunt, ita quod nuUa recta constitutio et status in eis est.
text_translation: And those who are of this complexion, often flare up in anger, but quickly forget it, lovers of goodness; as when a storm arises, then the sun appears; so that even the power of this phlegmatic complexion is such that they easily rise to anger, easily to joy; but they do not reach full old age. Of the melancholics, there are indeed other men, who are sad and timid and wandering in their minds, so that no right constitution and status is in them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed sunt ut yalidus yentus, qui omnibus herbis et fmctibus inutilis est. Unde in his crescit flegma, quod nec humidum nec spissum est, sed tepidum, et quod est ut liyor, qui tenax est, et qui se ut gummi in longum protrahit, et qui parat melan coliam, quae in primo ortu de semine Adae orta est de flatu serpentis, quoniam Adam consilium illius in cibo perfecii De melancoliae morho, Et baec melancolia nigra est et amara et omne malum efflat ac interdum etiam inflrmitatem ad cerebmm et ad cor quasi yenas ebullire facit atque tristitiam et dubietatem totius consolationis parat, ita quod homo nullum gaudium habere potest, quod ad superaam yitam et ad consolationem praesentis yitae pertinet.
text_translation: But they are like a strong wind, which is useless to all herbs and fruits. Therefore, in these, phlegm grows, which is neither moist nor thick, but warm, and which is like lior, which is tenacious, and which stretches itself like gum, and which prepares melancholy, which in its first origin arose from the seed of Adam from the breath of the serpent, because Adam completed his counsel in food regarding the disease of melancholy. And this melancholy is black and bitter and exhales all evil, and sometimes even causes weakness to the brain and to the heart, as if the veins boil, and prepares sadness and doubt of all consolation, so that a man cannot have any joy, which pertains to the life above and to the consolation of present life.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Haec autem melancolia naturalis est omni homini de prima suggestione diaboli, quoniam homo praeceptum dei 2b transgressus est in cibo pomi. Et de hoc cibo eadem melancolia in Adam et in omni genere eius creyit atqne omnem pestem in hominibus excitat. Sed quia praedictum '^iVHP fLegma tepidum est, non deprimit vim melancoliae, nt alia duo praedicta flegmata; qnorum altemm in hnmlditate et altemm in spissitudine et amaritndine sna tantam fortitndinem habent, qnod hnic melancoliae ita resistnnt, nt caldarins super ignem pendens ignem deprimit, ne exsurgat.
text_translation: This, however, natural melancholy is in every man from the first suggestion of the devil, because man transgressed the command of God in the food of the apple. And from this food the same melancholy was created in Adam and in every generation of him, and it excites all pestilence in men. But because the aforementioned phlegm is warm, it does not suppress the power of melancholy, nor do the other two aforementioned phlegmas; of which one is in humility and the other in thickness and bitterness, which have such great strength that they resist this melancholy so much that a caldarium hanging over fire suppresses fire, that it not rise up.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Homines vero hnius complexionis frequenter in ira sunt et timorem in multa ntilitate ad deum et ad homines babent. Et qnidam ex eis aliquantnlnm diu viYunt, qnoniam vis praedioti flegmatis eornm talis est, qnod nec omnino occidit nec omnino viyiflcat hominem, ut in aliquo incarcerato homine evenire solet, qni nec occiditnr nec abire permittitur. Sic homo, ut praedictnm est, ex quatuor humoribus viget, ut etiam mundus ex quatuor elementis constat.
text_translation: Indeed, people of this complexion are often in anger and have fear in much usefulness towards God and towards men. And some of them live a little while, since the force of their foretold phlegm is such that it neither entirely kills nor entirely gives life to a man, as happens in a certain incarcerated man who is neither killed nor allowed to leave. Thus, man, as foretold, lives from the four humors, just as the world also consists of the four elements.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e dementoruin cominixiione. Nam deus mundum quatuor elementis colligaTit, ita quod nnllum ab alio separari potest, quoniam mundus subsistere nequiret, si aliud ab alio separari yaleret. Sed indissolnbiliter sibi concatenata sunt. Ignis enim superat ac domat et inoendit aerem et fortior illo est; aer vero proximus igni ut fnllo facit enm flagrare et temperat eum, quoniam ignis quasi corpus aeris est atque aer quasi viscera et yelut alae et pennae ignis.
text_translation: Of the dementia's comminution. For God has bound the world with four elements, so that nothing can be separated from another, because the world would not be able to subsist if one thing were separated from another. But they are inseparably linked to one another. For fire indeed overcomes and subdues and floods the air and is stronger than it; air, however, is next to fire, so that it causes it to burn and tempers it, because fire is as the body of air and air is as the viscera and as the wings and feathers of fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t ut corpus non est absque yisceribus, sic nec ignis sine aere, quoniam aer commotio ignis est; quia ignis non arderet nec incenderetur, si aerem non haberet. Ignis quoqne est ardor et calor aquae et facit eam flnere, qnoniam aqua non esset liquida nec fiueret, sed fortior et indissolubilior ferro et calibe foret, si calorem ignis in se latentem non haberet; ut etiam in glacie notari potest. Sed aqua est frigns ignis et fortior igne, cum ignem extingnit.
text_translation: And just as the body is not without viscera, so neither is fire without air, because air is the motion of fire; for fire would not burn nor be ignited, if it did not have air. Fire is also the heat and warmth of water and causes it to flow, because water would not be liquid nor would it be, but stronger and more indissoluble than iron and heat would be, if it did not have the latent heat of fire within itself; as can also be noted in ice. But water is cold of fire and stronger than fire, when it extinguishes fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aqua enim frigida erat et non fluebat in initio creaturamm, cum terra erat inanis et vacua, sed spiritns domini ferebatnr snper aquas et eas calefecit, ut ignem in se haberent et ut liquefactae finerent. Et idem frigns aqnae naturaliter ex se emittit ignem et inde fervet. Nam aqua habet in se ignem et ignis in se naturaliter frigus aqnae, quoniam aqua non flueret, si ignem in se non haberet, et ignis nxLmquaiXL extingueretor, sed semper arderet, si frigus aqnae in eo non esset.
text_translation: Water was indeed cold and did not flow in the beginning of creatures, when the earth was empty and void, but the spirit of the Lord was carried over the waters and heated them, so that they would contain fire within themselves and so that they would be liquefied and completed. And the same cold of water naturally emits fire from itself and thus boils. For water has in itself fire and fire has in itself naturally the cold of water, for water would not flow if it did not have fire within itself, and fire would not be extinguished, but would always burn, unless the cold of water were in it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ignis est etiam temperamentam terrae frnctus eius flrmando et siccando atque ad maturitatem perducendo. Terra autem obstaculum ignis est, ne modum et mensuram suam transeat. Sed et aer est yentus et adiutorium aquae, sicut adiutorium ignis et retentaculum eius, ita quod retinet fluctuationem illius in recta mensura. Nam si aquam in recta mensura et itinere non teneret, illa supra modum efflueret et omnia, ad quae pertingeret, submergeret. Aqua vero facit aerem agilem et velocem in volando et fertilem in sudando, ita quod ille dat terrae fecunditatem, cum rorem ex se super eam emittit.
text_translation: And fire is also the temperament of the earth, causing its fruit to flower and dry and to bring it to maturity. But the earth is an obstacle to fire, so that it does not exceed its measure and its standard. However, air is wind and the helper of water, just as fire is the helper and retainer of water, so that it retains the fluctuation of water within the correct measure. For if water were not held within the correct measure and course, it would flow out beyond measure and submerge everything it reached. Water, indeed, makes air agile and swift in flying and fertile in sweating, so that it gives the earth fertility, when it emits dew from itself upon it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aer quoque est quasi pallium terrae, quia aestum et frigus ab ea depellit, cum illam temperat et cum terrae rorem infusione sua immittit. Terra autem est velut spongia et materia, quae attrahit et suscipit fecunditatem ab illo, quoniam, si terra non esset, aer officium suum, quo terram fecundat, non haberet. Sed aqua coagulatio terrae est et constringit et domat eam, ne diffluat. Terra vero sustinet et continet aquam et facit ei recta itinera et est sustentatio eius superius, ut rectnm iter habeat, et sub se, ne iniuste ascendat.
text_translation: Air is also like a cloak of the earth, because it repels heat and cold from it, when it tempers it and when it infuses it with the dew of the earth. But the earth is like a sponge and material that attracts and receives fertility from it, because, if the earth were not, air would not have its office, by which it fertilizes the earth. But water is the coagulation of the earth and constricts and controls it, so that it does not flow away. The earth indeed sustains and contains water and makes for it straight paths and is its support above, so that it has a straight path, and under itself, so that it does not ascend unjustly.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam subtus se obumbrat illam et super se continet eam. Bos autem, qui terram fecundat, ut supra dictum est, de temperamento ignis et aeris venit. De rore, Nam cum ignis et aer invicem offlcium suum in placido temperamento in aestatis tempore exercent, in placida et lucida aura absque commotione tempes tatum propter calorem, quem tunc invicem habent, rorem sudant, qui salubriter fecunditatem et fertilitatem ad utilitatem fructuum terrae velut semen infondunt.
text_translation: For beneath it it shades that [thing] and above it contains it. The bull, however, who fertilizes the earth, as has been said above, comes from the temperament of fire and air. From the dew, for when fire and air in turn exercise their office in a temperate temperament during the time of summer, in a placid and luminous air without the motion of tempests because of the heat which they then have in common, they sweat dew, which salubrily infuses fertility and fruitfulness for the utility of the fruits of the earth as if sowing seed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pruina. Cum autem aer in hieme ad frigus terrae se inclinat, ex hac coniunctione et collisione pruinam et ipse et frigus educunt, quae et herbas et erumpentes flores laedit et terram constringendo arefacit. Nunc autem, ut praefatum est, elementa, ex quibus mundus constat^ ^mrm ita sibi colligata et coneatenata sunt, quod nuinquam ab inyicem separari possunt, quoniam ignis non est absque aere nec aer absque aqua nec aqua absque terra; quamvis ignis fortiores yires habeat quam aer et aqua fortiores quam ignis ac terra fecundiores et fertiliores quam baec tria.
text_translation: On pruina. When, however, the air in winter inclines toward the cold of the earth, from this conjunction and collision pruina and the cold themselves are produced, which damage both herbs and emerging flowers and, by constricting the earth, harden it. Now, as has been said, the elements, from which the world is composed, are so tightly bound and interwoven that they can never be separated from each other, because fire is not without air, nor air without water, nor water without earth; although fire has stronger forces than air and water has stronger forces than fire, and earth is more fertile and productive than these three.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed asperitas unius lenitatem alterius subleyat, et lenitas alterius asperitatem istius mitigat, scilicet quod tali concordia et mensura sibi invicem naturaliter concordant, quod nuUum aliud disturbat, nisi quod aliquando propter iudicium dei ad ultionem provocata aut ignes aut tempestates aut effasiones seu infertilitates producunt. De nebula, In quibusdam enim montibus et in quibusdam yallibus ac in quibusdam aliis locis ex quibus aliquando per iudicium dei quaedam nebula primum nigra ascendit, quae postea, cum se dilatat, turbilenta flt et ali- quantulum malum et periculosum foetorem habet.
text_translation: But the roughness of one alleviates the softness of another, and the softness of another mitigates the roughness of this one, namely that by such concord and measure they naturally concord with each other, so that nothing else disturbs them, except that sometimes, because of the judgment of God provoked for vengeance, either fires or storms or emissions or infertilities produce. Of the cloud, for in some mountains and in some valleys and in some other places from which sometimes, by the judgment of God, a certain black cloud first rises, which afterwards, when it expands, becomes turbulent and has somewhat a bad and dangerous odor.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et haec cum se per mundum spargit, morbos et pestilentiam atque mortem et hominibus et pecoribus infert. Et aliquando quaedam nebula de humiditate aquarum ascendit et ea, quae in terris sunt, tangit aliquantulum et ita in mundum spargitur. Atque aliquantulum morbos et pestilentiam hominibus et pecoribus infert, sed eos non occidit, et primos erumpentes flores £ructuum aufert et fructus laedit, ita quod arbores et herbae folia sua contrahunt et quod arescunt, velut si calida aqua perfasa sint.
text_translation: And this, when it spreads throughout the world, inflicts diseases and plague and death upon humans and animals. And sometimes a certain mist rises from the moisture of waters and touches slightly those things that are on the earth and thus spreads throughout the world. And it inflicts a little disease and plague upon humans and animals, but does not kill them, and takes away the first emerging flowers of fruits and damages the fruits, so that trees and herbs draw in their leaves and wither, as if they had been scorched by hot water.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Alia autem quaedam nebula surgit de nimio calore et de superfluitate aeris et nubium et de humiditate earum, et periculosa non est. Sed et quaedam nebula de frigore et de humore terrae ascendit et quaedam nebula de diversis aquis, quae tamen pericula hominibus et pecoribus et fructibus terrae non inferunt, quia natura earum est, ut sic suo tempore surgant. Quod sol in mane rubet, cum oritur, hoc de frigore et humiditate aeris est, quia humiditas et frigus, quae tunc sunt, rubedinem oculis hominum inferunt.
text_translation: Another certain kind of cloud arises from excessive heat and the superfluity of air and clouds and from the humidity of them, and it is not dangerous. But also a certain cloud arises from cold and the moisture of the earth, and a certain cloud from various waters, which, however, do not bring harm to humans and animals and the fruits of the earth, because their nature is that they thus arise in their proper time. That the sun reddens in the morning when it rises, this is from the cold and humidity of the air, because the humidity and cold that are present then cause redness in the eyes of humans.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Simili modo etiam in sero ad vesperam, cum sol rubet, hoc de frigore aeris est, quod tunc ad occasum inclinatur. Quod quatuor sunt elementa tantum. Plura igitur HILDS6ARDIS yel pauciora quam qoatuor esse non possunt. Et ex duobns generibns constant superionun et inferiomm. Nam snperiora sunt caelestia, inferiora vero terrestria; et quae in superioribus yivunt, inpalpabilia sunt et ex igne et aere sunt; quae yero in inferioribus yersantnr, palpabilia et formata corpora sunt et ex aqua et limo constant.
text_translation: In a similar manner, even in the evening towards dusk, when the sun reddens, this is due to the cold of the air, which then inclines toward the sunset. That there are only four elements. Therefore, Hildegard says that there cannot be more or fewer than four. And from two kinds they consist, the superior and the inferior. For the superior are celestial, the inferior indeed terrestrial; and those that live in the superior are intangible and consist of fire and air; those indeed that dwell in the inferior are tangible and formed bodies and consist of water and mud.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De anima et spiritibus, Spiritus enim ignei et aerei sunt, homo autem aquosus et limosus est. De Adae creaHone, Nam cum deus hominem crearet, limus per aquam conglutinatus est, ex quo bomo formatus est, misitque deus in formam illam spiraculum yitae igneum et aereum. £t quia forma bominis ex limo et aqua foit, ex igne eiusdem spiraculi yitae limus caro factus est, et ex aere eius aqua, per quam limus conglu tinatus est, sanguis effecta est. Cum enim deus Adam crearet, splendor diyinitatis massam limi, de qua creatus est, circumfulsit, et ita limus ille illata forma in liniamentis membrorum exterius apparuit et interius yacua.
text_translation: On the soul and spirits. Spirits are fiery and airy, but man is watery and muddy. On the creation of Adam. For when God created man, the clay was glued together by water, from which man was formed. God sent into that form the fiery and airy breath of life. And because the form of man was made from clay and water, from the fire of that same breath of life the clay became flesh, and from the air of that same breath the water, by which the clay was glued together, became blood. For when God created Adam, the splendor of divinity surrounded the mass of clay, from which he was created, and thus that clay, given form, appeared in the outlines of the limbs externally and internally remained void.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tunc etiam deus interius in eadem ex eadem massa limi creayit cor et iecur et pulmonem ac stomacbum et yiscera et cerebrum ac oculos et linguam et reliqua interiora eius. Et cum deus spiraculum yitae in eum misit, materia eius, quae ossa et medulla ac yenae sunt, per idem spiraculum confortata sunt; et illud in eandem massam ita se dis tinxit, ut yermis in domum suam se intorquet et ut yiriditas in arbore est; et etiam ita confortata sunt, ut argentum in alium modum fit, cum illud faber in ignem proicit; et sic in corde eius sedit.
text_translation: Then also God created within the same mass of clay the heart and liver and lungs and stomach and viscera and brain and eyes and tongue and the rest of its inner parts. And when God sent the breath of life into him, his matter, which are bones and marrow and veins, were strengthened through the same breath; and it wove itself into the same mass so that the worm turns itself into its own home and that greenness is in the tree; and they were also strengthened so that silver is made in another way when the craftsman throws it into fire; and thus it sat in his heart.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tunc etiam in eadem massa de igne animae caro et sanguis facta sunt. De capillis. Et yiriditas animae spumam et bumiditatem ad caput, scilicet in cerebrum misit, ande et cerebrum bumidum est, et de bumiditate illa caput pilos emittit. De interioribm hominis, Anima autem ignea, yentosa et bumida est et totum cor bominis possidet. Jecur yero cor calefacit, pulmo illud tegit, stomacbus autem interios babitaculum in corpore bominis est ad suscipiendum cibos.
text_translation: Then also in the same mass from the fire of the soul flesh and blood were made. Concerning the hair. And the greenness of the soul sends foam and humidity to the head, that is, into the brain, from which the brain is humid, and from that humidity the head emits hair. Concerning the interiors of man, the soul, however, is fiery, suctioning and humid, and possesses the entire heart of man. The liver truly warms the heart, the lung covers it, the stomach, however, is the inner dwelling in the body of man for receiving foods.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cor proprietatem scientiae habet, iecnr proprietatem sensibilatis , polmo proprietatem folii et itineris rationabilitatiS os tuba eomm, qaae homo profert, et snsceptor refectionis corporis est et vocem profert, sed vocem non excipit; auris autem yocem excipit, sed yocem non profert. 2e atmbtts. Et duae aures velut duae alae sont, quae omnes sonos vocum inducunt et educunt, ut alae volucres iil aere ferunt. jDe octdis et naribm. Oculi quoque viae hominis et nares sapientia eius sunt.
text_translation: And the heart has the property of science, the liver the property of the sensible, the lung the property of the leaf and the journey of reason; the mouth, which is the common tube that man produces, is the receptacle of the body's nourishment and produces voice, but does not receive voice; the ear, however, receives voice, but does not produce it. About the ears. And the two ears are like two wings, which lead in and out all the sounds of voices, as the wings of birds carry through the air. About the eyes and nostrils. The eyes are also the paths of man and the nostrils are his wisdom.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic etiam per cetera membra factns est homo. Quod vn homme stmt dementa. Nunc autem, ut praefatum est, elementa, videlicet ignis, aer, terra, aqua in homine sunt et viribus suis in illo operantur ac in operibus illius sicut rota cum flexuris suis yelociter cir- cueunt. Nam ignis cum quinque praenominatis viribus suis in cerebro et in medullis hominis est, quoniam, cum primus homo de limo mutatus est, rutilans ignis in sanguine eius ex fortitudine dei ardebat, ut etiam sanguis rubet.
text_translation: And thus, through the other members, man is made. That a man is mad. Now, however, as stated before, the elements, namely fire, air, earth, water are in man and operate in him by their forces and in his works, as a wheel with its spokes quickly circles. For fire, with its five aforementioned forces, is in the brain and in the marrow of man, because, when the first man was changed from clay, glowing fire in his blood burned from the strength of God, so that even blood is red.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ignis ardorem in visu, geliditatem in odoratu, humorem in gustu, aerem in auditu, et motionem in tactu hominis demonstrant. Aer autem cum quatuor viribus suis, ut praedictum est, in spiramine et rationalitate hominis est. Ipse enim viventi spiramine, quod anima est, in homine ministrat, quod eum portat, ac ala volatus eius est, ubi homo spiramen in se inducit et educit, ut vivere possit. Et anima ignis est, qui totum corpus penetrat et hominem viviflcat.
text_translation: And fire demonstrates heat in sight, coldness in smell, humor in taste, air in hearing, and motion in touch of man. Air, however, with its four forces, as previously said, is in the spirit and reason of man. For indeed, to the living, the spirit, which is the soul, ministers in man, by which it carries him, and is the wing of his flight, where man takes in and expels the spirit within himself, so that he may live. And the soul is fire, which penetrates the entire body and vivifies man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aer quoque ignem accendit, et ignis per aerem in omnibus ardet. Aer etiam rorem in emissione, viriditatem in excitatione, flatum in motione, calorem in dilatatione hominis ostendit. Sed aqua cum quindecim viribus suis, quae praenominatae sunt, in humore et in sanguine hominis est. De sanguine, Aqua enim in homine est, cum sanguis in eo non deest. Et haec humiditatem in homine facit, ita quod etiam viriditas in eo viget et quod coagulatio ossium in eo perdurat.
text_translation: Air also ignites fire, and fire through air burns in all things. Air also shows moisture in emission, greenness in excitation, wind in motion, heat in dilation of man. But water, with its fifteen powers, which have been previously named, is in the humor and blood of man. About blood, water is in man, since blood is not present in it. And this causes humidity in man, so that greenness also prevails in it, and that the coagulation of bones in it endures.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De frigiditate autem venae in homine firmantur, qnod per ipsam sangois floit et; guttas habet ac totum corpns movet. Ipsa etiam camem. cam sangoine perfondit, nt dorare possit, yelut etiam terram conglntinat. Sed frigiditatem aquae ignis stiperat, ita qnod fluit, et aqna cum igne et frigiditate sua terram ita perfiindit, quod per ea firmatur. Et gelu, quod aquas per glaciem indurat, in lapidibus est, unde illi molliri non possunt, quemadmodum etiam ossa in carne hominis dura sunt.
text_translation: Of the coldness, however, the veins in man are firm, because through it the blood flows and has drops and moves the whole body. It also fills the chamber, so that it may be hardened, like the earth is glued. But the coldness of water is overcome by fire, so that it flows, and water with fire and its own coldness soaks the earth so that through it it is hardened. And frost, which hardens waters through ice, is in stones, from which they cannot be softened, just as also bones in the flesh of man are hard.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic aqua in sanguine hominis calorem, in spiratione eius aerem, in perfectione humorem, in purgatione inundationem, in vegetatione velocitatem, in confortatione succum, in fructificatione gustumy in erectione viridatem, in fortitudine humiditatem ac in omnibus compaginibus ipsius humectationem manifestat. Terra quoque cum septem viri bus suis, quae praescriptae sunt, in came ac in ossibus hominis est, et caro illis humida est et crescit. De carne.
text_translation: Thus water in the blood of man manifests heat, in his exhalation air, in perfection humor, in purgation overflow, in vegetation speed, in comfort juice, in fructification taste, in erection greenness, in strength moisture, and in all tissues of him moistening. Earth also, with its seven male principles, which are prescribed, is in flesh and in bones of man, and flesh is moist and grows. Of flesh.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed sicut terra cum igne et aqua firmatur, sic caro hominis cum venis et humiditate composita est. De geliditate autem eius coagulatio ossium fit. Sed ignis haec omnia superat, ita quod fortitudo in homine est. Caro enim hominis de terra est et frigidum humorem habet, sed sanguis illam calefacit; et si de ipso non calefieret, in lutum, ut prius fuit, rediret; et ideo de calore sanguinis sicut terra de calore solis firmatur. Sed tamen de moUitie sua sanguinea est et yires terrae in se habet.
text_translation: But just as earth is made firm with fire and water, so is the flesh of man composed with veins and moisture. From its coldness, the coagulation of bones occurs. But fire overcomes all these things, so that strength is in man. For the flesh of man is from earth and has cold moisture, but blood warms it; and if it were not warmed by it, it would return to mud, as it was before; and therefore, from the heat of blood, just as earth is made firm from the heat of the sun. But nevertheless, from its moisture it is sanguineous and has the forces of the earth within itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam de frigida humiditate sudat, de ardore aestuat, et absque frigiditate, quemadmodum terra, durare non posset. De generatione. Homo enim de frigiditate et caliditate fecundus est ac cum ceteris creaturis laetam vitam habet et de se genituram producit, quia calor viriditas eius ac frigiditas ariditas eius est; ac per haec omnia germinat. Cum autem homini senectus advenerit, omnis exterior calor in ipsum revertitur; alioquin vivere non posset. Et ita caro eius exterius frigescit et ipse interius calescit; unde quidquid vetus homo agit, in hoc cito fatigatur.
text_translation: For it sweats from cold humidity, burns from heat, and without cold, like the earth, could not endure. On generation. For the human being is fertile from cold and heat and, along with other creatures, has a happy life and produces offspring from himself, because heat is his greenness and cold is his dryness; and through these things all things germinate. When, however, old age comes upon the human being, all external heat returns into him; otherwise, he could not live. And thus his flesh externally cools and he internally heats; whence whatever the old man does, in this he quickly fatigues.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et animalia ei adhaerent, cum ipse illis pascitur et cum etiam illa pascit, et sic omnia fert, cum omnis creatura in eo est. Et terra cum came hominis in calore eius frigiditatem, in frigiditate eios calorem, in incremento viriditatem, in defectione ariditatem, in germinatione vivificationem, in multiplicatione sustentationem, in subportatione omnium membrorum ipsius compassionem ostendit. Et homo de igne sensualitatem et desiderium trahit, de aere autem cogitationes et vagationem, de aqua vero scientiam et motionem.
text_translation: And animals adhere to it, when it feeds on them and when they also feed on it, and thus it carries all things, since all creatures are in it. And the earth, with the flesh of man, in its heat coldness, in its coldness heat, in growth greenness, in defect dryness, in germination vivification, in multiplication sustenance, in the carrying of all its members compassion is shown. And man draws from fire sensuality and desire, from air however thoughts and wandering, from water truly knowledge and motion.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De Adae vivificatione, Adam enim cum terra fuit, ignis eum excitavit, et aer eum suscitavit, et aqua eum perfudit, quod totus movebatur. Tunc deus soporem in eum misit, et in hlB viribus coctus est, ita quod caro eius per ignem aestuabat et quod per aerem spiravit et quod sicut molendinum aqua in eo circuivit. Qui postquam evigilavit, propheta caelestium fuit et sciens in omni vi creaturae et in omni arte erat. De Adae prophetia. Et deus omnes cr«aturas illi dedit, quatinus virili vi eas penetraret, quoniam illas scivit et cognovit.
text_translation: On the Vivification of Adam. For Adam, when he was with the earth, fire awakened him, and air raised him up, and water poured over him, so that he was entirely moved. Then God sent sleep upon him, and in the forces he was cooked, so that his flesh boiled with fire and that through air he breathed, and that water circled around him as in a mill. When he awoke afterward, he became a prophet of the celestial and knew all the power of creation and was skilled in every art. On the Prophecy of Adam. And God gave all creatures to him, so that with virile force he might penetrate them, for he had known them and recognized them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam ipse homo omnis creatura est, et spiramen vitae in eo est, quod finem vitae non habet. De animae infusione, Quod enim anima in corpus mittitur, spiramen est, et a deo missum est, ac per corporalia opera, sive bona sive mala sunt, meritum accipit. Et haec opera quasi compago meritorum sunt. Sicut enim infans primo nescit, quod postea intelligit, quia, cum maturior erit, inteUectum, qui omnia intelligit, capit, et sicut tunc opera sua considerando et osculando amplectitur et sicut postea de senectute fatigatur: sic anima per opera proficiendo procedit.
text_translation: For the man himself is all creature, and the spirit of life in him has no end of life. Concerning the infusion of the soul: For whatever the soul sends into the body, it is a spirit, and it is sent by God, and through bodily works, whether good or bad, it receives merit. And these works are as it were a bundle of merits. For just as the infant first does not know what he later understands, because when he becomes more mature, he receives the intellect which understands all things, and as then, considering and embracing his works through kissing, and as later he becomes fatigued by old age: so the soul progresses by advancing through its works.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et de bonis operibus velut regali amictu cicumdatur, de malis vero pbfuscatur, quemadmodum etiam terra aquis perfunditur; et quemadmodum aquae in quibusdam locis fluunt, ita anima corpus perfundit et ipsum corpus excellit, sed et ipsa oculis exterioribus clausis cum prophetia multotiens futura videtj quia sine corpore se vivere recordatur. De Adae somno, Post primum namque somnum Adae prophetia eius vera fuit, quoniam nondum pecca verat, sed postea cnm mendacio permixta est.
text_translation: And concerning good works, it is surrounded as with a royal robe, but concerning evils it is obscured, just as the earth is flooded by waters; and just as waters flow in certain places, so the soul floods the body and the body itself excels, yet even with the external eyes closed it often sees the future with prophecy, because it remembers to live without the body. Concerning the sleep of Adam: After the first sleep of Adam, his prophecy was true, because he had not yet sinned, but afterward it was mixed with falsehood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t Adam • de terra creatus et cum elementis snscitatas matabatur, Eva vero de costa Adae mutata non est. De Evae malUia, Et idem Adam de viriditate terrae virilis et de elementis fortissimns erat, Eva aatem de medullis sais moUis fait et aeream mentem ac acutam et deliciosam vitam habait, qaoniam pondas terrae eam non pressit. Sed ut ipsa de viro est edacta, sic omne genos hamanam ex ipsa processit. Homo qaoqne in daas partes divisus est, scilicet vigiliarum et dormitionis.
text_translation: And Adam was created from the earth and with the elements was raised up and killed, but Eve was not changed from Adam's rib. Concerning Eve's wickedness, and the same Adam was from the viridity of the earth and the elements the strongest. Eve, however, was from the marrow of the sages soft and had an airy mind and a sharp and delightful life, since the weight of the earth did not press her. But as she was drawn out from the man, so all the human race has proceeded from her. Man also is divided into two parts, namely of waking and sleeping.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et corpus hominis duobus modis alitur, ita quod cibo repletuf et quod per somnum reficitur. Postquam autem anima corpus egressa faerit, in alio modo deinde cum ipso victura erit, et hoc illa, quae bona est, vix sustinet. IJnde etiam ad deum clamat et dicit: quando carnem meam induam, cum qua in diumis diebus vixi? Cum enim deus omnes creaturas creavit, dies una cum integra luce, quam nox nondum diviserat, apparuit. De exUio Adae. Sed postquam Adam peccavit, nox adesse coepit, et omnia elementa magnis tenebris obtenebrata sunt, in quibus Adam in exilium istud deductus est.
text_translation: But also the human body is nourished in two ways, so that it is filled by food and restored by sleep. After, however, the soul having departed from the body, then in another way it will live with it, and this, which is good, scarcely sustains it. From there also, it cries to God and says: when will I put on my flesh, with which I lived in the days of light? When indeed God created all creatures, the day with complete light, which night had not yet divided, appeared. Of Adam's exile. But after Adam sinned, night began to be present, and all elements were darkened by great darkness, in which Adam was led into this exile.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui dum lucem saeculi huius vidit, gaudebat, quoniam ipse tenebrosus erat, ac lacrimando dixit: alio modo vivere debeo, quam prius mihi vivere deus dederit. Et ita cum sudore laborare coepit. Sed antequam Adam et Eva divinum praeceptum praevaricati fuissent, ut sol in splendore fulgebant, qui splendor etiam ipsis quasi pro vestimento fuit. Qui dum praeceptum dei transgredientes amplius non falgebant, ut prius fecerant, sed obscuri facti sunt, et ita in obscuritate illa permanseruht.
text_translation: Who, when he saw the light of this age, rejoiced, because he himself was gloomy, and weeping, he said: I ought to live in another way than God previously gave me to live. And thus he began to labor with sweat. But before Adam and Eve had transgressed the divine command, the sun shone in splendor, which splendor was also to them as a garment. Who, when transgressing the command of God, no longer shone as they had done before, but became dark, and thus remained in that darkness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde cum viderent se non fulgere, sicut prius fulgebant, cognoverunt se nudos esse et foliis arboris, sicut ibi scriptum est, se obtexerunt. Et Adam ante praevaricationem absque operibus ut sol fulgebat, quod nondum aliquod opus fecerat. Sed post finem saeci:Qi iterum iusti ut sol fal^ebunt, sicut scriptum est: fulgebunt iusti sicut sol in regno patris eorum. Sed cum sanctis operibus fulgeCAUSAE ET LIB. U. bunt. Nam in splendore, quem tunc sancti habebunt^ sancta opera fulgent et posita sunt, ut pretiosi lapides in auro ponuntur.
text_translation: Therefore, when they saw themselves no longer shining, as they had previously shone, they recognized themselves as naked and covered themselves with the leaves of the tree, as it is written there. And Adam before the transgression, without works, shone like the sun, which had not yet done any work. But after the end of the age, the just shall shine like the sun, as it is written: the just shall shine like the sun in the kingdom of their father. But with holy works they shall shine. For in the splendor which the saints shall have at that time, holy works shine and are placed, as precious stones are placed in gold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quare Eva prius cecidU. Sed et si Adam transgressus fuisset prius quam Eya, tunc transgressio illa tam fortis et tam incorrigibilis fuisset, quod homo etiam in tam magna obduratione incorrigibilitatis cecidisset, quod nec salyari yellet nec posset. Unde quod Eva prior transgrediebatur, facilius deleri potuit, quia etiam fragilior masculo fuit. Caro et cutis Adae fortior et durior fuit lo quam hominum nunc sit, quia Adam de terra formatus fait et Eva de ipso.
text_translation: Why Eve fell first. But even if Adam had transgressed before Eve, then that transgression would have been so strong and so incorrigible that man would also have fallen, even in such great stubbornness of incorrigibility, that he would neither wish nor be able to be saved. Hence, because Eve transgressed first, she could more easily be destroyed, since she was also more fragile than the male. The flesh and skin of Adam were stronger and tougher than those of men now are, because Adam was formed from the earth and Eve from him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed postquam filios genuerunt, caro iliorum semper et semper fragilior et fragiLior facta est, et ita usque in novissimum diem erit. De diluvio, Cum autem Adam de paradiso expulsus est, aqua ante diluvium tantae velocitatis in cursu suo non erat nec tantae liquiditatis, ut postea facta est. Sed supra se quasi membranam habebat, quod eum aliquantulum retardabat, ita quod paulatim fluebat. Sed et terra tunc non fuit limosa sed arens atque fragilis, quod por aquam nondum transfusa fuit.
text_translation: But after they bore children, the flesh of their offspring became ever and ever more fragile, and thus it will remain until the last day. Concerning the flood: When indeed Adam was cast out of paradise, the water before the flood was not so swift in its course nor so fluid as it afterwards became. But it had above itself as if a membrane, which somewhat delayed it, so that it flowed gradually. But the earth then was not muddy but sandy and fragile, because through it the water had not yet been poured.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed de primo praecepto fructum supra modum dabat. Tunc etiam homines obliti erant deum, ita quod plus secundum pecora quam secundum deum faciebant. Unde multi magis pecora quam homines diligebant, ita quod etiam pecoribus tam femicae quam mares tali modo commiscebantur et communicabant^ quod imago dei iam paene in eis destituta fuit in eis. £t omne genus humanum in monstra transmutatum et transformatum, ita quod etiam aliqui homines secundum bestias mores et voces suas formantes constituebant currendo, ululando et vivendo.
text_translation: But from the first precept, fruit was given beyond measure. Then also, men had forgotten God, so that they did more according to cattle than according to God. Hence, many loved cattle more than men, so that even among cattle, both females and males were mixed and communicated in such a way that the image of God was almost entirely deprived in them. And all human kind was transformed into monsters, so that even some men formed their ways and voices according to beasts, running, howling, and living.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam bestiae et pecora ante diluvium tantae asperitatis nondum fuerunt, ut postea facta sunt. Nec homines illa nec illa homines fugiebant, nec ab invicem exterrita fuerunt. Sed bestiae et pecora cum hominibus hbenter manebant et homines cum illis, quod etiam in primo ortu simul originem paene sumpserunt. Sed et bestiae et pecora lambebant homines et homines illa, unde et magis se iuyicem in contrarietate amabant et sibi cobaerebant. Sed tamen Adam quosdam filios procreaverat, qui divina ratione ita repleti erant, quod se ad nullam.
text_translation: For the beasts and cattle before the flood were not yet so harsh as they later became. Neither men nor those men fled, nor were they terrified of each other. But beasts and cattle lived well with men and men with them, which even in the first origin they almost took together. But also beasts and cattle licked men and men them, from which they loved each other more in contrariety and embraced each other. But Adam had indeed created some sons, who were so filled with divine reason that they were directed to none.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: turpitudinem commiscere volebant, sed quod in sanctitate permanerent. IJnde et filii dei dicebantur. Qmre filii dei. Et bi aspiciebant et investigabant, ubi bomines illi essent, qui se pecoribus non commiscuissent et qui cum pecoribus destituti non essent, quamyis filii praevaricatorum essent, sicut superius dictum est. Unde et filii bominum dicebantur, quia tamen nec in formis suis nec cum pecoribus destituti erant. Et de filiabus istorum uxores duxerunt et ex eis filios procreaverunt, at scriptum est: videntes filii dei filias bominum, quod esseot pulcbrae.
text_translation: They wished to mix with turpitude, but that they would remain in sanctity. Hence they were called sons of God. Why the sons of God? And they looked and investigated where those men were, who had not mingled with the cattle and who were not destitute with the cattle, although they were sons of transgressors, as was said above. Hence they were called sons of men, because they were not destitute in their forms nor with the cattle. And from the daughters of those, they took wives and from them bore sons, but it is written: seeing the sons of God the daughters of men, that they were beautiful.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et quaedam bestiae et pecora adbuc sunt, quae boc modo, ut praedictum est, plurimum sibi de bumana natura ab bominibus contraxerunt. Tunc clamor huius iniquitatis ante oculos dei ascendit, quoniam imago dei contracta et elisa erat et rationalitas confusa in fornicatione. IJnde spiritus dei, qui in creatione mundi ferebatur, super aquas emisit aquas, et membrana aquarum. per quam aquae prius aliquantulum retardabantur, ne tanta velocitate fluerent, ut modo fluunt, dirupta est, et aqua in cursu suo velox facta est et bomines submersit.
text_translation: But also certain beasts and cattle are, as it has been said before, have taken from human nature very much from humans. Then the cry of this iniquity ascended before the eyes of God, because the image of God was contracted and exiled, and rationality was confused in fornication. From there the spirit of God, which dwelled in the creation of the world, emitted waters upon waters, and the membrane of waters, by which waters were previously somewhat delayed, so that they would not flow with such velocity as they now flow, was broken, and the water became swift in its course and submerged humans.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tunc aqua terram ita transfudit, quod velut ferruginea facta est et firma et quod in omnibus fructibus novum succum et fortiorem quam prius dedit et quod vinum emisit, quod antea non apparuit. Sed et lapides, qui cum terra creati sunt et qui terra operti erant, per aquam manifesti sunt et apparuerunt, et quidam eorum scissi suDt, qui prius integri fuerunt. De lapidum gignitione. Lapides autem nec antea nec postea creverunt exceptis illis, qui in fluminibus limpidi et globosi apparent, sed tantum cum terra creati modo per diluvium revelati sunt.
text_translation: Then water so thoroughly permeated the earth that it became as rusty and firm, and that it gave new and stronger juice than before in all fruits, and that it emitted wine, which had not appeared before. But also the stones, which were created with the earth and which had been buried in the earth, became visible and appeared through water, and some of them split, which had previously been intact. Concerning the generation of stones. Stones, however, neither before nor after grew, except for those which appear in rivers as clear and globular, but only those created with the earth were revealed in this way by the flood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De iri, Tunc deus posuit arcum suum in firmamento caeli ad roborandum firmamentum et ad resistendum aquis. Et idem arcus est igneus et colores aquarum babet, qui tain fortes contra aquas sunt sicut nubes, ita quod igne et coloribus suis aquas sustinet, ut rete pisces retinet, ne de se effludoit. Et post diluvium maior sapientia et maiores virtutes factae sunt in hominibus quam prius fuissent et apparuissent. Ante diluvium autem tota terra liominibus et bestiis plena fuit, nec aquis nec silvis ab invicem separati fuerunt, quia nondum magna flumina aut magnae silvae erant, sed tantum fontes et parvi nvuli, qui facile transvadari potuerunt, et pauca nemora, quae facile homines transcurrebant, Sed post diluvium quidam lo fontes et quidam rivuli in magna et periculosa flumina effosi sunt, et magnae silvae creverunt, quibus etiam homines et bestiae separati sunt, ita quod postea homines bestias et bestiae homines abhorrebant.
text_translation: On the rainbow, Then God placed His bow in the firmament of the heavens to strengthen the firmament and to resist the waters. And this same bow is fiery and has the colors of the waters, which are so strong against the waters as clouds, so that by fire and their colors the waters are held, as a net holds fish, so that they do not flow out from themselves. And after the flood greater wisdom and greater virtues were made in humans than before they had been and appeared. Before the flood, however, the entire earth was full of humans and beasts, and neither by waters nor by woods were they separated from one another, because as yet great rivers or great woods were not, but only springs and small clouds, which could easily be crossed, and few woods, which humans could easily traverse. But after the flood, some springs and some streams were dug out into great and dangerous rivers, and great woods grew, by which even humans and beasts were separated, so that afterwards humans abhorred beasts and beasts abhorred humans.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam etiam ante diluvium non pluit, sed tantum ros super terram descendit. Postquam autem terra perfusa est et firmata in diluvio aquis, terra naturaliter pluviam aquarum petit. De terrae sUu, £t terra modica est et prope fundum firmamenti est, quod si in medio firmamenti esset, tunc eam oporteret maiorem esse et tunc etiam facile caderet et dirumperetur, si tantam amplitudinem aeris sub se haberet, quantam super se habet. Sed et ipsa ad meridiem quasi descensus montis est, unde etiam ibi maiorem calorem de sole habet, quia sol et firmamentum ipsi viciniora ibi sunt.
text_translation: For even before the flood, it did not rain, but only dew descended upon the earth. Afterward, however, when the earth was drenched and solidified by the waters of the flood, the earth naturally seeks rainwater. Of the earth's own, and the earth is small and near the base of the firmament, which if it were in the middle of the firmament, then it would have to be larger, and then also easily fall and be destroyed, if it had as much breadth of air beneath it as it has above it. But also itself toward the south is as if the descent of a mountain, whence also there it has greater heat from the sun, because the sun and the firmament themselves are closer there.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ad aquilonem vero alta est ad- . versum poenas, et etiam ibi maius frigus est, quia nec firmamentum nec sol ibi prope terram sunt, sed quaedam maior amplitudo firmamenti. Quod homo constat de elementis. Nunc autem, ut supra dictum est, quemadmodum elementa mundum simul continent, sic etiam elementa compago corporis hominis sunt, atque effusio et officia eorum ita se dividunt per hominem, ut insimul contineatur, velut etiam per mundnm effnsa sunt et operantur.
text_translation: To the north indeed it is lofty against punishments, and even there greater cold exists, because neither the firmament nor the sun are there near the earth, but rather a greater expanse of the firmament. For man is composed of elements. Now, as has been said above, just as elements together contain the world, so also elements are the structure of the human body, and their effusion and functions are thus divided through man, so that they are contained together, just as they are also poured out and operate in the world.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ignis enim, aer, aqua et terra in homine sunt, et ex his constat. Nam ex igne habet calorem, ex aere halitum, ex aqua sanguinem et ex terra carnem, ita quod etiam de igne habet visum, de aere andituiu, de aqua motionem atque de terra incessum. Et ut mundus in prosperitate est, cum elementa bene et ordinate officia sua exercent, ita quod calor, ros et pluvia singillatim et moderate in tempore suo se diyidunt ac descendunt ad temperiem terrae et fructuum et multum fructum et sanitatem afferunt; quoniam, si simul et repente ac non in tempore suo super terrt«m caderent, terra discinderetur ac fructus eius et sanitas interiret: sic etiam, cum elementa ordinate in bomine operantur, eum conser vant et sanum reddunt; sed cum in eo discordant, eum infirmum faciunt et occidunt.
text_translation: Fire, indeed, air, water and earth are in man, and from these he is composed. For from fire he has heat, from air breath, from water blood and from earth flesh, so that even from fire he has sight, from air hearing, from water motion and from earth stepping. And as the world is in prosperity when the elements well and orderly perform their offices, so that heat, dew and rain separately and moderately in their time spread out and descend to the temperance of the earth and fruits and bring much fruit and health; because, if they were to fall upon the earth at once and suddenly, and not in their time, the earth would be torn apart and its fruits and health would perish: thus also, when the elements orderly operate in the body, they preserve it and make it healthy; but when they are discordant in it, they make it weak and kill it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam coagulationes bumorum a calore, bumiditate, sanguine et a carne in bominem descendentes et in eo existentes, si cum tranquillitate et iusto temperamento in illo operantur, sanitatem babent; si autem eum simul indiscrete tangunt et super eum in superfluitate cadunt, illum debilem faciunt et occidunt. Oalor enim ac bumiditas (et sanguis et caro propter transgressionem Adae in contraria flegmata in bomine mutata sunt. De flegmatis diversitate, Nam ab igneo calore siccum et ab aerea bumiditate bumidum ac ab aquoso sanguine spumaticum et a terrena came tepidum flegma trabitur et excitatur.
text_translation: Nam coagulationes humorum a calore, humiditate, sanguine et a carne in hominem descendentes et in eo existentes, si cum tranquillitate et iusto temperamento in illo operantur, sanitatem habent; si autem eum simul indiscrete tangunt et super eum in superfluitate cadunt, illum debilem faciunt et occidunt. Calor enim et humiditas (et sanguis et caro propter transgressionem Adae in contraria flegmata in homine mutata sunt. De flegmatis diversitate, Nam ab igneo calore siccum et ab aerea humiditate humidum ac ab aquoso sanguine spumaceum et a terrena carne tepidum flegma trahitur et excitatur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et si aliquod istorum supra modum crescit in bomine, ita quod ab alio non compescitur nec temperatur, bominem perdit et debilitat; si vero unumquidque modum suum recte servat, ita quod etiam sic ab alio temperatur, quod recte mensuram suam babere compellitur, bominem sanum continet et reddit. Cum enim unum in dominio potestatis superexcellit, aliud buic in seryitute subiacet, atque reliqua duo cum livore moderate sub sequuntur, et sic bomo in tranquillitate corporis est.
text_translation: And if any one of these exceeds in the body beyond measure, so that it is not restrained or tempered by another, it causes the person to lose and weaken; but if each one rightly maintains its own measure, so that even thus it is tempered by another, which compels it to have its proper measure, it keeps and makes the person healthy. For when one exceeds in the domain of power, another is subject to this servitude, and the remaining two follow moderately with a tinge of yellow, and thus the person is in the tranquility of the body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De humoribus, Nam cum quatuor sunt bumores, duo praecellentes flegmata dicuntur, et duo subsequentes dicuntur livores. tJnusquisque autem bumor, qui supereminens est, proximum se subsequentem quarta parte et dimidia parte tertiae partis superat; et ille, qui subiectus est, duas partes ac reliquam partem tertiae partis temperat, ne modum suum transeat. Qui enim primus bumor est, hoc modo secnndmu stiperat, qai dtio flegmata dicimtar; et secnndiis tertiiim, ac tertius quartom , qai dno, videlicet tertins et qnartTis, liyores vocantar.
text_translation: On the humors. For since there are four humors, two superior ones are called phlegmata, and two subsequent ones are called livores. Each humor, which is superior, surpasses the next by a quarter and half of a third part; and that which is subordinate tempers two parts and the remaining part of a third part, so that it does not exceed its measure. For indeed the first humor thus surpasses the second, which two are called phlegmata; and the second surpasses the third, and the third surpasses the fourth, which two, namely the third and fourth, are called livores.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et snperiores in habundantia sua minores transeant, et minores liabundantiam illorum sua vacuitate temperant. Et cum homo sic est, in traDquillitate est. Cum autem quilibet hiimor modum suum excedit, homo ille in periculo est. Sed cum aliquis livor praedictorum modum sutmi iniuste transierit, sufflcientes Tires non babet, ut supereminentes sibi humores vincat, nisi aut subsequente livore, si prae- cedens est, instigetur, aut a praecedente, si subsequens est, iuYetur.
text_translation: And the superiors, in their abundance, pass over the inferiors, and the inferiors temper their abundance with their own vacuity. And when a man is thus, he is in tranquillity. But when any humor exceeds its own measure, that man is in danger. However, when any of the aforementioned colors (livors) unjustly exceeds its measure, he does not have sufficient Tires to overcome the superior humors within him, unless either the subsequent color, if it is preceding, instigates, or from the preceding one, if it is subsequent, is helped.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et in quocumque homine huiusmodi livor supra mensuram suam in superfluitate se eztenderit, ceteri humores in illo pacifici esse non possunt, nisi tantum in illis hominibus sit, quos gratia dei infudit aut in fortitudine ut Samspnem aut in sapientia ut Salomonem aut in prophetia ut Iheremiam aut in quibusdam paganis, ut Plato fuit et sibi similes. Et ubi alii praedicti insaniunt, ibi erunt isti per gratiam dei in probitate fortissimi, quia gratia dei permittit eos interdum esse in aliqua vicissi- tudine, ita quod interdum sunt in inflrmitate, interdum in sanitate, interdum in timore, interdum in robore, interdum in tristitia, interdum in laetitia.
text_translation: And in whatever man such a lividity exceeds its measure in superfluity, the other humors cannot be peaceful in him, unless only in those men in whom God's grace has poured forth, or in fortitude as Samson, or in wisdom as Solomon, or in prophecy as Jeremiah, or in certain pagans, as Plato was and those similar to him. And where the others aforementioned are insane, there these will be by God's grace most strong in virtue, because God's grace permits them sometimes to be in some vicissitude, so that sometimes they are in weakness, sometimes in health, sometimes in fear, sometimes in strength, sometimes in sadness, sometimes in joy.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc reparat in eis deus, ita ut, cum sunt infirmi, facit eos sanos, cum timidi, facit eos robustos, cum tristes, facit eos laetos. Unde si in quolibet homine siccum flegma superexcellit humidum et humidum spumam et tepidum, ibi siccum flegma est velut domina, et humidum quasi ancilla, atque spuma et tepidum velut minora et sublatentia et livida seryitia. Et tunc haec duo posteriora secundum vires suas illorum superiorum dO liyor sunt. Et hic homo naturaliter prudens est et iracnndus ac yehemens in operibus suis.
text_translation: And this God repairs in them, so that when they are weak, He makes them healthy, when timid, He makes them strong, when sad, He makes them joyful. Therefore, if in any man dry phlegm exceeds moist and moist foam and warm, there dry phlegm is like a mistress, and moist like a servant, and foam and warm like lesser and hidden and livid serytia. And then these two latter ones according to their forces of those above are grieving. And this man naturally is prudent and irascible and vehement in his works.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et stabilis in his non est, quod siccitas ea consumit et iterum facile in eis exsurgit, ut flamma, quae cito cadit et cito resurgit. £t sanus est et diu yiyit, sed ad plenam senectutem non S pervenit, quia, postquam caro eius ab igne exsiccata faerit, plenum adiutorium de humido non habet. 4 De frenesi. Quod si forte spnma vel tepidum, quae tonc ibi sont attracta, liyor praedictorum flegmatum, scilicet sicci et hnmidii et iure iu quiete esse deberent, modum suum excesserint sicut undai quae supra modam in s aqua movetur: in venenum yertuntur, et ex eis talis tempestas oritur, quod nuUum eorum alii consentit et quod of&cium suum recte non exercent.
text_translation: And it is not stable in these, for dryness consumes them and again easily rises in them, like flame which quickly falls and quickly rises. And he is healthy and lives long, but does not reach full old age, because, after his flesh has become dried by fire, he does not have full help from the moist. On frenzy. If by chance spittle or warmth, which are there attracted, the fluid of the aforementioned phlegms, namely dry and humid, which by right should be in peace, have exceeded their measure, like waves which move beyond measure in water: they turn into poison, and from them such a storm arises, that none of them agrees with others and that they do not perform their duty correctly.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ista praedicta duobus superioribus ita obsunt, quod omnia quatuor sibi repugnant. Et homo ille, qui hanc diversitatem et con trarietatem in corpore suo patitur, freneticus erit, quia, cum interiores humores sibi invicem resistunt, homo ille furit et se ipsum decerpit, nisi funibus ligetur. Et hoc tam diu facit, usque dum praedicta spuma et praedictum tepidum attenuata in rectum ordinem suum redeunt, et langaevus non erit. Si autem humidum superexcellit siccum et sicoum spumam et tepidum, quae etiam tunc illorum subsequens livor sunt: hic homo naturaliter prudens est et stabilis et in his perseverat et sanus in corpore est ac longaevus erit.
text_translation: And these aforementioned things, when they are obstructed by the two upper ones, so that all four are opposed to each other. And that man, who suffers this diversity and contrariety in his body, will be frenetic, because, when the interior humors resist each other, that man rages and tears himself apart, unless he is bound with ropes. And he does this so long until the aforementioned foam and the aforementioned warm (things) are attenuated and return to their proper order, and he will not be long-lived. If, however, the humid surpasses the dry and with it the foam and the warm, which are also then followed by the subsequent lividity: this man is naturally prudent and stable, and perseveres in these things, and is healthy in body and will be long-lived.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: J e contradis. Sed si spuma et tepidum, quae tunc livor humidi et sicci sunt, modum suum transierint, ita quod spuma ascendit et velut feryentem aquosum fu mum facit et quod tepidum stillat, sic in discordia turbinem parantes cervicem hominis flectunt ac dorsum eftis ^ incurvant atque faciunt eum totum contractum, usque dum ab hoc malo cessaverit; sed diu sic vivere potest. De stuilHa, In quo autem siccum transcendit spumam et spuma humidum et tepidum, hic in stultitia interdum est iracundus ac interdum in stultitia laetus.
text_translation: If you contradict. But if the foam and the warm, which then are the liver of the moist and dry, have passed their limit, so that the foam ascends and makes as it were a fermenting watery smoke, and the warm drips, thus generating a whirlwind in discord, they bend the neck of the man and curve his back, making him altogether contracted, until they cease from this evil; but thus he can live long. Concerning foolishness, in which, however, the dry overcomes the foam and the foam the moist and warm, here in foolishness he is sometimes angry and sometimes in foolishness joyful.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et debilis so non est et aliquantum robustus et diu vivere potest, si voluntas dei est. De pardlysi. Quod si humidum et tepidum, quae tmic livor sicci et spumae sunt, modum suum velut periculosns ventus efQaverint, quasi in commotionem ventorum vertuntur atque periculosum sonum faciunt velut sonum tonitrui. Et sonus ille sonat in venas et in meduUas atque in tempora hominis illius. Unde qui haec patitur, paralyticus eiBcitar atque impotens totius corporis sui; et hoc tam dixL, usque dum praedicti liyores cessaverint et in rectum iter Buiim redierint.
text_translation: And the weak is not and somewhat strong and can live long, if the will of God is. On paralysis. Which if humid and warm, which tumid lividities and froth are, their manner as dangerous wind have done, as if turned into commotion of winds and make dangerous sound as sound of thunder. And that sound sounds in veins and in medullas and in temples of that man. Wherefore he who suffers these, paralyticus is excited and impotent of whole body of him; and this so said, until the aforementioned lividities ceased and into right path of him returned.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed diu vivere poterit, si deus permiserit. De bonis morihua. Sed si spuma excedit siccum et siooum humidum et tepidum, tunc homo ille bonos mores liabet et benevolus est ac tener corpore, sed diu non yiyit. De ameniia. Quod si humidum ac tepidum, quae etiam tunc livor spumae et sicci sunt, modum suum transeunt, mox humidum ut rota circumvolvitur atque hominem illum interdum in aquam praecipitat ac interdum lo in ignem, et tepidum in amentiam eum mittit.
text_translation: But he may live long, if God permits. On good manners. But if the foam exceeds the dry and the dry becomes humid and warm, then that man has good manners and is benevolent and has a tender body, but does not live long. On amenia. If the humid and warm, which at that time the bluishness of the foam and the dry are, exceed their measure, soon the humid revolves like a wheel and precipitates that man sometimes into water and sometimes into fire, and the warm sends him into madness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hic homo talis est, quod in ipso scientia evanescit, unde etiam amens efficitur. Nec est ad plenum sanus nec ad plenum infirmus. De insania. Cum vero in aliquo siccum excedit tepidum et tepidum humidum et spumam, quae subsequuntur: hic homo insanus est mente et corpore ac sibi^ met ipsi et aliis hominibus in moribus suis horribilis est ac inutiliter praeceps in omnibus causis suis et aliquantulum sanus, et diu vivere potest. De desperatione. Sed si humidum vel spuma, quae tunc livor sicci et tepidi sunt, modum suum efflaverint, ita quod humidum amarum fumum in illum emittit et quod eum spuma lividum ac lubricum ut testudinem facit, tunc hoc modo parant in eo sonum, velut sonum flantis aquilonis, atque cadunt super cor eiusdem hominis atque super sensus eius, ita quod nec in deo nec in homine nec in uUa creatura spem aut oonfidentiam habere potest.
text_translation: And this man is such that in him knowledge fades, whence he also becomes insane. He is neither fully healthy nor fully sick. On insanity. But when indeed in someone dryness exceeds warmth and warmth exceeds humidity and foam, which follow: this man is insane in mind and body and to himself and to others in his manners is horrible and in all his causes is precipitately useless and somewhat sane, and can live long. On desperation. But if humidity or foam, which then are the bitterness of dryness and warmth, exceed their measure, so that humidity emits bitter smoke into him and that foam makes him livid and slippery as a tortoise, then in this way they prepare in him a sound, like the sound of a howling north wind, and fall upon his heart and upon his senses, so that he cannot have hope or confidence in God or in man or in any creature.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et haec tam diu durant, usque dum praedicti livores ab horrore suo cessaverint. De timidis. Unde melius ilH esset mori quam vivere. Sed tamen diu vivere potest.
