Si autem tepidum superexcellit siccum et siccum humidum et spumam, iste multas pressnras in moribus suis in semetipso habet et iram ac tristitiam ac interdum laetitiam, quorum tamen nullum ad perfectum ducit, quia in his omnibus timidus est, ut Tinda in aqua, quoniam haec cuncta in semetipso timet.
text_translation: And these endure so long, until the aforementioned lividness ceases from its own horror. Concerning the timid. Hence, it would be better for them to die than to live. Yet nevertheless, they can live long. If, however, the tepid exceeds the dry and the dry the humid and the foam, this one has many pressures in his own habits and anger and sadness and sometimes joy, none of which leads to perfection, because in all these things he is timid, like Tinda in water, because he fears all these things within himself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et aliqui istorum diu viyunt, sed tamen maior numerus eorum cito in mortem deficit. Be mutis. Quod si humidum aut spuma, quae etiam tunc ibi sunt attractus liyor praedictorum flegmatum, scilicet tepidi et sicci, et iure in quiete esse deberent, modum saum excesserint: humidum velut ferventem aquosum fumum parat, et spuma obligat et strangulat rationalitatem in homine, ita quod loqui non potest sed quod obmutescit. Sed quoniam ille exterius loqui non valet, tanto sapientior interius in anima est, et quia etiam rationalitas in ipso exterius ligata est| scientia eius in ipso liquitur et sentit.
text_translation: And some of these live long, but nevertheless a greater number of them quickly falls into death. They are mute. For if the humid or the foam, which are also present there, are attracted to the aforementioned phlegmatic humors, namely the warm and dry ones, and if they have exceeded their proper measure, which should be in rest: the humid prepares as if a fervent watery smoke, and the foam binds and strangles the rationality in man, so that he cannot speak but becomes mute. Yet because he cannot speak externally, so much the more wise is he internally in the soul, and because the rationality in him is externally bound, his knowledge is liquid and felt within him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hic homo malus non est, sed et sanus in corpore et diu yiyit. De bonitate, Sed cum in aliquo homine humidum superexcellit spumam et spuma siccum et tepidum, quae sequuntur, tunc haec duo subsequentia secundum vires suas illorum duorum praecedentium, scilicet humidi et spumae, livor sunt, et iste homo naturaliter valde bonos mores habet et laetus est, et caro eius crescit, nec facile irasci potest nec amarus est; sed inflrmus est et longacTus non erit, quod siccitas in eo modica est.
text_translation: And this man is not bad, but is healthy in body and lives long. Of goodness, but when in a certain man the humid exceeds the foam and the foam exceeds the dry and warm, which follow, then these two subsequent ones according to the forces of those two preceding, namely the humid and the foam, are livor. And this man naturally has very good manners and is joyful, and his flesh grows, and he cannot easily become angry nor is he bitter; but he is weak and will not be long-lived, because the dryness in him is moderate.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ccmcrosis, Si vero siccum vel tepidum, quae tunc livor humidi et spumae sunt, modum suum transierint, eructum cum sonitu ac singultum faciunt in homine atque parant in illo cancrum et faciunt, quod illum vermes comedunt, atque faciunt carnes corporis illius in informis ulceribus exturgescere, ita quod etiam hoc modo in inturgente tumore brachium eius vel pes ipsius maior erit quam alter. Et hoc tam diu faciunt, dum cessaverint ab hac peste, unde diu non potest vivere.
text_translation: De coccrosis, Si vero siccum vel tepidum, quae tunc livor humidi et spumae sunt, modum suum transierint, eructum cum sonitu ac singultum faciunt in homine atque parant in illo cancrum et faciunt, quod illum vermes comedunt, atque faciunt carnes corporis illius in informis ulceribus exturgescere, ita quod etiam hoc modo in inturgente tumore brachium eius vel pes ipsius maior erit quam alter. Et hoc tam diu faciunt, dum cessaverint ab hac peste, unde diu non potest vivere.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De podagra. Quod si spuma transgreditur humidum et humidum siccum et tepidum, homo ille bonam scientiam habet, sed praeceps et nimis vehemens in scientia est, ita quod scientiam suam in diversa spargit ut paleam, quae a vento spargitur, et dominium appetit super alios habere. S Et sanus in corpore est, praeter quod facile in cruribus iDfirmatur et quod podagram facile attrahit; sed diu vivere potest, si deo ita placuerit. Qui se interficiunt. Sed si siccum aut tepidum, quae tunc liyor sunt spumae et humidi, cursum suum excesserint, tunc siccum supergreditur spumam et humidum atque complicat ea, et tepidum facit ferventem aquosum fumum in homine, ita quod homo ille se ipsum in mortem praecipitat, nisi aut a deo aut ab hominibus prohibeatur.
text_translation: De podagra. Quod si spuma transgreditur humidum et siccum et tepidum, homo ille bonam scientiam habet, sed praeceps et nimis vehemens in scientia est, ita quod scientiam suam in diversa spargit ut paleam, quae a vento spargitur, et dominium appetit super alios habere. Sed sanus in corpore est, praeter quod facile in cruribus infirmitur et quod podagram facile attrahit; sed diu vivere potest, si deo ita placuerit. Qui se interficiunt. Sed si siccum aut tepidum, quae tunc liquor sunt spumae et humidi, cursum suum excesserint, tunc siccum supergreditur spumam et humidum atque complicat ea, et tepidum facit ferventem aquosum fumum in homine, ita quod homo ille se ipsum in mortem praecipitat, nisi aut a deo aut ab hominibus prohibeatur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: !Et iste nec ad plenum sanus nec ad plenum infirmus est, sed- est ita inter utrumque. Et diu vivere potest, si custodiam habet. De guUa. In quo autem humidum siccum excedit et lo tepidum siccum et spumam, quae restant hic, adversa pati potest apud se ipsum et apud alios homines et tristis in mente sua est et magnam iram non habet, sed utilis in moribus suis est; sed nec valde infirmus est, praeter quod aliquando a peste, quae dicitur gutta, fatigatur; et lon- gaevus erit.
text_translation: !Et iste nec ad plenum sanus nec ad plenum infirmus est, sed est ita inter utrumque. Et diu vivere potest, si custodiam habet. De gutta. In quo autem humidum siccum excedit et et tepidum siccum et spumam, quae restant hic, adversa pati potest apud se ipsum et apud alios homines et tristis in mente sua est et magnam iram non habet, sed utilis in moribus suis est; sed nec valde infirmus est, praeter quod aliquando a peste, quae dicitur gutta, fatigatur; et longaevus erit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De instabUitate. Si autem siccum vel spuma, quae tunc livor sunt humidi ac tepidi, modum suum transeunt, siccum se sursum tendit in illo, ut herba illa, quae dicitur winda, et spuma parat in eo amarum ructum, atque sic faciunt pariter in ipso inordinatum cursum, ita quod ubique discurreret ac se totum discerperet, si permitteretur. Et istud tam diu durat in illo, dum isti livores in ipso cessaverint. Sed diu vivere potest, si deus et homines ipsum custodierint.
text_translation: On instability. If, however, the dry or the foam, which then are the lividities of the wet and warm, exceed their measure, the dry tends upward in it, like that herb which is called winda, and the foam prepares in it a bitter belching, and thus they make together in it a disordered course, so that it would run everywhere and scatter itself entirely, if permitted. And this lasts so long in it until these lividities cease in it. But it can live long, if God and men guard it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De iracundis. Quod si tepidimi transcendit humidum et humidum siccum et spumam, hic homo astutus est et pacem fugit et discordiam atque certamina amat; et aridus in corpore est, sed gulosus in cibo; nec sanitatem habet, sed tamen ex hoc in lecto non procumbit, sed ita ambulat; et diu vivere potest, sed ad perfectam senectutem non pervenit, quia, antequam ad senectutem perveniat, (moritur. De syncopi, Si autem siccum vel spuma, quae tunc livor sunt tepidi et humidi, modum suum transgrediuntur, siccum flammam suam exaltat, ita quod homo ille gulosus erit in crapula, et spuma facit eum cupidum escarum.
text_translation: On the irascible. If the warm exceeds the humid and the humid the dry and the foam, then this man is cunning and flees peace and loves discord and contests; and he is dry in the body, but gluttonous in food; he does not have health, yet from this he does not lie down on the bed, but walks thus; and he can live long, but does not reach perfect old age, because before reaching old age, (he dies). On syncope. If, however, the dry or the foam, which then are the bluish discoloration of the warm and the humid, exceed their measure, the dry elevates its flame, so that that man will be gluttonous in drunkenness, and the foam makes him desirous of food.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde ille oircuinvolyitur interdum ut rota, ita quod cadit interdum ut mortuu& Sed haec pestis spiritum illius apud se habet| ne moriatur, usque dum cessayerit. Ei; iste infirmus est et ad perfectum senectutem non pervenit. De instabUitate, Cum vero in aliquo spuma ezcellit tepidum et tepidum siccum et humidum, quae illud tunc in hoc modo ut attractus livor subsequuntur, quemadmodum et de ceteris supra scriptum est: tunc hic homo aliquando ineptam laetitiam et ineptam tristitiam ac aliquando in eptam iram habet, sed benevolus est; et foetidum ac sordidum flegma atque indignum odoratum et gustum in se patitur; nec sapiens est, et raro pervenit ad senectutem.
text_translation: Therefore, he is wound around sometimes as a wheel, so that he falls sometimes as the dead. But this plague has the spirit of him with itself, so that he does not die, until it ceases. He, this one is weak and does not reach perfect old age. On instability, When indeed in someone foam exceeds warm and warm dry and humid, which then in this manner as drawn pallor follow, as also from the rest above written: then this man sometimes has inappropriate joy and inappropriate sadness and sometimes inappropriate anger, but is kind; and he suffers in himself fetid and dirty phlegm and unworthy odorated and taste; and he is not wise, and rarely reaches old age.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e obsessis, Quod si siccum vel humidum, quae tunc ibi sunt attractus livor praedictorum flegmatum spumae et tepidi, et iure in quiete esse deberent, modum suum excesserint: ibi evanescit et deficit sientia animae illius et gustus et sensus eius, ac tunc aerei spiritus veniunt ac hominem illum impediunt et haeresim in eo parant ac eum circumvallant, quoniam scientia animae ipsius obdormi vit; atque hoc modo periclitatur, nisi deus depellat eos. De severiiate.
text_translation: If the dry or moist, which then are there, attract the lividity of the aforementioned phlegms' foam and warmth, which rightly should be in rest and should not exceed their measure: there the knowledge of that soul and its taste and senses fade and fail; and then the aerial spirits come and hinder that man, preparing heresy within him and surrounding him, because the knowledge of his own soul has fallen asleep in life; and thus he is in peril, unless God drives them away. On severity.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: IJnde idem in semetipso arescit, et sic diu vivere non valet. Si autem tepidum superexcellit spumam et spuma reliqua duo flegmata, videlicet siccum et humidum, quae tunc in livore secundum vires suas ei obiecta sunt: ille homo durus est nec ulli parcit et mali sciscitator et amarus in moribus suis, nec uUa sufflciunt ei; sed crassus est et sanus, sed diu vivere non potest. Item de frenesi. Sed si forte siccum vel humidum, quae tunc ibi sunt attractus livor praedictorum flegmatum^ videlicet tepidi et spumae, et iure in quiete esse deberent^ modum suum excesseriDt: ibi siccus livor supra modum suum ascendit et intorquet se in hominem atque fugat spiritum eius quasi ad momentum, et humidus livor parat in eo errores, ita quod se discerpit et mala ac insana verba emittit; sed et iracundus est et malus atque freneticus in cerebro et inquietus, atque raro ad senectam pervenit.
text_translation: From this, the same thing withers within himself, and thus cannot live long. If, however, the warm exceeds the foam and the remaining foam, the two phlegmas, namely dry and moist, which then in jaundice according to their forces are directed against him: that man is hard and spares no one, and is an evil seeker and bitter in his customs, and no things suffice for him; but he is thick and healthy, yet cannot live long. Also concerning frenzy. But if by chance the dry or the moist, which then are attracted by the jaundice of the aforementioned phlegmas, namely the warm and the foam, and rightly should be in rest, exceed their measure: there the dry jaundice ascends beyond its measure and twists itself into the human and drives away his spirit as to a moment, and the moist jaundice prepares in him errors, so that it tears itself apart and emits evil and insane words; but also he is angry and evil and frenzied in the brain and restless, and rarely reaches old age.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De sanitate. Quod si praedicti humores rectum ordinem sxiiLm et iustam mensuram, ut praefatam est, in bomine servanti ille in quiete et sanitate corporis est; si autem sibi invicem repugnaverint, debilem et infirmum, ut praescriptum est, eum faciunt. Qmd qtiattMr sunt htmores tantum. Ex uno autem bumore aut ex duobus aut ex tribus homo constare non potest, sed ex quatuor, ut invicem temperentur, sicut mundus ex quatuor elementis constat, quae sibi invicem concordant.
text_translation: Of health. That if the aforementioned humors maintain the correct order and proper measure, as previously stated, in the body, then he is in peace and health of the body; but if they mutually repel one another, they make him weak and infirm, as prescribed. That there are four humors only. From one humor or from two or from three a man cannot consist, but from four, so that they temper one another, as the world consists of four elements, which agree with one another.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De dei vindicta. Quae si aliquando propter iudicium dei terrores suos inordinate emiserint, multa pericula et lO mnndo et hominibus inferunt, ita quod ignis est velut lancea, aer ut gladius, aqua ut clipeus et terra ut iacula ad vindictam hominum provocata. Elementa enim subiecta sunt homini, et secundum quod ipsa tanguntur ab operibus hominum, sic officia sua interdum exercent. Nam quando homines se invicem intermiscent cum proeliis, terroribus, odio et invidia ac in contrariis peccatis, tunc evertunt se in alium et in contrarium modum aut caloris aut fri goris aut magnarum effusionum et inundationum.
text_translation: On the vengeance of God. Which, if ever, because of the judgment of God, they have inordinately emitted their terrors, bring many dangers both to the world and to humans, so that fire is like a spear, air as a sword, water as a shield, and earth as darts provoked by human vengeance. For the elements are subject to humans, and according to how they are touched by the works of humans, thus they sometimes perform their functions. For when humans intermingle with one another through battles, terrors, hatred, envy, and contrary sins, then they turn themselves into another and opposite mode, either of heat or cold or of great effusions and inundations.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc est de prima dispositione dei, quia eis sic a deo constitutum 2o est, ut secundum opera hominum aliquando operentur, quoniam ab operibus illorum tanguntur, ubi homo cum eis et in eis operatur. Cum autem homines in recto itinere sunt et moderate et bona et mala operantur, tunc illa exercent officia sua de gratia dei secundum necessitatem hominum. ^ De paenitentia. Sed cum eadem elementa terrores suos, ut dictum est, ad homines emittunt, tunc homines plorent, clament cum suspiriis et lacrimis, ut haec elementa suscipiant et ad sanguinem immaculati agni deferant, et gratia dei succurret eis.
text_translation: And this is concerning the first disposition of God, because thus they are constituted by God, that according to the works of men they sometimes act, because they are touched by those works, where man acts with them and within them. When, however, men are on the right path and moderately do good and bad works, then they exercise their offices by the grace of God according to the necessity of men. On repentance. But when the same elements, as has been said, emit their terrors toward men, then men weep, cry out with sighs and tears, that these elements may receive and carry to the blood of the unblemished lamb, and the grace of God pours forth upon them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De casu Luciferi, Lucifer tanta fortitudine porrectus est a caelo, quod non permittitur a Tartareo loco moveri. Nam si moveri posset, omnia elementa fortitudine sua immutaret, ita quod etiam firmamentum retrorsum incedere faceret et quod solem et lunam et stellas obfoscaret et ^ quod aquis cursum suum retineret et multa contraria in creaturis faceret. Huic adhaeret omnis turba daemonum. quomni qaidam maioris fortitudinis, quidam minoris sunt. Sed qoidam etiam sunt, qui cum hominibus saepius conyersantur et sacra loca minus fugiunt et etiam orucem domini et divina offioia minus abhorrent.
text_translation: On the fall of Lucifer, Lucifer was cast down from heaven with such strength that he is not permitted to be moved from the Tartarean place. For if he could be moved, by his strength he would alter all elements, so that even the firmament would move backward and would cause the sun and moon and stars to be darkened and ^ would retain the course of waters and would cause many contrary things in creatures. To this adheres the whole crowd of demons. For some are of greater strength, some of lesser. But some are also those who more frequently converse with humans and flee sacred places less and also less abhor the prayer of the Lord and divine offices.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hi onmes cum iUo in mundum machinantur. Sed ad magnitudinem illius diabolus est fere tantae fortitudinis et potestatis et malitiae, sicut et ille est quasi aviditas et velut yoluntas ipsius. Et quia ille moveri non permittitur, in mundum quasi Python daemonum mittitur. Nam virtutem deci piendi per simulationem habet et multa alia vitia, ita quod Adam in paradiso seduxit et dominum terrae eum nuncupavit, et flatum Luciferi in Antichristum per fortitudinem illius feret ad concipiendum eum; cuius fortitudo ad locum illum ascendet, a quo diabolus proiectus corruit; ubi zelus domini ignis factus in nigra materia procellarum tantae fortitudinis et amaritudinis est, quod ' saepe in elementis exurit et scindit et voce sua terrores emittit, ita quod diabolus prae terrore istius vires suas aperte exercere non audet, nisi ut far; unde et mendax est.
text_translation: And all these with him in the world scheme. But as to the magnitude of him, the devil is almost as strong, powerful, and malicious as he is, like greed and as the will of him. And because he is not allowed to move, into the world as a Python of demons is sent. For he has the virtue of deceiving through simulation and many other vices, so that Adam in paradise was seduced and called him the lord of the earth, and the breath of Lucifer in Antichrist through his strength will carry to conceive him; whose strength will ascend to that place from which the devil was cast down; where the jealousy of the Lord became fire in black matter of storms of such strength and bitterness, that 'often in elements burns and tears and with his voice emits terrors, so that the devil before this terror dares not openly exercise his powers, unless as far; hence and a liar he is.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Iste zelus etiam in novissimo die elementa exuret et scindet, qui est dei vindicta. Et quod beato lohanni evangelistae prohibuit, ne scriberet voces, quae tonitrua locuta sunt , hoc ideo fa.ctum est, quia omnes terrores et angustias tunc ostendebant, quae homines ante et postea passi sunt et patientur; quoniam, si ea praesciret homo, illa prae infirmitate carnis et prae nimiis horroribus ferre non posset; quoniam homo ea facilius tolerat, dum per tempora et per intervalla temporum contingunt, quam si homo ea una hora praesciret.
text_translation: This zeal will also in the last day burn and tear apart the elements, which is the vengeance of God. And that which the blessed John the Evangelist was prohibited from writing, namely the voices which thundered, was done for this reason, because all terrors and anguishes were then shown, which men suffered before and after and will suffer; because if man knew them beforehand, he would not be able to bear them on account of the weakness of the flesh and on account of excessive horrors; because man more easily endures them when they occur through times and through intervals of times, than if man knew them all in one hour.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ac ideo factum est, quia tonitruum tam terribile est et tantae fortitudinis ac horroris, quia si homo sciret, quid toDitruum esset, illud timeret, ut verum deum prae terrore illius, quoniam vocem suam audit, postponeret. Venti sunt super solem et subtus et circa eum et sua fortitudine ignem eius dissipant et dispergunt, quia si hoc non facerent, sol 1 Apoc. 10, 4. causAe et curae lib. n. tantum ardorem ignis emitteret, quod nec terra nec cetera elementa aut aliae creaturae hoc sufferre possent.
text_translation: And therefore it was made, because thunder is so terrible and of such strength and horror, because if a man knew what thunder was, he would fear it, even to the point of postponing the true God because of the terror of it, since he hears its voice. The winds are above the sun and beneath it and around it, and by their strength they dissipate and scatter its fire, because if they did not do this, the sun would emit such heat of fire that neither the earth nor the other elements nor any other creatures could endure it. 1 Apoc. 10, 4. causes and cures book n.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De furare. Nam si in homine humor unus alios superhabundans rectum ordinem suum et mensuram non tenuerit, homo ille debilis et infirmus erit; si vero duo inordinate pariter in homine exsurrexerint, ille durare non potest, quia aut furit aut omnino in corpore perit, quoniam haec recte mixta non sunt; aut si tres se simul supra modum suum extenderint, hic homo debilitatur et cito morietur. Si autem omnes quatuor inordinate efflaverint, homo ille cito in ictu oculi et in momento ab eis subito occiditur, quoniam ad nullum tempus subsistere posset, sed totus dirumperetur, velut omnia in novissimo die concutitmtur, cum quatuor elementa sibi inyicem repugnant.
text_translation: On madness. For if in a man one humor is superabundant over the others and does not keep its proper order and measure, that man will be weak and feeble; if indeed two humors rise in disorder together in a man, he cannot endure, because either he rages or entirely perishes in the body, because these are not properly mixed; or if three extend themselves together beyond their own measure, this man is weakened and will quickly die. If indeed all four flow out in disorder, that man is quickly killed in the blink of an eye and in a moment by them, because he could not subsist at any time, but would be entirely destroyed, as all things are shaken on the last day, when the four elements mutually repel one another.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De Adae ptasmatione. Deus fecit de limo hominem, is sed yir mutatus est de limo in camem, et ideo est propria causa et dominator creaturae. Atque terram operatur, ut fiructus pariat, et est fortitudo in ossibus, in venis ac in came, et est integro capite ac spissa cute atque fortitudinem suam apud se habet et semen producit sicut sol lumen. Mulier autem mutata non est, quia de came sumpta caro permansit, et ideo datum est ei artificiosum opus manuimi, et quasi aerea est, quoniam infantem in utero portat et eum generat.
text_translation: On the Creation of Adam. God made man from clay, and he was changed from clay into flesh, and therefore he is the proper cause and lord of creation. And the earth works so that it bears fruit, and strength is in the bones, in the veins and in the flesh, and he has his strength within himself and produces semen as the sun produces light. But the woman has not been changed, because the flesh taken from flesh remains, and therefore an artificial work of the hand has been given to her, and she is as if airy, because she carries the infant in the womb and gives birth to him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Atque divisum caput habet et tenuem cutem, scilicet ut infans, quem in ntero portat, aerem habere possit. De conceptu. Tale autem est initium ortus et coagulationis cuiusque hominis. In homine est voluntas, consideratio, potestas et consensus. Voluntas praecedit, quia quisque homo voluntatem habet illud vel illud facere. Et consideratio sequitur et aspicit, utrum causa illa sit conveniens aut inconveniens, sobria an impudica. Tunc potestas sequitur, quae opus perflciendi in se habet, ut iUud perflciat.
text_translation: And it has a divided head and thin skin, namely like an infant, whom the mother carries in the womb, so that air may be able to be present. On conception. Such, however, is the beginning of the origin and coagulation of each human being. In a human being there is will, consideration, power, and consensus. Will precedes, because each human being has will to do this or that. And consideration follows and examines whether that cause is convenient or inconvenient, sober or immodest. Then power follows, which has within itself the work of perfecting, so that it may perfect that.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et consensus sequitur, quia opus non potest perflci, quin consensus laudet, ut consentiat. Istae 9& quatuor vires sunt in ortu hominis. Timc quatuor elementa, quae quatuor humores in homine excitant, cum snperfltdiate et tempestate yenmnti ita qnod igniS| scilicei; siccnm, supra modnm volnntatem accendit; et qnod aer, yidelicet hnmidnm, snpra modnm considerationem movet; et qnod aqna, scilicet spnma, snpra modnm potestatem flnctnare facit; et qnod etiam terra, videlicet tepidnm, snpra modnm consensnm ebnllire facit.
text_translation: And consensus follows, because the work cannot be completed unless consensus praises, so that it consents. These four powers are in the origin of man. Then the four elements, which excite the four humors in man, with superfluity and tempest of the wind, so that fire, namely, thus indeed, beyond measure, ignites the will; and that air, namely, moist, beyond measure, moves consideration; and that water, namely, semen, beyond measure, causes power to flow; and that even earth, namely, warm, beyond measure, causes consensus to boil.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et baec omnia de nimietate snperflnitatis qnasi tempestatem facinnt et de sangnine veDenosam spnmam, qnod semen est, edncnnt, qnod, cnm in locnm snum cadit, sangnis mnlieris illi se coninngit, et ex boc sangninens erit. Prima enim inceptio bominis per delectationem, qnam serpens in pomo primo bomini insnflFlavit, exoritnr, qnia tnnc sangnis viri ex delectatione concntitnr. Unde et idem sangnis frigidam spnmam mnlieri immittit, qnae ex calore maternae camis coagnlatnr et in saDguineam formam extenditur; ac eadem spuma in eodem calore sic permanens postmodnm a sndore sicci cibomm matris in spissitudinem parvae bumanae staturae crescit, quousqne scriptura creatoris, qnae bominem formavit, eandem spissitndinem bumanae statnrae totam perfundit, quemadmodnm faber elatnm vas suum format.
text_translation: And all these things, through the superfluousness, as it were, make a tempest, and from the blood they produce a venomous foam, which is the semen, and they consume it, which, when it falls into its own place, the blood of the woman binds itself to it, and from this will be blood. For indeed the first beginning of man through delight, which the serpent first inspired into the man in the apple, arises, because then the blood of the man is bound through delight. Hence the same blood sends a cold foam into the woman, which is coagulated from the heat of the mother's garment and extended into a blood form; and the same foam, remaining thus in the same heat, afterwards from the dry taste of the mother's food grows into the thickness of a small human stature, until the writing of the creator, which formed the man, pours the same thickness of human stature entirely, as a craftsman forms his vessel.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in transgressione Adae fortitudo viri in genitali membro versus est in venenosam spumam, atque mulieris sanguis versus est in contrariam effusionem. De forti enim et de recta natnra viri sangnis eins semen babet, quia de terra caro factus est. Sed de recta natura mulieris sanguis eius, quia debilis et tenuis est, semen non babet, sed tantum tennem et parvam spumam emittit, quoniam de dnobus modis terrae et carnis non est, ut vir, sed tantum de came viri sumpta est; et ideo debilis et fragilis est et vas viri est.
text_translation: For in the transgression of Adam, the strength of the man in the genital member is turned into venomous foam, and the woman's blood is turned into a contrary effusion. For indeed, from the strong and right nature of the man, his blood has semen, because from the earth was flesh made. But from the right nature of the woman, her blood, because it is weak and thin, has no semen, but only emits a thin and small foam, because it is not from two modes of earth and flesh as the man, but only from flesh taken from the man; and therefore it is weak and fragile, and it is the vessel of the man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et de amore viri sanguis eius concutitur et velut spumam, sed magis sanguineam quam albam, ad semen viri emittit, quae se illi coniungit et qnae illud calidum et sanguineum facit et confortat. Nam postquam in locum suum ceciderit et iacuerit, friges cit. Et tam diu quasi venenosa spuma est, nsque dum ignis, videlicet calor, illnd calefacit, et dum aer, videlicet spiramen, illud exsiccat, et dnm aqna, scilicet fluor, illi puram liiimiditatem admittit, et usque dum terra, scilicet cutecula, illud constringit.
text_translation: And concerning the love of a man, his blood is disturbed and emits, as if spumy, but more blood than white, towards the man's semen, which unites with it and which makes it warm and bloody and comforts it. For after it has fallen into its place and lain down, it becomes cold. And as long as it is like venomous spumy, until fire, that is, heat, warms it, and until air, that is, spirit, dries it, and until water, namely flux, admits pure humidity to it, and until earth, namely cuticula, tightens it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc sic erit sanguineus, id est non totum sanguis, sed aliquantulum sanguine permixtum. £it quatuor humores, quos homo a quatuor elementis trahit, ita circa idem semen moderate et temperate manent, usque dum quasi caro coaguletur et confirmetur, ita quod forma hominis in eo figurari potest. Et tunc ez eo figuratur ut pictura forma hominis, et medulla ac yenae in totam formam illam se ut fila infigunt et per eam se totam diyidunt et yelut nodos compaginis in eo ubique faciunt, sed et yelut membrana oyi meduUam eius cirGumdant, quae postea in ossa erit.
text_translation: And then thus it will be sanguineous, that is, not entirely blood, but somewhat mixed with blood. Sunt four humors, which man draws from four elements, thus around the same semen they remain moderately and temperately, until as if flesh coagulates and is confirmed, so that the form of man in it can be shaped. And then from it the form of man is shaped as a picture, and marrow and veins in that entire form insert themselves as threads, and through it they divide themselves entirely, and like knots of ligaments they make everywhere in it, but also like a membrane they surround the marrow of it, which later will be in bones.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc ita tam plane et tam aperte figuratur ut forma aliqua, quam pictor aperte in formam aliquam figurat. Et ubicumque membra futura erunt, ibi diyisiones usque ad cuteculam, quae ea adhuc simul retinet, scinduntur, yelut limus, qui ad ardorem solis scinditur, ac tunc sicca caro fit siccata de yeneno, atque recta caro recto sanguine perfunditur. De calore «nim matris in pinguedinem coagulationis proficit nondum yiyens, sed in praefato calore persistens, et hoc opus in uno mense est, scilicet quo luna crescit et deficit; et sic etiam praedicta coagulatio crescit et pinguescit, quoniam, si in hac pinguedine non yiyificaretur, tota siccaretur, de qua siccitate mater multum tarda et dolens efficeretur.
text_translation: And then thus so plainly and so openly is formed as a certain form, which a painter openly forms into a certain form. And wherever the future members will be, there divisions down to the cuticle, which still retains them, are split, like mud which is split by the heat of the sun, and then dry flesh becomes dried from venom, and straight flesh is flooded with straight blood. Of the heat, "nim matris in pinguedinem coagulationis proficit nondum yiyens, but persisting in the aforementioned heat, and this work is in one month, namely that in which the moon grows and wanes; and thus also the aforementioned coagulation grows and becomes fat, because, if in this fatness it were not vivified, the whole would be dried up, from which dryness the mother would become greatly delayed and sorrowful.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De animae infusione. Deinde. sicnt deus yult et sicut fieri disposuit, yenit spiraculum yitae et formam illam matre nesdente tangit ut yehemens calidus yentus, yelut yentus, qui in parietem cum sono fiat, ac se infundit et infligit in omnes compages membrorum formae illius. Et sic omnes diyisiones membrorum eiusdem formae a se suayiter ita diyiduntur, ut flores ad calorem solis se diyidunt. Sed et adhuc tanta imbecillitas in eadem forma est, quod moyeri non potest, sed iacet, ut dormiat, et modice spirat.
text_translation: On the infusion of the soul. Then, thus does God will and thus has disposed to be made, the breath of life comes and touches that form, as a vehement hot wind, like the wind which, when it strikes a wall with sound, infuses itself and strikes into all the compositions of the members of that form. And thus all the divisions of the members of that same form are divided from themselves in such a way that flowers divide themselves toward the heat of the sun. But also, there is such weakness in that same form that it cannot move, but lies as if sleeping, and breathes slightly.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et spiritus totam formam illam pertransit eam replendo et confirmando in medalla et in yenis, ita quod tunc plus crescit, quam prius faceret, nsque dum ossa super meduUam extenduntur et dum yenae tantae fortitudinis fiunt, quod sanguinem retinere possunt. Et tunc infans matre sentiente movetur, quasi repente exoitetur, ac deinde semper mobilis permanet. Nam, ut praedictum est, Yivens yentus, qui anima est, per Yoluntatem omnipotentis dei figuram istam intrat et eam firmat vitalemque facit ac in ipsa undique circait, velut vermis, qui sericum texit, in quo ut in domo obtegitur et includitur.
text_translation: And the spirit passes through that entire form, filling and strengthening it in the medulla and in the veins, so that then it grows more than it previously did, until the bones extend over the medulla and until the veins become so strong that they can retain the blood. And then the infant, with the mother sensing it, moves, as if suddenly it were exsiled, and afterward always remains mobile. For, as was previously said, the living wind, which is the soul, enters this form by the will of the almighty God and establishes it, making it vital, and surrounds it entirely, like a worm that weaves silk, in which it is covered and enclosed as in a house.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Atque sic in hac figura sentit, abi anima dividi, incurvari et inclinari potest, et etiam omnia loca venarum considerat et primum ipsas exsiccat in modum cavae harundinis ac eas cami inflgit, quam in ruborem sanguinis calore ignis sui convertit, quod anima ignis est; unde et spiramine suo totam figuram illam perfandit, sicut et tota domus per ignem, qui in ipsa fit, illuminatur, et omnes venae loca sua, quibus contmentur, retinent, ut etiam terra cum aquis continetur.
text_translation: And thus in this figure she feels, from the soul being divided, bent, and inclined, and also considers all the places of the veins, and first dries them out in the manner of hollow reeds and inflates them, which she converts into the redness of blood through the heat of her own fire, which is the soul of fire; whence also by her breath she impregnates the entire figure with fragrance, just as the whole house is illuminated by fire which is made within it, and all the veins retain their places, by which they contain, as also the earth retains water with its waters.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Anima autem omnibus istis praedictis ut vivus aer sanguinem fluere faciens est, ac camem cum sanguineo livore in sedula humiditate contrahit, sicut esca per ignem in oUa coquitur, et ossa confortat easque in cames figit, quatinus eaedem cames per illa sustineantur, ne deflciant, quemadmodum homo domum suam lignis aedificat, ne cadendo destruatur. Et istud secundus mensis est, in quo praedicta figura in defectum et profectum lunae per animam confirmatur.
text_translation: The soul, however, among all these aforementioned things, is like a living air causing blood to flow, and it draws together the camem with sanguineous blackness in diligent humidity, just as food is cooked in a pot by fire, and it strengthens the bones and fixes them in the flesh, so that these same flesh may be supported by them, lest they fail, as a man builds his house with wood, lest it fall and be destroyed. And this is the second month, in which the aforementioned figure is confirmed through the soul in the defect and progress of the moon.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Anima, quod in venis est, et cum sanguine carnes et ossa complet, quod profectum lunae significat. Quod autem nondum se movere potest, hoc defectum lunae liesignat. Sic anima in figura ista residet onmiaque loca, cum quibus ipsa opera faciet, inspicit, quoniam se totam eandem figuram moturam sentit, sicut rota molendinum circuire facit. Et hoc secundum cursum solis in circulo suo incipit. Nam ut sol in mane exoritur, et ut cursum suum a mane in tertiam, a tertia in sextam, a sexta in nonam, a nona in vespemm protendit, sic et ipsa in oculis sedet, ut illos per fenestras videndo lucem praeparet, et etiam in pectore, ut cogitationibus circuire illam faciat, ac in corde, quatinus illam cum scientia volare ostendat, in ventre qnoqne omuia yiscera eius velat rete connectens eaqne in cavas cavemas confirmans, in qnibns cibi servantnr, per qnos praedicta figura pascitnr; qnos dentes molunt, qni sine mednlla velnt radix per hnmidnm ignem animae sine aqna consiti snnt , ita qnod idem cibi ventri immittnntnr, in qno nsqne ad temperatnm tempns manent.
text_translation: The soul, which is in the veins, and with blood completes flesh and bones, which signifies the moon's movement. That which is not yet able to move, this signifies the moon's defect. Thus the soul resides in this figure and inspects all places where it will perform its work, feeling that it will move the same figure entirely, as the wheel of the mill circulates. And this begins according to the course of the sun in its circle. For as the sun rises in the morning, and as its course extends from morning to the third hour, from the third to the sixth, from the sixth to the ninth, from the ninth to evening, so also it sits in the eyes, preparing light through the windows by seeing, and also in the chest, causing it to circulate through thoughts, and in the heart, so that it may show it flying with knowledge, in the stomach also, covering all its viscera with a net connecting them, and confirming them in the cavities where food is preserved, through which the aforementioned figure is fed; which teeth grind, which without marrow, roots are sustained by the fire of the soul without water, so that the same food is sent into the stomach, where they remain for a time according to the temperate time.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Anima qnoqne snccnm cibi in cerebmm, in cor, in folia pulmonis, in iecnr et in onmes venas dividit ac fortitndine caloris stomachnm et yiscera illa, qnae cibnm portant, confortat, ne difflnant, sed qnod in loco sno consistant. Et txinc etiam in cmra se decUnat, igneo calore sno illa confirmando ac onmia apposita aediiicia cmmm snstinendo, qnemadmodnm domns colnmnis confirmatnr et snstentatnr. Ac deinde etiam pedes et divisiones pednm, sicnt dens illos scribendo creavit, intrat, qnoniam sicnt pedes colnmnamm ipsas colnnmas, sic et pedes hominis cmra ipsins snstinent.
text_translation: The soul divides the blood into the brain, into the heart, into the leaves of the lungs, into the liver and into all veins, and strengthens the stomach and the viscera that carry food with the strength of heat, so that they do not diffuse, but remain in their own place. And also in the heart it lies down, confirming them with its fiery heat and sustaining all the buildings attached to them, so that the house of the columns is confirmed and sustained. And then also the feet and the divisions of the feet, as if the teeth were written, enter, because the feet support the columns themselves, and the feet of man support the heart itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ipsa enim anima flgnram corporis firmat, vivificat et illuminat, ideo qnod etiam in corpore flammans ignis est, sicnt et ignis in tota domo et in onmibns angnlis Incet et sicnt totam domtim illnminat. XJnde et homo velnt sol et Inna est, qnia sicnt prima hora diei diem ostendit, ita et anima per fenestras ocnlomm Inmen videndo aspicit, et nt tertia hora diei eandem animam dilatat, sic et anima cogitationes in pectore mnltiplicat; et qnemadmodnm etiam sol in sexta hora fortiter ardet ac omnia in potestate sna habet, ita et anima in corde plnrima sciens in operibns snis mnlta ostendit; et sicnt sol in nona in qnandam geliditatem se inclinat, (qnia opns diei iam perfecit, ita in anima est, cnm ipsa in ventre operatnr cum cibis, qnibns homo pascitnr, qni per solem radicant, crescnnt, perflcinntnr.
text_translation: The soul indeed establishes, vivifies, and illuminates the figure of the body, because even in the body there is a burning fire, just as fire in the entire house and in all its angles ignites and thus illuminates the whole house. Hence, man is like the sun and the moon, because just as the first hour of the day shows the day, so also the soul through the windows of the eyes sees and perceives; and just as the third hour of the day expands the same soul, so also the soul multiplies thoughts in the breast; and just as the sun in the sixth hour burns strongly and has all things in its power, so also the soul in the heart, knowing many things, shows many things in its works; and just as the sun in the ninth hour inclines toward some coldness (because the work of the day is already completed), so in the soul it is when it operates in the belly with foods, by which man is fed, which through the sun root, grow, and are perfected.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Girca vespernm antem sol snb terram se abscondit, et nox apparet, qnia anima in crnribns manet, qnae totmn hominem continent, et circa horam illam opere fessns et cibo vacnatns homo dormitat, dnm itemm sol oriatnr. Anima enim snccnm cibomm toto corpori recte dividit et reliqnnm emittit, qnemadmodnm vinnm a faece se £6ARDIS emundat, nec corpas in ullo loco ab anima vacuum est, quia ipsa calore suo totum corpus perfundit. Homo namque ex quatuor elementis creatus constat, quorum duo spiritalia, duo camalia sunt, ignis scilicet et aer spiritalia, aqua vero et terra carnalia.
text_translation: Circa vespernum, ante sol sub terram se abscondit, et nox apparet, quia anima in crinibus manet, quae totum hominem continent, et circa horam illam opere fessus et cibo vacuatus homo dormitat, dum item sol orietur. Anima enim succum cibum toto corpori recte dividit et reliquum emittit, quemadmodum vinum a faece se sacris emundat, nec corpus in ullo loco ab anima vacuum est, quia ipsa calore suo totum corpus perfundit. Homo namque ex quatuor elementis creatus constat, quorum duo spiritalia, duo carnalia sunt, ignis scilicet et aer spiritalia, aqua vero et terra carnalia.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et haec quatnor in homine in unum se coniungunt, ipsum quoque coquunt, ut sanguineus et camalis cum omnibus appendiciis suis fiat. Sed ignis et aqua sibi inyicem contraria sunt nec in unum habitare possunt, unde oportet, ut uterqae eorum per magistrum regatur. Aqua etiam igni resistit, ne ulterius, quam positus est, ardendo procedat, et ignis aquam constringit, ne ultra calorem siccitatis plus, quam iustum sit, fluat, et istae duae vires ignis et aquae totam terram cum aere nubium temperant, ut subsistant et non l^ deficiant.
text_translation: And these four in man join together into one, also cooking it, so that it becomes sanguineous and camalis with all its appendages. But fire and water are contrary to each other and cannot dwell together, whence it is necessary that each of them be governed by the master. Water also resists fire, so that it does not proceed further in burning than it is placed, and fire constricts water, so that it does not flow with more heat of dryness than is just. And these two forces, fire and water, temper the entire earth with the air of clouds, so that they may subsist and not fail.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic quoque in sanguine hominis est, qui de calore ignis rubet et de aqua aquosus est, quia, si sanguis in calore suo aquosus non esset, nequaquam flueret, sed aresceret et quasi squama caderet. Si etiam terra aquosa non esset, sicut stipula spargeretur, nec una integra creaao tura esse posset. Unde ad istas duas vires reliqua creatura pendet, et absque istis uulla forma formaretur, et si haec duo in unum ita coniuncta non essent, ceterae formae non subsisterent.
text_translation: Thus also in the blood of man is it, who reddens from the heat of fire and is watery from water, because, if blood were not watery in its heat, it would not flow at all, but would dry up and fall as if scales. If also the earth were not watery, it would be scattered like straw, and no complete creature could exist. Hence, all other creatures depend upon these two forces, and without these, no form could be formed, and if these two were not thus united into one, other forms would not endure.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deus etenim sic hominem de terrae creayit limo, quod ipse cum spiramine animae, terra aquosa, ignea et aerea consistit, et ita anima hominem cum quatuor elementis movet, quia per terram formata flgura cum digito dei consistit et per aquam miscetur, per aerem movetur atque per ignem coquitur. Corpus autem habet gustum, gustus delectationem, anima vero desiderium, desi derium autem voluntatem. Et anima est ut ignis, corpus vero ut aqua, et simul sunt.
text_translation: God indeed thus created man from the earth's clay, so that he with the spirit of the soul consists of watery, fiery, and airy earth, and thus the soul moves man with the four elements, because the figure formed by earth with the finger of God consists, and is mixed by water, moved by air, and cooked by fire. The body indeed has taste, taste has delight, but the soul has desire, and desire has will. And the soul is like fire, the body indeed like water, and they are together.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic homo opus dei est. Ideo etiam quodque opus, quod corpus postulat, anima in ipso operatur, ideoque anima operatrix est et corpus desiderat, animaque potentior corpore est, quoniam ipsa desiderium ipsius perflcit, nec ipsa possibilitatem sine corpore hominis haberet, quae hominem, qui opus dei est, pertransit et.movet; et ipsa absque corpore non esset, nec corpus cnin came et sanguine non nisi per animam xELOveretur; anima autem sine corpore Yiyere potest, nequaqiiam autem corpus sine anima yiyit, quae post novissimum diem indumentum suum reposcit et illud secundum desiderium suum tunc regit.
text_translation: Thus, man is the work of God. Therefore, also, whatever work the body demands, the soul operates within it, and thus the soul is the operative one and the body desires, and the soul is more powerful than the body, because it perfects its own desire, nor would it have the possibility without the human body, which passes through and moves the man, who is the work of God; and the soul would not exist without the body, nor would the body, which is not flesh and blood unless through the soul is sustained; the soul, however, can live without the body, but the body does not live without the soul, which after the last day requests its garment and then rules according to its own desire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic quoque homo in duabus xiatoris est, scilicet corpus et anima, ut etiam caro non sine sanguine nec sanguis sine came est, quae tamen in natura sua dissimiles sunt, et sicut anima nullatenus sine €K rpore sit et deus nullo modo sine opere est. Opus enim suum ante aevum et in aevo aetemaliter in ipso latuity quemadmodum anima invisibiliter in corpore latet. Anima autem sine corpore vivit, et post novissimum diem indumentum suum a deo desiderat, ut sibi illud attrahat; et ita etiam deus, qui ante aevum et in aevo vita sine principio fiiit, in constituto tempore indumentum suum, quod aetemaliter in ipso latuit, sibi attraxit.
text_translation: Thus also man is in two natures, namely body and soul, just as flesh is not without blood nor blood without flesh, which, however, in their nature are dissimilar, and just as soul in no way is without body and God in no way is without work. For his work indeed existed before the age and in the age eternally in him, just as the soul invisibly remains hidden in the body. However, the soul without the body lives, and after the last day desires its garment from God, that it may draw it to itself; and thus also God, who before the age and in the age was life without beginning, in the appointed time drew to itself its garment, which eternally remained hidden in him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc modo deus et homo unum sunt ut anima et corpus, quoniam deus hominem ad imaginem et ad similitudinem suam fecit. Ut autem quaeque res umbram habet, sic etiam homo umbra dei est et umbra ostensio facturae est et homo omnipotentis dei in omnibus miraculis suis ostensio est, ipseque umbra est, quia initium habet. Deus autem nec initium nec finem habet. Unde onmis caelestis harmonia speculum divinitatis est, et homo speculum onmium miraculomm est dei.
text_translation: And in this way, God and man are one as soul and body, because God made man in His image and in His likeness. Just as every thing has a shadow, so also man is the shadow of God, and the shadow is the manifestation of the creation, and man is the manifestation of the omnipotent God in all His miracles, he himself is a shadow, because he has a beginning. God, however, has neither beginning nor end. Therefore, every celestial harmony is a mirror of divinity, and man is the mirror of all miracles of God.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De generatione. Tunc etiam fiegmata et humores in eo cresctmt, secundum quod natura seminis eius fuit, quoniam secundum quod triticum aut siligo aut ordeum seminatur, secundum hoc etiam et grana naturaliter proferunt. Et sicut superflui humores in homine tam diu ao ad multam insaniam et in multa pericula vertuntur, usque dum sedati cessent et usque dum in rectam mensuram suam redeant, sic etiam infans de prima seminatione seminis in multas vicissitudines hac et illac vertitur, antequam spiritus se in eo moveat et anteqiiam in eo recte operetur.
text_translation: On generation. Then also the flegmata and humores in it grow, according to the nature of its seminis, because according to how triticum or siligo or ordeum is sown, according to this also the grana naturally produce. And just as superflui humores in man so long as they remain lead to much insaniam and many pericula, until they are sedati and cease and until they return to their rectam mensuram suam, so also the infans from the first seminatione of the seminis undergoes many vicissitudines here and there, before the spiritus moves within it and before it operates rightly.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De secimdina. Nunc etiam, postquam semen hominis Hildegardifl oansae et carae ed. Kaiser. recte in locmn suum cedderit, ita quod etiam in formam hominis figurari debet, tunc etiam de menstruo sanguine mulieris pellicula velut vasculum circa eandem formam crescit, quae ipsam comprehendit et circumdat, ne hac eft 5illac moveatur aut cadat, quia ille coagulatus sanguis ibi congregatur, ita quod eadem forma in medio eius iacet, ut homo in habitaculo domus suae.
text_translation: On the secundina. Now also, after the man's semen, Hildegardis oansae et carae ed. Kaiser. correctly into its place yields, so that it should also be shaped into the form of a human, then also from the menstrual blood of the woman, a membrane grows like a vessel around the same form, which surrounds and encircles her, so that this is not that which moves or falls, because that coagulated blood gathers there, so that the same form lies in the middle of it, like a man in the dwelling of his house.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Atque in eo habet calorem, et adiutorium in eo nutritur de nigro sanguine iecoris mulieris usque ad partum« De rationahilUate. Et infans tam diu in yase illo elausus iacet, usque dum rationalitas in eo plenitudinem suam habens erumpere yult. Itaque amplius claudi et silere non potest nec debet, quia infans in yentre matris clamare non potest. De partu. Sed cum iam partus instat, yas, in quod infans clausus est, scinditur, et yis aetemitatis, quae Eyam de latere Adae educit, mox yeniens adest atque omnes angulos habitaculi corporis mulieris de locis suis eyertit.
text_translation: And in it it has heat, and nourishment is provided in it from the black blood of the liver of the woman until the birth« De rationabilitate. And the infant thus long lies enclosed in that vessel, until reason, having its fullness in it, desires to emerge. Therefore, it can and must no longer be closed and silent, because the infant cannot cry in the womb of the mother. De partu. But when the birth is at hand, the vessel, in which the infant is enclosed, is torn apart, and the power of age, which Eve draws from the side of Adam, soon comes forth and turns out all the angles of the dwelling of the woman's body from their places.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et omnes compagines corporis mulieris fortitudini illi occurrunt et eam suscipiunt ac se aperiunt. Atque ita se continent, dum infans egrediatur, ac deinde iterum replicant se, ut prius fuerunt. Sed et anima infantis, interim dum infans egreditur, sentit yim aetemitatis, quae eam misit, ac interim laeta est. De sensudlUate, Postquam autem infans egressus fuerit, mox yocem fletus emittit, quia tenebras mundi sentit. Nam cum deus animam in humanum corpus mittit, scientia in ea est, quasi dormiat.
text_translation: And all the parts of the woman's body meet the strength and receive it and open themselves. And thus they restrain themselves while the infant is being born, and afterward they again repeat themselves as they were before. But also the soul of the infant, in the meantime while the infant is being born, feels the power of eternity which sent it, and in the meantime is glad. Of the feeling of sorrow, Afterward, however, when the infant has been born, immediately the voice of crying is emitted, because it senses the darkness of the world. For when God sends the soul into the human body, knowledge is in it, as if it sleeps.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed postquam corpus intrayerit, scientia in ipsa excitatur, cum se in carnem et in yenas infligit. De sdentia, Cum yero partus hominis institerit, ita quod diyina yis claustra matemi uteri aperit, tunc infans yirtutem dei ibi sentit, atque scientia in anima eins tunc ad discendum et ad capiendum quaeque ad plenum ex surgit, cum ad haec per optionem et per desiderium excitatur. Nam cum homo quodlibet opus yel quamlibet artem per optionem et per desiderium scire yult, spiritus sanctns rore sno viriditatem scientiae illins perfondit, nnde et discit et capit, quae discere vnlt.
text_translation: But after the body has entered, knowledge is aroused within it, when it inflicts itself upon flesh and veins. Of knowledge, when indeed the birth of man has begun, so that the divine power opens the cloister of the mother's womb, then the infant there senses the virtue of God, and knowledge in its soul then rises up fully to learn and to take in everything, when it is aroused toward these things through choice and through desire. For when a man wants to know any work or any art through choice and through desire, the holy spirit with the dew of his own greenness thoroughly pours out the viridity of knowledge, from which he both learns and takes in what he wants to learn.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sicnt enim pater et mater infanti sno respondent, cum ab eis aliqnid petit, sic etiam spiritns sanctus scientiam hominis ad qnamlibet artem adinvat^ cnm eam homo per optionem et per desi derinm et per laborem discere quaerit. Sed cum homo se ad malnm aliquod et ad malam artem vertit et illud discere cnpit, tunc diabolus hoc videns scientiam eius perversitate et versutia sua afflat, ut malum boc, quod discere cupit, cito discat, quia etiam homo scientiam boni vel mali habet.
text_translation: Indeed, the father and mother respond to the infant's own needs when he asks them for something, so also the Holy Spirit brings the knowledge of man to any art when he seeks it through choice and through desire and through labor. But when a man turns himself to some evil and to an evil art and desires to learn it, then the devil, seeing this, inspires his knowledge with his own perversity and cunning, so that this evil, which he desires to learn, he may quickly learn, because man also has the knowledge of good or evil.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postquam autem infans de ventre matris egressus fuerit, deinde mobilis, agilis et operans est et sudat et diversa flegmata atque hun^tes h^bet, secundum quod et natura complexionis de ortu eius est et secundmn quod etiam augeri et minui possunt de escis et de potibus. JDe Uicte. Sed et cum muHer semen de viro conciplt, ita quod in eo crescere incipit, tunc etiam de eadem naturali vi sanguis mulieris sursum ad ubera trahitur, et quod de esca et potn sanguis esse debebat, in lac ver- titur, nt ex hoc infans nntriatur, qui in ventre illius crescit.
text_translation: After the infant has exited from the mother's womb, then it is mobile, agile, and active, and it sweats and has diverse phlegms and humors, according to the nature of its complexion from its origin, and according to which they can also be increased and diminished from foods and drinks. From the woman, when the semen from the man is conceived, so that it begins to grow, then also by the same natural force the blood of the woman is drawn upward to the breasts, and that which should have been blood from food and drink is converted into milk, so that from this the infant, who is growing in her womb, may be nourished.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t ut infans in ntero matris suae crescit, sic et lac in nberibus eins augmentatur, nt ex hoc infans nutriatur. De mulieris subiectione, Nunc autem, ut mulier est servitium viri, usque dum unum efBciantur, ita est etiam mulier copulatio seminis viri sangnine suo, ita ut una caro fiant. Item de conc€ptu, Unde cum semen viri in locum suum cadit, tunc sanguis mulieris cum voluntate amoris illud suscipit et in se introrsum trabit, sicut spiramen in se aliquid tollit.
text_translation: And just as the infant grows in the mother's womb, so too does the milk increase in her breasts, so that the infant may be nourished. Concerning the subjection of women, Now, however, as the woman is the service of the man, until they become one, so too is the woman the union of the man's semen with her blood, so that they become one flesh. Concerning conception, Therefore, when the man's semen falls into its place, then the woman's blood, with the will of love, receives it and draws it inward, just as spirit takes something up into itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic sanguis muHeris cum semine viri miscetur, et unus sangnis fit, ita quod etiam caro eiusdem mulieris de hoc permixto sanguine fovetur, crescit et augmentatnr. Ac ideo sic est mulier una caro cum SS viro de viro. Sed caro viri de calore et de sudore mulieris interius et exterius coquitur, atque sic de spuma 5» et de sudore eiusdem mulieris in se introrsum trahit. Nam de fortissima vi voluntatis viri sanguis eius liquefactus diffluit atque ut molendinum circumvolyitur (et aliquid de spuma et de sudore mulieris in se suscipit, ac 5fiic caro eius de muliere miscetur, ita quod cum ea et de ea caro una fit; et quoniam vir et mulier sic una caro sunt, facile mulier de eodem yiro fetum concipit, ita tamen, si fecunda ad fetum est.
text_translation: And thus the blood of the woman is mixed with the seed of the man, and one blood is made, so that also the flesh of the same woman is nourished, grows, and is increased. And therefore thus is the woman one flesh with the man's man from the man. But the flesh of the man is cooked by the heat and by the sweat of the woman inwardly and outwardly, and thus from the foam and from the sweat of the same woman it draws inwardly. For from the most powerful force of the will of the man his blood is liquefied and flows, and as a mill it revolves around (and something of the foam and of the sweat of the woman it takes into itself, and the flesh of him is mixed with the woman, so that with her and from her flesh one is made; and because man and woman thus are one flesh, easily the woman conceives a child from the same man, thus however, if she is fertile for conception.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod autem vir ,et mulier una caro sic fiunt et sunt, boc in latere viri latitabat, ubi mulier de latere viri sumpta caro eius facta est, ac ideo vir et mulier tanto facilius ad conceptionem in sanguine et sudore suo sic in unum confiuunt. Sed yis aetemitatis^ quai( infantem de ventre matris suae educit, virum et feminam six; unam camem facit. De aduUerio. Quod si vir et mulier iustae copulationis suae obliti ad alienam copulam cum ardente libidine se verterint, ita quod iniusta copulatione alieno communicant, tunc vir sanguinem suum, qui sanguis rectae uxoris suae est, ad alienam mulierem coniunzit, similiter et mulier sanguinem suum, qui et sanguis recti mariti sui est, alieno viro copulavit.
text_translation: What, however, man and woman become one flesh thus, and are, this in the side of the man lay hidden, where the woman from the side of the man took flesh, and thus became her flesh, and therefore man and woman so much more easily in their blood and sweat thus unite into one for conception. But who of eternity, which infant from the mother's womb educates, man and woman thus; one flesh makes. Of adultery. What if man and woman forgetting their just copulation turn themselves to another copulation with ardent lust, so that by unjust copulation they communicate with another, then man his blood, which is the blood of his just wife, joins to another woman, similarly and woman her blood, which is the blood of her just husband, copulates with a foreign man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde et filii, qui de istis tam iustis quam iniustis maritis seu tam iustis quam iniustis uxoribus postea nascuntur, multotiens infelices emnt, quia de diversis moribus et de diverso sanguine, scilicet tam viromm quam muliemm, originem conceptionis suae contraxerunt. Et ideo huiusmodi parentes apud deum praevaricatores rectae institutionis dicuntur, quam deus in Adam et in Evam constituit. Et sicut Adam et Eva praeceptum dei praevaricati se et oitines filios suos in mortem tradiderunt, ita et isti, qui boc modo divinam institutionem contaminant, se ipsos et illos, qui sic ab eis nascuntur, poUuunt et in infelicitatem mittunt, quoniam rationalitas in eis polluta est et quia in moribus suis communes pecoribus se fecemnt.
text_translation: Therefore, the children who are born later from these as just as unjust husbands or as just as unjust wives often are born unhappy, because they have taken the origin of their conception from different customs and different blood, namely both man and woman. And therefore, such parents are called by God transgressors of the right institution, which God established in Adam and in Eve. And just as Adam and Eve, having transgressed God's command, handed themselves and their eight sons over to death, so too those who defile the divine institution in this way defile themselves and those who are born from them, sending them into misery, because their reason is defiled and because they have made themselves like common sheep in their customs.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: S Be semine tenui. Quod si etiam mulier aliqua ooncipit et mox alium virum ad se accedere permittit, cum adbuo semen illud tenue est, tunc multotiens sudor et caJor illias viri buic semini aliqnantaluin coniungitur, ii» quod inde quasi de foetido yento polluitur, velut, si qxiis ad coagulationem lactis alium liquorem adderet, illud exinde contaminaretur. De seminis diversitate, Homo autem ille, in quo siccum, id est ignis, superexcellit, durum ingenium ad discendum artes habet, sed postquam eas didicerit, fortiter illas in stabilitate retinet.
text_translation: S Be semine tenui. Quod si etiam mulier aliqua concipit et mox alium virum ad se accedere permittit, cum ad duo semen illud tenue est, tunc multotiens sudor et calor illius viri buic semini aliquantulum coniungitur, et quod inde quasi de foetido vento polluitur, velut, si quisquis ad coagulationem lactis alium liquorem adderet, illud exinde contaminaretur. De seminis diversitate, Homo autem ille, in quo siccum, id est ignis, superexcellit, durum ingenium ad discendum artes habet, sed postquam eas didicerit, fortiter illas in stabilitate retinet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In quo autem humidum, id est aer, superbabundat, facile ingenium ad discendum artes habet, sed stabilis in scientia esse non potest, quia post- la qnam aliqua didicerit, facile ea oblivioni tradit. Sed in qno spuma, id est aqua, superexcellit, velox quidem ingenium ad discendum artes babet, sed antequam eas ad perfectum didicerit, putat se eas scire, cum nesciat. Unde cam se in eis extulerit, deficit, quia nondum eas ad per- fectnm cepit. In quo vero tepidum, id est terra, superbabundat, durum et difficile ingenium ad discendum artes babet; sed cum de quibusdam cum difficultate tamen aliquid ceperit, prae duritia ingenii sui retinere illas non valet.
text_translation: In which, however, the humid, that is air, overflows, easily the nature has the ability to learn arts, but it cannot be stable in science, because after it has learned some things, easily it hands them over to oblivion. But in which foam, that is water, overflows, indeed a swift nature has the ability to learn arts, but before it has learned them perfectly, it thinks it knows them, although it does not know. Therefore, when it has exalted itself in them, it falls, because it has not yet grasped them completely. In which, however, the warm, that is earth, overflows, a hard and difficult nature has the ability to learn arts; but when, from some things, although with difficulty, it has taken something, because of the hardness of its nature, it cannot retain them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde taedio victus, cum eas retinere non potest, discere multotiens desistit atque idem, quod didicerat, dimittit. Sed tamen in quibusdam, quae ad terram et ad saecularia negotia respiciunt, aliquando prudens est. JDe carnis deledcUione. Venae autem, quae in iecore et in yentre masculi sunt, in genitalibus eius sibi occur- runt. Et cum yentus delectationis a medulla masculi egreditur, in lumbos eius cadit et gustum delectationis in sanguine moyet.
text_translation: Therefore, from the aversion to food, when he cannot retain them, he often ceases to learn and abandons what he had learned. But nevertheless, in some matters relating to the earth and secular affairs, he is sometimes prudent. On the corruption of the flesh's delight. The veins which are in the liver and in the belly of the male meet in his genitalia. And when the wind of delight exits from the marrow of the male, it falls into his loins and moistens the blood with the taste of delight.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quia locus lumborum aliquantum artos et strictus et clausus est, yentus ille in eo non multum se difPundere potest et ibi fortiter in delectatione ardet, ita quod in ardore illo sui obliyiscitur et quod se continere non yalet, quin spumam seminis educat, quoniam propter clausuram lumborum ignis delectationis fortius, quamyis rarius, in eo ardet quam in muliere. Nam quemadmodum in magnis undis, quae de fortibus yentis et procellis in fluminibus assurgunt, nayis periclitatur, ita quod yix interdum contineri et subsistere potest, sic etiain in procella delectationis natura viri difiQcile compesci et contineri valet^ Sed in ondis, qnae de leni irento snrgunt, et in procellis, quae de lenibus torbinibus ascendunt, navicula quamvis per laborem contineri poterit; et sic est natura muHeris in delectatione, quia facilius compesci potest quam natura delectationis viri.
text_translation: And because the place of the loins is somewhat narrow and tight and closed, that wind in it cannot spread much and there burns strongly in delight, so that in that burning it forgets itself and is not able to contain itself, but produces a froth of semen, because because of the closure of the loins the fire of delight burns more strongly, though less frequently, in it than in a woman. For just as in great waves, which arise from strong winds and storms in rivers, the ship is in danger, so that it sometimes can be contained and remain, so also in the storm of delight the nature of the man is difficult to restrain and contain. But in waves which arise from gentle winds and in storms which ascend from gentle whirlpools, the ship, although it can be contained through labor; and thus is the nature of a woman in delight, because it can be more easily restrained than the nature of the man's delight.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam delectatio in viro igni assimulatur, qui interdum extinguitnr, iiiterdum aocenditur, quia ignis, qui assidue arderet, molta consumeret; ita etiam delectatio interdum in viro surgit, lOinterdum cadit, quoniam, si semper in eo ferveret, vir eam sufferre non posset. JDe virUibus et colericis. Quidam autem masculi sont, qui viriles ezsistunt, et hi cerebrum forte et spissnm habent. Cuius exteriores venulae, quae pelliculam eius continent, aliquantum rubeae sunt.
text_translation: For delight in a man is like fire, which sometimes is extinguished, sometimes is lit, because fire that would burn constantly would consume much; thus also delight sometimes arises in a man, sometimes falls, because if it were always boiling within him, the man could not endure it. On the forces and choleric [humors]. Some men, however, are such that they are virile, and these have a strong and thick brain. The external veins, which contain the membrane of it, are somewhat red.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et color faciei eomm aliquantum rubicundus velut in quibusdam imaginibus videtur, qui rubeo colore colorantur, et spissas ac fortes venas habent, quae ardentem sanguinem cerei coloris portant, et spissi circa pectus sunt atque fortia brachia tenent; sed valde pingues non sunt, quoniam fortes venae et fortis sanguis ac fortia membra cames eorum in multa pinguedine incrassari non permittunt. De renibus. Yentus quoque, qui in lumbis eorum est, magis igneus quam ventosus est; qui duo tabernacula sibi subdita habet, in quae flat ut in follem.
text_translation: And the color of their face is somewhat ruddy, as appears in some images, which are colored with a red hue, and have thick and strong veins, which carry burning blood of a wax color, and thick around the chest and strong arms possess; but they are not very fat, because strong veins and strong blood and strong limbs of their flesh do not allow them to be thickened in much fat. Concerning the kidneys. The wind also, which is in their loins, is more fiery than windy; which has two subordinate tabernacles, into which it blows like into a bellows.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et haec tabemacula stirpem omnium virium hominis circumdant et ei in adiutorium sunt, ut aliqua parva aedificia iuxta turrim posita, quae illam defendunt. Ac ideo duo sunt, ut tanto fortius praedictam stirpem circumdent et solident ac teneant, et ut tanto fortius et aptius praefatum ventom excipiant et ad se trahant, et ut aequaliter eum emittant, velut duo folles, qui aequaliter flant in ignem. Unde etiam cum eandem stirpem in virtute sua erigunt, eam fortiter tenent, et ita eadem stirps frondet in prolem.
text_translation: And this tabema surrounds the stock of all human powers and is a help to it, like small buildings placed next to a tower, which defend it. Therefore, two are, so that they surround and strengthen and hold the aforementioned stock more strongly, and so that they receive the aforementioned wind more strongly and aptly, and draw it to themselves, and so that they emit it equally, like two bellows which blow equally into fire. Hence, when they raise the same stock in their strength, they hold it firmly, and thus the same stock flourishes into offspring.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: liem de Adae exUio. Cum enim Adam caecus et surdus in transgressione factus est, haec vis in exiliam et in alienam rem in eo ivit, et in praedicta loca geniCAUSAE ET LIB. 11. talium hominis furtiYe fagit et ibi remansit. Sed et Tiri isti, qnos praediximus, prudentes sunt et«b aliis timentur atque amplexiones ad feminas habent et alios viros aliquantnm devitare et fogere student, quia magis feminas quam viros diligunt. Nam femineam formam in coniuncti- one tam valde amant, quod se continere non possunt, quin sanguis eorum magno ardore ardeat, oum aliquam feminam viderint vel audierint vel cum eam in cogitationibus suis ad memoriam suam dujcerint, quia oculi eorum velut sagittae snnt ad amorem feminae, cnm eam viderint, et lo anditns eomm velnt validissimns ventns, cnm eam audie- ' rint, et cogitationes eomm qnasi procella tempestatum, quae contineri non potest, quin snper terram cadat.
text_translation: Of the exile of Adam. For when Adam became blind and deaf in his transgression, this force entered him into exile and into a foreign matter, and into the aforementioned places of generation, causing and book 11. such things of the human theft does and remains there. But also these Tiri, whom we previously mentioned, are prudent and are feared by others and have embraces with women and endeavor to avoid and flee from other men, because they love women more than men. For they so greatly love the female form in conjunction, that they cannot contain themselves, unless their blood burns with great heat, when they see or hear or in their thoughts lead some woman to their memory, because their eyes are as arrows toward the love of a woman, when they see her, and their voice is as a most strong wind, when they hear her, and their thoughts are as a storm of tempests, which cannot be contained, unless it falls upon the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Isti viriles yiri snnt et fabri in fertilitate sua vocantnr, quia ardenter semper fertiles sunt in plurimas et plurimas proles nt arbor, quae in se plurimos ramos late extendit. Et ideo etiam de magno igne amplexionis, qui in ipsis est, velut sagittae sunt.
