Qui si coniunctionem feminarum habent, tunc sani et laeti sunt; si autem eis caruerint, tunc in semet ipsis arescunt et quasi moribundi vadunt, nisi aut ex superfluitate somniorum aut cogitationum aut perversitate alterius rei spumam seminis sui de se excutiant, quia in tanto ardore libidinis sunt, quod etiam interdum ad aliqnam insensibilem creaturam, quae non vivit, accedunt et se illi ita contorquent, quod se ab eodem ardore quasi defendendo et alleyiando spumam seminis emittunt in illa libidine et poena ardoris, quae in ipsis est, fatigati, quoniam ipsis continentia gravis est.
text_translation: These virile men are called in their fertility, because they are always ardently fertile in producing many and many offspring, like a tree which in itself extends many branches widely. And therefore also from the great fire of embrace which is in them, they are like arrows. Who, if they have the union of women, then are healthy and joyful; but if they lack these, then they wither within themselves and as if dying go, unless either from the superfluity of sleep, or thoughts, or perversion of another thing, they cast forth the foam of their semen from themselves, because in such a burning heat of lust they are, that sometimes they approach even some insensible creature which does not live, and twist themselves to it so, that they emit the foam of their semen in that lust and punishment of the heat which is in them, fatigued, because to them continence is heavy.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde si isti aliqua neoessitate seu pndoris seu timoris seu amoris dei compulsi mnlieres fugere voluerint, eas ut venennm devitentdO et fugiant, qnia difficile et vix ulla reverentia et continentia ipsos inhibere poterit, quin feminas amplectentur, cum eas yiderint. Homines autem, qui de his nascuntur, mnltotiens acerbos mores habent et incontinentes in libidine sunt et tam perversi in moribus suis ad humanos mores snnt, velut informis forma est, quae ex informi et igne fere combusto ligno formatur, ad similitudinem pul chrae formae, quae de polcliro ligno fit, quoniam diabolica suggestio plurim^m societatem habet in coniunctione illa, cum in multa superfluitate seminantur.
text_translation: Therefore, if these, compelled by necessity or shame or fear or the love of God, women wish to flee, they should avoid and flee from them, because it is difficult and scarcely any reverence and continence can restrain them, lest they embrace women when they see them. Men, however, who are born of these, often have harsh manners and are lacking in continence in lust, and so perverse in their morals toward human morals, as an unformed shape which is formed from unformed and nearly burned wood, similar to the form of a beautiful shape which is made from multicolored wood, because the devil's suggestion has many associations in that union, as they are sown in much superfluity.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen, com isti in bene et recte moderato ardore amplexionis sunt, qui tunc ex ipsis nascuntur , valde sapientes et valde utiles ac valde veloces erunt atque virilis faciei et pulcbrae pulcbritudinis. De sangumeis. Alii autem mascnli sunt, qui calidum cerebrum habent et amabilem colorem faciei candore et rubedine permixtum ac pingues venas sanguine plenas atque spissum sanguinem recti et rubei coloris. Sed et delectabilem humorem in se habent, qui nec tristitia nec acerbitate oppressus est, et quem acerbitas melancoliae fugit et devitat.
text_translation: But nevertheless, those who are in a good and rightly moderate ardor of embrace, from whom those who then are born are very wise and very useful and very swift, and of manly face and beautiful beauty. Of the blood. Others, however, are masculine, who have a warm brain and a pleasant color of face mixed with whiteness and redness, and fat veins full of blood and thick blood of right and red color. But they also have a delightful humor within themselves, which is neither oppressed by sadness nor by bitterness, and which bitterness of melancholy flees and avoids.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam calidum cerebrum et rectam sanguinem habent, et quoniam humores eorum oppressi non sunt, pingues cames in corporibus suis habent. Consuetudo autem, quae in femoribus eorum est, magis ventosa est quam ignea, et ob hoc abstinentiam habere possunt, quia plurimus ventus, qui in femonbus eorum est, ignem in eis compescit et temperat. Et cum idem ventus et ignis in istorum duo tabemacula interdum cadunt, omnia officia sua in honore et sobrio amore perficiunt, ita quod etiam stirps eorum in fiorem honorifice frondet, quoniam isti aureum aedificium in recta amplexione dicuntur, quia rationalitas sentit in eis, unde sit, ac ideo moderatio in eis fit et humani gestus.
text_translation: And because they have a warm brain and right blood, and because their humors are not oppressed, they have plump flesh in their bodies. However, the custom that is in their thighs is more ventous than fiery, and for this reason they can have abstinence, because the greatest wind that is in their thighs restrains and tempers the fire in them. And when the same wind and fire in these two tabemacula sometimes fall, they perform all their offices in honor and sober love, so that even their stock flourishes honorably in bloom, because they are said to be an golden building in right embrace, because reason feels in them, whence it is, and therefore moderation is made in them and human gestures.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et his necesse est, ut se ad habitationem virorum coniungant, quoniam muliebris natura suavior et levior est quam virilis natura. Sed cum mulieribus in honestate et fertilitate esse possunt et se etiam ab eis abstinere valent et pulchris et sobriis oculis eas inspiciunt, quoniam, ubi oculi aliorum ad eas velut sagittae sunt, ibi oculi istorum ad ipsas honeste symphonizant, et ubi auditus aliorum quasi validissimus ventus ad ipsas sunt, ibi auditus istorum velut sonum citharae habent, et ubi cogitationes aliorum quasi procella sunt, ibi isti prudentes amatores in honorificentia vocantur.
text_translation: And these [women] must necessarily unite themselves to the residence of men, because the female nature is milder and lighter than the male nature. But since women can be honorable and fertile and can also abstain from them, and they look upon them with beautiful and temperate eyes, because where the eyes of others are directed toward them like arrows, there the eyes of these [men] harmonize honorably with them, and where the hearing of others is like a most powerful wind directed toward them, there the hearing of these [men] is like the sound of a lyre, and where the thoughts of others are like a storm, there these prudent lovers are called honorable.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Saepe autem multas poenas sustinent, ubi in possibilitate sua continent, sed in eis est temperata prudentia, quam feminea ars habet, qnae bonam continentiam ex feminea natura contrabit, et etiam intelligibilem intellectum habent. Sed qui de bis nascuntur, continentes et felices ac utiles et probi in omnibus operibus sunt et sine invidia manent, quod ventus et ignis in femoribus parentum eorum recte ipsos temperavit; quoniam ignis excedendo ventum non superavit, sed ventus ignem temperavit.
text_translation: Often, however, they endure many punishments, where in their own capacity they restrain, but in them is tempered prudence, which the feminine art possesses, which the good restraint from the feminine nature will produce, and they also have an intelligible intellect. But those who are born from these are continent, happy, useful, and virtuous in all works, and remain without envy, because wind and fire in the thighs of their parents rightly tempered them; for fire, by exceeding, did not overcome wind, but wind tempered fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et qui sic procedunt, utiles erunt; praedicti autem masculi, si absque mulieribus sunt, ingloriosi manent velut dies, qui sine sole est. Sed ut cum die et per diem sine sole fructus continentur, ne arescant, sic isti absque mulieribus manentes modice temperato tenore continentur; cum mulieribus autem iocundi sunt, velut dies cum sole clarus est. Et quia in visu, in audito et in cogitationibus suaves sunt, saepius quam aHi aquosam spumam et non coctam de se emittunt, quod et vigilando et dormiendo eis contingit.
text_translation: And those who proceed thus will be useful; but the aforementioned males, if they are without women, remain inglorious as a day without the sun. But as fruits, during a day and throughout a day without the sun, are kept so that they do not wither, so these, remaining without women, are kept with a moderately temperate manner; but with women, they are pleasant, as a clear day with the sun. And because they are pleasant in sight, in hearing, and in thoughts, they more frequently than water emit watery foam and uncooked from themselves, which happens to them both when awake and when sleeping.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Atque facilius quam quidam alii seu cum semet ipsis seu cum aliis rebus a calore libidinis solvuntur. De mdancolieis. Alii autem viri sunt, quorum cere- brum pingue est, et pellicula eiusdem cerebri et venae eius turbidae, atque austerum colorem faciei babent, ita quod etiam oculi eorum aliquantum ignei et viperei sunt, et duras et fortes venas babent, quae nigrum et spissum sanguinem in se continent, et grossas et duras cames babent atque grossa ossa, quae modicam medullam in se tenent, quae tamen tam fortiter ardet, quod cum mulieribus velut animalia et ut viperae incontinentes sunt.
text_translation: And more easily than certain others, whether with themselves or with other things, they are dissolved from the heat of lust. On melancholics. Others, however, are men whose brain is fat, and the membrane of their brain and their veins are turbid, and they have a stern color of face, so that even their eyes are somewhat fiery and viper-like, and they have hard and strong veins, which contain black and thick blood within them, and they have thick and hard sinews and thick bones, which contain little marrow within them, which nevertheless burns so fiercely that with women they are like animals and like vipers, being incontinent.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ventus, qui in lumbis eorum est, in tribus modis exsistit, ita quod igneus est et ventosus ac fumo melan- coliae permixtus, et ideo rectam dilectionem ad nullum babent; sed amari et avari et insipientes sunt et superflui in libidine ac sine moderatione cum mulieribus velut asini; unde si de hac libidine interdum cessaverint, facile insaniam capitis incurrunt, ita quod frenetici erunt. Et cum hanc libidinem in convictionem mulierum exercent, insaniam capitis non patiuntur; sed tamen amplexio, quam HILDE6ARDIS ad feminas sobrie deberent habere, tortuosa atque odiosa et mortifera est velut rapidorum luporum.
text_translation: And the wind that is in their loins exists in three ways, so that it is fiery and windy and mixed with the smoke of melancholia, and therefore they have no right affection; but they are loving and greedy and foolish, and excessive in lust without moderation with women like donkeys; whence if they sometimes cease from this lust, they easily incur madness of the head, so that they will be frenzied. And when they exercise this lust in the conviction of women, they do not suffer madness of the head; but nevertheless, the embrace, which Hildegardis should have with women soberly, is twisted and hateful and deadly like swift wolves.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quidam autem ex istis propter fortes venas et propter medullam fortiter in se ardentem libenter cum feminis secundum humanam naturam sunt, sed tamen eas odio habent. Quidam autem femineum sexum devitare possunt, quia feminas non diligunt nec eas habere volunt, sed in cordibus suis tam acres sunt ut leones, et mores ursorum habent; sed tamen utiles et prudentes sunt in operibus manuum suamm atque libenter operantur. Ventus autem delectationis, qui in duo tabernacula praedictorum virorum cadit, tanta immoderatione et tam repentino mota venit, quemadmodum ventus, qui totam domum repente et fortiter movet, et stirpem in tanta tyrannide erigit, quod eadem stirps, quae in florem florere - debebat , in acerbitatem vipereorum morum se intorquet et in huiusmodi malitia velut mortifera et occidens vipera in progenie sua malitiam habet, quia suggestio diaboli in libidine virorum istorum ita furit, ut, si possent, feminam in coniunctione hac mortificarent, quoniam nulla opera caritatis et amplezionis in eis sunt.
text_translation: Some, however, among them, because of strong veins and because of marrow burning strongly within them, are willingly with women according to human nature, yet they hate them. Some, however, can avoid the female sex, because they do not love women and do not wish to have them; but in their hearts they are as fierce as lions, and they have the manners of bears; yet they are useful and prudent in their manual works and willingly work. The wind of delight, which falls upon the two tabernacles of the aforementioned men, comes with such immoderation and sudden movement, as a wind that suddenly and strongly moves the whole house, and raises the vine in such tyranny, that the same vine, which should have bloomed into flower, twists itself into the bitterness of viper-like manners, and in such malice, like a deadly and killing viper, has malice in its offspring, because the suggestion of the devil in the lust of these men rages so much that, if they could, they would mortify a woman in this union, because there are no works of charity and consolation in them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde fllii aut flliae, quos sic de se producont, multotiens diabolicam insaniam in vitiis et in moribus suis habent, quoniam absque caritate emissi sunt. Nam qui de his nascuntur, saepe infelices erunt et tortuosi in omnibus mori bus suis, et ideo ab hominibus amari non possunt, nec ipsi mansionem cum hominibus libenter habent, quoaiam multis fantasmatibus fatigantur. Si autem cum hominibus manent, odio et invidia et perversis moribus cum eis sunt, et nullum gaudium cum eis habent.
text_translation: Therefore, sons or daughters, whom they thus produce from themselves, often have diabolical insanity in their vices and in their manners, because they are emitted without charity. For those who are born from these, often will be unhappy and twisted in all their manners, and therefore cannot be loved by humans, nor do they willingly dwell with humans, although they are tired by many fantasies. If, however, they dwell with humans, they are with them in hatred and envy and perverse manners, and they have no joy with them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quidam tamen ex his nati prudentes et utiles interdum flunt, sed tamen in eadem utilitate tam graves et contrarios mores ostendunt, quod inde nec diUgi nec honorari possunt, velut ignobiles lapides, qui sine nitore iacent, quasi extincti sint^ et qui inter nitentes lapides non diliguntur, quoniam pulchrum nitorem non habent. Be flegmaticis, Sed et alii mascuU sunt, qui pingue cerebrum et album et siccum habent, ita quod etiam venulae eiusdem cerebri magis albae quam rubeae sunt, et grossos ac squalidos oculos babent et muliebrem colorem in facie, et non serenam cutem, sed quasi extincti coloris; sed et latas et moUes venas habent, quae tamen non multum saDguinem in se continent, ita quod etiam idem sanguis aliquantum sanguineus non est, sed aliquantum spomosus.
text_translation: Indeed, some among them are born prudent and useful at times, yet they display such grave and contrary manners within the same utility that they cannot be dignified or honored, like ignoble stones lying without luster, as though extinguished, and among shining stones they are not esteemed, because they lack the beautiful luster. But others are phlegmatic, who have a fleshy, white, and dry brain, so that even the veins of the same brain are whiter than red, and they have gross and glossy eyes, and a feminine color on the face, not a serene complexion, but as though extinguished in color; yet they have wide and soft veins, which, however, do not contain much blood, so that the same blood is somewhat bloody but also somewhat spumous.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et carnes in corpore suo ad suf&cientiam sibi habent et molles secundum carnes feminarum, sed fortia membra, non autem audacem nec strenuum animum tenent. In cogitationibus vero et in loquela sermonum suorum audaces et strenui sunt ut ignis, cuius flamma repente surgit et repente cadit, ac etiam in habitu suo audaciam ostendunt, sed eam in operibus non habent et magis opinione quam opera in conversatione sua demonstrant. Yentus autem, qui in lumbis eorum est, modicum ignem habet, ita quod modice calet, velut aqua, quae vix calida 6st.
text_translation: And the flesh in their body has sufficiency for itself and is soft according to the flesh of women, but strong limbs, yet they do not hold a bold or strong mind. In thoughts indeed and in the speech of their words, they are bold and strong as fire, whose flame suddenly rises and suddenly falls, and also in their habit they show audacity, but they do not have it in deeds, and more by opinion than by work do they demonstrate in their conversation. The belly, which is in their loins, has a little fire, so that it warms a little, like water that is barely warm.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et duae domus eius, quae ut duo foUes esse deberent ad excitandum ignem, derelictae sunt in defectione nec yires habent, ut stirpem erigaDt, quoniam plenitudinem ignis in se non continent. Et isti in illa amplexione amari possunt, quod et viris et feminis cohabitare valent et quod fideles sunt; ita quod homines mortifero odio non habent, sed et in corporibus suis modicum gustum primae creaturae, ubi Adam et Eva sine carnali amplexione processerunt, cum tamen isti in illa et in ista genitura defi- ciant.
text_translation: And the two houses of it, which should be as two foUles to excite fire, have been abandoned in defect and do not have yires to raise up a stock, because they do not contain the fullness of fire within themselves. And those in that embrace may be loved, because they can cohabit both with men and women and because they are faithful; so that they do not have mortal hatred toward men, but also in their bodies have a slight taste of the first creature, where Adam and Eve proceeded without carnal embrace, although these in that and in this generation defect.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam in eis semen secundum genus virorum esse non potest, ideo nec in barba nec in aliis huiusmodi causis virorum viriles esse valent. Sed et quia etiam invidi non sunt, ideo de bona opinione, qua in natura sua debiLes sunt, feminas amant, quae etiam debiles sunt, quod femina in debilitate sua ut puer est, et inde idem viri aliquando modice incalescunt, ita quod interdum modicum barbae emittunt, velut aliqua terra, quae modicum graminis profert.
text_translation: And because in them semen cannot be according to the genus of men, therefore neither in the beard nor in other such causes of men can they be virile. But also because they are not envious, therefore from good opinion, by which in their nature they are weak, they love women, who are also weak, for woman in her weakness is like a boy, and therefore the same men sometimes slightly grow warm, so that sometimes they emit a little beard, like some land which produces a little grass.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed perfectionem aratri non habent, ut terram scandant, quia feminis ita coniungi non possunt, quemadmodum fertiles viri, sed steriles sunt. Unde etiam in animo suo non multum in libidine laborant, nisi quod eam interdum in opinione et optione habent. Et quia bonc defectum in corporibus babent, ideo etiam tardi in ingenio sunt, et venae temporum eorum non sunt plenae in yiriditate, sed ad similitudinem calamorum et quarundam berbarum fragiles venas babent, nec viriles dicuntur, qaoniam venae eorum frigidae sunt et quia etiam semen eorum tenue et incoctum velut spuma est, nec illud ad congruum tempus retinere possunt.
text_translation: But they do not have the perfection of plowmen, so that they may plow the earth, because women cannot thus join with men, as fertile men do, but they are sterile. Therefore, in their mind, they do not labor much in lust, except that they sometimes have it in opinion and choice. And because they have a good defect in their bodies, therefore they are also slow in mind, and the veins of their time are not full in viriditude, but similar to reeds and certain barbarians, they have fragile veins, nor are they called virile, since their veins are cold and because their semen is also thin and uncooked like foam, nor can they retain it at the proper time.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De mulieris ddectatione, Delectatio autem in muliere soli comparatur, qui blande et leniter et assidue terram calore suo perfondit, ut fructus proferat, quia si eam acrius in assiduitate incenderet, fructus magis laederet quam eos produceret. Ita etiam et delectatio in muliere blande et leniter sed tamen assidue calorem babet, ut prolem concipiat et pariat, quia, si semper in delectationis fervore maneret, apta ad concipiendum et ad generandum non esset.
text_translation: On the detection of a woman, Delectation, however, in a woman is comparable to that which gently and softly and consistently saturates the earth with its heat, so that it produces fruit, because if it were to ignite her more acridly in consistency, it would harm the fruit more than produce them. Thus also delectation in a woman has heat gently and softly yet consistently, so that it conceives and bears offspring, because if it were always to remain in the fervor of delectation, it would not be suitable for conceiving and generating.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Oum enim delectatio in muliere surgit, levior in ea est quam in viro, quoniam buiusmodi ignis in ea tam fortiter non ardet ut in viro. De viri ddedatione. Nam cum tempestas libidinis in masculo surgit, in eo ut molendinum circumvolvitur, quia etiam lumbi eius velut fabrica sunt, in quam medulla ignem mittit, ita quod et fabrica illa eundem ignem in genitalia loca masculi transfundit ac eum fortiter ardere facit. Sed cum ventus delectationis ex medulla feminae egreditur, in matricem, quae umbilico adbaeret, cadit et sanguinem mulieris ad delectationem movet, et quia matrix circa umbilicum mulieris amplum et velut apertum locum babet, ventus ille in ventrem eius se dilatat, et ideo lenius, quamvis prae bumiditate sua saepius, ibi in delectationem ardet, et ideo etiam aut prae timore aut prae pudore facilius quam vir a delectatione se continere valet, ita quod etiam spuma seminis ab ea rarius quam a viro eicitur et modica et tantae exiguitatis ad spumam viri ut frustum panis ad integritatem eius.
text_translation: Omnis enim delectatio in muliere surgit, levior in ea est quam in viro, quoniam huiusmodi ignis in ea tam fortiter non ardet ut in viro. De viri libidine. Nam cum tempestas libidinis in masculo surgit, in eo ut molendinum circumvolvitur, quia etiam lumbi eius velut fabrica sunt, in quam medulla ignem mittit, ita quod et fabrica illa eundem ignem in genitalia loca masculi transfundit ac eum fortiter ardere facit. Sed cum ventus delectationis ex medulla feminae egreditur, in matricem, quae umbilico adhaeret, cadit et sanguinem mulieris ad delectationem movet, et quia matrix circa umbilicum mulieris amplum et velut apertum locum habet, ventus ille in ventrem eius se dilatat, et ideo lenius, quamvis prae humiditate sua saepius, ibi in delectationem ardet, et ideo etiam aut prae timore aut prae pudore facilius quam vir a delectatione se continere valet, ita quod etiam spuma seminis ab ea rarius quam a viro eicitur et modica et tantae exiguitatis ad spumam viri ut frustum panis ad integritatem eius.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen multotiens evenit, cum praedicta spuma post delectationem ab ea non eicitur, quod venis matricis, quae candidae et pingues suiit, commiscetur, ut tandem per menstrua efduat et, quod residuum remanet, ab ea effanditur, aliquando etiam in ea dissipatur et conteritur et ad nihilum deduoitur, cum sine tactu viri in delectationem movetur. Sed et fertilis natura feminae frigidior est et magis sanguinea quam xiatura viri, et vires eius debiliores viribus viri sunt, et ideo etiam in delectatione lenius ardet quam vir, quia femina tantum vas est, ut prolem concipiat et pariat, ac ideo etiam ventus eius ventosus est et venae apertae atque membra eius solubiliora sunt quam viri.
text_translation: But nevertheless, it often happens that when the aforementioned foam is not expelled from her after pleasure, it commingles with the veins of the womb, which are white and fat, so that finally it is expelled through the menstruation, and what residue remains is expelled from her, sometimes also being scattered and crushed and reduced to nothing within her, when she is moved to pleasure without the touch of the man. But also the fertile nature of the woman is colder and more sanguine than the nature of the man, and her forces are weaker than the man's forces, and therefore also in pleasure she burns more gently than the man, because the woman is only a vessel to conceive and bear offspring, and therefore also her wind is windy and her veins are open and her limbs are more soluble than those of the man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Viri antem, qui fertiles sunt, si se a mulieribus continent, aliquantum infirmantur, sed non tantum quantum feminae, quod plus seminis quam mulieres effundunt. Sed feminae aridae, si viris carent, sanae sunt, si autem viros habent, debiles sunt. Sicut quoque inundatio pluviarum et tem- pestatum interdum exsurgit et interdum silet, et sicut miistum nunc per fervorem ebullit nunc cadit, sic etiam mali humores aliquando in homine eriguntur, aliquando tabescunt, quia, si in malo fortitudinis suae semper erecti essent, homo non posset .
text_translation: Men, however, who are fertile, if they abstain from women, are somewhat weakened, but not as much as women, because they ejaculate more semen than women do. But barren women, if they lack men, are healthy, but if they have men, they are weak. Just as also the inundation of rains and storms sometimes arises and sometimes is silent, and just as mist now boils up through heat and now falls, so also bad humors in a man sometimes rise up, sometimes wither away, because, if in their own bad strength they were always erect, man could not...

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: subsistere, sed cito periret. Sanguis enim in quolibet homine secundum incrementum et detrimentum lunae crescit et minuitur. 2e /tmoe mtdatiane et hiimorum, Nam cum luna ad plenitudinem suam crescit, tunc et sanguis in homine augetur, et cum luna decrescit, tunc et sanguis in homine minuitur. Sic semper est tam in muliere quam in viro. Gum enim sanguis in homine ad plenitudinem incrementi sui pervenerit, si tunc in homine non decresceret, homo durare non posset, sed totus dirumperetur.
text_translation: To subsist, but to perish quickly. For the blood in every man increases and decreases according to the increase and decrease of the moon. For when the moon grows to its fullness, then the blood in man increases, and when the moon decreases, then the blood in man decreases. Thus it is always, both in woman and in man. For when the blood in man reaches the fullness of its increase, if then in man it did not decrease, man could not endure, but would be entirely destroyed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De tempore gignUionis, Et cimi in incremento lunae sanguis in homine sic augetur, tunc homo etiam, scilicet tam femina quam vir, fertilis est ad fructum, videlicet ad generandum prolem, quia in augmento lunae, cum etiam augmentum sanguinis hominis est, forte et robustum est semen hominis, et in detrimento lunae, cum etiam detri- S mentum sanguinis est in homine, semen hominis debile et absque fortitudine est, velut faeces sunt, et ideo tunc magis defectus est ad prolem propagandam.
text_translation: On the time of begetting, and when, in the increase of the moon, the blood in man thus increases, then man also, namely both woman and man, is fertile for fruit, that is, for generating offspring, because in the increase of the moon, when also the increase of man's blood is, the man's semen is strong and robust, and in the decrease of the moon, when also the decrease of man's blood is in man, the man's semen is weak and without strength, like feces, and therefore then there is greater defect for propagating offspring.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si tnnc aliquA mulier prolem conceperit, sive masculus sive femina fderit, hic bomo infirmus et debilis et non virtnosus erit. In incremento ergo lunae sanguis tam in femina quam ia. masculo augetur et in detrimento lunae tam in feniina quam in masculo minuitur usque ad quinquagesimnnL aetatis suae annum, De menstruo, Sed cum in detrimento lunae sangpiis in masculo minuitur, in femina sanguis per menstruum tempus etiam minuitur. Quod si - menstruum tempus in augmento lunae alicui feminae occurrerit, tunc magis in tempore illo dolet, quam si in detrimento lunae ipsi accidisset, quoniam in augmento sanguis ei augeri debebafyi qui tunc per menstruum tempus minuitur.
text_translation: If then some woman conceives a child, whether male or female be born, this man will be weak and feeble and not virtuous. Therefore, in the increase of the moon, blood increases both in woman and in man, and in the decrease of the moon, it decreases both in woman and in man up to the fiftieth year of their age. Concerning menstruation, but when in the decrease of the moon blood decreases in the male, in the female blood also decreases during the menstrual period. If the menstrual period occurs during the increase of the moon in some woman, then she suffers more pain at that time than if it had occurred during the decrease of the moon, because in the increase, the blood should be increased which then during the menstrual period decreases.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Post quin quagesimum vero annum sanguis in bomine secundum cursum lunae iam nec augetur nec mintiitur tanta fortitudine et velodtate, ut prius fecit, sed usque ad oeto gesimum annum camem in illo aliquantulum plus incrassari facit quam antea solita fuisset, quia idem sanguis in eo iam et augeri et minui cessat. Post octogesimum annum tam caro quam sanguis masculi in eo defluunt et isutis contrahitur et rugae oriuntur, cum in iuventate cutis eius plana et extenta fuit, quia came et sanguine plena erat.
text_translation: After the fiftieth year indeed, the blood in the body according to the course of the moon is no longer increased nor diminished with such strength and speed as it did before, but up to the eightieth year the skin in it becomes somewhat more thickened than it was accustomed to before, because the same blood in it has ceased both to increase and decrease. After the eightieth year both flesh and male blood in it flow away and the skin contracts and wrinkles appear, since in youth its skin was smooth and extended, because it was full of flesh and blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam post octogesimum annum caro et sanguis in masculo defluunt, ipse debilis efficitur; unde cibo et potu velut puer semper bis indigens reficiendus est, ut, quod tunc came et sanguine minus babet, esca et potu suppleatur. Sed in mulieribus post quinquagesimum annum menstrua deficiunt exceptis illis, quae tantae sospitatis et fortitudinis sunt, quod in eis menstrua usque ad septuagesimum annum protrabuntur, et deinde sanguine, velut prius fecit, amplius non profiuente caro earum incrassatur usque ad septuagesimum annum, quoniam tunc per menstrua non attenuatur.
text_translation: And since after the eightieth year flesh and blood in the male flow away, he becomes weak; therefore, he must be restored by food and drink as a child always needing twice, so that what then flesh and blood lack is supplied by food and drink. But in women after the fiftieth year menstruation ceases except for those who are so full of comfort and strength that in them menstruation will be prolonged until the seventieth year, and then flesh, since blood no longer profits further, thickens up to the seventieth year, because then it is not attenuated by menstruation.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Post septuagesimum vero annum caro et sanguis in eis iam defluunt, et cutis earum contrabitur et rugae surgimt ac debiles fiunt cibo et potu ut puer saepius refocillandae, quia came et sanguine tuno eTacTiantar, et qnoniam debiliores masculis sunt, cum haec miseria senectutis usque ad octogesimum annum in masculis protrahatur. Sed et in animalibus brutis in axLgmento lunae sanguis augetur et in detrimento eius minoratur, sed tamen parcius quam in hominibus, exceptis illis animalibus, quae de sudore et humore terrae nascuntnr et nutriuntur, quae magis de veneno et de tabe quam de sanguine exsistunt, et exceptis piscibus, qni in aqua viyentes ex aqua exsistunt, qui modicum sanguinem habent.
text_translation: After the seventyeth year, flesh and blood in them already flow away, and their skin will contract, and wrinkles will arise, and they become weak by food and drink, as a child needing frequent nourishment, because flesh and blood are exhausted, and since they are weaker than men, because this misery of old age is prolonged in men up to the eightieth year. But also in brute animals, in the increase of the moon blood is increased, and in the decrease of it is diminished, but yet less so than in humans, excepting those animals which are born from sweat and the humor of the earth and are nourished, which exist more from poison and decay than from blood, and excepting fish, which living in water exist from water, which have little blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In arboribus quoque, quae in radicibus suis frondent, in lO augmento lunae succus augetur et in detrimento lunae minoratury et ideo, si in augmento lunae abscinduntur, -'tunc propter succom et humorem, qui in ipsis remansit, a yermibus et a carie plus consumuntur, quam si in detrimento lunae abscinderentur, quia, si in mino- ratione lunae abscinduntur, tunc succo in eis attenuato aliquantulum in quandam duritiam vertuntur, ita quod vermes in eis tanto minus crescunt et quod ea caries tanto minus laedere potest.
text_translation: In trees also, which flourish in their roots, in the increase of the moon the juice increases and in the decrease of the moon diminishes, and therefore, if they are cut during the increase of the moon, then because of the juice and the humor which remains in them, they are more consumed by worms and decay than if they were cut during the decrease of the moon, because, if they are cut during the diminution of the moon, then the juice in them, being somewhat attenuated, turns into a certain hardness, so that worms grow so much less in them and that decay can so much less damage them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De arhorum putatione, Positiones etiam et putationes arborum meliores sunt ad stabilitatem in detrimento lunae quam in augmento eius , quoniam si in augmento lunae fiunt, tunc propter crescentem et superhabundantem succum multotiens defluunt et minus radices capiunt et minus ad profectnm proveniunt, quam si in detrimento lunae fiant. Nam si in decrescente luna fiunt, tunc succo aliquantum minorato maior et fortior vis arborum interius latet, et ideo citius radices capiunt et stabiliuntur quam in superhabundanti succo, quia cum postea increscente luna sucous tunc etiam et putatio illa egreditur.
text_translation: On the pruning of trees, the positions and prunings of trees are better for stability during the waning of the moon than during its increase, for if they are done during the moon's increase, then because of the increasing and superabundant juice, they often flow away and the roots capture less and produce less growth than if they are done during the waning of the moon. For if they are done during the waning moon, then the juice somewhat reduced, a greater and stronger power of the trees lies hidden internally, and therefore the roots capture more quickly and become more stable than in superabundant juice, for afterward, when the moon increases, the juice then also and that pruning exits.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vinearum putatione. Cum etiam palmites vitis ad cnltnram vinearum abscinduntur , ad maiorem utilitatem et nbertatem fructuum provenitmt, si abscindantur in deorescente luna quam in crescente, quia quanto plus in angmento lunae inscinduntur, tanto plus succus et lacrimae 3b in ipsis effluunt. Et sic vitis aliquantulum aridior erit, qnam si in detrimento lunae inscindantur, quoniam tunc yis interius remanet et inscisio supercrescit et durescit ad crescentem lunam.
text_translation: On the Pruning of Vines. The shoots of the vine are cut back for the cultivation of the vines, for the greater utility and freedom of the fruits to come forth, if they are cut back in the waning moon rather than in the waxing, because the more they are cut back during the increase of the moon, the more juice and tears flow out of them. And thus the vine will be somewhat drier than if they are cut back during the waning of the moon, because then the vine remains internally and the cut grows over and hardens during the waxing moon.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De herbarum coUectiane, Nobiles autem et bonae herbae si in augmento lunae de terra abscinduntur vel eradicantur, cum plenae sunt, meliores ad electuaria et ad unguenta et ad quamque medicinam valent quam si in detrimento lunae colligantur. Be pomorum coUectione. Sed et quaeque olera et poma, quae in augmento lunae colliguntur, et cames pecorum, quae etiam occiduntur, maiorem ubertatem ad esum babent, quia tunc aut succo aut sanguine plenae sunt, quam si in detrimento lunae colligantur aut occidantur, excepto si diutius servari debuerint, quoniam tunc melius et utilius est propter constrictionem decrescentis lunae, qua constringuntur, ut olera et poma coUigantur et pecora occidantur in detrimento lunae, quatinus tanto diutius durare valeant.
text_translation: On the collection of herbs. Noble and good herbs, if they are severed from the earth or uprooted during the waxing of the moon when they are full, are more beneficial for electuaria and for unguenta and for any medicine than if they are gathered during the waning of the moon. Likewise, all herbs and fruits gathered during the waxing of the moon, and the meat of animals slaughtered at that time, have greater abundance for consumption, because then they are full of either juice or blood, than if they are gathered or slaughtered during the waning of the moon, except when they must be preserved for a longer time, because then it is better and more useful because of the constriction of the decreasing moon, by which they are constricted, so that herbs and fruits should be gathered and animals slaughtered during the waning of the moon, so that they may last so much the longer.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De segetvm incisione. Frumentum quoque, quod in augmento lunae per messores in messe abscinditur, plus farinae reddit, quam si in detrimento eius abscinderetur, quoniam in augmento lunae plenitudinem suam in se babet, quae in detrimento illius aliquantum strictior erit. Sed tamen quod in decrescente luna abscinditur, in virtute sua diutius durare potest, quam si in orescente lunae abscindatur. Et frumentum, quod in incremento lunae abscinditur, sed in terram pro semine proicitur, citius radices capit et citius in gramen surgit et citius plus graminis affert, si tamen minus frumenti, quam si in decrescente lunae abscindatur.
text_translation: On the cutting of crops. Grain, which is cut during the waxing moon by reapers in the harvest, yields more flour than if it were cut during the waning of the moon, because during the waxing moon it possesses its fullness within itself, which becomes somewhat more constricted during the waning. However, that which is cut during the waning moon lasts longer in its virtue than if it were cut during the waxing moon. And grain which is cut during the waxing moon but sown into the earth for seed takes root more quickly and rises more swiftly into grass and produces more grass, although less grain, than if it were cut during the waning moon.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De iempore seminandi. Quod autem in detrimento lunae abscinditur, si seminatum fuerit, tardius quidem surgit et crescit et minus graminis babet, sed tamen maiorem ubertatem frumenti affert, quam si in augmento lunae abscisum fuisset. Sed et unumquodque semen, quod in crescente luna in terram proicitur, citius quidem surgit et crescit et plus graminis babet, quod cum crescente luna erigitur, quam si in decrescente luna semiCAUSAE ET LIB. U. •» naretur, quoniam si tunc seminaretur, paulatim erumperet, usque dum bene confortatum exsurgeret.
text_translation: On the time of sowing. Whatever is cut off during the waning of the moon, if sown, indeed rises and grows more slowly and has less grass, but nevertheless yields a greater abundance of grain than if it had been cut off during the waxing of the moon. However, each seed that is cast into the earth during the waxing moon indeed rises and grows more quickly and has more grass, which is raised with the waxing moon, than if it were sown during the waning moon, for if it were sown then, it would gradually emerge until it was well established and sprouted.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de Adae sopcre, Sed antequam Adam praeceptum dei praevaricatus foisset, immissus est ei sopor et cibus est ei ostensus; postquam autem praevaricatus est, caro eius tantam debilitatem et fragilitatem incurrit, in quanta instabilitate caro mortui est ad camem viyentis liominis. Sed sopore postea recreabatur, sicut cibo refocillabatar. Et sic etiam in omnibas hominibus fit. Nam nt caro hominis cibo crescit, ita et medulla eius sopore. De samno.
text_translation: Item about Adam's sleep. But before Adam had transgressed God's command, sleep was given to him and food was shown to him; after he had transgressed, his flesh incurred such weakness and fragility, in what instability the flesh of the dead is compared to the living man. But later by sleep he was refreshed, as by food he was refueled. And thus also in all humans it happens. For as the flesh of man grows by food, so also his marrow by sleep. About sleep.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim homo dormit, tunc meduUa eius recreatur et crescit, et cum yigilat, tunc medulla eius aliquantnlum attenuatur et debilitatur, quemadmodum luna in augmento suo crescit et in detrimento suo decrescit et sicut radices herbarum in hieme viriditatem in se habent, quam in aestate in flores emittimt. Unde cum medulla hominis aut laboribus fatigata est aut vigiliis attenuata, tunc homo sopore deprimitur et sive stet sive sedeat sive iaceat facile obdormit, quia anima eius in ipso necessitatem corporis sui sentit.
text_translation: When a man sleeps, then his medulla is refreshed and grows, and when he is awake, then his medulla is somewhat attenuated and weakened, just as the moon increases in its growth and decreases in its decline, and as the roots of herbs in winter possess greenness within themselves, which in summer they emit as flowers. Hence, when the medulla of a man is either fatigued by labors or attenuated by vigils, then the man is oppressed by sleep and whether he stands or sits or lies down easily falls asleep, because his soul within perceives the necessity of his body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam meduUa vigiliis attenuata et debilitata mox vires animae suavissimum et dulcissimum ventum ex medulla producunt, qui venas colli et totam cervicem hominis perflat, et qui ad tempora transit et venas capitis occupat, et qui ita vitalem flantem flatum bominis deprimit, sic quod homo iUe tunc est et iacet velut insensibilis et nescius et impotens sui corporis nec intellectum nec cogitationes nec sensibilitatem tunc scienter habens excepto quod anima vitalem flatum inducit et emittit, sicut et vigilanti homini faoit, et quod eum tam dormientem quam vigilantem continet, et quod in eo est dum dormit, sicut et in eo est dum vigilat.
text_translation: Nam medulla vigiliis attenuata et debilitata mox vires animae suavissimum et dulcissimum ventum ex medulla producunt, qui venas colli et totam cervicem hominis perflat, et qui ad tempora transit et venas capitis occupat, et qui ita vitalem flantem flatum hominis deprimit, sic quod homo ille tunc est et iacet velut insensibilis et nescius et impotens sui corporis nec intellectum nec cogitationes nec sensibilitatem tunc scienter habens excepto quod anima vitalem flatum inducit et emittit, sicut et vigilanti homini fuit, et quod eum tam dormientem quam vigilantem continet, et quod in eo est dum dormit, sicut et in eo est dum vigilat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic homo obdormit, ut praedictum est. Tunc anima hominis illius viribus suis ad se coUectis medullam eius crescere facit et confortat ac per eam ossa firmat et sanguinem coagulat, camem etiam coquit et singula membra componit et sapientiam et scientiam in eodem homine dilatat laetante vita eius. Sic ergo homine dormiente maiorem calorem interius habet quam si vigilat, quia, cum homo Yigila.t, meduUa eius tenis efficitur et lubrica ac turbida, et ob hoc ille doimitat; cum autem dormit, medulla eius ardet, quoniam tunc crescit et pinguis et candida e£Qcitur.
text_translation: And thus the man falls asleep, as previously stated. Then the soul of that man, gathering its forces toward itself, causes his marrow to grow and strengthens it, and through it makes the bones firm and coagulates the blood, cooks the cam, and composes each limb, and expands wisdom and knowledge within the same man, rejoicing in his life. Thus, therefore, the man when asleep has greater internal heat than when he is awake, because when the man is awake, his marrow becomes tense and slippery and turbid, and for this reason he dozes; when, however, he sleeps, his marrow burns, because then it grows and becomes fat and white, is made.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pollutione nocturna. Unde etiam ipsa tunc mixltotiens in eodem ardore sanguinem ex superfluitate sua ad delectationem movet et spumam pollutionis nesciente homine ad genitalia mittens producit. Tunc etiam intei dum ex superhabundantia ciborum et potuum medulla ex:- ardescit, quoniam immoderati cibi et potus ignem medullae aliquantum subministrant et succus eorum medullam et sanguinem aliquantum in motionem ducunt. Et ita medulla inardescens delectationem gustus carnis in sanguine concutit et spumam pollutionis ignorante homine in genitalia educit, quod tamen a calore aestatis vel a calore indumentorum hominis non evenit vel raro contingit, Sed quia tunc corpus quiescit nec quicquam operatur, tunc anima, quae vigilante corpore in multis causis cum eo occupata fuit, scientiam suam, cum qua in corpore operatur, velut oculos suos in somniis educit et circumspicit, quoniam tunc diverso opere corporis non impeditur.
text_translation: On nocturnal emission. Hence, at that time, it mixes with the same fervor, moving the blood from its superfluity toward delight, and producing the foam of emission without the man knowing, sending it to the genitals. Then, also, while from the superabundance of food and drink the marrow begins to burn, because immoderate food and drink supply some fire to the marrow, and their juice leads the marrow and blood somewhat into motion. And thus, the burning marrow shakes the delight of the flesh's taste in the blood, and brings forth the foam of emission without the man knowing into the genitals, which, however, does not occur from the heat of summer or from the heat of the man's clothing, or rarely happens. But because then the body rests and nothing works, then the soul, which when the body was awake was occupied in many causes with it, brings forth its knowledge, with which it works in the body, as its eyes in dreams, and looks around, because then the different work of the body does not hinder.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de Adam et eius prophetia, Nam cum deus soporem in Adam misit, anima eius in vera prophetia interim multa vidit, quia nondum aliquod peccatum habebat. Ita et dormiente homine anima eius in vera prophetia multa videret, si idem homo peccatis gravatus non esset. De somniis, Sed tamen, quoniam anima hominis a deo est, aliquando vera et futura corpore dormiente videt et scit ea, quae futura sunt homini , quae ita interdum contingunt. Saepe etiam evenit, quod aut diabolica illusione fatigata aut turbata mente gravata perfecte non potest ea videre et decipitur.
text_translation: Item de Adam et eius prophetia. Nam cum deus soporem in Adam misit, anima eius in vera prophetia interim multa vidit, quia nondum aliquod peccatum habebat. Ita et dormiente homine anima eius in vera prophetia multa videret, si idem homo peccatis gravatus non esset. De somniis. Sed tamen, quoniam anima hominis a deo est, aliquando vera et futura corpore dormiente videt et scit ea, quae futura sunt homini, quae ita interdum contingunt. Saepe etiam evenit, quod aut diabolica illusione fatigata aut turbata mente gravata perfecte non potest ea videre et decipitur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam multotiens cogitationibus et opinionibus atque voluntatibus, quibus homo vigilans occu patur, his etiam et in somnis gravatur, et in eis interdum ita elevatur ut fermentum, quod massam farinae elevat, sive cogitationes illae bonae sive malae sint. Quod si bonae et sanotae sunt, gratia dei illi saepe yera"^ in eis ostendit; si autem vanae sunt, diabolus boG videns animam hominis illius multotiens tunc terret et mendacia sua cogitationibus illis iatermiscet.
text_translation: For often, by thoughts and opinions and wills, by which a vigilant man is occupied, these same things weigh upon him even in sleep, and in them sometimes he is so elevated as yeast which raises a mass of flour, whether those thoughts are good or bad. If they are good and holy, the grace of God often shows the truth in them; if however they are vain, the devil, seeing the soul of that man, often then terrifies and mixes his lies with those thoughts.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et de sanctis bominibus turpia saepe in irrisione sua demonstrat. Oum enim bomo aut inepta laetitia aut tristitia aut ira aut angustiis aat ambitione dominandi aut aliis buiusmodi causis in animo occupatus obdormit, boc ei diabolus illusione sua multotiens in somnis proponit, quoniam cum vigilaret, ea in ipso vidit. Sed et cum in delectatione lo camis interdum obdormit, banc ei etiam et diabolica illusio interdum obicit, ita quod viventium corpora ei ostendit et etiam aliquando corpora mortuorum, cum quibus aliquando familiaritatem babuit vel quos etiam nnmquam corporalibus oculis vidit, ita quod sibi videtur, quod cum eis boc modo in peccatis et in pollutionibus delectatur, quasi vigilet vel quasi illi etiara, qui defuncti sunt, vivunt; ita quod etiam turpia in semine suo sibi accidunt.
text_translation: But also, from the holy men, often shameful things are shown in their mockery. For when a man is occupied in his mind with inept joy or sorrow or anger or distress or ambition to rule or other similar causes, the devil by his illusion often presents these things to him in dreams, because when he was awake, he saw them in himself. But also, when he falls asleep in the place of delight, the devil's illusion sometimes strikes him, so that he sees the bodies of the living and even sometimes the bodies of the dead, with whom he sometimes had familiarity or whom he never saw with his bodily eyes, so that it seems to him that he delights in sins and pollutions with them as if he were awake or as if those who are dead still live; so that also shameful things befall him in his seed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam diabolus cum vigilante bomine cum suggestionibus suis bacbatur, ideo etiam eum et in somnis fatigat. Sed quod anima corpori infixa est, ei tam dormienti quam vigilanti quamvis invita multotiens consentit et diversos motus in eo movet; quia sicut aer in aqua rotam moIendiQi cireumfert et illud molere facit, sic etiam corpus et dormientis et vigilantis bominis ad diversa opera movet. De animae effectu, Nam sicut sol lux diei est, sic et anima lux vigilantis corporis est, et ut luna lux noctis est, ita etiam et anima lux dormientis corporis est.
text_translation: And since the devil, with the vigilant body, with his suggestions, is struck, therefore he also tires him even in sleep. But because the soul, fixed in the body, to it, whether sleeping or vigilant, although reluctant, often consents and moves diverse motions in it; for just as air in water surrounds the millstone and causes it to grind, so also the body, of the sleeping and vigilant man, moves to diverse works. Of the soul's effect, For just as the sun is the light of the day, so the soul is the light of the vigilant body, and as the moon is the light of the night, so also the soul is the light of the sleeping body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim corpus dormientis bominis in recta temperie est, ita quod medulla eius recta mensura et modo ipsum calefacit, et quod etiam tempestatem vitiorum et contrarietatem morum in se non babet, tunc saepissime vera videt, quia scientia animae eius tunc in quiete est, velut luna splendorem suum clare et pleniter emittit, cum in nocte abs- que tnrbine nubium et ventorum est. Si autem tempestas variarum et contrariarum cogitationum mentem et corpus 6» vigilaDtis occupat, ita quod ille in tempestate hac obdormit, tunc saepissime falsa sunt, quae dormiens yidet, quoniam scientia animae eins in his contrarietatibus hoc modo obnubilata est, quod vera videre non potest; yelut luna in tempestatibus nubium clare non valet apparere.
text_translation: When indeed the body of the sleeping human is in right temperance, so that its marrow warms it rightly in measure and manner, and also does not possess in itself the storm of vices and the contrariety of customs, then often it sees truly, because the knowledge of its soul then is in quiet, as the moon emits its brightness clearly and fully when in the night without turbulence of clouds and winds. If however the storm of various and contrary thoughts occupies the mind and body of the awake, so that he in this storm falls asleep, then often what the sleeping one sees is false, because the knowledge of his soul is thus obscured in these contrarieties, so that it cannot see truly; as the moon in storms of clouds cannot clearly appear.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam anima ignis est, halitum dormientis hominis moderate inducit et educit, ne corpus illud vastetur; yelat figulus yas suum ad ignem coquens cayet, ne ignis snpra modum calidus aut frigidus sit, quia si immoderate calidas esset, yas fragile factum ex toto dilaberetur. De halitu. Quod si homo halitum ingredientem et egredientem se non haberet, tunc etiam corporali motioue careret nec sanguis eius liquidus esset nec flueret, sicut etiam nec aqua fluit sine ductu aeris.
text_translation: And since the soul is fire, it moderately introduces and withdraws the breath of the sleeping man, so that that body may not be wasted; just as the potter covers his vessel when cooking it over fire, so that the fire may not be excessively hot or cold, for if it were excessively hot, the vessel would be entirely shattered. On the breath. If a man did not have the breath entering and exiting, then he would also lack bodily movement, and his blood would not be liquid nor flow, just as water does not flow without the duct of air.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De animae et carnis contrarietate. Sed et anima est spiraculum ad bona tendens, caro autem ad peccata, et raro et yix corpus interdum continere potest, quin peccet, sicut et sol yermiculis resistere non yalet, quin de terra in illo loco exeant, quem ipse in splendore et calore suo calefacit. Sed et anima est insufflatio corporis ut follis ignis, quia, ubi ligna et prunae ponuntur, ibi follis ignem accendit; ita etiam et anima cum corpore et ossibus et neryis et came compaginata ad quaeque opera se inclinat; nec haec dimittere potest, quamdiu in corpore est, quia meduUa ossibus et cami atque omnibus membris ita inflxa est ut homo, qui trunco ligni sic infigitur, quod se ab illo eraere non yalet.
text_translation: On the opposition of soul and flesh. But the soul is a breath tending toward good, whereas flesh tends toward sins, and rarely and mix the body sometimes can contain, unless it sins, just as the sun cannot resist worms, unless they emerge from the earth to that place which he himself warms with his splendor and heat. But the soul is also the blowing of the body like the bellows of fire, because where wood and brushwood are placed, there the bellows kindles fire; so also the soul, joined with the body and bones and nerves and flesh, inclines itself toward every work; and it cannot cease doing so as long as it is in the body, because the marrow of the bones and the flesh and all the members are so inflamed that a man, who is thus fixed in the trunk of wood, cannot remove himself from it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Attamen ut aqua ignem interdum extinguit, ne supra modum ardeat, sic et anima per gratiam dei adiuta et per rationalitatem admonita yitia peccatorum aliquando deprimit, ne supra modum exsurgant et excrescant. Sed postquam medulla dormientis crescendo recreata fuerit, et postquam anima totam compaginem dormientis corporis composuerit, ipsa suayem yentum, quem de meduUa ad quietem hominis emisit, ad se recolligit, et sic homo eyigilat. Cum autem interdum saepe yigilat, et cum iterum totiens repente obdormit, tunc meduUa nondum plenitudinem et fortitudinem suam recepit, nec etiam membra ad recreationem suam composita smit.
text_translation: Nevertheless, as water sometimes extinguishes fire, lest it burn beyond measure, so also the soul, aided by the grace of God and admonished by reason, sometimes suppresses the sins of sinners, lest they rise and grow beyond measure. But after the marrow of the sleeping one has been refreshed by growth, and after the soul has composed the entire structure of the sleeping body, it collects back to itself the vapor which it emitted from the marrow toward the quiet of man, and thus man wakes. When, however, man often awakens and again repeatedly falls asleep suddenly, then the marrow has not yet received its fullness and strength, nor have the limbs been composed for their recreation.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed homine saepe evigilante et saepe deinde citius obdormiente mednlla et membra eius tanto suavius et lenius refocillantur, velut infans, cum saepe sugit et saepe sugere desistit, interim vires suas ad refocillationem suam recolligit. De anvmae excitatione, Multotiens etiam eyenit, qnod homo dormiens in latere seu in aliqua alia parte corporis sui graviter et dure iaceat seu ut tactu alicuius rei tangatur aut ut infirmitate corporis sui gravetur.
text_translation: But when a man often awakens and then quickly falls asleep again, his medulla and limbs are thus more sweetly and gently refreshed, as an infant who often sucks and often ceases to suck, gathering his forces again for his own refreshment. Concerning the excitation of the soul, often also occurs that a man lying asleep on his side or on some other part of his body lies heavily and hard, or is touched by the contact of some thing, or is weighed down by the weakness of his own body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t quia homo sensibilis est, anima gravedines et contrarietates istas sentiens et corpus suum ex hoc debilitari videns vires suas recolligit et ventum, quam a medulla emisit, retrahit et hominem sic a sopore suscitat. Sed et cum aliquando strepitus aut sonitus aut vox in clamore circa corpus dormientis fit, ita quod aer ob hoc reboat, aer ille exterior aerem, qui in homine est, reverberat, quoniam et elementa in homine sunt. £t anima in homine aerem motum sentiens illum retractis viribus suis ad vigilandum movet.
text_translation: And because man is sensitive, the soul perceives these heavinesses and contrarieties and, seeing the body thus weakened, gathers its forces again and draws back the wind which it had emitted from the medulla, thereby rousing the man from sleep. But also, when sometimes a noise, sound, or voice in a shout occurs around the sleeping body, so that the air thereby resounds, that external air reverberates the air which is in the man, because elements are also in the man. And the soul, sensing the moved air in the man, moves it back with its forces toward wakefulness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Saepe vero evenit, quod aliquis homo vel repentiuo strepitu vel tactu vel alio quolibet modo repente et difficulter evigilet. Unde venae et sanguis eius in contrarium modum excitantur, ita quod multotiens ob hoc in corpore dolet aut quod acutam febrem aut tertianam febrem incurrit, quia anima eius in ipso repentina festinatione mota est. Sed cum tandem recta moderatione homo evigilat, acutior in ingenio et hilarior in vultu multotiens apparet, quod tunc omnia membra eius compaginata sunt in requie.
text_translation: Often indeed it happens, that some man either by sudden noise or touch or by some other manner suddenly and with difficulty awakens. Wherefore his veins and blood are excited in the opposite manner, so that often on account of this in the body there is pain or that he incurs an acute fever or tertian fever, because his soul in the very sudden haste has been moved. But when finally the man awakens with correct moderation, more acute in nature and more cheerful in countenance often appears, which then all his limbs are compaginated in rest.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De aonmi nimietaie. Si quis autem multum et supra modum dormit, diversas et malas febres inde facile habebit atque caliginem oculorum inde incurrit, quia oculi eius in dormitione supra modum diu clausi sunt, velut si aliquis splendorem solis diu inspiceret et inde caliginem oculorum sibi inducerei Si quis autem moderate dormit, Banitatem inde habebit. Qui vero multnm et supra modum vigilat, debilitatem corporis incurrit et aliquantum vires HILDE6ARDIS inde perdit et in sensn aliqiiantam eYacaator, atqne caro^ qnae circa ocalos eins est, inde dolet et mbet ac evolyitar.
text_translation: De aonio nimietate. Si quis autem multum et supra modum dormit, diversas et malas febres inde facile habebit atque caliginem oculorum inde incurrit, quia oculi eius in dormitione supra modum diu clausi sunt, velut si aliquis splendorem solis diu inspiceret et inde caliginem oculorum sibi induceret. Si quis autem moderate dormit, Banitatem inde habebit. Qui vero multum et supra modum vigilat, debilitatem corporis incurrit et aliquantum vires Hildegardis inde perdit et in sensu aliquantam eYacaator, atque caro, quae circa oculos eius est, inde dolet et mbet ac evolyitar.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen aciem et pnpillam et visam ocoloram non moltam laedit; qai aatem moderate yigilat, sanitatem corporis retinebit. Saepe aatem evenit, qaod homo yigil est et dormire non potest, cum animus eins diversis cogitationibas et qualitatibas atque contrarietatibas occapatas est aut magna laetitia detentus. Nam cum in tristitia seu in timore seu in angustiis seu in ira seu in aliis his diyersitatibus et cogitationibus est, tunc multotiens sanguis, qui in eo est, in inquietudinem vertitar; et yenae, quae suavem ventum soporis suscipere debebant, aliquantum contrahunlur, ita quod eum excipere non possont.
text_translation: But the acuity and the pupil and the vision of the color are not much harmed; who, but moderately watches, will retain the body's health. Often, but it happens that a man is watchful and cannot sleep, when his mind is occupied with various thoughts and qualities and contrarieties or detained by great joy. For when in sadness or in fear or in distress or in anger or in other these diversities and thoughts is, then often the blood, which is in him, turns into restlessness; and the veins, which should receive the gentle wind of sleep, contract somewhat, so that they cannot receive him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t cum etiam interdum aliquid viderit vel audierit vel aliud ei quicquam acciderit, unde ultra modum laetari poterit, tunc venae eias ad laetitiam referuntur nec saavem ventum soporis retinere valent, ita quod ille rectam temperiem in se non habet, et inde pervigil manet, usque dnm re illa in mente sua contentus resipiscat, ac si venae ad rectum modum redeunt, et homo ille dormitat. Sed et cum homo gravi infirraitate aliquando opprimitur, tunc etiam sanguis et humores, qui in ipso sunt, in qaandam contrarietatem vertuntur et velut quasdam procellas et tempestates in eo faciunt, et ita propter diversitates istas quiescere non valet, sed insomnis et pervigil contra sospitatem et voluntatem suam semper permanet.
text_translation: And when, sometimes, he sees or hears something or something else happens to him, from which he may be excessively joyful, then his veins are referred to joy and cannot often retain the wind of sleep, so that he does not have the right temperance within himself, and thus remains wakeful until that thing in his mind is content and recovers, as if the veins return to the right measure, and that man sleeps. But when a man is sometimes oppressed by grave weakness, then the blood and humors, which are in him, turn into a certain contrary state and as if they make certain storms and tempests in him, and thus because of these diversities he cannot rest, but remains insomniac and wakeful against his sleep and his own will always.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Homo quoqne cum dormit, in alio visu est quam cum vigilat, et ideo cum post dormitionem evigilat, quia tunc in sopore fuit, visu suo claritatem cito capere non potest, et cum in SOtenebris est, eundem visum, quem in tenebris habuit, ad claritatem cito convertere non valet. Simili modo, qui in claritate est, visum suum ad alium modam in tenebns repente retorquere (non potest, nisi post aliquam moram, velut cum homo verba exterius sonat, cogitatio interius latet.
text_translation: Man also, when he sleeps, has a different sight than when he is awake, and therefore, when he awakens after sleep, because he was in sleep, he cannot quickly grasp clarity with his sight, and when he is in darkness, he cannot quickly convert the same sight which he had in darkness toward clarity. Similarly, he who is in clarity cannot suddenly turn his sight back toward darkness (he cannot do so, unless after some delay, just as when a man speaks words externally, thought lies internally hidden.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De exercUio, Masculus autem, qui in corpore sanus est, si diu ambulat aut si diu erectus stat, inde non multum laeditar, quia in commotione corporis est, ita tamen, ne supra modum vadat aut stet. Qui vero debilis est, sedeat, quia, si iste multnm iret aut staret, inde laederetur. Mulier autem, quia fragilior yiro est, et quia diversam calvariam habet, moderate ambulet et stet, sed magis sedeat, quam discurrat, ne inde laedatur. Qui yero in equo vadit, inde non multum laeditur, quamvis inde fatigetur, quoniam in aere et in aura yersatur, sed pedibus et cmribus suis interdum subveniat, ita quod eos in motione et extensione interdum exerceat.
text_translation: On Exercise. The man, however, who is healthy in body, if he walks for a long time or stands upright for a long time, is not greatly harmed, because in the movement of the body, nevertheless, he should not go beyond measure or stand beyond measure. He who is indeed weak, let him sit, because if this one were to go or stand much, he would be harmed. The woman, however, because she is more fragile than the man, and because she has a different head covering, let her walk and stand moderately, but let her sit more than run, lest she be harmed. He who indeed rides on a horse is not greatly harmed, although he is fatigued, because he is carried in air and in breeze, but let him sometimes assist with his feet and arms, so that they are sometimes exercised in motion and extension.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: lO 2e sanguinea, Quaedam autem feminae pinguis naturae sunt et molles et deliciosas carnes habent et graciles venas atque rectum sanguinem absque tabe. Et quoniam venae earum graciles sunt, ideo minus sanguinis in se babent, atque caro earum tanto plus crescit et tanto plus sanguine permixta est. Et istae claram et albam faciem habent et in amplexione amoriis sunt et amabiles atque in artibus subtiles et per se ipsas in animo suo continentes et modice emuentem sanguinem in rivulis menstrui temporis patiuntur, atque vasculum matricis earum fortiter positum est ad pariendum, unde etiam fecundae sunt et virile semen concipere possunt.
text_translation: 10. 2e sanguinea. Quaedam autem feminae pinguis naturae sunt et molles et deliciosas carnes habent et graciles venas atque rectum sanguinem absque tabe. Et quoniam venae earum graciles sunt, ideo minus sanguinis in se habent, atque caro earum tanto plus crescit et tanto plus sanguine permixta est. Et istae claram et albam faciem habent et in amplexione amoriis sunt et amabiles atque in artibus subtiles et per se ipsas in animo suo continentes et modice emuentem sanguinem in rivulis menstrui temporis patiuntur, atque vasculum matricis earum fortiter positum est ad pariendum, unde etiam fecundae sunt et virile semen concipere possunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen plurimos pueros non generantur, et si istae absque maritis sunt, ita quod prolem non pariunt, facile dolent in corpore; si autem maritos habent, sanae sunt. Quod si guttae sanguinis in menstruo tempore ante naturale tempus in istis clauduntur, ita quod non effluunt, tunc interdum aut melancolicae erunt aut dolorem lateris patientur, aut vermis in came earum erescet, aut effluentes glandes, quae scrofulae dicuntur, in eis erumpent, aut lepra, quae tamen moderata est, in eis crescei De fkgmaticis, Sed quaedam aliae feminae sunt, quarum cames non multnm crescunt, quia grossas venas habent et aliquantum sanum sanguinem et album sed modicum veneni in se continentem, unde album colorem contrahit.
text_translation: But nevertheless, many children are not generated, and if these women are without husbands, so that they do not bear offspring, they easily suffer in the body; if, however, they have husbands, they are healthy. If, however, drops of blood in the menstrual time are blocked before the natural time in these women, so that they do not flow out, then sometimes they will be melancholic or suffer pain in the side, or a worm will grow in their flesh, or effluent glands, which are called scrofulae, will erupt in them, or a leprosy, which is nevertheless moderate, will grow in them. Of the flegmatici, but certain other women exist, whose flesh does not grow much, because they have thick veins and somewhat healthy blood and white, but a little poison contained within them, from which they take on a white color.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et severam faciem et subnigri coloris habent et strenuae et utiles sunt ac aliquantum virilem animum tenent, atque nec nimis parom nec nimis mnltum sed moderate effluentes rivulos sanguinis in menstruo tempore sustinent. Et quoniam grossas yenas habent, plurimum fecundae sunt in prole et facile concipiunt, quod etiam matrix et omnia viscera earum fortiter posita sunt. Sed viros sibi attrahunt et eos post se ducunt, et ideo viri eas amant. Quod si a viris se continere Yolunt, se continere a coniunctione eorum possunt, nec inde multum quamvis parum debilitantur.
text_translation: And they have a stern face and a darkish color, and are strong and useful, and hold somewhat a manly spirit, and sustain in the menstrual time rivulets of blood neither too little nor too much, but moderately flowing. And because they have thick veins, they are very fertile in offspring and conceive easily, since also their womb and all their viscera are strongly situated. But they attract men and lead them after themselves, and therefore men love them. For if they wish to restrain themselves from men, they can restrain themselves from their union, and they are not much weakened thereby, even though a little.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen, si viros in coniunctione devitaverint, difflciles et graves in moribus suis erunt, si autem cum viris fuerint, ita quod se a coniunctione eorum continere noluerint, incontinentes et superfluae secundum viros in libidine erunt. Et quod etiam aliquantum viriles sunt propter viriditatem, quam in se habent, aliquantum lanuginis circa mentum interdum emittunt. Si autem rivulus sanguinis in menstruo tempore ante naturale tempus in eis strangulatur, tunc interdum aut insaniam capitis, quae est frenesis, incurrunt, aut spleneticae aut hydropicae erunt, aut exstantes cames, quae semper in ulceribus sunt, in eis crescunt, aut in aliquo membro suo supercrescentem camem, velut quaedam pustula in aliqua arbore vel in aliquo pomo est, emittent.
text_translation: But nevertheless, if they avoid men in conjunction, they will be difficult and grave in their manners; if, however, they are with men, so that they do not wish to contain themselves from their conjunction, they will be incontinent and superfluous according to men in lust. And they are also somewhat virile because of the greenness they possess within themselves, and sometimes emit downy hair around the chin. If, however, the stream of blood is strangled in them before the natural time during the menstrual period, then sometimes they incur either insanity of the head, which is frenesy, or they will be splenetic or hydropic, or emerging tumors, which are always in ulcers, will grow in them, or they will emit a superabundant tumor in some member, as a certain pustule is in some tree or in some fruit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De colerica, Aliae autem quaedam feminae sunt, quae tenues carnes sed grossa ossa habent et moderatas venas et spissum ac mbeum sanguinem, et pallidi coloris in facie sunt et pmdentes sunt ac benevolentiam tenent, et eis reverentia ab hominibus exhibetur, et timentur. Sed plurimum sanguinem in menstruis patiuntur, et matrix in eis fortiter posita est, ac fecundae sunt. £t viri mores eamm amant, sed tamen eas aliquantum devitando fugiunt, quoniam ipsae illos alliciendo iUos post se non trahunt.
text_translation: On the choleric, Other certain women are, who have thin flesh but thick bones and moderate veins and thick and mucous blood, and are pale in color on the face and prudent and hold benevolence, and reverence is shown by men toward them, and they are feared. But they suffer much blood in menstruation, and the womb in them is firmly placed, and they are fertile. And men's customs they love, but nevertheless they somewhat avoid them, because they do not drag them after themselves by enticing them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si in coniunctione maritomm sunt, castae sunt et fidem uxomm illis servant atque cum eis sanae sunt in corpore, et si maritis camerint, dolebunt in corpore, et debiles emnt tam de hoc, quod nesciunt, cui hondni femineam fidem servare possint, quam de hoc, quod maritos non habent. Et si fluenta menstrui temporis prius, quam iustum sit, in eis cessayerint, facile paralyticae erunt et in humoribus suis diffluunt, ita quod in eisdem humoribus snis infirmae erunt aut quod in iecore dolebunt aut quod etiam facile nigrum tumorem dragunculi incurrunt aut quod ubera earum de cancro ingrossantur.
text_translation: If in the union of husbands they are, they are chaste and keep the faith of wives to them and are healthy with them in the body, and if they are absent from husbands, they will suffer in the body, and they will be weak both because of this, that they do not know to whom they can keep the female faith, and because of this, that they have no husbands. And if the flow of the menstrual time ceases before it is just, they will easily become paralyzed and their humors will flow, so that in their same humors they will be weak or they will suffer in the liver or they will also easily incur a black tumor of the dragon or their breasts will become swollen from cancer.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De melancolica, Sed aliae quaedem feminae sunt, quae macres cames habent et grossas venas ac moderata ossa et sanguinem magis livosum quam sanguineum, et quae etiam faciem velut glauco et nigro colore permixtam lo habent. Et istae etiam yentosae et yagae in cogitationibus snis sunt et taediosae in molestia tabescentes, et sunt etiam diffluentis naturae, ita quod etiam interdum melancolia fatigantur. Sed et plurimum sanguinem in menstruo tempore patiuntur et stenlei^ sunt, quia debilem et fra- gilem matricem habent.
