text_translation: Of melancholia, Indeed, other women are such that they have lean flesh and thick veins and moderate bones and blood more livid than sanguineous, and they also have a face mixed with a glaucous and blackish color. And these women are also sorrowful and languid in their thoughts and tedious in wasting away, and they are also of a flowing nature, so that sometimes they are fatigued by melancholia. But they also suffer much blood during the menstrual time and are narrow, because they have a weak and fragile womb.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde semen yiri nec concipere nec retinere nec calefacere possunt, et ideo etiam saniores, fortiores et laetiores sunt absque maritis quam cum eis, qaoniam, si cum maritis fuerint, debiles reddentur. Sed yiri ab eis declinant et eas fugiunt, quia ipsae yiros affa- 2Q biliter non alloquuntur, et quoniam yiros modice diligunt. Et si istae ad horam aliqu9tn delectationem camis habuerint, cito tamen in eis deficit. Sed quaedam ex his, si cum robustis et sanguineis maritis fuerint, tunc interdum, cum ad fortem aetatem yelut quinquaginta annorum per- yenerint, saltem infantem unum pariunt.
text_translation: Therefore, the semen of men cannot conceive, retain, or warm, and therefore they are also healthier, stronger, and happier without husbands than with them, since, if they are with husbands, they become weak. But men turn away from them and flee from them, because they do not speak kindly to men, and because they moderately love men. And if these women have some pleasure of the bed at a certain time, however quickly it is lacking in them. But some of them, if they are with strong and sanguineous husbands, then sometimes, when they arrive at a strong age like fifty years, at least they bear one child.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem cum aliis maritis fuerint, quorum natura debilis est, tunc ab illis non concipiunt, sed steriles permanebunt. Quod si menstrua in eis defecerint, antequam iustum secundum naturam feminarum fuerit, tunc aliquando podagram aut intumentia crura habebunt aut insaniam capitis, quam melancolia ezcitat, incurrent, aut dolorem dorsi et renum aut cito corpore intumescent, quoniam tabes et foeditas iUa, quae per menstrua in corporibus eanun purgari de buit, in eis obstmsae remanent; et si istis in infirmitate non succurritur, ita quod ab ea per adiutorium dei seu per medicinam liberatae non fuerint, cito morientur.
text_translation: If, however, they are with other husbands, whose nature is weak, then they do not conceive from them, but remain sterile. If their menstruation ceases before it is just according to the nature of women, then sometimes they will have gout or swollen legs, or insanity of the head, which melancholy excites, or pain in the back and kidneys, or quickly swell the body, because the decay and foulness, which should be purged from their bodies through menstruation, remain obstinate in them; and if help is not given to them in this infirmity, so that they are not liberated from it through the aid of God or through medicine, they will quickly die.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ccUvitie. Homo, cuius calvitium grande et latixm est, fortem calorem in se habet. Et calor iste et sudor capitis eius crines eiciunt, sed humor halitus ipsius fertilis est et carnes, ubi barba crescit, humectat, ita quod ibi plurimi crines exsurgunt. Sed homo, qui penuriam criniam in barba habet, sed habundantiam in vertice, iste frigidiis et aliquantum infertilis est, et cum halitus cames circa os hominis eiusdem tangit, easdem cames infecundas facit.
text_translation: On Baldness. A man whose baldness is great and wide has a strong heat within him. And that heat and the sweat of his head drive out the hairs, but the vapor of his breath is fertile and moistens the flesh where the beard grows, so that many hairs arise there. But a man who has a scarcity of hair in the beard but abundance on the crown is cold and somewhat infertile, and when the vapor of the flesh around the mouth of the same man touches those same fleshes, it makes them infertile.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed postquam alicui homini crines in calvitio ceciderint, amplios nullo medicamine restaurari poterunt, quoniam humectatio et viriditas, quam in cute capitis, id est in hirneschedele suo, prius habebat, iam examit, nec amodo ulla viriditas ibi exsurgere poterit, unde etiam capilli amodo ibi non renascentur. Et ideo saepissime evenit, ut quomm cal vitium grande et latum est, homm barba etiam grandis et lata fit, et qui tenuitatem et penuriam in barba habent, hi tanto plus in vertice crinibus habundant.
text_translation: But after a man's hair has fallen out in baldness, no longer can it be restored by any medicine, because the moisture and greenness which the scalp, that is, his criniceps, previously had, has now been consumed, and no greenness can now arise there, whence also the hair will not regenerate there. And therefore, it often happens that when baldness is great and widespread, the man's beard also becomes large and wide, and those who have scarcity and lack in the beard have so much more in the vertex in hair.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De capitis dolore, Acuta febris et cotidiana et tertiana atque quartana, sed et aliae febres, melancoliam interdum tangunt, et illa sic tacta aquosum fumum ad ;aput et ad cerebmm hominis mittit atque illud gravi et continuo dolore infirmari facit. De emigranea, Sed et emigranea de melancolia et de omnibus malis humoribus, qxd in homine sunt, nascitnr, et medietatem capitis hominis occupat et non totum , ita quod nunc in dextra parte capitis nunc in sinistra est; videlicet cum humores superhabundant , dextram partem occupat, cum vero melancolia supercreverit , sinistram.
text_translation: On headache, Acute fever and daily and tertian and quartan fever, but also other fevers, sometimes touch melancholy, and that melancholy thus touched sends a watery smoke to the head and to the brain of man and makes that weakened by a heavy and continuous pain. On migraine, But also migraine arises from melancholy and from all the bad humors which are in man, and occupies half the head of man and not the whole, thus that now is in the right part of the head now in the left; namely, when humors are excessive, it occupies the right part, when indeed melancholy has grown excessive, the left.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam emigranea tantam fortitudinem in se habet, quod si totum caput hominis simul occuparet, homo pati non posset. £t haec difficile expelli poterit, quia, quod melancoliam interdum compescit, malos humores interdum excitat, et quod malos humores interdum sedat, melancoliam interdum crescere facit, et ideo difficile curationem recipit, quoniam melancolia et mali humores difficile simul sedantur. Item de capitis dolore. Cibus vero, qui madidum succum in se habet, velut succus herbamm hortomm et velnt succus pomorum est, sine arido cibo panis saepius comestus dolorem capitis homini interdum infert, qui tamen cito sedatur, quoniam de leni succo oritur.
text_translation: For migraine possesses such strength that if it were to occupy the entire head of a man at once, the man would not be able to endure it. And this is difficult to expel, because that which sometimes restrains melancholy sometimes excites bad humors, and that which sometimes calms bad humors sometimes causes melancholy to grow, and therefore it receives treatment with difficulty, because melancholy and bad humors are difficult to calm simultaneously. Also concerning headache. Indeed, food that contains moist juice, such as the juice of herbs from gardens and the juice of fruits, when eaten more frequently with dry bread, sometimes inflicts headache upon a man, which nevertheless is quickly soothed, because it arises from a mild juice.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et flegma in homine multotiens superhabundat et ita exsur- ' git et ad caput illius tendit et venas temporum, quae frontem confortant, concutit et sic frontem dolere facit. De vertigine. Cum etiam homo absque magistratione doctrinae superiorum et sine ulla necessitate per yoluntatem suam plurimis et diversis cogitationibus frequenter occupatur, iste bumoribus rectum iter aufert, ita quod ille lO interdum praeceps, interdum tardus absque recto ordine suo est9 et ideo caput eiusdem hominis in vertiginem yertitur, ita quod scientia et sensus illius evaeuantur.
text_translation: But also phlegm in man often superabounds and thus exsurgit and tends toward his head and shakes the veins of the temples, which strengthen the forehead, and thus causes the forehead to ache. Concerning vertigo. When also a man, without the guidance of higher teachings and without any necessity by his own will, is frequently occupied with many and various thoughts, this humor deprives the right course, so that he is sometimes precipitous, sometimes slow, without his proper order, and therefore the head of that man turns into vertigo, so that his knowledge and senses fade away.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De amentia. Et cum praedictae pestes simul omnes convenerint, ita quod in capite hominis simul furiunt, bominem illum in amentiam ducunt et evertunt et inanem rectae scientiae faciunt, velut cum navis a procellis mota confringitur. Unde multi putant ipsum a daemone possessum esse ; quod non est; sed daemones huic pesti et dolori accurrunt et insidias ponunt, secundum quod officia insaniae so eorum sunt; et tamen proprietatem yerborum suorum ibi non habent, quoniam homo ille a daemone possessus non est.
text_translation: On madness. And when the aforementioned plagues have all come together, so that they rage together in the head of a man, they lead that man into madness, overturn him, and make him empty of right knowledge, as a ship moved by storms is broken. Hence many think that he is possessed by a demon; which is not the case; but demons rush to this plague and pain and set traps, according to which the offices of insanity are theirs; and yet they do not possess the property of their own words there, because that man is not possessed by a demon.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam si daemon proprietatem verborum suorum divina permissione in aliquo homine habuerit, spolia sua verbis et furiis ibi in loco spiritus sancti tam diu exercet, quousque deus illum expellit, quemadmodum de caelo eum expulit. 26 cerehro, Cerebrum a bonis et a malis humoribus, qui in homine sunt, tangitur; ac ideo semper molle et humidum est. Quod si forte aliquando exsiccatur, mox in inflrmitatem ducitur. Ipsum enim valde humidum et pingue naturaliter est ac etiam est materia scientiae, sapientiae et intellectus hominis, ut haec emittendo et retrahendo contineat; sed et vires cogitationum retinet.
text_translation: For if a demon has the property of its words by divine permission in some man, its spoils are exercised there by words and fury in the place of the Holy Spirit as long as God expels it, just as He cast it out of heaven. 26 cerebro, The cerebrum is touched by good and bad humors which are in man; and therefore it is always soft and moist. If by chance it dries out at any time, it is soon led into weakness. For it is naturally very moist and fatty, and also is the material of man's knowledge, wisdom, and intellect, so that it contains these by emitting and retracting; but also retains the powers of thought.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cogitationes autem cum in corde sedent, aut suavitatem aut amaritudinem habent; suavitas vero cerebrum impin- guat et amaritudo eum evacuat. Et cerebrum itinera habet ut fumarium foramina, ubi fumus educitur, et itinera illa sunt in oculis, in aoribus, in ore, in naribus, ac ibi demonstrantur. Cum autem suavitas in cogitatione est, tunc oculi et aures atque loquela hominis in laetitia se ostendunt. Cum yero amaritudo in illa est, oculi la crimas et in ira ac in tristitia auditum et loquela demonstrant.
text_translation: However, when thoughts dwell in the heart, they have either sweetness or bitterness; sweetness indeed fattens the brain and bitterness evacuates it. And the brain has paths like the openings of a smoke chamber, where smoke is emitted, and those paths are in the eyes, in the ears, in the mouth, in the nostrils, and there they are demonstrated. When, however, sweetness is in thought, then the eyes and ears and the speech of man show themselves in joy. When, however, bitterness is in it, the eyes show tears and in anger and in sadness hearing and speech demonstrate.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Oculi autem hominis ad similitudinem firmamenti facti sunt. JDe oculis. Nam pupilla oculi similitudinem solis habet, et niger seu griseus color, qui circa pupillam est, similitudinem lunae, et exterior albedo similitudinem nubium. Oculus autem ex igne et aqua constat. Igne enim continetur et roboratur, ut sit; aqua vero ad yisum ducitar. Quod si sanguis super oculum hominis superhabundaverit, oculum suffocat ad videndum, quia aquam, quae oculo visum praebet, exsiccat, vel si sanguis ibi supra modum minoratus fiierit, aqua, quae in oculo visum subministrare debuit, iam vires suas non habet, quoniam qui in sanguine ipsi vires velut columna subportare debebat, deficit.
text_translation: The eyes of man are made in the likeness of the firmament. De oculis. For the pupil of the eye has the likeness of the sun, and the black or gray color which is around the pupil has the likeness of the moon, and the exterior whiteness has the likeness of clouds. The eye, however, is composed of fire and water. For fire contains and strengthens it, so that it is; water, however, leads to vision. If blood should abound excessively over the eye of man, it suffocates the eye from seeing, because it dries out the water which the eye provides for vision, or if blood there should be diminished beyond measure, the water which was to supply vision in the eye no longer has its strength, because he who was to bear the strength in the blood, as a column, is lacking.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde in senibus oculi caligant, quia iam in ipsis subtrahitur et aqua cum sanguine in eis attenuatur. Sed iuvenes senibus clarius vident, quoniam venae eorum adhuc in sanguine et aqua recte temperatae sunt, quia ignis et aqua nondum in ipsis calorem et frigus supra modum suum exsiccaverunt et attenuaverunt. De ocidis griseis, Homo autem, qui griseos oculos aquae similes habet, eos praecipue de aere contrahit ; unde et infirmiores aHis oculis (sunt, quia aer de diversa aura caloris et frigoris ac humiditatis saepius mutatur, et etiam idem oculi facile de malo et debili et humido aere et de nebula laeduntur, quoniam, sicut haec puritatem ipsius aeris impediunt, ita etiam oculos de aere contractos laedunt.
text_translation: Therefore, in old age the eyes grow dim, because in them [the eyes] it is subtracted and water with blood is attenuated. But young people see more clearly than the elderly, because their veins are still properly tempered with blood and water, since fire and water have not yet excessively dried out and attenuated the heat and cold in them. Regarding gray eyes, the human being who has gray eyes similar to water contracts them especially from the air; hence, those with such eyes are weaker, because the air frequently changes from diverse currents of heat, cold, and humidity, and also the same eyes are easily harmed by bad, weak, and moist air and by mist, because just as these [factors] hinder the purity of the air itself, so too they harm eyes contracted from the air.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be octdis igneis, Sed qui igneos oculos habet, qui similes sunt nigrae nubi iuxta solem sitae, eos de calido vento austri naturaliter accepit, et sanitatem habent, qiiia de calore igni$ sunt. Sed pulvis ac quilibet malus foetor eos laedit, quoniam serenitas pulverem et puritas incog- ' nitum foetorem negligit. De oculis diversi eoloris. Qui autem ocolos similes nubi habet, in qua iris apparet, eos de aere diversarum aurarum contraxit, quae nec ariditatem nec humiditatem in stabilitate possident, et infirmi sunt, quoniam de instabili aere oriuntur, et in caUda aura obscurum visum habent, quia de igne non sunt, in pura vero pluyiali aura acutum vismn habent, quoniam magis de humiditate quam de igne sunt.
text_translation: Be eight fiery ones, But he who has fiery eyes, which are similar to black clouds situated near the sun, received them naturally from the warm south wind, and they have health, because they are from heat of fire. But dust and any bad odor injure them, because serenity neglects dust and the unknown foul odor. Of eyes of diverse colors. But he who has eyes similar to clouds in which the iris appears, received them from the air of various winds, which neither possess aridity nor humidity in stability, and they are weak, because they arise from unstable air, and in warm breeze have obscure vision, because they are not from fire, but in pure rainy breeze have acute vision, because they are more from humidity than from fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed omnis fulgor, scilicet solis et lunae ac luminum atque pretiosomm lapidum et metallorum atque omnium aliarum rerum eis nocivum est, quia de aere lo diyersarum aurarum est. De octdis turbulentis. Sed qui oculos habet similes turbidae nubi, quae nec ad plenum ignea nec ad plenum turbida est, sed aliquantnm glauca, eos de livida humiditate terrae accepit, quae diversas inutilitates herbarum ac ver- miculorum terrae producit, et moUes sunt ac carnes rubeas eiciunt, quia de livore orti sunt.
text_translation: But every glare, that is, of the sun and moon and lightning and precious stones and metals and all other things harmful to them, because it is from the air of the various auroras. Of the eight turbulent ones. But those who have eyes similar to the turbid cloud, which is neither fully fiery nor fully turbid, but somewhat glauca, they have received from the livid humidity of the earth, which produces various uselessness of herbs and vermiculorum of the earth, and they are soft and eject red flesh, because they arose from lividness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed nec humido aere nec pulvere nec malo foetore nec fnlgore cuiusque rei laeduntur, de qnibus acies aliorum oculorum sauciatur, ut praedictum est, quamvis aliis quibusdam infirmitatibus interdum laedantur. Nam sicut nuUa adversitas obstat, quin de livida humiditate terrae inutiles herbae et vermiculi procedant, ita et visus oculorum istorum praef atis adversitatibus non laeditur. De oculis nigris. Qui vero nigros seu turbilentos oculos habet, ut aliquando nubes est, eos praecipue de terra contraxit; et firmiores et acutiores aliis oculis sunt et acumen oculorum diu retinent, quoniam de fortitudine terrae sunt.
text_translation: But neither by humid air, nor by dust, nor by bad odor, nor by the lightning of any thing are they harmed, from which the sharpness of other eyes is injured, as previously stated, although sometimes they are harmed by other infirmities. For just as no adversity prevents useless herbs and worms from proceeding from the livid humidity of the earth, so also the sight of these eyes is not harmed by the aforementioned adversities. Concerning black eyes. But those who have black or turbid eyes, as sometimes clouds are, they especially drew them from the earth; and they are stronger and sharper than other eyes, and the sharpness of their eyes is retained for a long time, because they are from the fortitude of the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed facile de humore terrae et de humiditate aquarum et paludum laeduntur, quemadmodum et terra de malo humore et de magna humiditate aquarum et paludum inficitur. De oculorum attmgvne. Sed cum cerebrum hominis aliquo modo supra modum impinguatur, pinguedo illa malum humorem et sudorem ad oculos mittit. Qui hamor et sudor cum oculos saepius madefacit et inficit, albuginem de spissitudine humoris et sudoris illius contrahunt, et nisi cito depellatur, cum adhuc recens est, ita exspissatur.
text_translation: But easily from the humor of the earth and from the humidity of waters and marshes they are harmed, just as the earth is infected by bad humor and by great humidity of waters and marshes. Concerning the affection of the eyes. But when the brain of man in some way is excessively fat, that fat sends bad humor and sweat to the eyes. This humor and sweat, when they frequently moisten and infect the eyes, cause the white of the eye to contract from the thickness of that humor and sweat, and unless it is quickly expelled while still fresh, it thus becomes thickened.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: qnod postea prae spissitudine ut fellis auferri non poterit, et ita albugo in oculo hominis nascitur. Sed et ex fri^- ditate humorum et ex melancolia eadem albugo oritur. De octdorum lacrimis. Cum autem a noxas humoribixs in oculis alicuius hominis aqua superhabundaverit, eadem aqua ignem, qui in oculis illis est, suffocat et absorbet, et sic idem oculi humescunt, quasi lacrimentur, et caligare incipiunt. Ad auditum. Gumque stomachus hominis infirmatur, ex ipso interdum flegma in caput eius extenditur et ad aures cadit atque auditum hominis confundit.
text_translation: Quod postea prae spissitudine ut felle auferri non poterit, et ita albugo in oculo hominis nascitur. Sed et ex frigore humorum et ex melancolia eadem albugo oritur. De oculorum lacrimis. Cum autem a noxae humoribus in oculis alicuius hominis aqua superabundaverit, eadem aqua ignem, qui in oculis illis est, suffocat et absorbet, et sic idem oculi humescunt, quasi lacrimentur, et caligare incipiunt. Ad auditum. Quumque stomachus hominis infirmatur, ex ipso interdum flegma in caput eius extenditur et ad aures cadit atque auditum hominis confundit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed flegma aliquando minoratur, aliquando augetur, et facile curari et depelli potest. Mali quoque humores in homine aliquem fumum faciunt et eundem fumum ad caput et ad cerebmm emittunt, et fumus idem ad aures tenditur et auditum obdurat ac diu ibi manet et difficile curatur. De dentium dolore, Et quaedam minutissimae venulae cuteculam, scilicet membranam, in qua cerebrum positum est, circumdant et se ad ginguivas dentium et ad ipsos dentes eztendunt.
text_translation: But phlegm is sometimes diminished, sometimes increased, and can be easily cured and expelled. The bad humors in man also produce some smoke, and the same smoke is emitted to the head and to the brain, and the same smoke is directed to the ears, hardens hearing, and remains there for a long time and is difficult to cure. Concerning the pain of teeth, and certain very tiny veins surround the cuticle, that is, the membrane, in which the brain is placed, and they extend to the gums of the teeth and to the teeth themselves.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quae dum malo et superfluo atque putrido sanguine replentur, et dum a spuma in purgatione cerebri vitiantur, putredinem et dolorem ex «(cerebro ad ginguivas dentium et ad ipsos dentes portant. Et ideo eadem caro, quae circa dentes est, sic intumescit et maxillae hominis illius, et ita homo idem in ginguivis dentium dolet. Si autem homo dentes suos cum aqua lavando in terdum saepe non purgat, exinde livor in came, qui eirca dentes est, interdum crescit et augetur et ex hoc eadem caro infirmatur, ac ita de livore iilo, qui circa dentes inve teratus est, vermes in dentibus aliquando nascuntur, et sic caro circa dentes intumescit, et homo inde dolet.
text_translation: Which, when filled with bad, superfluous, and putrid blood, and when vitiated by the foam in the purgation of the brain, carries putrefaction and pain from the brain to the gums of the teeth and to the teeth themselves. And therefore the same flesh, which is around the teeth, swells thus and the jaws of that man, and so the man himself suffers pain in the gums of the teeth. If, however, a man does not often cleanse his teeth with water in the third [instance], from this a bluish discoloration in the flesh, which is around the teeth, sometimes grows and increases, and from this the same flesh is weakened, and thus from the bluish discoloration, which is rubbed around the teeth, worms sometimes arise in the teeth, and thus the flesh around the teeth swells, and the man suffers pain from this.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De faciei rubore. Sed homo, qui in infirmitate, dum in lecto decumbit, in facie rubet, sanguinem ab infirmis visceribus infirmum et venenosum habet, et ideo in facie rubet. Nam malus humor de venis eius egreditur, qui cames ipsius intrat et pertransit, ita quod exinde in debilitate extolluntur et ingrossescunt, velut minutissimis foraminibus perfossae sint. Et homo iste tristis non est, sed aliquantum laetus, et in iniirmitate sua sufferri potest.
text_translation: On the redness of the face. But the man, who is in weakness, when lying in bed, his face reddens, having blood that is weak and venomous from the weak viscera, and therefore his face reddens. For a bad humor exits from his veins, which enters and passes through his flesh, so that from there they rise and swell in weakness, as if they were dug out with the smallest holes. And this man is not sad, but somewhat joyful, and in his weakness he can suffer.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: ^ De faciei pallore, Qui autem in infirmitate sua pallet et macer est, in illo melancolia ad paralysim coniungitur, ita quod ex hoc frigidus erit, et ideo etiam propter frigus illud in facie pallet et profectum in carnibus suis non habet, ut in illo crescant, et iste in infirmitate sua tristis est et facile in iram movetur. Sed et stomachus dat visceribus hominis humiditatem et vesica tribuit eis aquositatem; unde.etiam ipsa viscera cibos semper hoc et illac lO fandunt et refundunt et ita impinguescunt ac diversos livores habent.
text_translation: Of the pallor of the face. Whoever, in his weakness, is pale and emaciated, in him melancholy is joined to paralysis, so that from this he will be cold, and therefore also because of that coldness in the face, he is pale and does not have a growth in his flesh, so that they may grow in him, and this one, in his weakness, is sad and easily moved to anger. But also the stomach gives the viscera of man humidity and the bladder gives them aqueousness; whence even the viscera themselves always here and there in place chew and return the food, and thus they become swollen and have various discolorations.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De splenis tumore. Sed cum stomachus diversis et noxiis escis irretitur, et cum vesica diversis et noxiis potibus sauciatur, malos humores visceribus afferunt, ac sic malum fumum ad splen emittunt. De cordis dolore. Et illud ex hoc inflatur et intumescit et sauciatur, atque tumore et dolore suo dolorem cordi infert et circa illud livorem parat. Sed et adhuc idem cor forte est et dolori huic resistit. Cum autem praedicti humores in visceribus et in splene hominis superhabundaverint et cordi multas passiones intulerint, ad melancoUam revertuntur et illi se commiscent.
text_translation: On the tumor of the spleen. But when the stomach is flooded with diverse and harmful foods, and when the bladder is injured by diverse and harmful drinks, bad humors are brought to the viscera, and thus emit bad smoke to the spleen. On the pain of the heart. And this (the spleen) swells and swells and is injured, and by its tumor and pain inflicts pain on the heart and prepares a blackness around it. But also still the same heart is strong and resists this pain. When however the aforementioned humors have superabounded in the viscera and in the spleen of the human and have brought many passions to the heart, they return to melancholy and mix with them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic melancolia conunota cum illis in indignatione surgit et cum nigro et malo fumo ad cor ascendit atque multis et repentinis fatigationibus illud fatigat. Unde homines illi tristes et acerbi effecti modicum cibi et modicum potus sumunt, ita quod in corpore attenuantur et quod vix aliquando in corpore subsistunt. Et hi etiam multas eructuationes emittunt. De animae domo, Sed cum quilibet homo domum parat, in illa ianuam et fenestras ac fumarium facit, ita videlicet ut per ianuam egrediatur et ingrediatur, ut quaeque necessaria excipiat, et ut per fenestras lumen habeat, et ut etiam igne accenso fumus per fumarium transeat, ne domus per fnmum corrumpatur.
text_translation: And thus melancholy, noted with them in indignation, arises and with black and bad smoke ascends to the heart and fatigues it with many and sudden fatigues. Hence those people, made sad and bitter, take a little food and a little drink, so that they are weakened in the body and scarcely ever subsist in the body. And these also emit many eructations. Concerning the house of the soul, but when any man prepares a house, in it he makes a door and windows and a chimney, so that through the door it may go out and come in, so that whatever is necessary may be received, and so that through the windows light may be had, and also that when fire is kindled, smoke may pass through the chimney, so that the house may not be corrupted by smoke.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic et anima in corde velut in domo sedens cogitationes velut per ianuam eius emittit et immittit et eas quasi per fenestras considerat ac vires eanim velut accenso igne ad cerebrum velut ad fumarium transducit, ut eas ibi discernendo discutiat. Si enim homo cogitationes non haberet, scientiam non haberet, sed esset ut domus, quae nec ianuam nec fenestras nec famarium habet. Cogitationes autem scientiae boni et mali auctores sunt et ordinatio omnium rerum, et haec cogitationes dicunjj^ur.
text_translation: Thus, the soul sits in the heart as in a house, emitting and receiving thoughts as through its door, and considering them as through windows, and leading the forces of the soul, as fire kindled, to the brain as to a chimney, so that there it may discern and break them apart. For if a man had no thoughts, he would have no knowledge, but would be like a house which has neither door nor windows nor chimney. Thoughts, however, are the authors of good and bad knowledge and the ordering of all things, and these thoughts are called.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cogitationes auctores snnt bonitatis, sapientiae et yanitatis ac similium, ut de corde malae cogitationes exeunt, et hoc est ianua. £t ideo de corde iter procedit ad elementa, cum quibus homo operatur, quod cogitat. £t yires cogitationum ad cerebrum ascendunt, et cerebrum eas continet, quod cerebrum est humiditas totius corporis, quemadmodum ros omnia humectat. Cum autem quidam mali et foetidi humores in homine suscitantur, quendam noxium fumum ad cerebrum emittunt.
text_translation: For thoughts are the authors of goodness, wisdom, justice, and similar qualities, and from the heart proceed evil thoughts, and this is the door. And therefore from the heart the process proceeds to the elements, with which man operates, which he thinks. And the forces of thoughts ascend to the brain, and the brain contains them, for the brain is the humidity of the entire body, just as dew moistens all things. When, however, certain bad and foul humors are aroused in man, they emit some noxious smoke toward the brain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pulmonis dolore. Unde cerebrum conmiotam eundem fumum per quasdam venas ad pulmonem ducit et ipsnm inficit, ita quod aliquantum inflatur et quod halitum ali quantulum difficile emittit et quod ei malum odorem reddit. Sed haec inflatio puhnonis hon multum periculosa est, quia facile curari potest. De asmate, Sed alii quidam homines sunt, quorum caput sanum et tam forte (est , quod praedicti mali humores cerebrum eorum laedere non valent, quoniam ad illud pertingere non possunt.
text_translation: On the pain of the lungs. From whence the brain conducts the same smoke through certain veins to the lung and infects it, so that it swells somewhat and emits a little breath with difficulty and gives it a bad odor. But this inflation of the lung is not very dangerous, because it can be easily cured. On asthma. But certain other men are, whose head is healthy and so strong that the aforementioned bad humors cannot injure their brain, because they cannot reach it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t quia ipsi humores surgere non possunt, ad cerebrum in canali gutturis eorundem hominum remanent et illud ad debilitatem ducnnt, ita quod homines illi cum difficultate (halitum emittnni Nam cum humores isti in gutture hominis huius iacent, pubnonem eius sorde et tabe et putredine inficiont et Yulnerant, velut oculi alicuius hominis aliquando per dolorem exeuntis tabis et ulcerum laeduntur. Unde et pulmo ad guttur se erigit et aditum halitus occupat, ita quod homo ille vix halitum emittit.
text_translation: And because these humors cannot rise, they remain in the brain through the channel of the throat of those men and lead to debility, so that those men emit breath with difficulty. For when these humors lie in the throat of this man, they infect and wound his lung with dirt, tabes, and putrefaction, just as the eyes of some man are sometimes wounded by tabes and ulcers through pain. Hence, the lung raises itself toward the throat and occupies the passage of breath, so that that man scarcely emits breath.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De tussi, £t quia putredo circa pulmonem iacet, homo ille multum et putridum fiegma eicit; alioqnin oito deficeret, quoniam infirmitas haec aliquando periculosa est. De halitus foetore in aura nehulosa. Alii autem homines sunt, qui in nebulosa et humida temperie aeris concipiniitur, unde etiam foetentem et male odorantem halitum et xnale odorantem sudorem semper habent. Et foetidus balitns eorum atque mali humores, qui in ipsis sunt, usque ad cerebrum eorum pertingunt et illud ita in infirmitate fatigant, quod multotiens velut in oblivionem sui ducuntur.
text_translation: On cough, and because putrefaction lies around the lung, that man ejects much and putrid phlegm; otherwise he would lack it, because this weakness is sometimes dangerous. On the foulness of breath in misty air. Other men are those who are conceived in misty and humid air, whence they also have always a foul and ill-smelling breath and ill-smelling sweat. And the foul cough of them and the bad humors, which are in them, reach up to their brain and weary it so much in weakness that they are often led as if into oblivion of themselves.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui si flegma de capite eiciunt, minus dolent, quia cere- lO bmm eorum purgatur; si vero flegma non eiciunt, grayius in capite torquentur, quoniam cerebrum eorum a noxiis humoribus non revelatur. Et foetens halitus istorum ad polmonem transit et eum fatigat, ita quod etiam aliquando rancam vocem habent. Sed tamen dolor iste non multum pericnlosum est, quia cito curari poterit. 2e conceptu in plenUunio. Quidam autem homines, qui in plena luna et temperata aura concipiuntur, quae nec nimis calida nec nimis frigida est, sani sunt et ayari in comestione ciborum, ita quod diversos cibos indifferen- ter snmunt.
text_translation: Who, if they expel phlegm from the head, suffer less, because their brain is purged; if indeed they do not expel phlegm, more severely are tormented in the head, because their brain is not revealed from noxious humors. And the fetid breath of these people passes to the lung and fatigues it, so that sometimes they have a rough voice. But nevertheless this pain is not very dangerous, because it can be quickly cured. In conception during fullness. Some indeed men, who are conceived in full moon and temperate air, which is neither too hot nor too cold, are healthy and do not have aversion in the consumption of food, so that they take various foods indifferently.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quamvis diversos cibos indifferenter comedant et comedere possint, a quibusdam tamen noxiis cibis se abstinere deberent, velut venator, qui inutiles feras abire permittit, utiles autem capit. De epeUis indigestione. Si quis istorum diversos cibos immoderate et indiscrete sumpserit, tunc de diversis humoribus istorum ciborum iecur eius laeditur et indurescit, ita quod salubrem succum, quem velut unguentum in quaeque membra et in compagines membrorum et in wcddarun mittere deberent, de diversis et noxiis humoribus sauciatum haberet, ita quod etiam aliquando caro eius in aliqno loco membrorum in tumorem vertitur, et quod eadem caro scinditur, et quod etiam aliquod membrum eius ita laeditur, quod in eo claudicat.
text_translation: And although they may eat various foods indifferently and are able to eat them, nevertheless they should abstain from certain harmful foods, just as a hunter permits useless beasts to depart but takes the useful ones. On indigestion from a meal. If anyone of these has immoderately and indiscreetly taken various foods, then the liver of that person is harmed and hardened by the diverse humors of those foods, so that the healthy juice, which should be sent like an ointment to each member and to the joints of the members and to the udders, is injured and corrupted by the diverse and harmful humors, so that sometimes the flesh in some part of the members turns into a tumor, and that same flesh is torn, and that even some member of him is so harmed that it limps.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De nervorum indignatione. Sed tamen isti in infirmi- tate hac diu vivere possunt. De conceptu et epatis indignatione. Sunt etiam alii Hildegardis canBae et curae ed. Kaiser. bomines, qui in decrescente Irina et in turbine diversae aurae concipiuntur. Et ex his quidam semper tristes sunt et diversos mores habent. Propter tristitiam autem, quam babent, iecur eorum infirmatur et multis et minutissimis foraminibus perforatur, yelut caseus, qui parvissima et multa foramina babet.
text_translation: On the indignation of the nerves. But nevertheless, these can live long in this weakness. On the concept and the indignation of the liver. There are also others, in Hildegard's *Canae et curae* edited by Kaiser, people who are conceived in decreasing Irina and in the whirlwind of diverse winds. And from these, some are always sad and have diverse manners. Because of the sadness they have, their liver becomes weak and is perforated by many and very small holes, like cheese, which has very small and many holes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t ideo nec edaces nec ayari ad comedendum et ad bibendum sunt, sed modicum manducant et bibunt. £t quoniam modicum manducant et bibunt, iecur eorum marcidum erit velut holmeide et deficit» De epatis vasculo. Sed iecur est in bomine qnasi vasculum, in quod cor et pulmo atque stomacbus succos suos efifundunt, quos illud iterum in omnia membra refnndit, velut yas aliquod iuxta fontem positum, quod aquam de fonte acceptam iterum in alia loca diffandit.
text_translation: And therefore neither gluttonous nor inflated are they for eating and for drinking, but they eat and drink little. And because they eat and drink little, their liver will be withered like holmide and will be deficient from the liver's vessel. But the liver is in the body like a vessel, into which the heart and lung and stomach pour their juices, which it again pours back into all the members, like a vessel placed next to a spring, which water accepted from the spring again spreads out into other places.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum iecur perforatum est et marcidum , ut praedictum est, bonos succos a corde et a pulmone atque a stomacho recipere non potest, et ita succi et humores illi ad cor et ad pulmonem et ad stomachum revertentes velut quandam inundationem faciunt. £t si infirmitas haec in quolibet homine increverit, diu vivere non potest. 2e conceptu, dum sol est in cancro. Sed alii homines sunt, qui concipiuntur, cum sol in cancro est et cum sol iam plenitudinem suam habens temperiem aeris in recta qualitate temperat, et hi sani snnt in iecore.
text_translation: But when the liver is perforated and decayed, as previously stated, it cannot receive good juices from the heart and from the lung and from the stomach, and thus the juices and humors returning to the heart and to the lung and to the stomach cause a kind of inundation. And if this weakness has taken hold in any man, he cannot live long. And in conception, when the sun is in Cancer. But there are other men who are conceived when the sun is in Cancer and when the sun, having already attained its fullness, tempers the temper of the air in proper quality, and these are healthy in the liver.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De cordis dolore. Et quoniam in iecore sani sunt, aliquando defectum in corde sentiunt, quia mali humores a iecore fugientes ad splen et ad cor tendunt et iLla fatigant, quoniam iecur superare non valent. Cumque homo cruda poma vel cruda pira seu cruda olera seu alios crudos cibos sumpserit, qui nec per ignem nec per aliud quodcumque condimentum temperati sunt, in stomacho eius excoqui facile non possimt, quia prius temperati non sunt. De splenis dolore, Et sic mali humores eorundem ciborum, qui per ignem seu per aliud aliquod condimentnm, scilicet aut salis aut aceti, temperari et fugari debuerant et temperati et fagati non sunt, ad splen ascendunt et illud in tumorem et dolorem vertunt.
text_translation: On the pain of the heart. And since in the healthy liver they are, sometimes they feel a defect in the heart, because bad humors fleeing from the liver towards the spleen and towards the heart weary it, because the liver cannot overcome them. When a man has taken raw fruits or raw pears or raw vegetables or other raw foods, which are not tempered by fire or by any other seasoning, in his stomach they cannot be easily digested, because they were not previously tempered. On the pain of the spleen. And thus the bad humors of those foods, which should have been tempered and driven away by fire or by some other seasoning, namely by salt or vinegar, and which are not tempered and driven away, ascend to the spleen and turn it into a tumor and into pain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quia splen madiduiu est et quia humoribus humectari debet, tam malos quam bonos humores recipit. Gum ergo praedicti mali hnmores insurrexerint, ad splen (ascendunt et illud saadimt et dolere £EU2iunt. De stomacho et eius indigestione. Stomachus quoque in corpore hominis ita positus est, quod omnes cibos suscipit et digerit ; et tenax est et aliquantum interius rugosus, ut cibos ad digestionem retinere possit, ne citius in digestionem labantur, velut cementarius lapides ita incidit, ut cementum capiant et teneant, ne diffluat aut cadat.
text_translation: For the spleen is moist and must be moistened by humors, both good and bad humors; and when the aforementioned bad humors rise, they ascend to the spleen and cause it to swell and feel pain. Concerning the stomach and its indigestion. The stomach is also placed in the human body in such a way that it receives and digests all foods; and it is tenacious and somewhat internally rough, so that it may retain foods for digestion, lest they flow away too quickly in digestion, just as a mason strikes stones so that they may take and hold cement, lest they flow away or fall.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: lO Sed cum quidam homines aliquando quosdam cibos supra modum sumpserint, scilicet crudos et incoctos et semicoctos ac superflue et immoderate pingues atque grayes aut aridos et siccos, tunc interdum cor et iecur ac pulmo et alius 'calor, qui in homine est, tantum et tam fortem ignem stomacho administrare non possunt, ut cibi isti excoquantur; unde in stomacho coagulantur et indurescunt et mucidi fiunt, ita quod etiam stomachum interdum aliquantum aut yiridem aut glaucum aut liTidum aut multum liyorem, id est slim, habentem reddunt, et quod etiam inter- dum yelut putridum sterquilinium malos humores et malos foetores per totum corpus emittunt, et quod etiam yelut yirida et madida ligna incensa malum fumum ubique in corpore effundunt.
text_translation: But when certain men sometimes consume certain foods beyond measure, namely raw and uncooked and half-cooked and excessively and immoderately fatty and heavy or dry and dry, then sometimes the heart and liver and lung and another heat, which is in man, cannot administer so strong a fire to the stomach that these foods are digested; wherefore in the stomach they coagulate and harden and become mucous, so that even the stomach sometimes becomes somewhat green or glaucous or clear or very shiny, that is slimy, and that also sometimes like putrid feces, bad humors and bad odors are emitted throughout the body, and that also like green and wet wood burned, bad smoke is poured out everywhere in the body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod enim quidam cibi in homine indurantur, hoc de diyersis infirmitatibus est, quia si nimius et iniustus ardor in homine est, comestum cibum perurit, yel si nimium et iniustum firigus in eo est, comestus cibus in eo digeri non potest, sed prae frigore in eo coadunatur et coagulatur; et ita in homine manet et sic ille dolet. De sifac extensione aut ruptura. Quidam autem homines sunt, qui molles cames corporis habent siye macilenti siye pingues sint, et his interior cutecula, quae intestina comprehendit, pinguis et tenuis est, ita quod aut de quibusdam infirmitatibus aut laboribus aut de casu aut de extensione yentris, cum cibis repletur, facile rumpitur.
text_translation: What indeed certain foods become hardened in man, this is from diverse infirmities, because if excessive and unjust heat is in man, the eaten food is scorched, or if excessive and unjust cold is in him, the eaten food cannot be digested in him, but because of the cold it is gathered together and coagulated; and thus it remains in man, and so he suffers. Concerning the extension or rupture of the face. Some men are, who have soft flesh of the body whether they are gaunt or fat, and their inner cuticle, which surrounds the intestines, is fat and thin, so that whether from certain infirmities or labors or from accident or from extension of the abdomen, when filled with food, it is easily ruptured.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen eadem cutecula in mulieribus spissior et tena cior est propter partns earum quam in viris, et ideo saepius et citius in yiris quam in muILeribus scinditor. De renihus et quare duo sint. Eenes vero firmamentum et retentaculum caloris corporis hominis sunt et femur hominis continent, quemadmodum armati milites, qui dominum suum defendunt. Et ideo duo sunt, ut tanto fortius et convenientius ignem in se retineant. Et hoc tam in femina quam in yiro faciunt, quod lumbis virorum assunt et quia etiam matrici feminarum iunguntur.
text_translation: But nevertheless the same cutecula is thicker and more tenacious in women because of their parts than in men, and therefore it tears more frequently and quickly in men than in women. Concerning the kidneys and why there are two. The kidneys indeed are the firmament and reservoir of the body's heat of man and contain the thigh of man, just as armed soldiers who defend their lord. And therefore there are two, so that they may hold the fire within themselves more strongly and conveniently. And this is done both in woman and man, because they are attached to the loins of men and also joined to the womb of women.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et in pinguedinem involuti sunt, ne ulla frigiditate aut contrarietate laedantur, quin vires suas habeant. In ipsos autem renes fortissimae venae positae sunt, quae eos fortiter tenent, et per quas etiam totum . corpus hominum sustentatur. Sed cum homo in eis dolet, hoc ex infirmitate stomachi fit. De iliaca, Nam de dolore stomachi dolor lateris nascitur, et de dolore lateris nascitur dolor ilium. Ciun enim stomachus de noziis et malis cibis infirmatur, ita quod fortes et mali cibi in eo digeri non possunt, dolor ut quidam fumus et ut quaedam nebula ex eo in latus egreditur, velut acerbus fumus de virido ligno ascendit, et fumus ille stomachi ad ilia extenditur ut nigra nubes, et ilia fumum istum suscipiunt, ita quod fumus iste de assidua consuetudine ad ilia semper tendit, quemadmodum fumus lignorum ad famarium properat.
text_translation: But they are also wrapped in fat, so that they are not harmed by any coldness or contrary forces, but that they may have their own strength. In them, however, the kidneys are placed with the strongest veins, which hold them strongly, and through which also the entire human body is sustained. But when a man suffers in them, this arises from the weakness of the stomach. Of the ilium, indeed, from the pain of the stomach arises the pain of the side, and from the pain of the side arises the pain of the ilium. For the stomach is weakened by harmful and bad foods, so that strong and bad foods cannot be digested in it, pain emerging from it as if some smoke and some mist, like bitter smoke rising from green wood, and that smoke of the stomach extends to the ilium as a black cloud, and the ilium receives that smoke, so that this smoke, from constant habit, always tends toward the ilium, just as the smoke of woods rushes toward the hearth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ita quaeque infirmitas et dolor stomachi ad consuetum locum illum iliorum propter malam et assiduam consuetudinem multotiens cadit et ibi hominem dolere facit. De virilibus. Yirtuti autem, quae in lumbis virorum est, scilicet vento, qui ex meduUa procedit, duae sibi coniunctae vires velut duo tabemacula assunt ardorem, qui in viro est, attestantes, quae etiam ignem stirpis fortiter in se tenent. Et quadam cutecula involuti sunt, ne vis earum deficiat, quae eis in adiutorium est, ut stirpem elevare possint.
text_translation: And thus each weakness and pain of the stomach falls repeatedly into that accustomed place of the iliacs because of bad and constant habit, and there it causes the man to suffer. Concerning virile matters. Indeed, the virtue which is in the loins of men, namely the wind which proceeds from the medulla, two joined forces take on the heat which is in the man, testifying, which also strongly hold the fire of the stock within themselves. And they are wrapped in some cuticula, so that their power may not fail, which is their aid, so that they may be able to raise the stock.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si quis masculus his duabus viribus forte naturaliter per defectum aut per abscisionem caret, virilem viriditatem non habet nec illum virilem ventum, qni stirpem ad fortitudinem suam erigit. Ed ideo ad exara- . tionem feminae velut terrae eadem stirps erigi non potest^quia vento virium suarum vacuata est, qui eam ad iter producendae prolis confortare debebat, quemadmodum etiam aratrom terram fodere non yalet, quod ferro exarationis caret. De hoscei infkUione.
text_translation: But if any male lacks these two forces naturally by defect or by amputation, he does not possess the virile greenness nor that virile wind which raises the stock to its strength. Therefore, the same stock cannot be raised for the plowing of the woman as the earth, because it is emptied of the wind of its own forces, which should have strengthened it to produce offspring, just as the plow also cannot plow the earth, which lacks the iron of plowing. On this weakness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Virilis enim fortitudo in virilibus magna est, sed interdum aut de malis bumoribus ant intefdum de noxio sudore aut interdum de superfluitate delectationis in virilibus quaedam indigna bumiditas aut ulcus aut inflatio insurgit, ita quod eadem virilia inflantur et malis ulceribus laeduntur. De dissuria, Homo autem, qui urinam retiuBre non potesty illi stomacbus frigidus est et vesica, et ideo potus in eis ad perfectam coqui non poterit; et antequam in eis coquatur, ut tepida aqua effluit, velut si aqua ad ignem ponatur et cum illa incalescere inceperit, superius effluat, antequam ferveat; sicut etiam in infantibus est, qui urinam retinere non possunt, quoniam nec stomacbus nec vesica eorum perfectum calorem babent sed frigiditatem.
text_translation: For indeed, masculine strength in the male organs is great, but sometimes from bad humors, or sometimes from noxious sweat, or sometimes from the superfluity of delight in the male organs, certain shameful humidity, or ulcer, or inflammation arises, so that the same male organs swell and are wounded by bad ulcers. On dysuria, indeed, the man who cannot hold back urine, his stomach and bladder are cold, and therefore drink in them cannot be prepared to perfection; and before they are prepared, as hot water flows out, as if water is placed upon fire and when with it it begins to heat, it flows out above before it boils; just as also in infants who cannot hold back urine, because neither their stomach nor their bladder have perfect heat but coldness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De podagra. Ille vero, qui molles cames et delicatas in corpore suo babet et qui diversos et delicatos cibos frequenter comedit, podagram facile incurrit. Nam si quis duos bonos et sanos cibos comedit et tertium malum et infirmum, illi duo boni et sani cibi bunc tertium malum et infirmum exsuperant, ut comedentem hominem minus laedat. Si autem quispiam bomo duos malos et inflrmos cibos comedit et eodem tempore tertium bonum et sanum cibum, illi duo mali et inflrmi cibi bunc tertium bonum et sanum cibum opprimunt et eum ad sanitatem bominis raro exsurgere permittunt.
text_translation: On gout. Indeed, he who has soft and delicate flesh in his body and who frequently eats various and delicate foods easily falls into gout. For if someone eats two good and healthy foods and a third bad and weak, those two good and healthy foods overcome the third bad and weak one, so that the eater is less harmed. If, however, someone eats two bad and weak foods and at the same time a third good and healthy food, those two bad and weak foods overcome the third good and healthy food and rarely allow the person to rise to health.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t saepe contingit bominibus, qui diversis cibis utuntur, quod inde facile inflrmantur. Cmn ergo illi, qui molles et delicatas cames in corpore babent, diversis et delicatis cibis supra modum utuntur, mali bumores superbabundanter in eis inundant et crescnnt, ita quod contineri non possunt, quin bac et illac in S hominibus illis inordinate fluant et sic tandem ad inferiora descendunt ei in cruribus ac in pedibus eorum insanire incipiunt. Cumqae nnllum ezitum ibi habuerint, sarsnm ad superiora corporis, unde yenerunt, ascendere non yalent, sed ibi in inferioribus manent et in liyorem yertuntur et obdurantur, et sic homo ille in cruribus et in pedibus suis podagram sentit et dolet, ita quod yix incedere valet.
text_translation: And it often happens to humans who use various foods, that from there they are easily weakened. Since those who have soft and delicate meats in their body use various and delicate foods excessively, evil humors superabundantly flood them and grow, so that they cannot be contained, but here and there in their bodies flow inordinately and thus descend finally into the lower parts, into their legs and feet, and begin to become insane. And when they have no exit there, they cannot ascend back to the upper parts of the body from where they came, but there they remain in the lower parts and are turned into a hardened state and become hardened, and thus that man feels podagra in his legs and feet and suffers, so that he can hardly walk.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In mulieribus quoque, quae moUes et delicatas cames habent et quae etiam delicatis cibis supra modum utuntor, mali etiam humores, ut praedictum est, crescunt, sed tamen podagram non tam facile incurrunt, et humores isti ad purgationem menstruorum transeunt, et sic illae a podagra liberantur. Be fisttUa. Sed et fistula est quoddam genus podagrae. Nam fistula de maHs et superfluis humoribus nasdtur, quia, cum mali et superflui humores in homine superhabundant, ad aliquem locum corporis eius transeunt yel ad crura seu ad pedes illius descendunt, et ibi acumine superfluitatis suae cutem eius perforant et pauiatim effluunt; et quia in homine illo semper augentur, ibi cutem semper effluendo sanari non permittunt.
text_translation: In women also, who have soft and delicate flesh and who also make excessive use of delicate foods, the bad humors, as previously stated, increase, yet they do not so easily incur podagra, and these humors pass on to the purification of menstruation, and thus they are freed from podagra. But fistula is also a certain kind of podagra. For fistula arises from bad and superfluous humors, because when bad and superfluous humors in a man are in excess, they pass to some part of his body or descend to his legs or feet, and there, by the sharpness of their superfluity, perforate his skin and gradually flow out; and because in that man they are always increasing, they do not allow the skin there to heal by flowing out.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: ' ^ Qmre menstruwm, Cum autem fluxus cupiditatis Eyam intrayit, omnes yenae eius in fluyium sanguinis apertae sunt. Unde omnis femina procellas sanguinis in se habet, ita quod ad similitudinem continentiae et effusionis lunae guttas sanguinis sui tenet et effundit, et quod omnia membra eius, quae yenis compaginata sunt, aperiuntur. Nam ut luna crescit et decrescit, ita sanguis et humores per menstruum tempus in muliere purgantur, alioquin durare non posset, quia humidior yiro est, et magnam infirmitatem incideret.
text_translation: Quam menstruum, Cum autem fluxus cupiditatis iam intrayit, omnes venae eius in fluxum sanguinis apertae sunt. Unde omnis femina procellas sanguinis in se habet, ita quod ad similitudinem continentiae et effusionis lunae guttas sanguinis sui tenet et effundit, et quod omnia membra eius, quae venis compaginata sunt, aperiuntur. Nam ut luna crescit et decrescit, ita sanguis et humores per menstruum tempus in muliere purgantur, alioquin durare non posset, quia humidior viro est, et magnam infirmitatem incideret.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Pudicitia autem claustrum integri tatis in yirgine est, quia opus yiri transiliyit idem opus ignorans, et ideo menstrualis sanguis in yirgine magis sanguineus est quam in femina, quoniam yirgo adhuc est clausa. Nam postquam yirgo corrumpitur, deinde magis liyorem in menstruo sanguine habet, quia corrupta est, quam prius haberet, cum yirgo esset. Et cum puella adhuc in integritate yirgo est, tunc in ea sunt menstma quasi guttae de yenis; postquam autem corrumpitur, tunc gnttae efflutmt ut riyulas, quia per opus yiri solvuntur, et ideo ut rivulus sant, quoniam venae in opere illo solutae simt.
text_translation: Chastity, however, is the prison of integrity in a virgin, because the work of the man passed over the same work ignorantly, and therefore menstrual blood in a virgin is more sanguineous than in a woman, because the virgin is still closed. For after the virgin is corrupted, then more liquid in menstrual blood is had, because it is corrupted, than it had before, when the virgin was. And when the maiden is still in the integrity of a virgin, then in her are menstrua as drops from the veins; after that, however, when she is corrupted, then the drops effluent as streams, because through the work of the man they are dissolved, and therefore like a stream, because the veins in that work are dissolved.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gum enim claustrum integritatis in virgine rumpitor, rapUo illa sanguinem emittit. Mulier enim sic institata est, quod per sanguinem suum semen viri concipere et retinere debet, et ideo etiam debilis est et frigida et hiunores in ea infirmi. Unde etiam ipsa semper infirmaretur, nisi quod sanguis eius per menstrua purgatur, ut cibus in olla purgatur, com spumam de se eicit. De Evae corruptione. Omnes autem yenae mulieris lO integrae et sanae permansissent , si Eva in pleno tempore paradisi perstitisset.
text_translation: For indeed the prison of integrity in the virgin is broken, and she takes and emits blood. For woman is thus constituted, that through her blood she should conceive and retain the man's semen, and therefore she is also weak and cold, and the humors in her are weak. Wherefore also she would always be weak, unless her blood is purged through menstruation, as food is purged in a pot, casting forth foam from itself. On the corruption of Eve. Indeed all the veins of woman would have remained whole and healthy, if Eve had remained in the full time of paradise.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gum enim illa serpentem consentiendo ei inspexisset, visas eius, quo. caelestia vidit, extinctus est, et cum eum in consensu audiyit, auditus eius, quo caelestia audiyit, obstrusus est, atque in gustu pomi splendor, qoi in ea lucebat, obscuratus est. Ut autem succus a radice arboris incipit et ita sursum ad omnes ramusculos arboris tendit, sic est in muliere in menstruo tempore. Nam in tempore riyulorum sanguinis eias yenae, quae cerebrmn continent et quae yisum et auditum retinent, ad effosionem sanguinis concutiuntur, et yenae, quae coUum et dorsnm et renes tenent, yenas quoque iecoris et yiscerum ac nmbilici ad se trahunt, atque unaquaeque yena se in aliam ita efiundit, ut yiror arboris ramos yirescere facit, et yenae, quae renes continent, rotam illam dissolyunt, in quam renes compaginati sunt, et eam contrahunt et retrabunt, ut ungues alicuius ayiculae abscisi yenis suis contrahuntur et remittuntur.
text_translation: For indeed, when she had looked upon the serpent with consent, the vision of it, by which she saw celestial things, was extinguished, and when she heard it in the consent, the hearing by which she heard celestial things was blocked, and the splendor of the fruit, which shone in it, was darkened. As, however, the juice from the root of the tree begins and thus ascends upward to all the small branches of the tree, so it is in a woman during the menstrual time. For in the time of the menstrual flow, the veins which contain the brain and which retain vision and hearing are shaken toward the effusion of blood, and the veins which hold the neck and back and kidneys also draw toward themselves the veins of the liver and viscera and navel, and each vein pours itself into another, so that the vigor of the tree's branches causes them to grow, and the veins which contain the kidneys dissolve that wheel in which the kidneys are connected, and they contract and draw it back, so that the nails of some small part, when cut off, are contracted and sent back by their own veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quare menstruum. Sed quemadmodum yalidus yentus tempestatem in flumine moyet, sic etiam in onmibus bumo- ribus mulieris tempestas moyetur, ita quod idem humores sanguinis intermiscentur et quod aliquantum sanguinei fixmt et quod hoc modo cum sanguine purgantur, et sic fit riyulus sanguinis in muliere. Et ideo tunc in illo tempore caput mulieris infirmatur et oculi eius languent et totom eius corpus debile est. Sed tamen oculi ex hoc non caligant, si recto tempore et recta moderatione idem rivuli effluxerint.
text_translation: Why menstruation. But just as a strong wind moves a storm in a river, so also in all the humors of a woman the storm is moved, so that the same humors of blood intermingle, and that some of the blood is fixed, and that in this way they are purged with blood, and thus a stream of blood is formed in the woman. And therefore at that time the woman's head is weakened and her eyes grow weak and her entire body is weak. But nevertheless her eyes do not become dim from this, if the same streams flow out at the right time and with proper moderation.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ante ipsum autem initium huius effusionis membra, quae semen viri suscipere debent, aperiuntur, ita quod tunc facilius quam alio tempore concipiunt. Similiter vero, cum iam menstrua in fine sunt, ita quod iam deficiunt, mulieres facile concipiunt, quia membra earum adhuc tunc aperta sunt; sed in alio tempore tam facile non concipiunt, quoniam membra earum tunc aliquantum constricta sunt, velut in arbore est, quae yiriditatem suam in aestivo tempore ad producendum flores emittit, in hiemali autem introrsum eam ad se trahit.
text_translation: Before this effusion, however, the members which should receive the man's semen are opened, so that at that time they conceive more easily than at another time. Similarly, indeed, when the menstruation is already at its end, so that it is already deficient, women conceive easily, because their members are still open at that time; but at another time they do not conceive as easily, because their members are then somewhat constricted, as is the case with a tree which in summer time emits its greenness to produce flowers, but in winter draws it inward toward itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De conceptu. Sed cum mulier in coniimctione viri est, tunc calor cerebri eius, qui delectationem in se habet, gustum eiusdem delectationis in eadem coniunctione praenuntiat et seminis effasionem. Et postquam semen in locum suum ceciderit, praedictus fortissimus calor cerebri illud sibi attrahit et tenet, et mox etiam renes eiusdem mulieris contrahuntur, et omnia membra, quae in menstruo tempore ad apertionem parata sunt, modo ita clauduntur, quemadmodum fortis vir rem aliquam in manu sua claudit.
text_translation: On conception. But when a woman is in the union of her husband, then the heat of her brain, which has delight in itself, announces the taste of that same delight in the same union and the emission of semen. And after the semen has fallen into its place, the aforementioned most powerful heat of the brain draws it to itself and holds it, and soon the kidneys of the same woman contract, and all the members which were prepared in the menstrual time for opening are now closed, as a strong man closes something in his hand.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et deinde menstruus sanguis semen hoc commiscet et sangoineum facit et incamat. Et postquam caro fuerit, idem sanguis vasculum illi circumducit ut vermiculus, qui sibi de se ipso domum parat. Ac sic vasculum iUud de die in diem parat, quousque homo in eo formetur et dum idem homo spiraculum vitae accipit, atque deinde cum eodem homine crescit et tam firmiter stabilitur, quod se de loco suo movere non potest usque ad exitum eiusdem hominis. De Eva, Prima enim mater humani generis posita erat ad similitudinem aetheris, quia ut aether stellas inte gras in se continet, sic ipsa integra et incorrupta sine dolore genus humanum in se habebat, cum ei dictum est: crescite et multiplicamini.
text_translation: And then the menstrual blood mixes with this semen and makes it sangoineum and incamates it. And after the flesh has been formed, the same blood surrounds the vessel like a worm, which prepares a home for itself. And thus that vessel prepares day by day until the human being is formed, and while the same human being receives the breath of life, and then with the same human being it grows and becomes so firmly established that it cannot move from its place until the end of that same human being. Of Eve, for Eve was indeed the first mother of the human race, placed in the likeness of aether, because as aether contains stars within itself, so she, whole and uncorrupted without pain, had the human race within herself, when it was said to her: multiply and increase.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc in pleno dolore fit. De conceptu. Nam femina est nunc velut terra aratro arata et semen viri suscipit et in sanguinem suum inS volvit et calore suo calefaoit, et sic illud crescit, dum spiraculum vitae in ipsum mittitur et dum maturum tempus venit, ut procedat. De partu. Cum autem proles de femina procedere debet, tunc tantus terror et tantus tremor super eam fit, qnod omnis femina in terrore hoc tremet, et quod venae eins sanguine superhabundanter effiindunt, et quod omnes • Gompagines membrorum eius yulnerantur, et quod cum lacrimis et eiulatu solvuntur, ut dictum est: in dolore paries; et in tali scilicet dolore, ut in fine t^mporum terra mutabitur.
text_translation: And this occurs in full pain. Concerning conception. For the woman is now as the plowed land, and she receives the man's semen, and it is turned into her blood, and it is heated by her heat, and thus it grows, until the passage of life is sent into it, and until the time of maturity comes, so that it may proceed. Concerning childbirth. When, however, the offspring must proceed from the woman, then such terror and such trembling come upon her, that every woman in this terror will tremble, and that her veins pour out blood excessively, and that all the joints of her limbs are wounded, and that with tears and lamentation they are loosened, as it was said: in pain the wall is broken; and in such pain, that at the end of times the earth will change.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Onmes ergo feminae sanguinem magis cum livore permixtum habent quam viri, quod ipsae apertae sunt ut lignum, in quo cordae ad citharizandum positae snnt, et quia etiam fenestrales et ventosae (sunt , ita quod etiam elementa in eis vehementiora sunt quam in viris, et quod hnmores etiam in eis plus quam in yiris habnndant. De fecunditate. Rivnlus autem menstrui temporis in muliere est genitiva viriditas et floriditas eius, quae in prole frondet, quia, ut arbor Yiriditate sua floret et frondet et fructus profert, sic femina de viriditate rivulomm menstrui sanguinis flores et frondes in fructu ventris sui educit.
text_translation: Therefore, all women have blood more mixed with darkness than men, because they themselves are open as wood in which strings are placed for playing the lyre, and because they are also window-like and windy, so that even the elements in them are more vehement than in men, and that humors also abound in them more than in men. On fertility. Indeed, the stream of the menstrual time in a woman is her generative greenness and bloom, which sprouts in offspring, because, as a tree through its own greenness blooms and sprouts and bears fruit, so a woman from the greenness of the menstrual blood's stream causes flowers and sprouts to grow in the fruit of her womb.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed ut arbor, quae viriditate caret, infructiferum lignum dicitur, ita et femina, quae viriditatem floriditatis suae in forti aetate non habet, infertilis nuncupatur. Qnemadmodum autem iuvenis arbor viriditatem quidem habet, sed flore fructuum adhuc caret, et cum ad fortitudinem ramorum pervenerit, in viriditate flores et fructus profert, sed tandem in plenitudine temporis, ex quo radicata foit, viriditas eius contrahitur, ita quod aliquantum ex parte durum lignum fit et etiam ex parte fragile et putridum: sic etiam in femina erit.
text_translation: But as a tree which lacks greenness is called unfruitful wood, so also a woman who lacks the greenness of her flowering in strong age is called infertile. Just as, however, a young tree indeed has greenness, but still lacks the flower of fruits, and when it reaches the strength of its branches, it produces flowers and fruits in greenness, but finally, in the fullness of time, from which it was rooted, its greenness is drawn back, so that somewhat in part it becomes hard wood and also in part fragile and rotten: so also in a woman it will be.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam puella viriditatem crescendo ad fortiorem aetatem habet, sed sanguine flori- ditatis suae caret; in forti autem aetate, cum membra eius confortata sunt, viriditas sanguinis floriditatem educit ad prolem. Sed in plena et perfecta aetate sanguis eius minuitur, ita quod etiam viriditas floriditatis sangninis ipsius evanescit et quod caro eius contrahitur et durior et tenacior erit, sed tamen debilior, quam prius foisset. Sed minores puellulae effluentes rivulos menstrui sanguinis non babent et ideo etiam prolem non concipiunt, quod membra earom nondum perfecta sunt, yelut ibi perfectio non est, ubi tantum fondamentum domus positum est et murus nondum perfectus.
text_translation: For the girl, greenness increases as she approaches a stronger age, but she lacks the blood of her own floridness; however, in a strong age, when her limbs are strengthened, the greenness of the blood brings forth floridness to offspring. But in full and perfect age, her blood diminishes, so that even the floridness of her own blood's greenness fades, and her flesh contracts, becoming harder and more tenacious, yet still weaker than it was before. But younger girls do not have flowing rivulets of menstrual blood, and therefore do not conceive offspring, because their limbs are not yet perfected, as where perfection is not, where only the foundation of the house is laid and the wall is not yet perfected.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum autem ad duodecimum annum peryenerit, iam membra eius usque ad quindecimmn annum confortantur, velut murus perficitur, qui supra fiindamentum ad plenitudinem suam ponitur. A quinto decimo vero anno iam compago membrorum eius usque ad yicesimum annum perficitur, yelut domus, quae tra hibus et tecto iam perfecta est, in quam tunc quaeque utensilia ponuntur. Et ita femina deinde in yenis et in compaginibus membrorum suorum perfecta iam yirile semen concipere et retinere et calefacere potest, quod si aliqua femina ante yicesimum aetatis suae conceperit fetum, boc aut de nimio calore naturae suae et mariti sui aut de multa amplexione amborum fit; sed tamen fetum infirmum et in aliqua re debilem producit.
text_translation: When, however, it has arrived at the twelfth year, then its limbs are already strengthened up to the fifteenth year, as a wall is perfected, which is placed upon the foundation to its fullness. From the fifteenth year indeed, the structure of its limbs is perfected up to the twentieth year, as a house which, through walls and roof, is already completed, into which then all utensils are placed. And thus, a woman afterward, in her genitalia and in the structure of her limbs, being perfected, is able already to conceive and retain and warm virile semen; which if any woman conceives a fetus before the twentieth year of her age, this is either from the excessive heat of her own nature and her husband's, or from the frequent embraces of both; but nevertheless produces a weak and in some respect feeble fetus.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De menstrui defectu, A quinquagesimo yero anno aut interdum a sexagesimo femina circa fenestralia loca sua implicatur et crescit, ita quod et riyulus menstrui sanguinis in domum suam, scilicet in membra, reyertitur ut ager, qui post multum transactum laborem iam amplius nulla semina fructuum et frumenti potest suscipere aut gignereaut ad perfectum (producere praeterfloresautalia bona gramina. Et boc erit in muliere usque ad octogesimum annum, a quo deinceps a yiribus suis omnino inclinatur.
text_translation: On the defect of menstruation, At the fiftieth year indeed or sometimes at the sixtieth, a woman is entangled around her window-like places and grows, so that the rivers of menstrual blood return to its home, that is, to the limbs, as a field which, after much labor performed, can no longer receive or generate any seeds of fruits and grain, or produce perfect (to produce flowers or good grasses). And this will be in a woman until the eightieth year, from which onwards she is entirely turned away from her forces.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam a quinquagesimo anno aut aliquando in quibusdam feminis a sexagesimo menstrua cessant et matrix implicari et contrabi incipit, ita quod amplius prolem concipere non possunt, nisi interdum eyeniat, ut aliqua ex qualibet superfluitate yix semel interim usque ad octogesimum annum prolem concipiat, in qua tamen aliquis defectus interdum continget, yelut in illis multotiens eyenit, quae infra yicesimum annum tenerae iuyenculae exsistentes in illa teneritudine concipiunt et pariunt.
text_translation: Nam from the fiftieth year or sometimes in certain women from the sixtieth year menstruation ceases and the womb begins to be entangled and constricted, so that they can no longer conceive offspring, except occasionally eveniat, so that some from any superfluity ex once in between until the eightieth year conceive offspring, in which however some defect occasionally occurs, velut in those who eveniat multotiens, who existing as tender juvenculae under the twentieth year in that tenderness conceive and bear offspring.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ab octogesimo yero anno femina a yiribus suis ad defectum inclinatur yelut dies, qui ad occasum tendit. De menstrui retentiane. In quibasdam autem iuvenciilis mulieribus multotiens guttae riyulorum sanguinis earum prae tristitia sic effluentem sanguinem mulieris valde constringunt, qaoniam venae bunc sanguinem ad effusionem riyuli portant, quae de suspiriis contrahuntur et arescunt.
