Sed sicut arbor in aestate de sole floret et frondet, ita etiam menstrua mulieram de laetitia multotiens aperiuntur, et ut frigidus yentus et gelu et biems folia et ramos arborum arefacit, sic etiam riyuli sanguinis, qui de muliere manare debebant, per tristitiam saepe arescunt.
text_translation: From the eightieth year, a woman inclines from her strength toward defect, as a day that tends toward sunset. On the retention of menstruation. In certain young women, the drops of the rivuli of their blood, through sadness, thus constrict the flowing blood of the woman, because the veins carry this blood for the effusion of the rivulus, which are contracted and dried up from sighs. But as a tree in summer from the sun flowers and fronds, so also the menstruation of the woman is often opened from joy, and as a cold wind and frost and breeze dry up the leaves and branches of trees, so also the rivuli of blood, which should flow from the woman, often dry up through sadness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum etiam in quibus- lO dam feminis de superfluitate infirmitatum earum humores, qui in eis sunt, in diyersum et contrarium malum superhabundant et effluunt, yenas, quae riyulos sanguinis portant, in ipsis constringunt, ita quod menstrua earum deficiunt, quia tempestates humorum iniustum frigus et diyersitatem caloris parant, ita quod sanguis earum interdum frigidus est et quod interdum feryet; unde etiam diyerso modo in eis hac et illac discurrit; et tunc yenae, quae tempore suo effluere debebant, propter ariditatem, quam in se habent, obligantur et non effluunt.
text_translation: And when, in some women, the humors, which are in them, abound excessively and flow out in diverse and contrary evils, they constrict the veins which carry the streams of blood, so that their menstruation fails, because the tempests of the humors produce unjust cold and diverse heat, so that their blood is sometimes cold and sometimes feryet; hence, in them, it flows in diverse ways here and there; and then the veins, which should flow at their time, are bound because of the dryness they possess, and do not flow.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et aliae quaedam feminae sunt, quae infirmas cames et crassas habent, quae magis de debilitate et de foeditate earum crescunt, quam de recta yiriditate illarum. Et cames istae ita supercrescont yenas et eas ita opprimunt, quod tam fortiter constringuntur, quod riyuli sanguinis earum obrauntur et quod recto tempore suo non possunt effluere, quod ora earundem yenarum ita onerata et conclusa sunt, quod riyulos sanguinis sui efFundere non possunt.
text_translation: But also certain other women are there, who have weak and thick flesh, which grow more from weakness and from foulness of them, than from proper greenness of them. And these fleshes so overgrow the veins and so press them, that they are so tightly constricted, that the rivers of their blood are obstructed and that they cannot effluent at their proper time, because the mouths of their veins are so burdened and closed, that they cannot effluent their own blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde etiam et matrix ipsarum feminarum ita superhabundanter impinguescit, quod iter eius tam fortiter constringitur, ut yas aliquod clau- ditur, ne illud effluat, quod in se tenet; et ita sanguis eius constrictus tempore suo profluere non posset. De calvaria. Sed quod calyaria in capite mulierum diyisa est, hoc est yelut yia, per quam yenae aperiuntur, com riyulos menstrui temporis educunt. Et eo tempore ipsa calyaria aperitur et yiam yenis facit^ ut purgatio menstraarum in mulieribus perficiatur.
text_translation: Therefore, the womb of women is so excessively pressed against that its passage is so tightly constricted that it is closed, so that it does not flow out, which it holds; and thus its blood, constricted at its time, cannot flow forth. Of the calvaria. But that calvaria in the head of women is divided, this is like a way, through which veins open, with which the menstrual time brings forth. And at that time the calvaria opens and makes a way for the veins, so that the menstrual purification in women is completed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed postquam purgatio illa finitur, calyaria concluditur et venas continet, ne amplius sanguinem educant, velut quaedam congeries lapidum et lignorum flumen retinet, ne supra modum effluat. Sed interdum quaedam mulieres dolorem diver sarum febrium habent et dolorem stomachi et lateiis ac renum, et dolores isti calvariae resistunt, ne iusto tempore suo claudatur, velut quaedam procellae inundando loca obstrusa transeunt, et ita rivuli sanguinis in muliere illa in iniusto tempore inordinate effluunt.
text_translation: But after that purge is finished, the calyaria is closed and contains the veins, so that they no longer draw out blood, as though a certain collection of stones and wood retains a river, so that it does not flow out beyond measure. But sometimes certain women have pain from diverse fevers and pain of the stomach and of the sides and of the kidneys, and these pains resist the calvaria's closure, so that it is not closed at its proper time, as though certain storms, flooding blocked places, pass through, and thus rivulets of blood in that woman flow out in an unjust time in disorder.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc mulier illa in dolore est velut aliquis vir, qui ferro vulneratus est, et ideo eo tempore cum cautela se observet, ne inde plus laedatur, quia etiam medicina cum timore ei adhibenda est. De conceptu et partu, Mulieres autem, quae infirmos humores cum sagittis, quod gutta (in eis est, in se habent, cum magnis doloribus gignunt, quia cum claustra infantis reserantur, humores tempestatem faciimt, ita quod locatia loca exitus eius aut intumescendo aut ulcerando prohibentor, quemadmodum inundantes et irruentes fluctus rectum iter rivuli repente prohibent, ita quod rivulus ille in recto itinere suo fluere non potest, et quod etiam notari non potest, ubi iter illius sit.
text_translation: And then that woman is in pain as though some man who was wounded by iron, and therefore at that time she must observe herself with caution, lest she be harmed more, because even medicine must be applied to her with fear. Concerning conception and childbirth, Women, however, who have weak humors with arrows, which a drop (that is in them, they have within themselves, give birth with great pains, because when the passages of the infant are opened, the humors cause a storm, so that the places where the exit of it is either swells or ulcerates, preventing it, just as overflowing and rushing waves suddenly prevent the straight course of a river, so that that river cannot flow in its straight journey, and also cannot be noted where its course is.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hac oppressione tam diu iter infantis prohibetur, usque dum per gratiam dei spiraculum vitae, quod anima est, se excutit cum corpore, sive infans vivat sive mortuus sit. Quod si mulier magnitndinem pinguedinis camis habet, tunc iter infantis prae pinguedine matemae camis intumescit et obstruitur, ita quod praegnans multum laborat, usque dum per gratiam dei spiraculum vitae, quod in infante est, se cum corpore suo excutit. Si autem femina recte temperata est, ita quod nec nimis pinguis nec nimis macra nec infirma exsistit, tunc iter infantis cum iniusto tumultu in ea non prohibetur.
text_translation: And this oppression lasts so long that the infant's passage is hindered, until by the grace of God the opening of life, which is the soul, shakes itself loose with the body, whether the infant lives or is dead. If, however, a woman has the magnitude of the fat of the womb, then the infant's passage swells and is blocked because of the fat of the mother's garment, so that the pregnant woman suffers greatly, until by the grace of God the opening of life, which is in the infant, shakes itself loose with its body. If, however, a woman is rightly tempered, so that she is neither too fat nor too thin nor weak, then the infant's passage is not hindered by unjust tumult within her.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen ab initio constitutum laborem et mater et infans patientur, et labores illi recto moderamine eveniunt. Et feminae, quae pingues non sunt nec infirmae, etiam si multum macrae sunt, iter infantis in eis non prohibetur. Sed tamen constitutum ab initio laborem sustmebant. Sed plurimi infantes de tnmulta hamonim et de pingaedine matris suffocantur, ita quod moriantar, cum rectam iter eis prohibetar, ut praediotum est. Periculosum est autem eo tempore uUam medicinam periclitanti feminae dare, dum in partu laborat, quia aut de superfluis humoribus aut de pinguedine camis aut de instanti alia infirmitate laborat, et ideo medicinae, quae malos et superfluos bumores compescunt, non sunt ei dandae in exitu infantis propter eiusdem nascentis infantis periculum, quoniam, sicut homo suffocaretur, si sub terram lO poneretur, ita etiam de yiribas et de fumo aromatum et herbarum infans suffocaretur, si ei instante tempore exitus sui adhibentur.
text_translation: But nevertheless, from the beginning, labor is established, and both mother and infant endure it, and those labors occur with proper moderation. And women who are not fat nor weak, even if they are very thin, do not prohibit the infant's passage within them. But nevertheless, from the beginning, labor was sustained. But many infants are suffocated from the tumult of the harmonium and from the fatness of the mother, so that they die, when the proper passage is prohibited to them, as was previously mentioned. It is dangerous, however, at that time, to give any medicine to a woman in peril, while she labors in childbirth, because she labors either from superfluous humors, or from the fatness of the womb, or from another immediate infirmity, and therefore medicines which restrain bad and superfluous humors are not to be given to her at the infant's exit, on account of the danger to the nascent infant, for as a man would suffocate if he were placed under the earth, so also an infant would suffocate from the fumes and smoke of aromatics and herbs, if the exit of its own birth were applied to it at that instant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de conceptu. Mulier namque cum yirile semen conceperit, conceptus seminis illius tam fortis est, quod i& onmem menstruum sanguinem mulieris sibi attrahit, velut fistula aut yentosa, quam minutor ad camem hominis ponit, multum sanguinem et tabem ad se ducit. Et semen illud in muliere primitus in lacte est, deinde in coagulatione et postea in came, velut lac coagulatio et coagu- lationem caseus subsequitur. Et sic infans in menstruo sangnine illo iacet et nutritur usque ad partunu Item de partu.
text_translation: Item concerning conception. For when a woman conceives virile semen, the conception of that semen is so strong that it draws to itself all the menstrual blood of the woman, as a fistula or ventosa, which gradually places it near the man's chamber, drawing much blood and matter to itself. And that semen in the woman first is in milk, then in coagulation, and afterward in flesh, as milk is coagulated and coagulation is followed by curd. And thus the infant lies in that menstrual blood and is nourished until birth. Item concerning birth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum autem partus institerit, infans CTun inundatione sanguinis egreditur, velut ioundatio aquarum aut lapides aut ligna in cursu suo educit. Sed liyor et foetor menstrui sanguinis adhuc in ea remanent, qoia ab ea tam cito purgari non possunt, quae postea paulatim ab ea per subsequentia tempora purgantur. Sed purgatio mulieris, quae in natura sua sicca est et quae in homoribus non multum habundat, post partum per pauca tempora completur, purgatio autem illius, quae in natura saa homida est et quae in humoribus habundat, diutius protrahitur quam illius, quae sicca est et quae multos homores in se non habet.
text_translation: When, however, the birth has begun, the infant is expelled as if a foundation of waters or stones or wood in its course carries away. But the smell and foulness of menstrual blood still remain in her, because they cannot be purged from her so quickly, which are later gradually purged from her through subsequent times. But the purification of a woman who in her nature is dry and who does not abound in humors is completed after childbirth in a short time, whereas the purification of her who in her nature is moist and who abounds in humors is prolonged longer than that of her who is dry and who does not contain many humors.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De infantis tenerUate, Quod autem infantes mox non ambolant, com nati faerint, hoc ideo est, quoniam de tenui semine homo concipitur et quia caro et ossa eius mnltam fragilitatem in se tunc habent, cum adhnc infans est, et qnoniam magna fortitndo in homine est, cnm se totnm snrsnm erigit ad ambnlandnm; qnod cetera animalia non facinnt, qnoniam illa cnm nata fuennt, mox pedibns snis incednnt. Et hoc ideo est, qnia ad terram prona inclinata snnt, velnt etiam infans manibns et pedibns reptat, anteqnam ad ambnlandnm se snrsnm erigere possit.
text_translation: On the frailty of the infant. That, however, infants do not walk soon after they are born, this is because a human is conceived from a thin seed, and because their flesh and bones then contain much fragility, while still an infant, and because great strength is required in a human when he raises himself entirely upward to walk; which other animals do not do, because they, when born, immediately walk on their feet. And this is why they are inclined toward the earth, and even the infant may crawl on hands and feet before it can raise itself to walk.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen qnamvis animalia mox nata pedibns se sostentent, sedere tamen non possnnt, sicnt infans sedet, cnm nondnm snpra pedes snos se erigere potest. Et qnia animalia fortitndinem in cmribns et in pedibns habent, ideo nata mox incednnt. Sed qnoniam homo de nmbilico snrsnm fortitndinem habet, et qnia in pedibns et in cmribus^ dnm infans est, debilis exsistit, idcirco tnnc ambulare non potest. Qtiod hamo non natat. Sed quia homo grayis est in corpore, et manibns operatnr et pedibns incedit et sursum erectus est, et qnia etiam nec mnltnm aerens nec aqnosns est, ideo natnra eius non habet, ut in aqna natet, nisi secundum hoc, qnod interdnm per se discit.
text_translation: But although animals born soon sustain themselves on their feet, they cannot sit, as an infant sits, when they are not yet able to rise above their feet. And because animals have strength in their legs and feet, therefore they soon walk. But because man has strength upward from the navel, and because in the feet and legs while an infant, it is weak, therefore at that time it cannot walk. That man does not swim. But because man is heavy in the body, and with the hands works and with the feet walks and is raised upward, and because also not very airy nor aquatic, therefore his nature does not have, to swim in water, unless according to this, that sometimes by himself learns.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Animalia vero fortitudinem in cmribns habent et prona incedunt et velut yento in cmribns yehnntnr, et ideo naturaliter quaedam eomm natare possnnt, quia nt in terra prona incednnt, ita etiam in aqua prona natant, quod homo non facit, qnoniam totns erectus incedit et se inclinans et prostemens natat. De uberibtis, Circa umbilicum yero mnlieris, scilicet snperius et inferius umbilicum, quaedam yenul£ke se inyicem complicant, qnamm quaedam sursum ad nbera se extendunt, quaedam deorsnm ad matricem descendunt, et istae omnes ' de succo cibomm et potnnm repletionem capinnt et nutriuntnr, illae tamen magis, quae ad ubera, qnam illae, quae ad matricem extendnntnr.
text_translation: Animals indeed have strength in their bodies and walk prone, and are carried like the wind in their bodies, and therefore some can swim naturally, just as they walk prone on land, so too they swim prone in water, which humans do not do, because humans walk entirely upright and swim by inclining themselves and lying prone. Regarding the breasts, indeed around the navel of the woman, that is, the upper and lower parts of the navel, some small veins entwine into themselves, than some extend upwards towards the breasts, some descend downwards towards the womb, and all these take fullness from the juice of food and drink and are nourished, those however more which go to the breasts than those which extend to the womb.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Interim antem dum mulier puella est, nbera ei crescnnt, nsqne dnm yennlae, qnae ad matricem extendnntur, riynlos menstmomm edncunt; et tunc crescere desistunt, excepto qnod postea yelnt spongia interdum replentur. Item de conceptu et lacte. Conceptns autem in muliere menstrua sibi attrahit, ita quod rivuli illi, qui ab eadem mnliere prius educebantur, ad eundem conceptum tunc transeunt. Qui cum in camem et in ossa formari incipit, ita qnod etiam a vitali spiritu motum accipit, venulae & illae, quae ad ubera ascendunt, de vitali motu eiusdem conceptus et de virtute elementorum se aperiunt et dein?
text_translation: Meanwhile, before a woman is a girl, her members grow, until the vaginas, which extend toward the womb, begin to menstruate; and then they cease to grow, except that afterward the sponge is sometimes filled. Also regarding conception and milk. Conception, however, in a menstruating woman draws itself to her, so that those rivulets, which were previously expelled from the same woman, then pass to that conception. Which, when it begins to form in the chamber and in the bones, so that it also receives movement from the vital spirit, the venulae and those which ascend to the breasts open themselves from the vital movement of that conception and from the virtue of the elements and then?

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: ceps de succo ciborum et potuum, quibus corpus mulieris nntritnr, lac ad ubera ferunt. Et lac albedinem habet, qnia cnm eibus et potus sumitur, in praegnante muliere in dnas partes se dividit, ita quod altera pars ex eo supplementnm matrici tribuit, altera lacti in uberibus subvenit. Sic etiam sangnis in duabus naturis excistit, scilicet quod mbet, cum in quiete est, et quod albedinem emittit, cum in commixtione maris et feminae movetur. Nam et de IJ^ cibo frumenti et de aliis coctis cibis lac albedinem capit, qnia fmmentum albam farinam habet et cibus, dum coquitnr, albam spumam eicit; ita etiam cibus et potus praegnantis mulieris velut albam spumam per succum suum ad nbera illius transmittit.
text_translation: The head of the juice of foods and drinks, by which the body of the woman is nourished, sends milk to the breasts. And milk has whiteness, because when food and drink are taken, in the pregnant woman it divides into two parts, so that one part from it supplies the uterus, the other aids milk in the breasts. Thus also blood exists in two natures, namely that which, when at rest, and that which emits whiteness, when the mixture of man and woman moves. For also from the food of grain and from other cooked foods milk receives whiteness, because grain has white flour and food, when cooked, emits white foam; thus also food and drink of the pregnant woman, as it were, white foam through their juice transmits to her breasts.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postqnam autem infans natus fnerit, et postquam mulier pnrgatnr, venulae illae, quae ad matricem descendunt, contrahuntur et interim menstma edacunt, dum venulae illae, quae ad ubera ascendnnt, in lacte apertae sunt. Quod autem lac in nberibus habundat, interim dnm infans sugit, hoc ideo est, (quod cum infans sugit, per praedictas venulas lac sugendo ad ubera trahit et sugendo semper aditum praedictamm venulamm ad ubera aperit. De menstruis, Qui postquam sugere desistit, lac in mnliere deficit et evanescit, et ita venulae, quae ad matricem tendunt, iterum aperiuntur et rivulos suos secundum tempora sua educunt.
text_translation: After the infant is born, and after the woman is purified, those veins which descend toward the womb contract, and during that time the menstrual blood is consumed, until those veins which ascend toward the breasts are opened in milk. That milk which abounds in the breasts, while the infant suckles, is thus caused, (for when the infant suckles, through the aforementioned veins milk is drawn to the breasts by suckling, and by suckling it always opens the aforementioned veins toward the breasts). Concerning the menstrual blood: when the infant ceases to suckle, milk in the woman becomes deficient and disappears, and thus the veins which tend toward the womb are again opened, and they lead their streams according to their own times.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed dum mulier infantem lactat, si interim concipit, adhuc absque periculo infantis lactare potest, usque dum conceptus in camem et in ossa in ipsa formatur; et tunc infantem lactare desistat, quia fortitudo et sanitas lactis ad conceptum per menstma descendit, S ita qnod lac ab uberibus eiusdem mulieris magis livorem quam sanitatem deinceps emittit. De digestione. Cam autem homo comedit, vennlae, quae gustum sentiunt, eum per corpus dividunt, et subtiliorem succum eorundem ciborum interiores venae, scilicet iecoriSy cordis et pulmonis a stomacbo suscipiunt et per totum corpus deportant, et ita sanguis in bomine augetar et corpus nutritur, velut ignis per foUem ad incendium excitatur et sicut per ventum et rorem gramen viret et crescit.
text_translation: But when a woman is nursing an infant, if she conceives in the meantime, she can still nurse the infant without danger to it, until the conception forms in the chamber and in the bones; and then she ceases to nurse the infant, because the strength and health of the milk descend to the conception through the menstruum, so that milk henceforth emitted from the breasts of the same woman sends forth more darkness than health. On digestion. As for the man who eats, the veins, which perceive taste, divide him through the body, and the more subtle juice of those foods is received by the inner veins, namely those of the liver, heart, and lungs from the stomach, and carried throughout the body; and thus blood increases in the body and the body is nourished, just as fire is excited toward combustion through the bladder, and as grass grows and increases through wind and dew.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam sicut follis ignem ezcitat, et ut ventus et ros gramina educunt, sic etiam succus ciborum et potuum sanguinem et tabem atque carnem bominis exsurgere et augmentari faciunt. Sed ut foUis ignis non est et ut ventus et ros gramina non sunt, ita quoque nec succus ciborum sanguis nec succus potuum tabes, sed succus ciborum secundum sanguinem coloratur et in ipso est, et succus potuum secundum tabem colorem accipit et in ipso manet, et sanguinem ad tabem ita extollunt et ezsurgere faciunt, ut fermentum totam massam farinae, id est pastam, et in ipsis ita permanent et eis boc modo coadunantur et cum eis et in eis evanescunt et consumuntur.
text_translation: Nam just as the bellows excite fire, and as wind and dew nourish grass, so also the juice of foods and drinks causes the blood and phlegm and flesh of man to rise and grow. But just as the bellows do not produce fire, and as wind and dew are not grass, so neither is the juice of foods blood nor the juice of drinks phlegm, but the juice of foods is colored according to the blood and resides in it, and the juice of drinks receives the color of phlegm and remains in it, and thus they elevate and cause the blood to rise toward phlegm, as fermentation elevates the entire mass of flour, that is, the dough, and in them they remain thus and in this way coalesce with them, and with them and in them they fade and are consumed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De egestione. Quod autem in comestis cibis et potibus defoetatum est, ad inferiora bominis descendit et in putredinem vertitur, et postquam putredinem acceperit, ab bomine eicitur, quemadmodum cum uvae super tortular ponuntur, vinum in vas ezcipitur, et quod residuum est, scilicet folliculi, abiciuntur. De sanguine, Et cum bomo bibit, subtilior succus, qui in potu est, tabem sanguinis augmentat, et quod defoetatum est, in potu ad inferiora descendit, et postquam ezcoctum fuerit, de bomine egreditur, velut liquor vini in vase superius natat et faeces in fundo iacent.
text_translation: On excretion. Whatever has been expelled from foods and drinks, descends to the lower parts of the body and turns into decay, and after it has received decay, it is expelled from the body, just as when grapes are placed upon a press, wine is received into a vessel, and the residue, namely the dregs, are cast away. On blood. And when man drinks, the more subtle juice, which is in the drink, increases the decay of blood, and whatever has been expelled, in the drink descends to the lower parts, and after it has been boiled, it exits from the body, just as the liquid of wine in the vessel floats on top and the dregs lie at the bottom.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam sanguis de succo ciborum augetur et tabes sanguinis de potu, nec sanguis sine tabe esse poterit, quemadmodum nec cibus sine potu in bomine, quoniam si sanguis tabem non baberet, durus esset nec undas fluendo baberet; sicut etiam, si bomo comederet et non biberet, totus aresceret nec vivere posset. Unde bomo, qui malos et superfluos cibos come dit, malum sanguinem nutrit, et ille, qui malos et superCAUSAE ET LIB. II. fluos pottLS samit, malam tabem in se auget, quoniam mali suocsi cibornm et potaum sanguini et tabi eius coadunantur.
text_translation: Nam sanguis de succo ciborum augetur et tabes sanguinis de potu, nec sanguis sine tabe esse poterit, quemadmodum nec cibus sine potu in homine, quoniam si sanguis tabem non haberet, durus esset nec undas fluendo haberet; sicut etiam, si homo comederet et non biberet, totus aresceret nec vivere posset. Unde homo, qui malos et superfluos cibos comedit, malum sanguinem nutrit, et ille, qui malos et super CAUSAE ET LIB. II. fluos potus sumit, malam tabem in se auget, quoniam mali suosi ciborum et potuum sanguini et tabe eius coadunantur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ntthrimento, Oum homo comedit et bibit, vitalui tractus rationalitatis, qui in homine est, gustum et subti liorem succum et odorem eorum sursum ad cerebrum eius ducit et venulas eius replendo calefacii Sed cetera eorundem ciborum et potuum, quae in stomachum perveniunt, cor et iecur et pulmo calefaciunt, et de eodem gustu et subtiliori succo et odore in venulas suas trahunt, ita quod inde replentur et fomentantur et nutriuntur, velut si ari« lo dom et siccuin intestinum in aquam ponitur, quod inde moUificatur et intumescit et repletur.
text_translation: On nutrition. When a man eats and drinks, the vital passage of reason, which is in man, carries the taste and more subtle juice and odor of them upward to his brain and warms his veins by filling them. But the other parts of the same foods and drinks, which reach the stomach, warm the heart and liver and lung, and from the same taste and more subtle juice and odor they draw into their own veins, so that they are filled and warmed and nourished, as if a dry and dry intestine were placed in water, which is thereby softened and swollen and filled.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ita cum homo manducat et bibit, de succo ciborum et potuum venae eius replentur et fomentantur, ita quod succus ille in venis sanguinem et tabem fomentat et de succo venarum sanguis, qui in came est, rubedinem sibi attrahit. De esurie, Postquam comestio in putredinem vadit et arescit, venae de succo suo evaouantur, et sanguis camis rubedinem perdit et aquosus fit. Tunc venae repleri deaiderant, et sanguis camis rubedinem affectant. Et hoc esuries est, quam homo patitur.
text_translation: Thus, when a man eats and drinks, his veins are filled and nourished with the juice of foods and drinks, so that this juice in the veins nourishes blood and waste, and from the juice of the veins, the blood that is in the flesh draws redness upon itself. Concerning hunger: When the meal turns to putrefaction and dries up, the veins are evacuated of their juice, and the blood of the flesh loses its redness and becomes watery. Then the veins are unable to be filled again, and the blood of the flesh is affected by redness. And this is hunger, which a man suffers.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De sUi, Cum homo comedit, in comedendo laborat ut mola in molendo, et de labore comestionis homo incalfiscit et ezsiccatur et ita interius arescere incipit, quod sitis est. Et tunc modiGe Hbat et iterum comedat, et cum iterum in comedendo in calore arescit, iterum sitit, et tnnc iterum bibat, et sic faciat, interim dum comedit, quia si homo in cibo, soilicet interim dum comedit, non biberet, gravis mente et corpore ef&seretur nec bonum succum sanguinis pararet nec bonam digestionem ezinde ao haberet.
text_translation: Of the Blood, When a man eats, in eating he labors as a mill in grinding, and from the labor of eating the man becomes pale and dried up, and thus begins to become dry internally, which is thirst. And then he should drink and eat again, and when again in eating he becomes dry in heat, again he thirsts, and then again he should drink, and thus do, while eating, because if a man in food, scilicet while eating, did not drink, he would become heavy in mind and body and would not prepare good juice of blood nor would have good digestion exinde to have.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem in oibo suo supra modum bibit, malam inundationem procellarum in humoribus corporis sui faoit, ita quod recti humores in eo disperguntur. Et interim dum cibi in putredinem et in ariditatem vertuntur, de calore illo venae et sanguis humectationem quaerunt, et hoc sitis est, et tunc homo moderate bibat et ariditatem in se humectet, alioquin molestiam et tarditatem mentis Hildegardis causae et ourae ed. Kai ser. mLDEGARDIS et corporis incurrit.
text_translation: If, however, in his own vessel he drinks beyond measure, a bad inundation of storms in the humors of his body occurs, so that the right humors in him are dispersed. And in the meantime while foods turn into putrefaction and into dryness, from that heat the veins and blood seek moistening, and this is thirst, and then the man should drink moderately and moisten the dryness within himself, otherwise the causes of Hildegardis and urae ed. and the body incur suffering and mental sluggishness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et homo, qui Yiyentem vitam in se habet et qni diYersis cibis nubitar, maiorem indigentiam potnom habet, interim dum comedit, qnam animalia habeant, qnae feno et gramine pascuntnr. De darmUume. Homo post cibom statim non dormiat, antequam gustus et succus ac odor eorum ad loca sna perveniant. Sed post cibum per aliquam brevem moram a dormitione se contineat, ne si repente post cibum dormierit, eadem dormitio gustum et succum ac odorem ciborum in iniusta et contraria loca ducat et ne velnt pulverem hac et illac in venis dispergat.
text_translation: And the man, who has in himself a living life and who is nourished by diverse foods, has greater power of need, while he eats, than animals have, which are pastured on hay and grass. Of sleep. Man after food should not immediately sleep, before the taste and juice and odor of them reach their own places. But after food, by some brief delay, he should restrain himself from sleep, lest if he sleeps suddenly after food, the same sleep should lead the taste and juice and odor of foods into unjust and contrary places and lest they carry the powder here and there dispersed in the veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si homo per brevem moram se continuerit et tunc ad dormitionem se per modicam horam deposuerit, caro et sanguis eins ex hoc crescit, et sanus inde efficitur. De nocturnali sUi, Saepe autem contingit, quod homo et in die et in nocte, cum evigilat, de calore et de ariditate ciborum sitit. Sed ille caveat, ne mox bibat, dnm sopor adhuG in ipso est, quia infirmitates sibi induceret et sanguinem et humores suos in iniustas procellas ezci2(taret. Sed postquam evigilaverit, per aliquam moram a potu se contineat, quamvis multum sitiat, usque dum sopor pleniter ab eo recesserit.
text_translation: But if a man restrains himself for a brief period and then lays himself down to sleep for a short hour, his flesh and blood thereby grow, and he becomes healthy from this. Regarding his nocturnal state, Often, however, it happens that a man, both in the day and in the night, when he awakens, thirsts from the heat and dryness of foods. But he should beware not to drink immediately, while sleep still remains in him, for he would thereby induce infirmities and agitate his blood and humors into unjust tempests. But after he has awakened, let him restrain himself from drinking for a certain period, although he may be very thirsty, until sleep has fully departed from him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De potu. Et tunc, sive sanus sive infirmus sit, post dormitionem si sitit, vinum aut cerevisiam bibat et non aquam, quia aqua tunc sangninem et humores eius magis laederet quam eos iuvaret. De paralysi fatigatione. Homo, qui de gutta paralysi fatigatur, humores destitutos in se habet, velut undae aquarum sunt, quae in inquiete moventur. £t ideo idem so homo in moribus et in gestu suo moderatus esse non potest, et propterea vinum ieiunus bibat, vel si vinum habere non poterit, cerevisiam de ordeo aut de siligine factam ieiunus bibat, vel si nihil istorum habere poterit, aquam cum pane coquat et per pannum colet et aquam 35illam tepidam bibat et sic per singulos dies fiaciat, et undae procellarum guttae in eo compescuntur.
text_translation: On the use of drink. And then, whether he be healthy or sick, after sleeping, if he is thirsty, let him drink wine or beer and not water, because water would then harm his blood and humors more than it would help them. On paralysis and fatigue. A man who is fatigued by a drop of paralysis has destitute humors within him, like waves of waters which are moved in turmoil. And therefore the same man cannot be moderate in his manners and in his gestures, and therefore let him drink wine on an empty stomach, or if he cannot have wine, let him drink beer made from barley or from hops on an empty stomach, or if he cannot have any of these, let him cook water with bread and strain it through a cloth and drink it warm and thus let him do this daily, and the drops of stormy waves are restrained within him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si idem homo cito corpore defecit, modicum ieiunus bibat, ut pTaedictum est, si autem sanus corpore est, sufEcienter de yino aut de cerevisia aut de aqua cum pane cocta ieinnus bibat, et gutta in eo quiescet. 2e cotidiana. Qui autem cotidianas febres habet, quae diversis cibis nascuntur, ieiunus nihil bibat, quod interius siccus est, quoniam si ieiunus biberet, potus ille corpus eins pertransiret et eum magis infirmum quam sanum redderet. Sed prius comedat, ut yenae eius de succo ciborum prius excipiendo fomententur, et sic potum de vino sumat, et eum non laedit.
text_translation: If the same man quickly becomes weak in body, let him drink a little during fasting, as was previously said; but if his body is healthy, let him drink sufficiently from wine, or from beer, or from water boiled with bread during fasting, and the gut will rest. 2e cotidiana. He who has daily fevers, which arise from various foods, during fasting let him drink nothing, because if he were to drink during fasting, that drink would pass through his body and make him more weak than healthy. But let him eat first, so that the veins of his body may be nourished by the juice of foods taken beforehand, and thus let him take a drink from wine, and it will not harm him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si vinum non habet, cerevisiam bibat, et si illa caret, medonem sumat, vel si illam non habet, aquam coquat et eam infrigidari per mittat et sic bibat. De tertiana et quartana. Qui tertianas et quartanas febres habet, ieiunus non bibat nisi pro magna necessitate, si eum maxima sitis apprehendit, et tunc modicum frigidae aqnae ieiunus bibat. Sed cum prandet, yinum bibat, et hoc salubrius est ei quam aquam bibat ; si vero vino caret, oerevisiam de ordeo factam bibat vel si illa caiat, medonem sumat, vel si illum non habet, aquam tK ctam et infrigidatam bibat.
text_translation: If wine is not available, let him drink beer, and if that is lacking, let him take mead, or if that is not available, let him boil water and send it to be cooled and then drink it thus. Concerning tertian and quartan fevers. He who has tertian and quartan fevers should not drink while fasting unless for great necessity, and if he is seized by great thirst, then let him drink a little cold water while fasting. But when he eats, let him drink wine, and this is more wholesome for him than to drink water; if indeed wine is lacking, let him drink beer made from barley, or if that is lacking, let him take mead, or if that is not available, let him drink water covered and cooled.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t nuUus homo ieiunus bibat, nisi per infirmitates compellatur, ut ieiunus bibat; et tunc ei salubrius est, ut vinum bibat quam aquam. Quod si aliqnis absque necessitate vinum ieiunus bibit, avarum in cupiditate escarum et potuum eum facit et amentem et stnltum in sensibus suis. De comestione et dbo. Cum autem homo ieiunus est , cibum, qui de frugibus est et de farina factus, primum comedat, quoniam siccus cibus est et sanam fortitadinem homini tribuit; et calidum cibum primitus come- dO dat, ut stomachus eius incalescat, et non frigidum cibum, quod si primitus firigidum cibum comedit, stomachum eius ita frigidum facit, quod postea vix per calidos cibos incalescere valet.
text_translation: And no man fasting should drink, unless compelled by infirmities to drink while fasting; and then it is more salubrious for him to drink wine than water. If, however, any man drinks wine while fasting without necessity, it makes him greedy in the desire for food and drink and foolish and dull in his senses. On eating and drinking. When, however, a man is fasting, he should first eat food made from fruits and flour, because it is dry food and gives healthy strength to man; and he should first eat warm food, so that his stomach may be warmed, and not cold food, for if he first eats cold food, his stomach becomes so cold that afterward it scarcely can be warmed by warm foods.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed calidum cibum primum comedat, usque dum stomachus eius bene incalescat. Et si deinde frigidum dbum comedat, calor, qui stomachum eius pertransivit, subsequens frigos in cibo superat. Et omnia 8» & pomaria et ea, quae succos habent et bumiditatem velut herbulae, in prima comestione sua devitet, quod tabem et livorem ac inquietudinem in humoribus ei inferrent, quae postea, dum iam aUquantum cibi sumpsit, comedere potest, et tunc ei magis sanitatem quam debilitatem conferunt.
text_translation: But first eat warm food, until his stomach is well warmed. And if he then eats cold food, the heat which has passed through his stomach overcomes the subsequent cold in the food. And all things and fruits and those which have juices and humidity like herbs, he should eat in his first meal, because they would bring weariness and pallor and restlessness into his humors, which he can eat later, when he has already taken some amount of food, and then they confer more health than debility upon him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De prandio, Homini autem sano in corpore bonum et salubre est propter bonam digestionem, ut a cibo prandii se contineat usque fere ante mediam diem et circa me diam diem. Infirmo vero et debili ac in corpore deficienti bonum et salubre est-, ut mane prandeat, quatinus vires, quas per se non habet, de cibis saltem capiat. Ad noctem quoque eosdem cibos homo comedere potest et eosdem potus sumere, quos in die sumpsit, si voluerit, et tunc etiam tam tempestive ante noctem comedat, ut, antequam ad dormiendum se collocet, deambulationem suam habere poesit.
text_translation: On the meal, For a healthy man in the body, it is good and healthy because of good digestion, that he should restrain himself from food until almost before midday and around midday. But for the weak and feeble man and one deficient in the body, it is good and healthy, that he should eat in the morning, so that the strengths, which he does not have himself, may at least be taken from food. To the night also, the same foods the man may eat and the same drinks take, which he took during the day, if he wishes, and then also as timely before night he may eat, so that, before he places himself to sleep, he may have his walking.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De potus diversitate. Pretiosum autem et forte vinum bibitum venas et sanguinem hominis iniusto modo commovet et hv^ores ac omnem humiditatem, qtii in homine ^ sunt, ad se trahit, velut purgatoriae potiones faciunt, et ita cum periculo ante maturum tempus urinam interdum educit. Quod Hunonicum vinum non facit, quia tam fortes vires in se non habet, quod humores hominis supra modum conamovere possit. £t ideo vires pretiosi vini aut pane imposito aut aqua infusa minuantur, quia nec sano nec infirmo homini valet ad bibendum sic non temperatum.
text_translation: On the diversity of drinks. Indeed, precious and strong wine, when drunk, moves the veins and blood of man in an unjust manner, and draws to itself the humors and all humidity that are in man, as purgative potions do, and thus sometimes brings forth urine before the proper time with danger. That Hunonic wine does not do, because it does not possess such strong forces as could excessively move the humors of man. And therefore, the forces of precious wine are reduced either by being placed upon bread or by water poured in, because it is not suitable for drinking by neither a healthy nor an infirm man in such an untempered manner.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed necesse non est, ut Hunonicum vinum sic temperetur, quoniam fortes vires in se non habet. Sed tamen si aliquis homo ei aquam infundere voluerit aut panem impo nere et sic bibere, tanto suavior quidem ad bibendum est, sed non tanto sanior. Vinum autem natnraliter aliquid aquatile in se habet, quia et de rore et de pluvia nutritur. Et inde etiam est, quod homo, qui vinum bibit, quamvis illud assidue bibat, et non aquam, in sanguine suo tamen aquatiles humores habet.
text_translation: But it is not necessary that Hunnic wine be thus tempered, because it does not possess strong forces within itself. However, if someone wishes to pour water into it or place bread into it and drink it thus, it is indeed sweeter for drinking, but not necessarily healthier. Wine, however, naturally contains something watery within itself, because it is nourished both by dew and by rain. And from this also comes the fact that a man who drinks wine, although he drinks it frequently and not water, nevertheless has watery humors in his blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de sanguine. Nam sanguis aridus et siccos esset et non flueret, sicut nec medulla fluit, si livorem aquae U in se noa haberet; et si saaguis hamidns non esset, caro subsistere non posset, sed velat terra esset De dibcrum differmtia in hieme. Homo, (qui in nimio frigore hiemis, cum ipse interius frigidus est, valde calidos dbos comedit , melancoliam sibi facile attrahit et eam in se excitat. Qui autem valde Mgidos cibos comedity cum ipse interius frigidus est, febres inde acquirit.
text_translation: Item de sanguine. Nam sanguis aridus et siccos esset et non flueret, sicut nec medulla fluit, si livorem aquae ut in se non haberet; et si sanguis humidus non esset, caro subsistere non posset, sed velat terra esset de differmtia in hieme. Homo, qui in nimio frigore hiemis, cum ipse interius frigidus est, valde calidos cibos comedit, melancoliam sibi facile attrahit et eam in se excitat. Qui autem valde magnos cibos comedit, cum ipse interius frigidus est, febres inde acquirit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qoi vero in frigore hiem^is, cum ipse interius frigidus est, temperatoB cibos comedit, id est nec nimis calidos nec nimis frigidos, ex eis modicam refectionem consequitur, quia lo boniun sanguinem ei non conferunt. Qui autem in tempore frigoris in valde calido loco temperatos cibos comedity scilicet qui nec nimis calidi nec nimis frigidi sunt, cibi iUi eum non laedunt, sed tamen calor, quem ipse tunc habet, debiiitatem ei infert. i& De frigoris temperamento Homo, qui in frigore hiemis oomedere yult, locum sibi faciat, qui nec nimis calidus nec nimis frigidus sit, sed temperatus, et etiam nec nimis calidos nec nimis frigidos cibos comedat, sed temperatos, et ita sanitatem in cibis retinebit.
text_translation: Those who, however, in the cold of winter, when he himself is internally cold, eat temperate foods, that is, neither excessively hot nor excessively cold, from them obtain only a modest nourishment, because those foods do not contribute good blood to him. Those who, however, in the time of cold, in a very hot place, eat temperate foods, that is, which are neither excessively hot nor excessively cold, those foods do not harm him, but nevertheless the heat which he then has inflicts debility upon him. Concerning the tempering of cold: A man who wishes to eat in the cold of winter, let him make for himself a place which is neither excessively hot nor excessively cold, but temperate, and also let him eat neither excessively hot nor excessively cold foods, but temperate ones, and thus he will retain health in foods.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tamen quamvis calorem in vestibus sibi faciat, in frigido looo, interim dum oome^it, non sedeat, quia si frigidum aerem comedendo in se trahit, ille eum infirmum reddit. Calor autem de prunis ad dorsum hominis ascendens, dum comedit, eum saniorem reddit, quam si calor ignis ei in faciem acoedat. De aestdUia intemperie et ciborum diversitate. Homo autem, qui in aestate, cum interius valde calidns est, valde calidos cibos comedit, guttam in se facile excitat ; si vero, cum in aestate valde calidus interius est, valde frigidos ciboB comedit, Aegma in se parat.
text_translation: And yet although he makes heat for himself in his clothes, in the cold place, meanwhile while he eats, he should not sit, because if he draws cold air into himself by eating, it makes him weak. Heat, however, rising from prunes to the back of man, while he eats, makes him healthier than if the heat of fire approaches his face. Concerning the summer's intemperance and diversity of foods. But a man who in summer, when internally very hot, eats very hot foods, easily excites a drop within himself; if, however, when in summer he is very hot internally, he eats very cold foods, he prepares Aegma within himself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t ideo homo in aestate temperatos in calore et in frigore cibos sumat, et hi bonum sangoinem et sanam camem ei conferunt. Et in aestate cum homo interius valde calidus est, si tunc multum comedit, de multa comestione sanguis eius supra modum inoalescit et humores in malum modum vertuntur, atque cames corporis eius inflantur et iniuste elevantur, quia calor aeris tunc nimius est. Nam si tunc modicum comedit, debilitatem ei non infert, sed sanitatem ei retinet.
text_translation: And therefore man in summer should take foods tempered in heat and in cold, and these confer good blood and sound camem upon him. And in summer when man is very hot internally, if then he eats much, from much eating his blood exceeds excessively and humors turn in a bad way, and the cames of his body swell and rise unjustly, because the heat of air then is excessive. For if then he eats a little, it does not bring weakness to him, but retains his health.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In hieme autem, cum homo valde frigidus interius est, si multum comedit, sanitatem ei infert et pinguem facit. Sed tamen omni tempore homo caveat, ne ferventes et fumigantes ab humore cibos comedat, sed postquam cocti fuerint, sustineat, usque dum fervor et fumus humoris eorum abscedat, quod si eos feryentes et fumigantes come derit, ventrem eius inflando sustoUunt et lepram in eo facile excitant. Sed et dum homo magnam tristitiam patitur, sufiEicienter de convenientibus sibi cibis comedat, ut per cibos refocilletur, quoniam tristitia eum gravat.
text_translation: In winter, however, when a man is very cold within, if he eats much, it inflicts health upon him and makes him plump. But nevertheless, at all times a man should beware, that he does not eat foods hot and smoky from the humor, but after they have been cooked, he should wait until the heat and smoke of their humor have departed; for if he eats them hot and smoky, they swell his belly and easily excite leprosy within him. But also, when a man suffers great sorrow, he should sufficiently eat from foods suitable for him, so that through foods he may be refueled, for sorrow burdens him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Dum autem magnam laetitiam habet, modicum comedat, quoniam sanguis eius in apertione vagationis tunc disso lutus est, ne si timc multum comederit, humores, qui in sanguine eius sunt, in procellas febrium vertantur. Et homo in hieme non multum bibat, quia aer humores eius tunc humectat, quoniam si tunc multum biberit, humores, qui in se sunt, evertunt et sibimet infirmitates inducit. Et vinum aut cerevisiam in hieme bibat et aquam, si potuerit, devitet, quia eo tempore aquae sanae non sunt propter humiditatem terrae.
text_translation: While she has great joy, let her eat a little, because her blood is then dissolved in the openness of wandering, so that if she fears to eat much, the humors which are in her blood may turn into fevers. And man in winter should not drink much, because the air moistens his humors then, since if he drinks much at that time, the humors which are in him will be overturned and he will bring upon himself infirmities. And let him drink wine or beer in winter and water, if he can, avoid, because at that time waters are not healthy because of the humidity of the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In aestate vero plus quam in hieme bibat et secundum modum et qualitatem ciborum, quos tunc sumit, quoniam humores in ea tunc exsiccantur ; et aqua tunc bibita minus eum laedit propter siccitatem terrae quam in hieme. Et in aestate, cum homo interius valde calidus est, et si etiam tunc sanus in corpore est, aquam tepidam moderate bibat et mox hac et illac parumper deambulet, ut in eo incalescat. Et hoc ad sanitat.em corporis ei utilius est quam si vinum bibat.
text_translation: In summer indeed drink more than in winter, and according to the manner and quality of foods which he then takes, because the humors in it then become dried out; and water then drunk harms him less because of the dryness of the earth than in winter. And in summer, when a man is very hot internally, and if he is also healthy in the body, let him drink warm water moderately and soon walk a little here and there, so that he may warm up in it. And this is more useful for the health of the body than if he drinks wine.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem in corpore infirmus est, et si in aestate est, vinum aqna mixtum aut cerevisiam bibat, quia hoc eum magis refocillat, quam si aquam bibat. Sed tamen omni tempore tam aestatis quam hiemis homo caveat, ne supra mo dum bibat, quoniam nimia pluvia terram infundendo laedit; sic etiam ille, qui supra modum bibit, corpns suum ad inutilitatem diversorum humorum ducit. Nec etiam homo supra modum sibi potmn subtrahat, quoniam si in abstinentia potois aridum se fecerit, gravitatem mentis et corporis inde incurrit.
text_translation: If, however, the body is weak, and if it is in summer, let him drink mixed wine and water or beer, because this refreshes him more than if he drinks water. But still, at all times, whether in summer or winter, a man should beware not to drink beyond measure, because excessive rain, by pouring down, damages the earth; thus also he who drinks beyond measure leads his body to the uselessness of various humors. Nor should a man withhold drink from himself beyond measure, because if he makes himself dry through abstinence, he incurs the weight of mind and body from this.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nec cibi, quos comedit, bonam digestionem nec sanitatem corporis ei interius inferre possunt, yelut etiam terra gravis et dura et arida fit nec bonos fructus affert, si humectatio pluviae ei subtrahitur. Gam autem yenter hominis cibis et potibus repletur, necesse est, ut purgationem digestionis habeat. De minutione. Gum venae hominis sanguine plenae Biint, a noxio livore et ab humore digestionis suae per lO incisionem purgari debent. Cum autem vena hominis inciditur, sangnis eius quasi repentino pavore concutitur, et qnod tunc primum egreditur, sanguis est, et putredo ^t digestio sanguinis simul emanant, et ideo quod tunc emanat varios coiores habet, quoniam tabes et sanguis est.
text_translation: Nor can the foods which he eats bring good digestion or health to his body internally, just as also heavy, hard, and dry earth becomes and does not bear good fruits, if the moistening of rain is taken away from it. For indeed the intestine of man is filled with foods and drinks, and it is necessary that it have a purgation of digestion. On diminution. When the veins of man are full of blood, they must be purged from noxious bile and from his own digestive humor by an incision. When however the vein of man is cut, his blood is shaken as if by sudden fear, and what then first flows out is blood, and rot and digestion of blood simultaneously emanate, and therefore what then emanates has various colors, because decay and blood are.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde postquam tabes cum sangnine emanaverit, purus sangnis egreditur, et tunc a minutione cessandum est. Qni autem sangninem in vena minuit, tunc si sanus et fortis oorpore est, eum ad mensuram hanc minuat, quam f ortis et sitiens vir uno tractu halitus in aqua bibere potest. Quod si debilis corpore est, tunc sanguinem in vena ad mensuram hanc minuat, quae ovum rectae moderationis includere potest. Nam minutio sanguinis, quae sapra modum fit, corpus debilitat, sicut et inundatio plnviarum, quae sine moderatione supra terram cadit, eam laedit.
text_translation: Therefore, after the consumption with blood has emanated, pure blood exits, and then cessation from reduction must be made. He who reduces blood in the vein, then if the body is healthy and strong, let him reduce it to this measure, which a strong and thirsty man can drink in one gulp in water. If the body is weak, then let blood in the vein be reduced to this measure, which can contain an egg of proper moderation. For the reduction of blood, which is done beyond measure, weakens the body, just as the flooding of rains, which falls without moderation upon the land, damages it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Dla autem minutio, quae recta fit mensura, malos hnmores aufert et corpus curat, quemadmodum et pluvia, quae paulatim et moderate super terram cadit, eam innndat et salubrem ad producendum fructus facit. De minutionis diversitaie. Nunc autem , ut prae- ^ dietnm est, quod primum de vulnere et de incisione venae egreditur, sanguis est, et in eo venenum et pestiferi humores simul emanant. Deinde cum emanatio illa in rectum ruborem et in alium colorem mutatur, sanguis et humores aequalis mensurae sunt.
text_translation: For the minute, which is made right by measure, removes bad humors and heals the body, just as rain, which falls gradually and moderately upon the earth, waters it and makes it healthy for producing fruit. On the diversity of minute. Now, as was previously said, that which first flows out from the wound and from the incision of the vein is blood, and in it poison and pestiferous humors are emitted together. Then, when that emission changes into a proper redness and into another color, blood and humors are of equal measure.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tunc si idem sanguis amplius emanaverit, boni et mali humores cum reliquo sangnine simul subsequuntur. £t tunc ab eadem effusione cessaDdtim est, quia, si tuno ampHus illa effasio emitlitiiry livores sanguine amisso superhabundant et melancoliam. ac alias pestes in eodem bomine excitant, quoniam paupertas sanguinis eis resistere non valet. Sed et quemadmodrun esuries corpori vires minuit, ita etiam immoderata minixtdo sanguinis corpus debile facit, moderata vero corpori sanitatem tribuit, sicut et moderatus .
text_translation: Then if the same blood flows forth more abundantly, the good and bad humors along with the remaining blood follow in succession. And then from the same effusion it is ceased, because, if at that time the effusion is more abundant, the lividness, having lost blood, superabounds and causes melancholy, and other plagues in the same body, because the paucity of blood cannot resist them. But just as hunger weakens the body's strength, so also immoderate mixture of blood makes the body weak, but moderate mixture bestows health upon the body, as also moderate [blood] does.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: cibus et potus corpns sublevant. Quando minmtur. Qui fortis et sanus atque crassns in corpore est, ille in tertio mense minuat, quia in duobus augmentis et in duobus detrimentis lunae sanguis in homine iam ad se rediit et venae eius plenae sunty quoniam, si prius minuerit, se debilem faoit, quia sanguis nondum yires suas ad plenujn recepit, yel si diatins distulerit, sanguis in eo superbabundans inficitur, id est quasi in faeces vertitnr. In detrimento autem lunae minuaty soilicet in prima die, cum luna decresoere incipit, aut in seounda aut in tertia aut in quarta aut in quinta aut in sexta die, et tunc cesset, quia prior et postera minutio tam utilis non erit; et non minuat m incremento Iniiaey quod minutio illa nociva est, quoniam tunc tabes sangnini iutermixta facile ab eo separari non poteet.
text_translation: Food and drink elevate the body. When it diminishes. He who is strong and healthy and stout in the body, he diminishes in the third month, because in two increases and two decreases of the moon, the blood in man has already returned to itself and his veins are full. Because, if he diminishes earlier, he makes himself weak, because the blood has not yet received its fullness, or if he delays the diminution, the blood in him becomes superabundant and is infected, that is, as if it turns into feces. In the decrease of the moon, the diminution occurs, that is, on the first day, when the moon begins to decrease, or on the second or third or fourth or fifth or sixth day, and then it should cease, because the prior and subsequent diminution will not be useful; and it should not diminish during the increase of the moon, because that diminution is harmful, because then the phlegm mixed with the blood cannot be easily separated from it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in augmento lunae sanguis et tabes simul in homine quasi moderate fluunt nec se tunc facile separari permittunt, sicut fluvius, qui in loco suo temperate cutrens modum sunna servat; in detrimento autem lunae sanguis concuti et inundare magis incipit et tabem supereffluere fEUsit, velut fortis inundatio aquae, quae foetida in se ostendit et spumam de se eicit. Sed minutio in venis magis senibos congruit quam iuvenibus, quoniam sanguis in venis senum plus tabe permixtus est quam sanguis in venis iuvenum.
text_translation: For in the increase of the moon, blood and decay flow together in man as if moderately, nor then do they easily permit themselves to be separated, as a river which in its place temperately flowing preserves the measure of its course; but in the decrease of the moon, blood begins more to shake and flood, and decay overflows, as it were a mighty flood of water which shows itself foul and casts forth foam from itself. But diminution in veins is more suitable for the aged than for youths, because blood in the veins of the aged is more mixed with decay than is blood in the veins of youths.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: . Masculus tamen^ si necesse fuerit, in duodecimo anno aetatis suae venam incidere potest, quod sanguis in eo iam oonfortatus est, sed tamen ad mensuram illam minuat, 35quantum duae testae nucis unius capere valent, et hoo faciat semel in anno et usque ad quintum decimum annum, quinto decimo autem anno, quia iam sanguis BT LIB. U. bominis fortitudinem habet et venae eias plenitudinem, tunc, si sanns est corpore, seonndam mensuram illam sangniQem minuat, qnam sitiens yir ono halitn in aqna faibere potest, nt supra dictum eet; et boc faciat usqne ad qninqnaginta annos.
text_translation: Masculus tamen, si necesse fuerit, in duodecimo anno aetatis suae venam incidere potest, quod sanguis in eo iam confortatus est, sed tamen ad mensuram illam minuat, quantum duae testae nucis unius capere valent, et hoc faciat semel in anno et usque ad quintum decimum annum, quinto decimo autem anno, quia iam sanguis BT LIB. U. bominis fortitudinem habet et venae eias plenitudinem, tunc, si sanus est corpore, secundam mensuram illam sanguinem minuat, quam sitiens vir uno halitum in aqua faebere potest, ut supra dictum est; et hoc faciat usque ad quinquaginta annos.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Post qninqnagesimnm vero axmnm, quo iam sangnis et flegma in mascnlo indpinnt decrescere et corpns eins arescere, semel in anno venam incidat et medietatem mensnrae iUins, qnam ante in minntione sangninis babnit, minuat, et boc nsqne ad octoginta annos fiaciat. Postea vero nulla incisio venae illi prodest, sed lO magis enm laedet, qnia iam viror sanguinis in eo amit, . nisi magna innndatio et emptio bnmomm ipsi forte snperveniat, et tunc propter banc magnam necessitatem modicnm sangninis in vena emittat.
text_translation: After the fiftieth year, wherein the blood and phlegm in the male begin to decrease and his body wither, once a year incise the vein and reduce the half of the measure of that which before in the measurement of blood had, and this until eighty years do. Afterwards indeed no incision of the vein benefits him, but ten more harms him, because the vigor of blood in him is lost, unless a great flood and purchase of humors strongly comes upon him, and then because of this great necessity a small amount of blood in the vein emit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed qnia post octoginta annos venae masculi iam deficiunt et minntio sanguinis ei non valet, berbis, scilicet cardi nigri et similibns in corpore sno pnstnlas intnrgescere faciat, nt ita noxins bnmor, qui est inter cntem et camem, mmpentibus pnstulis emnat. Mnlier antem magis plnres noxios bumores et magis noxiam tabem in corpore sno qnam mascnlns babet. De wwMins minuHone. Unde et ipsa a duodecimo anno aetatis snae eandem constitntionem et eandem observationem minntionis sangninis in vena obeervet, qnam et mascnlns babet, exoepto qnod ipsa minntionem snam nsqne ad centesimnm annnm prolongabit, qnoniam de noxiis bnmoribns et de infecta tabe maior necessitas ei inenmbit qnam viro.
text_translation: But since after eighty years the veins of the male already fail and the diminution of blood no longer holds, females, that is, the black hearts and similar things in their body cause pustules to swell, so that thus the noxious humors, which are between the abdomen and the chest, are emitted through multiple pustules. A woman, however, has more noxious humors and more noxious sickness in her body than a male. On the diminution of women. Therefore, she also from the twelfth year of her age observes the same constitution and the same observation of the diminution of blood in the vein as the male has, except that she will prolong her own diminution even until the hundredth year, since from the noxious humors and from the infected sickness a greater necessity will be imposed upon her than upon a man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qnod etiam menstmum tempns ita ostendit. Nam si menstmo tempore de noxiis bnmoribns et de tabe non pnrgaretur, tota intnmesceret et inflaretnr nec vivere posset. Post centesimnm antem annnm sangninem in vena non minnat, qnod tnnc tam sangnine qnam bnmoribns profluentibns iam pnrgata est. Sed si forte postea aliqnos bnmores in se senserit, tnnc pnstnlas intnrgescere faciat et in eisdem locis, in qnibns coctnrae solent in bomine fieri. De venis. Sdendnm est, qnod in cepbalica plnres 'bnmores habnndant qnam in mediana ant qnam in epatica, qnia etiam oepbalicae plnres venae adhaerent, qnae bnmores portant, quam medianae aut epaticae; et ideo salubrius esty ut in cepbalica sanguis saepius minuatur quam in aliis venis.
text_translation: That also the menstrual time shows thus. For if during the menstrual time the noxious humors and the tabes were not purged, the whole body would swell and not be able to live. After the hundredth year, the blood in the vein does not diminish, for then both the blood and the flowing humors have already been purged. But if afterward someone feels some humors in himself, then let the pustules swell in those places where coctions are usually made in the body. Concerning the veins. It should be noted that in the head there are more humors abound than in the middle or in the liver, for the head also has more veins adhering, which carry the humors, than the middle or the liver; and therefore it is healthier that the blood in the head be diminished more often than in other veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam qui multum flegma in capite et in pectore habei, aut cui caput reboat, ita quod auditus eios inde aliquantum obduratur, sangninem in cephaJica minnat, sed tamen praecaveat, ne multum sanguinis ibi emittat, ne forte oculi eins exinde caligare incipiant, quoniam quaedam venulae, quae ad oculos tendunt, ipsis adbaerent, ita ut, si multus sanguis ab ea aufertur, etiam venalae sibi adhaerentes sanguine evacuantur et ita homo in oculis caligare incipit.
text_translation: For he who has much phlegm in the head and in the chest, or whose head resounds, so that his hearing is somewhat dulled thereby, let him bleed in the cephalic veins, but nevertheless beware that he does not emit too much blood there, lest perhaps his eyes begin to grow dim, because certain small veins which tend toward the eyes adhere to them, so that if much blood is taken away from them, even the veins adhering to the eyes are evacuated of blood, and thus the man begins to grow dim in the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: J e scarificatione. Cui oculi a malis humoribus caligare incipiunt aut cui ulcerosi sunt vel cui caro circa oculos evolYitur, retro aures et inter cervicem comibns aut yentosis sanguinem moderate minuat, et hoc ter vel quater in anno faciat, vel si saepius ibi pro necessitate scarificare vult, tanto minus sanguinis emittat, ne si sanguinem supra modum abstulerit, inde laedatur. £t ea parte corporis in vena sanguinem minuat, qua parte dolet. Si quis in lingua dolet, ita quod aut tumet aut suiret, eam aut cum modico flebotomo aut cum brema parumper inoidat, ut ibidem livor erumpat, et melius habebit.
text_translation: De scarificatione. Cui oculi a malis humoribus caligare incipiunt aut cui ulcerosi sunt vel cui caro circa oculos evolvetur, retro aures et inter cervicem combinis aut ientosis sanguinem moderate minuat, et hoc ter vel quater in anno faciat, vel si saepius ibi pro necessitate scarificare vult, tanto minus sanguinis emittat, ne si sanguinem supra modum abstulerit, inde laedatur. Et ea parte corporis in vena sanguinem minuat, qua parte dolet. Si quis in lingua dolet, ita quod aut tumet aut suint, eam aut cum modico flebotomo aut cum brema parumper inoidat, ut ibidem livor erumpat, et melius habebit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t qui in dentibus dolet, cum modico flebotomo aut cum brema camem, quae circa eundem dentem est, id est 2& eanefleic^f uno vubiere modicum incidat, ut tabes ibidem egrediatur, et melius habebit. Qui autem triste cor et oppressam mentem habet, et qui in quolibet latere aut in puhnone dolet, in mediana sanguinem minuat, sed tamen moderate, ut fortitudinem cordis retineat, ne si supra modum ibi sanguinem minuerit, in corde minuat. Quod si quispiam in corde dolet, antequam in vena sanguinem minuat, in dextro brachio in mediana sanguinem auferat, et in corde melius habebit.
text_translation: And he who suffers in the teeth, with a moderate phlebotomy or with a swelling near the same tooth, that is, if with one wound a little incision is made, so that decay may emerge there, and he will be better. He who, however, has a sad heart and an oppressed mind, and who suffers in any side or in the lung, let him reduce the blood in the median, but yet moderately, so that the strength of the heart may be retained, lest if he reduces the blood too much there, he may reduce it in the heart. If, however, anyone suffers in the heart, before reducing the blood in the vein, let him remove the blood in the median of the right arm, and he will be better in the heart.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si quis vero in epate et in splene dolet, aut si quis in collo et in gutture quasi con dusionem spiraminis sentit, aut qui caHginem in oculis patitur, in epatica sanguinem minuat, et melius habebit. Quod si in epatica aut in cephalica satis de sanguine absCAUSAE ET LIB. H. tulexit, micus inde dolebit, quam si multum sanguinis in mediana auferat. Et tam in hieme quam in aestate in utroque bracbio in praedictis venis homo licenter miiluere poterit secundum qualitatem infirmitatum suarum.
text_translation: If indeed someone suffers pain in the liver and in the spleen, or if someone feels as though there is a constriction of breathing in the neck and throat, or if someone suffers blindness in the eyes, let him reduce the blood of the liver, and he will be better. For if sufficient blood has been taken away from the liver or from the head without cause, he will suffer more than if much blood is removed from the middle. And both in winter and in summer, a man may freely draw blood from both arms in the aforementioned veins according to the quality of his own infirmities.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et in his tribus venis, scilicet in cephalica, in mediana et in epatica praedpue minutio sanguinis fiat, quia ipsae venae ▼elut caput et fundamentum aliarum venarum sunt, et quoniam omnes ceterae venulae ad ipsas tendunt et ipsis aonezae ^unt; et ideo ceterae venulae raro incidantur, quoniam eas incidi non oportet, nisi pro magna necessi- lo tate, ne forte ariditatem incurrant, quoniam si qua istaTum principalium inciditur et sangnine evacuatur, tunc et veoiulae, quae ad illas annezae sunt, aliquantum de malis humoribus, qui in ipsis sunt, minuuntur.
text_translation: And in these three veins, namely in the cephalica, in the mediana, and in the epatica, the diminution of blood especially occurs, because these veins are like the head and foundation of other veins, and because all other venulae tend toward them and are annexed to them; and therefore the other venulae are rarely incised, because it is not fitting to incise them, unless for great necessity, lest they incur dryness, because if any of these principal ones is incised and the blood is evacuated, then the venulae which are annexed to them are somewhat reduced in the bad humors which are in them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si vero aliqua eamndem venularum inciditur, principales venae et aliae .venuke ei adhaerentes non multum ob hoc minorationem sanguinis sui in se sentiunt. Nam si de magno flumine multi rivuli effiuunt, et si tunc aliunde per voluntatem hominum aliqua vena ab eodem flumine educitur, onmes rivuli, qui ab eo fluunt, minorationem aquarum eis sentiunt. Si autem de aliquo rivulo ei adhaerente aliqua vena aquae edudtur, fluvius ille et alii rivuli egredientes minorationem aquarum suarum ob hoc non multum sentiunt, sed tantum rivulus ille, de quo eadem vena educta est, in se ipso minoratur et arescit.
text_translation: If indeed some of the same veins are affected, the main veins and others adhering to them do not feel much reduction of their own blood within themselves. For if from a great river many rivulets flow out, and if then from elsewhere, by the will of humans, a vein is drawn from the same river, all the rivulets flowing from it feel a reduction of their waters. If, however, from a rivulet adhering to it, a vein of water is drawn, that river and other flowing rivulets do not feel much reduction of their own waters, but only that rivulet from which the same vein is drawn becomes smaller and dries up within itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et in- cisio praedictarum principalium venarum in inflexione brachiorum fiat, quia cum ibidem vena inciditur, humores magis ibi coadunantur quam in loco illo, ubi nulla inflexio est. Quod si venula aliqua aut super artum aut super pedem aut in pollice aut aliunde in corpore hominis pro aliqua infirmitate inciditur, praeter inflerionem brachiorum, prodesse quidem aliquantum illi poterit, sed nulla incisio tantum valet quantum illa, quae in inflexione brachiorum fit in praedictis tribus principalibus venis.
text_translation: But also, an incision should be made in the aforementioned principal veins during the flexion of the arms, because when the vein is cut there, the humors gather more there than in that place where there is no flexion. If, however, any small vein is cut either over a joint or over the foot or in the thumb or elsewhere in the human body for some infirmity, apart from the flexion of the arms, it may indeed help somewhat, but no incision is as effective as that one made in the flexion of the arms in the aforementioned three principal veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Homo autem sive masculns sive femina sit, dum in iuventute sua et in longitudine et in grossitudine naturaliter crescit, venam ad minuendum sanguinem interim non incidat, quamvis HILDEGARDI necessitatem habere yideatnr, qoia, dum venae et saBguis in homine natoraliter crescunt, si quis venas illas incidit et sanguinem emittit, homin^n illum dehilem in corpore et in moribus ac in sensu suo aliquantum velut vacuiun facit. Sed si necesse habuerit, cocturas interim fadat et per scarifieationem sanguinem emittat, quod tabes in Ban» guine iuvenum magis habundat qnam in sanguine senum.
text_translation: The human being, whether male or female, while in his or her youth and in length and thickness naturally grows, let the vein for diminishing blood not be incised, although it may seem necessary to Hildegard, because, when veins and blood in a human being naturally grow, if someone incises those veins and emits blood, that human being becomes pale in the body and in manners and in sense somewhat as if a void is made. But if it has been necessary, let coagulations be made in the meantime and let blood be emitted through scarification, which decay is more abundant in the blood of youths than in the blood of elders.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postquam autem homo tempus aetatis iUius transieiit, quod iam amplius in corpore naturaliter non crescit, vehit post viginti annos est, si tunc propter infirmitatem necesse habuerit, venas incidat, sed tamen moderate sanguinem emittat. Si vero sanus in corpore est, venas non incidat, sed scarificationem habeat et cocturas faciat, quoniam venae et sanguis eius nondum ad fortitudinem suam pervene i5runt. Gum autem ad perfectam aetatem scilicet triginta annorum pervenerit, tunc, sive infirmus sive sanus sit, san guinem in venis licenter minuat, quia venae et sangnis ipsius ad perfectionem suam iam pervenerint, et sanitatem corporis sui retinebit.
text_translation: After the man has passed the time of his age, which no longer naturally grows in the body, when he has reached twenty years, if at that time he must, because of weakness, incise the veins, yet let him emit blood moderately. If, however, he is healthy in the body, let him not incise the veins, but have scarification and make cocturas, because the veins and his blood have not yet reached their strength. When, however, he has reached the perfect age, namely thirty years, then, whether he is weak or healthy, let him freely reduce the blood in the veins, because the veins and his blood have already reached their perfection, and he will retain the health of his body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De cruoris differentia. Homo autem, cuius sanguis de vena emissus turbidum eolorem habet , ut halitus hominis est, et qui inter colorem illum nigras maculas hs^t et in circuitu suo, id est in ambitu, quasi cerosus est, cito morietur, nisi deus restituat enm ad vitam. Nam turbidus color in sanguine ostendit, quod humores in frigiditate ad mortem moti sunt, nigri autem a^aimum in sangnine melancoliam ad mortem motam esse demonstrant, circuitns vero in sanguine cerosus fel etiam ad mortem motum esse denuntiat.
