text_translation: On the difference of blood. But a man whose blood, when emitted from the vein, has a turbid odor, like the breath of a man, and which, among that color, has black spots and in its circuit, that is, in its surrounding, is waxy, will quickly die, unless God restores him to life. For the turbid color in the blood shows that the humors, moved by coldness, are directed toward death; the black ones, however, in the blood demonstrate that melancholy is moved toward death; the waxy, indeed, in the blood announces that gall is also moved toward death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si color sangninis hominis turbidns est et cerosus absque nigris macnlis, mortem evadere potest, sed tamen mnltum infirmatnr, qnia quamvis hnmores in frigiditate ad mortem moti snnt, nigri tamen stramun melancoliae nondum moti snnt. Et ideo ille mortem evadet Si autem niger est et turbidns absque ceroso colore, tnnc homo ille in magna desperatione est, ita ut non liberetnr, nisi deul liberet eum; sed tamen evadet mortem, quoniam quamvis melancolia et humores ad mortem moti dnnt.
text_translation: If the color of a man's blood is turbid and waxy without black spots, he may escape death, but nonetheless is greatly weakened, because although the humors are moved toward death by coldness, the black stramun of melancholia is still unmoved. And therefore he will escape death. But if it is black and turbid without a waxy color, then that man is in great desperation, so that he will not be freed unless God frees him; yet he will escape death, because although melancholia and the humors are moved toward death, they are not.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: fel tameii in loco suo absque motione manet; et idcirco idem homo non morietar. Sed si isti colores in incisione venamm simnl apparent, pericnlosom est, et homo ille non potest evadere mortem, nisi deus soscitet enm, quia et humores et melanoolia et fel ad mortem pariter moti snnt. Si antem colores isti separati sunt, ita quod duobus juxas color istorum deeet, mortem evadere potest, quamvis multom doleat. Et haec est praeecientia in colore saagQiniB hominum, qui aut scinnt aut nesciunt se adhuc infirmari.
text_translation: The bile remains in its place without movement; and therefore the same man will not die. But if these colors appear together in the incision of the veins, it is dangerous, and that man cannot escape death unless God helps him, because the humors and melancholy and bile are all moved toward death together. But if these colors are separated, so that each of these colors is beside the other, he can escape death, although he may suffer much. And this is the premonition in the color of the blood of people, who either know or do not know that they are still becoming weak.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t exspiratio melancoHae turbida est, et ipsa lo etiam habet yires Mlis, ita quod cerosa est; sic est melan colia. Homo autem, qui in vena sanguinem minuit, se per triduum a radiorum solis claritate et a claritate flammantis ignis observet, qaia in hoc triduo illa claritate visa sangois in homine concutitar et contremiscit atque de- feotum coidi saepe infert. De minuUonis diaeta, Glaritas vero diei temperata est et absque multum radiante sole exsistens minuto non obest.
text_translation: And the exhalation of melancholia is turbid, and it itself also has the vital forces of the bile, so that it is waxy; thus is melancholia. But the man who reduces blood in the vein, let him observe himself for three days from the brightness of the sun's rays and from the brightness of a flaming fire, since in this three-day period the blood, seen with that brightness, shakes and trembles in the man and often brings about a defect in the heart. Concerning the diet of reduction, indeed the brightness of the day is tempered and existing without the sun shining much, it does not harm in moderation.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et omni tempore et praecipue in sanguinis minutione ab ardore solis et ab ardore ignis sanguis, qui 2o circa ocoios hominis est, fervet et pellicula, scilicet membrana, quae oculos continet, exspissatur et sic caliginem ▼isai inducit. Minutas autem sanguine in vena diversos cibos et assos et eos, qui diversum succum habent, et eroda pcHna et cruda olera non comedat, quia haec in ▼enis suis tanc livorem potius quam sanguinem augerent. Et forte vinum non bibat, quoniam sanguinem eius concuteret et eum fadle amentem faceret; sed competentem cibom et onom ferculum aut duo comedat, ita quod sibi boneste sufficiat, et suave et purum vinum bibat.
text_translation: But also at all times and especially in the diminution of blood from the heat of the sun and from the heat of fire, the blood which is near the eyes of man becomes hot and the pellicula, that is, the membrane which contains the eyes, thickens and thus causes darkness in vision. However, let him not eat in the veins diverse foods and roasted ones and those which have diverse juices, and let him not eat eroda pcHna and raw vegetables, because these would increase livor in their veins rather than blood. And perhaps let him not drink wine, because it would agitate his blood and make him easily foolish; but let him eat suitable food and good food, either one or two meals, so that he may be adequately nourished, and let him drink sweet and pure wine.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc per duos dies fadat, quia sanguis eius attenuatns adhuc in motione est. In tertia vero die sanguis vires suas recipit et ad loca sua difonditur. Gaseum antem minutus devitet, quoniam tanc livorem sanguini subministrat et reotom ac purum sanguinem non parat, sed cum infirmi- c tate eum unpingnat. Nunc autem, qui multum sanguinem habet et venas sangaine plenas, si sanguinem non purgat per minutionem incisionis in vena ant searificationiSy sangais eius aliqnantom cerosus erit et infirmns, et sic homo ille infirmitatem incnrrit.
text_translation: And this occurs over two days, because his blood is still attenuated and in motion. On the third day, however, the blood receives its strength and spreads to its proper places. The drops, however, avoid, because they then provide blackness to the blood and do not produce rotten or pure blood, but strike it with weakness. Now, however, he who has much blood and full blood vessels, if he does not purge the blood through reduction of incision in the vein before coagulation, his blood will be somewhat waxen and weak, and thus that man incurs weakness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quando minuatur. Cnm ergo aliqnis bomo venam ad minutionem sangoinis incidere volnerit, hoc ieianns faciat, qnia cnm homo ieiunns est, humores, qui in ipso exsistunt, a sanguine aliquantulum separati sunt, et sanguis in homine tunc recto et bene temperato modo fluit, velnt rivulus, qui in loco suo absque ulla commotione ventonun aut aeris recte et ordinate manat. Sed postquam homo cibum acceperit, sanguis in eo aliquantulum fortius inundare incipit, et ita humores se illi aliquantum intermiscent, nec tunc facile ab invicem separari possunt.
text_translation: When it is diminished. When therefore some man wishes to wound a vein for the diminution of blood, this should be done when fasting, because when a man is fasting, the humors which exist in him are somewhat separated from the blood, and the blood in man then flows straight and in a well-tempered manner, like a rivulet which in its place flows without any motion of winds or air rightly and orderly. But after a man has received food, the blood in him begins somewhat more strongly to inundate, and thus the humors mingle somewhat with it, and then they cannot easily be separated from one another.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ideo, cum homo ieiunus est, venam incidat, ut humores a sanguine separati tanto facilius effluant, excepto aliquo homine in multa debilitate et inflrmitate exsistente, qui modicum cibum capiat, antequam venam incidat, ne deficiat. Be scarificatione. Qui scarificari vult, hoc ieiunio fiat, quia tunc tabes a sanguine separata efQuit. Nam postquam homo prandit, sanguis tabi intermiscetur, et si tunc homo scarificari voluerit, sanguis cum tabe effluit.
text_translation: And therefore, when a man is fasting, let the vein be cut, so that the humors separated from the blood may flow out more easily, except for some man existing in great weakness and infirmity, who should take a little food before the vein is cut, lest he faint. On scarification. He who wishes to be scarified, let it be done with this fasting, because then the phlegm separated from the blood flows out. For after a man eats, blood and phlegm are mixed, and if at that time a man wishes to be scarified, blood with phlegm flows out.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ne autem homo in corde deficiat, modieum panis et vini sumat, antequam scarificationem accipiat. Omni tempore scarificatio bona et utilis est, ut noxii humores et livores, qui in homiDe sunt, minuantur. Nam inter cutem et camem livores maxime habundant, et hi praecipue laedunt homines. Scarificatio autem magis iuvenibus quam senibus congmit, quoniam iuvenes in humoribus plus habundant quam senes. Et scarificatio magis in aestate quam in hieme eonvenit, quia homines in aestate magis novos cibos et novos et virides succos comedunt quam in hieme, unde -: novos etiam livores attrahuni Qui vero moUes et pingnes carnes habent, in uno mense per scarificationem bis sanguinem extrahant.
text_translation: Let the man not fail in the heart, but take a little bread and wine before receiving the scarification. At all times, scarification is good and useful, so that the harmful humors and livors, which are in men, may be diminished. For between the skin and the camem livors abound most, and these especially injure men. Scarification, however, pertains more to young men than to the elderly, because young men have more humors than the elderly. And scarification is more suitable in summer than in winter, because men eat more new foods and new green juices in summer than in winter, whence they also attract new livors. Those, however, who have soft and fat flesh, by scarification should draw blood twice in one month.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui vero macres sunt, hoc in quolibet mense semel faciant, si opus fuerit. Et qui in oculis aut in^ auribus seu in toto capite dolet, comu vel ventosam in confinio colli et dorsi ponat. Qni vero in pectore dolet, cornn in scapulis figat, aut si in lateribns dolet, id in utroque brachio et ubi manus finitur, ponat, vel si in cruribus dolet, illud ad ilia figat, aut si in iliis torquetur, id inter nates et poplitem ponat, id est in coxas. In locum antem, in quem ventosa vel comu ponitur, non plus quam ter vel quater una eadem hora, qua sanguis ibi extrahitur, ponatur.
text_translation: Those who are indeed macres, let them do this once in each month, if needed. And those who have pain in the eyes or in the ears or in the entire head, let them place a comu or ventosam at the junction of the neck and back. Those who have pain in the chest, let them place a comu in the scapulas, or if pain is in the sides, let them place it in both arms and where the hand ends, or if pain is in the legs, let them place it towards the hips, or if twisted in the hips, let them place it between the buttocks and the knee, that is, in the thighs. As for the place where the ventosa or comu is placed, it should not be placed more than three or four times in the same hour, by which the blood is drawn there.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In suris vero aut in tibiis scarificatio non fiat aat raro fiat, quia in eis sanguis plus habundat quam buniores, nisi tamen propter magnam necessitatem humorum fiat; nec humores illuc trahantur, quoniam totum corpus per crura sustentatur. Qui autem modo per scaiificationem humores et sanguinem minuunt, non oportet, ut se a olaritate solis aut ignis aut cibis tam caute observent sicut deberent, si venam incidissent; sed cibis reficiantur, ut l^ prius solebant, quoniam per scarificationem fistulae illae, id est venae, quae vitam hominis in se portant et quae compaginem membrorum compingunt, incisae non sunt, qnia cum una huiusmodi fistula inciditur, ceterae omnes incisionem illam sentiunt et ei compatiuntur.
text_translation: In the thighs indeed or in the shins, scarification should not be made, or if made, rarely, because in them blood abounds more than humors, unless indeed it is made for the great necessity of humors; nor should humors be drawn there, because the whole body is sustained through the legs. Those however who at present by scarification reduce humors and blood, it is not necessary that they observe themselves carefully from the heat of the sun or fire or foods as they should if they had incised a vein; but they should be nourished with foods as they used to be, because through scarification those fistulas, that is, the veins, which carry the life of man within them and which join the connection of the members, are not incised, because when one of these fistulas is incised, all the others feel that incision and sympathize with it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem 2o equo aut bovi aut asino vena incidenda est in minutione sanguinis, et si idem animal forte et corpulentum est, tunc quantum picarius capere potest aquae sanguis ei minuatur. De minutione hrtUi. Si vero equus aut bos sive asinus debilis et macer fuerit, ad dimidium picarium sanguis ei minuatur, scilicet secundum propriam crassitudinem et tenuitatem virtutis suae. Sed post minutionem molle pabulum et aridum ac suave fenum ei ad comedendum dabi« tur.
text_translation: If, however, a vein is to be incised in the bloodletting of a horse, ox, or ass, and if the same animal is strong and corpulent, then the blood is reduced by as much as the animal can take in a piculum of water. Concerning the reduction in man. If, however, the horse, ox, or ass is weak and emaciated, then the blood is reduced by half a piculum, namely according to its own thickness and thinness of its virtue. But after the bloodletting, soft fodder and dry, sweet hay will be given to it to eat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc etiam per duas septimanas aut per unam aut per quatuor dies post minutionem quiescat , quatinus vires suas recipiat, quod semper in labore est. Et tribus mensibus transactis in quarto mense eidem animali sanguis in vena iterum minuatur et non prius, nisi pro magna necesffltate infirmitatis, quod tam mali humores in huiusmodi animalibus non habundant sicut in homiae. Ovi autem sanguis in vena saepe minuatur, sed tamen modicum sanguinis, quod propter diversam auram facile infir matar.
text_translation: And then also for two weeks or for one or for four days after reduction, let it rest, in order that it may receive its strength, which is always in labor. And after three months have passed, in the fourth month, the same animal again has blood reduced in the vein, but not before, unless for great necessity of infirmity, because in such animals bad humors do not abound as in humans. However, in the sheep, blood in the vein is often reduced, but still only a little blood, which because of a different air is easily made weak.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in humida et in bene temperata aara ovibus vena incidenda est, quoniam mali humores in eis tunc crescnnt; in sicca autem aura incisio ista vitanda est, quoniam humores in eis tunc minuuntur, De cocturis. Ustio autem, scilicet ooctura, omni tempore bcma et utilis est, quod si discrete fit, intercutaneos humores et livores minuit et sanitatem corporibus afiPert. Et tam iuvenibus quam senibus congma est, iuvenibus scilicet, quod cum caro et sanguis eorum per iuventutem crescunt, tnnc etiam mali humores in eis augentor; senibas autem, quoniam cum caro et sanguis cum senectute in eis minuuntur, tunc inter cutem et camem eorom livores remanent.
text_translation: For in humid and well-tempered air, the vein of the sheep should be incised, because the bad humors in them then grow; but in dry air, this incision should be avoided, because the humors in them then decrease. On cooking. Roasting, that is cooking, is at all times well and useful, because if done separately, it reduces the intercutaneous humors and discolorations and brings health to the bodies. And it is congenial to both young and old: to the young, because as their flesh and blood grow through youth, then also the bad humors in them increase; to the old, because as their flesh and blood decrease with old age, then between the skin and flesh of them discolorations remain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed senibus aliquantum salubrior est quam iuvenibus, quod came et sanguine eorum labentibus et cute contracta tabes inter cutem et carnem tanto magis discurrit. luvenibus vero ita salnbris non est ut senibus, quia, ut caro in eis augetur et dum sanguis in eis fervet et dum cutis eomm tenuis et extensa est, tunc multotiens sanguis, qui eis sanitatem et fortitudinem confert, com noxiis humoribus per ustionem cocturae effiuit. Sed tamen coctnra iuvenibus salubrior est in hieme quam in aestate, quia cum ipsi et aestas simul fervent, cavere debent, ne in aestate sanguinem per cocturam cum livore endttant.
text_translation: But to the elderly it is somewhat more healthy than to the young, because as their flesh and blood flow and their skin is contracted, the decay between the skin and flesh spreads all the more. To the young, however, it is not so healthy as to the elderly, because as their flesh increases and while their blood boils and while their skin is thin and stretched, then often the blood, which confers upon them health and strength, flows out with noxious humors through the burning of cooking. But nevertheless cooking is more healthy for the young in winter than in summer, because when they and summer together boil, they must beware lest in summer the blood through cooking with lividity endure.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ideo in hieme cocturam faciant, quia hiems humida et Mgida est et ipsi oalidi, et sic facilius sanguinem in se retinent et humores emittunt. Senibus autem coctura praecipue congma est in aestate, quoniam cum ipsi iam frigescunt, aestas calida est, et ita humores in eis per calorem aestatis excitati in coctura effiount, quia calorem, quem ad expellendum livores in se non habent, iUum per aestatem habent. Qui vero cooturam fftdunt, cutem tantum perfringant, ne forte carne profundius perforata sanitas hominis cum sanguine magis effluat quam tabes et quam noxii humores.
text_translation: And therefore, in winter they should make a broth, because winter is humid and rigid, and they themselves are hot, and thus they more easily retain blood within themselves and emit humors. For the elderly, however, the broth is especially suitable in summer, because when they are already growing cold, summer is warm, and thus the humors within them, excited by the heat of summer, are poured out in the broth, because they have the heat of summer, which they lack in themselves to expel the lividness. Those who indeed make the broth, let them only break the skin, so that the health of the human, along with blood, may flow more than decay and harmful humors.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui autem perfectae aetatis S sunt et pingues in corpore, cocturam tantum per circulum unius anni habeant et postea per dimidium annum illa careant, et tunc itemm, si voluerint, eam in alio looo oor- ' poris sui ponant. Sed qui macilenti sunt, cocturani per dimidium annum teneant et ea allata dimidio anno transacto itemm, si voluerint, cocturam faciant, sed tamen in alio loco corporis sui, quia, si coctura in uno eodemque loco corporis saepius et diutius fit, caro ibi multam saniem colligit et sic debilitatem incurrit et indurescit, vel si dintius et indiscrete in ullo homine tenetur, tunc malis hmnoribus et livoribus deficientibus sanitas et sanguis bominis eicitur.
text_translation: Whoever, however, are of perfect age and fat in body, let them have coction only for the course of one year, and afterwards for half a year let them lack it, and then again, if they wish, let them place it in another place of their pores. But those who are emaciated, let them maintain coction for half a year, and after half a year has passed, again, if they wish, let them perform coction, but nevertheless in another part of their body, because, if coction is made often and for a long time in one and the same place of the body, flesh there collects much phlegm, and thus weakness is incurred and hardening occurs, or if it is held more widely and without discretion in any man, then health and blood of the man are expelled by bad humors and blackness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Adulescentibus autem coctura per decem hebdomadas sufficiat. Cum autem coctura facienda est, lo axLt isca aut meduUa fasarii aut nodo de lineo panno facto intendatur, non autem ferro, quia illud multos livores indiscrete attrabit, nec sulpbure, quoniam et camem putrefacit et foetentem reddet, nec etiam thure, quia illud flammas emittit et cutem arescere facit. Nam isca et meduUa fusarii et lineus pannus leniorem ignem habent quam alia incendibilia et cutem tantum perstringunt et camem non perforant, quia ubi cutis tantum vulneratur, ibi humores emanant et non sanitas hominis; ubi autem caro cum cute per ignem perforatur, sanitas hominis cum tabe egreditur.
text_translation: For adolescents, cooking for ten weeks is sufficient. When cooking is to be done, the armpit or marrow of a fowler or a node made of linen cloth should be used, not iron, because iron will cause many bruises indiscriminately, nor sulfur, because it both causes putrefaction and makes it smelly, nor also thyme, because it emits flames and makes the skin dry. For the armpit and marrow of the fowler and linen cloth have a gentler fire than other combustibles and only tighten the skin and do not perforate the flesh, because where the skin is only wounded, there the humors emanate and not the health of man; where, however, the flesh with the skin is pierced by fire, the health of man exits with decay.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si eam diutius quilibet voluerit babere et panno circumligare, tunc meduUa de corylo sumpta et modica stuppa de lino circumposita imponat. Quod si eam absque ligamine et parvo tempore vult babere, tunc stuppam de lino aut crines de lepore imponat. Homo autem, si necesse fuerit, a duodecimo anno aetatis suae cocturam facere potest usque ad sexagesimum annum aetatis suae ; et postea illam non habeat, quia deinde magis eum laedit, quam ei sanitatem conferat^ nisi magna necessitas infirmitatis eum illam habere cogat.
text_translation: But if anyone wishes to have it longer and wrap it with a cloth, then apply marrow taken from the hazel and a small amount of flax pulp placed around it. If, however, he wishes to have it without a bandage and for a short time, then apply flax pulp or hare's hair. But a man, if necessary, can make a decoction from the twelfth year of his age up to the sixtieth year of his age; and after that, he should not have it, because afterward it harms him more than it confers health, unless great necessity of illness compels him to have it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui autem in oculis aut in auribus aut in toto capite dolet, retro aures modice uratur nullo ligamine ibi utens, et qui in dorso dolet, inter scapulas modice uratur aut in brachiis, ubi ligamen habere possit, coquatur; et qui in iliis dolet, in fine ossis illius et dorsi uratur, et qui multos humores in toto corpore habet, inter tibiam et suram ante in crare coquatur et ibi ligamen habeat. Et sicut ille, qui sanHildegardis cansae et cnrae ed. Eaiser.
text_translation: Whoever suffers in the eyes or in the ears or in the whole head, let him be gently warmed behind the ears, using no ligature there; and whoever suffers in the back, let him be gently warmed between the scapulae or in the arms, where a ligature may be used, and let him be wrapped; and whoever suffers in the hips, let him be warmed at the end of that bone and the back; and whoever has many humors in the whole body, let him be wrapped between the shin and the calf before the ankle, and let him have a ligature there. And as he, who is healed by Hildegardis Causae et Curae ed. Eaiser.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: ^ guinem ad venam minait, interdnm cessat, ita et iste faciat, qui cocturam parat. Et cnm aliqaantiim tempos in die cessaverit, itemm se coqnat. Qui autem coctoram faciiy pannum de canabo sumat et eum vel ter vel quater in oera intingat et super corticem de ulice ponat, quia cum cera super cocturam fortiter iacet, et cocturae superponat, ita quod omni parte pannus ille corticem excedat. Et sic pannus ille foetorem cocturae continet, ne procedat.
text_translation: He who reduces [something] to the vein, in the meantime ceases, so also he who prepares the cooking should do. And when he has ceased for some time during the day, again he should cook. He who prepares the cooking, take a cloth from canae and immerse it either three or four times in air and place it over the bark of the oak, because when wax is strongly placed over the cooking, and the cooking is placed over it, so that the cloth extends beyond the bark on all sides. And thus that cloth contains the smell of the cooking, so that it does not spread.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quanto plus foetor in coctura continetur, ne multum extendatur, tanto plus livor extrahitur et non sanguis. Si autem foetor eocturae extenditur, ita quod non continetur, tunc tanto plus sanguinis de coctura egreditur et tanto plus livor interius manet. Et cortex de cupro super cocturam non valet, qnia succus cupri contrarius est carni hominis. Sed et cnm panniolus, qui super cocturam ponitur, iam plenus est livoris, ita quod panoiolus ille de livore concaluit, tunc homo panniolum illum de coctura auferat et alium superponat, quod si tunc super cocturam diutius iacuerit, humores, qui tunc ibi attracti sunt, arescunt.
text_translation: Nam quanto plus foetor in coctura continetur, ne multum extendatur, tanto plus livor extrahitur et non sanguis. Si autem foetor cocturae extenditur, ita quod non continetur, tunc tanto plus sanguinis de coctura egreditur et tanto plus livor interius manet. Et cortex de cupro super cocturam non valet, quia succus cupri contrarius est carni hominis. Sed et cum panniolus, qui super cocturam ponitur, iam plenus est livoris, ita quod panniolus ille de livore concaluit, tunc homo panniolum illum de coctura auferat et alium superponat, quod si tunc super cocturam diutius iacuerit, humores, qui tunc ibi attracti sunt, arescunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem panniolum illum abstulerit, antequam livore incaluerit, eum laedit, quia livor, qui se illuc congregaverat , in camem redit. De excreatione. Sed quemadmodum terra semper humida est et ut multa foetida et ut multa inutilia de illa exsudant, sic etiam homo in carne sua humidns ea, quae foetida et putrida in ipso sunt, per excreationem de se eicit, quod de limo terrae est. Et si terra humida non esset, fertilis in fractibus non exsisteret sed arida, ita quod homo, si humiditatem in se non haberet, inflexibilis et infructuosus ad quaeque opera foret.
text_translation: If, however, the panniolum should be removed before the livor has become hot, it harms it, because the livor, which had gathered there, returns to the camem. On excretion. But just as the earth is always moist and as many things fetid and as many useless things exude from it, so also man in his flesh is moist, and those things which are fetid and putrid within him, he ejects from himself through excretion, which is from the mud of the earth. And if the earth were not moist, fertile in cracks would not exist but would be arid, so that man, if he did not have moisture within himself, would be inflexible and unfruitful for all works.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De animae igne, Anima autem hominis ignea est et quatuor elementa ad se trahit et in hoc etiam facit hominem circuire visu et auditu ac similibus actibus eius. Et anima est quaedam vis in homine, ut ignis in aqua, quod homo sine anima vivere non posset, ut etiam aqua S absque igne fluere non valeret, si eum in se non sentiret. De saliva, Sed anima vim unam, scilicet salivam, in homine de aqua sumit, cum aqua rationalitatem ad loquen dxim aspargit et praeparat, ut cbordae per ceram seu per bitamen iuvantur, quatinus pulchrum sonum habeant.
text_translation: Of the fire of the soul. The soul, however, of man is fiery and draws the four elements to itself, and in this it also causes man to move through vision and hearing and similar acts of it. And the soul is a certain power in man, like fire in water, which man could not live without a soul, just as water also could not flow without fire, if it did not feel it within itself. Of saliva. But the soul takes a single power, namely saliva, in man from water, when water sprinkles and prepares rationality for speaking, so that the strings are aided by wax or by pitch, in order that they may have a pleasant sound.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Saliva aut^n munda et pura esset, nisi quod anima ignea est; et sic saliva de igne animae velut spuma est, quemadmodum aqua de igne et de sole spumam eicit, scilicet de calore eorum. Et quoniam anima ignea est, idcirco etiam ei inest, ut aquosa sit, quoniam fenestrae animae oculi sunt, q\ii ignem et aquam in se babent. Sed et omnis bumiditas, quae in bomine est, aquosa est et ad iter rationalitatis tendit, ut ipsa rationalitas in bomine sonare possit.
text_translation: Saliva would be pure and clean, except that the soul is fiery; and thus saliva is like foam from the fire of the soul, just as water emits foam from fire and from the sun, namely from their heat. And because the soul is fiery, therefore it also has the property to be watery, because the openings of the soul's eyes are, which contain fire and water within themselves. But also every humidity that is in the body is watery and tends toward the journey of rationality, so that rationality itself may be able to sound in the body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam anima de cerebro et de visceribus aquam in salivam educit, ut bomo loqui possit, quia homo nec sonare nec verbum creare posset, si humorem in se non haberet, sed aridus esset; et ideo saliva velut bonum unguentum est, quod ut unguentum sanitatem advocat, ita et saliva visum et auditum ac odoratum et vocem et verba atque omnia utilia sanitatis in homine tenet et producit, De siomachi frigiditate. Qui autem frigidum stomacbum habet, ille plurimam aquam salivae eicit, quod calidus non est, et ideo etiam debilis est; qui vero calidum habet stomacbum, modicam aquam salivae eicit, quod interius aliquantum aridus est, et ideo graves febres facile incurrit.
text_translation: For the soul draws water from the brain and the viscera into saliva, so that man may speak, because man could neither sound nor create a word, if he did not have humor within himself, but were dry; and therefore saliva is as a good ointment, which, as an ointment calls forth health, so saliva holds and produces sight and hearing and smelling and voice and words and all useful things of health in man, On the coldness of the stomach. He who has a cold stomach, he ejects much water of saliva, because he is not warm, and therefore he is also weak; he who indeed has a warm stomach, ejects a moderate amount of saliva water, because within he is somewhat dry, and therefore easily incurs severe fevers.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De carne, Unaquaeque autem caro viridis est et de viriditate illa livorem babet. Sic etiam in camibus pecorum ostenditur, quae occiduntur, quoniam, si illae suspenduntur, guttae livoris effluunt. Homo autem, qui macras et tenues cames babet, livorem de se facilius exsudando emittit quam ille, qui pinguibus carnibus oneratus est, quoniam ille, qui macras et tenues cames in corpore suo habet, est ut caseus plurimis foraminibus perforatus, qui non est fortiter compressus.
text_translation: Of flesh, indeed each flesh is green and from that greenness it has livor. Thus also in the flesh of cattle that are slaughtered, because, if they are hung, drops of livor flow out. But a man who has thin and lean flesh more easily exudes livor from himself than he who is burdened with fatty flesh, because he who has thin and lean flesh in his body is like cheese pierced with many holes, which is not strongly compressed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et aer ac cetera eleraenta eum facile pertranseunt. Unde etiam facile bumores in se habet et plurimum excreat, quia tenues carnes habet. Tunc etiam calor et gustus ciborum et potuum de stomacho eius sur- gunt et ad iecur et ad cor et ad pulmonem ut venenum fumigant et ascendunt; sed calor iecoris et cordis ac pul9 monis buDC livorem pati non potest, sed eum a se ad pectns et ad guttur expellit, ut cibus, qui spumam de se emanare facit, cum ad ignem coquitur.
text_translation: And air and the other elements easily pass through him. Hence also easily has humors within itself and excretes much, because it has thin flesh. Then also heat and the taste of food and drink from his stomach rise and go to the liver and to the heart and to the lungs as if they were a venom, smoking and ascending; but the heat of the liver and the heart and the lungs cannot endure the bile, but expels it from itself to the chest and to the throat, as food which causes foam to emanate from itself when it is cooked by fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: JDe emunctione. Qui frigidum et infirmum stomachxun et infirma viscera habent, hi de infirmitate illa frigidnm et humidum fumum ad cerebrum suum emittunt, et ille fit ut coctum venenum, et sic a naribus et ab ore emittitur, ut etiam stellae in aere purgantur, ut etiam terra quaedam sordida et foetida de se emittit. De cerebri et salivae et emunctionis purgatione, Nam cerebrum fenestras habet, quae semper ventosae sunt et de quibus semper mollificatur et humidum fit, quae sunt oculi, aures et nares et os hominis, ita quod frigida et humida foeditas humorum ibi in exeuntes vias naiium et gutturis congregatur, quoniam cerebrum eam pati non potest, sed eam ad purgationem hominis emittit et pulsu aeris educitur.
text_translation: On Emunction. Those who have a cold and weak stomach and weak viscera, these emit from that weakness a cold and humid smoke to their brain, and it becomes a cooked poison, and thus it is emitted through the nostrils and the mouth, just as even the stars purify the air, and even some dirty and fetid earth emits from itself. On the purification of the brain, saliva, and emunction. For the brain has windows, which are always ventilated and through which it is always softened and made moist, which are the eyes, ears, and nostrils and the mouth of man, so that the cold and moist filth of humors gathers there in the outgoing channels of the naiium and throat, because the brain cannot endure it, but it emits it for the purification of man and is drawn out by the pulse of air.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si homini purgatio ista prohibetur aliquo modo, homo insaniret et aresceret, quia stomachus periret et cerebrum putresceret, qnoniam foetorem hxmc pati non possent, sicut nec mare sordida et immunda suffert, quin ea de se eiciat. Qui autem constrictas cames habent, hi tam duri in camibus suis sunt, ut caseus, qui tam fortiter compressus est, quod liquor ex eo fluere non potest, et ideo in carnibus eorum viror livoris remanet et ita in eis indurescit, quia hanc foeditatem excreare non possunt.
text_translation: If this purgation is prohibited in some way to a human, the human would go mad and wither away, because the stomach would perish and the brain would rot, since they could not endure the stench of the humors, just as the sea does not tolerate filth and uncleanness, but expels it from itself. Those who have constricted flesh are so hard in their flesh, like cheese, which is so tightly compressed that the liquid cannot flow from it, and therefore the vigor of the liver remains in their flesh and thus they harden in them, because they cannot excrete this filth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed humiditas ciborum et potuum et alii humoras tanto debiliores in eis sunt, quia exterius adiutorium livoris, qui in came est, non habent, quoniam prae constrictione et duritia camis istorum effluere non possunt. Et hi infirmi sunt interius in corpore et exterius in carae ulceribus, quia sordes livoris in eis remanent, nec eos excreare possunt. Ac ideo tam in pectore quam in venis et reliquo corpore multum dolent. Qui vero pingues carnes in corporibus suis habent, diversis humoribus habundant et facile tussi tant, sed modice excreant, quod aer ac cetera elementa in eos difficulter intrantes prae pinguedine et spissitudine camis facile exire non possunt nec humores ad purgatioCAUSAE ET LIB.
text_translation: But the humidity of foods and drinks and other humors are so much weaker in them, because they do not have external aid from the liver, which is in the flesh, because due to the constriction and hardness of the flesh of these, they cannot efflux. And these are weak internally in the body and externally in the skin's ulcers, because the filth of the liver remains in them, and they cannot excrete them. And therefore, both in the chest and in the veins and the rest of the body, they greatly suffer. Those who indeed have fatty flesh in their bodies abound in diverse humors and easily cough, but excrete moderately, because the air and other elements entering them difficultly, due to the fatness and thickness of the flesh, cannot easily exit nor humors to purgationCAUSAE ET LIB.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: U. riem excreationis movere. Qui enim in flegmate superliabundant et flegma non eiciunt, inflrmis et debilibus carnibus se onerant, et ideo sani non sunt nec sani esse possunt. Qui autem in flegmate superhabundant, si flegma eiciunt, aliquantum macilenti et sani in corpore flunt, quod sordes illas in se non retinent. Sed qui flegma non eiciunt et inde infirmitatem contrahunt, ut praefatum est, potionibus utantur et per eas purgentur. Be sternutaiione, Cum sanguis hominis in venis suis vigilans et velox non est, sed cum iacet velut dormiat, et lO ciim humores in eo veloces non sunt sed tepidi, anima hoc sentiens ex natura sua totum corpus per sternutationem concutit et sanguinem ac humores hominis iterum ad vigilantiam et ad statum suum redire facit.
text_translation: Quia qui in flegmate superhabundant et flegma non eiciunt, infirmis et debilibus carnibus se onerant, et ideo sani non sunt nec sani esse possunt. Qui autem in flegmate superhabundant, si flegma eiciunt, aliquantum macilenti et sani in corpore flunt, quod sordes illas in se non retinent. Sed qui flegma non eiciunt et inde infirmitatem contrahunt, ut praefatum est, potionibus utantur et per eas purgentur. De sternutatione: Cum sanguis hominis in venis suis vigilans et velox non est, sed cum iacet velut dormiat, et cum humores in eo veloces non sunt sed tepidi, anima hoc sentiens ex natura sua totum corpus per sternutationem concutit et sanguinem ac humores hominis iterum ad vigilantiam et ad statum suum redire facit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam si aqua per procellas et per inundationes non moveretur, putres- ceret; ita homo si non stemutaret vel si nasum per emunctionem non purgaret, interius putresceret. De sanguinis fluxu a naribus. Cum autem homines magnam iracundiam et contumacem mentem intra se habent, ita tamen quod ex aliqua causa inaudaciae seu timoris seu pudoris constricti haec per tristitiam vel prae impossibilitate non ostendunt nec proferunt, tunc interdum venae cerebri et colli ac pectoris eorum prae contrarietate ista erumpunt et franguntur et per aditum et per iter, quo odores ad nares portantur, efleruntur.
text_translation: For if water were not moved by storms and floods, it would rot; so too a man, if he did not sneeze or if he did not cleanse his nose through application, would rot internally. On the flow of blood from the nose. When, however, men possess great wrath and a stubborn mind within themselves, yet because of some cause are constrained by insecurity, fear, or shame, they do not display nor utter these things through sorrow or because of impossibility, then sometimes the veins of their brain, neck, and chest, due to the opposition of these things, burst and break, and flow out through the passage and through the route by which odors are carried to the nostrils.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et quidam sunt, qui aut cum vanis et variis cogitationibus occupantur, quas tamen perficere non possunt, aut cum vaga et dissoluta mente de loco in locum vagantur aut cum inconsuetis moribus aut cum resoluta lascivia hac et illac in mente sua proiciuntur et sic quasi in amentiam vertuntur, ita quod nec oculos nec faciem suam recto statu et gestu habere sciunt. Tunc de vagis cogitationibus istis venae cerebri et colli ac pectoris ipsorum erumpunt, ita quod sanguis de naribus eorum, ut praedictum est, efQuit, quia vanae cogitationes et vanae mentes illorum praedictas venas attollunt et sanguinem educunt.
text_translation: But there are also some who, either occupied with vain and varied thoughts, which they cannot however complete, or with a wandering and dissolute mind wander from place to place, or with unusual customs or with resolved licentiousness cast themselves here and there in their mind, and thus are turned as if into madness, so that they do not know how to hold their eyes or face in a correct posture and bearing. Then from these vain thoughts, the veins of their brain, neck, and chest erupt, so that blood from their noses, as previously mentioned, pours out, because the vain thoughts and vain minds of them raise and draw out the aforementioned veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et etiam cum homo interdum tam in came quam in venis supra modum in sanguine habnndat, tonc propter halitam, qnem per nares in se trahit et emittit, sanguis in naribus aditnm eruptionis facilius invenit qnam in alio loco corporis, ita quod venae cerebri et aliae venae, quae ibi vicinae sunt, ob ni miam superfltiitatem sanguinis ibi rumpuntur et effluunt. Quidam etiam homines sunt, in quibus sanguis superhabundat, ita quod de superfluitate eius interdum in spissitudinem et in nigredinem vertitur.
text_translation: And also when a man sometimes abounds excessively in flesh and in veins in blood, then because of the breath which he draws into himself and emits, blood finds more easily a place of eruption in the nostrils than in any other part of the body, so that the veins of the brain and other veins which are nearby there are broken and flow out because of the excess of blood there. Some men also are, in whom blood abounds excessively, so that from the superfluity of it sometimes it turns into thickness and into blackness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum isti interius bonas et sanas vires habent, hae superfluum et superhabundantem sanguinem in naribus eiciunt, ita qaod de hac efPusione cerebrum eorum purgatur et visus acuitur atque vires ipsorum in eis ad sanitatem reducuntur. Sed alii homines sunt, quorum sanguinem magnae febres et magnas ardor, qui in ipsis est, interdum ita movent, ut vinam, quod in utre movetur, et sic sanguinem in naribus edacunt. Et de hac sanguinis efPusione cerebrum aliquantom evacuatur et oculi caligant' et vires in homine illo debilitantur.
text_translation: But when those individuals internally possess good and healthy forces, these [forces] expel superfluous and overflowing blood from the nostrils, so that from this effusion the brain of them is purged and vision is sharpened, and their forces within them are restored to health. But other men exist, whose blood great fevers and great heat, which is in them, sometimes move, so that wine, which is moved in the wineskin, and thus blood is consumed from the nostrils. And from this effusion of blood the brain is somewhat evacuated, and the eyes become dim, and the forces in that man are weakened.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De coryza, Gum cerebrum hominis sanum et puram aliquando est, tunc etiam interdum turbines aeris et ceterorum elementorum ad cerebrum exsurgimt, qui diversos humores in ipsum trahunt et ab eo extrahunt et nebalosum fumum in via narium et gutturis eius parant, ita quod ibi nocivus livor contrahitur ut fumus nebulosao aquae. Et livor iste ibi etiam contrahit infirmitatem debilium humorum, ita quod ibi cum dolore per nares et per guttur expurgantur, quemadmodum ulcera, quae iam matura sunt, per ruptiones suas livores, qui in ipsis sunt, emittunt, et sicut etiam nullus cibus coqui potest, qain sordes, quae in eo sunt, per spumam purgationis eiciat.
text_translation: On a cold, The drops of the brain of a healthy and pure man are sometimes, then sometimes the winds of air and other elements rise up to the brain, which draw various humors into it and draw them out from it, and prepare a foul-smelling smoke in the passage of the nostrils and throat of it, so that there a harmful black bile is contracted like the smoke of misty water. And this black bile there also contracts the weakness of the weak humors, so that there with pain they are purged through the nostrils and through the throat, just as ulcers, which are already mature, emit through their ruptures the black bile that are in them, and just as also no food can be cooked, which the dirt that is in it is expelled through the foam of purging.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic facit anima in corpore hominis, cum omnes humores corporis in oculis, in auribus, in naribus, in ore et in digestione secundum naturam suam per ignem animae excoquuntur, ut cibus igne excoquitur, cum spumam eicit. Cum autem interdum homo novum et incognitum cibum comedit et novum et incognitum vinum aut alium novum potum bibit, tunc de novis humoribus illis alii humores laS in eo moventur et liquefacti per purgationem de naribus effiuunt, sicut etiam novum yinum in vas fusum sordes.
text_translation: And thus does the soul in the human body, when all the humors of the body in the eyes, in the ears, in the nostrils, in the mouth and in digestion according to their nature are boiled by the fire of the soul, as food is boiled by fire when it emits foam. When, however, a man sometimes eats new and unknown food and new and unknown wine or drinks another new drink, then from those new humors other humors are moved within him and liquefied, flowing out through the nostrils by purging, just as also new wine poured into a vessel leaves filth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: et faeces se purgando eicit. Quod si quis homo huiusmodi purgationem aliquo modo in se retineret seu prohiberet^ iie efflueret, ita se laederet, velut si digestionem vel si iirinam in se contineret, ne tempore suo procederent. Sed si alii humores ita superflue illis accedunt, quod ex hoc nimius dolor corporis excrescit, tunc medicinam faciat, ut tanto suavius effluant. De poiionibus, Potiones autem, quae stomachum pur- lO gant, non prosunt illis hominibus, qui valde infirmantur et qui hoc modo contriti sunt, quod semper a paralysi fatir gantur; nec etiam illis prosunt, qui huiusmodi mobilea humores in se habent, qui secundum inundationem fluminmn hac et iUac sine ulla stabilitate in eis assidue diffun- duntur, quia sumptis potionibus laeduntur magis quam ab eis sanentur.
text_translation: and feces he expels by purging himself. If any man of this kind were to retain or prevent this purging in any way, he would flow forth, thus harming himself, as if he were to hold back digestion or urine within himself, so that they would not proceed at their proper time. But if other humors so excessively flow into them that from this excessive pain of the body arises, then let him make medicine, so that they may flow forth more gently. Concerning potions, however, potions which purge the stomach are not helpful to those men who are very weakened and who are thus wounded, in that they are always afflicted by paralysis; nor are they helpful to those who have such mobile humors within them, which, like the inundation of rivers, flow hither and thither without any stability within them, constantly diffusing themselves, because by taking these potions they are harmed more than healed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam huiusmodi humores cum a stomacho emittuntur, et inter cutem et carnem et in venis frequenter discurrunt nec in 'stomacho pariter sunt, et ideo cum ab aliquo homine potio in stomachum mittitur, eos ibi ad expeUendum non inyenit;. JDe paralysi, Homines, qui de gutta paralysis con-» temntur, ut dictum est, et qui de praefatis humoribua ffttigantur, prosunt pulveres nobilium et bonarum herbarum et boni ac suaves odores pretiosorum aromatum, quo- niam ista noxium fumum, qui a praedictis humoribus procedens malos humores excitat, suavitate sua deprimunt et compescunt et attenuant.
text_translation: Nam such humores when they are emitted from the stomach, and frequently run between the skin and flesh and in the veins, nor are they in the stomach together, and therefore when a potion is sent into the stomach by someone, it does not find them there to expel them; Concerning paralysis, men who are considered to suffer from the drop of paralysis, as has been said, and who are tired from the aforementioned humors, benefit from powders of noble and good herbs and good and sweet odors of precious aromatics, since these suppress and restrain and weaken the noxious fumum, which proceeding from the aforementioned humors excites bad humors, by their own sweetness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Subscriptam autem potionem illi homines sumant, qui nec ad plenum sani nec ad plenum inflrmi sunt, et eis sanitatem confert. Sed et iUi 3o eam sumant, qui sani sunt, quoniam sanitatem eis retinet, ne inflrmentur, et iUi, qui a diversis et multis epulis pingues et Hvosos humores in se habent, quoniam limum ao faecem et tabem humorum iUorum aufert, et iUi etiam accipiant, qui aUquem cibum sumpserunt, unde in stomacho dolent, quia dolorem ilium mitigat et expeUit.
text_translation: Let the aforementioned potion be taken by those who are neither fully healthy nor fully sick, and it confers upon them health. But also let those who are healthy take it, because it retains their health, so that they do not become sick, and let those who, from diverse and many meals, have fat and viscous humors within them take it, because it removes the slime and feces and the taint of their humors, and let those also take it who have consumed some food from which they suffer stomach pain, because it mitigates and expels that pain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui autem eam sumere voluerit, eam aut in lunio aut in lulio, ante quam Augustus oriatur, ieiunus absque alio condimento accipiat, et noxios livores a stomacho aufert et eum pmv gat, ne in Augusto infirmetur. Quod si liomo ullum cibum comederit, unde stomachum suum ingravari senserit, iUe in Octobre illam sumat. Sed et alias potiones quilibet homo salubrius in praedictis mensibus quam in aliis naensibus sumere potest. De diaeta, Qui autem sanus esse vult, post naturaliter calidos cibos naturaliter frigidos comedat et post naturaloliter fngidos naturaliter calidos sumat et post naturaHter siccos naturaliter humidos et post naturaliter humidos naturaliter (siccos, sive coctos sive non coctos, qui in natura sua aut calidi aut frigidi sunt, ut sic invicem bene temperentur.
text_translation: Whoever wishes to take it, let him take it either in June or in July, before August comes, fasting without other seasoning, and removes harmful discolorations from the stomach and takes it, so that it does not weaken in August. If, however, any man eats food from which he senses his stomach to be burdened, let him take it in October. But also other potions any man may take more healthily in the aforementioned months than in other months. Concerning diet: Whoever wishes to be healthy, after naturally hot foods let him eat naturally cold foods, and after naturally cold foods let him take naturally hot foods, and after naturally dry foods let him take naturally moist foods, and after naturally moist foods let him take naturally (dry, whether cooked or uncooked, which in their nature are either hot or cold, so that thus they may be well tempered one with another.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De Adae creatione et JSvae formatione, Cum ergo deus Adam creavit, Adam dilectionem magnam in sopore habebat, com deus soporem in ipsum misit. Et deus fecit formam ad dilectionem viri, et sic femina dilectio viri est. Et mox cum femina formata est, virtutem illam creatio- ^ nis deus viro dedit, ut dilectione sua, quae femina est, filios procrecaret. Cum enim Adam inspexit Evam, totos sapientia impletus est, quia matrem, per quam filios procreare debebat, inspexit.
text_translation: On the Creation of Adam and the Formation of Eve. When therefore God created Adam, Adam had great love in sleep, since God sent sleep upon him. And God made a form for the love of a man, and thus woman is the love of a man. And soon when the woman was formed, God gave that virtue of creation to the man, that by his love, which is the woman, he might beget sons. For when Adam looked upon Eve, he was filled entirely with wisdom, because he looked upon the mother through whom he was to beget sons.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum autem Eva inspexit Adana, sic eum inspexit, quasi in caelum videret, et ut anima sursum tendit, quae caelestia desiderat, quoniam spes eius erat ad vimm. Et ideo una dilectio erit et esse debet viri et feminae et non aliena. Sed dilectio viri ad dilectionem feminae in calore ardoris est velut ignis ardentium montium, qui difficile extingui posset, ad ignem lig norum, qui facile extinguitur; dilectio autem feminae ad dilectionem viri ut suavis caJor de sole procedens, qui fructus producit, ad ardentissimum ignem lignorum, quoniam et ipsa suaviter in prole fructus profert.
text_translation: When, however, Eve looked upon Adam, thus she looked upon him, as if she saw heaven, and as the soul tends upward, which desires heavenly things, because her hope was toward life. And therefore one love should be and must be of the man and the woman and not alien. But the love of the man toward the love of the woman is like the heat of burning mountains, which is difficult to extinguish, toward the fire of ligaments, which is easily extinguished; the love of the woman toward the love of the man is like the sweet heat proceeding from the sun, which produces fruit, toward the most ardent fire of woods, because she herself sweetly produces fruit in offspring.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Magna autem dilectio, quae in Adam erat, cum Eva de ipso ezi vit, et dulcedo soporis illius, qua tunc dormivit, in transgressione eius in contrarium modum dulcedinis versa est. Et ideo, quia vir hanc magnam dulcedinem in se sentit et habet nt cervus ad foatem, sic ipse velociter ad feminam currit et femina ad ipsum in similitudine borrei areae, quae multis ictibus percutitur et ad calorem perducitur, cum grana in eo excutiuntur. De concupiscentia, Cum delectatio in homine surgit, ab igne meduUae excitatur.
text_translation: A great love, which was in Adam, when Eve lived from him, and the sweetness of that sleep, by which he then slept, in his transgression was turned into the opposite manner of sweetness. And therefore, because the man feels this great sweetness within himself and has it as a deer toward the fawn, thus he quickly runs to the woman and the woman to him in the similitude of the borei area, which is struck by many blows and brought to heat, when the grains within it are shaken. Of concupiscence, When delight arises in man, it is excited by the fire of the medullae.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ignis autem medullae diversis modis in homine surgit, scilicet aut de inepta laetitia dissolutionis aut de immoderatis cibis et potibus aut de vanis et superfluis cogitationibus, et ita hominem extra se ducit. Et ignis medullae hominis incendit delectationem, 10. quae gustum peccati habet, et tunc delectatio cum gustu fervorem in sanguine velut tempestatem facit, ita quod sanguis spumam facit et spumam illam similem lacti ad cavemas genitalium in dulcedine ducit, quia iam tunc cocta et matura est quoniam omnis cibus, postquam coqui- tur et postquam ad maturitatem perducitur, dulcior est, quam ante coctionem et quam ante maturitatem suam sit.
text_translation: Fire, however, of the marrow arises in man in diverse ways, namely either from unfit joy of dissolution or from immoderate foods and drinks or from vain and superfluous thoughts, and thus it leads man beyond himself. And the fire of the marrow of man kindles delight, which has the taste of sin, and then delight with taste produces fervor in the blood as if a storm, so that the blood produces foam and that foam, similar to milk, leads to the genital cavities in sweetness, because at that time it is already cooked and mature, since every food, after it is cooked and after it is brought to maturity, is sweeter than before cooking and than before its own maturity.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et in loco illo, ubi tota vis venarum est, dulcedinem hanc sentit, quia etiam dulcedo haec de venis, ut praedictum est, procedit, et ut etiam odor et sapor et tota vis vini in uno loco vasis egreditur. Quod si homo interdum c6gitationibus in delectatione fatigatur, tunc etiam aliquando absque tactu spumam tantum in genitalibus emittit, velut aqua, quae vento mota spumam de se producit. Si autem solo tactu delectatur, tunc spumam tenuem et turbidam ac semicoctam similem tenui lacti efPundit, quoniam alieno igne cocta non est.
text_translation: But also in that place, where the entire power of the veins is, it feels this sweetness, because this sweetness also comes from the veins, as has been said before, and also the odor and taste and the entire power of wine exit in one place of the vessel. If a man sometimes fatigues himself with thoughts in delight, then sometimes he emits only foam in the genitals without touch, like water which, moved by wind, produces foam from itself. If, however, he is delighted by touch alone, then he effuses a thin and turbid and half-cooked foam similar to thin milk, because it is not cooked by foreign fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam ut cibus suo naturali igne per se non coquitur, nisi alienus ignis addatur, ita etiam semen hominis non producitur ad plenum coctum, nisi alieno igne adiuvetur. Unde si homo cum alio homine vel cum alia sensibili et viventi creatura in delectatione est, tunc semen igne amborum excoctum et simile pingui et plenae medullae emittit. Et si tunc masculus in ipsa effusione seminis sui cum femina est, semen suum in rectum locum efiPundit, velut ille, qui coctam escam de olla in scutellam ad comedendum mittit.
text_translation: For just as food is not cooked by its own natural fire, unless an alien fire is added, so also the man's semen is not produced fully cooked, unless aided by an alien fire. Hence, if a man is with another man or with another sensible and living creature in pleasure, then the semen, cooked by the fire of both, emits something similar to fat and marrow full of nourishment. And if then the male is with the female in the ejaculation of his semen, he pours his semen into the proper place, just as he who sends cooked food from the pot into the dish for eating.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem cum femina non est, sed cum alia creatura natu rae suae contraria, tuiic semen sumn in iniustum locmn turpiter emittit, velut ille, qui coctnm cibum ex oLU sumens eum super terram fundit. £t hic est limus, de quo bomines formantur. JDe pMutione, In pollutione autem seminis, quae dormiente bomine absque visione somni tantum per natnram bominis contingit, ardor meduUae non movetur, unde et ipsa velut aqua, quae modico calore tepida est| emittitizr.
text_translation: If, however, he is not with a woman, but with another creature contrary to his own nature, your semen impudently emits itself into an unjust place, as he who takes cooked food from oil and pours it upon the earth. And this is the slime from which humans are formed. De pollutione, In pollution of semen, which occurs in a sleeping human without vision of a dream, solely by the nature of the human, the heat of the marrow is not moved, whence it also emits itself, as water which is warm with moderate heat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In poUutione vero, quae operante opere per visionem som ni fit, medulla bominis acriter ardet, ita quod eadem pollutio tunc velut fervens aqua educitur, non tamen ad plenum eocta, quia bomo tunc non vigilat Et si bomo aliquando in delectatione est ( et commotionem in corpore suo sentit, ita tamen quod genitalis spuma ob boc de eo non effluit: inde non debilitatur in coipore. Si vero ita in delectatione conmiOYetur, quod spuma baec ad articolum emissionis peryenerit, sed tamen intra in corpore aliqao modo continente retenta fiierit, infirmitatem bomo multotiens exinde incurrit, ita quod aut acutam febrem aut 20tertianas febres aut alias infirmitates inde babebit.
text_translation: In pollutione vero, quae operante opere per visionem somni fit, medulla hominis acriter ardet, ita quod eadem pollutio tunc velut fervens aqua educitur, non tamen ad plenum excuta, quia homo tunc non vigilat. Et si homo aliquando in delectatione est (et commotionem in corpore suo sentit, ita tamen quod genitalis spuma ob hoc de eo non effluit: inde non debilitatur in corpore. Si vero ita in delectatione conmotetur, quod spuma haec ad articulum emissionis pervenierit, sed tamen intra in corpore aliquo modo continente retenta fuerit, infirmitatem homo multotiens exinde incurrit, ita quod aut acutam febrem aut quartanas febres aut alias infirmitates inde habebit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De coniugii tempore in masculo. Masculus autem a quinto decimo anno aetatis suae gustum delectationis in se babet et tunc etiam spumam bumani seminis de vanis cogitationibus facile in eo exsudat, sed delectatio et semen eius nondum ad maturitatem in eo peryenerunt. Sed necesse est, cum semen eius adbuc immatnrum est, ut ei acerrima custodia adbibeatur, ne ad mulierem seu ad aliam libidinem suam explendam accedat, quod facile ex.boc insipiens et intellectus vacuus erit et scientia in eo deficit et facile insanae et incontinentis naturae fit, quod nondnm ad maturitatem illam pervenit, ut maturum semen de eo emittat.
text_translation: On the time of marriage in men. Indeed, a man from the fifteenth year of his age possesses within himself the taste of pleasure, and then also the foam of human semen easily exudes from him due to vain thoughts, but his pleasure and his semen have not yet reached maturity within him. However, it is necessary, when his semen is still immature, that he be subjected to the strictest custody, lest he approach a woman or another desire to satisfy his lust, which easily results in him becoming foolish and lacking in intellect, and knowledge within him is lacking, and easily he becomes of insane and incontinent nature, which has not yet reached that maturity to emit mature semen from him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t si masculus fortis in corpore est, tunc in sexto decimo anno aetatis suae ad maturitatem explendae cupiditatis accedit, vel si in corpore debilis est, tunc in septimo decimo anno aetatis suae maturitatem fertilitatis suae adipiscitur. £t deinde plenum intellectum et meliores et stabiliores mores in maturitate sua habet, quam prius ante Biaturitatem habuisset. A quinquagesimo autem anno pueriles et instabiles mores bomo deserit et stabiles mores apprehendit» et si viridis et fortis naturae est, tunc calor delectationis circa septuagesimum annum ab eo declinat, si vero debilis naturae faerit, tunc circa sexagesimum annum in illo attenuatur, et ita usque ad octogesimum axinmn; ab octogesimo autem anno iam iu eo extinguitur.
text_translation: And if a man is strong in body, then at the sixteenth year of his age he reaches the maturity of spending desire, or if he is weak in body, then at the seventeenth year of his age he attains the maturity of his fertility. And afterwards he has full intellect and better and more stable manners in his maturity than he had before maturity. From the fiftieth year, however, he leaves behind childish and unstable manners and apprehends stable manners; and if he is of green and strong nature, then the heat of delight declines from him around the seventieth year, if indeed he was of weak nature, then around the sixtieth year in him it becomes weaker, and thus up to the eightieth year; from the eightieth year, however, it is extinguished in him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Puella a duodecimo aetatis suae anno gustum delectationis in se sentit, et tunc etiam de lascivis cogitationibus spuma delectationis de iUa facile exsudat, quamvis eadem delec- tatio nondum matura ad semen sit. Et cum puella adhuc inunatura est, oportet ut diligentissima custodia coerceatur, ne in lasciviam vadat, quod tunc magis quam alio tempore in yagatione mentis dissoluta est. Nam cum adhuc in immatura aetate fertilis non est, si tunc non custoditur, nt praedictum est, sensus bonestatis et pudoris atque bonam scientiam prae inamatura delectatione et libertate facile perdit, ita quod etiam propter malam consuetudinem postmodum mores pecorum magis quam homintim imitatur.
text_translation: The girl, from the twelfth year of her age, feels within herself the taste of delight, and then also from lascivious thoughts the foam of delight easily exudes from that, although the same delight is not yet mature for semen. And since the girl is still unripe, it is necessary that she be kept under most diligent custody, lest she go into lasciviousness, which then more than at any other time is in the loose wandering of the mind. For since she is still in immature age and not yet fertile, if she is not guarded then, as was said before, the sense of modesty and shame and good knowledge are easily lost before immature delight and freedom, so that even because of bad habit later she imitates the manners of beasts more than human.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In quinto decimo autem anno aetatis illius, si viridis et humidae naturae fuerit, delectatio in ea iam matura et fertilis est, vel si debilis et infirmae naturae est, tunc in sexto decimo anno ad maturitatem fertilitatis pervenit, ac deinde matnrum et plenum intellectum habet et ad stabiliores mores accedit, quam prius fecisset. Sed et circa quinquagesimum aetatis suae annum puellares mores et gestus instabilitatis abicit et compositum ac stabilem tenorem in moribus suis deinde tenet.
text_translation: In the fifteenth year of her age, if she is of green and moist nature, delight in her is already mature and fertile; or if she is of weak and feeble nature, then in the sixteenth year she reaches the maturity of fertility, and afterward she has a mature and full intellect and attains more stable manners than she had done before. But also around the fiftieth year of her age, she abandons childish manners and gestures of instability, and afterward maintains a composed and stable manner in her habits.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem humidae et viridis ac fortis naturae est, tunc circa septuagesimum annum delectatio carnis in ea attenuatur, si autem tenuem et infirmam naturam habuerit, tunc circa sei^agesimum annum ab ea declin^t et circa octogesimum annum eam deserit, quemadmodum etiam de masculo praedictum est. Qui autem semen suum in libidine ut asini excutiunt, rubeos oculos et pellem oculorum spissam exinde contrahunt, et exinde etiam aliquantum in oculis caligant.
text_translation: If, however, it is moist, green, and of strong nature, then around the seventieth year the delight of the flesh in it is attenuated; if, however, it has a thin and weak nature, then around the sixtieth year it declines from it, and around the eightieth year it abandons it, as was also said about the male. Whoever, however, expels their semen in lust like an ass shakes, contracts red eyes and thick skin of the eyes from this, and from this also somewhat cloudiness in the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui vero moderate et cum disciplina hoc opus facmnt, non multum exinde in oculis per caliginem laeduntur. De medulla, Medulla autem in ossibus hominis firmamentum est totius corporis eius, et ipsa medulla aliquan tum spissa est et non inundans ac tantae fortitudinis et roboris in ossibus hominis ut cor in reliquo corpore, atque ardentissimo ardore ardet, ita quod calor eius super calorem ignis est, quoniam ignis extingui potest, ignis yero medullae non extinguetur, quamdiu homo yivit; sed calore et sudore suo ossa pertransit et tam ossa quam totum corpus hominis confortat.
text_translation: Those who, however, perform this work moderately and with discipline, are not greatly harmed in the eyes through darkness. Concerning the marrow, the marrow indeed in the bones of man is the foundation of his entire body, and the marrow itself is somewhat thick and not overflowing, and of such strength and robustness in the bones of man as the heart in the rest of the body, and it burns with the most ardent heat, so that its heat surpasses the heat of fire, because fire can be extinguished, but the fire of the marrow cannot be extinguished as long as man lives; but with its heat and sweat it permeates the bones and strengthens both the bones and the entire body of man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De tribus meduUae viribus, Nam calor ignis in medulla est ut ignis in lapide et tres vires in se habet. Una vi sanguinem incendit, ut fluat; alia diverso modo sanguinem tam a viro quam a muliere interdum edudt; tertia ardentem et dulcem gustum et sudantem ac ardentem ventum suavissimi amoris geniturae producit. De incontinentia. Et iste ardens ventus interdum in otioso et nuUa sollicitudine occupato homine surgit et in pectus eius flat et hominem illum aliquantum laetum facit et ita de pectore eius in cerebrum ascendit et illud totum ac venas eius ardente calore implet, atque tunc etiam pulmonem et cor tangit et sic ad genitalia loca vadit, in viro in lumbos, in muliere in umbilicum.
text_translation: On the Three Powers of the Marrow. For the heat of fire in the marrow is like fire in stone and possesses three powers within itself. One power ignites the blood, causing it to flow; another power, in a different manner, sometimes draws blood both from man and from woman; the third power produces a burning and sweet taste and a sweating, burning wind that generates the most sweet love. On Incontinence. And this ardent wind sometimes arises in a man who is idle and unoccupied by any concern, and it blows into his chest, making him somewhat joyful, and thus ascends from his chest into the brain, filling the entire brain and its veins with ardent heat; then it also touches the lungs and the heart, and so proceeds to the genital regions, in man to the loins, in woman to the navel.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc scien tia hominis sic obdormit per ignorantiam. De suggestione, Sed et tunc etiam suggestio diaboli in tyrannide tempestatis se his adiunget, et homo ille ad libidinem exardescit verecundiae oblitus. Sed et cum sol oritur, aer et ros ei in ministerio occurrunt, ita quod aer illi aliquam fngiditatem et ros aliquam humiditatem subministrat, per quae in calore ignis sui temperatur, sic quod calidus, frigidus et humidus ad onmem utilitatem fructuum terrae descendit.
text_translation: And then the knowledge of man thus falls asleep through ignorance. Concerning suggestion, But then also the suggestion of the devil in the tyranny of the storm will join to these things, and that man inflames himself with desire, forgetting the shame. But when the sun rises, air and dew come to him in service, so that the air gives him some coldness and the dew some humidity, through which he tempers the heat of his fire, so that warm, cold, and humid descend for the benefit of the fruits of the earth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De medullae temperie, Sicut sol terrae calorem affert, ita et medulla hominis toto corpori calorem tribuit, sed validus ventus de stomacho velut aer exiens ignem medullae aliquantum infrigidat, et humiditas de vesica velut ros procedeos ignem irrorando hninectat, ita quod ipse ignis rectam temperiem corpori hominis dat, et quod etiam tunc frigiditate et humiditate temperatur. Sed quemadmodum terrores tempestatum et grandinum aerem conturbant, ita quod in ministerio suo rectam frigiditatem soli non ministrat nec sol illi rectum calorem, sic etiam diversi cibi aliquando stomachum irretiunt, ita quod ipse ab eis commotus iustam refrigerationem calori medullae non potest immittere, unde eandem meduUam in procellas et in tumorem potius commoyet quam in temperiem.
text_translation: Of the marrow's temperament, Just as the sun brings heat to the earth, so does the marrow of man impart heat to the entire body, but a strong wind from the stomach, like air exiting, somewhat cools the fire of the marrow, and humidity from the bladder, like dew proceeding, moistening the fire, so that this fire gives the correct temperament to the human body, and that it is also tempered by coldness and humidity at that time. But just as the terrors of storms and hail disturb the air, so that in their service it does not minister the correct coldness to the sun nor does the sun minister the correct heat to it, so also diverse foods sometimes entangle the stomach, so that moved by them it cannot send the just cooling to the marrow's heat, whence the same marrow is rather agitated into storms and tumor than into temperance.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: lo De crapula. Nam cum homo diversas carnes et supra modum calidos ac alios delicatos cibos indifferenter et inordinate comedit, succus eorum succum medullae illius in contrariam tempestatem excitat, ita quod in delectationem surgit. £t ideo homo, qui cames comedere vult, eas moderate et simplici condimento coctas comedat et non nimis calidas nec nimis deliciose diversis faltionibus et condimentis paratas et irretitas, quia succus earum aliquantulum quandam societatem ad succum carnis hominis habet et delectationem medullae eius facile incutit, quemadmodum etiam squalidus et aridus ventus vires roris imminuit, ita quod aptam humiditatem calori solis subministrare non valet; sic etiam forte et pretiosum vinum vim vesicae hominis torret, ita quod ipsa rectam viriditatem medullae eius afferre non poterit, De vmo, Nam vinum est sanguis terrae et est in terra ut sanguis in homine et velut quandam societatem cum sanguine hominis habet et ideo etiam velut velocissimam rotam calorem suum ex vesica ad meduUam ducit et eam in ardentissimum ardorera evertit, ita quod et eadem me- dulla fervorem delectationis sanguini tunc tribuit.
text_translation: On Cramula. For when a man eats various meats and excessively hot and other delicate foods indiscriminately and immoderately, their juice excites the juice of the marrow in the opposite state, so that it arises in delight. And therefore, a man who wants to eat meats should eat them moderately and cooked with simple seasoning, and not too hot nor too deliciously prepared with various faltionibus and seasonings, because their juice has some kind of society with the juice of the man's flesh and easily implants delight in his marrow, just as also a cold and dry wind diminishes the forces of dew, so that it cannot supply suitable humidity to the heat of the sun; thus also strong and precious wine burns the force of the man's bladder, so that it cannot bring the proper greenness of its marrow. On wine. For wine is the blood of the earth and is in the earth as blood is in man and like having some kind of society with the blood of man and therefore also like the fastest wheel, its heat from the bladder to the marrow, and turns it into the most ardent heat, so that the same marrow then gives the heat of delight to the blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde homo, qui pretiosum et forte vinum bibere vult, illud aqua misceat, ut fortitudo et calor eius aliquantum debilitetur et temperetur; et etiam vinum illud, quod dicitur Hunonicum, aqua temperet, quatinus aqua amaritudinem et acerbitatem illius suavem faciat et deliniat, quod, sicut sanguis aridus est et non fluit absque humiditate aquae. sic etiam yinum absque admixtione aquae bibitum hominem torret et laedit ac ei sanitatem corporis minuit et delectationem carnis infert.
text_translation: Therefore, a man who wishes to drink precious and strong wine should mix it with water, so that its strength and heat are somewhat weakened and tempered; and also that wine, which is called Hunonicum, should be tempered with water, so that water may make its bitterness and sharpness sweet and smooth, for, as blood is dry and does not flow without the moisture of water. Thus also wine drunk without the mixture of water burns and harms a man, and reduces the body's health and brings pleasure of the flesh.
