source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t omnis cibus et potus boneste et temperate et moderate sumatur, ne bomo diversis humo ribus, qui in illis sunt, debilitetur et ne natura eius in contrarietate delectationis modum suum excedat, quod, sicnt terra ad fertilitatem fructuum laeditur, si eam sol sine temperie aeris et roris immoderate perurit, ita etiam homo ad sanitatem corporis laeditur et ad delectationem camis excitatur, si ardorem ciborum et potuum immoderate in se traxerit. Et si sanus est corpore, in cibo et potu se obseryet, ut praedictum est, quatinus sanus permaneat; si autem debilis est, esu camium moderate et discrete recreetur, sed tamen nullum vinum bibat, nisi aqua permixtum.
text_translation: And all food and drink should be taken honestly and temperately and moderately, lest man, through the diverse humors present in them, be weakened, and lest his nature exceed its proper measure in the contrariety of delight, which, as land is harmed in its fertility of fruits if the sun scorches it without the temperance of air and rain immoderately, so also man is harmed in his bodily health and excited toward the delight of the body, if he immoderately takes in the heat of foods and drinks. And if he is healthy in body, he should observe himself in food and drink as previously said, so that he may remain healthy; if, however, he is weak, he should be refreshed by eating food moderately and distinctly, but still drink no wine unless mixed with water.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De cogUatiom. Sed praefatus ardens ventus, qui ad delectationem carnis ex medulla bominis surgit, vanas cogi- . tationes etiam interdum advocat et producit, cum homo cogitat, quid sit et unde sit et quis gustus ille sit, quem in transgressione praecepti dei per pomum sibi Adam con traxit, aut cum aliquid videt aut audit, unde delectatio ei occurrit. Tunc etiam per diabolicam suggestionem cogitationes istae quasi nutum faciendo sibi ex medulla attrahunt praedictum ardentem ventum delectationis, ita quod iUe surgit et pectus transit ac cerebrum tangit atque iecur et cor transfigit et in genitalia membra cadit, ut praedictum est.
text_translation: Of cogitation. But the aforementioned ardent wind, which arises from the marrow of the human body for the delight of the flesh, sometimes also calls forth and produces vain cogitations, when a man thinks what it is and whence it is and what that taste is which Adam took for himself from the apple through the transgression of God's command, or when he sees or hears something, from which delight occurs to him. Then, through diabolical suggestion, these cogitations, as if by nodding to themselves from the marrow, draw forth the aforementioned ardent wind of delight, so that it rises and passes through the chest and touches the brain and transforms the liver and heart and falls into the genitalia, as was said before.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic illis contingit, qui delectationem suam cupiunt opere explere. De nocturna oppressione, Multotiens autem cum homo dormit, sanguis, qui in eo est, de igne medullae eius fortiter ardet, et sic de calore sanguinis aqua, quae in sanguine est, exsiccatur. Et tunc etiam diabolica ars, quae est in prima suggestione primae coagulationis, cum horao concipitur, se aliquando permissione dei sursum erigit et tempestatem circa hominem facit pavorem ei in somnis incutiendo , se videlicet per oppressionem fantasiae ostendendo velut ibi sit; et tamen ibi non est, quia si ibi esset, homo durare non posset; sicut etiam tonitma faciunt, cum • BT LIB.
text_translation: And thus it befalls those who seek to fill their pleasure through work. Concerning nocturnal oppression, often when a man sleeps, the blood within him burns fiercely from the fire of his marrow, and thus the water that is in the blood dries up from the heat of the blood. And then also the devil's art, which is in the first suggestion of the first coagulation, when the hour is conceived, sometimes by the permission of God raises itself upward and causes a storm around the man, instilling fear in his dreams, namely by showing itself through the oppression of the imagination as if it were there; yet it is not there, because if it were there, man could not endure; just as thunder also makes, when • BT LIB.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: II. horribiles sonitus emittnnt., per qnos hominibas terrores incntiant, ita quod exterriti pavescunt. Sed tamen ibi pleniter non sunt, sed in novissimo die totas virtutes suas in plenitudine ostendunt, cum omnia terrena concutiunt. De somniis, Ita et diabolus facit, cum quasi in nebuloso torbine se ostendit et hominem tam diu torquet, usque dum anima eins de prophetia somniorum ad se revertitur et excatitnr nesciens, quid terroris passa sit. Isti terrores facile omnibus hominibus accidunt, cum dormiunt, exceptis illis , qui valde securae et valde laetae naturae ( sunt .
text_translation: II. They emit horrible sounds, by which they incite terror in humans, so that terrified, they fear. But there they are not fully present, but on the last day they display all their powers in fullness, when they shake all earthly things. Concerning dreams, thus also the devil does, when he appears as if in a nebulous whirlwind and torments the man for so long, until the soul of him returns to himself from the prophecy of dreams and is extracted without knowing what terror it has suffered. These terrors easily occur to all humans when they sleep, except for those who are very secure and very joyful in nature.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: K Et bis dormientibus terrores isti raro eveniunt, quia magna et bonesta laetitia, quam habent naturaliter in se, non potest esse absque gustu boni spiritus, et ipsi etiam ex natara sna mansueti sunt et non faUaces nec dolosi in moribns suis. De complexione. Sed alii quidam homines sunt, qoi sanguinei dicvuitur, in quorum sanguine melancolia mxLltotiens surgit, quae sanguinem illum nigrum facit et qaae aquam, quae in iUo est, aridam parat, ita quod ex hoc homines tam vigilantes quam dormientes saepe gra- viter fatigantur.
text_translation: And those who sleep twice, these terrors rarely occur, because the great and good joy, which they naturally have in themselves, cannot exist without the taste of good spirit, and they themselves are also from their nature gentle and not deceitful nor crafty in their morals. On complexion. But certain other men are, who are called sanguine, in whose blood melancholy often arises, which makes that blood black and prepares the water that is in it to be dry, so that from this men both the wakeful and the sleeping are often heavily fatigued.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De Adae casu et melancolia, Nam cum Adam bonum scivit et pomum comedendo malum facit, in vicissitudine matationis ilHus melancolia in eo surrexit, quae sine snggestione diaboli non est in homine tam vigilante quam dormiente, quia tristitia et desperatio ex melancolia ascendnnt, quas Adam in transgressione sua habuit. Oum enim Adam divinum praeceptum praevaricatus est, in ipso momento melancolia in sanguine eius coagulata est, nt splendor recedit, cum lumen extinguitur, et ut stuppa ardens et fumigans foetendo remanet.
text_translation: On the Fall of Adam and Melancholy. For when Adam knew good and by eating the fruit made evil, in the vicissitude of change, melancholy arose in him, which is not in a man as awake as asleep without the suggestion of the devil, because sadness and despair arise from melancholy, which Adam had in his transgression. When indeed Adam transgressed the divine command, at that very moment melancholy coagulated in his blood, so that splendor recedes, when light is extinguished, and so that burning, smoky soot remains, stinking.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic factum est in Adam, qnia cum splendor in eo extinctus est, melancoli£b in sangnine eius coagulata est, de qua tristitia et desperatio in eo surrexemnt, quoniam diabolus in casu Adae melancoliam in ipso conflavit, quae homiDcm aliquando dnbinm et incredulnm parat. Sed quod forma hominis ligata est, ita quod se sursum supra modum elevare non potest, ideo timet deum et tristis est, ac sic miiltotieiis in tristitia illa desperat difPidens, ut deus eum observet.
text_translation: And thus it was done in Adam, since when the splendor in him was extinguished, melancholy was coagulated in his blood, from which sorrow and despair arose in him, because the devil in Adam's case mixed melancholy within him, which sometimes prepares man for doubt and incredulity. But since the form of man is bound, so that he cannot elevate himself above measure, therefore he fears God and is sad, and thus often in that sorrow he despaired distrustfully, so that God might observe him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quoniam homo ad imaginem dei formatus est, non potest omittere, quin deum timeat, et ideo grave est diabolo cum bomine conversari, qui illi resistit, quod homo deum tamen plus timet quam diabolus, ita quod bomo spem in deum habet, diabolus autem nullam ad ipsum tenet. Sed et suggestio diaboli multotiens in banc melancoliam se intorquet et bominem tristem et desperantem facit , ita quod multi buiusmodi bomines in desperatione se suffocant et conterunt, multi autem in hoc malo sibi ita resistunt, quod velut martyres in hac pugna sunt.
text_translation: And since man was formed in the image of God, he cannot omit to fear God, and therefore it is grave for the devil to converse with man, who resists him, so that man fears God more than the devil, so that man has hope in God, but the devil holds none towards him. But also the suggestion of the devil often twists itself into this melancholy, making man sad and desperate, so that many of these men suffocate and destroy themselves in desperation, but many others resist this evil so much that they are as martyrs in this battle.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De mentis oppressione, Et diabolus sic fatigat hominem tam yigilantem quam dormientem atque interdum eum dormientem hoc modo fatigat, quod putat, quod ipsum aliquid premat. De didboli odio. Quoniam diabolus virtutem hominis odit, ideo etiam omnes reliquas creaturas, quae virtuosae sunt in pecoribus et in herbis, et quae mundae et quae utiles sunt, odio habet. Qui ergo a diabolico fantasmate in die ac in nocte vigilando aut dormiendo fatigatur. medicamen a deo inde datum quaerat.
text_translation: On the oppression of the mind. And the devil thus fatigues man both when watchful and when sleeping, and sometimes fatigues him while sleeping in such a way that he thinks something presses upon him. On the devil's hatred. Since the devil hates the virtue of man, therefore he also hates all other creatures which are virtuous in cattle and in herbs, and which are pure and which are useful. He who therefore is fatigued by a devilish phantom during the day and during the night, whether watching or sleeping, let him seek medicine given by God from thence.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum homo in laetitia aut in tristitia aut in mansuetudine aut in ira aut in aliis his moribus est, tunc diu in his manere non potest, sed ad alios mores et motus eum declinare oportet. Et cum inter duas mutationes est, ita quod unum deserit et aliud incipit, anima hanc mutationem naturaliter sentit et quasi taedium habet, quod tot mutationes incipit, et facit, velut a corpore secedere velit, scilicet ut tunc facit, cum moriente corpore egreditur os hominis aperiendo, cum ita oscitat.
text_translation: But when a man is in joy or in sadness or in meekness or in anger or in other such manners, then he cannot remain long in these, but it is necessary for him to turn away to other manners and movements. And when he is between two changes, so that he leaves one and begins another, the soul naturally feels this change and has as it were weariness, that so many changes begin and are made, as if it wished to separate from the body, namely as it then does, when the body is dying, the mouth of man opens, when thus it gapes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De oscifatione, Et cum homo tunc alias mutationes aut alia opera strenue inceperit, anima iterum a taedio mutationis huius quiescit. Sed et cum alius homo in taedio est et hanc oscitationem in homine isto videt, tunc anima eius naturaliter facit, velut cum illa corpus suum deserere velit, cum os corporis eius oscitando aperit. De membrorum extensione. Cum autem malae febres in homine sust et cum mali humores in eo inundare incipiunt, gravitas corporis et taedium mentis eum occupat, et hoc anima naturaliter sentiens aliquantum de his mutationibus velut taedio affecta corpore cedit et ipsum corpus et venas eius aliquantum subrigit et extendit, velut tunc facit, cum de corpore exire debet.
text_translation: On Yawning. And when a man then begins other changes or other works strenuously, the soul again rests from the weariness of this change. But also when another man is weary and sees this yawning in that man, then his soul naturally does, as if it wishes to leave its body, when the mouth of his body opens through yawning. On the Extension of the Body's Members. But when bad fevers are in a man and when bad humors begin to flood him, the gravity of the body and the weariness of the mind occupy him, and this soul, naturally sensing this, somewhat yields to these changes as if affected by weariness in the body, and raises and extends his body and his veins somewhat, as if it does when it should exit the body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De litargia. Mali quoque humores in quibusdam hominibus interdnm fumum faciunt, qui usque ad cerebrum eorum ascendit et illud ita inficit, quod ex hoc stulti et obliviosi ac sensu vacui fiunt. 2e singuUu. Dolor etiam, qui dicitur singultus, nascitur de frigore stomachi, et frigus illud circa iecur flectitur et circa pulmonem tenditur, ita quod etiam vires cordis moventur; quemadmodum bomo de frigore tremit, et quod dentes hominis tremendo strident; et ita homo cum sonitu vocis suae singultum habet.
text_translation: On litargia. Also, the bad humors in some men sometimes produce smoke, which ascends as far as their brain and so corrupts it that from this they become foolish, forgetful, and void of sense. 2. Singultus. Also, pain, which is called singultus, arises from the coldness of the stomach, and that coldness is bent around the liver and stretched around the lung, so that even the strength of the heart is moved; just as a man trembles from cold, and the teeth of a man chatter with trembling; and thus a man has a hiccup with the sound of his own voice.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De melancolia et psalmo. Multotiens etiam melancolia in homine exsurgit et quendam fumigantem fumum in eo difiFundit, qui venas et sanguinem et camem illius tam diu contrahit, usque dum per corpus illius se diffundere desistit, scilicet usque dum cessat. Sed et saepius evenit, quod fel in homine superhabundat, ita quod etiam per corpus hominis se interdum in superfluitate sua diffundit; et ita homo in came suo quasi fixuras pun- gentes tam diu patitur, quousque superfluitas fellis sedatur.
text_translation: On melancholia and the psalm. Often, melancholia arises in a man and diffuses a smoky vapor within him, which draws the veins, blood, and camem of that man so long, until it ceases to spread throughout his body, that is, until it stops. But also more frequently occurs that bile superabounds in a man, so that it sometimes spreads throughout the man's body in its own superfluity; and thus the man suffers in his flesh as if puncturing fissures so long, until the superfluity of bile subsides.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De felle et poena Adae. Antequam Adam divinum praeceptum transgrederetur, quod modo fel in homine est, in eo ut cristallus lucebat et gustum bonorum operum in se habebat; et quod nunc etiam melancolia est in homine, in eo ut aurora fulgebat et scientiam et perfectionem bonorum operum in se habebat. Cum autem Adam transgressus est, splendor innocentiae in eo obscuratus est, et oculi eius, qui prius caelestia videbant, extincti sunt, et fel immutaium est in amaritudinem et melancolia in nigredinem impietatis, atque totus in alium modum mutatos est.
text_translation: On Bile and the Punishment of Adam. Before Adam transgressed the divine command, what is now bile in man was in him as crystal, shining and possessing the taste of good works; and what is now also melancholy in man was in him as dawn, shining and possessing knowledge and the perfection of good works. But when Adam transgressed, the splendor of innocence in him was obscured, and his eyes, which previously saw celestial things, were extinguished, and bile was changed into bitterness and melancholy into the blackness of impiety, and the whole man was changed into another manner.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic anima eius tristitiam contraxit et excusaHildegardia cansae et carae ed. Eaiser. tionem mox in ira quaesiyit. Nam de tristitia ira oritur, unde etiam a primo parente homines tristitiam et iram et quaeque sifoi nociya contraxerunt. J9e tristiiia et ira. Gum autem anima hominis sibi et corpori suo aliquid adversi senserit, cor et iecur et yenae eorum contrahit, et ita circa cor yelut nefoula exsurgit et cor obnubilat, et sic homo tristatur; post tristitiam vero ira exsurgit.
text_translation: And thus, its soul took on sorrow and soon sought out excuse. For anger arises from sorrow, whence also from the first parent men took on sorrow and anger and whatever things harmful. De tristitia et ira. When, however, the soul of man perceives something adverse to itself and its body, the heart and liver and their veins contract, and thus around the heart a noxious thing arises and the heart is obscured, and thus man becomes sorrowful; after sorrow, however, anger arises.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum interdum aliquid viderit vel audierit yel cogitayerit, de quo tristitiam habet, tunc etiam aliquando nebula tristitiae, quae cor eius occupayit, calidum fumum in omnibus humoribus et oirca fel eius parat et fel moyet, et sic ira de amoritudine fellis silenter exsurgit. Et cum homo iram non perficit, sed cum eam silenter tolerat, fel cessat. Quod si ira non cessaverit, tunc fumus iste ad melancoliam se extendens illam excitat, et illa de se nigerrimam nebulam emittit, quae ad fel transiens ab illo amarissimum fnmum extorquet, et ita cum eodem fumo ad cerebruin hominis transiens eum in capite primum insanire pariter faciunt et tunc ad yentrem eius descendunt et yenas illius ac interiora yentris concutiunt et hominem yelut in amentiam Vertunt.
text_translation: For when sometimes it sees or hears or thinks about something of which it has sadness, then also sometimes the cloud of sadness, which has occupied its heart, prepares a warm smoke in all the humors and the body's bile and moistens it, and thus anger from the bitterness of bile silently arises. And when a man does not complete anger, but when he silently endures it, the bile ceases. If anger does not cease, then this smoke extending to melancholy excites it, and it emits from itself a very black cloud, which, passing to the bile, extracts from it the most bitter smoke, and thus with the same smoke passing to the man's brain, they together cause him first to go mad in the head, and then descend to his belly and shake his veins and the interior of his belly, and the man is turned into madness like.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t sic homo quasi se ipsum ignorans iram perficit. Nam homo de ira magis furit quam de alia infirmitate insaniae. Et homo multotiens ab ira quasdam grayes infirmitates con trahit, quod cum contrarii humores de felle et de melancolia saepius in homine excitantur, illum aliquando infirmum faciunt, quoniam si homo amaritudine fellis et nigredine melancoliae careret, semper sanus esset. Unde fel et mdancoUa crescant. Ille autem homo, in quo fel maiores yires habet quam melancolia, iram in se facile domat; in quo autem melancolia maiores yires habet quam fel, iracundus est et facile irascitur.
text_translation: And thus a man, as if ignorant of himself, perfects anger. For a man rages more from anger than from any other infirmity of insanity. And a man often draws certain serious infirmities from anger, because when contrary humors from bile and from melancholy are frequently excited in a man, they sometimes make him weak, since if a man were devoid of the bitterness of bile and the blackness of melancholy, he would always be healthy. Therefore, bile and melancholy increase. He who has in himself greater powers of bile than of melancholy easily masters anger within himself; he, however, in whom melancholy has greater powers than bile, is irascible and easily angry.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quemadmodum acetum forte et acerbum de bono yino fit, sic etiam fel de bonis et suayibus escis crescit et de malis decrescit; melancolia autem de bonis et suayibus cibis decrescit, de malis yero et de amaris et de immundis et de male paratis cibis et de diyersis humoribus diyersarum infirmitatTiin crescit. Qni autem in facie nibet, cum irascitnr, huius saDguis de felle fervet et ita ad faciem dudtar, et iste repente et yalde in iram movetur, sed ira eins cito compescitur, ut aliquis magnns fervor, qui cito cadit, et ex hac ira non multum laeditur nec in corpore arescit.
text_translation: And just as vinegar becomes strong and sour from good wine, so also bile grows from good and sweet foods and decreases from bad ones; melancholy, however, decreases from good and sweet foods, but increases from bad ones, indeed from bitter and unclean and poorly prepared foods and from various humors of various weaknesses. He who, when he is in the face, nibbles, when he is angry, this blood from the bile heats and thus to the face doubts, and this one suddenly and greatly is moved to anger, but his anger is quickly restrained, as though some great heat, which quickly falls, and from this anger he is not much harmed nor does the body become hot.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et saepe se non yindicans absque vindicta pertransit. Sed qui in facie pallet, cum in iram movetnr,. buius ira talis est, quod in ipso melancolia excitatur, quae quidem sanguinem eius non movet, sed humores illius paulatim conturbat, ita quod homo ex hoc infrigidatur, lO et quod vires eius infringuntur et mollescunt, et sic in facie pallet iram tegens. Sed interim mala voluntas acerbae ultionis in eo surgit, quae ita perdurat, quod se non valet abstinere, quin iram suam vindicet.
text_translation: And often, not avenging himself without vengeance, he passes through. But he who in the face beats, when he is moved to anger, this anger is such that melancholy is excited in him, which indeed does not move his blood, but gradually disturbs his humors, so that man from this becomes chilled, and his forces are impaired and softened, and thus covering anger in the face. But meanwhile, bad will of bitter revenge arises in him, which so endures that he cannot refrain from avenging his anger.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De suspiriis, £t isti multotiens ab ira sua in cor- pore languent et arescunt, anima vero scientiam et intellectum habet. Unde cum cogitat, unde sit et quid sit, suspiria introrsum trahit, homine ignorante, unde suspiria illa sint. Oum autem interdum aut contumeliam aut molestiam aut aliud aliquid adversi corpus suum passurum senserit nec hoc prohibere se posse intellexerit, alta suspiria introrsum trahit. De lacrimis, Et ita de humoribus illis, qui in homine sunt, velut amarus fumus per tristitiam egrediens circa cor spargitur, qui tabem, scilicet aquam de sanguine cor- dis et de sanguine aliarum venarum, cum excussione gemituum superat ac eam sic sursum per venas ducendo ad venulas cerebri quasi fumando mittit et per illas ad oculos ducit, quia et oculi quandam cognationem ad aquam habent.
text_translation: Of sighs, and these [people] often languish and wither in their body from their own wrath, but the soul indeed has knowledge and understanding. Hence, when it considers, where it is and what it is, it draws sighs inward, the man being ignorant of where those sighs are from. But when sometimes it senses that it will suffer either contempt or molestation or some other adversity to its own body, and having understood that it cannot prevent this, it draws deep sighs inward. Of tears, and thus of those humors which are in man, like a bitter smoke issuing through sadness, spreading around the heart, which, namely, water from the blood of the heart and from the blood of other veins, with the vibrations of groans, overcomes and thus leads it upward through the veins to the little veins of the brain, as if smoking, and through them leads it to the eyes, because the eyes have a certain kinship with water.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ita aqua iUa ex oculis fluit et hoc lacrimae sunt. Nam aqua lacnmarum hoc modo de sanguine hominis per suspiria gemituum extrahitur, velut semen hominis ex medulla et de sanguine eius educitur. Lacrimae autem de tristitia abortae velut in amaro fumo sursum ad oculos ascendunt, ut praedictum est, et sanguinem hominis arefaciunt et carnem macerant et hominem ut mali cibi laedunt atque caliginem . oculis inferunt. Quae 10 vero de laetitia orinntur, leniores sunt qaam illae, quae de tristitia insurgunt.
text_translation: And thus that water flows from the eyes, and this is tears. For the water of tears is thus drawn from the human blood through sighs and groans, just as the semen of man is drawn from the marrow and from his blood. However, tears from sorrow, as if in bitter smoke, ascend upward to the eyes, as previously stated, and purify the human blood and waste away the flesh, and harm the man as bad food does, and bring darkness to the eyes. Those, however, which flow from joy are softer than those which rise from sorrow.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gum enim anima per tristitiam et per intellectum se aliquando recognoscit caelestem esse, sed bic in mundo peregrinam, et cum ei corpus in bono consentit, ita quod in sanctis operibus unum sunt, tiinc absque nebula et absque turbine fumi cum suspiriis gaudii et laetitiae per praedictas yenas suaviter lacrimas ad ocnlos mittit et eas ut suavem fontem educit. Et bae lacrimae cor hominis non conterunt nec sanguinem eius are faciunt nec carnem macerant nec oculis caliginem inferunt.
text_translation: For indeed, the soul sometimes recognizes itself as celestial through sadness and through intellect, but there, in the world, as a pilgrim, and when the body consents to it in good [manner], so that in holy works they are one, then without cloud and without the whirlwind of smoke with sighs of joy and gladness, through the aforementioned veins, sweetly sends tears to the eyes and brings them forth as a sweet fountain. And these tears do not break the human heart nor make the blood air, nor do they waste the flesh nor bring darkness to the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De paenitentia. Si quis paenitendo peccata sua deflet, hae lacrimae tristitia et laetitia permixtae sunt et tantum oppressa mente sine fumo educuntur; sed aliquantum sanguinem arefaciunt ac carnem macerant et caliginem ocnlis aliquantum inducunt, quod oppressa mente procedunt, usque dum per emendationem in eis laetitia oriatur. Qui autem de pingui natura sunt nati, pingues venas et molle cor habent et facile plorant et facile laetantur. Qui vero de arida natura nati sunt, durum cor ut callos habent, quae reliquae carni durior est, et raro et vix plorant et acres in mentibus suis sunt.
text_translation: On Penitence. If anyone weeps for his sins while penitent, these tears are mixed with sorrow and joy and are raised from a mind weighed down without smoke; but they somewhat purify the blood and make the flesh lean and somewhat introduce darkness into the eyes, which they proceed with a weighed-down mind, until through amendment joy arises in them. Those who are born of a fat nature have fat veins and a soft heart and weep easily and rejoice easily. But those who are born of a dry nature have a hard heart like calluses, which is harder than the remaining flesh, and rarely and scarcely weep and have sharp minds.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Lacrimae autem, quae cum suspiriis ad oculos trahuntur, si ab oculis non emittuntur, tunc ad humores, qui in corpore hominis sunt, revertuntur et eos acerbos et aceto similes parant et pectus ari dum faciunt. Sed tamen ipsae ad oculos pervenientes et non effluentes sed interius in homine retentae oculos non multum laedunt, quod ab eis non effluunt. De Adae prudentia, Adam quoque ante praevaricationem angelicum carnem et omne genus musicorum sciebat et vocem habebat sonantem, ut vox monochordi sonat.
text_translation: The tears, which are drawn to the eyes along with sighs, if they are not emitted from the eyes, then return to the humors which are in the human body, and they prepare them bitter and resembling vinegar, and they make the chest dry. But indeed, those tears which reach the eyes and are not effused but are retained within the human being do not greatly injure the eyes, because they do not effuse from them. From the Prudence of Adam, Adam also knew angelic flesh and every kind of musicians before the transgression, and he had a voice sounding, like the voice of a monochord.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In praevaricatione autem iUius de astutia serpentis intorsit se in meduUam et in femur eius quidam ventus, qui etiam nunc in omni homine est. Et de vento illo splen hominis impinguescit, et inepta laetitia et risus atque cachinni in homine excutiuntur. De cachinno et rim, Nam sicut in praevaricatione Adae sancta et casta natura prolem gignendi in alium modnm delectationis carnis matata est, ita etiam et vox superiorom gandiorom, quam idem Adam habebat, in contrarinm modnm risns et cachinnoram versa est.
text_translation: In the transgression, however, he, by the cunning of the serpent, twisted himself into the medulla and into the thigh of him a certain wind, which is also now in every man. And from that wind the spleen of man is struck, and inept joy and laughter and giggles are shaken out in man. Of the giggle and laughter, for as in the transgression of Adam the holy and chaste nature of procreation was turned into another manner of carnal delight, so also the voice of the higher and more beautiful, than the same Adam had, was turned into the opposite manner of laughter and giggles.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Inepta enim laetitia et risns velnt qnandam societatem ad delectationem camis habent, et ideo ventns ille, qni risnm sns- citat, de mednlla hominis exiens femnr eins et interiora concntit. Ac interdum prae nimia concnssione risus aqnam lacrimamm de sangnine Venarum oculis hoc modo educit^ quemadmodum etiam spuma seminis hominis aliquando de sangnine venamm per ardorem delectationis excutitur. De laetitia et risu. Gum enim scientia animae hominis nihil tristitiae et nihil adversi et nihil mali in hbmine sentit, tunc cor eiusdem hominis ad laetitiam se aperit, ut flores ad calorem solis se aperiunt, et mox iecnr ean- dem laetitiam recipit ac eam in se retinet, ut stomachus cibum in se continet.
text_translation: Inept indeed is that joy and laughter which some society has for the delight of flesh, and therefore that wind which stirs laughter, issuing from the marrow of man, shakes his thigh and inner parts. And sometimes, through excessive shaking, laughter draws water of tears from the blood of the veins to the eyes in this manner, just as also the foam of man's semen sometimes is shaken out from the blood of the veins through the heat of pleasure. On joy and laughter. For when the knowledge of the soul of man senses nothing of sorrow, nothing adverse, and nothing evil in the body, then the heart of that same man opens itself to joy, as flowers open themselves to the heat of the sun, and soon the same joy lies within it and it retains it within itself, as the stomach retains food within itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum sic homo aut de bonis ant de malis, quae sibi placent, laetatur, tnnc praedictns ventus interdum ex mednlla exiens femur illius primum tangit et ita splen occupat atque venas eiusdem splenis implet et se ad cor extendit et iecur replet ac ita homi- \iem ridere facit atque vocem eius similem voci pecomm in cachinnis edncit. Homo autem ille, qui cogitationibus snis velut ventus hac et illac facile proicitur, splen aliqnantum crassnm habet et ideo facile laetatur et facile ridet.
text_translation: And when thus a man, whether from good or from bad things that please him, rejoices, then the aforementioned wind, emerging from the medulla, first touches the thigh of that man and thus occupies the spleen and fills the veins of that same spleen, and extends itself to the heart and fills the liver, and thus causes the man to laugh and utters his voice similar to the voice of an animal in laughter. But that man, who is easily cast about here and there like the wind by his own thoughts, has a somewhat thick spleen and therefore is easily pleased and easily laughs.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et quemadmodnm tristitia et ira hominem debilitat et aridum facit, sic etiam immensus risus splen vnlnerat et stomachum fiEitigat et in motione sua humores iniuste discurrere facit. De pinguedine. Si qnis homo cames et alias escas supra modum pingues aut ullos cibos supra modum sanguine impinguatos comedit, potius ex eis infirmitatem quam sanitatem incnrrit, quod isti cibi supra modum impinguati ad rectam et ad salubrem digestionem prae nimia et lubrica humiditate, quae in ipsis est, in stomacho hominis remanere non possunt.
text_translation: But even as sorrow and anger weaken and dry up a man, so too does excessive laughter wound the liver and irritate the stomach, causing the humors to flow unjustly in their motion. On obesity. If any man eats meats and other foods excessively fat, or consumes any foods excessively fatty with blood, he incurs infirmity rather than health from them, because these foods, excessively fatty, cannot remain in a man's stomach for proper and healthy digestion, due to their excessive and lubricious moisture, which is in them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ideo homo recte temperatas in pinguedine cames et recte temperatos in sanguine cibos edat, ut eos usque ad bonam et rectam digestionem retinere possit. De hominis ariditcUe, Si aliquis homo multum aridus in corpore et in membris suis est, tunc modice pingaes cames et modice impinguatos sanguine cibos interdum comedat, ut ariditas et siccitas, quae in ipso sunt, aliquantum humectentur et temperentur. Nam cames animalium comestae carnem hominis impinguant et vinum sanguinem hominis augmentat magis quam alii cibi aut quam alii potns.
text_translation: And therefore, a man should eat properly tempered meats in fat and properly tempered foods in blood, so that he may retain them until good and proper digestion. Concerning the dryness of a man, if any man is very dry in his body and in his limbs, then he should occasionally eat moderately fatty meats and moderately blood-rich foods, so that the dryness and dryness which are within him may be somewhat moistened and tempered. For meats of animals eaten fatten the flesh of man, and wine increases the blood of man more than other foods or other drinks.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vino. Terra autem, quae in frumento fertilis est, si vinum gignit, hoc infirmis hominibus ad bibendum sanabilius est quam vinum, quod in fructifera terra nascitur, scilicet quae modicum frumenti gignit, etiam si istud pretiosius illo est. Vinum enim sanat et laetificat hominem bono calore et magna virtute sua. De cerevisia. Gereyisia autem caraes hominis incrassat et pulchrum colorem faciei eius praestat propter fortitudinem et bonum succum frumenti.
text_translation: On wine. The earth, however, which is fertile in grain, if it produces wine, this wine is more healing for the weak to drink than wine which is born in a fruit-bearing land, namely, which produces a little grain, even if this is more precious than that. For wine heals and rejoices man with good heat and great virtue. On beer. Beer, however, thickens the human body and gives a beautiful color to his face because of the strength and good juice of the grain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Aqua vero hominem debilitat et livorem circa pulmonem eius, si infirmus est, aHquando parat, quoniam aqua debilis est et fortem vir- ^ tutem non habet. Sed si homo sanus est, si tunc interdum aquam bibit, ei non oberit. De ebrietate. Cum autem homo supra modum et sine mensura vinum vel alium liquorem, unde inebriari potest, bibit, totus sanguis eius liquefit atque in venis ipsius hac et illac inordinate discurrit et diffunditur, ita quod etiam totus intellectus et sensus hominis illius confunditur, velut flumina de nimia inimdatione imbrium effiuunt et praecipitanter inundant.
text_translation: Water indeed weakens a man and causes a yellowish discoloration around his lungs, if he is infirm, when it prepares, because water is weak and does not possess strong virtue. But if a man is healthy, if then he occasionally drinks water, it will not harm him. Concerning drunkenness. When, however, a man drinks wine or another liquid, from which he can become drunk, beyond measure and without restraint, his entire blood liquefies and in his veins it runs and spreads disorderly here and there, so that even his entire intellect and sense are confused, as rivers, from excessive inundation of rains, flow forth and rapidly flood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quia in homine illo humanus in tellectus sine mensura confasus est, bona scientia in eo ad bona tendit, ita quod homo tunc multotiens quasi sancta verba inordinate et indiscrete per ignorantiam loquitur et quod etiam mala scientia in eo ad mala tendit, ita quod etiam turpia et mala verba absque pudore ignoranter pro fert, quia magis insanum et indomitum intellectum hinc habet quam rectum sensum, quoniam rationalitas tunc in eo suffocata et submersa est.
text_translation: And because in that man the human intellect is confused without measure, good knowledge in him tends toward good, so that man then often speaks as if holy words in an inordinate and indiscrete manner through ignorance, and also bad knowledge in him tends toward evil, so that he also utters vulgar and evil words without shame, ignorantly, because he has from here a more insane and undisciplined intellect than a right sense, since reason at that time in him is choked and submerged.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vomitu. Cmn homo immoderate frigidos cibos samit et tunc mox calidos, ita tamen, qxiod frigidi calidos exsaperant, ant si humidos immoderate sumit, ita quod bumidi siccos supra modum excedunt, tunc de diversitate procellarum harum in stomacho saepe homo vomitum pati- tnr, cum sic digestionem habere non potest, et ideo homo cibos in frigore et calore recte temperatos sumat, ne se ipsum per vomitum laedat. Qui autem de aliqua infirmitate Yomitum patiuntur et propter inordinatam comestionem, isti interius in stomacho frigidi sunt, ita quod calo- lO rem in se non habent, ut cibi in eis ad digestionem coqui possint, et tunc semicocti cibi sursum ascendunt, cum ad seoessum descendere non valent.
text_translation: On vomiting. When a man takes cold foods immoderately and then soon afterwards hot ones, so that the cold ones are consumed by the hot ones, or if he takes humid foods immoderately, so that the humids exceed the drys beyond measure, then often a man suffers vomiting from the diversity of these tempests in the stomach, since he cannot have digestion thus, and therefore a man should take foods properly tempered in cold and heat, so that he does not harm himself through vomiting. Those however who suffer vomiting from some infirmity and because of disordered eating, these are cold within the stomach, so that they do not possess heat within themselves, so that foods in them can cook for digestion, and then half-cooked foods ascend upwards, since they cannot descend to the exit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic homo multas infirmitates per vomitum patitur. Quod autem aliquis homo se ipsum ad yomitum provocet vel quod aliquod condi- mentum sumat, unde vomitum provocet, hoc ei salubre et sanum non est, quia vomitus sic provocatus venas et sanguinem eins extorqueret et in iniustum modum deduceret, ita quod etiam idem vomitus rectum iter exeundi noo haberet et ita homo saepe laederetur. Ideo homo vomi- tum in se non provocet, qui sanitati eius inutilis est, quoniam vomitus, qui per se in homine exsurgit non provocatus, melior est illo, quem homo aliquo condimento in se provocat.
text_translation: And thus a man suffers many ailments through vomiting. That, however, if any man provokes himself to vomiting or takes some condiment which provokes vomiting, is not healthy and wholesome for him, because vomiting thus provoked would extract his veins and blood and lead them in an unjust manner, so that even the same vomiting would not have the right course to proceed, and thus the man would often be harmed. Therefore, a man should not provoke vomiting in himself, which is useless to his health, because vomiting which arises in a man by itself, unprovoked, is better than that which a man provokes in himself with some condiment.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De indigestione. Sed cum humores aut de infirmitate aut' de infirmis et non sanis cibis moventur, ita quod calor ad frigus se coniungit et frigus ad calorem et humiditas ad siccitatem et siccitas ad humiditatem, tunc ipsi interdum indigestos cibos et potus expellunt et extrudunt. Et si huiusmodi infirmi cibi expelluntur, hoc bonum est homiDi ad sanitatem. Si autem cibi boni sunt, tunc obest homini ad corporis sanitatem, si expelluntur, quod venae bono succo eorundem ciborum evacuantur.
text_translation: On indigestion. But when the humors, either from weakness or from weak and not healthy foods, move, so that heat joins with cold and cold with heat, and moisture with dryness and dryness with moisture, then they sometimes expel and push out indigest foods and drinks. And if such weak foods are expelled, this is good for man toward health. But if the foods are good, then it is harmful to man for the body's health, if they are expelled, because the veins are evacuated of the good juice of those foods.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum etiam mali humores superhabundant in homine, tunc aliquando parant in eo nebulosum fumum, qui nec calidus nec firigidus 3& e8t . £t hic in viscera et circa stomachum et per totum orpus illius diffunditur et ceteras pestes, quae in sunt, commovet atque ipsius cibum, quem sumit, nec recto et naturali introitu permittit stomachum intrare nec recto et naturali exitu exire, sed facit eum fluctuantem et turbulentum, velut putei et plateae, atque yitalem et natura« lem aerem in eo attenuat, et ideo etiam cibus ad rectam et naturalem digestionem in ipso excoqui non potest, unde etiam semicoctus et quasi tenuis fluxus ab ipso egreditar.
text_translation: And when also the bad humors are in excess in man, then sometimes they produce in him a nebulous smoke, which is neither hot nor cold. And this spreads into the viscera and around the stomach and throughout the entire body of him, and stirs up the other pests, which are in him, and does not permit the stomach to receive food through a right and natural entrance nor to expel it through a right and natural exit, but makes it fluctuating and turbulent, like a well and a plain, and also attenuates the vital and natural air in him, and therefore also the food cannot be cooked in him to a right and natural digestion, from which also a half-cooked and as it were thin flux flows out from him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: . De dissinteria. Quaedam autem gracillimae venae et sanguineae cuteculam, scilicet membranam, in qua cerebrum hominis reconditum est, circumdant, et bae infixae sunt aliis maioribus yenis, quae ad cor et ad iecur et ad pulmonem et ad stomachum atque ad uniyersa eius intestina descendunt, et sanguinem ita maioribus venis illis subministrant, velut quidam parvi rivuli, qui aquas suas ad maiora flumina deferunt. Maiores autem illae venae sunt quasi magna flumina, quae fluenta sua per proyincias ducunt, et ut canales, qui in aedificia aquam portant et ibi effundunt.
text_translation: On dysentery. Certain very slender veins and sanguineous cuticle, namely the membrane in which the human brain is concealed, encircle, and these are fixed in other larger veins, which descend to the heart and to the liver and to the lung and to the stomach and to all of its intestines, and thus supply blood to those larger veins, as certain small rivulets which carry their waters to greater rivers. The larger veins themselves are as great rivers which conduct their currents through provinces, and as canals which carry water into buildings and there discharge it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum ergo mali humores in aliquo homine superhabundayerint et reliquas febres, quae in illo sunt, in commotionem dederint, tunc yelut quandam et incongruam inundationem in eo parant, quae quendam spissum et malum fumum fumigantem ad cerebrum illius surgere facit et quae yenulas illas, quae cerebrum circumdant, omnes in contrarium modum fluctuationis yertit. Tunc sanguis, qui in ipsis est, supra modum efQuens maiores yenas, quibus ipsae, ut praedictum est, infixae sunt, omnes moyet, ita quod et illae in contrariam inundationem effluunt et quod sanguinem suum per totnm corpus diflundunt et ad intestina et ad digestionem emittimt.
text_translation: When therefore the bad humors in a certain man have been in excess and the remaining fevers that are in him have caused commotion, then they prepare a certain kind of incongruous inundation in him, which causes a certain thick and bad smoky fume to rise to his brain and which turns all those venulas that surround the brain in the opposite mode of fluctuation. Then the blood, which is in them, pours out beyond measure into the larger veins, into which they, as previously said, are fixed, moving all of them, so that they also flow out in the opposite inundation and that their blood is diffused throughout the entire body and emitted to the intestines and to digestion.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic sanguis ille ao intestinis et yisceribus ac digestioni permixtus digestionem sanguineam facit, ita quod etiam ipse sanguis cum digestione egreditur. De aemoptoica, Sed cum mali et concreti ac yenenosi humores in homine superhabundayerint, sanguini, qui per yenas corporis discurrit, rectum meatum auferunt, et eundem sanguinem yitalia et yiscera iniusto itinere intrare cogunt, unde homo ille per excreationem et per nausiam cum periculo sanguinem evomit et interius arescit.
text_translation: And thus that blood, mixed with the intestines and viscera and digestion, creates a sanguineous digestion, so that even the blood itself exits with the digestion. Concerning aemoptoica, But when harmful and congealed and venomous humors in a man are superabundant, the blood, which flows through the veins of the body, is deprived of its proper channel, and the same blood is forced to enter the viscera and viscera by an unjust course, whence that man ejects blood through excretion and vomiting with danger and internally withers.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et aliquando mali humores, qui aquosi et tenues sunt, superhabundant in homine et sanguini yen.arum in recto meatu suo obsistunt et sanguinem sine egestione ciborum per posteriora transire faciunt et eundem hominem peri- culo substemunt ac eum in ariditatem yitae ducunt. Si antem per posteriora cum egressione ciborum egressi fuerint, eundem hominem multotiens purgant et sanitati restituimt. Quidam vero homines tantam amaritudinem cordis et cogitationum in se habent, quod eadem amaritudo splen lo constringit et arefacit, unde et bonus succus, qui splen yegetare deberet, ad pulmonem per iniusta intinera transit et ibi quandam concretionem cum sanguine parat, et ita homo ille multotiens cum periculo sanguinem excreat et evomiK Sed alii homines sunt, qui triste et maerens cor saepe habent, et eadem tristitia interiores venulas, quae songuinem per corpus ferunt, constringit, ita quod aliqua eoram permodicum yulneratur.
text_translation: But also sometimes the bad humors, which are watery and thin, abound excessively in man and obstruct the blood in the veins' proper channel, causing the blood to pass without evacuation of food through the posterior parts and thus subjecting the same man to danger and leading him to the aridity of life. If, however, they have exited through the posterior parts along with the evacuation of food, the same man is often purged and restored to health. Indeed, some men possess such bitterness of heart and thoughts within themselves that this bitterness constricts and dries up the spleen, so that the good juice, which should nourish the spleen, passes to the lungs through unjust channels and there prepares a certain concretion with the blood, and thus that man often with danger evacuates blood and vomits. But other men are such that they often have a sad and sorrowful heart, and this same sadness constricts the inner veins, which carry the blood through the body, so that some of them are slightly wounded.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde et illa guttas sanguinis ad interiora paulatim fundit, et ita homo ille aliquando sanguinem evomit, sed postquam idem lenitatem et laetitiam mentis receperit, eadem yenula ad sanitatem redit, et ita homo ille a yomitu sanguinis cessat. De ahsiinentia indiscreta. Cum yero quidam homines supra modum in cibis abstinentes sunt, ita quod iustam et congruentem refectionem ciborum corpori suo non tribuunt, et cum etiam alii instabiles et leyes in moribus suis sunt et alii multis et magnis languoribus occupati, tunc aliquando contingit, quod yelut tempestates in corporibus illorum oriuntur, cum elementa, quae in eis sunt, in contrarium modum yertuntur.
text_translation: Therefore, that [thing] gradually pours blood into the interior, and thus that man sometimes vomits blood, but after he has received mildness and joy of the mind, the same venula returns to health, and thus that man ceases from vomiting blood. On indiscreet abstinence. When indeed some men abstain from food beyond measure, so that they do not grant to their body a just and suitable replenishment of food, and when also others are unstable and light in their customs and others occupied with many and great weaknesses, then sometimes it happens that like tempests arise in their bodies, when the elements that are in them are turned in the opposite way.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam Cum ignis et aqua in praedictis hominibus sibi inyicem repugnant, saepe contingit, ut in aliqua compagine membrorum yel in alia aliqua parte corporis sibi repugnantes occurrant et ibi pustulam cum tumore camis exsurgere faciant. Et haec pustula trium generum est. JDe apostematibus. Nam quaedam est yelut nigra, quae de nimia fortitudine ignis inturgescit et pericnlum homini HILDE6ARDIS iaiert ac mortem miiiakir yelut irmptio nabinm, quae illa dimmpit et ad TiihilTim dncit, sapra qnae ceciderit.
text_translation: Nam cum ignis et aqua in praedictis hominibus sibi in vicem repugnant, saepe contingit, ut in aliqua compagine membrorum vel in alia aliqua parte corporis sibi repugnantes occurrant et ibi pustulam cum tumore carnis exsurgere faciant. Et haec pustula trium generum est. De apostematibus. Nam quaedam est velut nigra, quae de nimia fortitudine ignis inturgescit et periculum homini Hildegardis iactat ac mortem mikitir velut ruptio nubem, quae illa dimmit et ad Tibi Tim ducit, supra qua ceciderit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: — £t qnaedam yelut giisea est de tempestate praedictorom elementomm qnasi fnlgaraQtiiim et inoongmam plnviam emittentinm in homine orta, qnae qnidem corpori laesionem infert, sed tamen illnd omnino non interficit, sicnt grando, qnae frngibns laesionem fiicit sed radices eamm non eicit. — Sed et qnaedam velnt alba est de mnlta innndatione eomndem elementomm qnasi snpra modnm crescentinm flnminnm in homine exsnrgens, qnae ettam coipori debilitatem infert, sed tamen ilind per omnia non conterit, velut repentina innndatio et effnsio flnyiomm, qnae aliqna qnidem loca et aliqnlis frnges snperemanant, sed ea per totnm non anfemnt.
text_translation: — And certain signs are visible from the predicted tempest, as it were, lightning and innumerable rain issuing in man, which indeed inflicts injury on the body, but nevertheless does not altogether destroy it, like hail, which breaks and inflicts injury but does not uproot the roots. — But also certain white vapor is from great inundation of the same elements, as it were, rivers rising beyond measure in man, which indeed inflicts weakness on the body, but nevertheless does not shatter it entirely, like sudden inundation and effusion of rivers, which certain places and certain crops remain, but they do not wither entirely.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Pnstnla antem illa, qnae nigra est, pericnlosa est et fere incnrabilis, grisea vero vel alba aliqnantum leniores nigra snnt et cnrari possnnt. De itmare, A diversis qnoque humoribus tam bonis quam malis caro et venae hominis ingrossescunt, velut a fermento farina elevatur et intnmescit. Sed hnmores, qui a corde, a iecore, a pulmone, a stomacho et a ceteris interioribus procedunt, cum aliqnando in iniustam diversitatem et superfluitatem vertnntur, tnnc etiam interdum tenaces et lubrici et tepidi fiunt.
text_translation: The pustula, which is black, is perilous and nearly incurable; the gray or white ones, however, are somewhat milder than the black ones and can be cured. On the journey, the flesh and veins of man grow thick, as flour is elevated and swells from fermentation. But the humors, which proceed from the heart, from the liver, from the lung, from the stomach, and from the other interiors, when at times they turn into unjust diversity and superfluity, then also sometimes become tenacious, slippery, and warm.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t si tunc in homine remanent, eum in infirmitatem dncunt. Si autem extra eum erumpunt, saniorem eum reddnnt. De ulcere, Quod si in unum loctun vel in plnra loca in homine oeoiderint, ita qnod ibi ulcas unum ant plura feoerint, tuno homo permittat ea ad maturitatem peraovenire, ut efflnant, ne, si intus remanserint, gravius doleat. Et postquam humores in maturitate ibi effluxerint, tuno homo ille medioinam cum unguentis adhibeat. De soabie, Si vero mali humores in .
text_translation: And if then they remain in man, they lead him into weakness. If however they erupt outside him, they make him healthier. Of ulcer: If indeed they occur in one place or in many places in man, so that there ulcers one or many make, then man permits them to reach maturity by ripening, so that they flow out, lest, if they remain inside, he suffer more grievously. And after the humors have effluxed in maturity there, then that man applies medicine with unguents. Of sabie: If indeed the bad humors in.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: scabie per totum oorpui hominii erumperint, tunc iterom ad maturitatem et ad emanationem eorum aliquamdiu exspectet et usque dum oatis inter uloera rubedinem habere incipiat et exiiooarii ot tuno oonvenientibus ungnentis ungat, ne si tunc dintius exspectaverit, cutis in maiorem dolorem nlcerum et putredinis vertatur. De ictericia. Pestis, quae dicitur ictericia, de superfiiiitate fellis nascitur, quod de infirmis humoribus et de febi ibus ac de magna et frequenti ira fel effiuit.
text_translation: Scabies per totum corpus hominii erumpant, tunc iterum ad maturitatem et ad emanationem eorum aliquamdiu exspectet et usque dum oatis inter ulcera rubedinem habere incipiat et exsiccarii aut tunc convenientibus unguentis ungat, ne si tunc diutius exspectaverit, cutis in maiorem dolorem ulcerum et putredinis vertatur. De ictericia. Pestis, quae dicitur ictericia, de superficie felle nascitur, quod de infirmis humoribus et de febribus ac de magna et frequenti ira felle effluvit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et efiusionem hanc fellis iecur et cetera viscera excipiunt, et ipsa etiam efPusio totam camem hominis ita pertransit, velut acutum acetum novum vas transfondit et bominem laedit. Ac sic per contrarium colorem suum in homine cognoscitur. De exercitio et animae taedio. Sicut corpus hominis absque opere non est nec omittit, quin semper aliquod operetur, sic etiam anima in homine ex natura sua hoc officium habet, quod diverso opere corporis taedio affecta se interdum a cursu suo subducit, quasi dormitet; velut molendinum multa inundatione aliqua parte sui fractum aliquando a labore sistitur.
text_translation: And this effusion is received by the bile, liver, and other viscera, and the effusion itself thus passes entirely through the cavity of the human being, as if sharp vinegar were poured into a new vessel and harmed the person. And thus, by its opposite color, it is recognized in the human being. On exercise and the weariness of the soul. Just as the human body, without work, does not exist nor omit, but always performs some work, so too the soul in the human being by nature has this office, that when affected by the weariness of the body's work, it sometimes withdraws from its course, as if sleeping; like a mill, when some part of it is broken by much flooding, is sometimes stopped from labor.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic anima interim in quiete silentii est, usque dum corpus aliqua verecundia vel aliquibus terroribus coercetur et opprimitur. £t tunc iterum iia vires suas et cursum suum recipit et resurgit. Et tunc homini illi est, quasi novus sit et quasi novos mores habeat. De ira. Nam aliqui homines ex natura sua iracundi sunt. Et cum anima istorum in ipsis taedio affecta interim in quiete silentii est, tunc illis ex ira sua inter- dum aliquis defectus accidit, unde corpus ipsorum opprimitur; et ita anima vires suas recipit et resurgit.
text_translation: And thus the soul is in the quiet of silence, until the body is constrained and oppressed by some shame or certain fears. And then again it receives its strength and its course and rises up. And then to that man it is as if he were new and as if he had new manners. Concerning anger. For some men by their nature are irritable. And when the soul of these men, affected by weariness, is in the quiet of silence, then at times some defect occurs from their anger, by which their body is oppressed; and thus the soul receives its strength and rises up.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et etiam cum in .aliis hominibus animae taedio affectae interim in quiete silentii sunt, saepe contingit, ut corpora hominum iUorum aliqua molestia opprimantur, imde et ipsae animae excitatae priores vires recipiant et ad se redeant. Sunt et alii homines, in quibus, cnm animae eorum in ipsis prae labore et taedio oppressae silent, aliqua impatientia vel dubitatione corpora ipsorum constringuntur, et sic animae, quae in ipsis iam dormitabant, ex- citatae ad pristinas vires resurgunt.
text_translation: But also, when in other humans the souls, affected by weariness, are in the meantime in the silence of quiet, it often happens that the bodies of those humans are oppressed by some affliction, whence also the souls themselves, when excited, receive their former strength and return to themselves. There are also other humans, in whom, when their souls, in them, oppressed by labor and weariness, are silent, the bodies of those humans are constricted by some impatience or doubt, and thus the souls, which in them had already been sleeping, when excited, rise again to their former strength.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sunt quoque quidam homines, qui ex complexione sua saepius in ira exardescunt. De insania et ^epiyiensia. Et ira, quae in ipsis est, totum saDguinem eonim in sanguineam et in magnam inundationem saepe movet, et sic de inundatione ist-a qnasi quidam fumus et humor cerebrum eorum tangit et eos insanire facit, ita quod etiam scientiam ipsorum minuit. Et cum isti aliquando in iram moventur et quibusdam saecularibus angustiis aggravantur, hoc diabolus videns etiam flatu suggestionis suae eos terret; unde anima, quae in ipsis est, fatigata succumbit et se subtrabit, ac sic corpus deficiendo cadit atque ita in defectum iacet, usque dum anima iterum resumptis viribus exsurgit.
text_translation: There are also certain individuals who, due to their constitution, frequently blaze up with anger. Concerning insanity and epilepsy. And the anger that resides within them often moves all their blood into a sanguineous and great flood, so that from this flood, as it were, a certain smoke and humor touches their brain and causes them to become insane, so that even their knowledge is diminished. And when these individuals are sometimes moved to anger and are burdened with certain worldly afflictions, the devil, seeing this, also terrifies them by the whisper of his suggestion; thus, the soul that resides within them, being fatigued, succumbs and withdraws itself, and so the body, failing, falls and thus lies in a state of defect, until the soul, having resumed strength, rises again.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et isti, qui boc malo fatigantur, iracundum visum et faciem et iracundum motum habent. Et cum in terram cadunt, aliquando vocem extra naturalem vocem emittunt. £t iste praefatus morbus rarius ostenditur et difficile compesci potest. De epilepsia. Sed alii sunt alterius generis eiusdem morbi, ita quod inconstantes et leves in moribus suis sunt et impatientes, quorum anima, dum in ipsis supra modum his moribus fatigatur, se multotiens subtrabit et succumbit, et ita coipus velut viribus animae subtractis in terram cadit ac sic quasi mortuum iacet, usque dum iterum anima vires suas recipit.
text_translation: And those who are fatigued by this illness have an irascible look and face and an irascible movement. And when they fall to the ground, sometimes they emit a voice beyond natural voice. And this aforementioned disease rarely appears and is difficult to restrain. On epilepsy. But others are of another kind of the same disease, so that they are inconstant and light in their manners and impatient, whose soul, while in them beyond measure these manners fatigue, will often withdraw and succumb, and thus the body, as if the forces of the soul are subtracted, falls to the ground and thus lies as if dead, until the soul again receives its forces.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Isti autem blandam faciem et lenes gestus habent, et dum a morbo isto deiecti in terram ca dunt, interdum aliquam vocem, sed tamen lugubrem et naturalem, emittunt et multam spumam de ore eiciunt, sed tamen facile curari possunt. De hydropisi. Qui autem ex natura sua pingues in corpore non sunt, sed macilenti et tristes et magnis ac multis cogitationibus occupati, tunc tristitia, quae in ipsis est, sanguinem eorum exsiccat, et magnae ac multae cogitationes, quae etiam in eis sunt, flegma, quod in ipsis est, supra modum diminuunt, et hoc modo aqua in eis supercrescit et regnat.
text_translation: These, however, have a smooth face and gentle gestures, and while being cast down by this illness to the ground, they fall, and sometimes emit a voice, but it is mournful and natural, and they expel much foam from their mouth, but they can be easily cured. On hydropsy. Those, however, who are not naturally fat in body, but lean and sad and occupied with great and many thoughts, then the sadness that is in them dries up their blood, and the great and many thoughts that are also in them reduce the phlegm that is in them beyond measure, and in this way water in them overflows and reigns.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim sanguis et flegma in homine exa ruerint, tunc faeces digestionis suae ad vesicam in urina emittunt, et deinde, cum vesica calorem ex sanguine et flegmate non habet, urinam excoquere non potest, et ideo iam cmdam et non coctam non de recto loco itineris sui emittit, sed inter cutem et carnem extra naturam diffundit. 2e inflatione. Nam cum in hominibus istis sanguis fugatur et flegma diminuitur, aqua inter cutem et camem irmptiones suas facit et totum corpus inflat.
text_translation: When indeed blood and phlegm in man have flowed out, then the feces of his digestion are emitted into the bladder as urine, and afterwards, when the bladder has no heat from blood and phlegm, it cannot cook the urine, and therefore emits it raw and uncooked not from the proper place of its journey, but between skin and flesh, diffusing beyond nature. 2a inflatione. For when in these men blood flees and phlegm is diminished, water between skin and flesh makes its irruptions and the whole body swells.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quia sanguis eorum in ipsis aridus est et flegma exsiccatum, semper sitim habent , quoniam quidquid biberint nec ( ad sauguinem nec ad flegma vertitur, sed ad intercutaneam aquam. Et cum in homine illo aqua in cute lucet, ei medicina succurrendum est, quia si tunc diutius lO exspectatur, in tepidum humorem vertitur atque sanguineum colorem acquirit, qui tabe permixtus est, et ita homo periclitatur. Quidam vero homines, qui temperata corpora habent, ita quod in carne nec nimis pingues nec nimis macri sunt, temperatos etiam humores saepius in se habent et raro ab illo -^malo^, quod dicitur vich, fatigantur, quia humores, de quibus hoc malum nascitur, in eis non superhabundant.
text_translation: And because their blood in them is dry and their phlegm is dried up, they always have thirst, because whatever they drink is not turned into blood or phlegm, but into intercutaneous water. And when in that man the water in the skin shines, medicine must intervene, because if then it is waited upon longer, it turns into a warm humor and acquires a blood color, which is mixed with consumption, and thus the man is in danger. Indeed, certain men who have temperate bodies, so that in their flesh they are neither too fat nor too lean, also more often have temperate humors and rarely suffer from the evil called vich, because the humors from which this evil arises do not abound excessively in them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De tortionibus. Qui enim aut nimis pingues aut nimis macri sunt, malis humoribus saepe habundant, quoniam rectam institutionem et temperantiam in se non habent; unde aliquando de corde, de iecore, de pulmone, de stomacho et de visceribus eorum mali humores exsurgunt, qui ad melancoliam t«ndunt ac eam fumigare faciunt atque ita pessimum livorem in homine parant, yelut etiam litus aliquando in stanto et non fluente aqua putridus pulyis supercrescit et supernatat.
text_translation: On Tortions. Those who are either overly fat or overly thin often abound in bad humors, because they do not possess a proper regimen and temperance within themselves; whence, at times, bad humors arise from their heart, liver, lung, stomach, and viscera, which lead toward melancholy and cause it to fumigate, thus producing the worst blackness in a human being, just as also a shore sometimes in stagnant and non-flowing water, putrid dust grows and floats on top.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: £t livor iste aut iuxta stomachum aat iuxta viscera vel in alium locum inter cutem et camem cadit et ibi manet et hominem illum multa acerbitate torquet, velut eum ibi mordendo comedat. Sed tamen vitalem spiritum non habet ad mordendum hominem, sed tantum quandam acerbam amaritudinem; et velut oculi in eo apparent, atque in carne hominis iacet ut lens in came. AKquando etiam in longum extenditur, aliquando etiam in globositatem, ut vitellus ovi est, contrahitar atque interdum quandam spumam emittit, quae per totam corpus se diffondendo hominem dolere facit.
text_translation: And this blackness is either upon the stomach or near the viscera or in another place between the skin and flesh, and there it remains, and it torments that man with much bitterness, as if it were eating him there by biting. But nevertheless it does not have the vital spirit to bite the man, but only some bitter bitterness; and as eyes appear in it, and it lies in the flesh of man like a lens in flesh. When it also extends long, sometimes it becomes globular, like the yolk of an egg, and contracts, and sometimes emits some foam, which, spreading throughout the entire body, causes the man to suffer pain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e vermibus. Sed et cum eadem spuma stomachum aliquando pertransit, in eo quasdam vermes ebuUire faeit atque quosdam acerrimos et macros pediculos in came interdum crescere facit. Et ubi idem liyor in corporc hominis iacet, ibi etiam aliquando ex praedicta spuma gracillimi yermiculi, qui tarmi dicuntur, crescunt, yelut quidam vermiculi in aqua illa interdum nascuntur, quae in uno loco stat et non fluit. Et si tunc vermiculi isti in homine illo remanserint, ita quod ab eo non exierint, eum multum laedunt.
text_translation: De vermibus. Sed et cum eadem spuma stomachum aliquando pertransit, in eo quasdam vermes ebulire facit atque quosdam acerrimos et macros pediculos in carne interdum crescere facit. Et ubi idem lior in corpore hominis iacet, ibi etiam aliquando ex praedicta spuma gracillimi vermiculi, qui tarmi dicuntur, crescunt, velut quidam vermiculi in aqua illa interdum nascuntur, quae in uno loco stat et non fluit. Et si tunc vermiculi isti in homine illo remanserint, ita quod ab eo non exierint, eum multum laedunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De Itmbricis, De noxiis quoque et malis humoribus, qui in homine venenosi sunt et qui ut livor in homine se contrahunt et qui in nocivam longitudinem ut tepidum et putridum vinum vertuntur, vermes in homine facile nas cuntur, sed praecipue in infantibus et in pueris, quod humores eorum in ipsis aliquantum lacte permixti sunt. Sed vermes in homine a consuetudinalibus humoribus non nascuntur nec ab humoribus illis, qui acres sunt ut acetum, vel si tamen in hominibus istis, qui con suetudinales mores in se habent, aliquo modo crescere inceperint, cito deficient.
text_translation: On the skin, on the harmful and bad humors which in man are venomous and which contract like lividity in man and which turn into a noxious length as warm and putrid wine, worms are easily born in man, but especially in infants and children, because their humors are somewhat mixed with milk in them. But worms in man are not born from customary humors nor from those humors which are sharp as vinegar, or even if in those men who have customary manners within themselves they begin in some way to grow, they quickly become deficient.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si huiusmodi vermes in aliquo homine excreverint, eum debilem faciunt. De pediculis. Quidam autem homines sunt gracilia ossa et membra et graciles venas habentes, sed pingues et sanas carnes et bene temperatas, ita quod nec nimis clausae neo nimis apertae sunt; et plenam et bene in calore temperatam medullam tenent et ideo etiam plenum animum et potentem, sed aliquantulum in vanitate lascivum habent, et molles et delicati sunt. Et quia plenam medullam habent, ideo etiam spissam sed gracilem et albam ac sanam arvinam sine pediculis habent.
text_translation: If such worms are excreted in a man, they make him weak. Concerning lice. Some men, however, have slender bones and limbs and slender veins, but plump and healthy flesh and well-tempered, so that they are neither too closed nor too open; and they possess full and well-tempered marrow in heat, and therefore also a full and powerful spirit, but somewhat lustful in vanity, and soft and delicate. And because they have full marrow, they also have thick but slender, white, and healthy armpits without lice.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen dum eadem arvina interdum sudorem emittit, sudor ille paucos pediculos in cute exterius facit et nutrit. Sed et alii homines sunt, qui grossa ossa et grossa membra atque grossas venas habent sed macram et non multum ardentem meduUam, et ideo propter debilitatem medullae imprudentiores sunt ed edaciores et saepe esurientes, sed et per modicam horam fortiter laborare possunt, sed in labore durare non possnnt, quoniam caro eomm aliquantnm .
text_translation: But nevertheless, when the same arvina sometimes emits sweat, that sweat creates a few lice on the skin externally and nourishes them. But there are also other men who have thick bones and thick limbs and thick veins, but thin and not very hot marrow, and therefore, because of the weakness of the marrow, they are more imprudent and more voracious, and often hungry, but they can also labor strongly for a short time, but they cannot endure labor, because the flesh is somewhat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: aperta est et quasi tepida et venae eorum largius constrictae sunt. Et quoniam macram meduUam habent, arrina eorum tanto tenuior et debilior est. Qui cum interdum sudant, sudor ille cito cames hominis illius pertransit, quia aliquantum apertae sunt, ac ita plurimos pediculos in came parat, qui extra hominem illum immoderate ebulliunt. Et ideo homines isti non multum debiles sunt et aliquamdiu vivere possunt. Alii etiam homines sunt, (qui grossa ossa et membra et grossas venas habent et spissam ac pinguem medullam, ita quod ossa eomm sunt plena ardentis medullae, ac ideo de spissitudine et pinguedine ac de plenitudine medullae pradentes et probi sunt, et caro eorum fortis et aliquan- tulum dura et clausa, quia venis strictis constringitur.
text_translation: It is open and almost warm, and their veins are more constricted. And because they have marbled marrow, their skin is so much thinner and weaker. Those who sometimes sweat, that sweat quickly passes through the flesh of that man, because they are somewhat open, and thus prepares many lice in the flesh, which outside the man immoderately boil. And therefore these men are not very weak and can live for some time. Others are also men, (who have thick bones and limbs and thick veins and thick and fatty marrow, so that their bones are full of burning marrow, and therefore from the thickness and fatness and fullness of marrow they are prudent and good, and their flesh is strong and somewhat hard and closed, because it is constricted by tight veins.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam ut rete sibi invicem complexum est, sic etiam venae sibi invicem per totum corpus hominis complexae sunt. Et quoniam caro eorum aliquantulum fortis et dura est, modicum et tenuem sudorem emittimt, quia grossae etfortes venae cames 2o illorum ita constringunt, quod parcius sudorem emittunt. Tunc ex habundantia et calore meduUae et ex habundantia humorum, qui in illis sunt, quia erumpere non possunt, arvina, quae in eis est, aliquantulum in mborem sanguinis vertitur et infirma est et non sana, ita quod plurimi pediculi in ea cres- eunt, qui de carnibus illorum exire non possunt, sed in arvina illa remanent et eam ubique perforant et comedunt.
text_translation: Nam as a net entwines itself mutually, so also the veins entwine themselves mutually throughout the entire human body. And because their flesh is somewhat strong and hard, they emit a little and thin sweat, because the thick and strong veins of their flesh constrict so much that they emit sweat more sparingly. Then, from the abundance and heat of the marrow and from the abundance of the humors which are in them, since they cannot erupt, the arvina which is in them turns somewhat into the substance of blood and becomes weak and not healthy, so that many pediculi grow in it, which cannot exit from their flesh, but remain in that arvina and perforate it everywhere and eat it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde homines illi multum interius in corpore dolent nescientes, unde doleant, et tardi sunt in nullo delectati ac modicum comedunt et saepe deficiimt in corde et in viribus suis atque pallidum colorem in facie habent; qui color tamen plus viridis quam cerosus apparet. Et hi diu vivere non possunt, sed cito moriuntur, quia arvina eomm, ita ut praefatum est, a pediculis interius laeditur. De calculo. Homo iautem, qui in perfecta aetate est S et molies cames atque humidas in corpore suo habet et insuper diversis cibis et delicatis semper utitur atque ro busto et bono yino, facile calcalum sibi attrahit.
text_translation: Where those men greatly suffer within the body, not knowing why they suffer, and are slow in all things, not delighted at all, and eat little, and often become deficient in the heart and in their strength, and have a pale color on the face; this color, however, appears more greenish than waxy. And these cannot live long, but quickly die, because the arvina, as stated before, is injured internally by lice. On calculi. But a man who is in perfect age and has soft meats and moist ones in his body, and furthermore always uses various foods and delicacies and strong body and good wine, easily attracts calculi to himself.
