source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam de . praedictis cibis et potibus, cum etiam caro eius moUis et humida sit, quasi faex in loco illo, ubi urina ef&uere debet, coagulatur et in calculum durescit, sed tamen de calore et fortitudine urinae aliquantum tener est, dum adhuc infra hominem est, et ideo ille paulatim et niodicam urinam quamvis cum difficultate emittit. Si enim calculus infra hominem durus esset, homo cito periret. Sic est in muliere quam in viro, urina autem viri fortior est quam mulieris, sed tamen vir maiorem dolorem patitur quam mulier.
text_translation: For concerning the aforementioned foods and drinks, since the flesh is also soft and moist, it coagulates as if lees in that place where urine should effluere, and hardens into a stone; yet, from the heat and strength of the urine, it is somewhat soft while still within the body, and therefore he gradually emits a moderate amount of urine, although with difficulty. For if the stone were hard within the body, the man would quickly perish. Thus it is in a woman as in a man; however, the urine of a man is stronger than that of a woman, yet the man suffers greater pain than the woman.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod autem pueri aut iofantes de calculo stringuntur, hoc de malo et pravo lacte nutricum est, qno nutriti sunt vel nutriuntur. Nam cum nutrix infirma est vel cum diversis cibis et potibus et forti vino frequenter utitur, inde lac rectum saporem amittit et quasi foetidum erit, et tuDC lac istud foetidam coagulationem in loco effusionis urinae in puero vel in infanti facit et ita in calculum durescit. De gula. Homines autem, qui sani sunt et robusti, ita etiam quod eorum nervi fortes sunt et quod sunt ebriosi et gulosi studentes camibus et aliis deiicatis cibis et potibus, horum sanguis in cerosum colorem vertitur et deinde spissitudinem acquirit.
text_translation: That, however, children and infants are bound by calculi, this is from the bad and wrong milk of the nurses, by which they are nourished or are being nourished. For when the nurse is weak or frequently uses diverse foods and drinks and strong wine, thereby the milk loses its proper taste and becomes almost foul, and this foul milk causes a foul coagulation in the place of urine effusion in the child or infant, and thus hardens into a calculus. On gluttony. However, men who are healthy and strong, whose nerves are strong and who are drunken and gluttonous, students of camibus and other delightful foods and drinks, their blood turns to a waxy color and then acquires thickness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quia prae spissitudine rectum meatum habere non potest, quod etiam nec febri bus nec debilitate eorundem hominum, quoniam sani sunt, attenuatur, cames et cutes eorum pertransit et noxio humore inficit et quasi sordidas facit atque ulceribus replet. De ulceribus leprae. Sed et pauperes, qui sani sunt corpore et eandem naturam gulositatis habent , sed tamen delicatos cibos et potus assidue habere non possunt, tribus vel duabus septimanis aut una praedictam infiirmitatem sibi attrahere possunt, cum eosdem cibos et potus, quos habent, inordinate per gulam sumunt.
text_translation: And because, on account of thickness, it cannot have a straight passage, which is also not reduced by fever or weakness of those men, since they are healthy, the flesh and skin of them pass through and are infected by a noxious humor and are made almost filthy and filled with ulcers. On the ulcers of leprosy. But also the poor, who are healthy in body and have the same nature of gluttony, yet cannot consistently have delicate foods and drinks, may attract the aforementioned weakness to themselves in three or two weeks or even one week, since they take the same foods and drinks that they have in an excessive manner through gluttony.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic etiam de iuvenibus et pueris intelligi potest. Nam de camibus et diverso lacte et de forti vino haec infirmitas saepe crescit, non autem de pane nec de oleribus nec de cerevisia. De lepra, Sed et alii homines sunt spissam materiam carnis corporis habentes, qui iracundi sunt, et ira eorom commovet sanguinem, qui in ipsis est, ita quod circa iecur eomm cadit, et duritia ac sanguis eiusdem iecoris huic sangaini se commiscet, ac sic per totum corpus homini&^5 se difiundit et camem ac cutem eius confundit, ita quod outis illius scinditur et quod nares eius grossescunt et scindendo intumescunt.
text_translation: Thus also concerning young people and children it may be understood. For from foods and diverse milk and from strong wine this weakness often increases, not however from bread nor from oils nor from beer. Regarding leprosy, but also other men are those who have a thick consistency of flesh in their body, who are irritable, and their anger moves the blood which is in them, so that around the liver and in it falls, and the hardness and blood of the same liver mixes with this blood, and thus through the entire body of the man spreads and confuses his skin and flesh, so that his nose is split and his nose becomes thick and swells when split.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sunt et alii quidam homines de libidine incontinentes, ita quod nec continentiam habent nec eam habere quaerunt, unde sanguis eorum saepe et inordinate concutitur, velut olla ad ignem posita, quae nec ex toto fervet nec ex toto frigida est, et sic sordes in se retinet, quia tantas yires in se non habet, quod eas eiciat. Et cum homines isti sic in libidine ardent, ita qnod sanguis eorum frequenter inordinate concutitur, ita quod nec rectus sanguis est nec recta aqua nec recta spoma, tunc in- pravum liyorem et in tabem vertitur, et sic camem et cutem hominis corrumpit et in ulcera evertit.
text_translation: There are also certain individuals who are incontinent in lust, so that they neither possess continence nor seek to possess it, whence their blood is often agitated and in disorder, as a pot placed upon fire, which is neither entirely hot nor entirely cold, and thus retains filth within itself, because it does not possess such heat within itself as to expel them. And when these men burn thus in lust, so that their blood is frequently agitated in disorder, so that neither the blood nor the water nor the sperm is straight, then it turns into an evil humor and into decay, and thus corrupts the flesh and the skin of man and inverts it into ulcers.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De l^ae signis. Infirmitates autem istae sic dis- cemuntur. Lepra, quae ex gula et ebrietate est, mbeos tnmores et rubeas ebuUitiones emittit similes dragunculae. £t quae de iecore est, scissiones et nigredines in cute et in came hominis facit ad ossa. Sed quae de libidine est, latas snperficies ulcerum parat, velut cortices, et snb eis carnem mbeam. Et duae priores difficile sanari possunt, teitia vero facile emendatur. 2e gtUta. Homines, qui molles et perforatas cames habent et forti vino in superflnis potationibus insudant, saepe de peste, quae dicitur gutta, concutinntur.
text_translation: De lae signis. Infirmitates autem istae sic discernuntur. Lepra, quae ex gula et ebrietate est, multos tumores et rubeas ebullitiones emittit similes dragunculae. Et quae de iecore est, scissiones et nigredines in cute et in carne hominis facit ad ossa. Sed quae de libidine est, latas superficies ulcerum parat, velut cortices, et sub eis carnem mbram. Et duae priores difficile sanari possunt, tertia vero facile emendatur. De gutta. Homines, qui molles et perforatas carnes habent et forti vino in superfluis potationibus insudant, saepe de peste, quae dicitur gutta, concutinntur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam qni moUes cames habent, de nimiis potationibus mali hu mores, qui in eis sunt, in aliquod membmm eorum repente cadnnt et illud dissipant, velut ignita iacula et velut magnae et repentinae innndationes, quae interdum molendina et alia quaedam sibi proxima aedificia dissipant. Sic et isti humores membra illa, super quae cadunt, dirumperent, si eos nec gratia dei nec vitalis spiritus, qni in homine Hildeg»rdi cftusae et carae ed. Kaiser. HILDE6ABDIS est, prohibet.
text_translation: Nam qui mollis cames habent, de nimiis potationibus mali humores, qui in eis sunt, in aliquod membrum eorum repente cadunt et illud dissipant, velut ignita iacula et velut magnae et repentinae inundationes, quae interdum molendina et alia quaedam sibi proxima aedificia dissipant. Sic et isti humores membra illa, super quae cadunt, dirumperent, si eos nec gratia dei nec vitalis spiritus, qui in homine Hildegardi cuius et cara ed. Kaiser. HILDE6ABDIS est, prohibet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen quaedam membra dirumpnnt et qoaedam etiam velat mortna inutilia facinnt. De nervorum coniradione, Saepe contingit, qnod tempestates et procellae malorum hnmomm in aliqnod membrum hominis cadunt et ibi insania sua meatum sanguinis venarum eius claudunt, ita quod ille ibi in venis inundare non potest, unde et venae illae arescunt, quod meatu sanguinis carent. £t sic homo ille claudicare incipit. Et quia homo ab elementis factus est, ab elementis etiam snstentatur et in eis ac cum eis conversatur.
text_translation: But nevertheless, certain limbs break, and certain ones also make dead, useless things. Concerning the constriction of nerves, It often happens that tempests and storms of evils fall upon some part of a man, and there, by their insanity, they close the passage of his veins' blood, so that he cannot there flood the veins, whence those veins wither, lacking the passage of blood. And thus that man begins to limp. And because man is made from elements, he is also sustained by elements, and converses in them and with them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De febribus. Unde etiam diversos febres a diversis qualitatibus aeris et ceterorum elementorum in se babet, scilicet a calore, a frigore et ab humore, quae eum non deiciunt nec ei detrimentum faciunt, sed quae potius ei sanitatem inferunt, cum et pectus et stomachnm atque omnia interiora viscera eius per sudorem et per urinam purgant, cum modum suum non excedxmt, cum et qualitas aeris rectum modum in temperie servat. 3ed et cum nimius et inconsuetus ardor aeris surrexerit, tunc ali quando febres istae supra modum inardescentes in acutam febrem in quibusdam hominibus vertuntur, in nimio autem frigore aeris ita frigiditate constringuntur, quod tertianam febrem excitant; in humida vero qualitate aeris, quae et aquosa et foetida est, multotiens in tam livosam coagu lationem vertuntur, quod ex illa quartanam febrem quibusdam hominibus inferunt.
text_translation: On Fevers. Hence also diverse fevers arise from diverse qualities of air and other elements within them, namely from heat, from cold, and from humidity, which do not cast them down nor cause them harm, but rather bring them health, when both the chest and the stomach and all the inner viscera are purged through sweat and through urine, when they do not exceed their own measure, when also the quality of air preserves the proper mode of temperance. And when excessive and unusual heat of air arises, then sometimes these fevers, intensifying beyond measure, turn into acute fever in some men; but in excessive cold of air they are so constricted by coldness that they provoke tertian fever; in humid quality of air, which is both watery and foul, they often turn into such a livid coagulation, from which quartan fever is inflicted upon some men.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Homines autem, qui corpore sani sunt, ita quod mobiles humores, scilicet qui hac et iUac moveri possunt, in se non habent, in intemperata qualitate aeris aliquando infirmantur, cum recte temperata et tranquilla qualitate aeris sani sunt. Quod si isti acutam febrem aliquando habuerint, tunc si gratia dei voluerit, ut convalescant, ad quintam vel ad septimam diem cum dolore sudorem eiciunt et cito convalescunt, quia prius etiam sani in corpore fuerunt.
text_translation: Indeed, those people who are healthy in body, in that they do not have within themselves the mobile humors, namely those that can be moved here and there, are sometimes weakened by the untoward quality of air, when they are healthy with a properly tempered and tranquil quality of air. Indeed, if these people sometimes have an acute fever, then if by the grace of God it wills, so that they may recover, on the fifth or seventh day they sweat with pain and quickly recover, because previously they were also healthy in body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De paralysi. Qui vero mobiles humores in se habent, videiicet quod nullam stabilitatem rectitudinis nec in calore nec in frigore nec in humiditate tenent, sed qui hac et illac secmidam instabilem qualitatem aeris in corporibus hominnm proiciantar, at qaaedam flamina et andae aqaaram, qaae malta loca deyastant, et at glacies, qaae nec ad perfectam congelata sed qaae fragilis est: hi paralysi, id est gatta, ab humoribas istis fatigantar. Et si etiam aliqaando acatam febrem habaerint, ita qaod etiam per gratiam dei vita eoram protrahetar, tanc maltotiens, anteqaam sadorem cam dolore eiciant, aat ad yicesimam aat ad tricesimam yel eo amplias diem in dolore iacebant, qaod etiam prias freqaenter debiles et infirmi fa- lo nmt.
text_translation: De paralysi. Qui vero mobiles humores in se habent, videlicet quod nullam stabilitatem rectitudinis nec in calore nec in frigore nec in humiditate tenent, sed qui hac et illac secundam instabilem qualitatem aeris in corporibus hominum proiciantur, at quaedam flamina et aeris aquaram, quae mala loca destruunt, et at glacies, quae nec ad perfectam congelata sed quae fragilis est: hi paralysi, id est gutta, ab humoribus istis fatigantur. Et si etiam aliquando acutam febrem habuerint, ita quod etiam per gratiam dei vita eorum protrahetur, tunc multotiens, antequam sudorem cum dolore eiciant, aut ad vicesimam aut ad tricesimam vel eo amplius diem in dolore iacebant, quod etiam prius frequenter debiles et infirmi fa- lo nmt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cam enim aliquis homo de laboribas et angastiis ac de diversis qaalitatibas ciboram et potuam (infirmatar , ita qaod etiam de diversis qualitatibas ciboram et potuam diversi hamores et liyores in eo coadanantar, tanc anima in eo his diversitatibas qaassata et fatigata sac- cambit, ita qaod vitales motas saos aliqaantam subtrahit. De febribus et creticis diebus. Et sic nocivi hamores, qoi in homine sant, commoventur et febres accrescant, qoia anima vitales motas saos contraxit.
text_translation: For indeed, someone, because of labors and anguishes and because of the diverse qualities of foods and drinks, is weakened, so that even from the diverse qualities of foods and drinks, diverse humors and lymphs are coagulated within him, so that the soul in him is thus afflicted and fatigued, so that it subtracts some of the vital motions of the soul. Concerning fevers and critical days. And thus the harmful humors, which are in man, are moved and fevers increase, because the soul has contracted the vital motions of the soul.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tanc et sangnis in homine decrescit atqae viscera et alia interiora eias arescant. Et calor, qai in iecore et in reliqais interioribos esse debait ad vitom, ad exteriorem cutem ascendit, et frigas interins in homine permanet interins. Et sic anima iacet oppressa in corpore exspectans in dabio, atram exitura sit corpas an in eo permansara, et hoc saepissime asque ad septem dies facit, qaoniam de humoribus et livoribus illis se nondum excutere potuit.
text_translation: Tum et sanguis in homine decrescit et viscera et alia interiora eius arescant. Et calor, qui in iecore et in reliquiis interioribus esse debet ad vitam, ad exteriorem cutem ascendit, et frigus internum in homine permanet internum. Et sic anima iacet oppressa in corpore exspectans in dubio, an atram exitura sit corpus an in eo permansura, et hoc saepissime usque ad septem dies facit, quoniam de humoribus et livoribus illis se nondum excutere potuit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quae dam senserit, quod tempestates humorom illorum per gratiam dei aliqaantam cessare incipiunt, tunc intelligit, quod se de ipsis excutere potest, et ita vires suas ad se do colligit atque homores et livores illos de eodem corpore per sudorem expellit. Et sic homo ille sanitatem recipit. Multotiens antem contingit, quod anima prae nimio ardore et frigore eomndem humorum eos cum sudore pleniter ezpellere non potest, et sic etiam aut laetitia aut tristitia aat ira aat aogastiis hominis illius cum timore concussa se sabtrahit et iterum ita in silentio manet et aliquando 11 etiam usque ad tertiam vel quintam yel septimam vel tricesimam lacem vel ad plures vel ad panciores dies, ut praedictom est, dum iterum sentiat, quod per gratiam dei vires ad refocillandum corpus recipere possit.
text_translation: When the body senses that, by the grace of God, the tempests of their humors begin to cease, then it understands that it can cast itself free from them, and thus gathers its forces back to itself and expels those humors and lividities from the same body through sweat. And thus that man receives health. But often it happens that the soul, because of excessive heat and cold, cannot fully expel those same humors with sweat, and thus also either joy or sorrow or anger or the affections of that man, struck by fear, withdraws from itself and again remains thus in silence, and sometimes even up to the third or fifth or seventh or thirtieth or more or even to many days, as was previously said, until again it senses that, by the grace of God, it can receive forces to restore the body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et si homo iUe ad sanitatem corporis perventurus est, tunc postea lenius dolebit, quam prius fedsset, quoniam humores, qui in eo prius fnerunt, per priorem sudorem aliquantam modo minorati sunt. Quod si anima tantis humoribus et livoribus irretita est, quod eos de corpore hominis illius excutere non valet, ita quod nec gratiam dei sibi ad hoc adesse senserit, victa succumbit et per iudicium dei corpus deserit. Be acuta. Gum ergo homo acutam habet, humores, qui in eo sunt, in multum fervorem ardoris mutati sunt, et fervor eorundem humorum hominem illum comedere non permittit.
text_translation: And if that man is to arrive at the health of the body, then afterward he will suffer more gently than he previously was, because the humors which were in him before have been somewhat lessened by the prior sweat. If, however, the soul is so entangled with these humors and discolorations that it cannot expel them from the body of that man, so that it has not sensed the grace of God to be present for this purpose, it is conquered and succumbs, and by the judgment of God abandons the body. Be acute. Since, therefore, a man has acuteness, the humors within him are changed into great heat of ardor, and the heat of those humors does not permit that man to be consumed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed prae nimia siccitate, quae in ipso est, eum ad bibendum compellit, unde aquam bibat, ut lenius doleat. Postquam autem acuta febris hominem invaserit, non est ei salubre, ut medicinam accipiat ad depellendum eam, quod ipsum tunc usque ad sudorem pleniter non dimittit. Sed pro accepta medicina tanto diutius in eo latet et eum tanto diutius infirmari facit, quoniam noxii humores ab illo aperte et, ut iuste debent, expulsi non sunt. Febres autem interdum aut de superfluis cibis et potibus oriuntur aut de nimia dormitione aut de taedio et otiositate, cum homo non operatur.
text_translation: But because of excessive dryness, which is within him, it compels him to drink, so that he may drink water, so that he may feel less pain. However, after an acute fever has invaded a man, it is not healthy for him to receive medicine to drive it away, because then it does not allow him to sweat fully. But instead of receiving medicine, it lingers within him all the longer and makes him grow weaker all the longer, because the harmful humors have not been expelled from him openly and, as they justly should, have not been expelled. Fevers, however, sometimes arise either from superfluous foods and drinks or from excessive sleeping or from weariness and idleness, when a man does not work.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De febribus mterpolaUs, Gotidiana etenim febris de superfluitate aquae, scilicet flegmatis, quod est spumae, oritur et de debilibus cibis et de superfluitate potus vini. Febris autem, quae secunda die hominem fatigat, de superfluitate et de debilitate aeris, quod est humidi, nascitur. Tertiana vero de superfluitate sicci, quod est ignis, crescit, sed quartana de superfluitate melancoliae surgit. Sed et homo, qui molles cames in corpore suo habet, ita quod etiam spuma, quae tenax et tepida ac debilis est, in eo superhabundat, facile vermes in came sua contrahit.
text_translation: On intermittent fevers. Indeed, the daily fever arises from the excess of water, that is, phlegm, which is foam, and from weak foods and the excess of wine drinking. However, the fever which fatigues a man on the second day arises from the excess and weakness of air, which is humidity. The tertian fever, however, increases from the excess of dryness, which is fire, but the quartan fever arises from the excess of melancholy. Yet also a man who has soft flesh in his body, so that even foam, which is tenacious and warm and weak, abounds excessively in him, easily contracts worms in his flesh.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quod caro eius mollis est et spuma in eo debilis et tenax. pnstulae in eo facile ebuUiunt, in quibns vermis facile nasdtnr et hominem laedit. De diaeta, Nunc autem sicut caseo, qui in Yasculo suo premitur, coagulatio lactis semper superadditur, quousque perficiatur, sic etiam infanti et puero cibus et potus assidue subministrandus est, usque dum ad perfectam aetatem perducatur; alioquin infans et puer crescere non possent, sed perirent. Sed et seni ac decrepito cibus et potus adhibendus est, quod cum sanguis et caro in illo minuitur, his cibariis sublevandus est.
text_translation: For the flesh of it is soft and the foam in it is weak and tenacious. Pustules in it easily boil up, in which worms easily are born and injure the man. Concerning diet: Now, just as cheese, which in its own vessel is pressed, the coagulation of milk is always added upon, until it is perfected, so also food and drink must be administered to the infant and child assiduously, until they are led to perfect age; otherwise, the infant and child could not grow, but would die. But also to the old man and the decrepit, food and drink must be provided, for when blood and flesh in him diminish, he must be elevated by these foods.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Homo enim est ut lo terra. Si terra nimiam humiditatem habuerit, inde laesionem incurrit; si autem modica vel nulla humiditate tacta faerit, ei ad prosperitatem non valebit. Quod si discretam hnmiditatem habuerit, bonum ei est. Sic est in homine. Si homo multam et nimiam ac fiuentem humiditatem in se patitur, scilicet in oculis, in auribus, in naribus, in ore, inde magis laeditur quam sanetur. Si autem modicam vel nuUam effiuentem humiditatem in his nfembris habuerit, periculosum est ei; si yero discretam humiditatem habuerit, ad sanitatem illi ' est.
text_translation: Man is indeed like the earth. If the earth has excessive humidity, it incurs damage; but if it is touched with little or no humidity, it will not be able to prosper. If it has moderate humidity, it is good for it. Thus it is in man. If man suffers excessive and flowing humidity within himself, namely in the eyes, in the ears, in the nose, in the mouth, he is more harmed than healed. But if he has little or no flowing humidity in these members, it is dangerous for him; if he has moderate humidity, it is good for his health.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: in. De praedidis aiUem infirmitatibtis subscriptae medidnae a deo monstratae aut hominem liberabunt aut ipse morietur aut deus eum liberari non vult. De capUlorum casu, Oum adolescenti homini iam pri- mum cnnes cadere incipiunt, ( accipiat de arvina ursi et modicum fayillarum de triticeo aut de sUigineo stramine factarum, et haec simul commisceat et deinde cum isto totum caput suum inungat et ibi praecipue, ubi crines in capite suo effluere incipiunt.
text_translation: In. De praedidis autem infirmitatibus subscriptae medicinae a deo monstratae aut hominem liberabunt aut ipse morietur aut deus eum liberari non vult. De capillorum casu, Cum adolescenti homini iam primo cnes cadere incipiunt, (accipiat de arvina ursi et modicum fayllarum de triticeo aut de sugineo stramine factarum, et haec simul commisceat et deinde cum isto totum caput suum inungat et ibi praecipue, ubi crines in capite suo effluere incipiunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea diu abstineat se, ne caput suum ab ista unctione lavet. £t crines eius, qui nondum cecidemnt, per unctionem istam ita humectantur et confortantur, quod per longa tempora non cadent. Et 1 V. 22—24 « p. 184,83—186,2. sio saepe faciat et a layatione capitis sui se abstineat. Nam calor arvinae ursi natarae huias est, quod plnriinos crines producere solet, et favillae de triticeo aut de silig^eo stramine crines confortant, ne cito cadant. £t com b haec sibi contemperantur, ut praedictum est, crines hominis tanto diutius retinent, ne cadant.
text_translation: Afterwards, he should abstain for a long time, so that he does not wash his head with that anointing. And his hair, which have not yet fallen, are so moistened and strengthened by that anointing that they will not fall for a long time. And he should often do this and abstain from washing his head. For the heat of the bear's nature is such that it usually produces many hairs, and ashes from wheat or from silica straw strengthen the hairs, so that they do not fall quickly. And with these things, as previously stated, the hair of man retains for so long as not to fall.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De capitis dolare ex mekmcolia. Si diversis febribus melancolia tacta cerebrum hominis dolere facit, ille malvam accipiat et bis tantum salviae et ad stibcom in morta rio tundat et haec modico hoimoleo aspergat, vel si olemn hoc non habuerit, aceto ea modice aspergat, et sic a fronte per verticem usque ad occiput suum ponat et panno insimul liget et hoc per triduum faciat. Ac in his tribus diebus circa noctem aut houmoleo aut aceto renovabit et insuper sic faciat, usque dum melius habeat.
text_translation: On headache from melancholy. If touched by various fevers, melancholy causes the human brain to ache; let him take mallow and twice as much sage, and grind them in a mortar with antimony, and sprinkle this with a little oil, or if he does not have oil, sprinkle it with vinegar, and thus place it from the forehead through the crown down to his own occiput, and bind it with a cloth, and do this for three days. And during these three days, around night, either with oil or vinegar renew it, and furthermore do this until he feels better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam succus malvae melancoliam dissolvit, succus autem salviae eam exsiccat, botmoleim vero caput hoc modo fatigatum unguit, acetum autem acumen melancoliae extrahit. Unde haec simul temperata dolorem hunc capitis mitigant. ao De amentia. Si alicui cerebrum infrigidatum est, ita quod inde amens efficitur, accipe baccas lauri et eas in pulverem redige et tunc accipe farinam similae et pulverem istum baccarum ei commisce et sic cum aqua cniOi et crinibus capitis illius abrasis deich istum super totum caput illius pone et desuper cum pilleo de viltro facto constringe, quatinus caput eius interius incalescat, et ut ita obdormiat; et calorem cerebro confert.
text_translation: Indeed, the juice of mallows dissolves melancholy, the juice of sage dries it, balm indeed anoints the head thus fatigued, vinegar however extracts the sharpness of melancholy. Therefore, these tempered together mitigate this headache. Ad De amentia. If someone's brain has become chilled, so that from this they become mad, take laurel berries and reduce them to powder, then take flour of similar and mix that powder of the berries with it, and thus with water and the hair of his head abraded, place that over the entire head of him and compress it from above with a pill made of glass, so that his head internally warms up, and so that he thus falls asleep; and it brings heat to the brain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum deich iste exsiccatur, iterum alium eodem modo para et capiti illius superpone, et sic saepe fac, et ille sensus suos recipiet. so JDe emigrmea. Qui emigraneam patitur, aloe et bis tantum mjrrae accipiat et haec in minutissimum pulverem redigat ac deinde farinam similae toUat et his oleum papaveris addat et sic massam, scilicet quasi fermentum, faciat et cum hoc fermento totum caput usque ad aures et usque ad coUum tegat pilleo desuper posito et sic per triduum nocte ac die illud super caput suum ponat.
text_translation: But when this one dries up, prepare another in the same manner and place it upon his head, and thus do this often, and he will regain his senses. Concerning emigranea. He who suffers from emigranea, let him take aloes and twice as much myrrh, and reduce this to the finest powder, and then take flour of wheat and mix with this oil of poppy, and thus make a mass, namely like leaven, and with this leaven cover entirely the head down to the ears and down to the neck with a poultice placed over it, and thus place it upon his head day and night for three days.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Calor enim aloae et siccitas myrrae cum suavitate fariCAUSAE ET LIB. III. nae similae et frigiditate olei papavens temperatus honc dolorem capitis sedat, et fermentum hoc modo factum cerebro pingaedinem reddit. De capiHs dolore ex stomachi fufnositate. Com cibus, qoi madidum succum habet, hominem in capite dolere & facit, ille salviam et origanum ac feniculum aequali pondere accipiat et marrubii plus quam horum omnium sit, et ad succum contritis butyrum sufficienter addat; vel si illud non habuerit, arvina addita ex his unguentum faciat atque cum hoc oaput inungat, et melius habebit.
text_translation: Heat, indeed, of aloes and the dryness of myrrh, with the sweetness of farinae causae and the coldness of poppy oil, tempered thus, allays this headache, and this fermentation made in this way renders the brain fat. Concerning headache arising from the smokiness of the stomach. When food, which has moist juice, causes a man to suffer pain in the head, let him take equal weight of salviam and origanum and fenniculum, and let marubii be more than all of these together, and add sufficient boiled butter to the juice; or if he does not have that, let him make an ointment by adding arvina from these and anoint his head with it, and he will be better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam salvia et origanum ac marrubium siccae naturae sunt et ideo praedictos succos exsiccant; succus autem feniculi huoiidus est et hoc siccos suocos temperat. Unde ex his et butyro aut arvina, quae sana sunt, uDguento facto praefatum dolorem capitis alleviant. f Accipe oleum olivae et ^ modicum minus rosatae aquae et haec in sartagine fac fervere, et interim accipe nathscaden bene modicum minus quam olei olivae sit et haec simul in mortario tere et per pannum cola et succum istum in sartagine ad praedictum oleum et ad rosatam aquam funde et fac simul ^ denuo fervere.
text_translation: Nam salvia et origanum ac marrubium siccae naturae sunt et ideo praedictos succos exsiccant; succus autem feniculi huilidus est et hoc siccos suocos temperat. Unde ex his et butyro aut arvina, quae sana sunt, unguento facto praefatum dolorem capitis alleviant. Accipe oleum olivae et modicum minus rosatae aquae et haec in sartagine fac fervere, et interim accipe nathscaden bene modicum minus quam olei olivae sit et haec simul in mortario tere et per pannum cola et succum istum in sartagine ad praedictum oleum et ad rosatam aquam funde et fac simul denuo fervere.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod cum feceris, iterum per pannum eola et in novum vas fictile fonde. Deinde dolentem in vertice et in sua schedelim et in fronte ac in temporibus Gum hoc unguento, quando dolet, unge, atque frontem et tempora eias lineo ligamine cerato circumliga, ne unguentum istud abstergatur. De capitis dolare ex fkgmate. Qui de superfluo flegmate in fronte capitis dolet, pisam albam dentibus mastieando terat et hanc tritam purissimo melle contemperet et sic temporibus apponat et ligamine comprimat et sic faciat, usque dum melius habeat.
text_translation: When you have done this, again through the cloth eola and into a new earthen vessel pour. Then, the one suffering in the vertex and in his skull and in the forehead and in the temples, with this ointment, when it aches, anoint, and with a linen bandage of waxed thread encircle the forehead and temples of him, so that this ointment is not wiped away. On the headache from phlegm. He who suffers from excessive phlegm in the forehead of the head, let him crush white pepper with teeth and mix this crushed with purest honey and thus apply it at times and compress with a bandage and do so thus, until he has it better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Pisa enim aliquantulum flegmatica est, sed alba in pura et bona terra orescit atque ad succum masticata et temporibus apposita, quoniam venae temporum fortitudinem frontis tenent, acumen doloriB huius aufert. Calor quoque mellis, quod de diversis 9& 1 V. 27—31 = Migne 1132 A. floribas collectum est, frigus flegmatis temperat, nt praedictnm est. Ea qme seqwmtur ad viHum pulmonis spedanC, Accipe gakm et feniculum aequali pondere et bis tan tum de nuce muscata et tantum de bertram ut istorum duorum, ita sane ut nux muscata et bertram etiam aequalis ponderis sint, et baec pulveriza et tunc simul commisce et de pulyere isto ad pondus duorum nummorum cum tenui buccella panis cotidie ieixmus comede et statim modicum calidi vini bibe et etiam alias nobiles herbas, quae bonum odorem habent, et eas tam ieiunus quam pransus comede firequenter, ut bonus odor earum ad pulmonem transeat et foetentem halitum compescat.
text_translation: Pisa enim aliquantulum flegmatica est, sed alba in pura et bona terra orescit atque ad succum masticata et temporibus apposita, quoniam venae temporum fortitudinem frontis tenent, acumen dolori huius aufert. Calor quoque mellis, quod de diversis 9& 1 V. 27—31 = Migne 1132 A. floribus collectum est, frigus flegmatis temperat, ut praedictum est. Ea quae sequuntur ad vium pulmonis spedanC, Accipe gaulm et feniculum aequali pondere et bis tantum de nuce muscata et tantum de bertram ut istorum duorum, ita sane ut nux muscata et bertram etiam aequalis ponderis sint, et haec pulveriza et tunc simul commisce et de pulvere isto ad pondus duorum nummorum cum tenui buccella panis cotidie ieiunus comede et statim modicum calidi vini bibe et etiam alias nobiles herbas, quae bonum odorem habent, et eas tam ieiunus quam pransus comede frequenter, ut bonus odor earum ad pulmonem transeat et foetentem halitum compescat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui yero quolibet modo in pulmone dolet, pingues cames devitet et cibum, qui multo sanguine perfusus est, et coctum caseum, quia tabem circa pubnonem faciunt. Sed et pisam et lentem et cruda poma et cruda olera non comedat et nuces et oleum devitet; quod si carnes comedere vult, macras comedat, et si caseum comedit, non eoctum nec crudum, sed aridum edat, et si oleum comedere voluerit, modice comedat, aquam vero non bibat, quia circa pulmonem livorem et slim parat.
text_translation: Whoever, in any manner, suffers pain in the lung, should avoid fatty meats and food which is very bloody, and cooked cheese, because they cause decay around the lung. But also do not eat peas and lentils and raw fruit and raw vegetables, and avoid nuts and oil; if he wishes to eat meat, let him eat lean meat, and if he eats cheese, let him not eat boiled nor raw, but dry, and if he wishes to eat oil, let him eat it moderately, but truly do not drink water, because around the lung it prepares yellow bile and phlegm.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et novum et crudum mustnm, quod nondum in fervore ebulliendo sordes eiecit, non bibat; cerevisia autem eum non multum laedit, quod cocta est; vinum vero non bibat, ab humida autem et nebulosa aura se observet. De amentia. Si multis et divei^is cogitationibus scientia et sensus cuiuspiam hominis evacuantur, ita quod ille in amentiam vertitur, hun accipiat et ter tantum feniculi et so in aqua simul coquat et abiectis herbulis istis eandem aquam infrigidatam frequenter bibat.
text_translation: But also new and raw mustum, which has not yet expelled impurities through boiling heat, should not be drunk; however, beer does not greatly harm, because it is cooked; indeed, wine should not be drunk, but he should guard himself from humid and misty air. On madness. If knowledge and senses of a man are evacuated by many and diverse thoughts, so that he turns into madness, let him take and three times as much fennel and let him cook it with water together, and after discarding those herbs, let him frequently drink the same water cooled.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Succus enim hun malos humores constringit et retinet, ne modum suuoi in petulantia excedant, et hominem ad sensus suos reducit; succus autem feniculi ad rectum temperamentum laetitiae eos pertrahit. Et herbulae istae simul temperatae et suavi i V. 4-26 = Migne 1134 A— C. — ' v. 27—31 = Migne 1202 D— 1203 A. aqna ooctae hominem ad intellectum suom redire facinnt. Homo antem idem aridos cibos devitet, qnoniam hnmores, qni in eo destitnti snnt, in maiorem ariditatem amentiae dncerent; bonos vero et delicatos cibos comedat, qni ei bonnm snccnm in sanguine snbministrant et qni humores § suos ad rectitudinem suam redncunt et sensns einsdem hominis ab amentia evertnnt, Sed et pnltes similae aut butyro aut sagimine, non autem oleo factas comedat, quod inanitnm cerebmm replent et infrigidatum calefaciunt; oleum antem flegma attraheret, et ideo illud devitet; et lo vinum non bibat, quod dissipatos humores in eo magis et magis dissiparet; nec etiam medonem bibat, quoniam fortitndo mellis eosdem destitutos humores in eo magis destmeret.
text_translation: Succus enim humores malos constringit et retinet, ne modum suum in petulantia excedant, et hominem ad sensus suos reducit; succus autem feniculi ad rectum temperamentum laetitiae eos pertrahit. Et herbulae istae simul temperatae et suavi i V. 4-26 = Migne 1134 A— C. — ' v. 27—31 = Migne 1202 D— 1203 A. aqua octae hominem ad intellectum suum redire faciant. Homo autem idem aridos cibos devitet, quoniam humores, qui in eo destituti sunt, in maiorem ariditatem amentiae ducant; bonos vero et delicatos cibos comedat, qui ei bonum succum in sanguine subministrant et qui humores suos ad rectitudinem suam reducunt et sensus eiusdem hominis ab amentia everturnt, Sed et pulveres similae aut butyro aut sagumine, non autem oleo factas comedat, quod inanitum cerebrum replent et infrigidatum calefaciunt; oleum autem flegma attraheret, et ideo illud devitet; et lo vinum non bibat, quod dissipatos humores in eo magis et magis dissiparet; nec etiam medonem bibat, quoniam fortitudo mellis eosdem destitutos humores in eo magis detereret.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed nec aquam simplicem bibat, qnod sensns ipsins in maiorem inanitatem dednceret. Praedictum antem potum et cereyisiam bibat, quae destitutos humores et sensus ipsius in rectitudinem continent et furorem amentiae ab ipso eyertunt. Oaput autem suum pilleo ant de viltro ant de pnra laua facto operiat, quatinus a destitntis humoribus infrigidatum cerebmm leniter et panlatim ^ incalescat, non antem repente nec supra modum, ne pro repentino et nimio calore deterius habeat.
text_translation: But neither should he drink simple water, which would lead the senses themselves to greater emptiness. Let him drink the aforementioned drink and beer, which contain the deprived humors and senses of himself into rectitude and expel the fury of madness from him. But let him cover his head with a pillow made of glass or of pure wool, so that the brain, chilled by the deprived humors, may gradually and gradually warm up, not suddenly nor excessively, lest he suffer worse from the sudden and excessive heat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: f Accipe nncem muscatam et bis tantum de galan et haec in pulverem redige et etiam accipe radicem stierthelum et radicem plantaginis aequali pondere, ita tamen, quod pondus isto- ^ mm duorum sit minns qnam ponderis nncis muscatae, 6t modice tnnde sale addito. Et ex his onmibns cnm farina similae et cum aqua tennem sobriunculam fac et da dolenti ad sorbendnm. De ocuiorvm aUemuUione. Si sanguis et aqua in ocn- lis hominis aut prae senectute aut prae aliqua infirmitate supra modnm attenuantur, vadat ad viride gramen et illnd tam din inspiciat, dnm ocnli eius velut lacrimando madefiant, qnia viriditas graminis illius hoc, quod in oculis eins turbidnm est, aufert et eos puros et lucidos facit.
text_translation: Take now the nutmeg and twice as much of galan, and reduce this to powder; also take the root of stierthelum and the root of plantaginis in equal weight, however, so that the weight of these two is less than the weight of the nutmeg, and modestly add salt from there. And from all these things with the flour of millet and with warm water make a thin porridge and give it to the sufferer to drink. Concerning the alteration of the eyes. If blood and water in the eyes of a man, either due to old age or due to some infirmity, are excessively attenuated, let him go to green grass and let him look at it so much that his eyes become moist as if weeping, because the greenness of that grass removes what is turbid in his eyes and makes them pure and clear.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 1 V. 2—21 = Migne 1208 A. — 2 v. 30—32 = Migne 1211 A. HILDE6ARDIS S6d et ad flamen yadat aut novain aquam in aliqaod yas fandat et desuper inclinans homorem eiusdem aqoae oculis excipiat, et sic humor ille aquam oculorum suorum, quae iam in ipsis arescit, excitat et eos lucidos facit. Lineum quoque pannum accipiat et illum in pura et £rigida aqua intinguat ac eum sic temporibus et oculis suis cireumponens ligat, cavens, ne oculos interius tangat, ne ab aqua exulcerentur.
text_translation: Hildegardis sed et ad flamen dabit aut novam aquam in aliquod vas fundat et desuper inclinans humorem eiusdem aquae oculis excipiat, et sic humor ille aquam oculorum suorum, quae iam in ipsis arescit, excitat et eos lucidos facit. Lineum quoque pannum accipiat et illum in pura et rigida aqua intinguat ac eum sic temporibus et oculis suis circumponens ligat, cavens, ne oculos interius tangat, ne ab aqua exulcerentur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ideo autem lineo panno, quod suave est, oculos made&cies et frigida aqua, quatinus aqua oculorum per aquam istam ad visum resuscitetur. Quia enim oculi ignei sunt, de igne pellicula oculorum exspissatur, et cum aqua, ut praedictum est, tangitur, eadem pellicula per frigiditatem et humiditatem aquae attenuatur. De oculis griseis. Gum aliquis griseos oculos habens in eis aliquo modo caligat et dolet, cum dolor ille adhuc recens est, feniculum vel semen eius accipiat et terat et sic sumat succum eius et rorem, quem super recto gramine invenerit, et modicum farinulae similae et haec ut tortellum commisceat atque in nocte oculis suis super ponat et panno liget, et melius habebit.
text_translation: Therefore, however, I anoint the eyes with a soft cloth, with something sweet and cold water, so that the water of the eyes may be revived through that water to vision. For indeed the eyes are fiery, and from fire a film of the eyes thickens, and when touched by water, as previously said, the same film is attenuated by the coldness and moisture of the water. Of gray eyes. When someone having gray eyes in them in some way becomes clouded and aches, and when that pain is still recent, he should take fennel or its seeds, rub them, and thus take its juice and dew, which he finds upon a straight grass, and a little flour of similar kind, and mix these together as a tortellum, and place it upon his eyes during the night and bind it with a cloth, and he will have it better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Suavis enim calor feniculi cum rore et fortitudine similae temperatus dolores hos aufert. Nam grisei oculi de aere sunt, et ideo ros his temperamentis additur. De oculis igneis, Si homo, qui igneos oculos habet, S in eis caligat et dolet, succum violae accipiat et bis tantum de succo rosarum et succi feniculi secundum tertiam partem rosarum, et his modicum vini addat, et cum dormitam vadat, collyrium hoc circa oculos suos ungat, cavens, ne forte propter fortitudinem eius laedantur.
text_translation: Sweet indeed is the heat of fenugreek tempered with dew and the fortitude of similar things, removing these pains. For gray eyes are from the air, and therefore dew is added to their temperament. Of fiery eyes, if a man who has fiery eyes becomes clouded and suffers pain, let him take the juice of violet and twice as much of rose juice and juice of fenugreek according to the third part of roses, and add a little wine to these, and when he goes to sleep, let him anoint this collyrium around his eyes, taking care that they do not perhaps cause harm because of their strength.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si tamen oculos interius modice tetigerit, non multum inde laeduntur. Nam quod oculi isti de igne sunt, cum suave frigus violae et suave frigus rosarum cum suavitate feniculi et calore vini temperantur, quoniam onmia haeo in aestate vigent et de suavi calore crescunt, caliginem et dolorem 1 Y. 1—13 = Migne 1211 B. — 2 v. 14—20 = Migne 1157 A— B. — 8 V. 24-28 = Migne 1169 D. eorandeiu oculorum fugat. Viola enim de calido vento primum procedit, unde et praedictis temperamentis hos oculos sanat.
text_translation: But if, however, the eyes have been touched internally in moderation, they are not greatly harmed. For these eyes, which are of fire, are tempered by the gentle cold of violets and the gentle cold of roses with the sweetness of fenugreek and the heat of wine, since all these things are active in summer and grow from the gentle heat, driving away darkness and pain of the eyes. For violet indeed first proceeds from the warm wind, whence with these aforementioned temperaments it heals these eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e oculis diversi coloris. Qui oculos habet similes nubi, in qua iris apparet, et in eis caliginem aut alium dolorem patitur, caliminum accipiat et puro ac albo vino imponat et ad noctem, cum dormitum yadit, calimino ablato eodem vino cilia oculorum suorum exterius illiniat, praecavens, ne oculos interius tangat, ne acumine calimini laedantur et maiorem caliginem incurrant. Nam caliminum aequa- K liter calorem et frigus in se habet et sic cum calore vini temperatum pravos humores, qui oculos laedunt, aufert.
text_translation: Of eyes of diverse colors. He who has eyes similar to clouds, in which the iris appears, and in them suffers cloudiness or another pain, let him take calamintha and apply it with pure and white wine, and at night, when he sleeps, after removing calamintha, with the same wine he should externally anoint the eyelashes of his eyes, taking care not to touch the eyes internally, lest the sharpness of calamintha harm them and cause greater cloudiness. For calamintha has equal heat and cold within itself, and thus, when tempered with the heat of wine, it removes the bad humors which harm the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem oculos interius tetigerit, eos laedit, quod nec calor frigus eius in se superat nec frigus calorem, et ideo acumen ipsius oculos exulceraret et caliginem eis indu- ceret, si eos interius tangeret Nam caliminum nec calorem nec frigus aliud in se superans temperatum, ut praedictum est, oculos istos sanat. Dc octMs turhtdeniis. Si aliquis oculos similes turbidae nubi habet , quae nec ad plenum ignea nec ad plenum turbida est, sed aliquantum glauca, et in eis caliginem et dolorem sustinet, feniculum, si in aestate est, terat, vel si in hieme est, semen eius, et tritum daro ovi bene despumato imponat, et cum dormitum se collocat, ita oculis superponat, et suavis calor feniculi cum frigore clari ovi temperatus caliginem et dolorem eorundem oculorum minuit.
text_translation: If, however, it touches the eyes internally, it harms them, because neither the heat nor the cold of it overcomes itself, nor does the cold overcome the heat, and therefore the sharpness of the eyes would ulcerate and darkness would be induced in them, if it touched them internally. Namely, the cold, neither heat nor cold overcoming anything else within itself, tempered, as previously stated, heals these eyes. De octo turbidiniis. If someone has eyes similar to a turbid cloud, which is neither entirely fiery nor entirely turbid, but somewhat glaucous, and in them sustains darkness and pain, then if it is summer, grind fennel, or if it is winter, its seeds, and apply the ground substance to a well-deaerated egg, and when he lies down to sleep, place it thus over the eyes, and the gentle heat of the fennel tempered with the cold of the clear egg reduces the darkness and pain of those eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Feniculum quippe cum claro ovi, quod aliquantum lividum est, temperatum oculis istis de livida humiditate terrae contractis salubre remedium affert. De ocuUs ni^is. Qui nigros seu turbulentos oculos habet, ut aliquando nubes est, et in eis aliquo modo caligat et dolet, succum ruthae accipiat et bis tantum puri liquoris mellis et modicum boni et clari vini his commisceat, ac sic micam triticei panis his imponat atque super oculos suos in nocte cum panno liget.
text_translation: Fennel, indeed, tempered with a clear egg that is somewhat bluish, offers a wholesome remedy for these eyes affected by the bluish moisture of the earth. Of black eyes. He who has black or stormy eyes, as clouds are sometimes, and in them in some way is obscured and aches, let him take the juice of rue and mix twice as much pure liquid of honey and a little good and clear wine with these, and thus place a piece of wheat bread crushed with these upon his eyes and bind them with a cloth during the night.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quia enim oculi 1 V. 19—27 = Migne 1157 B. — « v. 30-35 = Migne 1155 C. isti de terra sunt, calor mthae et calor mellis cnm calore yini, qui succnm suum de terra traxit, eis prosnnt, cum mica panis ipsis imponitur, quae etiam vires suas de terra habet. Be octUorum aUmffine. Si quis albuginem in oculis patitur, cum eadem albugo receas est, recens fel bovis accipiat et ita recenter oculis suis in nocte superponat et ligamine panni liget, ne labatur, et sic per triduum faciat, quod amaritudo eiusdem fellis dolorem hunc sdndit et aufert.
text_translation: Because the eyes are of the earth, the heat of chalk and the heat of honey with the heat of wine, which drew its juice from the earth, are beneficial to them, when a crumb of bread is placed upon them, which also has its strength from the earth. Be octuorum aulffine. If anyone suffers from whiteness in the eyes, when the same whiteness is present, let them take fresh cow's bile and apply it freshly over their eyes at night, and bind it with a cloth bandage so that it does not slip, and do this for three days, so that the bitterness of the same bile sends away and removes this pain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen modice faciat, ne inde laedatur, si supra modum hoc fecerit. Post triduum autem fenugraecum sumat et rosato oleo immittat, et ita oculis superponat, quoniam hoc suavius est, et panno liget, quia etiam suayis frigiditas fenugraeci cum suavi temperamento rosati olei eandem albuginem cum praefato felle scissam suayius aufert. De octdortim Iticrimis, Si quis humentes oculos, quasi lacrimentur, habet, folium de ficu, quod nocte rore perfnsum est, tollat, cum illud sol in ramusculo suo iam calefecit, et ita calidum oculis suis ad compescendum humorem superponat, usque dum in eo modice incalescat Quod si folium ficus non habuerit, folium de erlen accipiat et item noctumo rore perfusum ac postea radio solis calefactum oculis suis, ut praedictum est, apponat, et sic non omni die faciat, sed tertia die et semel tantum per diem.
text_translation: But nevertheless, let it be done moderately, lest it be harmed, if it is done beyond measure. After three days, however, let it take fenugreek and immerse it in rose oil, and thus apply it over the eyes, because this is sweeter, and let it be bound with a cloth, because also the coldness of fenugreek, with the sweet temperament of rose oil, more sweetly removes the same whitish discharge as the aforementioned bile. Concerning eight days of Iticrimis, if anyone has watery eyes, as if weeping, let him take a fig leaf which has been soaked with dew at night, when the sun has already warmed it on its little branch, and thus apply it warm to his eyes to restrain the humor, until it is moderately warmed. If he does not have a fig leaf, let him take a leaf of erlen, and likewise soaked with night dew and afterwards warmed by the sun's ray, apply it to his eyes as said above, and thus do it not every day, but on the third day and once only per day.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem folia haec non habuerit, ^gummi de parsico vel de prino accipiat et testae nucis modice imprimat ac eam sic ad ignitum laterem vel ad ignitam et clausam fomacem modice calefactam oculis circumponat, dum incalescat. Et hoc semper quarta die faciat et semel in die, ne si supra modum fecerit, madentes oculos plus laedat quam eis succurrat. Nam calor ficus et frigiditas erlen naturae huius sunt, quod humiditatem ad se trahunt. fiore autem perfundentur, ut suavitate eius viriditas eonun temperetur, et etiam calore solis, qui succum eorum ad lenitatem trahit, ne oculos laedat.
text_translation: If, however, it does not have these leaves, let it take gum from parsico or from prino and lightly press it upon the shell of the nut, and let it thus be placed around the burned side or around the burned and closed fomacem, lightly heated, on the eyes, until it warms. And let this be done always on the fourth day and once a day, so that if it is done beyond measure, it may harm the moist eyes more than it may help them. For the heat of figs and the coldness of erlen are the nature of this, which draw humidity to themselves. However, flowers are poured over, so that the greenness may be tempered by their sweetness, and also by the heat of the sun, which draws their juice toward mildness, so that it may not harm the eyes.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et gummi de 1 V. 4—11 = Migne 1323 C. — « v. 24— = Migne 1222 B— C. parsico aut de prino virtutem eorondem lignorom in se habet, et natnra sua humiditatem oculorum in se trabit cum calore, quem aut de latere aut de fomace accipit, quia ille lenis et non acer esse debet. £t ita, ut praedictum est, humor oculorum cessabit. Be audUus diminutione. Si de aliquo flegmate aut alio modo infirmitatis auditus hominis confunditur, album thus accipiat et ex eo fumum super vivo carbone faciat et fumum istum in aurem obduratam ascendere permittat, sed tamen raro hoc faciat, ne si supra modum hoc fecerit, lO inde peius habeat.
text_translation: Sed et gummi de 1 V. 4—11 = Migne 1323 C. — « v. 24— = Migne 1222 B— C. particula aut de primo virtutem eorumdem lignorum in se habet, et natura sua humiditatem oculorum in se trahit cum calore, quem aut de latere aut de fumace accipit, quia ille lenis et non acer esse debet. Et ita, ut praedictum est, humor oculorum cessabit. Be auditus diminutione. Si de aliquo flegmate aut alio modo infirmitatis auditus hominis confunditur, album thus accipiat et ex eo fumum super vivo carbone faciat et fumum istum in aurem obduratam ascendere permittat, sed tamen raro hoc faciat, ne si supra modum hoc fecerit, inde peius habeat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calidus fumus albi tburis, qui purior fumo alterius thuris est, alieno igne excitatus malum fumum, qui cerebrum et auditum hominis confundit, fugat. De dentium ddore. Qui de putrido sanguine et de purgatione cerebri in dentibus dolet, absinthium et ver- benam aequali pondere accipiat et eas in claro et bono vino in nova oUa coquat et vinum illud sic coctum per pannum colet atque modico zuccaro addito vinum istud bibat, sed et herbas istas calidas et, sicut praedictum est, coctas, cum dormitum vadit, maxillae, ubi in dentibus dolet, circumponat et panno desuper liget.
text_translation: Nam calidus fumus albi thuris, qui purior fumo alterius thuris est, alieno igne excitatus malum fumum, qui cerebrum et auditum hominis confundit, fugat. De dentium dolore. Qui de putrido sanguine et de purgatione cerebri in dentibus dolet, absinthium et verbenam aequali pondere accipiat et eas in claro et bono vino in nova olla coquat et vinum illud sic coctum per pannum colet atque modico succharo addito vinum istud bibat, sed et herbas istas calidas et, sicut praedictum est, coctas, cum dormitum vadit, maxillae, ubi in dentibus dolet, circumponat et panno desuper liget.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hoc faciat, dum sanetur. Vinum enim istud cum praedictis herbis temperatum et bibitum venulas, quae se a membrana cerebri ad ginguivas dentium extendunt, interius purgat, et eaedem herbae maxillis circumpositae dolorem dentium exterius mitiganty quia calor absinthii cum calore verbenae et cum calore vini temperatus dolores istos sedat. t ^Qui in dentibus dolet, cum modico flebotomo aut cum brema camem, quae circa eundem dentem est, id est zanefleiSf uno vuhiere modicum incidat, ut tabes ibidem egre- diatur, et melius habebit.
text_translation: And let this be done while he is being healed. For this wine, tempered with the aforementioned herbs and drunk, purges the veins which extend from the membrane of the brain to the gums, and the same herbs, placed around the jaws, mitigate the pain of the teeth externally, because the heat of absinthium, together with the heat of verbena and the heat of tempered wine, allays these pains. Whoever suffers pain in the teeth, with a small flebotomo or with a bremma camem which is around the same tooth, that is zanefleiSf, let one pierce slightly with a small instrument, so that the tabes may emerge there, and he will be better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De dentium firmiMe. Qui sanos et firmos dentes habere vult, in mane, cum de lecto suo surgit, puram et Mgidam aquam in os suum sumat et ita per modicam 1 V. 14—22 = Migne 1172 D. — 2 v. 28—31 = p. 122,2o-26 = Migne 1193 C. — S v. — p. 174,4 = Migne 1211 B-C. horam in ore sao teneat, scilicet ut livor, qoi circa dentes suos est, emollescat; et sic ipsa aqna, qnam in ore tenet, dentes lavet, et hoc saepe faciat, et livor circa dentes amplins non crescet, sed sani permanebunt.
text_translation: On the Firmness of Teeth. Whoever wishes to have healthy and firm teeth, when rising from his bed in the morning, should take pure and clear water into his mouth and hold it in his mouth for a short time, namely so that the blackness which is around his teeth may soften; and thus the very water which he holds in his mouth will wash the teeth, and he should do this often, and the blackness around the teeth will not increase, but they will remain healthy. /1 V. 14—22 = Migne 1172 D. — 2 v. 28—31 = p. 122,2o-26 = Migne 1193 C. — S v. — p. 174,4 = Migne 1211 B-C.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be vermibus in dentibtis. Si autem vermis dentes hominis corrodit, ille aloe et myrram aequali pondere aceipiat et ea in testeo vase artum foramen in eollo habente super yivos carbones de fago factos posito incendat ac fomum illum per angustam harundinem ad dentem, in quo dolet, transire permittat labiis apertis, sed dentibus compressis, ne supra modum idem fiimus guttur intret^ et hoc aut bis vel ter in dic faciat et per quinque dies sic faciat, et curabitur.
text_translation: Concerning worms in the teeth. If, however, the worm corrodes the teeth of a man, let him take aloes and myrrh in equal weight and place them in a vessel with a hole in the eolo, set over live beech charcoal, and ignite it; let the smoke pass through a narrow reed to the tooth where it aches, with the lips open but the teeth pressed together, so that the same does not enter the throat excessively. And let this be done twice or three times a day, and thus for five days, and it will be healed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gum enim calor aloae et calor myrrae cum aequo calore et frigiditate ignitorum carboI5num excitantur, fortitudo fnmi eorum vermes dentium extinguit. De uvula. Quod si idem fumus guttur tetigerit, fortitudo eius guttur et uhun arescere facit. De cordis dolore^ Si mali humores in visceribus et in splene hominis superhabundaverint et cordi multas passiones per melancoliam intulerint, iUe galangae et hertramum aequali pondere accipiat et album piper ad quartam partem unius istorum, vel si album piper non habuerit, accipiat perfrecruth quater tantum, quantum albi piperis est, et haec in pulverem redigat.
text_translation: For indeed the heat of aloes and the heat of myrrh, together with the equal heat and coldness of the ignited charcoal, are excited; the strength of their smoke extinguishes the worms of the teeth. On the uvula. If the same smoke has touched the throat, the strength of it causes the throat and uvula to become dry. On the pain of the heart. If the bad humors in the viscera and in the spleen of a man have exceeded in abundance and have imposed many passions upon the heart through melancholy, let him take galangal and hertramum in equal weight, and white pepper to the fourth part of one of these, or if he does not have white pepper, let him take perfrecruth four times as much as white pepper is, and reduce these to powder.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde farinam fabae tollat et ei praedictum pulverem addat, atque haec omnia simul cum suoco fenugraeci absque aqua et absqne vino et absque alio liquore commisceat. Quo facto ex omnibus his tortellos paret et eos ad calorem solis exsiccet ; unde in aestate, dum solem habere potest, eos fEkciat, quatinus in hieme eos habeat. Deinde eosdem tortellos tam pransus quam ieiunus comedat. Nam calor galangae et calor hertrami et calor albi piperis seu calor pefercrut et calor fabae cum frigiditate fenugraeci temperati et ad sanum calorem solis positi, ut praedictum est, dolorem 1 V.
text_translation: Then take the flour of beans and add to it the aforementioned powder, and mix all these things together with the juice of fenugreek without water and without wine and without any other liquid. When this is done, make tortellus from all these and dry them in the heat of the sun; whence in summer, while he can have the sun, he should make them, so that he may have them in winter. Then let him eat the same tortellus both when he has eaten and when he is fasting. For the heat of galangal and the heat of hortrami and the heat of white pepper or the heat of pepper and the heat of beans tempered with the coldness of fenugreek and placed in the healthy heat of the sun, as previously said, pain 1 V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: — p. 175,4 = Migne 1184 C-1135 A. cordis mitigat. Postea liqniriciam toUat et quinqnies plos de fenicalo et succer ad pondus liqniricii atqne modicnm mellis et ex hoc potnm, id est lutirdranc, faciat et tam pransns qnam ieinnns contra dolorem cordis bibat. Oalor enim liqniricii et calor fBnicnli et calor znccari cnm calore mellis temperati livorem, qni cor hominis, nt praedictnm est, dolere facit, anfemnt. f Accipe ergo albnm piper et ad eins tertiam partem cnminnm atqne fenngraecnm, qnantnm medietas cnmini pensat, et haec pnlveriza et, anteqnam in corde defectnm sentias vel cnm ibi dolere inceperis, ipsnm pnlverem modico pane tam ieinnns qnam pransns modice mandnca.
text_translation: The heart is mitigated. Afterwards, let it take liquiricium and five times plum and sugar in the weight of liquiricium and a little honey, and from this make a drink, that is luterdranc, and let him drink both prandium and iennium against the pain of the heart. For indeed the heat of liquiricium and the heat of feniculi and the heat of sugar with the heat of honey tempered, the yellow bile, which the human heart, as previously said, causes pain, anfebrunt. Take therefore white pepper and to a third part of it cuminum and fennegræcum, as much as half a cuminum weighs, and powder this, and before you feel a defect in the heart or when you begin to feel pain there, let him chew this powder with a little bread both iennium and prandium moderately.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pulmonis dolore. Oum mali et foetidi hnmores famnm ad cerebmm hominis emittnnt, qnod enndem fnmnm ad pnlmonem dncit et illnm dolere facit, homo ille kmchumrt accipiat et eam in aqua coqnat et non in vino, qnia herba haec cnm vino cocta nimis fortis esset, et ita coctam in oUa dimittat et per septimanam bibat per pannnm colatam. Et cnm potns iste defecerit, iternm alium eodem modo coquat. leinnns antem per singnlos dies ao enm bibat et postqnam comederit, nsqne dnm sanetnr.
text_translation: On the pain of the lungs. When bad and foul humors emit smoke to the brain of man, that same smoke leads to the lung and makes it painful, the man who receives whichever [herb] and cooks it in water and not in wine, because this herb when cooked with wine is too strong, and let it be placed in a pot and drink it filtered through a cloth for a week. And when that drink has finished, again cook another in the same way. Lentils, however, for each day and indeed drink and after he has eaten, until he is healed at home.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam de dolore cordis et calore stomachi pnlmo mnltotiens infirmatnr, qnia infirmitatem frigiditas herbae huius cnm snavitate aqnae temperata sedat. fAccipe antem baccas UHdsalder et bis tantnm widlene, nt baccae wakdlders snnt, et hertram bis tantnm, nt umUene est, et haec in pnro et bono vino coque; quod postqnam feceris, ita in oUa dimitte et cmdnm et in modica fmsta incisnm alant impone ac sic per pannnm colatnm per duas vel tres septimanas ieiunus modice bibe ; simili modo et post cibum do tnum .
text_translation: Nam de dolore cordis et calore stomachi pulmo multotiens infirmatur, quia infirmitatem frigiditas herbae huius cum savitate aquae temperata sedat. Fac cape antem baccas Uva ursina et bis tantum videline, ut baccae uva ursina sunt, et hertram bis tantum, ut umbram est, et haec in puro et bono vino coque; quod postquam feceris, ita in olla dimitte et coddum et in modica fistula incisum alant impone ac sic per pannum colatum per duas vel tres septimanas ieiunus modice bibe; simili modo et post cibum done.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: bibere potes et ita dum saneris. f Accipe qnoqne diUe et libestichel ter tantum, ut dUle est, et heHerneeBele t«ntnm, nt diUe est, atque cnm pnro et bono vino coctas et ita in oUa dimissas, nsqne dnm saporem snnm tenent, per pannnm colatas bibere potes et ieinnus et pransus, sed tamen modice et discrete. Set alii homines sunt, qui in nubilosa et hnmida temperie aeris concipinntnr, unde etiam foetentem et male odorantem halitom et male odorantem sudorem semper habent.
text_translation: You may drink and thus until you are healed. Receive also quonque dies and libestichellum three times as much as is suitable, and herbenebelle as much as is suitable, and cooked with pure and good wine and thus placed into olla, until they retain their taste, filtered through pannum, you may drink them at iennus and pransus, but nevertheless moderately and discreetly. But other men are, who are conceived in a cloudy and humid temperie of air, from which also they always have a foul and ill-smelling halitum and ill-smelling sudor.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et foetidus halitos eorom atqae mali humores, qui in ipsis sunt, usque ad cerebrum eorum pertingunt et illud in infirmitate fatigant, quod multotiens velut in oblivione sui ducuntur. Quod si llegma de capite eiciunt, minus dolent, quod cerebrum eorum purgatur; si vero flegma non eiciimt, gravius in capite torquentur, quoniam cerebrum suum a noxiis humoribus non relevant. Et foetens halitus istorum ad pul monem transit et eum fatigat, ita quod etiam aliquando raucam vocem habent, sed tamen dolor iste non multum periculosus est, quod cito curari potest.
text_translation: And the foul breath of them and the bad humors, which are in them, reach up to their brain and weary it in weakness, which often leads them as if into forgetfulness of themselves. Which if they expel phlegm from the head, they suffer less, because their brain is purged; if indeed they do not expel phlegm, they are more severely tormented in the head, because their brain is not purged from noxious humors. And the foul breath of these people passes to the lung and fatigues it, so that sometimes they have a hoarse voice, but nevertheless this pain is not very dangerous, because it can be quickly cured.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 2e epatis duritie, Si quis diversos cibos inunoderate et indiscrete sumpserit et sic de diversis humoribus cibo rum illorum iecur eius laeditur et indurescit, hic minner hufladecha accipiat et bis tantum de radicibus plantaginis et tantum mus, quod est circa viscum piri, ut minner hufUUhdechaj et praefatas herbas in modicas et tenues incisiones incidat et incisiones istas subula aut alio minuto instrumento perforet et in foramina ista praedictum mus infigat et sic in purum vinum ponat.
text_translation: For the hardness of the liver, if someone has consumed various foods in an inmoderate and indiscriminate manner, and thus the liver is harmed and hardened due to the diverse humors of those foods, let him take the following: minner hufladecha, and twice as much of the roots of plantain and mus, which is around the mistletoe of the pear, so that minner hufladecha and the aforementioned herbs are cut into small and thin incisions, and these incisions are pierced with a subula or another small instrument, and the aforementioned mus is inserted into these openings and then placed in pure wine.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et pondus nummi accipiat tumorem illum, qui aut in folio aut in ramusculo nuci velut faba aut velut pisa intumuit, et hoc in praedictum vinum mittat, et deinde tam pransus quam ^ ieiunus non coctum sed tantum vino impositum bibat. Calor enim et frigus minner huflaiMedia inflationem iecori aufert, calor autem plantaginis, ne iecur coaguletur et ne indurescat, prohibet. Sed frigus muses visci piri livorem eius minuit, et tumor ille, qui in folio aut in ramusculo nuci est, amaritudine sua pravos humores, qui iecur laedunt, aufert.
text_translation: But also let the weight of a coin receive that tumor, which has swelled in the leaf or in the small branch of the nut like a bean or like a pea, and let this be placed into the aforementioned wine, and then, whether full or fasting, let him drink it not cooked but only placed in wine. For heat and cold, by the least degree, remove the swelling of the liver; heat, however, from plantain prevents the liver from coagulating and hardening. But cold from the viscera of the pear reduces its yellowness, and that tumor, which is in the leaf or in the small branch of the nut, by its bitterness removes the bad humors which harm the liver.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et haec non coquantur, sed tantuni vino non cocto imponentur, ut tanto suavius ad iecur perveniant. Moretum quoque, qui sic dolet, in potu saepe bibat, quoniam dolor iecoris multotiens de superfluitate sanguinis oritur, quem calor et succus moreti, quod velut 1 V. 18—25 = Migne 1206 B— C. de genimine sangninis est, sedat. Cibum etiam, quem comedit, aceto temperatum comedat, quoniam calor et acumen aceti iecur constringit. Sed et triticeum panem illum manducet, quem quidam homines inter discissam et aridam porcinam scapulam camium aliquando pro delectamento ponunt et vino perfundunt.
text_translation: And these are not cooked, but are merely placed upon unboiled wine, so that they may reach the liver more sweetly. Also, the one who suffers thus should often drink moretum as a drink, because the pain of the liver often arises from the superfluity of blood, which the heat and juice of moretum, which is as it were 1 V. 18—25 = Migne 1206 B—C. of the origin of blood, calms. Also, let him eat food tempered with vinegar, because the heat and sharpness of vinegar constricts the liver. But also let him eat wheat bread, which some men sometimes place for pleasure between the cut and dry pork shoulder camium and pour wine over it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: INam aridus succus buius scapulae, cum excitatur calore vini et pani superfunditur, idem panis sic temperatus iecur constringit, ne infletur. Sed tamen vinum illud, cum quo hae carnes perfusae sunt, non bibat, quod, quod nocivum in eisdem camibus lO ftiit, idem vinum pertransivit et in se traxit. De splenis dolore. Cum homo aliquando crudum cibum comedit, mali humores eorundem ciborum, quod per nullum condimentum temperati sunt, ad splen interdum ascendunt et illud dolere faciunt, unde idem homo ceri- folium accipiat et modicum minus de aneto et cum triticeo pane in aceto velut offas et velut condimentum faciat et sic frequenter comedat.
text_translation: Nam aridus succus buius scapulae, cum excitatur calore vini et pani superfunditur, idem panis sic temperatus iecur constringit, ne infletur. Sed tamen vinum illud, cum quo hae carnes perfusae sunt, non bibat, quod, quod nocivum in eisdem camibus lO ftiit, idem vinum pertransivit et in se traxit. De splenis dolore. Cum homo aliquando crudum cibum comedit, mali humores eorundem ciborum, quod per nullum condimentum temperati sunt, ad splen interdum ascendunt et illud dolere faciunt, unde idem homo ceri- folium accipiat et modicum minus de aneto et cum triticeo pane in aceto velut offas et velut condimentum faciat et sic frequenter comedat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam suave frigus cerifolii dolorem splenis, qui et de calidis et frigidis humoribus surgit, purgat et sanat, frigiditas autem aneti splen confortat et triticeus panis splen crescere facit, acetum vero illud acumine suo purgat. Postea etiam semen lini accipiat et illud in sartagine coquat et ita de sartagine ablatum expressa aqua in saccellum fusum loco splenis, quanto calidius pati potest, cum saccello superponat. Nam semen lini calidum et livosum est, et cum illud in suavitate aquae ad vires suas excitatur, calore et humiditate sua splen tangit et sanat.
text_translation: For the pleasant cold of cerifolii purges and heals the pain of the spleen, which arises both from hot and cold humors, but the coldness of anethi comforts the spleen, and wheat bread causes the spleen to grow, but vinegar purges by its sharpness. Afterwards also let him take the seed of flax and cook it in a cloth, and thus, after being removed from the cloth, let the expressed water be poured into a bag and placed upon the spleen as warm as it can endure, with the bag laid upon it. For the seed of flax is warm and yellow, and when it is excited by the sweetness of water to its own forces, it touches and heals the spleen by its heat and moisture.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De stomachi dolore. Cum indigesti cibi in stomacho hominis interdum coagulantur et indurescunt, stomachum dolere faciunt, quapropter ille dactilosam accipiat et ad quartam eius partem abrotanum et quinquefolii minus, quam abrotani sit, et haec ad succum in mortario terat et postea in bono atque puro vino coquat, ita tamen quod vinum hoc succum herbamm istamm duabus parti- 1 V. 12—25 == Migne 1160 B— C. bus superet, et deiDde per pannam colat et sic in vitreum vas aut in novam oUam refundat.
text_translation: On stomach pain. When indigestible foods in a man's stomach sometimes coagulate and harden, they cause the stomach to ache. Therefore, he should take dactylospermum and abrotanum, and quinquefolium, less of the latter than abrotanum, and grind these in a mortar with the juice, then cook them in good and pure wine, so that the wine surpasses the herbs in quantity by two parts. Then, let it be filtered through a cloth and poured into a glass vessel or a new jar.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea vinum istud sic cum praedictis herbis temperatum in sartaginem fundat et ei ignitum calibem aut bis aut ter immittat et sic in idem vinum , interim dum per calibem fervere incipit, galangae tritum et modicum piperis imponat, vel si galangae non habuerit, 'bertramvm tritum immittat et ignito calibe calefactum ieiunus bibat. Sed et idem vinum per quinque dies modice ieiunus bibat ignito tamen calibe prius calefactum.
