Quod cum per quinque dies feceiit, triticeum panem aut farinam similae accipiat et cum praefato vino ignito calibe calefacto sobriunculam faciat vitello ovi propter bonum saporem addito absque sagimine et absque oleo et iterum per quinque dies haec ieiunus Gomedat.
text_translation: Afterwards, that wine thus tempered with the aforementioned herbs is poured into a sartaginem and heated with a hot calidum, and it is introduced either twice or thrice into it; and thus into the same wine, meanwhile while it begins to boil through the calidum, ground galangae and a little pepper are placed upon it, or if one does not have galangae, ground bertramum is introduced, and after being heated by the ignited calidum, one drinks it fasting. But also the same wine, for five days, is drunk in moderation fasting, having been previously heated by the ignited calidum. When this has been done for five days, one receives either a wheat bread or flour of millet, and with the aforementioned wine heated by the ignited calidum, prepares a sobriunculam with veal and egg, adding for good flavor without sagimine and without oil, and again for five days this is eaten fasting by the gometat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quibus transactis iterum praedictum vinum ignito calibe calefactum bibat, usque dum melius habeat. Nam dactilosa calore suo stomachum confortat, abrotanum autem calore suo gicJi ab eo compescit, quinquefolium vero calore suo eum roborat, et calor vini livorem eius aufert, et fortitudo calibis haec onmia fortissime confortat. Deinde calor galangae et calor piperis seu calor hertrami omnia ista confortat contra dolorem stomacbi. Sed triticeus panis seu farina de triticeo cum praedicto vino stomachum impinguat et ei vires suas, ut praefatum est, cum fortitudine igniti calibis restituit, et hoc absque sagimine et absque oleo fiet, quod sagimen stomachum lubricum parat et oleum illum exulcerat.
text_translation: After these things, again let the aforementioned wine, heated in a heated chalice, be drunk until it is better. For dactylosa with its heat comforts the stomach, abrotanum however with its heat gicJi restrains it, quinquefolium indeed with its heat strengthens it, and the heat of the wine removes its pallor, and the strength of the chalice strongly comforts all these things. Then the heat of galangae and the heat of pepper or the heat of hertrami comforts all these things against the pain of the stomach. But wheat bread or wheat flour with the aforementioned wine fattens the stomach and restores its strength, as previously stated, with the strength of the heated chalice, and this will be done without sagimine and without oil, for sagimine prepares the stomach to be slippery and oil ulcerates it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et crudum ysopum vino impositum frequenter comedat et idem vinum bibat, quod ysopum ei utilius est ad infirmitatem istam quam illi, qui in pulmone dolet. JDe indigestione. Si homo comestum cibum interdum digerere non potest, succum aristologiae longae ad pondus duorum nummorum accipiat et succum bibinellae ad pondus nummi unius et succum citocaciae ad pondus oboli unius ac zinziber ad pondus oboli unius et deinde 1 v. 27—30 =- Migne 1156 C. — 2 v.
text_translation: But also let him frequently eat raw hyssop placed in wine and drink the same wine, for hyssop is more useful for this weakness than for those who suffer in the lung. On indigestion. If a man sometimes cannot digest eaten food, let him take the juice of long aristolochia weighing two coins, and the juice of bibinella weighing one coin, and the juice of citocacia weighing one obol, and ginger weighing one obol, and then 1 v. 27—30 =- Migne 1156 C. — 2 v.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: — p. 179,i « Migne 1193 A— B. modicum farinae similae accipiat et cum succis istis tortellos ad latitudinem nummi sed aliquantum spissos faciat et eos ad solem vel in fomace iam fere infrigidata coquat. Et homo ille, qui supra dicto modo infirmatur, si interius calidus est, ita quod cibus in eo exustus est, de his tor- tellum unum in mane diei ieiunus sumat, yel si interius frigidus est, ita quod comestus cibus in eo prae frigore congelatur et compressus est, duos aut tres tortellos in mane ieiunus accipiat.
text_translation: — p. 179,i « Migne 1193 A— B. let him take a little flour of similae and with these juices make tortellos the width of a coin but somewhat thick and cook them in the sun or in a furnace already nearly cooled. And that man who is weakened in the aforementioned manner, if internally hot, so that food in him is scorched, let him take one of these tortellum fasting in the morning; or if internally cold, so that eaten food in him is frozen and compressed by the cold, let him take two or three tortellos fasting in the morning.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cibus, quem primum postea comedit, ius yel sobriuncula sit, et deinde alios bonos et lO suaves cibos comedat. Et sic tam diu faciat, dum se in stomacho liberatum sentiat. Nam calor aristologiae longae aliquantum acutus et aliquantum fortis exsistens cum frigiditate bibinellae temperatus prayos humores commovet in homine, et calor zmziberi eos solvit, sed frigiditas cito- caciae eos repente educit, farina autem similae confortat stomachum, ne ab his laedatur.
text_translation: And the food, which he eats first afterwards, is indeed temperate, and then he should eat other good and sweet foods. And thus he should do so as long as he feels himself liberated in the stomach. For the heat of long aristologiae, somewhat acute and somewhat strong, existing with the coldness of bibinellae tempered, moves the first humors in man, and the heat of simiberi dissolves them, but the coldness of cito-caciae suddenly brings them forth, whereas flour, however, similar ones comforts the stomach, so that it is not harmed by these.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum haec omnia ad solem, cuius calor fortis est, vel ad calefactam fomacem, cuius calor sanus est, excoquuntur et dolenti hbmini, ut praefatum est, dantur, stomachum eius purgant, ut prae- dictum est. t Accipe zinziber et pulveriza illud et cum modico succo herbae, quae ringla dicitur, commisce ac tunc de pulvere isto cum modica farinula fabae fac tortellos et eos in fornace, quae iam a calore ignis aliquantum cessavit, coque et sic tortellos istos tam pransus quam ieiunus comede.
text_translation: And when all these things are cooked toward the sun, whose heat is strong, or toward the heated fomacem, whose heat is healthy, as previously stated, they are given to the suffering man, purging his stomach, as previously said. Take ginger and powder it, and mix it with a little juice of the herb called ringla, and then from this powder with a little flour of beans make tortellis, and cook them in the oven, which has somewhat ceased from the heat of fire, and thus eat these tortellis both when full and when fasting.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De sifac ruptura. Si in aliquo homine interior peilicula, in qua intestina clausa sunt, aliquo casu scinditur, ille ebeck accipiat et bis tantum de consolida maiore et herbas in bono vino coquat et postquam eas coxerit, ab eodem vino eas separet et deinde eidem vino modicum pulveris de zittmre facti immittat et tantum zuccaris, quantum ehech fuit, et satis de cocto melle et ita rursum absque praefatis herbis idem vinum modice fervere faciat ac deinde per saccellum velut pumm potum coUat et post cibum et ad noctem ita bibat et hoc saepe faciat.
text_translation: De sifac ruptura. Si in aliquo homine interior pellicula, in qua intestina clausa sunt, aliquo casu scinditur, ille ebeck accipiat et bis tantum de consolida maiore et herbas in bono vino coquat et postquam eas coxerit, ab eodem vino eas separet et deinde eidem vino modicum pulveris de zittmre facti immittat et tantum zuccaris, quantum ehech fuit, et satis de cocto melle et ita rursum absque praefatis herbis idem vinum modice fervere faciat ac deinde per saccellum velut pumm potum coUat et post cibum et ad noctem ita bibat et hoc saepe faciat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim frigiditas ebech et frigiditas consolidae ac frigiditas 12 zitmris sibi coniungniitar, bono frigore et bona virtate sua interiorem pellicalam intestinonim coUigunt et consolidant, quod si eaedem herbulae calidae essent, interiorem hanc pelliculam arescere facerent. Sed suavis calor vini et calor succaris et calor mellis, cum alterato calore excitantur, scissam interiorem pelliculam istam sanant. Sed et praedictas herbulas, quae in praefato vino coctae sunt, idem vir super eundem locum, ubi interior pellicula intestinorum rupta est, calidas liget, et scissuram eius colli gunt, ut praefatum est.
text_translation: When indeed the coldness of intoxication and the coldness of consolidation and the coldness of 12 zitmris are joined together, they gather and consolidate the inner membrane of the intestines by their good cold and good virtue, which if the same herbs were hot, would make this inner membrane parch. But the sweet heat of wine and the heat of raisins and the heat of honey, when excited by altered heat, heal this split inner membrane. But also the aforementioned herbs, which are cooked in the aforementioned wine, the same man on the same place where the inner membrane of the intestines is torn, binds them hot and closes the split of it, as mentioned above.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Badicem quoque consolidae maioris in minuta frnsta incidat ac in vinum eadem cruda frusta ponat, quatinus ez eis saporem capiant, et hoc vinum semper bibat, usque dum sanetur, quoniam frigiditas eiusdem consolidae cum calore vini irruptionem illam contrahit. De renitm dolore, Quod homo in renibus et in lumbis interdum dolet, hoc multotiens de infirmitate stomachi fit; tunc idem homo rutham et absinthium aequalis ponderis accipiat atque plus his de arvina ursi et haec simul contundat et deinde cum his circa renes et circa lumbos suos, ubi dolet, iuxta ignem fortiter se inungat.
text_translation: Take also the larger consolida, cut it into small pieces, and place the same raw pieces into wine, so that they may take the taste from it, and let this wine be drunk always until he is healed, because the coldness of the same consolida, together with the heat of the wine, counteracts that eruption. Concerning the pain of the kidneys: When a man sometimes aches in the kidneys and in the loins, this often arises from the weakness of the stomach; then let the same man take rue and wormwood of equal weight, and also more of this from bear's arvina, and grind these together, and afterward, with these, anoint himself strongly around the kidneys and around his loins where it aches, near fire.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Dolor enim renum et lumborum multotiens ab iniustis humoribus exsurgit. Sed cum calor ruthae et calor absinthii et calor arvinae ursi sibi contemperantur, iniuste frigidos humores istos fugant. De Uiaca. Si malus fumus de stomacho ad ilia hominis extenditur et ibi eundem hominem dolere facit, ille salviam accipiat et quinquies tantum stichumre, ut salviae est, et decies tantum ruthae, ut salviae est, ac herbas istas in nova olla cum aqua usque ad primam ferventem ebullitionem coquat, et deinde expressa aqua easdem herbas ita coctas et calidas super locum, ubi praedicto modo dolet, ponat et panno desuper liget.
text_translation: Indeed, the pain of the kidneys and loins often arises from unjust humors. But when the heat of ruthae and the heat of absinthii and the heat of arvinae ursi are mixed together, they drive away these unjust cold humors. Of Uiaca. If bad vapor from the stomach extends to the hips of a man and causes him to suffer there, let him take salviam and stichumre five times as much as salvia, and ruthae ten times as much as salvia, and cook these herbs in a new pot with water until the first fervent boiling, then pour out the water and place these same herbs, thus cooked and hot, on the place where he suffers in the aforementioned manner, and cover with a cloth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum calor salviae et calor sticktvurz et calor ruthae sibi contemperantur et cum in calefacta aqua excitantur, virtute sua malos hu mores, de quibus dolor iliorum oritur, fugant. 1 v. 15—20 = Migne 1155 C. — 2 y. 25—32 =- Migne 1154 C-D. De lcUeris dolore. Accipe linsamum et minns flius de parsico, ita quod linsamo quarta parte flius excedat, et in sartagine ut gluthen coque. Deinde toUe birboumes mistd et in mortario tere ad succum, ita quod de succo plus sit, quam fliusis et quam succus birboumes sit mistelles, et baec in sartagine ad praedictum linsamum et ad flius pone et iterum denuo similiter fac fervere.
text_translation: Nam cum calor salviae et calor sticturae et calor ruthae sibi contemperantur et cum in calefacta aqua excitantur, virtute sua malos humores, de quibus dolor iliorum oritur, fugant. 1 v. 15—20 = Migne 1155 C. — 2 y. 25—32 = Migne 1154 C-D. De lumborum dolore. Accipe linsamum et minus flius de parsico, ita quod linsamo quarta parte flius excedat, et in sartagine ut glutin coque. Deinde tolle birboumes mistum et in mortario tere ad succum, ita quod de succo plus sit, quam flius est et quam succus birboumes sit mistellus, et haec in sartagine ad praedictum linsamum et ad flius pone et iterum denuo similiter fac fieri.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si medullam cervi non habueris, sepum iuvenis tauri adde eodem pondere, ut medulla cervi esse deberet. Quo facto baec per pannum subula ubique perforatum cola et in lO novum fictile vas ceratum funde et dolentem in latere, ubi dolet, ad ignem perunge. De osscei tumore. ^Si interdum a raalis humoribus in virilibus viri inflatio pessimi tumoris insurgit, quae illum ibi dolere facit, ille feniculum accipiat et ter tan- tum fenigraeci ac modicum butyri vaccarum et haec simul contundat et desuper ponat, et haec malos humores doloris in virilibus extrahunt.
text_translation: If you do not have the marrow of a deer, add the sebum of a young bull in equal weight, as the marrow of a deer should be. Once done, take this through a perforated cloth and pour it into a new ceramic vessel, and apply it to the burning fire on the side where the pain is. On the tumor of the bone. If occasionally a swelling of the worst tumor arises from the rare humors in the virile parts of a man, causing pain there, let him take fennel and three times as much Greek fennel and a little cow's butter, and crush these together and place them on top, and these draw out the bad humors of pain from the virile parts.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Suavis enim calor feniculi cum frigiditate fenigraeci et cum suavi calore butyri temperatus praefatum dolorem minuit. Nam calor feniculi et fri- giditas fenigraeci pravos humores illos minuit, et suavis calor butyri eundem dolorem sanat. Deinde vir tortellos, ex quibus cerevisia fit, accipiat et cum modica calida aqua eos modice madefaciat et sic calefaciat ac super praefatum tumorem ponat. Nam praedicti tortelli in suavitate cali- dae aquae temperati acuto succo suo pravos humores illos extrahunt et ibi dolentem hominem sanat.
text_translation: Sweet heat of fenugraecum, with the coldness of fenugraecum and with the sweet heat of butter tempered, lessens the aforementioned pain. For the heat of fenugraecum and the coldness of fenugraecum reduce those bad humors, and the sweet heat of butter heals the same pain. Then let the man take tortellum, from which beer is made, and with a moderate amount of warm water moisten them slightly and thus warm them and place them upon the aforementioned tumor. For the aforementioned tortellum, when tempered with the sweetness of warm water, extract those bad humors with their sharp juice and heal the ailing man there.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si quis ergo in virilibus hoc modo doiet, accipiat feniculum et ter tantum fenigraeci ac modicum kusmalz et haec simul contundat et desuper ponat, et malos humores extrahit; deinde accipiat malzkuchin et eum modica calida aqua modice madefaciat ac desuper ponat. De stranguria. Si quis urinam prae frigidate stomachi retinere non potest, vinum igne calefactum saepe 1 V. 1—12 = Migne 1202 C. — 2 y. i3_25 = Migne 1157 D. — 8 V. 33— p. 182,9 = Migne 1154 D.
text_translation: If therefore anyone suffers in the virile parts in this manner, let him take fennel and threefold Greek fennel and a little cumin and crush these together and place them on top, and thus extract the bad humors; then let him take malt cake and moisten it slightly with warm water and place it on top. Concerning stranguria. If anyone cannot retain urine because of the coldness of the stomach, let him often drink wine heated by fire. 1 V. 1—12 = Migne 1202 C. — 2 y. i3_25 = Migne 1157 D. — 8 V. 33— p. 182,9 = Migne 1154 D.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: bibat et omnes cibos suos aceto commisceat atque acetum, qnomodocunque poterit, saepe bibat, et boc modo in stomacbo et in vesica calidus erit. Nam calor vini ad ignem alteratus et ita in potu sumptus stomacbum et vesicam calefacit et urinam ad rectam digestionem retinet ac ei rectam digestionem et calorem infert. Sed et salviam in aqua coquat et eandem aquam per pannum colat ac eam ita calidum saepe bibat, et ille urinam retinebit et curabitur.
text_translation: Let him drink and mix all his foods with vinegar, and let him drink vinegar, however he can, often, and in this way the stomach and bladder will be warm. For the heat of wine, altered into fire and thus consumed in drink, warms the stomach and bladder and retains urine for proper digestion, imparting to it proper digestion and heat. But also let him boil sage in water and let him strain the same water through a cloth and let him drink it thus warm often, and he will retain urine and be healed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Salvia enim livorem, qui de frigiditate humorum in stomacbo et in vesica ortus est, qui etiam ante digestionem urinam expellit, calore suo aufert. De sterUitate masctdi, Masculus ergo, qui diffluentem naturam seminis babet, ita quod non germinat, bic hasenzeppun accipiat et ad eius tertiam partem ertpe/fer, 5 et quanta pars quarta ertpefferes est, tantum de winda sumat et aliquantum de alio usitato pipere, atque baec cum iecore iuvenis birci, qui iam maturus est ad generandum, coquat additis etiam aliquantum crudis et pinguibus porcinis carnibus.
text_translation: Salvia enim livorem, qui de frigiditate humorum in stomacho et in vesica ortus est, qui etiam ante digestionem urinam expellit, calore suo aufert. De sterilitate masculi, Masculus ergo, qui diffluentem naturam seminis habet, ita quod non germinat, hasenzeppun accipiat et ad eius tertiam partem ertpefer, et quanta pars quarta ertpefferes est, tantum de ventra sumat et aliquantum de alio usitato pipere, atque haec cum iecore iuvenis birci, qui iam maturus est ad generandum, coquat additis etiam aliquantum crudis et pinguibus porcinis carnibus.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc berbis istis abiectis ao cames bas comedat et etiam panem in aquam, in qua buiusmodi cames coctae sunt, intingat et eum manducet et boc modo praedictas carnes coctas saepe comedat, quatinus de succo earum semen suum fortitudinem germinandi accipiat, si iustum iudicium dei boc fieri permiserit. Pro sterUitate feminae, 2 MuUer autem, cuius matrix interius frigida et tenuis est ad concipiendum prolem, boc modo iuvetur, si deus ita voluerit, ut fecunda fiat.
text_translation: And then, when these things are cast out, let the person eat meat and also dip bread into the water in which such meat has been cooked, and eat it, and in this way often eat the aforementioned cooked meats, so that from their juice the semen may receive strength for generating, if the just judgment of God permits this to be done. For the sterility of the woman, 2 The woman, whose womb is internally cold and thin for conceiving offspring, is helped in this way, if God so wills, that she may become fertile.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Accipe ergo matricem agnae aut matricem vaccae, quae in bac maturitate sunt, quod gignere possunt, et cum adbuc mundae sunt, ita quod nullo fetu gravatae sunt nec gravatae fuerant, et eas cum lardo et cum aliis quibusdam pinguibus camibus et cum sagimine coque et da feminae ad comedendum, cum illa in coniunctione mariti est aut cum marito postmodo cito iungetur. Et buiusmodi camibus frequenter utatur, et succus matricis praedictorum pe1 V. 12—24 = Migne 1226 A.
text_translation: Take therefore the womb of a ewe or the womb of a cow, which are in this maturity, which can give birth, and which are pure and clean, so that they are not burdened with any fetus nor have been burdened, and cook them with fat and with other certain fat things and with sagimine, and give them to the woman to eat, when she is in union with her husband or when she will join him quickly. And use such things frequently, and the juice of the wombs of the aforementioned pe1 V. 12—24 = Migne 1226 A.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: — « v. 25-p. 183,4 = Migne 1324 D. corum succo matricis eiasdem feminae coniungitur, ita qnod inde aliquantum impinguatur et confortatur, et si deus voluerit, tanto facilius concipit, quod multotiens iudido dei fit, quod virtus generandi hominibus aufertur. De podagra. Homo, qui in cruribus et in pedibus suis podagram sentit et inde dolet, cum idem dolor recens est, iile plurima cornua vel ventosas cruribus suis circumponat, scilicet a talo incipiens absque incisione cutis, ut humores sibi attrahant, et deinde a loco illo auferat et ea superius ponat, quatinus iterum ibi subteriores humores sibi attra- hant.
text_translation: — « v. 25-p. 183,4 = Migne 1324 D. corum succo matricis eiasdem feminae coniungitur, ita quod inde aliquantum impinguatur et confortatur, et si deus voluerit, tanto facilius concipit, quod multotiens iudicium dei fit, quod virtus generandi hominibus aufertur. De podagra. Homo, qui in cruribus et in pedibus suis podagram sentit et inde dolet, cum idem dolor recens est, iile plurima cornua vel ventosas cruribus suis circumponat, scilicet a talo incipiens absque incisione cutis, ut humores sibi attrahant, et deinde a loco illo auferat et ea superius ponat, quatinus iterum ibi subteriores humores sibi attrahant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic faciat cutem non incidendo nec rumpendo, usque dum ad nates suos perveniat; postquam autem hoc modo ad nates pervenerit, ligamen in superiori parte genu sibi circumponat, ne humores, quos cornibus illuc contraxit, rursus descendant, et mox in confinio dorsi et posteriorum cum comibus aut ventosis scarificando sanguinem et malos humores emittat. Sic autem faciat, et dolor podagrae illius cessabit. f Accipe hertram et ad tertiam partem ponderis eius de zingiber et modicum piper et haec pulveriza atque ieiunus comede et vinum postea bibe.
text_translation: And thus let him do this, not cutting nor breaking the skin, until he reaches his buttocks; after he has thus reached the buttocks, let him place a ligament around the upper part of the knee, so that the humors which have been drawn up there by the horns may not again descend, and soon in the region of the back and the posterior parts with combs or ventosas, let him scarify the blood and expel the bad humors. Thus let him do, and the pain of that gout will cease. Take hertram and a third part of its weight of ginger and a little pepper, and pulverize this, and eat it fasting, and afterwards drink wine.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De fistula, 8i quis fistulam de malis et superfluis humoribus ortam in corpore suo alicubi patitur, purgatorias potiones saepe accipiat, quatinus superhabundantes humores in se minuat. Quod si postea in loco eiusdem doloris cutis contrahitur, velut ibi sanari velit, et iterum deinde per humores scinditur, iterum purgatorias potiones accipiat, ne mali humores in eo superhabundent, et sic saepe faciat, et lenius dolebit. Et quia tam fistula quam podagra difPicile curari poterunt.
text_translation: On the fistula, if someone suffers from a fistula that has arisen in their body from bad and superfluous humors, let them often take purgative drinks, in order to reduce the excessive humors within them. If afterward, in the place of the same pain, the skin contracts, as if it wishes to be healed, and then again is split by humors, again let them take purgative drinks, so that the bad humors do not become excessive in it, and thus often do this, and the pain will be less severe. And because both fistula and gout can be difficult to cure.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ulcere, Si ulcus aliquod aut pustula hominem acerbe dolere facit, antequam rumpatur, hic accipiat lineum pannum et nova cera perfundat et desuper oleo olivae inungat et sic super ulcus ponat. Et per hoc ulcus suavius mollificatur et suavius rumpitur et humores extra- huntur et suavius sanatur. Si vero pustula illa est, quae dicitur segena, illi ne superponas, quod periculosa est. Ubi etiam trofo et mali humores rupta cute absque venenato ulcere in aliquo loco corporis humani coadunati efHuunt, tunc homo ille accipiat hiboz et succum eius in mortario terendo exprimat et huic succo mel addat, et ita ut succus hihozes tertia parte mel illud excedat, et ubi dolet, se liniat.
text_translation: On ulcer, If any ulcer or pustule causes a man to suffer acutely, before it ruptures, he should take a linen cloth and pour over it new wax, and anoint it with olive oil on top, and thus place it upon the ulcer. And by this the ulcer is softened more gently and ruptures more gently, and the humors are expelled and healed more gently. If indeed the pustule is that which is called segena, do not place anything upon it, for it is dangerous. Where also trofo and bad humors, ruptured skin without poisoned ulcer, are gathered together in some place of the human body, then that man should take hiboz and press out its juice in a mortar by grinding, and add honey to this juice, and so that the juice of hiboz exceeds the honey by a third part, and where it aches, he should anoint himself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et etiam statim clarum de albugine ovi factum superliniat et panno desuper liget et hoc tam diu faciat, dum sanetur. De insomnietate. Qui autem aliqua contrarietate occu patus dormire non potest, si in aestate est, accipiat feniculum et bis tantum millefolii et in aqua modice coquat atque aqua expressa herbas istas caUdas temporibus et fronti ac capiti circumponat et desuper panno liget. Sed et yiridem salviam accipiat et eam vino modice aspergat et sic supra cor suum et circa coUum suum ponat, et dormitione alleviabitur.
text_translation: And even immediately, make clear from the white of the egg, and apply it over, and bind it with a cloth above, and do this as long as it is healed. Concerning insomnia. He who, however, is troubled by some contrary thing and cannot sleep, if it is in summer, let him take fennel and twice as much yarrow, and boil them in a moderate amount of water, and let him pour out the water, and with the hot water of these herbs, apply them at times around the forehead and head, and bind above with a cloth. But also let him take green salad, and sprinkle it with a little wine, and place it thus over his heart and around his neck, and he will be alleviated in sleep.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si in hieme est, semen feniculi et radicem miUefolii in aqua coquat et temporibus ac capiti, ut praedictum est, circumponat atque salviam pulverizatam et in vino modice madefactam super cor et circa collum ponat ac panno desuper ligato confirmet, quod herbas virides in hieme habere non valet, et ut dictum est, ad dormitionem melius habebit. Nam calor feniculi inceptionem somni inducit, calor autem millefolii sonmum stabilem facit, et calor salviae cor tardum parat et venas colli deprimit, quatinus somnus accedat.
text_translation: If it is in winter, let the seed of fennel and the root of millefolium be boiled in water, and let them be applied around the head at the times, as previously mentioned, and let powdered sage, slightly moistened with wine, be placed upon the chest and around the neck, and let it be confirmed with a cloth tied over it. For green herbs cannot be kept in winter, and as said, it will be better for sleep. For the heat of fennel induces the beginning of sleep, and the heat of millefolium makes sleep stable, and the heat of sage prepares a slow heart and depresses the veins of the neck, so that sleep may come.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et hae herbae cum in suavitate calefactae aquae ad calorem suum excitantur, temporibus circumponuntur, ut venas eorum comprimant, et etiam fronti ac capiti apponuntur, quatinus cerebro quietem inferant, sed et semen feniculi et millefolii in aqua coquuntur propter suavitatem eiusdem aquae, pulvis autem salviae vino imponitur, ut ex eo ad remedium istud excitetur. Be aromatihus, Nunc autem de praedictis infirmitatibus subscriptae medicinae a deo demonstratae aut homi1 V.
text_translation: And these herbs, when heated in water's sweetness, are stirred up to their own heat, and are placed around the times, so that they compress their veins, and also are applied to the forehead and head, so that they bring rest to the brain, but also the seeds of fennel and yarrow are cooked in water because of the sweetness of that same water, but the powder of sage is applied with wine, so that from it this remedy is excited. Be aromatihus, Now however, the medicines written above for the aforementioned ailments are demonstrated by God or homi1 V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 1—8 = Migne 1172 A. — 2 y. 9-22 = Migne 1156 D — 1157 A. — 8 V. — p. 185,2 « p. 165,28-94. nem liberabunt aut ipse morietur aut deus eum liberari non vult. Nam diversae et nobiles herbae et pulveres atque condimenta nobilium berbarum inordinate comesta sanis bominibus non proderunt sed potius laesionem eis inferunt, ita quod sanguinem eorum arefaciunt et cames eorum macerant, quoniam bumores illos in eis non inveniunt, in quibus vires suas exercere possunt.
text_translation: Neither will they free him, nor will he himself die, nor does God wish to free him. For various and noble herbs and powders and condiments of noble barbarians, when eaten immoderately, will not benefit healthy people, but rather inflict harm on them, so that they affect their blood and waste away their flesh, because they do not find the humors in them, in which they can exercise their powers.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in bominibus vires non augent nec carnes eorum crescere faciunt, sed tantum malos bumores, quibus adversantur, minuunt. Sed cum ab aliquo sumuntur, boc discrete et rationabili necessitate fiat, et cum pane vel in vino vel cum alio aliquo condimento ciborum, et raro ieiuno bomini accipiantur, alioquin pectus sumentis suffocant et pulmonem eius laedunt ac stomacbum illius incidentes debilem reddunt, quoniam absque condimento sumpta sunt; quod sicut pul- vis terrae, quem bomo introrsum trabit, eum laedit, sic et ista inordinate accepta bominem magis ad detrimentum quam ad sanitatem ducunt.
text_translation: Nam in hominibus vires non augent nec carnes eorum crescere faciunt, sed tantum malos humores, quibus adversantur, minuunt. Sed cum ab aliquo sumuntur, hoc discrete et rationabili necessitate fiat, et cum pane vel in vino vel cum alio aliquo condimento ciborum, et raro ieiuno homini accipiantur, alioquin pectus sumentis suffocant et pulmonem eius laedunt ac stomachum illius incidentes debilem reddunt, quoniam absque condimento sumpta sunt; quod sicut pulvis terrae, quem homo introrsum trahit, eum laedit, sic et ista inordinate accepta hominem magis ad detrimentum quam ad sanitatem ducunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in cibo vel post cibum hcir»niis ideo praecipue accipienda sunt, quod tunc humores ciborum illorum attenuant et hominem acceptum cibum digerere faciunt, excepto si homo huiusmodi infirmitates habet, contra quas nobiles et fortes herbas aut pretiosum pulverem ieiunus accepturus sit. IV. Item de menstrui retentione. Mulier ergo , quae obstrusa menstrua patitur et inde dolet, accipiat anesum et febrefugiam aequali pondere et wullenam aliquantum plus, quam unius istorum sit, et eas coquat aperto et fluento flumine, quod et sole et aere temperatur, et tunc etiam sumat lateres et eos in ignem ponat et assnm balneum cum praedicta aqua et praedictis herbis faciat.
text_translation: Indeed, in food or after food, hirciniis should be especially taken, because then the humors of those foods attenuate and make the man able to digest the accepted food, except if the man has such infirmities, against which he will take noble and strong herbs or precious powder fasting. IV. Likewise concerning retention of the menses. Therefore, a woman who suffers from obstructed menses and thus suffers pain, let her take anesum and febrefugiam in equal weight and wullenam somewhat more than one of these, and let her cook them in an open and flowing river, which is tempered by both sun and air, and then also take lateres and put them in fire and make a bath with the aforementioned water and aforementioned herbs.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cumque hoc balneum intraverit, super scabellam herbas istas calidas ponat et desuper sedeat atque easdem 1 V. — p. 187,12 = Migne 1174 B. berbas genitali membro et ita sursum ad umbilicom et toto umbilico calidas circumponat. Et si interim infrigidatae fuerint, eas iterum in praedicta aqua calefactas in eisdem locis sibi circumponat et hoc faciat, quamdia in balneo illo sedeat, ut per bumores berbarum istarum cutis et caro illius exterius et matrix interius mollificetur et ut yenae eius, quae clausae sunt, aperiantur.
text_translation: When this bath has entered, place these hot herbs on the stool and sit upon them, and place the same herbs on the genital member and thus upward to the navel and around the entire navel with hot herbs. And if they have become cold in the meantime, again heat them in the aforementioned water and place them around the same places and do this while sitting in that bath, so that through the humors of these herbs, the skin and flesh of that part externally and the womb internally may be softened, and so that the channels of it, which are closed, may be opened.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calor 'anesi bumores commovet; calor autem febrefugiae sanat et calor wuUene fluxum parat. Et cum herbulae istae simul temperantur, et cum calores earum in calefacta aqua fluminis excitantur, quae aqua yiyi fontis sanior et suavior est, quoniam in aperto ab aliis elementis tangitur, et cum etiam ignitis lateribus commoyentur, qui etiam aliis lapidibus similes non sunt, quod per ignem cocti sunt, unde sani exsistunt: cum suavitate balnei menstrua provocant, ut praefatum est.
text_translation: Indeed, heat moves the humors; heat, however, heals fevers and prepares the flow of wounds. And when these herbs are tempered together, and when their heats are excited in the heated water of the river, which water is healthier and more pleasant, because it is touched by other elements in the open, and when they are also moved by the fiery sides, which are not similar to other stones, because they are cooked by fire, from which they become healthy: with the sweetness of the bath, they provoke the menstrual, as previously stated.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde sumat rifelhere et ad eorum tertiam partem millefolium et rutham quasi tertiam partem millefolii et tantum de aristologia longa, ut rifelhere et millefolii est, et plurimum de dictampno et haec in mortario tundat et sic bono et puro vino in nova olla coquat ac coctas cum vino in saccellum fundat et insuper apertum gariofel, quantum habere potest, et de albo pipere minus quam gariofili, et haec simul terat et satis de novo et recente melle, qaod absque sorde est, addat et in optimo vino fervere faciat atque ad praedictas herbas in saccellum fundat et ex his claretum paret et per singulos dies et ieiuna et pransa illud bibat, sed non in praefato balneo, quoniam balneum hominera aliquantum ad digestionem constringit.
text_translation: Then take rifelhere and to their third part yarrow and rutham as the third part of yarrow and as much of long aristolochia as rifelhere and yarrow are, and most of dictamnus, and this in the mortar pound and thus cook in good and pure wine in a new pot, and after cooking, pour the cooked mixture with wine into a saccellum, and on top add open gariofel as much as it can hold, and of white pepper less than of gariofel, and this together grind and add sufficiently of new and recent honey, which is without dirt, and make it boil in the best wine and pour it into the saccellum with the aforementioned herbs, and from these prepare a clear liquid, and drink this each day both fasting and after meals, but not in the aforementioned bath, because the bath of man somewhat constricts digestion.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum frigiditas rifdhere calore millefolii et calore ruthae et calore aristologiae longae et calore dictampni et alterato calore vini ac calore aperti gariofili, quod haec infilrmitati magis convenerit quam aliud gariofel, et calore albi piperis, quod etiam menstrua provocat, et calore recentis mellis, quod etiam his convenit, temperatur : clausa viscera mulieris aperiuntur et coagulatio menstruorum indurata solvitur, velut praedictum est Faciat quoque sobriunculam ex ovis et sufficiente sagiCAUSAE ET LIB.
text_translation: And when coldness is cooled by the heat of millefolium and the heat of rutha and the heat of long aristolia and the heat of dictampni and the altered heat of wine and the heat of open garofilo, which is more suitable for this weakness than other garofilo, and the heat of white pepper, which also provokes menstruation, and the heat of fresh honey, which also suits these, it is tempered: the closed organs of the woman are opened and the hardened coagulation of menstruation is dissolved, as previously stated. Make also a sobriuncula from eggs and sufficient sagicausae et lib.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: IV. mine addito modico succo de levistico et hoc ante cibnm et post cibum suum utatur, quoniam, cum frigiditas ovorum et frigiditas levistici cum calore vini et calore sagiminis temperatur, huiusmodi coagulationem in muliere solvit. Et sic faciat per quinque aut per quin- decim dies, vel usque dum solvatur. Sed interim dum praefatam constrictionem sanguinis patitur, bovinas cames et alios et grossos cibos devitet, quod eam constringunt, sed suaves comedat et vinum bibat; sed si etiam interim aquam bibitura est, aquam putei bibat, aquam vero A- lientis et fluentis fontis devitet, quoniam aliis aquis aliquantum asperior est; sed aquam fluentium fontium coquat et refrigerari permittat, antequam eam bibat, quia ista hoc modo parata suavis efficitur.
text_translation: IV. Let her add a small amount of juice from levistico, and let her use this before and after her meal, because, when the coldness of the eggs and the coldness of levistico are tempered by the heat of wine and the heat of sagimine, this kind of coagulation in a woman is dissolved. And let her do this for five or fifteen days, or until it is dissolved. But in the meantime, while she suffers from the aforementioned constriction of blood, let her avoid bovine meats and other heavy foods, which constrict her, but let her eat soft foods and drink wine; but if she also drinks water during this time, let her drink well water, but let her avoid water from Alientis and flowing springs, because other waters are somewhat harsher; but let her boil water from flowing springs and permit it to cool before she drinks it, because this way prepared, it becomes sweet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De menstruorum fluxu. Igitur mulier, quae iniusto tempbre multa menstrua inordinate patitur, lineum pannum in frigida aqua intingat et femoribus suis saepe circumponat, ut interius infrigescat, quatinus per frigiditatem linei panni et frigidae aquae iniustus fluxus sanguinis retineatur. Et etiam ebech in aqua coquat, et sibi in femoribus et in umbilico calidum circumponat, et frigiditas illius ac contraria virtus, quam in se babet, contrario flnxui, qui et in femoribus et in umbilico habundat, resistit, et ideo etiam eisdem membris cocta et calida superponitur, quod calefacta sanitatem eis affert.
text_translation: On the flow of menstruation. Therefore, a woman who suffers from excessive menstrual flow at an unjust time should soak a linen cloth in cold water and often wrap it around her thighs, so that internally she may become chilled, in order that the unjust flow of blood may be restrained by the coldness of the linen cloth and the cold water. And also let her boil ebech in water, and apply it warm around her thighs and navel, and the coldness of that [decoction] and its opposing virtue, which it possesses, resists the excessive flow that abounds in the thighs and navel; therefore, the same decoction, boiled and warm, is also applied upon those limbs, which, when warmed, brings health to them.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Pandoniam qaoque in vinum ponat, ut inde saporem habeat, et sic illud saepe bibat, et calor pandonii cum calore vini temperatus iniustum calorem sanguinis restringit. Deinde omnes venas suas, scilicet quae in cruribus et in ventre ac in pectore et in brachiis suis sunt, sursum maDibus suis leniter comprimendo saepe moveat, quatinus coerceantur, ne iniustum iter sanguini faciant. Sed et caveat, ne nimis laboret et ne multum in eundo fatigetur, ne inde idem sanguis moveatur, et etiam caveat, ne duros aut 1 V.
text_translation: Pandoniam quoque in vinum ponat, ut inde saporem habeat, et sic illud saepe bibat, et calor pandonii cum calore vini temperatus iniustum calorem sanguinis restringit. Deinde omnes venas suas, scilicet quae in cruribus et in ventre ac in pectore et in brachiis suis sunt, sursum membribus suis leniter comprimendo saepe moveat, quatinus coerceantur, ne iniustum iter sanguini faciant. Sed et caveat, ne nimis laboret et ne multum in eundo fatigetur, ne inde idem sanguis moveatur, et etiam caveat, ne duros aut 1 V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 15-20 = Migne 1211 C. — 2 v. 20—23 = Migne 1187 A. — 3 V. 25—27 = Migne 1182 C. - v. — p. 188,4 » Migne 1211 D. amaros cibos comedat, ne ei iniustam digestionem parent, sed mollia et suayia cibaria interim sumat, quatinus eam interius sanent, et yinum ac cerevisiam bibat, quatinus ex his ita confortetur, ut sanguinem retinere possit. JDe partus difficuUate, Sed et si praegnans mulier in partu multum laborat, tunc cum timore et magno moderamine suaves herbulae, scilicet feniculum et aserum in aqua coquantur et expressa aqua ita calidae circa femora et circa dorsum eius ponantur et ita desuper panno ligato sAviter teneantur, ut dolor et claustra illius tanto suavius et facilius solvantur.
text_translation: Let her eat bitter foods, lest they cause unjust digestion, but let her take soft and sweet foods in the interim, so that they may heal her internally, and let her drink wine and beer, so that from these she may be thus comforted, so that she may be able to retain blood. Concerning difficulty in childbirth, but also if a pregnant woman labors greatly in childbirth, then let sweet herbs, namely fennel and asarum be boiled in water, and the expressed water thus warm be placed around her thighs and around her back, and let them be held thus tightly bound with a cloth above, so that the pain and constrictions of her may be thus more sweetly and easily loosened.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam mali et frigidi humores, qui in femina sunt, eam praegnantem interdum constringunt et claudunt, sed cum suavis calor feniculi et suavis calor aseri in suavitate aquae ad ignem excitantur et ita femo ribus et dorso illius circumponuntur, quoniam in eisdem locis plus quam in aliis constrictionem patitur, ad apertionem eadem membra provocant. De salivae et emunctionis purgatione. Ut autem de saliva et de excreatione et emunctione homo purgetur, accipiat agrimoniam et bis tantum fenigraeci et in mortario tundat et succum earum exprimat et etiam storchesnabel tundat et succum eius, quantum obolus ponderat, succo agrimoniae et fenigraeci addat.
text_translation: Nam mali et frigidi humores, qui in femina sunt, eam praegnantem interdum constringunt et claudunt, sed cum suavis calor feniculi et suavis calor aseri in suavitate aquae ad ignem excitantur et ita femoribus et dorso illius circumponuntur, quoniam in eisdem locis plus quam in aliis constrictionem patitur, ad apertionem eadem membra provocant. De salivae et emunctionis purgatione. Ut autem de saliva et de excreatione et emunctione homo purgetur, accipiat agrimoniam et bis tantum fenigraeci et in mortario tundat et succum earum exprimat et etiam storchesnabel tundat et succum eius, quantum obolus ponderat, succo agrimoniae et fenigraeci addat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde accipiat tantum de galanga, quantum istorum trium fuit, et sto racis, quantum sex nummi ponderant, et polipodii ad pensam duorum nummorum et haec in pulverem redigat ac pulverem istum praefato succo quasi coagulando comprimat et ex eo pillulas ad quantitatem fabae faciat; postea de celidonia maiore succum exprimat et de eodem 30SUCCO, quantum quarta pars nummi pensat, suscipiat ac in illo pillulam unam circumvolvendo intingat et ad solem ponat, ut exsiccetur.
text_translation: Then take as much of galanga as the three of them had, and stoe racis as much as six coins weigh, and polypodii to the weight of two coins, and reduce these to powder, and compress that powder with the aforementioned juice as if coagulating, and from it make pills the size of a bean; then extract juice from greater celidonia, and take from the same juice as much as a quarter of a coin weighs, and dip one pill in it while turning around, and place it in the sun to dry.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic de aliis pillulis faciat unamquamque in eodem succo, quantum quarta pars nummi ponderat, intinguendo ac ita ad calorem solis eas exponat, non autem ad ignem lignorum nec ad ignem fomacis. 1 V. 5—11 = Migne 1157 D— 1158 A. — 2 y. 18— p. 189.8 = Migne 1176 B— C. Quod si calorem solis Don habnerit, in lenem yentnm aut ad lenem anram ponai, nt snaviter exsiccetor. j^am calor agrimoniae et calor galangae et virtns storacis et calor polipodii ac calor celidoniae maioris Mgidos hnmores, de quibus flegma in homine nascitur, superat, et frigiditas fenigraeci ae fri giditas starkesndbdes frigns eorundem humorum dissipat.
text_translation: And thus, from other pills, let each be made in the same juice, as much as a quarter of a coin weighs, by soaking and then exposing them to the heat of the sun, not however to the fire of wood or to the fire of the furnace. If the heat of the sun is not available, place them in a mild wind or upon a mild fire, so that they may be dried gently. Now, the heat of agrimonia and the heat of galangae and the virtue of storacis and the heat of polipodii and the heat of celidoniae maioris surpasses, and the coldness of fenigraeci and the coldness of starkesndbdes frigens dissipates, the humors from which phlegm in man is born.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed et celidonia maior humores in homine inundare facit, ceterae autem praefatae herbae eos retinent, quatinus leniter ab homine egrediantur. Et ad solem pillulae istae exsiccabuntur, quoniam calor eius sanus est , et non ad ignem lignorum aut fomacis, quod ignes istorum quasi defectum babent, quantum ad solis calorem. Gum autem homo has pillulas sumere voluerit, stomachum suum et ▼entrem agninis vel aliis pellibus circumponat, ut calidus fiat, quoniam calor earum sanus est, et non multum ^ad ignem accedat, quod venae eius inflarentur et sanguis supra modum inundaret et ita humores in iniustum exitum deducerentor; sed calore vestium utatur et iam ante solis ortum eas sumat, quod aurora eo tempore suavis et lenis est.
text_translation: But also greater celidonia causes the humors in man to flood, whereas the other aforementioned herbs retain them, so that they may gently exit from man. And these pills will be dried by the sun, because its heat is healthy, and not toward the fire of wood or fomacis, which these fires have a defect compared to the sun's heat. But when a man wishes to take these pills, he should place his stomach and the belly over lamb's skin or other skins, so that it becomes warm, because their heat is healthy, and not much toward fire, which would cause his veins to swell and blood to flood excessively, and thus the humors would be led to an unjust outcome; but he should use the heat of clothing and take them already before the sun's rising, because at that time the dawn is sweet and gentle.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et vel qninque vel novem pillulas accipiat, ita quod unamquamque singillatim melle modice intingat et singillatim glutiat, quoniam mel calidum et dulce est; quod si mel non habuerit, in offa de triticeo pane facta singillatim in cocleari sumat propter bonum saporem eiusdem offae. Et cum eas sumpserit, modice et paulatim in umbroso loco et non in calore solis deambulet, usque dum solutionem sentiat, quoniam calor et splendor solis humores cerebri in iniustum modum forte educerent.
text_translation: And let him take either five or nine pills, so that each one individually is dipped slightly in honey and each one individually swallowed, because honey is warm and sweet; if he does not have honey, let him take individually in a small dish a piece of bread made from wheat flour, because of the good taste of that bread. And when he has taken them, let him walk moderately and gradually in a shady place and not in the heat of the sun, until he feels a solution, because the heat and brightness of the sun would strongly draw out the humors of the brain in an unjust manner.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Girca mediam vero diem, postquam solutionem in se senserit, vel si eam obdurato stomacho nondum habere poterit, primum ius vel sobriunculam de farina similae factam sorbeat, ut aut viscera per solutionem commota per suavitatem eiusdem iuris vel sobriunculae sanentnr aut induratus stomachus hoc modo mollificetur. Item de sanguinis fluxu. Gui ergo multus sanguis 1 V. — p. 190,1« = Migne 1158 C. HILDEGARDrS de naribus fluit, accipiat anetum et bis tantum millefolii et has herbas virides fronti ac temporibus et pectori sno circumponat.
text_translation: Around midday, after having sensed the dissolution within itself, or if the hardened stomach has not yet been able to have it, first let it take in either the ius or the sobriunculam made from the flour of simile, so that either the viscera moved by the dissolution may be healed by the sweetness of the same ius or sobriunculam, or the hardened stomach may be softened in this manner. Also regarding the flow of blood. Whoever therefore has much blood flowing from the nose, let him take anetum and twice as much millefolium, and let him apply these green herbs around the forehead, temples, and his own chest.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam siccitas et frigiditas aneti ardorem sanguinis extinguit, et calor millefolii sanguinem ad se trahit et eum retinet, ne supra modum inundet. Et hae herbulae virides esse debent, quoniam virtus earum in viriditate praecipue viget; sed et fronti et temporibus ac pectori circumponentur, quod ibi vis venarum est, quae sanguinem portant. Quod si in hieme est, herbas pulverizet et ipsum pulverem modico vino aspersum in saccellum ponat et fronti ac temporibus ac pectori superponat, quemadmodum praedictum est, quoniam calor vini ad restringendum sanguinem eidem pulveri vires tribuit.
text_translation: For the dryness and coldness of aneti extinguish the heat of blood, and the heat of millefolii draws the blood to itself and retains it, so that it does not overflow excessively. And these herbs should be green, because their virtue especially flourishes in greenness; but also applied to the forehead and temples and chest, where the power of the veins is, which carry the blood. If it is in winter, let her crush the herbs and place the powder, sprinkled with a little wine, into a small bag and apply it to the forehead and temples and chest, as previously mentioned, because the heat of the wine imparts strength to the powder for restricting the blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be coriza, Si nimius dolor prae multo fluore in n^ibus hominis excreverit, ille feniculum et quater plus de aneto accipiat ac super lapideam tegulam tecti vel super tenuem laterem igne calefactum ponat et hac et illac feniculum illud et anetum verset, ut fumiget, ac fumum istum et odorem eius naribus et ore introrsum in se trahat, et deinde etiam idem feniculum et idem anetum sic super praedictum lapidem calefactum cum pane comedat. Hoc autem per triduum aut per quatuor aut per quinque dies faciat, quatinus ille fluor capitis et narium tanto suavius solvatur et ut illi effluentes humores tanto suavius separentur.
text_translation: De coriza. Si nimius dolor prae multo fluore in nubis hominis excreverit, ille feniculum et quater plus de aneto accipiat ac super lapideam tegulam tecti vel super tenuem laterem igne calefactum ponat et hac et illac feniculum illud et anetum verset, ut fumiget, ac fumum istum et odorem eius naribus et ore introrsum in se trahat, et deinde etiam idem feniculum et idem anetum sic super praedictum lapidem calefactum cum pane comedat. Hoc autem per triduum aut per quatuor aut per quinque dies faciat, quatinus ille fluor capitis et narium tanto suavius solvatur et ut illi effluentes humores tanto suavius separentur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calor et humiditas feniculi iniuste difiusos et iniuste separatos humores colligit et contrahit, et sicca frigiditas aneti eos exsiccat, cum simul super ignitum lapidem propter sanam naturam illius temperantur, ut praedictum est. JDe potionibus accipiendis. Homo, qui potiones facere et sumere vult, zinziber accipiat et dimidiam partem tanti de liquiricio et tertiam partem de isituar^ quantum de zinzibero, et haec in pulverem redigat et colet, et deinde eundem pulverem simul penset.
text_translation: Nam calor et humiditas feniculi iniuste diffusos et iniuste separatos humores colligit et contrahit, et sicca frigiditas aetatis eorum exsiccat, cum simul super ignitum lapidem propter sanam naturam illius temperantur, ut praedictum est. De potionibus accipiendis. Homo, qui potiones facere et sumere vult, zinziber accipiat et dimidiam partem tanti de liquiricio et tertiam partem de situar quantum de zinzibero, et haec in pulverem redigat et colet, et deinde eundem pulverem simul penset.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea sumat tantum de 1 V. 14—26 = Migne 1157 B— C. — 2 v. -p. 192,ii =: Migne 1137 A— B. zaccaro, quantum palvis iste pensat. Qaod cam feceiit, omnia simal penset ad pondos triginta nanmioram. Deinde accipiat de parissima farinala similae, qaantam dimidia testa nacis capit, et tantam lactis de citocacia, qaantam incisa penna scriptoris in incisione tenere potent, id est sicat scriptor pennam in incaasto intingit, et sic de praedicto palvere et de farinala et de lacte citocaciae tenaissimam massam velat tortellam faciat et hanc massam in qaataor partes dividat et ad solem in Martio vel in Aprili siccet, qaoniam in mensibas istis radias solis ita temperatas est, quod nec nimis calidus nec nimis frigidus (est , et ideo sanitatem praecipue affert.
text_translation: Then take only from 1 V. 14—26 = Migne 1157 B—C. — 2 v. -p. 192,ii =: Migne 1137 A—B. Zaccaro, as much as he thinks in hair. That which he did, all things weigh together to the weight of thirty coins. Then take from the finest flour of similar, as much as half the head of a newborn can hold, and as much milk from citocacia, as much as a cut pen of the scribe can hold in an incision, that is, the scribe dips the pen in the ink, and thus from the aforementioned powder and flour and milk of citocacia make a very tight mass and form a tortellam, and divide this mass into four parts and dry it to the sun in March or in April, because in these months the sun's rays are so tempered, that it is neither too hot nor too cold, and therefore especially promotes health.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si in istis mensibus lac de praedicta citocacia habere non poterit, ita quod eum oportet differre ad Maium, tunc etiam praefato tortello in Maio facto ad solem etiam Maii exsiccet et sic ad opportunum tempus obseryet. Nam calor zinziberi et frigiditas jsfitmris humores congregant, et calor et humiditas zucchari eos retinet et humectat, calor vero et fortitudo similae eos continet, ne iniuste effluant, succus autem citocaciae frigiditate sua eos suaviter et competenter 2o educit, cum sic temperantur, ut praedictum est.
text_translation: If in these months milk from the aforementioned citocacia cannot be obtained, so that it must be delayed until May, then also the aforementioned tortello made in May towards the sun of May should be dried and thus observed at the opportune time. For the heat of ginger and the coldness of jsfitmris gather the humors, and the heat and moisture of sugar retain and moisten them, while the heat and strength of similae contain them, so that they do not unjustly flow forth. But the juice of citocacia, by its own coldness, gently and properly leads them, when thus tempered, as previously stated.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Zinziberum autem et zituar et 'zuccharum et similia bonos bumores in homine retinent, et citocacia malos humores educit; quod si citocacia absque eis, quae praedicta sunt, bomini sola daretur, et bonos et malos humores educeret, quod bonos humores nullum temperamentum bonarum berbarum retineret. In praedictis autem mensibus haec potio parabitur, quod et sol et aura boni temperamenti tunc sunt. Et qui tunc potionem sumere voluerit, quartam partem de praedicta massa ieiunus bibat.
text_translation: Zingiberum autem et zituarum et succharum et similia bonos humores in homine retinent, et citocacia malos humores educit; quod si citocacia absque eis, quae praedicta sunt, bomini sola daretur, et bonos et malos humores educeret, quod bonos humores nullum temperamentum bonarum herbarum retineret. In praedictis autem mensibus haec potio parabitur, quod et sol et aura boni temperamenti tunc sunt. Et qui tunc potionem sumere voluerit, quartam partem de praedicta massa ieiunus bibat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si sto- machus illius tam fortis et spissus est, quod tactum potionis huius non sentit, iterum dimidiam partem tertiae partis praefati tortelli accipiat et dimidiam totam cum lacte citocaciae illiniat et sic iterum in sole siccatum ieiunus sumat. Antequam vero aliquis potionem istam accipiat, ad ignem, si frigus est, se calefaciat et sic sumat, et postquam eam sumpserit, in lecto vigilando modicum requiescat et deinde surgens hac et illac modice deambulet, ita tamen, ne frigus patiatur.
text_translation: If indeed the stomach of that person is so strong and thick that it does not feel the effect of this potion, let him again take half of the third part of the aforementioned tortelli, and mix half of it entirely with the milk of citocaciae, and thus again, when dried in the sun, take it fasting. Before anyone takes this potion, if it is cold, let him warm himself by the fire and thus take it, and after having taken it, let him rest a little while lying awake in bed, and then, rising, walk moderately here and there, nevertheless so that he does not suffer from cold.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De diaeta. Post solutionem autem triticeum panem, non siccum, sed in sobriuncula intinctum comedat et puUos gallinarum et porcinas cames atque alias suaves cames edat, grossum autem panem et bovinas carnes et pisces et alios grossos cibos et assos exceptis assis piris devitet, et a caseo et a crudis oleribus atque crudis pomis se abstineat, yinum autem, sed tamen modice, bibat et aquam dimittat. Glaritatem quoque solis et ignis fugiat et sic per ti'es dies haec observet.
text_translation: On diet. After the resolution, however, let him eat wheat bread, not dry, but soaked in sobriuncula, and let him eat young chickens and pork meat and other sweet meats, but let him avoid thick bread and beef meat and fish and other thick foods and roasted meats except for roasted pears, and let him abstain from cheese and from raw vegetables and raw fruits, but let him drink wine, yet temperately, and let him drink water. Let him also flee from the glare of the sun and fire, and thus let him observe these things for three days.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nihil inveni, nihil scripsij Accipe iecur piscis illius, qui welra dicitur, et ligno de tilia superpone et tunc igne facto lignum istud cum eodem iecore super vivos carbones pone, ut iecur illud exsiccetur, sic tamen, ne fumus et fiamma eiusdem ignis iecur istud tangat, et tam diu idem iecur exsiccari permitte, dum exsiccatum in pulverem redigere possis. Quod si lignum illud interim ab igne consumi videris, antequam iecur exsiccetur, tunc iterum alio eiusdem generis illud superpone.
text_translation: I found nothing, nor did I write. Take the liver of that fish which is called velra, and place it upon wood of lime tree, and then, having made fire, place that wood with the same liver upon live coals, so that the liver may be dried, yet in such a way that the smoke and flame of that fire do not touch that liver, and allow the same liver to be dried as long as you can reduce it to powder. If, however, you see that wood consumed by fire before the liver is dried, then again place another of the same kind upon it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et postquam exsiccatum fuerit, in pulverem redige. Deinde toUe nucem muscatam et fenigraecum et haec super griestein igne modice calefactum sicca et in pulverem redige. Et tunc pulverem istum et illum, quem de iecore praedicti piscis fecisti, in panniolum simul liga modico musco addito, ita quod praedictus pulvis de iecore welre factus tribus partibus pulverem nucis muscatae excedat, et quod pulvis fenigraeci tantus sit, ut pulvis praefati iecoris, et tecum semper porta, et pulvis iste sanitatem corporis tui retinet.
text_translation: And after it has been dried, reduce it to powder. Then take the muscat nut and fenigraecum, and dry these over ash, moderately heated, and reduce them to powder. And then tie together in a cloth the aforementioned powder and that which you made from the liver of the aforementioned fish, adding a little musk, so that the aforementioned powder made from the liver exceeds the powder of the muscat nut by three parts, and that the fenigraecum powder is so much that the powder of the aforementioned liver, and always carry it with you, and this powder retains the health of your body.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De incontinentia, Si homo aliquando ita in delectatione commovetur, quod spuma ad articulum demissionis pervenerit, sed tamen intra in corpore aliquo modo continente retenta fuerit, et inde ille infirmari inceperit, mtham et modicum minus de absinthio accipiat et succum earum exprimat et huic succo zuccarum et plus de melle, quam 1 V. — p. 193,9 = Migne 1156 C— 1156 A. I zuccari sit, addat et tantum vini, quantum succorum istorum est, et ignito calibe in nova olla aut in patella quinquies calefaciat, et postquam modicum comederit, illud calidum bibat.
text_translation: On Incontinence. If a man is ever so moved by delight that foam reaches the articulation of emission, yet remains retained within the body in some manner, and from this he begins to grow weak, let him take a little less absinthium, express the juice of those things, and to this juice add sugar and more honey than 1 V. — p. 193,9 = Migne 1156 C— 1156 A. If sugar is present, add also as much wine as there is of these juices, and in a new pot or patella heat it five times with fire, and after he has eaten a little, let him drink this warm liquid.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si in hieme est, ita quod praedictas herbas habere non potest, baccas lauri et bis tantum de dictampno pulverizet et post modicum cibi in calido vino ignito calibe calefacto bibat. Et sic noxius livor, qui in eo remansit, cum urina et digestione egreditur. Nam in aestate succi isti haberi possunt. Calor enim et frigus ruthae cum calore absinthii coagulatos humores istos ab homine repellit et calor zuccari cum calore mellis hominem hoc modo infirmantem sanant.
text_translation: If it is in winter, so that it cannot have the aforementioned herbs, let it crush laurel berries and twice as much of the aforementioned dittany, and after a little food, drink it in hot wine, heated in a calidarium. And thus the noxious blackness that remained in it is expelled with urine and digestion. For in summer these juices can be had. For heat and cold of rue with the heat of wormwood drive away these coagulated humors from the man, and the heat of sugar with the heat of honey heal the man thus weakened.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum calor vini cum iterato calore calibis et cum praedictis succis temperatur, et cum haec quinquies ad confortationem sui calefaciuntur, post modicum cibum, ne homo in debilitate deficiat, sumantur, et idem homo melius habebit. Sed et in hieme, cum succi herbarum istarum haberi non possunt, cum calor baccarum lauri et calor dictampni cum iterato calore vini temperantur et cum iterato calore calibis confortantur, praedictam infirmitatem, ut praefatum est, ab homine depellunt.
text_translation: And when the heat of wine, with the repeated heat of calidus and with the aforementioned juices is tempered, and when these are five times warmed for their strengthening, after a little food, so that the man does not become weak, they are taken, and the same man will be better. But also in winter, when the juices of these herbs cannot be obtained, when the heat of laurel berries and the heat of dictampni, with the repeated heat of wine, are tempered and strengthened with the repeated heat of calidus, as stated above, they drive away the aforementioned weakness from the man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be octdorum caligine, Sed homo, qui de superfluitate libidinis in oculis caligat, sive masculus sive femina sit, toUat cuteculam fellis piscis illius, qui dicitur welra^ et hanc cuteculam felle efTuso ad solem exsiccet, ut, si quid mali humoris in illa remansit, per fortitudinem caloris solis auferatur, quod fortitudo eiusdem nimis fortis esset oculis, ut eos laederet. Deinde eandem cuteculam purissimo et optimo vino intingat, ut per suavitatem et fortitudinem eiusdem vini temperetur et mollificetur, et ad noctem, cum se in lectum ad repausandum ponit, eam super oculos suos ponat et desuper panno liget, ita tamen ne humiditas eiusdem cuteculae oculos interius tangat, ne per fortitudinem illius laedantur.
text_translation: Be oculorum caligine, Sed homo, qui de superfluitate libidinis in oculis caligat, sive masculus sive femina sit, tollat cuteculam fellis piscis illius, qui dicitur wels, et hanc cuteculam felle effuso ad solem exsiccet, ut, si quid mali humoris in illa remansit, per fortitudinem caloris solis auferatur, quod fortitudo eiusdem nimis fortis esset oculis, ut eos laederet. Deinde eandem cuteculam purissimo et optimo vino intingat, ut per suavitatem et fortitudinem eiusdem vini temperetur et mollificetur, et ad noctem, cum se in lectum ad repausandum ponit, eam super oculos suos ponat et desuper panno liget, ita tamen ne humiditas eiusdem cuteculae oculos interius tangat, ne per fortitudinem illius laedantur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et circa mediam noctem auferat nec diutius iacere permittat, ne fortitudo eius oculos trans- verberet et ne illos ita laedat. In tertia autem nocte et in quinta ac in septima idem faciat, non autem in secimda HildegardiB causae et ourae ed. Eaiser. nec in quarta nec in sexta, ne si supra modum hoc fecerit, inde laedatur. Sed et si de alia qualibet peste quispiam homo caliginem senserit, istud, ut praedictum est, cum eadem cutecula ad reparandum visum faciat.
text_translation: And about midnight, let him remove and do not permit him to lie longer, lest his fortitude strike the eyes and damage them thus. On the third night and on the fifth and on the seventh night, let him do the same, but not on the second HildegardiB causae et urae ed. Eaiser. nor on the fourth nor on the sixth, lest if he does this beyond measure, he be harmed thereby. But also if anyone senses cloudiness from another kind of pestilence, let this, as previously said, be done with the same cuticula to repair the sight.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum praefata cutecula hoc modo, ut dictum est, temperatur, caliginem oculorum aufert, aut deus hoc fieri non permittit. Contra luxuriam. Igitur ut homo delectationem et libidinem camis in se extinguat, accipiat in aestate anetum et bis tantum de hachmenm et lunchwur/s et modicum plus quam hachinmze atque radicem iris Illyricae bis tantum ut bachininze et aschdoch tantum ut iris Illyricae ac omnia haec in acetum incidat et ex eis condimentum faciat et sic frequenter cum omnibus cibis suis comedat.
text_translation: Nam cum praefata cuticula hoc modo, ut dictum est, temperatur, caliginem oculorum aufert, aut deus hoc fieri non permittit. Contra luxuriam. Igitur ut homo delectationem et libidinem carnis in se extinguat, accipiat in aestate anetum et bis tantum de hachmen et lunchwurz et modicum plus quam hachinum et radicem iris Illyricae bis tantum ut bachinum et aschdoc tantum ut iris Illyricae ac omnia haec in acetum incidat et ex eis condimentum faciat et sic frequenter cum omnibus cibis suis comedat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In hieme autem haec pulverizet et pulverem istum item cum escis suis manducet, quod viriditatem earundem herbarum tunc habere non potest. Nam siccitas et frigiditas aneti calorem delectationis extinguit, et fngidus succus hachiminz pravo succo illi resistit, et frigidus et non delectabilis succus lunchwurz pravam dulcedinem hanc aufert, ac virtuosa frigiditas iris Illyricae eandem delectationem superat, et venenosa frigiditas ascheloch pravum venenum delectationis minuit.
text_translation: In winter, however, let her pulverize this and also eat this powder with her meals, for at that time these herbs cannot have their greenness. For dryness and coldness of aneti extinguish the heat of delight, and the cold juice of hachiminz resists the right juice against it, and the cold and unpleasing juice of lunchwurz removes this bad sweetness, and the virtuous coldness of iris Illyricae overcomes the same delight, and the venomous coldness of ascheloch lessens the bad venom of delight.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Contra fantasiam, Qui a diabolico fantasmate in die aut in nocte vigilando aut dormiendo fatigatur, accipiat cingulum de pelle helun et cingulum de pelle capreolae factum et hos duos cum quatuor minutis clavis de calibe factis simul confirmet, scilicet clavo uno ad ventrem, uno ad dorsum, et ad utrumque latus uno. Et cum clavum illum, qui ad ventrem erit, infigit, dicat: ,In fortissima vi omnipotentis dei ad tutamentum meum te adiuro'. Et cum illum, qui ad dorsum erit, imponit, dicat: ,In fortissima vi omnipotentis dei ad tutamentum meum te benedico'.
text_translation: Against fantasy, whoever is fatigued by a diabolical phantom during the day or night, whether watching or sleeping, let him take a belt made from the hide of a helun and a belt made from the hide of a capreola, and let him confirm these two together with four small nails made from calibe, namely one nail to the abdomen, one to the back, and one to each side. And when he drives in the nail that is to the abdomen, let him say: "By the strongest power of the omnipotent God for my protection, I adjure you." And when he places the one that is to the back, let him say: "By the strongest power of the omnipotent God for my protection, I bless you."

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum illum, qui ad dextrum latus erit, imponit, dicat: ,In fortissima vi omnipotentis dei ad tutamentum 1 V. 8—17 = Migne 1158 C— D. menm te constituo'. Et cum illum, qui ad sinistrum latus erit, infigit, dicat: ,In fortissima vi omnipotentis dei ad tutamentum meum te confirmo'. Et hoc cingulo accinctus sit homo omni tempore tam in die quam in nocte, et diaholica fantasmata eum ahhorrehunt ac magica verha eum minus laedimt cum praedicta henedictione ex omni parte corporis sui per ista munitur et circumcingitur.
text_translation: And when he who will be on the right side places it, let him say: "In the most powerful strength of the almighty God for my protection I constitute you." And when he who will be on the left side fastens it, let him say: "In the most powerful strength of the almighty God for my protection I confirm you." And with this girdle girded about him, let the man be at all times both day and night, and demonic phantoms shun him and magical words injure him less, since with the aforementioned benediction he is armed from every part of his body and surrounded by it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calihs est firmamentum et omamentum aliarum rerum et est quasi quaedam adiunctio ad vires hominis, quemadmodum homo fortis est. Sed in helim quaedam fortitudo est, et capreola mundum animal est, et ideo diaholici spiritus ista dedignantur et ea ahhorrent. De ohlivione. Homo, qui ahsque voluntate sua ohliviosus est, urentem urticam accipiat et eam ad succum contundat et deinde illi modicum houmolei addat et, cum dormitum vadit, pectus suum et tempora sua cum eo perungat et hoc saepe faciat, et ohlivio in eo minuetur; acutus enim calor urentis urticae et calor boumolei constrictas venas pectoris et temporum excitat, quae a vigili sensu aliquantum dormitant.
text_translation: For the heaven is the firmament and the ornament of other things and is as it were a certain addition to the powers of man, as a strong man is. But in helim there is a certain strength, and capreola is a world animal, and therefore diabolical spirits disdain these things and abhor them. On forgetfulness. A man who, by his own will, is forgetful, let him take a burning nettle and crush it into juice and then add a little humulus to it, and when he goes to sleep, let him anoint his chest and temples with it and let him do this often, and forgetfulness in him will decrease; for the acute heat of the burning nettle and the heat of humulus excite the constricted veins of the chest and temples, which from the vigilant sense sleep a little.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De singultu, Qui singultum patitur, plurimum de zuccharo accipiat et illud in calida aqua dissolvat et aquam hanc calidam hihat, qaoniam calor zuccari cum suavitate calefactae aquae ariditatem, quam frigus singultus homini infert, humectat, quod etiam calida aqua infirmitati magis convenit quam vinum, quoniam aqua haec suavis est, vinum autem forte. Sed et siccum zuccharum comedat et gariofilum ieiunus frequenter manducet, sed zitwar pransus frequenter comedat et hoc etiam per mensem unum faciat.
text_translation: On Hiccups: Whoever suffers from hiccups should take much sugar and dissolve it in warm water and drink this warm water, because the heat of the sugar with the sweetness of the warmed water moistens the dryness which cold inflicts on the hiccuping man, which warm water is more suitable for weakness than wine, because this water is sweet, wine however is strong. Also eat dry sugar and frequently chew garlic, but after meals frequently eat food and do this also for a month.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam zuccharum siccitatem eiusdem hominis minuit, ao calor quoque gariofili, cum idem homo ieixmat, eum pertransit et calidum facit, calor autem cituaris fortis est et frigus praefatae pestis depellit; et in pranso homine hoc facit, quoniam, si ieiunus esset, fortituda eiusdem 1 V. 13_17 = Migne 1169 A— B; v. p. 219. — 2 y. 27—80 = Migne lUl C. 13 dttMiris ieiunum laederet. Et his idem homo per mensem utetur, quatinus tanto plus ab eis roboretur.
text_translation: Nam succharum siccitatem eiusdem hominis minuit, aut calor quoque garofili, cum idem homo ieiunat, eum pertransit et calidum facit, calor autem citius fortis est et frigus praefatae pestis depellit; et in pranso homine hoc facit, quoniam, si ieiunus esset, fortitudo eiusdem 1 V. 13_17 = Migne 1169 A— B; v. p. 219. — 2 y. 27—80 = Migne lUl C. 13 detrimentum ieiunum laederet. Et his idem homo per mensem utetur, quatinus tanto plus ab eis roboretur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Conira venenum, Pulvis contra venenum et contra magica verba; et sanitatem et fortitudinem atque prosperitatem illi confert, qui eum apud se portat. Accipe storkesnahU radicem unam cum foliis suis et malvae radices duas cum foliis suis atque plantaginis septem radices cum foliis suis, et herbas istas cum eisdem radicibus suis in medio mense Aprili et in media die evelle et super humidam terram pone et modice cum aqua asperge, ut aliquamdiu virides permaneant.
