text_translation: Conira venenum, Pulvis contra venenum et contra magica verba; et sanitatem et fortitudinem atque prosperitatem illi confert, qui eum apud se portat. Accipe stirps radicem unam cum foliis suis et malvae radices duas cum foliis suis atque plantaginis septem radices cum foliis suis, et herbas istas cum eisdem radicibus suis in medio mense Aprili et in media die evelle et super humidam terram pone et modice cum aqua asperge, ut aliquamdiu virides permaneant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum ad vesperam dies inclinat, ad splendorem solis pone, usque dum sol deficiat. Occidente autem sole eas inde tolle et interim per noctem illam super humidam terram pone et modica aqua asperge, ne cito arescant. Et tunc in primo mane aurorae sequentis diei aurora surgente in ipsum folgorem aurorae usque ad tertiam horam diei iterum super humidam terram absque aspersione aquae pone usque ad mediam diem, cum sol ad austrum vergit. Deinde ad mediam diem eas toUe et ad austrum in splendorem solis usque ad nonam horam eiusdem diei pone, postea eas toUe et super pannum pone ligneo assere supposito, ita ne dissipentur aut conterantur, et sic eas permitte iacere usque modicum ante mediam noctem.
text_translation: And when the day inclines toward evening, place it toward the splendor of the sun, until the sun fails. But when the sun sets, take them away from there, and during that night place them upon moist earth and sprinkle them with a little water, so that they do not quickly dry out. And then, on the first morning of the following day, when the dawn rises, place them again toward the splendor of the dawn until the third hour of the day, upon moist earth without sprinkling water, until midday, when the sun turns toward the south. Then, at midday, take them away and place them toward the south in the splendor of the sun until the ninth hour of the same day. Afterwards, take them away and place them upon a cloth with a wooden peg inserted, so that they may not scatter or be crushed, and thus allow them to lie in this manner until a little before midnight.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et tunc, cum rota aquilonis ut rota molendini se circumfert in tenebras rever tens, quia nullam claritatem habere potest et quoniam tunc omnia tenebrosa et noctuma mala fngitiva sunt, quia nox ad diem timc inclinari debet, scilicet paululum wite mediam noctem: easdem herbas pone in altam fenestram aut insuper liminare ianuae aut in aliquem hortum, ut temperatum aerem habere possint, ut ab aere tangi yaleant, et sic iaceant usque post pusillom mediae noctis.
text_translation: And then, when the wheel of the north wind, like the wheel of a mill, turns itself into darkness reverting, because it cannot have any clarity, and because then all things are dark and nocturnal evil fleeting, because night should incline toward day at a time, namely a little have the middle of the night: place those same herbs in a high window or upon the threshold of the door or in some garden, so that they may have temperate air, so that they may be touched by the air and be well, and thus lie until after a little of the middle of the night.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed media nocte iam transacta eas de loco, in quo iacent, aufer ac eas modico digito tere et sic in novum pixidem pone atque modicum bisemum adde, ita tamen, ne odor herbarum istarum ab odore bisemi superetur. Et Jnsemo iste aliquamdiu continet eas, ne putrescant. Deinde quilibet homo herbas istas sic paratas pro fuganda infirmitate et pro retinenda sospitate cotidie ad oculos et ad aures et ad nares ac ad os snum adhibeat, ut odorem earum excipiat.
text_translation: But after midnight has passed, remove them from the place where they lie, and with a moderate finger rub them, and thus place them in a new jar and add a little bisemum, so that the scent of these herbs is not overcome by the scent of the bisemum. And this Jnsemo keeps them for some time so that they do not rot. Then, each person should use these prepared herbs daily for avoiding illness and for maintaining safety, applying them to the eyes and to the ears and to the nostrils and to the mouth, so as to inhale their scent.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et si magna delectatio in viro est, eas ligatas in pannum a lumbis usque ad stirpem ponat; mulier autem ad umbilicum, et refrigerium sentiet. Et si quis- piam cibum comederit, unde dolet, eas in summitatem arti vasculi superius immittat, in quo vinum est, non tamen ut a yino tangantur, sed ut vinum illud odorem earum tantum suscipiat, et yino hoc modicam sobriunculam, id est suffen, paret et ita sorbeat. Sed et si quis lO venenum sumpserit vel si quis magica arte fatigatur, vinnm odore isto perfasum bibat, et melius habebit.
text_translation: And if great delight is in a man, let them be bound in a cloth from the loins down to the root; but a woman to the navel, and she will feel refreshment. And if anyone has eaten some food from which pain arises, let them be placed at the summit of the upper vessel, in which wine is, not however that they be touched by the wine, but that the wine may receive only the scent of them, and let this wine prepare a little sobriunculam, that is suffen, and thus drink it. But also if anyone has taken poison or if anyone is fatigued by magical art, let him drink wine perfumed with this scent, and he will be better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et qui praefatas herbas, ut praedictum est, temperatas apud se portat, sospitatem et fortitudinem retinebit, quod eaedem herbae per omnes horas et per omnem temperiem tam noctis quam diei temperatae sunt. De spasmo. Cum crampho hominem alicubi in corpore suo laedit, houmoleum accipiat et in eodem loco, ubi dolet, cum eo se fortiter perungat, aut si houmoleum non habet, cum alio aliquo pretioso unguento ibi se perungat. Quod si nec hoimoleum nec aliud aliquod unguentum habere poterit, tunc per eundem locum, ubi de crampho dolet, manus suas fortiter movendo hac et illac trahat, et dolor ille cessabit.
text_translation: And he who carries the aforementioned herbs, as previously stated, tempered with him, will retain safety and strength, for these same herbs are tempered throughout all hours and every condition, both of night and day. Concerning spasm. When a cramp afflicts a man somewhere in his body, let him take houmoleum and anoint himself strongly in the same place where it aches, or if he does not have houmoleum, let him anoint himself there with some other precious ointment. If he cannot have either houmoleum or any other ointment, then let him move his hands strongly back and forth over the same place where the cramp aches, and that pain will cease.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calor et virtus houmolei seu virtus aliorum unguentorum fumum melan- coliae ibi fugat, et cum etiam locus eiusdem doloris manu leniter fricatur, idem dolor fugatur. De tortionihm, ^ Homo, qui stechedtm patitur, metram accipiat et eam ad succum fortiter contundat et deinde modicum de butyro vaccarum ei addat et cum illo, ubi dolet, se perungat, et curabitur, quod calor et virtus metrae cum calore et suavitate butyri eundem dolorem fagat et mitigat.
text_translation: Nam calor et virtus humori seu virtus aliorum unguentorum fumum melancholiae ibi fugat, et cum etiam locus eiusdem doloris manu leniter fricatur, idem dolor fugatur. De torsionibus, Homo, qui stichis patitur, metram accipiat et eam ad succum fortiter contundat et deinde modicum de butyro vaccarum ei addat et cum illo, ubi dolet, se perungat, et curabitur, quod calor et virtus metrae cum calore et suavitate butyri eundem dolorem fugat et mitigat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: f^Accipe salviam minus quam cUuwar et feniculum plus quam salviae et cituwar. Postea sume 1 V. 17—19 = Migne 1280 D. — 2 v. 28—31 = Migne 1177 c. — 3 V. 33— p. 198,4 = Migne 1186 D; v. p. 209. merlinsen et vicwur bis tantum ut merlinsen et synape, quod in campo crescit, eodem pondere ut vicwur atqne herbam, in qua cleddun crescunt, minus, quam merlinsen sit. De ira et tristitia. Gum aliquis homo in iram aut in tristitiam provocatur, mox vinum ad ignem calefaciat et modice frigida aqua commisceat et bibat, et sic fumus melancoliae, qui in eo ad iram surrexit, compescitur.
text_translation: Take fennel less than cumin and fenugreek more than fennel and cumin. Afterwards take 1 V. 17—19 = Migne 1280 D. — 2 v. 28—31 = Migne 1177 c. — 3 V. 33— p. 198,4 = Migne 1186 D; v. p. 209. merlinsen and vicwur twice as much as merlinsen and synape, which grows in the field, of equal weight as vicwur and the herb in which cleddun grow, less than merlinsen is. On anger and sadness. When any man is provoked into anger or sadness, quickly heat wine to fire and mix with cold water moderately and drink, and thus the fumes of melancholy, which arose in him to anger, are restrained.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ira, Qui autem in ira ita movetur, quod inde dolendo infirmatur, accipiat baccas lauri et eas super ignitum laterem arefaciat (ac etiam herbas salviae et amaraci ac eas ad solem siccatas pulverizet atque pulverem istum et alium praedictum pulverem de baccis lauri factum in pixidem simul ponat, ita quod pulvis baccarum lauri pulverem salviae excedat et pulvis salviae pulverem amaraci, et sic naribus suis propter bonum odorem apponat. Deinde quoque partem eiusdem pulveris modico vino frigido imponat et sic commisceat, ac cum illo frontem et tempora ac pectus suum perungat.
text_translation: On anger, He who, however, is moved by anger to such an extent that he is weakened by sorrow, let him take laurel berries and apply them over a heated stone (and also let him take herbs of sage and amaraci, and dry them in the sun, then powder them, and let him place that powder and the other aforementioned powder made from laurel berries into a jar together, so that the powder of laurel berries exceeds the powder of sage, and the powder of sage exceeds the powder of amaraci, and thus let him apply it to his nostrils because of the good smell). Then also let him take a part of that same powder and mix it with a little cold wine, and thus let him anoint his forehead and temples and his chest with it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam baccae lauri cali dam et siccam siccitatem habent et humores, quos ira in homine arefecit, humectat, et calor amaraci cerebrum per iram conmiotum sedat et siccus calor salviae humores, quos ira destruxit, congregat. Et cum baccae lauri super ignitum laterem propter sanitatem, quae in ipso est, ex siccantur et cum amaraco et salvia ad solem propter fortitudinem illius, ut praefatum est, ponuntur, et cum sic temperantur, bono calore suo praedictam infirmitatem mitigant.
text_translation: Nam baccae lauri calidam et siccam siccitatem habent et humores, quos ira in homine arefecit, humectat, et calor amaraci cerebrum per iram commotum sedat et siccus calor salviae humores, quos ira destruxit, congregat. Et cum baccae lauri super ignitum latere propter sanitatem, quae in ipso est, exsiccantur et cum amaraco et salvia ad solem propter fortitudinem illius, ut praefatum est, ponuntur, et cum sic temperantur, bono calore suo praedictam infirmitatem mitigant.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et etiam pulvis earum non calefacto vino propter naturalem suavitatem eius temperatus venas frontis et venas temporum ac venas pectoris, quae per iram commotae sunt, sedant, ut praefatum est. De octdorum caligine ex fletu, Qui de fletu lacrimarum in oculis caligat, hic millefolium vel radicem eius accipiat et ad succum modice tundat et tritam oculis ad noctem superponat, ita tamen ne succus eius oculos inte1 V. 82— p. 199,8 — Migne 1175 D— 1176 D.
text_translation: And also the powder of them, not heated by wine, because of its natural sweetness, tempered the veins of the forehead and the veins of the temples and the veins of the chest, which are moved by anger, calms, as mentioned before. Concerning the cloudiness of the eight-day from weeping: He who, from weeping tears, clouds the eyes, let him take millefolium or its root and moderately grind it to make a decoction, and apply the ground mixture to the eyes at night, nevertheless so that the juice does not cover the eyes. V. 82— p. 199,8 — Migne 1175 D— 1176 D.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: rius tangat, et desuper panno liget fere usque ad mediam noctem et tunc auferat. Hoc facto mox optimo et purissimo vino cilia oculorum modice circumliniat. Nam calor millefolii sanat et succus eius aliquantum acer est et ulcera mundat, et quod etiam yinum calidum est et ali- quantum acrum est, acerbitatem succi millefolii aufert, et sic oculi sanantur. De risu immoderato. Qui multo et immoderato risu motus et concussus dolet, nucem muscatam pulverizet et huic bis minus zuccari addat et sic calefacto vino imponat to et illud tam ieiunus quam pransus bibat.
text_translation: Let the redness touch, and cover with a cloth nearly until midnight and then remove. After this, then with the best and purest wine, lightly apply around the eyelashes. For the heat of yarrow heals and its juice is somewhat sharp and cleanses ulcers, and since the wine is also warm and somewhat bitter, it removes the sharpness of yarrow's juice, and thus the eyes are healed. On immoderate laughter. He who suffers from movement and concussion due to much and immoderate laughter, let him crush a muscat nut and add twice as much sugar to it, and thus apply it to the wine heated, and let him drink it whether fasting or having eaten.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Immoderatus enim risus pulmonem arefacit et iecur concutit, sed calor nucis muscatae iecur sanat, et calor et succus zuccari pulmonem renovat. Et cum haec alterato calore vini temperantur et ita sumuntur, bonos humores per immoderatum risum destructos restituunt. De ebrietate. Ut ebrius ad se redeat, caniculatam tollat et eam in frigidam aquam ponat et cum eadem aqua frontem et tempora ac guttur suum madefaciat, et ad se redeundo melius habebit, quod cum frigiditas cani- culatae frigiditati aquae coniungitur, procellam et calorem, qui in venis frontis et temporum hominis sunt, sedat.
text_translation: Excessive laughter indeed damages the lungs and shakes the liver, but the heat of mace nut heals the liver, and the heat and juice of sugar restores the lungs. And when these are tempered with the altered heat of wine and thus taken, they restore the good humors destroyed by excessive laughter. Concerning drunkenness. So that a drunkard may return to himself, let him take up caniculata and place it into cold water, and with the same water let him moisten his forehead and temples and his throat, and returning to himself he will be better, for when the coldness of caniculata is joined to the coldness of the water, it calms the storm and heat which are in the veins of the forehead and temples of man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si in autumno est, de cruda vite palmitem cum recentibus foliis accipiat et fronti et temporibus ac gutturi suo circumponat, et refrigerabitur, quoniam maior virtus eo tempore in palmitibus vitis est quam alio tempore, et ideo tunc propter naturam et cognationem vini procellam et inundationem vini, quae in venis frontis et temporum ac gutturis hominis sunt, mitigat. Si omnia haec praedicta habere non poterit, feniculum vel semen feniculi comedat, et lenius habebit, quod suavis calor et suavis virtus feniculi insaniam vini in ipso compescit.
text_translation: If it is in autumn, let him take a palm leaf from a raw vine with fresh leaves and apply it to his forehead, temples, and throat, and he will be cooled, because greater virtue is in the palm leaves of the vine at that time than at another time, and therefore then, because of the nature and kinship of wine, the storm and flood of wine, which are in the veins of the forehead, temples, and throat of man, are mitigated. If he cannot have all these things forementioned, let him eat fennel or fennel seeds, and he will have it more gently, because the sweet heat and sweet virtue of fennel checks the madness of wine in him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ad vomitum. Qui nausiam patitur, ciminum accipiat et ad eius tertiam partem piper atque bibinellae velut quartam partem cimini et haec pulverizet et puram fari- 1 V. 33— p. 200,11 =« Migne 1138 B. nam similae accipiat et pnlyerem istum farinae huic immittat, et sic cum vitello ovi ac cum modica aqua tortellos aut in calida fomace aut sub calidis cineribus faciat et tortellos istos comedat, sed et praedictum pulverem super panem positum manducet.
text_translation: Ad vomitum. Qui nausiam patitur, ciminum accipiat et ad eius tertiam partem piper atque bibinellae velut quartam partem cimini et haec pulverizet et puram fari- 1 V. 33— p. 200,11 =« Migne 1138 B. nam similae accipiat et pulverem istum farinae huic immittat, et sic cum vitello ovi ac cum modica aqua tortellos aut in calida fomace aut sub calidis cineribus faciat et tortellos istos comedat, sed et praedictum pulverem super panem positum manducet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum frigiditas cimini et frigiditas bibinellae et frigiditas yitelli ovi cum calore piperis et cum calore farinae similae temperantur et cum suayitate aquae componuntur ac cum suavi calore fornacis, ut praedictum est, excoquuntur, iniuste calidos et iniuste frigidos humores, qui nausiam homini inferunt, compescunt. Accipe ergo Jcumel et ad eius tertiam partem piper atque bibinellae velut quartam partem ktmeles et haec pulveriza et accipe dtmst similae et pulverem istum in farinam hanc mitte et sic cum vitello ovi ac modica aqua tortellos fac in calida fomace vel herde et tortellos istos comede, sed et praedictum pulverem super panem positum manduca.
text_translation: Nam cum frigiditas cimini et frigiditas bibinellae et frigiditas vitelli ovi cum calore piperis et cum calore farinae similae temperantur et cum suavitate aquae componuntur ac cum suavi calore fornacis, ut praedictum est, excoquuntur, iniuste calidos et iniuste frigidos humores, qui nausiam homini inferunt, compescunt. Accipe ergo Juglans et ad eius tertiam partem piper atque bibinellae velut quartam partem cuminum et haec pulveriza et accipe dustum similae et pulverem istum in farinam hanc mitte et sic cum vitello ovi ac modica aqua tortellis fac in calida fornace vel herde et tortellis istos comede, sed et praedictum pulverem super panem positum manduca.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De dissinteria, Qui dissinteriam patitur, ovorum vitellos albugine abiecta accipiat et illos in scutella terendo pulset. Quod cum fecerit, ciminum et modicum piper tritum in testas ovorum reponat et ad ignem asset et dolenti, postquam modicum comederit, ad manducandum tribuat. Vitellus enim ovi siccus et livore permixtus est et quaeque ad se trahit et ea, quae soluta sunt, constringit, et pullus ex eo nascitur, et ideo solutos humores contrahit; ciminum quoque frigiditate sua soluta constringit; piper autem haec duo calore suo temperat, ut tanto fortius solutos humores contineat, ut praedictum est.
text_translation: On dissinteria. He who suffers from dissinteria should take the yolks of eggs, having cast away the white, and let him pound them in dishes. When he has done this, let him put crushed cumin and a little pepper into the eggshells, and set them to the fire, and give them to the patient to eat after he has eaten a little. For the yolk of the egg is dry and mixed with bile, and it draws everything to itself and constricts what is loosened, and from it the chick is born, and therefore it draws the loosened humors; cumin also, by its coldness, constricts the loosened; pepper, however, tempers these two with its heat, so that they hold the loosened humors more strongly, as has been said.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sumat etiam alios viteIIos ovorum et ad ignem in sartagine ab eis sagimen exprimat, et cum illo et cum pura farina similae factis tortellis iIIos post modicum cibum manducet, quod sagimen vitellorum cum calore et fortitudine similae temperatum eandem soIutioDem sedat. Quidcumque autem idem infilrmus interim comedit, calidum comedat, quod stomachus et intestina eius atque humores in ipso infrigidati sunt; et quae mollia sunt et quae suavem saporem habent, manducet et pullos gallinarum et ceteras molles carnes et pisces; sed allec et salinonem devitet, bovinas quoque carnes et caseum et grossa et cruda olera et porrum devitet atque siliginem ed ordeaceum panem et quidcumque assum est, praeter assam piram, non comedat.
text_translation: Let him also take other yolk of eggs and extract the sagimen from them by heating in the sartagine; and with it and with pure flour of similae made into tortellis, let him eat them after a little food, which the sagimen of the yolk tempered with the heat and strength of similae calms the same solution. Whatever the same weak person eats in the meantime, let him eat warm, because his stomach and intestines and humors in him have been chilled; and let him eat those that are soft and those that have a sweet taste, and the young chickens and other soft meats and fish; but let him avoid ale and salted fish, also the beef and cheese and coarse and raw vegetables and leek, and let him avoid the siliginem and the ordeaceum bread and whatever is assumed, except the roasted pear, not eat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: l^am omnia haec pulverem intestinorum duritia et as- peritate sua exasperant, et aqua livorem in eis parat; et ideo interim dum vitanda sunt; vinum autem calore ea constringit. De fltixu sanguinis. Si quis fiuxum sanguinis habet, accipe duos vitellos ovi et eos distempera et succum lo metrae adde, quantum medietas unius vitelli, et aceti, quantum duae testae ovi capiunt, et tunc modicum de pulvere cinamomi et de pulvere zituar minus, quam pulveris cinamomi sit, eis immitte, et cum haec simul miscueris, cum modica aqua suffun aliquantum spissum inde para et da illi, qui fiuxum sanguinis patitur, modice calidum tam ieiuno quam pranso ad sorbendum, et sic saepe fac, et melius habebit.
text_translation: Already all these things irritate the powder of the intestines by their hardness and roughness, and water prepares a yellowish color in them; therefore, they should be avoided for a time; wine, however, by its heat constricts them. Concerning the flux of blood. If anyone has a flux of blood, take two calves' eggs and temper them, and add the juice of lo metrae as much as half a calf, and vinegar as much as two eggshells can hold, and then a little of the powder of cinnamon and of the powder of zituar, less than the powder of cinnamon is, put into them, and when you have mixed these together, with a little water, make a thick consistency from it and give it to the one who suffers from flux of blood, slightly warm, both when fasting and after a meal, to drink, and do this often, and he will be better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: liem de fluxu aanguinis per poateriora. Qui fluxum sanguinis patitur, bramberecruiJi et bis tantum hluthcruth accipiat et modice tundat, scilicet usque dum succum reddat, et sic in vinum ponat et inter cibum et post cibum sed non leiunus bibat. Bramiberecruffi enim et blulhcrtUh quasi de liquore sanguinis succum habent, et haec cum vino temperata calore et &igore suo effiuentem sanguinem sedant; et a pranso homine sumuntur, ut sanguinem constringant, qui tunc cibis hominis accurrit.
text_translation: Liber de fluxu sanguinis per posteriora. Qui fluxum sanguinis patitur, bramberecruiJi et bis tantum hluthcruth accipiat et modice tundat, scilicet usque dum succum reddat, et sic in vinum ponat et inter cibum et post cibum sed non leiunus bibat. Bramiberecruffi enim et blulhcrtUh quasi de liquore sanguinis succum habent, et haec cum vino temperata calore et &igore suo effiuentem sanguinem sedant; et a pranso homine sumuntur, ut sanguinem constringant, qui tunc cibis hominis accurrit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Earinam quoque sinulae accipiat et ex ea cum solo melle et modico sale tortellos faciat et eos comedat. Nam farina similae calore et fortitudine sua carnem hominis crescere facit etso iniustum iter sanguinis concludit, et mel calore et succo suo iniustum sanguinem sedat, ac calor salis eum siccat Et cum simul temperantur, iniuste effluentem sanguinem continent. Interim autem dum infirmitatem hanc patitur, panem triticeum comedat, sed siligineum et ordeaceum de- vitet, bovinas quoque cames et porcinas et omnes pisces, qui squamas non habent, ac caseum et cruda olera et cruda poma et quidquid assum sit, devitet.
text_translation: Let her also take wheat, and from it with pure honey and a little salt make tortelli, and let her eat them. For wheat flour, by its heat and strength, causes the flesh of man to grow and stops the unjust course of blood, and honey, by its heat and juice, calms the unjust blood, and the heat of salt dries it. And when they are combined together, they contain the unjust effluent blood. Meanwhile, however, while she suffers this weakness, let her eat triticum bread, but let her avoid siligineum and ordeaceum, also bovine and pork meats and all fish that do not have scales, and let her avoid cheese and raw vegetables and raw fruits and whatever is taken, avoid.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Reliquas autem carnes, quae moUes sunt, et reliquos pisces comedat et ius pisae sorbeat, ipsam autem pisam et lentem et fabam devitet. Sed et omnem cibum calidum ne comedat, sed temperatum accipiat, ita quod nec calidus nec Mgidus sit. Suave quoque vinum bibat, sed aquam interim devitet, quod haec omnia nociva sunt homini infirmitatem hanc habenti, ut etiam de dissinteria dictum est. De emoptoica passione. Si mali et concreti et venenosi humores in homine superhabundaverint et eum sanguinem excreare et vomere per aliqua tempora fecerint, interim nullam medicinam ille homo sibi adhibeat, ne sanguis per medicinam exterritus ipsum interius exulceret et plus solito effluat.
text_translation: The remaining meats, which are soft, and the remaining fish, let him eat, and let him drink the broth of fish, but let him avoid the fish itself and lentils and beans. Moreover, let him not eat any hot food, but let him take temperate food, so that it is neither hot nor cold. Let him also drink sweet wine, but let him avoid water in between, for all these things are harmful to a man suffering from this weakness, as was also said regarding dysentery. Regarding the passion of emaciation. If bad, congealed, and venomous humors have excessively abounded in a man and have caused him to excrete and vomit blood for some time, then during this period let that man not apply any medicine to himself, lest the blood, terrified by the medicine, ulcerate within him and flow forth more than usual.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed postquam idem sanguis ho minem illum aliquantum fatigare cessaverit, salviam in leni et suavi vino modice aqua mixto coquat modico quoque boumoleo aut butyro addito, et sic cocto per paunum colet et modice et non ad sufficientiam nec ieiunus sed pransus libat. Salvia enim interiori foeditati humorum resistit, vinum autem cum suavitate aquae temperatum infirmum illum refocillat et confortat, boumoleum vero aut butyrum illum interius sanat. De emorroidis.
text_translation: But after the same blood has ceased to fatigue the man somewhat, let sage be cooked in a mild and sweet wine mixed with a little water, and also with a little boumoleum or butter added; and thus, after being cooked for a short time, let it be cooled slightly and not to sufficiency, and not fasting but having eaten, let him drink it. For sage resists the inner filth of the humors, but wine tempered with the sweetness of water refreshes and strengthens the weak man, and indeed boumoleum or butter heals him internally. About hemorrhoids.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si mali et aquosi aut tenues humores superhabundaverint in homine et sanguinem absque egestione ciborum per posteriora illius transire fecerunt, ille huiusmodi fiuxum sanguinis in se restringere non quaerat, quoniam, si aliter fecerit, idem sanguis in maiorem stuporem vertetur et in maiore eflFusione emanabit. Item de sanguine, 2 Si sanguis in homine malis et aquosis humoribus excitatus per posteriora eius cum egestione ciborum effluere ceperit, ille eum non restringat, quod purgationem ei affert et quoniam ei pro purgatione est.
text_translation: If malous and aqueous or thin humors have superabounded in a man and have caused blood to pass through his lower parts without consumption of food, he should not seek to restrict this kind of flowing blood within himself, because, if he does otherwise, the same blood will turn into greater stupor and will emanate in greater effusion. Also regarding blood, 2 If blood, stimulated in a man by malous and aqueous humors, begins to flow out through his lower parts along with consumption of food, he should not restrict it, because it brings purgation to him and because it is for his purgation.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si supra modum idem cum egestione per eum 1 V. 9-22 = Migne 1154 D— 1155 A. — 2 y. 30— p. 203, = Migne 1180 D. ' trausit, gamandream oleribus et aliis bonis herbulis addat, ex quibus cibum faciat et ita eundem cibum moderate comedat; et gamandrea praedictum sanguinem minuit et eum restringit, olera autem et aliae herbae eum refocillant. Item de emoptoica, Si ex amaritudine cordis et cogitationum splen et pulmo hominis laeduntur, ita quod homo in his interius oppressus sanguinem excreare et evomere cogitur, eandem emissionem sanguinis in se repente non restringat, ne inde interius exulceratus sanguinem magis et magis evomat; sed cum idem yomitus sanguinis in eo aliquamdiu cessaverit, in pollice dexterae manus sanguinem minuat, quatinus sanguis illuc tendat, qui in slnistra parte hominis eundem dolorem affert.
text_translation: But if the same thing is exceeded in degree through him, let him add gamandrea with oils and other good herbs, from which he makes food and thus eats that same food moderately; and the aforementioned gamandrea reduces and restricts the blood, but the oils and other herbs refresh it. Likewise concerning emoptoica: If the spleen and lung of a man are harmed by the bitterness of the heart and thoughts, so that a man is internally oppressed and compelled to excrete and vomit blood, let him not suddenly restrain that emission of blood, lest he be more and more vomited from within, ulcerated internally; but when the same vomiting of blood has ceased in him for some time, let him reduce the blood in the right thumb, so that the blood may tend there, which in the left part of the man causes the same pain.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic ille curabitur. Item de sanguine, Si per tristitiam interiores venulae in homine ita constringuntur, quod ille interdum sanguinem evomit, eundem sanguinem restringere non festinet, sed in quiete et in silentio sustineat, usque dum per se in ipso cesset, ne si repente restringere curaverit, inde potius laedatur quam iuvetur, quod efduere tandem per se cessabit. De erisipHa, Oum pustula, quae freislicha dicitur, in homine cum tumore se inflaverit, muscas accipiat et capitibus earum abiectis eas conterat, (et eis tritis circa tumorem illum circulum faciat, quod venenum muscarum istarum resistit veneno pustulae illius, ne ultra procedat.
text_translation: And thus he will be healed. Also concerning blood: If, through sadness, the inner veins in a man constrict so that he sometimes vomits blood, he should not hasten to restrain that blood, but endure it in quiet and silence until it ceases of its own accord, lest if he suddenly restrains it, it be harmed rather than helped, for eventually it will cease of itself. Concerning erysipha, a pustule called freislicha in a man, when it swells with a tumor, let him take flies and crush their heads, and with the crushed heads make a circle around the tumor, for the venom of these flies resists the venom of that pustule, so that it does not proceed further.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde rubeam testudinem, quae absque concha est, sumat et ea trita cum ipsa circulum faciat circa circulum illum, quem cum musca facit, et livor eius tabem praedicti tumoris Cjpmpescit, ut cesset, quod, quod malum est, malo repugnat. Postea succum lilii accipiat et cutem, quae circa circulum est, quem cum testudine fecit, cum eo perungat, quoniam idem succus dolorem iUum fugat et sanitatem affert. Deinde folium vehedisteHes accipiat et illud super eandem pustulam ponat atque de pura farina similae tortellum faciens eum super idem folium et super totum tumorem illum cum panno desuper liget, quati nus mollificetur, ut per se rumpatur, quoniam frigiditas et succus vehedisteles periculosa non sunt, sed cum calore similae duritiam illam mollem reddunt.
text_translation: Then take a red tortoise shell, which is without a shell, and grind it, making a circle around the circle which the fly makes, and its bruise checks the decay of the aforementioned tumor, so that it ceases, for that which is evil repels evil. Afterwards, take the juice of the lily and the skin which is around the circle which was made with the tortoise shell, and anoint with it, for the same juice drives away that pain and brings health. Then take the leaf of vehedistella and place it upon the same pustule, and making a tortellum from pure flour of simile, place it upon the same leaf and upon the entire tumor, binding it with a cloth above, so that the swelling becomes softened, so that it may rupture by itself, for the coldness and juice of vehedistella are not dangerous, but with the heat of simile they make that hardness soft.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si per se rupta non fuerit, cum arida et lignea spina seu alia arida astula dirumpat, non autem ullo ignito vel frigido ferro aut acu, quoniam pustula et tumor ille materiam igneam et frigidam exhorresceret , ita quod periculum facile subsequeretur. Sed interim dum homo tumorem pustulae huius patitur, ab igne et a frigore et a yento et ab humido aere se muniat, atque ab omni calido et asso et grosso cibo et a yino se abstineat et cruda olera et cruda poma devitet, quod omnia noxios humores in eo augerent et accenderent, sed solunmiodo pulchrum et triticeum panem in aqua comedat et aquam bibat, quo niam illo tempore eidem homini propter suavitatem eius periculosum non est.
text_translation: If it has not been ruptured by itself, when a dry and wooden thorn or another dry plant breaks it, not however by any heated or cold iron or needle, because that pustule and tumor would cause the matter to become fiery and cold, so that danger would easily follow. But in the meantime while the man suffers this tumor and pustule, he should arm himself against fire and cold and wind and damp air, and abstain from all hot and fatty and heavy food and wine, and eat raw herbs and raw fruit, because all these would augment and inflame noxious humors within him, but only eat beautiful and wheat bread in water and drink water, so that at that time for him because of its pleasantness it is not dangerous.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si idem homo delicatus est, cum puro yitello oyi simplicem sobriunculam de farina similae absque sagimine et absque caseo faciat et infrigidatam manducet. Haec autem tam diu obseryet, dum dolorem illum mitigatum cessasse yiderit. Vinum autem interim yitabit, quoniam yenas eius impleret et ita tumorem illum intumescere faceret. Galidos quoque cibos non comedat, quod illi calore suo humores commoyerent, et ita tumor ille augmentaretur. Contra cancrum et ad qmelihet ulcera et ad dolorem capitis, Accipe yiolas et succum earum exprime ac eum per pannum cola et ad tertiam partem succi huius oleum oliyae pensa et deinde ad quantitatem succi yiolae hircinum sepum pondera et haec simul in olla noya fac feryere, et sic unguentum fit.
text_translation: If the same man is delicate, let him prepare with pure veal a simple, sober dish made of sima flour without sagimine and without cheese, and let him eat it chilled. Let him observe this as long as he sees the pain cease to be mitigated. Meanwhile, let him avoid wine, because it would fill his veins and thus cause the tumor to swell. Let him also not eat gall-related foods, because they would move the humors with their heat, and thus the tumor would increase. Against cancer and for ulcers and for headache, take violets and press their juice, and let it be filtered through a cloth, and then mix a third part of this juice with pounded olive oil, and then weigh goat fat in the quantity of the violet juice, and let these be boiled together in a noya pot, and thus an ointment is made.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Tunc locum corporis, ubi cancer aut alii yermes hominem comedunt, drcumunge et etiam desuper imge; et morientur, cum de eo gustayerint. Sed et alia ulcera, ubi homo dolet, cum ipso unguento perunge, et si quis in capite dolet, frontem eius in trans yersum cum eo unge. 1 V. 25—33 = Migne 1170 D. De scabie. ^jHomo, qui diversa ulcera et scabiem patitur, cerifolium accipiat et ter plus de polipodio quam cerifolii ac enulae quinquies plus, quam cerifolii sit, et haec in aqua coquat.
text_translation: Then the place in the body where cancer or other vermes consume the man, circumunge and also desuper imuge; and they will die, when they have tasted of it. But also other ulcers, where the man suffers, anoint with the same unguent, and if anyone suffers in the head, anoint his forehead in trans yersum with it. 1 V. 25—33 = Migne 1170 D. De scabie. ^jThe man who suffers diverse ulcers and scabies, let him take cerifolium and ter plus of polipodio than cerifolii, and enulae quinquies plus than cerifolii, and let these be cooked in water.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea aqua hac expressa et per pannum colata eam in sartaginem fundat atque modicum novi thuris et sulphuris addat et novam et recentem aryinam porci plus, quam aliorum praedictorum sit, addat, ut ita simul in sartagine super ignem aliquantum yelut nnguentum exspissetur. Et cum hoc unguento dolens circa ulcera et super ulcera se inunguat. Sed et prae- lO dictas herbas coctas et calidas aqua aliquantum expressa cuti et ulceribus superponat, hoc autem per quinque dies faciat, ut cutis et caro eius per istud transfundatur, et postea idem dolens balneo se abluat, quatinus livores isti et foetor de ipso auferantur.
text_translation: Afterwards, the water thus expressed and filtered through a cloth is poured into the sartaginem, and a little new thuris and sulfur is added, and also fresh and recent pork fat is added, more than the aforementioned herbs, so that thus, together in the sartaginem over fire, it may melt somewhat into an ointment. And with this ointment, the patient anoints himself around the ulcers and upon the ulcers. But also, the aforementioned herbs, boiled and warm, expressed somewhat with water, are applied to the skin and ulcers, and this is done for five days, so that the skin and flesh of him may be transfused through this. Afterwards, the same patient bathes himself in a bath, in order that these lividnesses and odor may be removed from him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cerifolium aeque ca- lorem et frigus habet, et cum haec calore polipodii et calore enulae et suavitate aquae et cum calore sulphuris et cum calore thuris et cum calore arvinae et cum alieno igne temperatur, pravos humores in homine fagat. Cerifolium autem, quod calorem et frigus habet, iniustum calorem et iniustum frigus ulcerum et scabiei aufert, calor autem polipodii pravos humores exsiccat, et calor enulae eos fugat, calor quoque thuris sanat et calor sulphuris eos attenuat, calor autem aryinae ulcera et scabiem leniter sanat, cum recens est.
text_translation: For cerifolium possesses equally heat and cold, and when these are tempered with the heat of polypodii and the heat of enulae and the sweetness of water and with the heat of sulfur and with the heat of thuris and with the heat of arvinae and with alien fire, it corrupts the humors in a man. Cerifolium, however, which has heat and cold, removes unjust heat and unjust cold of ulcers and scabies; the heat of polypodii dries up the corrupt humors, and the heat of enulae drives them away; the heat of thuris heals, and the heat of sulfur lessens them; the heat of aryinae gently heals ulcers and scabies when it is fresh.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ictericia, Qui gelewesuch habet, yerbenam acoipiat et bis tantum de cephania, ut yerbenae est, et nimmoli ter tantum ut yerbenae, yel si cephaniam non habuerit, saxifricam eodem pondere, ut yerbenae est, accipiat et herbas istas in optimum yinum ponat in yasculo recondito superius fortiter cooperto. Et yinum istud per novem dies ieiunus bibat, sed pransus modice bibat atque cum eodem vino et ovo ac sagimine paret et comedat. £t cum dormitum vadit, idem vinum ignito calibe calefactum bibat et se calido vestimento tegat, et ut sudet; hoc 1 v.
text_translation: On jaundice: Whoever has jaundice should take herb (herbanam) and twice as much from cephalica, as much as the herb is, and take saxifraga of the same weight, as the herb is, if he has not cephalica; let him place these herbs into the best wine in a sealed vessel, strongly covered above. And let him drink this wine for nine days fasting, but if he eats, let him drink moderately with the same wine and eat with it an egg and sage. And when he goes to sleep, let him drink the same wine heated in a hot caldron, and cover himself with a warm garment, and let him sweat; this 1 v.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 1—15 ^ Migne 1160 C. autem faciat, dum sanetur. Galor enim verbenae et calor cephaniae ac calor nimmoli seu frigiditas saxifricae aliqnantum acutos succos habent, et cum calore vini temperantur et in potu sumuntur vel cum calore vini vel calore sagi minis ac cum frigiditate ovi propter bonum saporem componuntur, ut praefatum est, acerbitati fellis et melancoliae resistunt et praedictam pestem sedant. Potus autem iste a ieiuno homine praecipue sumetur, quatinus membra eius citius pertranseat, quam si pransus sit; quin etiam ignito calibe confortabitur, ut tanto fortius eandem pestem compescat.
text_translation: But let it be made, while it is being healed. For Galor and verbenae and the heat of cephaniae and the heat of nimmoli or the coldness of saxifricae have somewhat sharp juices, and they are tempered with the heat of wine and are taken in drink either with the heat of wine or the heat of sagi minis and with the coldness of eggs, for the sake of good taste, as was said before, and they resist the acerbity of bile and melancholia and calm the aforementioned pestilence. This drink should be taken especially by a fasting man, so that it may pass through his limbs more quickly than if he had eaten; indeed, it will also be strengthened by the ignited calibe, so that it may restrain the same pestilence all the more strongly.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De epylempsia. Qui a caduco morbo fatigatur, sanguinem talpae accipiat et eum exsiccet et etiam rostrom anetae scilicet feminae ac ungues anseris etiam feminae absque cute et came accipiat et haec in pulverem radendo aut terendo redigat, ita quod pulvis rostri anetae bis tantus sit ut pulvis anseris et quod sanguis talpae bis excedat pulverem, qui de rostro anetae factus est, et haec simul in pannum cum praedicto sanguine ligata in loco, ubi talpa noviter terram eiecit, per triduum ponat.
text_translation: On Epilempsia. Whoever is fatigued by the falling disease, let him take the blood of the mole and dry it, and also take the beak of the goose, that is, the female, and the nails of the goose, even the female without skin and flesh, and reduce these to powder by grinding or rubbing, so that the powder of the goose's beak is twice as much as the powder of the goose, and the blood of the mole is twice as much as the powder made from the goose's beak, and this, together with the aforementioned blood, tied in a cloth, place in the location where the mole newly throws the earth, for three days.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea inde ablata, ubi glacies est, ponat, ut congeletur, et iterum inde ablata in sole siccari faciat. Deinde sumat partem iecoris cuiusvis animalis et volucris, quae comeduntur, quantum de his habere potuerit, et ex ipsis cum parva farinula -25 similae tortellos paret et de praedict-o pulvere minus addat, quam praedictorum iecorum sit, et modicum ciminum et sic comedat. Nam quod talpa aliquando se ostendit, ali quando etiam se abscondit, et quod terram fodere solet, sanguis eius huic morbo, qui interdum sentitur, interdum celatur, resistit.
text_translation: Afterward, having removed it, place it where there is ice, so that it may freeze, and again, having removed it, let it be dried in the sun. Then take a part of the liver of any animal or bird that is eaten, as much as it may be able to have of these, and from these with a little flour prepare tortellus similar to this, and from the aforementioned powder add less than there is of the aforementioned livers, and a little cumin, and thus eat. For that which the mole sometimes shows itself, sometimes also hides itself, and which usually digs the earth, its blood resists this disease, which sometimes is felt, sometimes conceals itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et quod etiam fortitudo anetae in rostro est et quod cum rostro munda et immunda tangit, etiam huic morbo, qui praeceps et tacitus est, repugnat. Ita etiam et ungues anseris, qui aqua perfusi sunt et qui etiam quaeque sordida tangunt, gich eidem morbo sedat, quoniam idem morbus cum gutta saepe excitatur. Bostrum 1 V. — p. 208,8 == Migne 1334 A— B. autem boc et nngaes isti feminae esse debent, et non maris, propter tacitumitatem eius, quoniam femina magis quam masculus silet, sicut et morbus iste silet usque ad horam illam, qua bominem deiecit.
text_translation: And also the fortitude of the anetae is in the snout, and that with the snout touches both pure and impure things, and even this disease, which is sudden and silent, opposes. Thus also the claws of the geese, which are soaked with water and which also touch each thing filthy, calm the same disease, because the same disease is often excited with a drop. Bostrum 1 V. — p. 208,8 == Migne 1334 A— B. but this and these claws should be of women and not of men, because of their silence, for woman is more silent than man, just as this disease is silent until that hour when it casts out the man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et simul commixta in locum, ubi talpa fodit, ponenda simt, quod eadem terra sanior quam alia est, quatinus ibi succum et viriditatem suam cum succo et viriditate terrae accipiant, quod etiam prius succo terrae perfasa sunt, et deinde etiam per glaciem constringuntur, ut foeditas, quae in eo (est , comprimatur. Ac etiam ad solem exsiccabuntur, ut si quid lo foeditatis in eis remansit, per ardorem solis auferatur. Sed et iecora tam animalium quam volucrum cum simila commiscebuntur, quoniam iecora sicca sunt et virtutem suam de terra babent et etiam livores ad se trabunt, quatinus illa cum calore et fortitudine similae pravos bumores morbi huius extrabant, cimino addito, quod frigiditate sua iniustos ardores morbi buius compescit.
text_translation: And simultaneously mixed, they should be placed in the place where the mole digs, because the same earth is healthier than others, so that there they may receive their own juice and greenness along with the juice and greenness of the earth, which they were previously perfected by the juice of the earth, and then also constricted by ice, so that the filth which is in them may be compressed. And also they will be dried toward the sun, so that if anything of filth remains in them, it may be removed by the heat of the sun. But also the excreta of both animals and birds are mixed with them, because the excreta are dry and have their virtue from the earth and also attract lividness to themselves, so that those [excreta] with the heat and strength of the similar [substances] may expel the bad humors of this disease, with the addition of the coldness of the ice, which by its coldness checks the unjust heats of this disease.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui autem praefatom morbum patitur, per quinque dies praefatos tortellos comedat, et si nondum convaluerit, per alios quinque dies eis utatur. £t si nondam proderit, item per alios quinque dies eos comedat, et ita septies eodem numero quinque dies faciat. Fanem autem et cames etiam interim comedat cum apio et petrosolino coctas, quoniam cames istae aliquantum siccae sunt nec malos livores in se babent, et etiam suavis frigiditas apii et petrosolini sordes et febres stomacbi pur- gant, qnae etiam morbum istum aliquando suscitant.
text_translation: Whoever suffers the aforementioned illness, let him eat the aforementioned tortellos for five days, and if he has not yet recovered, let him use them for another five days. And if this still does not help, let him eat them again for another five days, and thus seven times, in the same number of five days, he should do this. Fenum and meats should also be eaten in the interim, cooked with celery and petroselinum, because these meats are somewhat dry and do not contain bad humors, and also the sweet coldness of celery and petroselinum purges the filth and fevers of the stomach, which sometimes provoke this illness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si bovinas carnes edere volaerit, recentes sumat, et si in aestate est, eas per diem in aquam ponat, vel si in bieme est, per noctem, quoniam aqua totum livorem, qui in eadem carne est, aufert, et postea coctas comedat. Etso etiam agninas cames edere potest, sed necesse non est, ut eas in aquam ponat, ut bovinas cames, quod tamen suaves 8unt. Porcinas vero cames interim non comedat, quod naturae buius sunt, quod libidinem facile in bomine excitant et quod lepram atque caducum morbum et vermem, qui bominem in came rodit, nutriunt; anguillam quoque devitet et reliquos pisces, qui squamas non babent, quoniam in natura sua aliquantum livoris tenent yenenosi, unde etiam squamis carent.
text_translation: If one wishes to eat beef, let them take recent ones, and if it is summer, let them place them in water during the day, or if it is winter, during the night, because water removes all the black bile that is in the same flesh, and afterwards let them eat them cooked. And if one also wishes to eat lamb flesh, it is not necessary to place them in water as with beef, although they are sweet. But let them not eat pork flesh, because it is of their nature, which easily excites lust in the body and nourishes leprosy and the wasting disease and the worm that gnaws the flesh of man; let them also avoid eels and other fish that do not have scales, because in their nature they hold somewhat of black bile, from which they also lack scales.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Gaseos quoque et ova et cruda olera et cruda poma et quidquid assum est, interim devitet, quoniam caseus huic morbo pro veneno est et ova et cruda olera et cruda poma noxios humores in eo excitant, et quae assa sunt, eidem morbo guttam subministrant. Vinum autem non forte sed suave et aqua permixtum atque cerevisiam bibat. De hydropisi, Qui ydropum morbum habet, pavonem scilicet masculum accipiat et cum ysopo in aqua putei coquat, non autem in aqua salientis fontis, et sic carnes illas comedat.
text_translation: Gaseous also and eggs and raw vegetables and raw fruit and whatever is taken, in the meantime it becomes deviated, because cheese is poison for this disease and eggs and raw vegetables and raw fruit excite harmful humors in it, and those that are cooked administer a drop to the same disease. Wine, however, should not be strong but sweet and mixed with water and beer, drink it. Concerning dropsy, whoever has the dropsy disease, take a male peacock and cook it with ispus in well water, not however in the water of a spring, and thus eat those meats.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea cor et os, quod in genu illius volvitur, et ungues ipsius toUat et ex his pulverem faciat, ita quod pulvis eorundem unguium et quod pulvis cordis eius tribus partibus pulverem ossium genuum iUius ezcedat. Deinde batheniam et ter tantum de levistico atque modicum de sagimine anguillae accipiat et haec in vino coquat atque per pannum colat et huic vino praedictum pulverem immittat et ita bibat, hoc autem per decem et octo dies faciat. Cames enim pavonis siccae sunt et fortes contra ydropum morbum, et ideo de masculo propter fortitudinem suam esse debent.
text_translation: Then the heart and the mouth, which is rolled in his knee, and touch his nails and make powder from them, so that the powder of those nails and the powder of his heart exceed the powder of the bones of his knee in three parts. Then take bathenium and only a third part of levisticum and a little of saginum of eel, and cook these in wine and strain through a cloth, and put the aforementioned powder into this wine and drink it thus. Let this be done for eighteen days. For the dry meats of peacock are strong against the water disease, and therefore they should be taken from the male because of their strength.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed cum ysopo coquantur, quod ille in siccitate et suavi frigore suo superfluam humiditatem eiusdem morbi exsiccat, et in aqua putei coquantur, quo niam hic quoque siccas venas aquarum habet et magis ydropum morbum consumit quam aqua salientis fontis, quae aliquantum magis aquosa est quam aqua putei; et ideo haec eundem morbum humiditate sua augerent. Gor autem pavonis pulverizatum suffocat morbum istum; sed os, quod in genibus volvitur, quoniam venas cruris ligat, etiam eundem morbum ligat, ne ultra procedat.
text_translation: But when cooked with hyssop, which by its dryness and gentle coolness dries the superfluous humidity of the same disease, and when cooked in well water, which has dry veins of water and consumes the disease of dropsy more than the water of a springing fountain, which is somewhat more watery than well water; and therefore this one would increase the same disease through its own humidity. However, powdered peacock chokes this disease; but the mouth, which wraps around the knees, because it binds the veins of the legs, also binds the same disease, so that it does not proceed further.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Ungues vero fixuram eiusdem morbi, ne quasi in saliente aqua exsurgat, deprimit, aqua vero eiusdem morbi calorem batheniae fugit, frigiditas autem levistici mentem hominis illius laetificat, quod tristis est, et sagimen anguillae fixu1 V. 9—22 = Migne 1289 D. ram eiusdem morbi constringit, quae eum surgere facit. Calor quoque vini eum magis quam aqua sedat, et haec omnia insimul temperata,ut praedictum est,morbum istum minuunt. De colica. Homo, quem vich fatigat, modicnm zinziberi et plurimum cinamomi accipiat et haec pulverizet.
text_translation: The nails indeed fix the form of the same disease, so that it does not rise as if in flowing water, it presses down; water indeed of the same disease escapes the heat of the bath, but the coldness of the levistici delights the mind of that man, who is sad, and the sagimen of the eel fixed V. 9—22 = Migne 1289 D. ram of the same disease constricts, which causes it to rise. The heat of wine also calms it more than water, and all these things together tempered, as predicted, reduce this disease. Concerning colic. The man, whom the vix fatigues, should receive a small amount of ginger and much cinnamon, and pulverize these.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde tollat salviae minus quam zinziberi et feniculi plus quam salviae et tanaceti minus quam salviae atque haec in mortario ad succum terat ac per pannum colet. Deinde mel in vino modice coquat et ei parum albi piperis addat, vel si illud non habuerit, parum de ninunolo et lo praedictum pulverem ac praedictum immittat succum. Postea merlinsen sumat et tormentillae bis tantum ut merlinsen et sinape, quod in campo crescit, eodem pondere, ut tormentilla ponderata est, atque herbam iUam, in qua minutissimae filiantropos crescunt, minus, quam merlimen sit, et haec in mortario ad succum terat et tritam in saccellum ponat atque praefatum mellitum et pulverizatum vinum superfandatur et ex hoc velut ItUridranc faciens.
text_translation: Then it raises the sage (salvia) less than ginger (zingiberis) and fennel (fenum) more than sage and tanacetum (tanaceti) less than sage, and this in the mortar to be ground into a paste and collected through a cloth. Then honey is moderately cooked in wine and a little white pepper is added to it, or if it does not have that, a little of ninunolo and the aforementioned powder, and the aforementioned juice is poured in. Afterwards, take merlinsen and tormentilla twice as much as merlinsen and mustard, which grows in the field, in the same weight as tormentilla was weighed, and the herb in which the minutest filiantropos grow, less than merlinsen is, and this in the mortar to be ground into a paste and the ground material is placed in a small sack, and the aforementioned honeyed and powdered wine is poured over it, and from this it is made as Ituridranc.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui autem praedictum dolorem patitur, potum istum, quantum uno halitu bibere potest, ieiunus bibat et simili modo ad noctem, cum se in lectum reponit, et hoc faciat, usque dum sanetur. Nam vich et de calidis et de frigidis malis humoribus nascitur, sed tamen magis de frigidis quam de calidis humoribus est. Unde calor cinamomi et calor salviae et calor feniculi et calor tanaceti et calor mellis ac calor vini et calor albi piperis vel calor nimmoli et calor sinapis campi et calor filiantropos cum frigiditate merlinsen et frigiditate tormentillae temperati iniuste calidos et iniuste frigidos humores, de quibus vich nascitur, minuunt, cum praedictum poculum homo sumit, scilicet ieiunus et cumSO dormitum vadit, quatinus ieiuno eosdem humores exsurgere vetet et pranso malos humores ciborum compescat.
text_translation: Whoever suffers the aforementioned pain should drink this potion, as much as one can drink in one breath, fasting, and similarly before nightfall, when he lies down to bed, and let him do this until he is healed. For this pain arises both from hot and from cold maladies of the humors, but more from cold than from hot humors. Hence, the heat of cinnamon and the heat of sage and the heat of fennel and the heat of tanacetum and the heat of honey and the heat of wine and the heat of white pepper or the heat of nigella and the heat of field mustard and the heat of philanthropos, tempered with the coldness of merlinsen and the coldness of tormentilla, when the aforementioned potion is taken by a person, fasting and after sleeping, prevents the same humors from rising when fasting and restrains the bad humors of foods when eating.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De lumbricis. Si de noxiis et malis humoribus, qui in homine venenosi sunt, vermes in aliquo homine excre 1 V. 4—22 = Migne 1186 D— 1137 A; v. pag. 197. 2 V. 33— p. 210,9 = Migne 1169 A; v. pag. 211. Hildegardis oausae et curae ed. Eaiser. yerint, ille succum ardentis urticae accipiat et succum blandoniae pari pondere et succum de foliis nuci, quantum illorum duorum est, vel si folia nuci non habuerit, de cortice eiusdem arboris tantum succi toUat ac modicum aceti addat et plurimum mellis et in noya olla fervere faciat et spumam superius abiciat; et postquam efferbuerit^ de igne toUat et per quindecim dies istud modice ieiunus bibat, ne propter fortitudinem illius laedatur; sed post cibum sufficienter bibat, quoniam cibi fortitudini illius tunc resistunt.
text_translation: On worms. If worms are excreted in a man because of noxious and evil humors, which are venomous in man, let him take the juice of burning nettle and the juice of blandonia in equal weight, and the juice of walnut leaves, as much as there is of the two, or if he has not walnut leaves, only the juice of the bark of the same tree, and let him add a little vinegar and much honey, and let him boil it in a pot and let the foam rise above; and after it has boiled, let him take it from the fire and let him drink it moderately for fifteen days, so that he is not harmed by the strength of it; but after food, let him drink sufficiently, because then the food resists the strength of it.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum enim calor ardentis urticae et calor blandoniae aequo calori et aequo frigori nuci contemperantur, et cum ita in potu sumuntur, ut praefatum est, virtute istorum vermes in homine moriuntur; sed acetum et mel his additur, ut propter saporem istorum sumi possit. De pediculis, Si quempiam hominem pediculi interius in corpore laeduut, ita quod ab eo non egrediuntur, fel anguillae accipiat, et ei ter minus de acerrimo aceto addat ac tantum meUis, ut istorum duorum est, et haec in sartagine coquat.
text_translation: When indeed the heat of the burning nettle and the bland heat are tempered equally with equal heat and equal cold, and when they are thus taken in drink, as was previously stated, the power of these worms dies in the human; but vinegar and honey are added to them, so that they may be taken on account of their taste. Concerning lice: If any man has lice within the body, so that they do not emerge from him, let him take the gall of eel, and let him add three times less of the sharpest vinegar, and as much honey as there is of these two, and let this be cooked in a sartagium.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea zinziber sumat et bis tantum longi piperis et simili pondere bsisiliam atque haec pulverizet et insuper ebur ad tertiam partem basiliae tollat et etiam de rostro vulturis ad mediam partem eboris et ad praefatum pulverem ab his scabendo radet; mox pulverem istum in praedictum acetum ponat et denuo simul fervere faciat. Quod cum fecerit, haec in saccellum ponat, quatinus per illum velut luterdranc transeat, et sic in novo fictili vase recipiat.
text_translation: Then she shall take ginger and twice as much long pepper, and similarly in weight basilicum, and she shall powder these; in addition, she shall take ivory to a third part of the basilicum, and also from the beak of the vulture to a half part of the ivory, and from these she shall scrape it; then she shall place this powder into the aforementioned vinegar and cause it to boil again together. When she has done this, she shall put this into a small bag, so that it may pass through it as though through a luterdranc, and thus receive it into a new ceramic vessel.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde idem homo, quem pediculi intevius laedunt, ita quod ab eo non egrediuntur, potum hunc cotidie ieiunus bibat, et ad noctem, cum, in lectum se reponit, pediculi in eo deficient et morientur, et sic arvina in ipso renovabitur. Calor enim ac amaritudo fellis anguillae pediculos debilitat et calor et acerbitas aceti eos macerat et calor ac siccitas eboris eos exsiccat ac rostrum vulturis, quod frigidum est et quod diversitate cadaverum inficitur et sudore cerebri eiusdem vulturis perfunditur, 1 V.
text_translation: Then the same man, whom the lice of the intestine afflict, so that they do not depart from him, let him drink this potion fasting every day, and at night, when he lies down to bed, the lice in him will fail and die, and thus the arvina in him will be renewed. For the heat and bitterness of the eel's bile weakens the lice, and the heat and acidity of vinegar macerates them, and the heat and dryness of wax dries them out, and the beak of the vulture, which is cold and which is infected by the variety of corpses and is soaked with the sweat of the brain of the same vulture,

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 15—31 = Migne 1283 D— 1284 D. eos mortificat. Et etiam haec calore mellis et forti calore longi piperis, qui calore alii piperis fortior est, et frigiditate basiliae temperantur et alterato calore accenduntur et in fictile vas ponuntur, ne in moUitie putrescant; in ieiuno et pranso sument pediculos extinguunt, quod cum homo ieiunus poculum hunc sumit, pedicuios in eo citius extinguit, et cum pransus eumisumit, humores ciborum, de quibus pediculi nascuntur, attenuantur.
text_translation: 15—31 = Migne 1283 D— 1284 D. They kill them. And also these are tempered with the heat of honey and the strong heat of long pepper, which is stronger in heat than other pepper, and are moderated by the coldness of basil, and are ignited with altered heat and placed in a ceramic vessel, so that they do not rot in moisture; they are taken in fasting and after a meal to extinguish lice, for when a man fasting takes this cup, he extinguishes lice in it more quickly, and when he has eaten and takes it, the humors of food, from which lice arise, are attenuated.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Contra lumbricos. Accipe succum heUhernejsielim et succum ivullene pari pondere et succum de foliis nuci, lO quantum illorum duorum est, vel si folia nuci non habueris, tolle tantum succi de cortice eiusdem arboris, id est nuci, et modicum aceti adde et plurimum mellis et in nova oUa fac fervere et spumam superius abice, et cum efferbuerit, de igne toUe. Et per quinque dies illi, qui patitur vermes in ventre, da in potu modice ieiuno, sed post cibum sufficienter bibat.
text_translation: Against worms. Take the juice of helbernice and the juice of ivellene in equal weight, and the juice from the leaves of the nut, ten times the quantity of the two former, or if you do not have leaves of the nut, take only the juice from the bark of the same tree, that is, the nut, and add a little vinegar and plenty of honey, and in a new pot make it boil, and remove the foam from the top, and when it has boiled, take it off the fire. And for five days, give to the person suffering from worms in the stomach, in moderation during fasting, but after a meal, drink sufficiently.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De calcido. Qui calculum in se habet, recens fel iuvenis tauri accipiat et bis tantum de sanguine eius et haec exsiccet ac sumat etiam saxifricam et pulverizet eam et de pulvere hoc tantum tollat, qaantum feUis huius est, et in subtilem panniolum simul liget et sic in forte et bonum ac purum vinum ponat atque ita frequenter ieiunus bibat et simUi modo, postquam comederit, non autem infra cibum, quoniam a cibo consumeretur. Nam amaritudo fellis huius cum calore praefati sanguinis et cum frigiditate saxifricae atque cum calore vini temperata coagulationem calculi in homine dissolvit.
text_translation: On the stone. He who has a stone within him should take fresh bile of a young bull and take twice as much of its blood, and dry this and also take saxifraga and powder it, and take as much of this powder as there is of this bile, and tie it together in a fine cloth, and thus place it in strong, good, and pure wine, and drink it frequently fasting, and in a similar manner, after eating, but not below the meal, because it would be consumed by food. For the bitterness of this bile, together with the heat of the aforementioned blood and with the coldness of saxifraga, and with the heat of the wine, tempered, dissolves the coagulation of the stone in man.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De lepra. Qui de gulositate et ebrietate leprosus efficitur, stercus hirundinum accipiat et quater tantum de herba, quae lappa dicitur, rubeos flores habentem, et ex his pulverem faciat; arvinam quoque ciconiae accipiat et modicum plus arvinae vulturis et haec in sartagine frixet et hoc facto praedictum pulverem et modicum sulphuris 1 V. 9—17 = p. 210,1-9. — 2 V. 18—25 = Migne 1323 C. — 3 V. 29— p. 212,4 = Migne 1308 A-B. 14 huic aryinae intermisceat ac ex his hoc modo unguentnm faciat et de hoc in asso balneo se perungi iubeat et deinde in lectnm se collocet.
text_translation: On leprosy. He who becomes leprous from gluttony and drunkenness should take the dung of swallows and four times the herb called lappa, which has red flowers, and from these make a powder; he should also take arvina of the stork and a little more arvina of the vulture, and grind these in a sartagine; then mix this aforementioned powder and a little sulfur with this arvina, and make an ointment from these, and command him to anoint himself in a bath of ashes, and then to lie down on a bed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic usque ad quintum diem vel ad plures dies faciat. Cum enim calor stercoris himndinum et frigiditas lappae cum calore arvinae ciconiae et cum frigiditate arvinae vulturis et calore sulphuris temperantur, putredines ieprae huius aufert, quoniam stercus hirundinum eas dissolvit, pulvis autem filiantropos eas transmordet, arvina vero ciconiae et vulturis et amari tudo sulphuris eas extrahunt. Et sanabitur, nisi deus eum sanari nolit. Item de lepra, Homo, qui de iracundia lepram contrahit, vadat in locum, ubi sanguis modicus equomm in terram defiuit, cum sanguis eorum in vena minuitnr, i^ et ubi munda animalia occiduntur, et ipsum sanguinem cum terra eodem sanguine infecta tollat.
text_translation: And thus do this until the fifth day or for several days. For indeed, the heat of the dung of swallows and the coldness of lappae with the heat of the arvinae of storks and with the coldness of the arvinae of vultures and the heat of sulfur temper, and thus removes the putrefactions of this leprosy, because the dung of swallows dissolves them, but the dust of filiantropos devours them, and indeed the arvinae of storks and vultures and the bitter stuff of sulfur extract them. And he will be healed, unless God does not wish to heal him. Moreover, regarding leprosy, a man who contracts leprosy from anger, let him go to a place where the blood of horses in moderation flows into the earth, when their blood in the vein diminishes, and where pure animals are killed, and let him take that blood with the earth infected by the same blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Deinde sanguinem istum cum terra, super quam effluxerat, mixtum in caldario cum aqua fervere faciat et sic balneum sibi praeparet, ita tamen ne tanta sit aqua, quod sanguini illi vires auferat, sed ut sit usque ad guttur suum; et ita in balneo sedeat. Sed et de eodem sanguine et de eadem terra modico saccello immittat, quod in faciem suam ponat, si ibi dolet. Qui cum de balneo exierit, in lectum se reclinet atque eundem saccellum cum praedicto sanguine et terra super cor suum ponat, ne debilitetur et ut nocivum sanguinem a corde suo depellat.
text_translation: Then let him mix that blood with the earth upon which it flowed out, in the caldarium with water, causing it to boil, and thus prepare a bath for himself, however, so that the water is not so much that it takes away the strength from that blood, but that it reaches up to his throat; and thus sit in the bath. But also from the same blood and the same earth, let him put into a small sack, which he places on his face, if it aches there. When he comes out of the bath, he should lie down on the bed and place the same sack with the aforementioned blood and earth on his chest, so that he does not become weakened and to drive away the harmful blood from his heart.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic quater vel quinquies vel amplius faciat. Nam calor sanguinis huius cum temperamento terrae hoc sanguine infectae leprae resistit, velut inimicu? inimicum superat; haec autem dif&cilia SO homini videntur, quoniam natura hominis naturae animalium contraria est, et quod homo sanguinem animalimn abhorrret. Et homo iste sanabitur, aut deus eum sanari non vult. De incontinentiae lepra, Si homo de libidine aut 1 V. 12—27 = Migne 1319 C-1820 A. — » v.
text_translation: And thus do it four times, five times, or more. For the heat of this blood, together with the temperament of the earth infected by this blood, resists leprosy, as an enemy overcomes an enemy; these things, however, appear difficult to man, because the nature of man is contrary to the nature of animals, and that man abhors the blood of animals. And this man will be healed, or God does not wish him to be healed. Regarding the leprosy of incontinence, if a man from lust or 1 V. 12—27 = Migne 1319 C-1820 A. — » v.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 34— p. 213,ii = Migne 1176 D-1177 A. incontinentia leprosus efficitar, agrimoniam accipiat et secundum eins tertiam partem ysopum ac aseri bis tantum, ut istorum duorum est, et herbas istas in caldario coquat et sic balneum ex his faciat ac menstruum sanguinem, quantum habere poterit, admisceat et ita balneo se imponat. Sed etiam de arvina anseris quantum volaerit, accipiat et bis tantum arvinae gallinarum ac modicum stercoris gallinarum et inde unguentum faciat.
text_translation: The leprosy caused by incontinence is treated by taking agrimony and, according to its third part, hyssop and aseri twice as much as these two, and cooking these herbs in a caldario, making a bath from these, and mixing in menstrual blood as much as he can, and applying the bath to himself. But also take from the arvina of geese as much as they wish, twice as much from the arvina of hens and a little of the dung of hens, and make an ointment from that.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum de.praedicto balneo exierit, eodem unguento se perungat ac in lectum se recollocet. Et sic frequenter faciat, dum sanetur. Calor namque agrimoniae et frigiditas ysopi ac calor aseri cum calido menstruali sanguine temperatus putredinem istius leprae aufert. Agrimonia enim et ysopus ac aserum eam exsudare faciunt, menstrualis autem sanguis eam opprimit et concutit, velut inimicus inimicum suum, quia illa de diversis humoribus mulierum egreditur.
text_translation: And when he comes forth from the aforementioned bath, let him anoint himself with the same ointment and lie back upon the bed. And thus frequently let him do this until he is healed. For indeed the heat of agrimony and the coldness of yarrow, and the heat of arsenic tempered with warm menstrual blood, removes the putrefaction of this leprosy. For agrimony and yarrow and arsenic cause it to exude, but the menstrual blood oppresses and shakes it, as an enemy shakes its enemy, because it comes forth from the diverse humors of women.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed arvina anseris et arvina gallinarum eam leniter unguit et sanat, stercus vero gallinarum sordes, quae residuae sunt, extrahit, et sic ille sanabitur, aut deus non permittet. De gutta. Homo, qui mollem camem habet et de superfluis potationibus de gutta, id est gichf in aliquo membro suo fatigatar, petrosolinum accipiat et quater tantum de rutha et in patella de oleo olivae frixet, yel si oleum hoc non habuerit, cum hircino sepo infrixari faciat, et herbas istas ita calidas loco, ubi dolet/ imponat et panno desuper liget.
text_translation: But the fat of the goose and the fat of the hens gently anoints and heals it, but the excrement of the hens, which remains as dirt, extracts it, and thus he will be healed, or God will not permit it. On the drop. The man who has a soft membrane and is fatigued in some limb from the drop, that is gicht, due to superfluous potations, let him take petroselinum and grind it four times with rutha and in a patella with oil of olive, yel if he does not have this oil, let him be rubbed with hircino sepo, and let him apply these herbs thus hot on the place where it aches, and let him bind it over with a cloth.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: !Nam frigiditas succus petrosolini inundantes humores guttae compescit, calor autem acerbi succi rathae eosdem humores constringit, ne supra modum grossentur, oleum autem aut hircinum sepum eos pertransit et dissolvit, et ita faciunt, cum simul temperantur, ut praefatum est. De scrofulis. Si quis orfune in corpore suo habet, antequam rumpantur, accipiat huflathcha, maiores scilicet, quae exterius dbae et interius viridies sunt, et iuxta caudam abrumpat secundum latitudinem orfune et cetera abiciat ^ V.
text_translation: Nam frigiditas succus petrosolini inundantes humores guttae compescit, calor autem acerbi succi rathae eosdem humores constringit, ne supra modum grossentur, oleum autem aut hircinum sepum eos pertransit et dissolvit, et ita faciunt, cum simul temperantur, ut praefatum est. De scrofulis. Si quis orfune in corpore suo habet, antequam rumpantur, accipiat huflathcha, maiores scilicet, quae exterius dbae et interius viridies sunt, et iuxta caudam abrumpat secundum latitudinem orfune et cetera abiciat ^ V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 21—26 = Migne 1159 B. — 2 v. 82— p. 214,4 = Migne 1165 C; 1206 A— B. et super illud, quod retiuuit, mel lineat et sic per tres dies et per tres noctes super orfune ponat, et cum illud exsiccatur, iterum novum eodem modo paratum superpouat, et orfune minorari incipient. Quarta autem die similam de triticea farina accipiat et illam cum melle cnechde ad latitudinem earundem orfime et eis primum acoleiam superponat et desuper eundem deich et sic per novem dies faciat.
text_translation: And upon that which remained, apply honey and thus for three days and three nights place it upon the wounds, and when that dries, again prepare a new one in the same manner and cover it, and the wounds will begin to diminish.
