On the fourth day, however, take a similar amount of wheat flour and mix it with honey and all to the width of those same wounds, and upon them first lay a coating and above that the same decoction, and thus do so for nine days.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et cum deich ille exsiccatur, eum cum novo melle iterum renovabit. Et sic faciat, dum evanesoat. De claudicatione. Si quis in aliquo loco membroram suorum claudicare incipit, dictampnum accipiat, et quod in medio velut cor est, abiciat, et quod remansit, fortitcr in aqua coquat, ac interim dum ita coquitur, bis tantum, ut dictampni est, husmuses addat et bis tantum urentis urticae, ut husmuses est, et haec simul in aqua coquendo commisceat. Et postquam cocta fuerint, aqua modice expressa ita caiida super iuncturam membri illius et super venas eius, in quibus homo claudicare incipit, ponat, et cum infrigidari incipiunt, iterum calefaciat et desuper ponat, atque hoc saepe faciat.
text_translation: And when that thing dries up, it shall be renewed with new honey again. And thus do this until it fades away. On lameness. If anyone begins to limp in some part of his limbs, let him take dictampnum, and cast away that which is in the middle as if it were a heart, and let him cook what remains strongly in water, and while it is thus cooking, add husmuses twice, as much as dictampnum is, and twice as much of burning nettle, as much as husmuses is, and mix these together while cooking in water. And after they have been cooked, let the water be moderately pressed out and thus cold be placed upon the joint of that limb and upon its veins, in which the man begins to limp, and when they begin to grow cold, again warm them and place upon them, and do this often.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod autem in medio dictampno est, quod aridum est, abicietur, et calor reliqui dictampni, cum torpore husmuses temperatur, malos humores, qui membro hominis debilitatem inferunt, fugat, et calor urentis urticae eosdem humores corrodit, ut minuantur, cum in suavitate calefactae aquae temperantur, ut praedictum est; et sic ille curabitur, nisi deus nolit. De febre acuta. Homo, qui modicum comedit et multum bibit, in acuta febre laborans aquam bibat, ne si vinum bibat, magis et magis inardescat et inde plus accipiat; aquam autem aut frigidam aut calefactam et deinde infrigidatam bibat et suavius dolebit, quod eum reMgerat et ardentem calorem in eo extinguit, quoniam, si aqua calida esset, ardorem acutae febris accenderet.
text_translation: What is in the middle of the dictampno, which is dry, should be discarded, and the remaining heat of the dictampno, tempered by the torpor of the humors, drives away the bad humors that cause weakness in the human body; and the heat of the burning nettle corrodes these same humors, so that they diminish, when tempered by the sweetness of heated water, as previously stated; and thus he will be cured, unless God does not wish it. On acute fever. A man who has eaten a little and drunk much, suffering from acute fever, should drink water, so that if he drinks wine, he may not become more and more inflamed and receive more from it; but he should drink either cold or heated water and then cooled again, and he will suffer less, which will restrain him and extinguish the burning heat within him, for if the water were hot, it would ignite the heat of the acute fever.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de fehre acuta. ^jpostquam acuta febris ho1 V. 10-20 = Migne 1177 B. — » v. 34— p. 215,28 = Migne 1237 D— 1238 D. minem invaserit, hoc remedium faciat, ut deinde aliquantum lenius habeat et ut aliquaDtum usque ad sudorem suavius doleat. Accipiat ergo modicum de sicco ligno ahorn et bis tantum de sicca salice et non viridi, atque in frigidam aquam quasi scalpens modicum de his abradat et etiam tantum agrimoniae, ut salicis est, accipiat et eidem aquae imponat et eam hoc modo confectam frequenter bibat, et lenius dolebit.
text_translation: Item de febre acuta. Postquam acuta febris hominem invaderit, hoc remedium faciat, ut deinde aliquantum lenius habeat et ut aliquantum usque ad sudorem suavius doleat. Accipiat ergo modicum de sicco ligno alno et bis tantum de sicca salice et non viridi, atque in frigidam aquam quasi scalpens modicum de his abradat et etiam tantum agrimoniae, ut salicis est, accipiat et eidem aquae imponat et eam hoc modo confectam frequenter bibat, et lenius dolebit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam sicca ahorn et sicca salix esse debet, ne recens succus eorundem lignorum dolentem plus laedat quam remedium afferat, et lenitas succi dhorn amaritu- lo dinem succi salicis temperat. Itaque frigiditas lignorum istorum iniusto calori acutae febris hoc modo resistit, et suavis calor agrimoniae eundem calorem acutae febris retinety ne augmentetur, et hoc in frigida aqua eiit, et non in calefacta nec in vino, ne aut calefacta aqua aut calore vini calor acutae febris in furorem vertatur.
text_translation: Nam dry maple and dry willow should be, so that the fresh juice of these woods may not harm more than it brings remedy, and the sweetness of the maple's juice tempers the bitterness of the willow's juice. Therefore, the coldness of these woods resists the unjust heat of acute fever in this way, and the gentle heat of agrimony retains the same heat of acute fever so that it does not increase, and this is poured into cold water, and not into heated or into wine, so that neither the heated water nor the heat of wine turns the heat of acute fever into a frenzy.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Hoc autem remedium idem homo faciet a prima hora seu a prima die accessionis febris huius usque ad sudorem suum. Quod si remedium istud, usque dum febris haec roborata fuerit, distulerit vel prope &iem eius, scilicet ad quintam vel ad sextam diem, cum iam idem homo aut cito sudorem ad sanitatem emittet aut cito vitam perdet: tunc praedictus potus ad remedium non multum proderit, quoniam aeger ille aut citius sanitatem recipiet aut mortem subibit.
text_translation: This remedy, however, the same man will administer from the first hour or from the first day of the onset of this fever until his sweat. If, however, this remedy is delayed until this fever is strengthened, or near its time, namely on the fifth or sixth day, when the same man either quickly emits sweat toward health or quickly loses life: then the aforementioned drink will not be of much help as a remedy, because that sick person either quickly receives health or death will come upon him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: JDe cotidiana, Qui cotidianam febrem patitur, dost^ accipiat et gamforam et dornellae plus quam istorum duorxmi sit, et haec puiverizet atque pulverem istum in ipsa accessione febris calido vino immittat et sic illud bibat atque in lectum se collocet et dormiat. Calor enim dost et gamforae cum fiigiditate domellae temperati et in ipsa ao accessione febris huius, quatinus tanto citius sedetur, cum calido vino sumpti praedictam febrem fugant. Ad tertianam, Homo, quem febris secunda die fatigat, millefolium accipiat ac bis tantum polipodii et 1 V.
text_translation: De cotidiana, Qui cotidianam febrem patitur, dosi accipiat et gamboram et dormellae plus quam duorum sit, et haec pulverizet atque pulverem istum in ipsa accessione febris calido vino immittat et sic illud bibat atque in lectum se collocet et dormiat. Calor enim dosi et gamborae cum frigiditate dormellae temperati et in ipsa ad accessione febris huius, quatinus tanto citius sedet, cum calido vino sumpti praedictam febrem fugant. Ad tertianam, Homo, quem febris secunda die fatigat, millefolium accipiat ac bis tantum polypodii et 1 V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 25=29 = Migne 1175 A. — « v. 33— p. 216,« Idigne 1175 D. in suavi et bono vino eas coqoat ac sic per pannum colat atque in ipsa accessione eiusdem febiis yinum hoc bibat, herbas autem istas ita in vino per triduum dimittat, quatinus idem vinum ab eis tanto melius temperetur, et sic per triduum bibat, et si necesse fuerit, eas iterum dmilibus novis herbis renovet. Nam calor millefoHi et calor polipodii cum alterato calore vini temperati et, ut praefatum est, in potu sumpti febrem istam mitigant.
text_translation: In sweet and good wine, let them cook them and thus cover them with a cloth, and in the very act of adding the same, let the feverish person drink this wine; but let those herbs be thus left in the wine for three days, so that the same wine may be thus more temperately tempered from them, and thus let him drink it for three days, and if necessity requires, let them be renewed again with new herbs. For the heat of millefolium and the heat of polypodii, together with the altered heat of the tempered wine, and as was previously stated, when taken in drink, mitigate this fever.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item ad tertianam. Qui tertianam febrem patitor, hiin accipiat et minorem huflathdecha aequali pondere hXines et ter tantum de refano ut istorum duorum est, et in vino coquat ac per pannum col t; atque gariofel sumat et bis tantum galangae et zinziberi tertiam partem, ut istorom duorum est, et haec in pulverem redigat atque ex his cum praedicto vino, quod per pannum colavit, purum potom fadat et illo in ipsa accessione atque sequentibus novem diebus utatur, ut plenius adiuvetur.
text_translation: Item ad tertianam. Qui tertianam febrem patitor, hiin accipiat et minorem huflathdecha aequali pondere hXines et ter tantum de refano ut istorum duorum est, et in vino coquat ac per pannum col t; atque gariofel sumat et bis tantum galangae et zinziberi tertiam partem, ut istorom duorum est, et haec in pulverem redigat atque ex his cum praedicto vino, quod per pannum colavit, purum potom fadat et illo in ipsa accessione atque sequentibus novem diebus utatur, ut plenius adiuvetur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam calor hun et frigus minoris huflathedechae cum calore rafani et calore gariofel et calore galangae et cum bono calore zinziberi temperata et in potu sumpta praefatam febrem fugant. Ad quartanaa. Si quis febres quartanas patitnr, rubiam et herbam veprium aequali pondere accipiat et KJyrachwurzy ter tantum, ut istorum duorum est, atque in vino coquat. Deinde clarum et optimum vinum in patellam fundat et huic ignitum calibem ita ferventem immittat, ac secunda vice eidem vino in eadem patella iterom eundem ignitum calibem immittat, et hoc decies cum eodem vino et eodem calibe faciat.
text_translation: Nam calor humoris et frigus minoris humoratus cum calore raphani et calore garofilo et calore galangae et cum bono calore zinziberi temperata et in potu sumpta praefatam febrem fugant. Ad quartanam. Si quis febres quartanas patitur, rubiam et herbam veprium aequali pondere accipiat et kyrachwurzy ter tantum, ut istorum duorum est, atque in vino coquat. Deinde clarum et optimum vinum in patellam fundat et huic ignitum calibem ita ferventem immittat, ac secunda vice eidem vino in eadem patella iterum eundem ignitum calibem immittat, et hoc decies cum eodem vino et eodem calibe faciat.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Postea sumat vinum iUud, quod cum praedictis herbis coxit, et vino huic cocto, cni calibem immisit, infundat atque ita simul una tantum vice fervere faciat, et sic in ipsa accessione praedictae febris illud bibat et ita, dum sanetur. Cum enim frigiditas rubiae et calor herbae vepriiun et calor hrachwure cum alterato calore 1 V. 9—17 = Migne 1203 C. — 2 y. 22—33 = Migne 1192 B-C. CA. vini temperantnr et itenmi cam fortitudine et alterato calore calibis roborantur, ut praefatum est, febrem istam mitigant.
text_translation: Afterwards, let him take that wine which was cooked with the aforementioned herbs, and having poured this cooked wine into the calidum, let him pour it in and thus cause it to boil together only once, and thus drink it during the very onset of the aforementioned fever, and so until he is healed. For indeed, the coldness of the rubia and the heat of the herb of vepriiun and the heat of hrachwure with the altered heat of the calidum temper the wine, and likewise the strength and altered heat of the calidum strengthen, as was said before, this fever.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cantra vermem. Homo, quem vermis in aliquo loco corporis sui comedit, cretam accipiat et bis tantum de cfidum et ex his cum acetoso vino yelut tenue cementum faciat atque istud loco, ubi vermem patitur, cum penna immittat et hoc per singulos dies usque ad quartam diem faciat. Postea sumat aloe et secundum eius tertiam partem myrram et simul terat, atque ex bis cum recente cera emplaustrum paret et cannibeo panno superponat et ita super locum doloris per duodecim dies liget.
text_translation: Contra vermem. Homo, quem vermis in aliquo loco corporis sui comedit, cretam accipiat et bis tantum de cidum et ex his cum acetoso vino felut tenue cementum faciat atque istud loco, ubi vermem patitur, cum penna immittat et hoc per singulos dies usque ad quartam diem faciat. Postea sumat aloe et secundum eius tertiam partem myrram et simul terat, atque ex bis cum recente cera emplaustrum paret et cannibeo panno superponat et ita super locum doloris per duodecim dies liget.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam creta calida est et crida frigida, et sic calor cretae cum frigiditate cridae et cum calore et acumine vini temperatus vermes mortificat; sed calor aloae cum calore myrrae augmentatns putredines eorundem ulcerum extrahit et eundem locum sanat. De ccUibe. Ut calibs fortis sit, eum in sanguine leonis aut hirci intinge. Quod autem adamans sanguinem hirci intinguitur, ut calibe incidi possit, ibi calibs hircinum sanguinem, in quo intinctus est, primum tangit, et sic fortitudinem capit et adamantem incidit.
text_translation: Nam creta calida est et creta frigida, et sic calor cretae cum frigiditate cretae et cum calore et acumine vini temperatus vermes mortificat; sed calor aloae cum calore myrrae augmentans putredines eorundem ulcerum extrahit et eundem locum sanat. De cubo. Ut cubus fortis sit, eum in sanguine leonis aut hirci intinge. Quod autem adamantem sanguinem hirci intingitur, ut cubo incidi possit, ibi cubus hircinum sanguinem, in quo intinctus est, primum tangit, et sic fortitudinem capit et adamantem incidit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Cum videris boves aut de noxio sanguine aut de forti labore infirmari, accipe conchas arenarum, quae in Htore iacent, et eas in pulverem redige et pulverem istum cum herba illa, quae bathenia dicitur, in aquam mitte, ita quod de pulvere isto plus sit quam batheniae, et da eis in potum. Et aridum fenum eis interim ad comedendum dabis. Sed cum fluor reumatis de naribus eorum fluit, ita quod in g^tture sonant, quasi tussim habeant, myrram et thus accipe, ita quod myrrae plus sit quam thuris, et superdO vivos carbones pone et in nares eorum fumum istum fiimigare fac.
text_translation: When you see oxen weakened either from noxious blood or from arduous labor, take the shells of the sands lying in Hore, and reduce them to powder, and put that powder together with that herb called bathenia into water, so that the powder is more than the bathenia, and give it to them to drink. And during that time, you will give them dry hay to eat. But when the rheumatic fluid flows from their nostrils, so that they sound in the throat, as if they had a cough, take myrrh and frankincense, so that there is more myrrh than frankincense, and place them upon live coals, and cause that smoke to be fumigated into their nostrils.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In humida autem et leni aura, quod tunc facile infirmari incipiunt, dille et sueriheilum wurzela accipe, scilicet d%Ue plus quam merthelum ivureda sit, atque pabulo eorum intermisce, ut comedant, et sanitatem in eis retinebunt, ne inflrmentur. De of^Ums, Si oves infirmari incipiant, accipd feniculum HILDEaARDlS et dille^ ita ut fenicaliim dUle ezcedat, et in aquam pone, ut aqua ab eis saporem accipiat, et da eis ad bibendum, et meliorabuntur. De equo.
text_translation: In humidâ autem et leni aura, quod tunc facile infirmari incipiunt, dille et sueriheilum wurzela accipe, scilicet dille plus quam merthelum ivureda sit, atque pabulo eorum intermisce, ut comedant, et sanitatem in eis retinebunt, ne infirmentur. De ovis, Si oves infirmari incipiant, accipe feniculum Hildegardis et dille, ita ut feniculum dille excedat, et in aquam pone, ut aqua ab eis saporem accipiat, et da eis ad bibendum, et meliorabuntur. De equo.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si fluor reumatis de naribus equi fluit, ita quod inde huset, heUerneeekm et plus de luhistikd^ quam heiternezelum sit, in aqua coque et damp istum ita calidum in nares et in os eius freno eius imposito transire permitte, et sanabitur. Quod si equus in yentre dolet, item heiternezzelum et plus de libesteche^ quam heUerneezeU sit, pabulo eius saepe intermisce, ut ita simul comedat, et curabitur. De asino. Si asinus in capite dolet, ita quod huset^ accipe asserum de fago, id est cineres fagi, de quibus lexiva facta est, et cum idem cineres iam calidi sunt, damp illum in os et in nares asini illius transire facias, et curabitur.
text_translation: If the rheumatic fluid flows from the horse's nostrils, so that it flows out, [and] heiternezzelum and more of libesteche than heiternezzelum is, cook it in water and let that steam pass so warm into the nostrils and into the mouth of the horse, with the bit placed upon it, and it will be healed. If the horse suffers pain in the belly, similarly heiternezzelum and more of libesteche than heiternezzelum is, often mix it with his fodder, so that he eats it at the same time, and it will be healed. Concerning the ass: If the ass suffers pain in the head, so that it flows, take the ash of beech, that is, the ashes of beech, from which the leachate was made, and when those same ashes are already hot, let them pass into the mouth and into the nostrils of that ass, and it will be healed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si etiam asinus in ventre dolet, maiores huflatheche ad furfures in aqua modice calefactos incidendo, id est scarhende, misce et hoc saepe fac, et curabitur. De porco. Gum porcus qualibet infirmitate gravatur, accipe conchas testudinis, id est sneckenhmj et modicum plus de diUe, quam concharum istarum sit, et haec simul conuninue et ita in az eius pone, ut comedat, et etiam heiternezdum in aqua coque et ita coctam cum eadem aqua (jus eius immisce, ut sic devoret, et hoc saepe fac, et curabitur.
text_translation: If even the ass suffers in the belly, the greater ones [should] take furfures in water slightly heated, that is, scorching, mix and do this often, and it will be cured. Concerning the pig. When the pig is burdened by any illness, take the shells of the tortoise, that is, snails, and a little more of dilute, than the shells of these, and place these together continuously and thus in its food, so that it eats, and also cook heterne in water and thus cooked with the same water, mix its juice, so that it eats thus, and do this often, and it will be cured.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De capra. Si capra aliquo modo infirmatur, folia de eiche ei sufficienter et saepe ad comedendum da, ut ita comedat, et hoc saepe fac, et curabitur. Item de equo et bove. Si equo aut boyi aut asino venam incidere volueris in minutione sanguinis et si idem animal forte et corpulentum est, tunc, quantum picarium capere potest aquae, sanguinem ei minue. Si vero equus aut bos aut asinus debilis et macer fuerit, ad dimidium picarium sanguinem ei minue, scilicet secundum propriam crassitudinem et tenuitatem virtutis suae, et post minutionem i V.
text_translation: Of the goat. If the goat is in any way weakened, give it sufficient and often leaves of eiche to eat, so that it eats thus, and do this often, and it will be healed. Also about the horse and ox. If you wish to incise a vein in the reduction of blood in the horse or ox or ass, and if the same animal is strong and corpulent, then reduce the blood as much as the animal can take of the picarium. If indeed the horse or ox or ass is weak and emaciated, reduce the blood to half the picarium, namely according to its own thickness and thinness of its virtue, and after the reduction i V.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 4—11 = Migne 1169 B. — « v.29— p.219,io = p.l27,.o-87. molle pabnlum et aridum ac suave fenum ei ad comedendum dabis. Et tunc per duas septimanas aut per unam aut per quatuor dies post minutionem quiescat, quatinus vires suas recipiat, quod semper in labore est. Et tribus mensibus transactis in quarto mense eidem animali sanguinem in vena ei minue et non prius, nisi pro magna necessitate infirmitatis, quod tam mali humores in his animalibus non habundant sicut in homine.
text_translation: 4—11 = Migne 1169 B. — « v.29— p.219,io = p.l27,.o-87. molle pabulum et aridum ac suave fenum ei ad comedendum dabis. Et tunc per duas septimanas aut per unam aut per quatuor dies post minutionem quiescat, quatinus vires suas recipiat, quod semper in labore est. Et tribus mensibus transactis in quarto mense eidem animali sanguinem in vena ei minue et non prius, nisi pro magna necessitate infirmitatis, quod tam mali humores in his animalibus non habundant sicut in homine.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De ove, Oyi sanguinem saepe in vena minue, sed tamen modicum sanguinis. lo Accipe storhendbdes radicem unam cum foliis suis et malvae radices duas cum foliis suis atque plantaginis septem radices cum foliis suis et herbas istas cum eisdem radicibus suis in medio mense Aprili. . . De dolore linguae. Si quis in lingua dolet, ita quod aut tumet aut suiret, eam cum modico flebotomo aut cum brema uno vulnere parumper incide, ut ibidem livor erumpat, et melius habebit.
text_translation: On the sheep, Oyi often reduces the blood in the vein, but still a little blood. Take one root of the plant with its leaves and two roots of mallows with their leaves, and seven roots of plantain with their leaves, and these herbs with their same roots in the middle of April. On the pain of the tongue. If someone has pain in the tongue, so that it swells or becomes sore, cut it with a small phlebotomy tool or with a brema with one wound for a short time, so that a bruise appears there, and he will be better.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pectore. Si quis in pectore huset, ita quod ibi primum dolere incipit, accipe salviam et libestichel aequali modo et feniculi bis tantum, ut istorum duorum est, et haec tam diu simul in bonum vinum pone, dum idem vinum saporem ez eis capiat, et tunc abiectis eisdem herbulis idem vinum calefac et ita calidum post cibum bibe, dum saneris. Unde Ckgeeeele, Mali humores in quibusdam hominibus interdum doum faciunt, qui usquc ad cerebrum eorum ascendit et illud ita inficit, quod ex hoc agezzel et obliviosi, id est uergezzen^ fiunt.
text_translation: De pectore. Si quis in pectore habet, ita quod ibi primum dolere incipit, accipe salviam et libestichel aequali modo et feniculi bis tantum, ut istorum duorum est, et haec tam diu simul in bonum vinum pone, dum idem vinum saporem ex eis capiat, et tunc abiectis eisdem herbulis idem vinum calefac et ita calidum post cibum bibe, dum saneris. Unde Ckgeeeele, Mali humores in quibusdam hominibus interdum doum faciunt, qui usque ad cerebrum eorum ascendit et illud ita inficit, quod ex hoc agezzel et obliviosi, id est vergezzen, fiunt.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Accipe ergo heUiheirnezzelum et eam ad succum contunde et tunc ei modicum houmolei adde, et cum dormitum vadis, pectus tuum et tempora tua cum eo perunge et hoc saepe fac, et oblivio in te minuetur. 1 v. 11—16 = p. 196,6-8. — 2 V. 16—19 = Migne 1193 C = p. 122,20-22. — « V. 20—26 « Migne 1186 B— C. — v 30-34 =r Migne 1169 A— B; v. p. 195. V. De vitae signis, Gtun homo sanus in corpore est, si ocnlos puros et perlucidos habet, ouiuscunque coloris sint, signum vitae tenet; ita, si oculi eius hoc raodo perlucidi sunt, quemadmodum candida nubes est, per quam interdmn velut weUden nubes apparet, et iste yiyet nec cito morietur.
text_translation: Take therefore Heilheirnezzelum and crush it into juice, and then add a little humolei to it, and when you have slept, anoint your chest and your temples with it, and do this often, and forgetfulness will decrease in you. 1 v. 11—16 = p. 196,6-8. — 2 v. 16—19 = Migne 1193 C = p. 122,20-22. — « v. 20—26 « Migne 1186 B—C. — v. 30-34 = Migne 1169 A—B; v. p. 195. v. De vitae signis, Gtun homo sanus in corpore est, si oculi puros et perlucidos habet, quorumcunque coloris sint, signum vitae tenet; ita, si oculi eius hoc modo perlucidi sunt, quemadmodum candida nubes est, per quam interdum velut ut nubes apparet, et iste vivet nec cito morietur.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De octdis. Nam visus animae in oculis hominis huius potens est, cum oculi ipsius puri et perlucidi sunt, quoniam anima in corpore eius potenter sedet, quatinus plura opera in ipso operetur. Oculi enim hominis fenestrae animae sunt. De oculis turhidis et signo mortis. Qui autem turbidos oculos habet, cum sanus est, ita quod oculi eius non sunt perlucidi, cuiuscunque coloris sint, signum mortis tenet ; item, si oculi eius hoc modo turbidi sunt, velut aliqua nubes est, quae superius ita spissa est, quod sub ea quasi weithden nubes considerari non potest, et iste cito infirmabitur et mors subsequetur.
text_translation: On eight. For the vision of the soul in the eyes of man is powerful, when the eyes themselves are pure and clear, since the soul powerfully dwells in his body, so that many works are performed within it. For the eyes of man are the windows of the soul. On turbid eyes and the sign of death. He who, although healthy, has turbid eyes, so that his eyes are not clear, whatever their color may be, holds the sign of death; also, if his eyes are thus turbid, as if some clouds are above, so thick that beneath them, as if weithden clouds, cannot be perceived, and this man will quickly grow weak and death will follow.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: In yisu enim oculomm hominis istius anima potens non est, qnia pauca opera ibi operabitur et quasi obnubilata sedet, velut homo, qni in opinione et in dubio est, quando sedem suam deserat et quando domum suam egrediatur. De signis vitCLe. Homo etiam cum sanus est, cuius colbr in genis sub cute rubeus seu moderate rubeus est, ita quod color iste sub cute cemitnr, ut in pomo, quod perlucidum et purum est: signum vitae habet, cum rubens color in genis eius sub cute videtur, ut in candida nnbe evenit, per quam interdum weitden nubes apparet.
text_translation: Indeed, in that of the eyes of this man, the powerful soul is not, because few works will be done there and as if obscured it sits, like a man who is in opinion and in doubt, when he leaves his seat and when he goes forth from his house. Concerning the signs of life. A man, even when healthy, whose color in the cheeks under the skin is red or moderately red, so that this color under the skin is recognized, as in an apple which is clear and pure: it has the sign of life, when the red color in his cheeks under the skin appears, as in a white cloud, through which sometimes clouds are seen.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et iste vivet nec cito morietur. Nam rubeus color, qui sub cnte in genis hominis apparet, ut praedictum est, igneom spiramen vitae, scilicet animae, est, quod anima ignis est, Et ideo in genis ostenditur, quoniam anima in corpore illo secura sedet et cito ezitura non est. De signo mortis. Oum autem homo sanus est, si tuno rubeus seu moderate rubeus color in genis eius snper cutem iacet, ita quod nulla cutis sub eadem rubedine in genis considerari potest: signum mortis habet, cum rubedo in genis eius tam fortiter pressa super cutem iacet, quod sub ea catis yideri non valet, ut in rubeo pomo, sub cuius rubedine nuUa cutis videtur, sed tantum superius quaedam rubedo; et iste cito infirmabitur, et mors subsequetur.
text_translation: And this one will live and not die quickly. For the red color, which appears under the chin in the cheeks of man, as has been said, is the breath of life, namely the soul, since the soul is fire; and therefore it is shown in the cheeks, because the soul sits securely in that body and is not quickly exited. Concerning the sign of death. But when a man is healthy, if the red or moderately red color lies upon the skin of his cheeks, so that no skin beneath the same redness in the cheeks can be seen: he has the sign of death, when the redness in his cheeks lies so strongly upon the skin that beneath it the skin cannot be seen, as in a red apple, beneath whose redness no skin appears, but only above some redness; and this one will quickly grow weak, and death will follow.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Rubeus enim color iste in genis super cutem iacens igneum spiramen yitae animae est, quoniam anima in homine illo vim snam extra corpus suum demonstrat et se in corpore debilem et incertam ostendit, velut homo, qui interdum ad ianuam domus suae procedit, cum de ea egressurus est. Item de signo mortis. Yox quoque, quae semper serena in aliquo homine fait, et cum ille adhuc sanus atque lO in firmitate est, si tunc eadem vox in aliquam raucitatem mutatur, ita quod homo ille tunc raucus manet in consuetudine absque aliqua infirmitate: mortem significat, velut si serena bucina, quae claram vocem semper reddidit, aliquo modo corrumpitur, in raucitatem vertitur.
text_translation: Red is indeed that color on the cheeks lying upon the skin, a fiery spirit of life of the soul, because the soul in that man demonstrates the healthy force outside his body and shows itself weak and uncertain in the body, like a man who sometimes goes to the door of his own house as if about to go out. Also regarding the sign of death. The voice, which is always serene in a certain man, and when he is still healthy and in firmness, if then the same voice changes into some hoarseness, so that the man then remains hoarse in custom without any weakness: it signifies death, like if a serene horn, which always gave a clear voice, is somehow corrupted and turned into hoarseness.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam ubi vox in homine semper serena fuit, hoc impetus scientiae fecit, quoniam in sede sua diu manere debuit, unde et idem homo sapiens est; qui si absque infirmitate iu immobilem raucitatem vertitur, ibi anima praeparat se ad iter hoc, quod corpus suum deserat; et vox, quae rauca, id est obumbrata, in homine sano semper foit in consuetudine absque infirmitate illius, si tunc in serenitatem mutatur, ita quod homo ille postmodum serenam vocem in consuetudine habet absque alia infirmitate: mortem designat, velut si inutilis bucina, quae semper rauca fait, aliquo modo in serenitatem coercetur, serenam vocem compulsa ad horam reddit, quae tamen cito cadit et silet Ubi enim vox rauca in homine est absque infirmitate illius, ibi anima intra corpus illud plus in secreto quam in aperto extra idem corpus operatur; unde et idem homo astutus est.
text_translation: For where the voice in a man is always serene, this was caused by the impetus of science, because it had to remain long in its place, whence also the same man is wise; who, if without infirmity he turns into immobile hoarseness, there the soul prepares itself for this journey, which the body will abandon; and the voice, which is hoarse, that is, shadowed, in a healthy man always was in custom without the infirmity of that, if then it changes into serenity, so that that man afterwards has a serene voice in custom without other infirmity: it denotes death, as if an useless horn, which was always hoarse, is somehow forced into serenity, the serene voice compelled at the hour returns, which however quickly falls and is silent. For where the hoarse voice in a man is without the infirmity of that, there the soul within that body operates more in secret than openly outside the same body; whence also the same man is cunning.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si eadem vox absque infirmitate in stabilem serenitatem mutatur, ibi se anima cito exituram ostendit, cum se extra idem corpus aperte demonstrat, cum prius in eo silenter operabatur. Sed et cum homo in lectum deiectus infirmatur, qualicunque infirmitate detineatur, si caro in facie sufflata eet, ut caro faciei dormientis hominis, cum de somno surgit, et si oculi eius perlucidi sunt, ut cisterna, et aJiquantum aquatiles, iste sine dubio de infirmitate non convalescet, sed morietur.
text_translation: But if the same voice, without infirmity, changes into stable serenity, there the soul shows its quick departure, as it clearly demonstrates itself outside the same body, whereas before it silently worked within it. But also when a man is thrown onto a bed and becomes weak, whatever infirmity holds him back, if the flesh on the face is swollen, like the flesh of a sleeping man's face when he rises from sleep, and if his eyes are clear, like a cistern, and somewhat watery, without doubt he will not recover from the infirmity, but will die.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quod caro faciei hominis huius inflata est, hoc de diversis infirmis humoribus est, qui se in facie illius ostendunt; quod autem oculi eius perlucidi sunt, ut praedictum est, hoc ideo est, quod anima ignem suum in eisdem oculis demonstrat; quod vero aliquantum aquatiles sunt, hoc ideo est, quod ignis animae flammas suas ad exitum producit, quod de eodem corpore cito exitura est. Qui autem faciem aliquantum inflatam habet, ut dormiens bomo, et si etiam oculi eius perlucidi sunt, sed tamen aquatiles non sunt, hic in magna angustia et in magno periculo mortem vix evadet; faciem enim infirmi huius mali humores inflant, sed oculi eius perlucidi ignem animae in se esse demonstrant, sed si aquatiles non sunt, ibi anima flammas suas ad exitum non producit, sed eas ad vitam reservando subtrahit.
text_translation: Nam that the flesh of the face of this man is swollen, this is from various weak humors, which show themselves in his face; that however his eyes are bright, as predicted, this is because the soul shows its fire in those same eyes; that indeed they are somewhat watery, this is because the fire of the soul produces its flames for exit, which will quickly depart from the same body. He however who has a somewhat swollen face, like a sleeping boar, and if also his eyes are bright, but yet are not watery, this one in great distress and in great danger will barely escape death; for indeed the face of this weak man is swollen by bad humors, but his eyes bright show the fire of the soul within them, but if they are not watery, there the soul does not produce its flames for exit, but withholds them for life.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vUae signo, Quod si oculi eius sunt perlucidi, sed aliquantum turbidi et multum aquatiles, cito convalescet et vivet. Nam quod oculi infirmi huius perlucidi non sunt, hoc ideo est, quoniam anima ignem suum ad exitum in eos non misit; quod vero turbidi sunt, hoc de superfiuitate sanguinis est, qui se ibi ad vitam ostendit; et quod multum aquatiles sunt, hoc inde est, quod idem superfiuus sanguis de spumando se purgat et tabem de se ad vitam emittit.
text_translation: On the sign of sorrow: If his eyes are clear, yet somewhat turbid and very watery, he will quickly recover and live. For the fact that the eyes of this weak person are not clear is because the soul has not sent its fire to them; that they are turbid is because of the superfluity of blood, which there reveals itself to life; and that they are very watery is because the same superfluous blood purges itself by frothing and emits waste from itself toward life.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De mortis signo. Homo etiam, qui semper in consuetudine sapiens et prudens fuit. cum sanus in corpore esset, si iste infirmitate desipit, ut homo, qui in mente sua exterritus est et in eadem insipientia permanserit, morietur, et non vivet. Quod enim iste in consuetudine sapiens et prudens fuit, hoc velut de pennis rationalitatis animae erat; qui dum in infirmitate stabiliter desipit, anima pennas rationalitatis subtrahit et se ad exitum praeparat.
text_translation: On the sign of death. A man also, who was always wise and prudent in custom, although he was healthy in body, if he is foolish in weakness, like a man who is terrified in his mind and remains in the same foolishness, will die and will not live. For indeed, that he was wise and prudent in custom, that was as it were the feathers of reason's soul; who, while firmly being foolish in weakness, the soul removes the feathers of reason and prepares itself for the end.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De vitae signo, Qui autem semper sapiens et prudens de pennis rationalitatis animae fuit, cum sanus erat, si infirmus in lecto desipit, anima in rationalitatem quasi de vita se subtrahit; et si tunc deinde in eadem infirmitate ad priorem pmdentiam in stabilitate repente redierit et illam ita tenuerit, anima pennas rationalitatis, quas prius ostendit, iterum nunc ad vitam demonstrat, et sic ille mortem vix evadet. Item de mortis signo, Sed et ille, qui in consuetudine semper insipiens et imprudens fuit, cum sanitatem in corpore habuit, in illo anima pennas rationalitatis ad perfectum non ostendit ; et si in infirmitate sic intelligibilis et in eodem intellectu permanserit, cum in lecto infirmus decumbit, morietur et non vivet, quoniam anima intellectum et vias, quas in alia vita habitura est, iam in exitu suo mani festat.
text_translation: Of the sign of life, Whoever, however, is always wise and prudent of the wings of rationality of the soul, when healthy, if, when ill, he lies in bed and is foolish, the soul withdraws from rationality as if from life; and if then, in the same illness, he suddenly returns to the former prudence in stability and holds it thus, the soul demonstrates the wings of rationality, which it previously showed, again now toward life, and thus he scarcely escapes death. Also, of the sign of death, But also he who, in habit, was always foolish and imprudent, when he had health in the body, in that the soul did not display the wings of rationality to perfection; and if in illness he remains thus intelligible and in the same intellect, when he lies ill in bed, he will die and not live, because the soul, the intellect and the ways which it will habit in another life, already in its exit, manifests.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Qui vero, cum sanus esset, semper insipiens et imprudens fuit, quod anima in ea pennas rationalitatis ad plenum non expandit, et infirmus intelligibilis fit: anima ad intellectum et ad vias alterius vitae se praeparat; sed si deinde in ipsa infirmitate ad priorem insipientiam repente redierit et in illa sic permanserit, mortem vix evadet, quoniam anima ad consuetum locum et ad consuetum statum, quem prius in eodem corpore habuit, se iterum recipit, quod de illo nondum exibit.
text_translation: Whoever, indeed, when healthy was always foolish and unwise, because the soul in it does not fully spread the wings of reason, and becomes weak in understanding: the soul prepares itself for understanding and for the ways of another life; but if then, in that weakness, it suddenly returns to the prior foolishness and remains thus in it, it will scarcely escape death, because the soul again receives itself into the accustomed place and accustomed state which it previously had in the same body, from which it has not yet departed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De pulsu et vitae signis. In quacumque enim ininfirmitate homo in lecto iaceat, si in vena in dextro brachio eius pulsum ordinate et temperate dat, ut homo, qui halitum suum ordinate et temperate introrsum trahit et emittit: vivet et non morietur. Nam quamvis magna infirmitas de ardentibus febribus humorum in homine sit, anima tamen ordinate modum spiraminis sui in se retinet, cum de corpore exitura non est, et ideo pulsus venae ordinatus et temperatus est, quod anima venam ad exitum non movet.
text_translation: On the pulse and signs of life. Indeed, in whatever infirmity a man lies on the bed, if in the vein of his right arm the pulse is given orderly and temperately, as a man who draws in and emits his breath orderly and temperately: he will live and not die. For although a great infirmity from burning fevers of humors in man exists, the soul nevertheless retains orderly the measure of its breathing within itself, since it is not exiting from the body, and therefore the pulse of the vein is orderly and temperate, which the soul does not move the vein towards exit.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Item de mortis signis. Si autem vena in dextro brachio infirmantis hominis festinat, ut homo, qui iam praeparando suffocatur, et nuUo ictu cessat: hic moritur, quoniam anima ad exitum cogitur; et tunc illa blande venam movet et se ab illa solvit, unde pulsus eius ad mortem tunc festinat. Item de mtae signis. Sed si eadem vena inter hanc festinationem unum ictom aut duos ictus ordinate dederit et iterum deinde festinat, ut prius festinavit, anima in festinatione illa angustias ad exitum se habere ostendit, et idcirco venam illam festinanter movet; sed unum ictum aut duos ordinate demonstrat, quoniam in homine illo yitae recordatur et ad yitam praesentem se retorquet, et ita homo ille non moritur, sed vivet.
text_translation: Item, de signis mortis. Si autem vena in dextro brachio infirmantis hominis festinat, ut homo, qui iam praeparando suffocatur, et nullo ictu cessat: hic moritur, quoniam anima ad exitum cogitur; et tunc illa blandè venam movet et se ab illa solvit, unde pulsus eius ad mortem tunc festinat. Item, de signis mortis. Sed si eadem vena inter hanc festinationem unum ictum aut duos ictus ordinate dederit et iterum deinde festinat, ut prius festinavit, anima in festinatione illa angustias ad exitum se habere ostendit, et idcirco venam illam festinanter movet; sed unum ictum aut duos ordinate demonstrat, quoniam in homine illo vitae recordatur et ad vitam praesentem se retorquet, et ita homo ille non moritur, sed vivet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed in dextro brachio pulsus venae praecipue considerandus est, quod haec signa, quae vitam et mortem praetendunt, in dextro brachio praecipue notari possunt, quoniam maxima virtus in dextra est et ipsa semper operatur; in sinistro vero quasi torpor est et in ipsa nihil operatur. In inflexione quoque dextri brachii et in inflexione dextri cruris, scilicet sub genu, pulsus venae discemi veraciter pote^, quod ibi fortitudo animae est. Nam anima iuncturas membrorum in potestate sua fortiter tenet, et cum corpus exitura est, easdem iuncturas solvit, et ideo pulsus eorundem venarum procellas ad mortem in se ostendit; si vero anima exitura non est, quamvis corpus hominis multum doleat, pulsus venarum earundem iunctarum quietus et ordinatus est, quoniam anima ad exitum se non praeparat.
text_translation: But in the right arm, the pulse of the vein should be especially considered, because the signs that threaten life and death can be especially noted in the right arm, since the greatest strength is in the right and it always acts; in the left, however, there is almost a torpor and nothing acts in it. In the flexion of the right arm and in the flexion of the right leg, namely under the knee, the pulse of the vein can be truly discerned, because there the strength of the soul is. For the soul strongly holds the joints of the limbs in its power, and when the body is about to depart, it loosens those same joints, and therefore the pulse of those same veins reveals storms toward death; but if the soul is not about to depart, although the human body suffers greatly, the pulse of those same veins remains quiet and orderly, because the soul does not prepare itself for departure.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be urmarum significationibus. Quidquid homo bibet, sive vinum sive cerevisiam sive medonem sive aquam, quaelibet urina qualitatem sive sanitatis seu infirmitatis ostendit, de quocumque potu procedat; nec urina ista melior illa est, quod onmes urinae de faece sunt, quamvis potus iste pretiosior illo sit. De dissuria, Homo autem, qui urinam retinere non potest, illi stomachus frigidus est et vesica, et ideo potus in eis ad perfectum coqui non poterit, et antequam in eis coquatur, ut tepida aqua eMuit, velnt si aqua ad ignem ponatur et de illo calescere inceperit, superius efQuat, antequam ferveat; sicut etiam in infantibus est, qui urinam retinere non possunt, quoniam nec stomachus nec vesica eorum perfectum calorem habent, sed frigiditatenu De paralysi, Sed si homo frigidus et paralysim habens est, cum in lecto in inflrmitate decumbit, huius urina alba est, ut ebullitio novi musti, et spissa, ut yinum est de motu, cum ducitur.
text_translation: On the signs of urine. Whatever a man drinks, whether wine or beer or mead or water, whatever urine quality, whether of health or sickness, it shows, from whatever drink it proceeds; and this urine is not better than that, because all urines come from the feces, although this drink is more precious than that. On retention of urine. But the man who cannot retain urine, his stomach and bladder are cold, and therefore the drink in them cannot be fully cooked, and before it is cooked in them, it is like warm water boiled, or if water is placed on fire and begins to heat from it, it rises before it boils; as it is also in infants who cannot retain urine, because neither their stomach nor their bladder has perfect heat, but coldness. On paralysis. But if a man who is cold and paralyzed lies in bed in weakness, his urine is white like the boiling of new must, and thick like wine from movement, when it is drawn.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quod frigus et paralysis in eo semper in motu sunt, ideo urina eius ut ebullitio musti alba et spissa est, cum infirmatur. Nam de fiigore alba est, quoniam calor aut ruborem aut alium colorem urinae infert, et spissa est, .quoniam humores in eodem homine destructi sunt nec rectum iter habent, quemadmodum cum aqua in procellis inundat et inspissatur; et si moriturus est, tunc spissitudo urinae eius ad fundum cadit, quoniam humores in eodem homine se separant et lo se ad mortem praeparabt, et eadem urina superius pura remanet, quod tabo a sanguine separata.
text_translation: Nam quod frigus et paralysia in eo semper in motu sunt, ideo urina eius ut ebullitio musti alba et spissa est, cum infirmatur. Nam de frigore alba est, quoniam calor aut ruborem aut alium colorem urinae infert, et spissa est, quoniam humores in eodem homine destructi sunt nec rectum iter habent, quemadmodum cum aqua in procellis inundat et inspissatur; et si moriturus est, tunc spissitudo urinae eius ad fundum cadit, quoniam humores in eodem homine se separant et se ad mortem praeparabit, et eadem urina superius pura remanet, quod tabes a sanguine separata.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: est; et tabo superior est, et sanguis ad fnndum cadit atque.inter superiorem puritatem et inferiorem spissitudinem est ipsa ut infrigidata aqua, quae cito congelabitur, et est etiam ut iam cadens nix, quod a se ab invicem bene discemi possunt, quod flegma est, quodsicutrecens glacies infrigidatur, et quod sicut niz exsolvitur vel siccatur, quoniam a sanguine et ab humoribus separatur. Et haec signa mortis sunt, yelut etiam montuosae et variae ac nigrae et griseae nubes tempestatem saepe designant.
text_translation: it is; and the upper part is wasted, and the blood falls to the bottom, and between the upper purity and the lower thickness it is like chilled water, which quickly freezes, and it is also like falling snow, which can be well separated from itself, which is phlegm, which as fresh ice is chilled, and which as snow melts or dries, because it is separated from the blood and from the humors. And these are signs of death, just as also mountainous and varied and black and gray clouds often indicate a storm.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si nec puritas nec spissitudo nec ut iam recens et imperfecta congelatio nec ut iam cadens nix qualitates eiusdem urinae a se singillatim separatae sunt, ut praedictum est, sed si invicem sibi intermixtae sunt, ita quod singillatim discemi non possunt: yiyet et convalescet; velut magnae tempestates non erunt, ciun aequales nubes in caelo aequaliter apparent. Nam tabo et sanguis ac flegma in eodem homine simul coniuncta sunt, et ideo homo ille tunc non moritur, sed si in eadem urina modica puritas superius apparuerit et nullum signum quasi iam cadens nix, tunc idem homo multum dolet et vix mortem evadet, quoniam tabo a sanguine et a flegmate ad plenum separari non potest, quod modica puritas superior est, cum nulli plocke inferius videntur.
text_translation: If neither purity nor thickness nor, as yet, recent and imperfect congelation nor, as yet, falling snow are the qualities of the same urine separated from one another individually, as has been said before, but if they are mixed with one another mutually, so that they cannot be distinguished individually: then it will recover and convalesce; just as great tempests will not be, although equal clouds appear equally in the sky. For tabo and blood and phlegm are joined together in the same man, and therefore that man does not die, but if in the same urine a slight purity appears above and no sign as though falling snow, then the same man suffers greatly and barely escapes death, because tabo cannot be fully separated from blood and from phlegm, which the slight purity above is, since no pulchra appear below.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et sic homo ille vivet. Qui autem acutas et fortes febres habet, huius urina de ardore ferventis sanguinis rubea et spissa est, quod propter mbentem fervorem Hildegardis oansae et curae ed. Kaiser. I sangoims bnmores iacent, velat in dormitione sint, nec officia sua exercent; et ideo gefeime eonindem hmnoram ad fdndmn descendit et sanguineus est, quoniam iu ruboxe et in fervore suo motus est. Et baec acuta febris nec appetitum nec gustum ciborum sentit, quod bumores ofEcia sua non implent.
text_translation: And thus that man will live. He who, however, has acute and strong fevers, his urine is red and thick from the heat of fervent blood, which, because of the moving heat, Hildegardis oansae et curae ed. Kaiser. I sanguinis humores lie, veiled in sleep, and do not perform their functions; and therefore the effluvia of the same humors descend to the base and are sanguineous, because in redness and in its fervor it is moved. And this acute fever neither feels appetite nor taste of food, because the humors do not fulfill their functions.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed si aliquis bomo banc acutam febrem babet, cupidus in cibis est, et si modum suum in comedendo excedit, febris baec tanto plus furit, quoniam bumores in officiis suis non sunt. Quod si potum requirit et si lOmultum bibit, ex boc non laeditur; quod si non biberet, exsiccaretur. Et si pridem ardentis complexionis erat, vinum in bac peste bibitum ipsum laedit. Unde tunc aquam bibat. Si vero, antequam infirmaretur, frigidae complexionis fuit, tunc si in bac infirmitate vinum bibit, ipsum non laedit.
text_translation: But if someone has a sharp fever, he is eager for food, and if he exceeds his measure in eating, this fever rages more, because the humors are not in their functions. If he requires drink and drinks much, he is not harmed from this; if he did not drink, he would dry up. And if he was previously of a heated complexion, wine drunk in pestilence harms him. Therefore, then drink water. If indeed, before becoming weak, he was of a cold complexion, then if in this weakness he drinks wine, it does not harm him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et si moriturus est, urina eius varia liniamenta in se ostendit, quasi findatur velut glacies, quae in solutione finditur; et bas varietates bumores faoiunt, quod se ad mortem illius separant, et quod in ipsis varietatibus vias morti praeparant; ac superius modice pura est, quoniam a tabe et a sanguine separata est; sed a fundo usque ad puritatem illam spissa est urina, quoniam bumores ad mortem in descensu iacent nibil operantes. Quod si nec liniamenta nec puritas nec spissitudo in eadem urina a se singillatim separata sunt, ita ut separatim videri possunt, sed si ita intermixta sunt, quod discemi non possunt: vivet et convalescet, quemadmodum multae periculosae tempestates non oriuntur, cum nubes in caelo aequaliter apparent.
text_translation: And if he is about to die, his urine shows varied markings within itself, as though it is broken like ice, which is broken in solution; and these variations are made by the humors, which separate themselves from his death, and which in these variations prepare ways for death; and above it is moderately pure, because it is separated from decay and from blood; but from the bottom up to that purity, the urine is thick, because the humors towards death lie in descent doing nothing. If neither the markings nor the purity nor the thickness in the same urine are separately separated from one another, so that they can be seen separately, but if they are so mixed together that they cannot be discerned: he will live and recover, as many dangerous storms do not arise when clouds in the sky appear equally.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam tabo et sanguis et bumores se ab invicem non separant, sed ad vitam sibi coniuncta sunt. Sed si in eadem urina quaedam varietates videntur, sed non per totam urinam, bumores aliquantum separari incipiunt. Sed ad plenum boc non perficere possunt. Et si in eadem urina superius modica puritas est, tabo ab bumoribus separari incipit, et si tunc subtus nuUa varietatis liniamenta discemi possunt, bumores ab invicem se non separant, et ideo bomo ille vivet.
text_translation: Nam tabes et sanguis et humores se ab invicem non separant, sed ad vitam sibi coniuncta sunt. Sed si in eadem urina quaedam varietates videntur, sed non per totam urinam, humores aliquantum separari incipiunt. Sed ad plenum hoc non perficere possunt. Et si in eadem urina superius modica puritas est, tabes ab humoris separari incipit, et si tunc subtus nulla varietatis liniamenta discemi possunt, humores ab invicem se non separant, et ideo homo ille vivet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sed tamen quaedam spissitudo inseparata ibi apparet, quod bumores ab inCAUSAE ET LIB. V. vicem separati non sont; tiinc homo ille multum dolet et mortem vix evadet. De malis humoribus. Et quidam bomines sunt, qui nec dolorem stomachi nec dolorem lateris patiuntur, sed qui alios malos humores in se habent; hi cum morituri sunt, interius arescunt, quod repentinus stupor repentinae pestis, videlicet maioris complexionis, in eis arripit, et idcirco in semetipsis arescunt.
text_translation: But there appears a certain inseparable thickening, because the humors are not separated by the cause and book V. in turn; then that man greatly suffers and barely escapes death. On bad humors. And there are certain men who neither suffer the pain of the stomach nor the pain of the side, but who have other bad humors within them; these, when they are about to die, internally wither, because a sudden stupor of a sudden plague, namely of a greater complexion, seizes them, and therefore they wither within themselves.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et horum urina rectum colorem, cum in urinali excipitur, habet, quod prius sani fuerunt; et cum eadem urina suscepta fuerit, item relin- quatur in urinaH, usque dum infrigidetur. Et si homo ille moriturus est, ipsa urina, cum infrigidata fuerit, mox pallescit, quoniam idem homo per ariditatem interius frigidus est, et circa finem pura est, quoniam tabe ab humoribus et a sanguine separatur, et superius erit, velut modico pulvere aspersa sit, quod humores hominis exterriti sunt, et velut pulverem de se emittunt, cum ad mortem se praeparant, quemadmodum pulvis dispergitur, cum tangitur; et hoc signum mortis est.
text_translation: And the urine has the correct color, when received in the urine vessel, which it had when the person was healthy; and when the same urine is taken, it is left behind in the urine, until it becomes chilled. And if that man is about to die, the urine, when chilled, quickly turns pale, because the same man is internally cold due to dryness, and at the end it is pure, because it is separated by decay from the humors and from the blood, and it will be above, as if sprinkled with a little powder, which the humors of the man are terrified, and as if they emit powder from themselves when preparing for death, just as dust is dispersed when touched; and this is the sign of death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si eadem urina non pallescit et circa finem pura non est, et si etiam superius ^ non erit, velut modico pulvere aspersa sit, sed non aequalis est: vivet et convalescet, (quemadmodum grandes tempestates non fiunt, cum aequales nubes in caelo aequaliter apparent, quoniam tabo et sanguis et ceteri humores, qui in eodem homine sunt, ad mortem nondum ab invicem se- parantur. Et si eadem iirina aliquantum rubea est et circa iinem pura ac superius in medio velut pretiosum vinum, quod quasi modicum sagiminis superius natare in se ostendit, procul dubio homo ille multum dolet et mortem vix evadet.
text_translation: If the same urine does not become pale and is not pure at the end, and if it is also not above, as if sprinkled with a little powder, but is not equal: the person will live and recover, (just as great storms do not occur when equal clouds appear equally in the sky, because the tumor and blood and other humors, which are in the same person, have not yet separated from each other towards death. And if the same urine is somewhat red and is pure at the end and above in the middle as if a precious wine, which as if a little sagiminis shows its nature in itself, undoubtedly that man suffers greatly and barely escapes death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam in infirmitate hac rubea urina ostendit, quod sanitas eiusdem hominis finienda non est, quod eadem sanitas infirmitatem eiusdem hominis calore pertransit et incalescere incipit; et ita urina haec rubea efi&citur. Sed quod circa finem pura est, tabem ab humoribus et a sanguine separari demonstrat, et quod superius velut bonum vinum in medio inunctum e&t, quasi modicum sagiminis supernatet, dolorem iecoris indicat, quod de superflua 15 • pingaedine dolet.
text_translation: Nam in infirmitate hac rubea urina ostendit, quod sanitas eiusdem hominis finienda non est, quod eadem sanitas infirmitatem eiusdem hominis calore pertransit et incalescere incipit; et ita urina haec rubea efficitur. Sed quod circa finem pura est, tabem ab humoribus et a sanguine separari demonstrat, et quod superius velut bonum vinum in medio inunctum est, quasi modicum sagiminis supernatet, dolorem iecoris indicat, quod de superflua pinguedine dolet.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Unde etiam signnm, id est spumam, eiusdem pinguedinis eicit. Et sic magnam infirmitatem et periculum eadem urina quidem ostendit, sed tamen rubor eius yitam quasi vix haerentem ostendit. Be cotidiana. Homo, qui cotidianas febres habet, si urina eius spissa est et si etiam colorem habet similem colori vini, cito convalescet. Nam cotidianae febres de iniusta commotione humorum nascuntur, unde et urina hominis illius spissa est, quod humores iniuste moti snnt ; et etiam eaedem febres ab iniusta digestione oriuDtur, cum aut egestio iniuste dura aut iniuste solubilis est; et ideo urina eiusdem hominis quasi colorem vini, quod mutatum est, ostendit.
text_translation: Hence also the sign, that is, foam, of the same fatness ejects. And thus the same urine indeed shows great weakness and danger, but nevertheless its redness shows life as if barely clinging. They are daily. The man who has daily fevers, if his urine is thick and if it also has a color similar to the color of wine, will quickly recover. For daily fevers arise from unjust movement of humors, whence also the urine of that man is thick, because the humors are unjustly moved; and also those same fevers arise from unjust digestion, when either the digestion is unjustly hard or unjustly soluble; and therefore the urine of that same man shows a color like wine, which has been changed.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: 8i autem urina haec pura et lucida est secundum aquam, morietur, quoniam sanguis eiusdem hominis in frigus yersus est, et ideo ceteri humores simul confluunt, velut lac coagulatur, quod calorem et sanguinem non habet; unde etiam eadem urina pura et lucida est, quoniam cum humoribus mixta non est, quod illi offlcia sua non exercent. Quod si urina haec aliquantum palUda et spissa est et aliquantum aquosa, homo ille multum dolet et mortem vix evadet.
text_translation: If, however, this urine is pure and clear as water, he will die, because the blood of that man has turned toward cold, and therefore the other humors flow together, as milk curdles, which has neither heat nor blood; hence also this urine is pure and clear, because it is not mixed with humors, which do not exercise their functions. If, however, this urine is somewhat pale and thick and somewhat watery, that man suffers greatly and barely escapes death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam quod idem homo frigus intra se habet, urina eius pallida et aliquantum aquosa est, et quod etiam humores in eo velut procella quasi ad mortem inundant, eadem urina spissa est, et ideo etiam idem homo diu dolet, sed tamen ad mortem non perveniet. Item de tertiana. Qui autem tertianas febres habet, si urina eius, cum iam excipitur, sanguinea est, sed si de rubedine sanguinis non mutatur, sed si ita rubea permanet, et si etiam spissa flt: non moritur, sed diu languet.
text_translation: For indeed, the same man who has cold within himself has urine that is pale and somewhat watery, and when the humors within him flood like a storm as if toward death, the same urine is thick, and therefore the same man laments for a long time, but nevertheless does not reach death. Also concerning tertian fever. But whoever has tertian fevers, if his urine, when already taken, is bloody, but if it does not change from the redness of blood, but if it remains so red, and if it is also thick: he does not die, but lingers for a long time.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Hu mores enim in homine illo in ferventem ardorem versi sunt, et ideo urina eius sanguinea est et propter fortitudinem eiusdem ardoris in rubore illo durat. Et quod idem humores in homine illo fervent, digestio eius recta et naturalis esse non potest, unde eit succus eiusdem uri nae aliquantum commiscetur; et ideo est spissa. Sed idem homo ob hanc infirmitatem diu languet, sed non moritur, quod humores in eo ab invicem se non separant. Et cum eadem urina in urinale noviter excepta est et rubea est, si tunc deinde aliquantum in pallorem mutatur, et si etiam in ea varia liniamenta ut venulae apparent, scilicet rubeae et aquosae ac turbidae: signum mortis est, et morietur, Nam urina, quae propter ardorem et calorem eisdem ho- b.
text_translation: Humores enim in homine illo in ferventem ardorem versi sunt, et ideo urina eius sanguinea est et propter fortitudinem eiusdem ardoris in rubore illo durat. Et quod idem humores in homine illo fervent, digestio eius recta et naturalis esse non potest, unde ex succus eiusdem urinae aliquantum commiscetur; et ideo est spissa. Sed idem homo ob hanc infirmitatem diu languet, sed non moritur, quod humores in eo ab invicem se non separant. Et cum eadem urina in urinale noviter excepta est et rubea est, si tunc deinde aliquantum in pallorem mutatur, et si etiam in ea varia liniamenta ut venulae apparent, scilicet rubeae et aquosae ac turbidae: signum mortis est, et morietur. Nam urina, quae propter ardorem et calorem eisdem hominibus...

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: minis rubea est, si in pallorem vertitur, vires fortitudinis caloris ad plenum non habet, quoniam idem homo interius in&igidatus est; et tunc etiam varia liniamenta diversi coloris in urina illa apparent, quod humores in eodem homine ab invicem separantur, qui diversos colores secundum lO officia sua habent; scilicet rubei, quoniam calor et sanguis a frigidate separantur, aquosi autem, quod tabo et sanguia de sanguine et de calore secedit, turbidi vero, quoniam melancolia et similes humores ab invicem separantur.
text_translation: The redness is minimal, if it turns pale, because it does not have the full strength of heat's fortitude, since the same man internally is rigid; and then also various streaks of various colors appear in that urine, because the humors in that man are separated from each other, which have various colors according to their own functions; namely red, because heat and blood are separated from cold, watery, because tabo and sanguia separate from blood and from heat, turbid indeed, because melancholy and similar humors are separated from each other.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod si eadem urina varia liniamenta a diversis humoribus habet, sed si in ea rubea liniamenta non videntur, sanguis et calor eius adhuc commixti sunt et non separati; unde etiam adhuc vitam ostendit; sed homo ille multum dolet et mortem vix evadet. Item de quartana. Qui vero quartanas febres habet, si urina eius spissa est et rubea et si varia liniamenta in ea apparent ut venae: homo ille dolet, ita quod aridus fit, sed tamen vivet. Nam si urina hominis huius spissa est, digestio eius ad urinam se commiscet, et ideo illa inspissatur; et si rubea est, calor adhuc in sanguine manet nec se a sanguine separat.
text_translation: If the same urine has varied streaks from different humors, but if red streaks are not visible in it, blood and its heat are still mixed and not separated; hence it still shows life; but that man suffers greatly and will scarcely escape death. Likewise concerning quartana. He who has quartana fevers, if his urine is thick and red, and if varied streaks appear in it like veins: that man suffers, so that he becomes dry, but nevertheless he will live. For if the urine of this man is thick, his digestion mixes with the urine, and thus it becomes thickened; and if it is red, heat still remains in the blood and does not separate from the blood.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem varia liniamenta habet, diversi humores adhuc sibi cohaerent; et ita homo ille de infinuitatibus istis quidem arescit, sed tamen, quod solutio humorum in eo nondum est, vivere poterit. JDe signo mortis in urina, Si autem urina fuerit alba ut venenum et similis coagulato lacti et si in medio sui est similis nubi, quae purpurea et alba atque turbida est: signum mortis est, et morietur. Urina enim alba ut venenum et similis coagulato lacti ostendit, quod naturalis calor ab eodem homine recessit, quapropter et urina eius alba est; et ideo etiam venenosa, quae in humoribus illis faermit, coagalantur, quod rectum calorem non habent; et etiam in medio sui est ut nubes, quae purpurea et alba et turbida apparet, quoniam melancolia in medio humorum quasi in fortitudine eorum est; scilicet purpurea, quod colorem suum quasi in vulnus vertit, cum iam solvitur; et alba, quoniam ab invicem diflftuit yenenum eius apparet et quod in fortitudine eius deficiat demonstrat; et turbida, quoniam malum et naturalem fumum, quem prius in se habuit, nunc emittit.
text_translation: If, however, it has varied lineaments, diverse humors still adhere to one another; and thus that man indeed becomes weak from these infirmities, yet nonetheless, because the resolution of humors within him is not yet complete, he may live. Concerning the sign of death in urine: If, however, the urine is white as poison and similar to coagulated milk, and if in the middle of itself it is similar to a cloud that is purple, white, and turbid: this is the sign of death, and he will die. For urine white as poison and similar to coagulated milk indicates that natural heat has departed from that man, therefore his urine is white; and thus also venomous, which in those humors coagulates, because they do not possess rectitude of heat; and also in the middle of itself it is like a cloud that appears purple, white, and turbid, because melancholy is in the middle of the humors as if in their fortitude; namely, purple, because its color turns as if toward a wound, when it is already dissolving; and white, because the poison of it appears diffused from one another and demonstrates that it lacks fortitude; and turbid, because it emits evil and natural smoke, which it previously held within itself.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Quod eadem urina similis est nubi, quae purpurea et alba et turbida est, ut praefatum est, et si tunc etiam circa finem suum aliquantum pura est, ita quod per onmia spissa non est: homo ille multum dolet et mortem vix evadet; sed tamen citius convalescet quam ille, cuius urina rubea est. Nam urina purpurea melancoliam vulneratam demonstrat, et alba eam iam in defectu esse ostendit, et turbida illam fumum suum ad defectum emittentem manifestat; sed circa finem suum puritas, ita quod in medio et non per omnia spissa est, declarat humores hominis illius ad plenum adhuc a se invi cem separatos non esse , cum circa finem eiusdem urinae puritas est et in medio eius spissitudo, quoniam idem humores adhuc sibi cohaerent.
text_translation: That same urine is similar to a cloud which is purple and white and cloudy, as previously stated, and if then also at its end it is somewhat pure, so that it is not thick throughout: that man suffers greatly and scarcely escapes death; yet he will recover more quickly than he whose urine is red. For purple urine demonstrates a wounded melancholy, and white urine shows that it is already in defect, and cloudy urine manifests that it emits its smoke towards defect; but at its end purity, so that it is pure in the middle and not throughout, declares that the humors of that man are still not fully separated from themselves, since at the end of that urine's passage purity is present and thickness in the middle, because the same humors still adhere to themselves.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Et ita homo ille mortem evadet et sanitatem citius recipiat quam homo iste, cuius urina rubea est, quoniam ille interius magnum ardorem habet, de quo cito solvi non poterit. De flvminum diversUate, Sed notandum est, quod aqua fluminum secundum temperiem aeris mutatur. Gum enim magnus ventus est, procellae in undis et in terrore oriuntur. Et cum sol in fervore suo ardet, aquae fervent et spumam habent. In tranquillo autem et temperato aere aquae lenes sunt et quietae ac pulchrae.
text_translation: And thus that man will escape death and quickly receive health than this man, whose urine is red, because he has great heat internally, from which he cannot quickly resolve. Concerning the diversity of rivers, it should be noted that the water of rivers changes according to the temperature of the air. When indeed a great wind is, storms arise in waves and in terror. And when the sun burns in its fervor, waters boil and have foam. In calm and temperate air, however, waters are gentle and quiet and beautiful.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Sic etiam in unna hominis est. Nam cum periculosa infirmitas ut magnus ventus in homine surgit, ita quod anima illius a corpore solvatur, magnae etiam procellae in urina eius multipUcantur. Cum autem nimius fervor humorum in homine est ut ardens sol, tunc etiam urina in homine secundum calorem et ardorem in eo apparet. Cum enim bomo in reoto et in tranqaillo modo corporis sni est, tnnc etiam nrina eins secnndum rectam temperiem demonstratnr. De urinae mspectione, Urina antem infirmi ad considerationem sospitatis ant infirmitatis suscipienda est, cnm ille post dormitionem evigilat, qnod nrina eins tunc recte temperata et colorata est secnndnm qnalitatem einsdem infirmi.
text_translation: Thus also in one man is it. For when a perilous infirmity arises in a man as a great wind, so that his soul is loosened from the body, great tempests also multiply in his urine. When, however, excessive heat of humors in a man is as a burning sun, then also urine in a man appears according to the heat and ardor in him. For when a man is in recto and in tranquillo mode of his body, then also his urine is demonstrated according to the right temperance. Of urine's inspection, Urine indeed of the infirm is to be considered for the taking up of suspicion and infirmity's acceptance, when he awakens after sleep, that his urine then is rightly tempered and colored according to the quality of the same infirm.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Nam cum homo dormit, in qniete sine ulla motione est, et ideo humores et sudor, qui in eo suntj ad qualitatem suam currunt, secundum quod tunc homo se interius habet. Si autem infirmus dormire non potest, tnnc ad considerationem qualitatis eius urina ipsius suscipiatur, quam in nocte emittit, vel quam circa crepusculum diei eicit, quod temperies noctis vel temperies, quae circa ortum diei est, humores, qui in illo sunt, qualitatem, quae in ipso est, aliquantum composuit.
text_translation: For when a man sleeps, he is in quiet without any motion, and therefore the humors and sweat, which are in him, flow back to their quality, according to what the man then has within himself. If, however, the weak one cannot sleep, then to consider the quality of his urine, let his urine be received, which he emits in the night, or which he expels around the twilight of the day, which the temperaments of the night or the temperaments, which are around the rising of the day, compose the humors, which are in him, somewhat to the quality that is in him.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: De terrae immobiUtate et egestionis discretione. Terra autem et limus immobiles sunt, ita quod ab aura moveri non possunt; sed aliquantum odorem emittunt, cum aut pluvia iam descendit aut cum iam in proximo pluvia descendere debet; et sic fit in egestione hominis. Nam in egestione perfecta signa yitam et mortem praesignificantia cognosci non possunt, quod egestio hominis ut terra immobilis est. Sed tamen in foetore eius vita aut mors hominis aliquantum discemi potest, sed tamen cum diffi- cultate et raro.
text_translation: On the immobility of the earth and the distinction of egestion. The earth and the mud are immobile, so that they cannot be moved by the wind; but they emit some odor when either the rain has already descended or when the rain is about to descend soon; and thus it happens in the egestion of man. For in perfect egestion, the signs that foreshadow life and death cannot be known, because the egestion of man is like the immobile earth. But in its odor, life or death of man can be somewhat distinguished, but with difficulty and rarely.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Attamen si egestio secundum consuetudinem suam multum foetet, ut semper foetere solet, non est signum mortis, quod calida putredo in foetore illo apparet; si autem eadem egestio non multum foetet et si idem foetor, quam tunc habet, alienus est a consuetudinali foetore: signum mortis est, quod calor in putredine illa . deficit et quoniam in alienum modum versus est, cum etiam humores in eodem homine eversi sunt. Quod si etiam ipsa egestio nigra et arida est, signum mortis est, quod melancolia eandem digestionem in nigredinem et in aridi- tatem evertit, ubi humores eiusdem homines ad mortem se praeparaverunt.
text_translation: Nevertheless, if the discharge, according to its customary nature, greatly stinks, as it always does, it is not a sign of death, because warm putrefaction appears in that stench; but if the same discharge does not greatly stink, and if the same stench it has is foreign to its customary stench: it is a sign of death, because heat in that putrefaction lacks, and because it is turned in a foreign manner, since the humors in that same man are also overturned. That if the discharge itself is black and dry, it is a sign of death, because melancholy overturns the same digestion into blackness and into dryness, where the humors of that same man have prepared themselves for death.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Si autem egestio nigra et arida est et tamen secundnm rectam consaetudinem foetet, homo ille vix eyadere potest mortem, quamyis melancolia in nigredinem et in ariditatem egestionis hominis defectmn saiun ostendat, cam idem homo interius aliqaantabi in nigredinem et in ariditatem per melancoliam versus rectam tamen ealorem in foetore eiasdem putredinis declaret. Si vero alienam foetorem a consuetadinali foetore habet, mortale est signam, qaoniam rectas calor hominis in defectam patredinis illias subtractas est.
text_translation: If, however, the discharge is black and dry and yet smells according to the proper custom, that man scarcely can endure death, although melancholy shows the defect in the blackness and dryness of the discharge, as the same man internally, through melancholy, turns toward the proper heat in the smell of the same putrefaction. If, however, it has a strange smell different from the customary smell, it is a sign of death, since the proper heat of the man is subtracted from that defective putrefaction.

source_text: Causae et Curae (Hildegard von Bingen), ed. Kaiser 1903
text: Be aquarum diversitate et balneorum.
